First Citizen:
Before we go any further, listen to me.

All:
Speak, speak.

First Citizen:
Have you all decided that you would rather die than starve?

All:
We have. We have.

First Citizen:
First, you know that Caius Marcius is the greatest enemy of the people.

All:
We know it, we know it.

First Citizen:
Let's kill him, and we'll get grain at our own price.
Is that the decision?

All:
No more talking about it; let it be done. Let's go, let's go!

Second Citizen:
One word, good citizens.

First Citizen:
We are considered poor citizens, and the patricians are considered good.
What they have in excess would relieve us. If they would give us just their surplus food while it was still fresh, we might think they were relieving us out of humanity.
But they think we are too much of a burden. The hunger that afflicts us, the source of our misery, is like a list that details their abundance. Our suffering is their gain. Let us get revenge for this with our pikes before we become as thin as rakes. For the gods know I say this out of hunger for bread, not thirst for revenge.

Second Citizen:
Do you want to go specifically against Caius Marcius?

All:
Against him first. He's a complete dog to the common people.

Second Citizen:
Do you consider the services he has done for his country?

First Citizen:
Very well; and I would be happy to give him a good report for it, but he pays himself by being proud.

Second Citizen:
No, but don't speak maliciously.

First Citizen:
I tell you, what he has done famously, he did it for that reason: though soft-hearted men might be content to say it was for his country, he did it to please his mother and to be proud, which he is, to the very height of his virtue.

Second Citizen:
What he cannot help in his nature, you consider a vice in him. You must not say he is greedy in any way.

First Citizen:
If I must not, I don't need to be short of accusations;
he has more than enough faults to get tired of repeating them.
What are these shouts? The other side of the city has risen up. Why are we standing here talking? To the Capitol!

All:
Come, come.

First Citizen:
Wait! Who is this coming?

Second Citizen:
The worthy Menenius Agrippa; one that has always loved the people.

First Citizen:
He's honest enough. I wish all the rest were so!

MENENIUS:
What work are you doing, my countrymen? Where are you going with bats and clubs? What's the matter? Please, speak.

First Citizen:
Our business is not unknown to the senate; they have
had a hint for the last two weeks about what we intend to do,
which now we'll show them in actions. They say poor
suitors have strong words; they will soon know we
have strong arms too.

MENENIUS:
Why, masters, my good friends, my honest neighbors,
Will you destroy yourselves?

First Citizen:
We cannot, sir, we are destroyed already.

MENENIUS:
I tell you, friends, the patricians have the most charitable care for you. For your wants, your suffering in this famine, you might as well strike at the heavens with your staffs as lift them against the Roman state, whose course will continue on the way it takes, breaking ten thousand restraints of a stronger link than can ever appear in your way. For the famine, the gods, not the patricians, make it, and your knees to them, not your arms, must help. Alas, you are transported by calamity to a place where more awaits you, and you slander the leaders of the state, who care for you like fathers, when you curse them as enemies.

First Citizen:
Care for us! True, indeed! They've never cared for us
yet: they let us starve while their store-houses are crammed with grain; they make laws for usury to support usurers; they repeal daily any wholesome act established against the rich, and provide more piercing laws daily, to chain up and restrain the poor. If the wars don't eat us up, they will; and that's all the love they have for us.

MENENIUS:
Either you must
confess yourselves to be wonderfully malicious,
or be accused of foolishness. I will tell you
a pretty tale: it may be you have heard it;
But, since it serves my purpose, I will venture
to make it a little more stale.

First Citizen:
Well, I'll hear it, sir: yet you must not think to
brush off our disgrace with a tale: but, if it pleases you, deliver it.

MENENIUS:
There was a time when all the body's members
rebelled against the belly, and accused it like this:
That it just remained like a gulf
in the middle of the body, idle and inactive,
always storing the food, never sharing
the same labor as the rest, where the other instruments
did see and hear, devise, instruct, walk, feel,
and, mutually participating, did minister
to the common appetite and affection
of the whole body. The belly answered--

First Citizen:
Well, sir, what answer did the belly make?

MENENIUS:
Sir, I will tell you. With a kind of smile,
which never came from the lungs, but just like this--
for, look you, I can make the belly smile
as well as speak--it mockingly replied
to the discontented members, the mutinous parts
that envied what it received; just as you malign our senators because they are not like you.

First Citizen:
Your belly's answer? What!
The kingly-crowned head, the vigilant eye,
the counselor heart, the arm our soldier,
our steed the leg, the tongue our trumpeter,
with other defenses and small helps
in this body of ours, if they--

MENENIUS:
What then?
By heavens, this fellow speaks! What then? what then?

First Citizen:
Should be restrained by the greedy belly,
which is the sewer of the body--

MENENIUS:
Well, what then?

First Citizen:
The former agents, if they complained,
What could the belly answer?

MENENIUS:
I will tell you;
if you'll give a small amount of what you have little of--
patience for a while, you'll hear the belly's answer.

First Citizen:
You're taking a long time.

MENENIUS:
Note this, my good friend;
your most serious belly was deliberate,
not rash like its accusers, and thus answered:
'It is true, my incorporated friends,' he said,
'That I receive the general food first,
which you live on; and it is fitting,
because I am the store-house and the shop
of the whole body: but, if you remember,
I send it through the rivers of your blood,
even to the court, the heart, to the seat of the brain;
and, through the body's passages and offices,
the strongest nerves and small inferior veins
receive from me that natural sustenance
by which they live: and though that all at once,
you, my good friends,'--this says the belly, mark me,--

First Citizen:
Yes, sir; very well, very well.

MENENIUS:
'Though all at once you cannot
see what I deliver out to each,
yet I can make my accounts clear, that all
of you receive back from me the best of it all,
and leave me only the bran.' What do you say to that?

First Citizen:
It was an answer. How do you apply this to us?

MENENIUS:
The senators of Rome are this good belly,
and you are the mutinous members. For if you examine
their counsels and their cares, and digest things rightly
concerning the well-being of the common people, you will find
no public benefit that you receive
that doesn't proceed or come from them to you,
and in no way from yourselves. What do you think,
you, the great toe of this assembly?

First Citizen:
I, the great toe! Why the great toe?

MENENIUS:
Because, being one of the lowest, basest, poorest
of this most wise rebellion, you go first.
You scoundrel, who is the worst in a race,
you lead first to gain some advantage.
But get your stiff bats and clubs ready:
Rome and its rats are about to battle;
one side must suffer.
Hail, noble Marcius!

MARCIUS:
Thanks. What's the matter, you rebellious rogues,
that, by scratching the poor itch of your opinions,
you make yourselves into scabs?

First Citizen:
We always have your good word.

MARCIUS:
Anyone who gives good words to you would flatter something beneath contempt. What do you want, you dogs, who like neither peace nor war? The one frightens you, the other makes you proud. Anyone who trusts you, where he should find you lions, finds you hares; where he should find you foxes, he finds you geese. You are no more reliable than a burning coal on ice, or a hailstone in the sun. Your virtue is to praise the man whose offense overcomes him and to curse the justice that was done. Anyone who deserves greatness deserves your hate; and your affections are like a sick man's appetite, who desires most that which would make his illness worse. He that depends on your favors swims with fins of lead and tries to chop down oaks with rushes. Hang you! Trust you? With every minute you change your mind, and call him noble who was just now your enemy, and him vile who was your hero. What's the matter, that in these different parts of the city you cry out against the noble senate, who, under the gods, keep you in awe, which otherwise would make you feed on one another? What are they seeking?

MENENIUS:
For corn at their own prices; of which, they say,
the city is well stored.

MARCIUS:
Hang them! They say!
They'll sit by the fire and presume to know
what's being done in the Capitol; who's likely to rise,
who thrives and who declines; they side with factions and spread rumors of
conjectural marriages; making some parties strong
and weakening those they don't like,
even below their own cobbled shoes. They say there's
enough grain!
If only the nobility would lay aside their pity
and let me use my sword, I'd make a pile of corpses
with thousands of these quartered slaves, as high
as I could thrust my lance.

MENENIUS:
No, these people are almost thoroughly persuaded;
for though they lack discretion in abundance,
they are extremely cowardly. But, I beg you,
what does the other group say?

MARCIUS:
They have dispersed. Hang them!
They said they were hungry, and sighed out proverbs,
that hunger breaks stone walls, that dogs must eat,
that meat was made for mouths, that the gods did not send
corn for the rich men only. With these scraps of sayings,
they voiced their complaints; which being answered,
and a petition granted to them, a strange one—
to break the heart of generosity,
and make bold power look pale—they threw their caps
as if they would hang them on the horns of the moon,
shouting their rivalry.

MENENIUS:
What was granted to them?

MARCIUS:
Five tribunes to defend their common wisdom,
of their own choice: one is Junius Brutus,
Sicinius Velutus, and I don't know—by God!
The mob should have first torn the roofs off the city
before they prevailed with me like that. In time, it will
take over power and bring forth greater issues
for insurrection to argue about.

MENENIUS:
This is strange.

MARCIUS:
Go, get home, you fragments!

Messenger:
Where's Caius Marcius?

MARCIUS:
Here. What's the matter?

Messenger:
The news is, sir, the Volsces are in arms.

MARCIUS:
I'm glad of it. Then we shall have a way to get rid of
our stale surplus population. See, our best elders are coming.

First Senator:
Marcius, it's true what you have recently told us;
the Volsces are in arms.

MARCIUS:
They have a leader,
Tullus Aufidius, who will put you to the test.
I sin in envying his nobility,
and if I were anything but what I am,
I would wish I were only he.

COMINIUS:
You have fought together.

MARCIUS:
If half the world were at war with the other half, and he
were on my side, I would revolt just to make
my wars only with him. He is a lion
that I am proud to hunt.

First Senator:
Then, worthy Marcius,
accompany Cominius to these wars.

COMINIUS:
It is your former promise.

MARCIUS:
Sir, it is;
and I am steadfast. Titus Lartius, you
shall see me once more strike at Tullus' face.
What, are you stiff? Are you staying out?

TITUS:
No, Caius Marcius;
I'll lean upon one crutch and fight with the other,
before I stay behind from this business.

MENENIUS:
Oh, a true-bred man!

First Senator:
Your company is requested at the Capitol, where, I know,
our greatest friends are waiting for us.

TITUS:

COMINIUS:
Noble Marcius!

First Senator:

MARCIUS:
No, let them follow:
The Volsces have much grain; take these rats there
to gnaw their storehouses. Worshipful mutineers,
your valor shows itself well. Please, follow.

SICINIUS:
Was there ever a man as proud as this Marcius?

BRUTUS:
He has no equal.

SICINIUS:
When we were chosen as tribunes for the people,--

BRUTUS:
Did you notice his lip and eyes?

SICINIUS:
No, but his taunts.

BRUTUS:
When he's provoked, he won't hesitate to mock the gods.

SICINIUS:
He would mock the modest moon.

BRUTUS:
The present wars are consuming him; he has grown
too proud to be so valiant.

SICINIUS:
Such a nature,
tickled with good success, disdains the shadow
which he treads on at noon. But I do wonder
how his insolence can tolerate being commanded
under Cominius.

BRUTUS:
Fame, which he aims for,
in which he is already well-regarded, cannot
be better held or more attained than by
a position below the first. For whatever goes wrong
shall be the general's fault, even though he performs
to the utmost of a man, and fickle judgment
will then cry out about Marcius, 'Oh, if he
had been in charge of the business!'

SICINIUS:
Besides, if things go well,
the high opinion that so sticks on Marcius shall
rob Cominius of his merits.

BRUTUS:
Come:
Half of all Cominius's honors go to Marcius,
though Marcius didn't earn them, and all his faults
will be seen as honors for Marcius, though indeed
he doesn't deserve any of it.

SICINIUS:
Let's go from here, and hear
how the mission is being handled, and in what manner,
more than his usual singularity, he goes
on this present action.

BRUTUS:
Let's go.

First Senator:
So, your opinion is, Aufidius,
that the Romans have gotten into our councils
and know how we proceed.

AUFIDIUS:
Isn't it yours?
Whatever has been thought of in this state,
that could be put into action before Rome
found out? It hasn't been four days
since I heard from there; these are the words: I think
I have the letter here; yes, here it is.
'They have raised a force, but it is not known
whether for the east or west. The famine is great;
the people are rebellious; and it is rumored,
Cominius, Marcius your old enemy,
who is more hated in Rome than by you,
and Titus Lartius, a most valiant Roman,
these three lead this preparation
wherever it is headed. Most likely it's for you.
Consider it.'

First Senator:
Our army is in the field.
We never doubted that Rome was ready
to answer us.

AUFIDIUS:
Nor did you think it was foolish
to keep your great plans hidden until
they had to show themselves; which, in the planning,
it seemed, was discovered by Rome. By this discovery,
we will be hindered in our aim, which was
to take many towns before Rome
even knew we were on the move.

Second Senator:
Noble Aufidius,
take your commission; hurry to your troops.
Let us alone to guard Corioli.
If they lay siege to us, to remove them,
bring your army. But, I think, you'll find
they haven't prepared for us.

AUFIDIUS:
Oh, don't doubt that;
I speak from certainties. No, more,
some parts of their force are already out,
and heading this way. I leave you, your honors.
If Caius Marcius and I happen to meet,
it's sworn between us we will always fight
until one can do no more.

All:
The gods assist you!

AUFIDIUS:
And keep your honors safe!

First Senator:
Farewell.

Second Senator:
Farewell.

All:
Farewell.

VOLUMNIA:
I pray you, daughter, sing; or express yourself in a
more comfortable way. If my son were my husband, I
should rejoice more freely in that absence where he
won honor than in the embraces of his bed where
he would show the most love. When he was still
tender-bodied and the only son of my womb, when
youth with handsomeness drew everyone's gaze his way, when
for a day of kings' entreaties a mother would not
sell an hour of seeing him, I, considering
how honor would suit such a person, that it was
no better than a picture-like to hang by the wall if
renown didn't make it stir, was pleased to let him seek
danger where he was likely to find fame. To a cruel
war I sent him; from where he returned, his brows
bound with oak. I tell you, daughter, I did not leap
for joy more at first hearing he was a boy
than now in first seeing he had proved himself a
man.

VIRGILIA:
But if he had died in the business, madam; what then?

VOLUMNIA:
Then his good reputation would have been my son; I
would have found my offspring in that. Hear me profess
sincerely: if I had a dozen sons, each equally loved
and none less dear than yours and my good Marcius,
I would have rather had eleven die nobly for their
country than one voluptuously grow fat out of action.

Gentlewoman:
Madam, the Lady Valeria has come to visit you.

VIRGILIA:
I beg you, give me leave to go to my room.

VOLUMNIA:
Indeed, you shall not.
I think I hear your husband's drum,
see him pull Aufidius down by the hair,
as children do to a bear, the Volscians running from him.
I think I see him stamp like this, and call like this:
'Come on, you cowards! you were conceived in fear,
though you were born in Rome.' His bloody brow,
wiping it with his mailed hand, then he goes forth,
like a harvest-man who's tasked to mow
everything or lose his pay.

VIRGILIA:
His bloody brow! O Jupiter, no blood!

VOLUMNIA:
Away, you fool! It becomes a man more
than gold on his trophy. The breasts of Hecuba,
when she suckled Hector, did not look lovelier
than Hector's forehead when it spit forth blood
at a Grecian sword, in contempt. Tell Valeria,
we are ready to bid her welcome.

VIRGILIA:
Heavens bless my lord from the fierce Aufidius!

VOLUMNIA:
He'll beat Aufidius's head below his knee
and tread upon his neck.

VALERIA:
My ladies both, good day to you.

VOLUMNIA:
Sweet madam.

VIRGILIA:
I am glad to see your ladyship.

VALERIA:
How do you both? You are clearly homebodies.
What are you sewing here? A fine pattern, in good
faith. How is your little son?

VIRGILIA:
I thank your ladyship; well, good madam.

VOLUMNIA:
He would rather see swords and hear a drum than look at his school-master.

VALERIA:
On my word, he is his father's son. I'll swear, he is a
very pretty boy. On my honor, I looked at him on
Wednesday for half an hour straight. He has such a
confident expression. I saw him run after a gilded
butterfly, and when he caught it, he let it go
again, and after it again, and he falls over and over,
and again; caught it again. Whether his
fall enraged him, or what it was, he set his
teeth and tore it. Oh, I guarantee it, how he ripped
it to pieces!

VOLUMNIA:
One of his father's moods.

VALERIA:
Indeed, la, he is a noble child.

VIRGILIA:
A rascal, madam.

VALERIA:
Come, lay aside your sewing; I must have you play
the idle housewife with me this afternoon.

VIRGILIA:
No, good madam; I will not go out of doors.

VALERIA:
Not out of doors!

VOLUMNIA:
She shall, she shall.

VIRGILIA:
Indeed, no, with your patience; I will not cross the
threshold until my lord returns from the wars.

VALERIA:
Fie, you confine yourself most unreasonably. Come,
you must go visit the good lady who has just given birth.

VIRGILIA:
I will wish her speedy strength, and visit her with
my prayers; but I cannot go there.

VOLUMNIA:
Why, I ask you?

VIRGILIA:
It is not to save labor, nor that I lack love.

VALERIA:
You want to be another Penelope; yet, they say, all
the yarn she spun in Ulysses' absence did but fill
Ithaca full of moths. Come; I wish your fine linen
were as sensible as your finger, that you might stop
pricking it for pity. Come, you shall go with us.

VIRGILIA:
No, good madam, pardon me; indeed, I will not go out.

VALERIA:
In truth, la, go with me; and I'll tell you
excellent news about your husband.

VIRGILIA:
Oh, good madam, there can be none yet.

VALERIA:
Truly, I am not joking with you; there came news from
him last night.

VIRGILIA:
Indeed, madam?

VALERIA:
In earnest, it's true; I heard a senator speak of it.
This is how it is: the Volsces have an army out; against
whom Cominius the general has gone, with one part of
our Roman force. Your lord and Titus Lartius are set
down before their city Corioli; they have no doubt of
prevailing and making it a short war. This is true,
on my honor; and so, I pray, go with us.

VIRGILIA:
Give me an excuse, good madam; I will obey you in everything hereafter.

VOLUMNIA:
Let her alone, lady. As she is now, she will only
spoil our good cheer.

VALERIA:
In truth, I think she would. Fare you well, then.
Come, good sweet lady. Please, Virgilia, cast your
solemnity out the door and go along with us.

VIRGILIA:
No, in a word, madam; indeed, I must not. I wish
you much fun.

VALERIA:
Well, then, farewell.

MARCIUS:
Over there comes news. I'll bet they have met.

LARTIUS:
My horse against yours, no.

MARCIUS:
It's a deal.

LARTIUS:
Agreed.

MARCIUS:
Say, has our general met the enemy?

Messenger:
They are in sight of each other, but have not spoken yet.

LARTIUS:
So, the good horse is mine.

MARCIUS:
I'll buy him from you.

LARTIUS:
No, I'll neither sell nor give him. I will lend him to you
for half a hundred years. Summon the town.

MARCIUS:
How far away are these armies?

Messenger:
Within this mile and a half.

MARCIUS:
Then we shall hear their alarm, and they ours.
Now, Mars, I beg you, make us quick in our work,
so that we with smoking swords may march from here
to help our friends on the field! Come, blow your trumpet.
Titus Aufidius, is he within your walls?

First Senator:
No, nor a man that fears you less than he,
which is less than a little.
Hark! Our drums
are bringing out our youth. We'll break our walls
rather than let them pound us up. Our gates,
which still seem shut, we have only pinned with rushes;
they'll open by themselves.
Listen, far off!
There is Aufidius; listen, what work he makes
among your divided army.

MARCIUS:
Oh, they are at it!

LARTIUS:
Let their noise be our instruction. Ladders, ho!

MARCIUS:
They don't fear us, but come out of their city.
Now put your shields before your hearts, and fight
with hearts more proven than shields. Advance,
brave Titus.
They disdain us much more than we thought,
which makes me sweat with anger. Come on, my fellows.
Anyone who retreats, I'll take him for a Volscian,
and he shall feel my sword's edge.

MARCIUS:
May all the diseases of the south fall on you,
you shames of Rome! You herd of—boils and plagues
plaster you all over, so that you may be abhorred
further than you are seen and one infects another
against the wind for a mile! You souls of geese,
that have the shapes of men, how have you run
from slaves that apes would beat! By Pluto and hell!
All hurt from behind; backs red, and faces pale
with flight and feverish fear! Reform and charge forward,
or, by the fires of heaven, I'll leave the enemy
and make my wars on you. Watch out, come on!
If you'll stand fast, we'll beat them back to their wives,
as they followed us to our trenches.
So, now the gates are open. Now prove to be good support.
Fortune widens them for the followers,
not for the fleers. Watch me, and do the same.

First Soldier:
That's foolhardiness; not me.

Second Soldier:
Me neither.

First Soldier:
See, they have shut him in.

All:
He's done for, I guarantee it.

LARTIUS:
What has become of Marcius?

All:
Slain, sir, doubtless.

First Soldier:
Following the fleeing soldiers at their very heels,
he enters with them; who, upon the sudden,
slammed their gates shut. He is all by himself,
to face the whole city.

LARTIUS:
Oh, noble fellow!
Who knowingly out-dares his senseless sword,
and, when it bows, stands up. You are a jewel, Marcius.
A whole carbuncle, as big as you are,
would not be so rich a jewel. You were a soldier
just as Cato wished, not fierce and terrible
only in your strikes; but, with your grim looks and
the thunder-like percussion of your sounds,
you made your enemies shake, as if the world
were feverish and did tremble.

First Soldier:
Look, sir.

LARTIUS:
Oh, it's Marcius!
Let's get him out, or stay here with him.

First Roman:
I will carry this to Rome.

Second Roman:
And I this.

Third Roman:
A plague on it! I thought this was silver.

MARCIUS:
Look at these movers who value their time
at a cracked coin! Cushions, leaden spoons,
worthless irons, clothes that hangmen would
bury with those who wore them, these base slaves,
before the fight is even done, pack up. Down with them!
And listen, what noise the general makes! To him!
There is the man my soul hates, Aufidius,
piercing our Romans. Then, valiant Titus, take
enough men to secure the city,
while I, with those that have the spirit, will hurry
to help Cominius.

LARTIUS:
Worthy sir, you are bleeding;
your exercise has been too violent for
a second round of fighting.

MARCIUS:
Sir, do not praise me;
my work has not yet warmed me up. Fare you well.
The blood I drop is rather medicinal
than dangerous to me. To Aufidius thus
I will appear, and fight.

LARTIUS:
Now may the fair goddess, Fortune,
fall deeply in love with you; and may her great charms
misguide your opponents' swords! Bold gentleman,
may prosperity be your page!

MARCIUS:
May your friend be no less
than those she places highest! So, farewell.

LARTIUS:
You worthiest Marcius!
Go, sound your trumpet in the market-place;
call all the officers of the town there,
where they shall know our mind. Away!

COMINIUS:
Catch your breath, my friends. Well fought;
we have come off like Romans, neither foolish in our stands,
nor cowardly in retreat. Believe me, sirs,
we shall be charged again. While we have been fighting,
we have heard, in between and on gusts of wind,
the charges of our friends. You Roman gods!
Lead their successes as we wish our own,
so that both our forces, meeting with smiling faces,
may give you thankful sacrifice.
What's your news?

Messenger:
The citizens of Corioli have come out,
and have given battle to Lartius and to Marcius.
I saw our party driven to their trenches,
and then I came away.

COMINIUS:
Though you speak the truth,
I think you don't speak it well.
How long has it been since?

Messenger:
Over an hour, my lord.

COMINIUS:
It's not a mile; we just heard their drums.
How could you take an hour for a mile,
and bring your news so late?

Messenger:
Spies of the Volsces
chased me, so that I was forced to detour
three or four miles around, otherwise I, sir,
would have brought my report half an hour ago.

COMINIUS:
Who is that over there,
that looks as if he were flayed? Oh gods,
he has the look of Marcius; and I have
seen him like this before.

MARCIUS:

COMINIUS:
A shepherd knows not thunder from a drum
any more than I know the sound of Marcius' tongue
from every lesser man.

MARCIUS:
Am I too late?

COMINIUS:
Yes, if you come not in the blood of others,
but covered in your own.

MARCIUS:
Oh, let me embrace you,
in arms as sound as when I courted, in heart
as merry as when our wedding day was done,
and candles burned for bed!

COMINIUS:
Flower of warriors,
how is it with Titus Lartius?

MARCIUS:
As with a man busy with decrees:
condemning some to death, and some to exile;
ransoming one, or pitying another, threatening another;
holding Corioli in the name of Rome,
just like a fawning greyhound on a leash,
to let it slip at will.

COMINIUS:
Where is that slave
who told me they had beaten you to your trenches?
Where is he? Call him here.

MARCIUS:
Let him alone;
he told the truth. But as for our gentlemen,
the common soldiers—a plague on them! Tribunes for them!—
the mouse never shunned the cat as they did flee
from scoundrels worse than they.

COMINIUS:
But how did you prevail?

MARCIUS:
Will time allow me to tell? I do not think so.
Where is the enemy? Are you lords of the field?
If not, why do you stop until you are?

COMINIUS:
Marcius,
we have fought at a disadvantage and did
retreat to achieve our purpose.

MARCIUS:
How is their army arranged? Do you know on which side
they have placed their most trusted men?

COMINIUS:
As I guess, Marcius,
their troops in the vanguard are the Antiates,
their most trusted men; over them is Aufidius,
their very heart of hope.

MARCIUS:
I beseech you,
by all the battles in which we have fought,
by the blood we have shed together, by the vows
we have made to remain friends, that you directly
set me against Aufidius and his Antiates;
and that you not delay the present, but,
filling the air with raised swords and darts,
we prove ourselves this very hour.

COMINIUS:
Though I could wish
you were taken to a gentle bath
and balms applied to you, yet I dare never
deny your asking. Take your choice of those
that can best aid your action.

MARCIUS:
Those are the ones
that are most willing. If any such are here—
as it would be a sin to doubt—that love this painting
in which you see me smeared; if any fear
his person less than a bad report;
if any think brave death outweighs a bad life
and that his country is dearer than himself;
let him alone, or as many so minded,
wave thus, to express his disposition,
and follow Marcius.
Oh, just me alone! Are you making a sword of me?
If these displays are not just for show, which of you
is not worth four Volsces? None of you is not
able to bear against the great Aufidius
a shield as hard as his. A certain number,
though thanks to all, must I select from all. The rest
shall carry on the business in some other fight,
as the cause will demand. Please you to march,
and four shall quickly draw out my command,
which men are best inclined.

COMINIUS:
March on, my fellows.
Make good on this display, and you shall
share in everything with us.

LARTIUS:
So, let the ports be guarded. Keep to your duties,
as I have set them down. If I send for you, dispatch
those centuries to our aid. The rest will serve
for a short hold. If we lose the field,
we cannot keep the town.

Lieutenant:
Fear not our care, sir.

LARTIUS:
Go, and shut your gates upon us.
Our guide, come; lead us to the Roman camp.

MARCIUS:
I'll fight with no one but you, for I hate you
worse than a promise-breaker.

AUFIDIUS:
We hate each other equally.
Not even Africa owns a serpent I loathe
more than your fame and the envy it brings. Stand your ground.

MARCIUS:
Let the first one who moves die the other's slave,
and may the gods judge him after!

AUFIDIUS:
If I fly, Marcius,
chase me like a hare.

MARCIUS:
Within these three hours, Tullus,
I fought alone in your Corioli walls,
and did what I pleased. It's not my blood
that you see me masked in; for your revenge,
gather your power to the highest.

AUFIDIUS:
If you were Hector,
that was the whip of your bragged about ancestors,
you would not escape me here.
Officious, and not valiant, you have shamed me
in your condemned support.

COMINIUS:
If I were to tell you over again your day's work,
you wouldn't believe your deeds. But I'll report it
where senators will mix tears with smiles,
where great patricians will attend and shrug,
and in the end admire, where ladies will be frightened,
and, gladly shaken, hear more; where the
dull tribunes,
that, with the musty commoners, hate your honors,
shall say against their hearts, 'We thank the gods
our Rome has such a soldier.'
Yet you only came for a small part of this feast,
having fully dined before.

LARTIUS:
Oh general,
here is the horse, we are the decorations.
Had you seen--

MARCIUS:
Please now, no more. My mother,
who has a right to praise her blood,
grieves me when she praises me. I have done
as you have done; that's what I can do; motivated
as you have been; that's for my country.
He that has only accomplished his good will
has surpassed my act.

COMINIUS:
You shall not be
the grave of your deserving; Rome must know
the value of its own. It would be a concealment
worse than a theft, no less than a slander,
to hide your doings; and to silence that,
which, if praised to the highest spire of praise,
would seem but modest. Therefore, I beseech you,
as a sign of what you are, not to reward
what you have done—before our army, hear me.

MARCIUS:
I have some wounds on me, and they smart
to hear themselves remembered.

COMINIUS:
If they didn't,
they might well fester against ingratitude, and treat themselves with death. Of all the horses,
of which we have taken good ones and a good supply, of all
the treasure gained in this field and city,
we give you a tenth, to be taken out
before the general distribution, at
your own choice.

MARCIUS:
I thank you, general;
but I cannot make my heart consent to take
a bribe to pay my sword. I refuse it,
and I stand on my common part with those
that have witnessed the doing.

MARCIUS:
May these same instruments, which you profane,
never sound again! When drums and trumpets shall
prove to be flatterers in the field, let courts and cities be
made all of false-faced flattery!
When steel grows soft as the parasite's silk,
let him be made a cover for the wars!
No more, I say! For the fact that I have not washed
my nose that bled, or defeated some weak wretch—
which, by the way, many others here have done without being noticed—
you shout me forth
in exaggerated acclamations,
as if I loved for my small achievements to be fed
with praises sauced with lies.

COMINIUS:
You are too modest;
more cruel to your good reputation than grateful
to us that give you true praise. By your patience,
if you are incensed against yourself, we'll put you,
like one that means to harm himself, in manacles,
then reason safely with you. Therefore, be it known,
to us and to all the world, that Caius Marcius
wears this war's garland. In token of which,
my noble horse, known to the camp, I give him,
with all his fine trappings. And from this time,
for what he did before Corioli, call him,
with all the applause and clamor of the army,
CAIUS MARCIUS CORIOLANUS! Bear
the title nobly ever!

All:
Caius Marcius Coriolanus!

CORIOLANUS:
I will go wash;
and when my face is clean, you shall see
whether I blush or not. However, I thank you.
I mean to ride your horse, and at all times
to wear your good title
to the best of my ability.

COMINIUS:
So, to our tent,
where, before we rest, we will write
to Rome of our success. You, Titus Lartius,
must go back to Corioli. Send to Rome
the best men, with whom we may negotiate,
for their own good and ours.

LARTIUS:
I shall, my lord.

CORIOLANUS:
The gods begin to mock me. I, who just now
refused most princely gifts, am forced to beg
of my lord general.

COMINIUS:
Take it; it's yours. What is it?

CORIOLANUS:
I once stayed here in Corioli
at a poor man's house; he treated me kindly.
He cried to me; I saw him taken prisoner;
but then Aufidius was in my sight,
and anger overwhelmed my pity. I request you
to give my poor host his freedom.

COMINIUS:
Oh, well begged!
Were he the butcher of my son, he should
be as free as the wind. Deliver him, Titus.

LARTIUS:
Marcius, his name?

CORIOLANUS:
By Jupiter! I forgot.
I am weary; yes, my memory is tired.
Have we no wine here?

COMINIUS:
Let's go to our tent.
The blood on your face is drying; it's time
it should be looked after. Come.

AUFIDIUS:
The town is taken!

First Soldier:
It will be given back on good terms.

AUFIDIUS:
Condition!
I wish I were a Roman; for I cannot,
being a Volscian, be who I am. Condition!
What good condition can a treaty find
for the side that is at mercy? Five times, Marcius,
I have fought with you; so often have you beaten me,
and would do so, I think, should we encounter
as often as we eat. By the elements,
if ever again I meet him face to face,
he's mine, or I am his. My rivalry
doesn't have the honor in it that it had; for where
I thought to crush him in an equal force,
true sword to sword, I'll attack him some way,
either by anger or craft, to get him.

First Soldier:
He's the devil.

AUFIDIUS:
Bolder, though not so subtle. My valor is poisoned
just by suffering defeat by him. Because of him,
my valor shall abandon itself. Neither sleep nor sanctuary,
being naked, sick, nor temple nor Capitol,
the prayers of priests nor times of sacrifice,
all restraints on fury, shall lift up
their rotten privilege and custom against
my hate for Marcius. Wherever I find him, were it
at home, under my brother's guard, even there,
against the laws of hospitality, I would
wash my fierce hand in his heart. Go to the city;
learn how it is held, and who must be
hostages for Rome.

First Soldier:
Will you not go?

AUFIDIUS:
I am expected at the cypress grove. I pray you—
it's south of the city mills—bring me word there
how the world goes, so that to its pace
I may spur on my journey.

First Soldier:
I shall, sir.

MENENIUS:
The soothsayer tells me we shall have news tonight.

BRUTUS:
Good or bad?

MENENIUS:
Not according to the prayers of the people, for they
do not love Marcius.

SICINIUS:
Nature teaches beasts to know their friends.

MENENIUS:
Pray you, who does the wolf love?

SICINIUS:
The lamb.

MENENIUS:
Yes, to devour him; as the hungry commoners would devour the noble Marcius.

BRUTUS:
He's a lamb indeed, that bleats like a bear.

MENENIUS:
He's a bear indeed, that lives like a lamb. You two
are old men. Tell me one thing that I shall ask you.

Both:
Well, sir.

MENENIUS:
In what fault is Marcius lacking, that you two
do not have in abundance?

BRUTUS:
He's poor in no one fault, but stored with all.

SICINIUS:
Especially in pride.

BRUTUS:
And surpassing all others in boasting.

MENENIUS:
This is strange now. Do you two know how you are
judged here in the city, I mean by us of the
right-hand side? Do you?

Both:
Why, how are we judged?

MENENIUS:
Because you talk of pride now—will you not be angry?

Both:
Well, well, sir, well.

MENENIUS:
Why, it's no great matter; for a very little thief of an
occasion will rob you of a great deal of patience.
Give your tempers free rein, and be angry at
your pleasure; at the least if you take it as a
pleasure to be so. You blame Marcius for
being proud?

BRUTUS:
We don't do it alone, sir.

MENENIUS:
I know you can do very little alone, for your helpers
are many, or else your actions would become wonderfully
singular. Your abilities are too infant-like for
doing much alone. You talk of pride. Oh, that you
could turn your eyes toward the napes of your necks,
and make but an internal survey of your good selves!
Oh, that you could!

BRUTUS:
What then, sir?

MENENIUS:
Why, then you would discover a pair of undeserving,
proud, violent, testy magistrates, also known as fools, as
any in Rome.

SICINIUS:
Menenius, you are known well enough too.

MENENIUS:
I am known to be a humorous patrician, and one that
loves a cup of hot wine with not a drop of diluting
Tiber in it; said to be somewhat imperfect in
favoring the first complaint; hasty and quick to anger
over trivial matters; one that converses more
with the buttock of the night than with the forehead
of the morning. What I think I say, and spend my
malice in my breath. Meeting two such statesmen as
you are—I cannot call you Lycurguses—if the drink
you give me tastes bad, I make a
crooked face at it. I can't say your worships have
delivered the matter well, when I find the word "ass"
compounded with the major part of your syllables. And
though I must be content to bear with those that say
you are reverend grave men, yet they lie deadly that
tell you you have good faces. If you see this in
the map of my microcosm, does it follow that I am known
well enough too? What harm can your blind
perceptions glean out of this character, if I be
known well enough too?

BRUTUS:
Come, sir, come, we know you well enough.

MENENIUS:
You know neither me, yourselves, nor anything. You
are ambitious for poor knaves' caps and bows. You
wear out a good wholesome forenoon hearing a
case between an orange-wife and a faucet-seller;
and then adjourn the controversy of three pence to a
second day of audience. When you are hearing a
matter between two parties, if you happen to be
pinched with colic, you make faces like
mummers; set up the bloody flag against all
patience; and, in roaring for a chamber-pot,
dismiss the controversy, making it more entangled
by your hearing. All the peace you make in their
case is calling both the parties knaves. You are
a pair of strange ones.

BRUTUS:
Come, come, you are well understood to be a
more perfect jester for the table than a necessary
judge in the Capitol.

MENENIUS:
Our very priests would have to become mockers if they were to encounter such ridiculous subjects as you are. When you speak your best to the point, it is not worth the wagging of your beards; and your beards do not deserve so honorable a grave as to stuff a tailor's cushion, or to be entombed in a donkey's packsaddle. Yet you must be saying, Marcius is proud; who, in a cheap estimation, is worth all his predecessors since Deucalion, though perhaps some of the best of them were hereditary hangmen. Good day to your worships. More of your conversation would infect my brain, being the herdsmen of the beastly commoners. I will be bold to take my leave of you. How now, my ladies, as fair as you are noble—and the moon, if she were earthly, would be no nobler—where are you following your eyes so fast?

VOLUMNIA:
Honorable Menenius, my boy Marcius is approaching; for
the love of Juno, let's go.

MENENIUS:
Ha! Marcius coming home!

VOLUMNIA:
Yes, worthy Menenius; and with most prosperous
approval.

MENENIUS:
Take my cap, Jupiter, and I thank you. Hoo!
Marcius coming home!

VOLUMNIA:
No, it's true.

VOLUMNIA:
Look, here's a letter from him. The state has
another, his wife another; and, I think, there's one
at home for you.

MENENIUS:
I will make my very house reel tonight! A letter for
me!

VIRGILIA:
Yes, certainly, there's a letter for you; I saw it.

MENENIUS:
A letter for me! It gives me an estate of seven
years' health; in which time I will make a face at
the physician. The most sovereign prescription in
Galen is but quackery, and, compared to this preservative,
of no better report than a horse-drench. Is he
not wounded? He was accustomed to come home wounded.

VIRGILIA:
Oh, no, no, no.

VOLUMNIA:
Oh, he is wounded; I thank the gods for it.

MENENIUS:
So do I too, if it's not too much. Does he bring a
victory in his pocket? The wounds become him.

VOLUMNIA:
On his brows. Menenius, he comes home for the third time
with the oaken garland.

MENENIUS:
Has he disciplined Aufidius soundly?

VOLUMNIA:
Titus Lartius writes that they fought together, but
Aufidius got away.

MENENIUS:
And it was time for him too, I'll guarantee him that.
If he had stayed by him, I would not have been so
fidiused for all the chests in Corioli, and the gold
that's in them. Is the senate aware of this?

VOLUMNIA:
Good ladies, let's go. Yes, yes, yes; the senate
has letters from the general, in which he gives my
son the whole credit for the war. He has in this
action outdone his former deeds doubly.

VALERIA:
In truth, there are wondrous things spoken of him.

MENENIUS:
Wondrous! Yes, I guarantee you, and not without his
true earning of it.

VIRGILIA:
May the gods grant them to be true!

VOLUMNIA:
True! Nonsense.

MENENIUS:
True! I'll be sworn they are true.
Where is he wounded?
God save your good worships! Marcius is coming
home. He has more cause to be proud. Where is he wounded?

VOLUMNIA:
In the shoulder and in the left arm. There will be
large scars to show the people, when he shall
stand for his position. He received seven wounds in the body in the repulse of
Tarquin.

MENENIUS:
One in the neck, and two in the thigh—there are
nine that I know of.

VOLUMNIA:
He had, before this last expedition, twenty-five
wounds upon him.

MENENIUS:
Now it's twenty-seven. Every gash was an enemy's grave.
Hark! The trumpets.

VOLUMNIA:
These are the ushers of Marcius. Before him he
carries noise, and behind him he leaves tears.
Death, that dark spirit, lies in his strong arm;
which, being raised, descends, and then men die.

Herald:
Know, Rome, that all alone Marcius did fight
within Corioli's gates, where he has won,
with fame, a name for Caius Marcius; these
in honor follow: Coriolanus.
Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus!

All:
Welcome to Rome, renowned Coriolanus!

CORIOLANUS:
No more of this; it offends my heart.
Please now, no more.

COMINIUS:
Look, sir, your mother!

CORIOLANUS:
Oh,
you have, I know, petitioned all the gods
for my prosperity!

VOLUMNIA:
No, my good soldier, stand up;
my gentle Marcius, worthy Caius, and
newly named by deed-achieving honor—
what is it?—Coriolanus must I call you?—
But oh, your wife!

CORIOLANUS:
My gracious silence, hail!
Would you have laughed if I had come home in a coffin,
you who weep to see me triumph? Yes, my dear,
such eyes the widows in Corioli wear,
and mothers that lack sons.

MENENIUS:
Now, may the gods crown you!

CORIOLANUS:
And are you still alive?
Oh my sweet lady, pardon me.

VOLUMNIA:
I don't know where to turn. Oh, welcome home.
And welcome, general. And you are all welcome.

MENENIUS:
A hundred thousand welcomes. I could weep
and I could laugh, I am light and heavy. Welcome.
May a curse begin at the very root of his heart,
who is not glad to see you! You are three
that Rome should dote on. Yet, by the faith of men,
we have some old crab-trees here at home that will not
be grafted to your liking. Yet welcome, warriors.
We call a nettle but a nettle and
the faults of fools but folly.

COMINIUS:
Always right.

CORIOLANUS:
Menenius, ever, ever.

Herald:
Give way there, and go on!

CORIOLANUS:

VOLUMNIA:
I have lived
to see my very wishes inherited
and the buildings of my imagination realized. Only
there's one thing missing, which I have no doubt
our Rome will bestow upon you.

CORIOLANUS:
Know, good mother,
I would rather be their servant in my own way,
than rule with them in theirs.

COMINIUS:
On, to the Capitol!

BRUTUS:
All tongues speak of him, and the bleary-eyed
are wearing glasses to see him. Your chattering nurse
lets her baby cry in a fit of excitement
while she talks about him. The kitchen maid pins
her best linen around her grimy neck,
climbing the walls to see him. Stalls, ledges, windows,
are smothered up, roofs filled, and ridges lined
with people of various complexions, all agreeing
in their earnestness to see him. Seldom-seen priests
press among the common crowds and pant
to get a common standing place. Or veiled ladies
commit the war of white and red in
their nicely decorated cheeks to the wanton spoil
of Phoebus's burning kisses. Such a commotion,
as if whatever god who leads him
had slyly crept into his human powers
and gave him a graceful posture.

SICINIUS:
Suddenly,
I guarantee he'll be consul.

BRUTUS:
Then our office may,
during his power, go to sleep.

SICINIUS:
He cannot moderately carry his honors
from where he should begin and end, but will
lose those he has won.

BRUTUS:
In that there's comfort.

SICINIUS:
Do not doubt
the commoners, for whom we stand, but they
upon their ancient malice will forget,
with the least cause, these his new honors, which
that he will give them cause, I have as little question
as he is proud to do it.

BRUTUS:
I heard him swear,
if he were to run for consul, he would never
appear in the market-place nor put on
the threadbare garment of humility;
nor, showing his wounds to the people, as is the custom,
beg for their stinking breaths.

SICINIUS:
That's right.

BRUTUS:
It was his word. Oh, he would rather miss it
than get it by the request of the gentry to him,
and the desire of the nobles.

SICINIUS:
I wish for nothing better
than for him to hold that purpose and to put it
into execution.

BRUTUS:
It's most likely he will.

SICINIUS:
It shall be to him then, as our good intentions are,
a sure destruction.

BRUTUS:
So it must turn out
for him or our authorities. For an end,
we must suggest to the people in what hatred
he has always held them; that to his power he would
have made them mules, silenced their advocates and
taken away their freedoms, holding them,
in human action and capacity,
of no more soul nor fitness for the world
than camels in war, who have their food
only for bearing burdens, and sore blows
for sinking under them.

SICINIUS:
This, as you say, suggested
at some time when his soaring insolence
shall touch the people—which time will not be lacking,
if he is provoked; and that's as easy
as setting dogs on sheep—will be his fire
to kindle their dry stubble; and their blaze
shall darken him forever.

BRUTUS:
What's the matter?

Messenger:
You are sent for to the Capitol. It is thought
that Marcius will be consul.
I have seen the dumb men throng to see him and
the blind to hear him speak. Matrons threw gloves,
ladies and maids their scarves and handkerchiefs,
upon him as he passed. The nobles bowed,
as to Jove's statue, and the common people made
a shower and thunder with their caps and shouts.
I never saw the like.

BRUTUS:
Let's go to the Capitol,
and carry with us ears and eyes for the time,
but hearts for the outcome.

SICINIUS:
I'm with you.

First Officer:
Come, come, they are almost here. How many are running
for consulships?

Second Officer:
Three, they say. But everyone thinks
Coriolanus will win it.

First Officer:
That's a brave fellow; but he's terribly proud, and
doesn't like the common people.

Second Officer:
Faith, there have been many great men that have
flattered the people, who never loved them; and there
are many that they have loved, they know not
why. So that, if they love without knowing why,
they hate on no better a ground. Therefore, for
Coriolanus neither to care whether they love or hate
him shows the true knowledge he has of their
disposition; and out of his noble carelessness, he lets
them plainly see it.

First Officer:
If he didn't care whether he had their love or not,
he would waver indifferently between doing them neither
good nor harm. But he seeks their hate with greater
devotion than they can give him, and leaves
nothing undone that may fully reveal him as their
opposite. Now, to seem to affect the malice and
displeasure of the people is as bad as that which he
dislikes, to flatter them for their love.

Second Officer:
He has deserved worthily of his country, and his
ascent is not by such easy degrees as those who,
having been supple and courteous to the people,
tipped their hats, without any further deed to have them
enter into their estimation and report. But he has so
planted his honors in their eyes, and his actions
in their hearts, that for their tongues to be
silent, and not confess so much, would be a kind of
ungrateful injury; to report otherwise, would be a
malice, that, giving itself the lie, would draw
reproof and rebuke from every ear that heard it.

First Officer:
No more of him; he is a worthy man. Make way, they
are coming.

MENENIUS:
Having decided about the Volsces and
to send for Titus Lartius, it remains,
as the main point of this our follow-up meeting,
to reward his noble service that
has thus stood for his country. Therefore,
please you, most reverend and grave elders, to desire
the present consul, and last general
in our well-found successes, to report
a little of that worthy work performed
by Caius Marcius Coriolanus, whom
we met here both to thank and to remember
with honors like himself.

First Senator:
Speak, good Cominius.
Leave nothing out for the sake of length, and make us think
rather our state is deficient in rewarding
than we are in extending it.
Masters of the people,
we request your kindest ears, and after,
your loving motion toward the common body,
to grant what is passed here.

SICINIUS:
We are gathered here
for a pleasing treaty, and have hearts
inclined to honor and advance
the theme of our assembly.

BRUTUS:
Which we shall be the more
blessed to do, if he remembers
a kinder value of the people than
he has hitherto prized them at.

MENENIUS:
That's off, that's off.
I wish you had rather been silent. Would you please
hear Cominius speak?

BRUTUS:
Most willingly;
but yet my caution was more pertinent
than the rebuke you give it.

MENENIUS:
He loves your people,
but don't tie him down to be their bedfellow.
Worthy Cominius, speak.
No, keep your place.

First Senator:
Sit, Coriolanus; never be ashamed to hear
what you have nobly done.

CORIOLANUS:
I beg your honor's pardon.
I would rather have my wounds heal again
than hear how I got them.

BRUTUS:
Sir, I hope
my words didn't make you leave your seat.

CORIOLANUS:
No, sir. Yet often,
when blows have made me stay, I have fled from words.
You didn't flatter me, therefore you didn't hurt me. But
your people, I love them as they deserve.

MENENIUS:
Please now, sit down.

CORIOLANUS:
I would rather have someone scratch my head in the sun
when the alarm was sounded than idly sit
to hear my nothings made monstrous.

MENENIUS:
Masters of the people,
how can he flatter your multiplying spawn—
that's a thousand to one good one—when you now see
he would rather venture all his limbs for honor
than one of his ears to hear it? Proceed, Cominius.

COMINIUS:
I shall lack a voice. The deeds of Coriolanus
should not be uttered feebly. It is held
that valor is the chiefest virtue, and
most dignifies the person who has it. If it is,
the man I speak of cannot in the world
be singly matched. At sixteen years,
when Tarquin raised an army for Rome, he fought
beyond the mark of others. Our then dictator,
whom I point to with all praise, saw him fight,
when with his beardless chin he drove
the bristled lips before him. He stood over
an over-pressed Roman and in the consul's view
slew three opponents. He met Tarquin himself,
and struck him on his knee. In that day's feats,
when he might have played the woman in the scene,
he proved the best man in the field, and for his reward
was crowned with the oak wreath. His youth
having thus become a man, he grew like a sea,
and in the brunt of seventeen battles since
he has won all the garlands from other swords. For this last,
before and in Corioli, let me say,
I cannot praise him enough. He stopped the fleeing soldiers;
and by his rare example made the cowards
turn terror into sport. As weeds before
a vessel under sail, so men obeyed
and fell below his prow. His sword, death's stamp,
wherever it marked, it took. From head to foot
he was a thing of blood, whose every motion
was timed with dying cries. Alone he entered
the mortal gate of the city, which he painted
with inescapable destiny. He came off without aid,
and with a sudden reinforcement struck
Corioli like a planet. Now all is his.
When, by and by, the din of war began to pierce
his ready sense, then straight his doubled spirit
re-quickened what in flesh was tired,
and to the battle he came, where he did
run reeking over the lives of men, as if
it were a perpetual spoil. And until we called
both field and city ours, he never stood
to ease his breast with panting.

MENENIUS:
Worthy man!

First Senator:
He cannot but be a fitting recipient for the honors
which we devise for him.

COMINIUS:
He kicked at our spoils,
and looked upon precious things as if they were
the common muck of the world. He covets less
than misery itself would give; he rewards
his deeds by doing them, and is content
to spend the time to end it.

MENENIUS:
He's truly noble.
Let him be called for.

First Senator:
Call Coriolanus.

Officer:
He appears.

MENENIUS:
The senate, Coriolanus, is well pleased
to make you consul.

CORIOLANUS:
I still owe them
my life and services.

MENENIUS:
It then remains
that you speak to the people.

CORIOLANUS:
I beseech you,
let me skip that custom, for I cannot
put on the gown, stand naked and ask them,
for my wounds' sake, to give me their vote. Please
let me pass this doing.

SICINIUS:
Sir, the people
must have their voices; neither will they skip
one bit of ceremony.

MENENIUS:
Don't put them to it.
Please, go and follow the custom and
take to you, as your predecessors have,
your honor with your form.

CORIOLANUS:
It is a part
that I will blush in acting, and might well
be taken from the people.

BRUTUS:
Did you notice that?

CORIOLANUS:
To brag to them, "I did this, and this";
show them the un-aching scars which I should hide,
as if I had received them for the price
of their breath only!

MENENIUS:
Don't insist on it.
We recommend to you, tribunes of the people,
our purpose to them. And to our noble consul,
we wish all joy and honor.

Senators:
To Coriolanus, may all joy and honor come!

BRUTUS:
You see how he intends to use the people.

SICINIUS:
May they perceive his intent! He will demand of them,
as if he despised what he requested
should be in their power to give.

BRUTUS:
Come, we'll inform them
of our proceedings here. On the marketplace,
I know, they are waiting for us.

First Citizen:
Once, if he asks for our votes, we ought not to deny him.

Second Citizen:
We may, sir, if we want to.

Third Citizen:
We have the power in ourselves to do it, but it is a
power that we have no power to use. For if he shows us
his wounds and tells us his deeds, we are to put our
tongues into those wounds and speak for them. So, if
he tells us his noble deeds, we must also tell him
our noble acceptance of them. Ingratitude is
monstrous, and for the multitude to be ungrateful,
would be to make a monster of the multitude. Of which
we being members, we would make ourselves
monstrous members.

First Citizen:
And to make us seem no better, a little help
will suffice. For once when we stood up for the grain, he
himself did not hesitate to call us the many-headed multitude.

Third Citizen:
We have been called so by many; not because our heads
are some brown, some black, some auburn, some bald,
but because our wits are so diversely colored. And
truly I think if all our wits were to issue out of
one skull, they would fly east, west, north, south,
and their agreement on one direct way would be at
once to all the points of the compass.

Second Citizen:
Do you think so? Which way do you judge my wit would
fly?

Third Citizen:
No, your wit will not come out as soon as another man's
will. It's strongly wedged up in a block-head, but
if it were at liberty, it would, for sure, go southward.

Second Citizen:
Why that way?

Third Citizen:
To lose itself in a fog, where, being three parts
melted away with rotten dews, the fourth would return
for conscience's sake, to help to get you a wife.

Second Citizen:
You are never without your tricks. You may be, you may be.

Third Citizen:
Are you all resolved to give your voices? But
that's no matter, the majority wins. I
say, if he would be inclined to the people, there was
never a worthier man.
Here he comes, and in the gown of humility. Mark his
behavior. We are not to stay all together, but to
come by him where he stands, by ones, by twos, and
by threes. He is to make his requests to
individuals; in which every one of us has a single
honor, in giving him our own voices with our own
tongues. Therefore, follow me, and I will direct you how
you shall go by him.

All:
Agreed, agreed.

MENENIUS:
Oh sir, you are not doing it right. Have you not known
that the worthiest men have done it?

CORIOLANUS:
What must I say?
'I pray, sir'—a plague on it! I cannot bring
my tongue to such a pace. 'Look, sir, my wounds!
I got them in my country's service, when
some of your brethren roared and ran
from the noise of our own drums.'

MENENIUS:
Oh me, the gods!
You must not speak of that. You must ask them
to think of you.

CORIOLANUS:
Think of me! Hang them!
I wish they would forget me, like the virtues
which our divines lose by them.

MENENIUS:
You'll ruin everything.
I'll leave you. Please, speak to them, I beg you,
in a wholesome manner.

CORIOLANUS:
Tell them to wash their faces
and keep their teeth clean.
So, here comes a pair.
You know the reason, sir, for my standing here.

Third Citizen:
We do, sir. Tell us what has brought you to it.

CORIOLANUS:
My own desert.

Second Citizen:
Your own desert!

CORIOLANUS:
Yes, but not my own desire.

Third Citizen:
How is it not your own desire?

CORIOLANUS:
No, sir, it was never my desire yet to trouble the
poor with begging.

Third Citizen:
You must think, if we give you anything, we hope to
gain by you.

CORIOLANUS:
Well then, I ask, what is your price for the consulship?

First Citizen:
The price is to ask for it kindly.

CORIOLANUS:
Kindly! Sir, I ask, let me have it. I have wounds to
show you, which shall be yours in private. Your
good voice, sir; what do you say?

Second Citizen:
You shall have it, worthy sir.

CORIOLANUS:
A deal, sir. There are two worthy voices
begged in all. I have your alms. Goodbye.

Third Citizen:
But this is a little odd.

Second Citizen:
If it were to give again—but it doesn't matter.

CORIOLANUS:
I pray you now, if it may stand with the tune of your
voices that I may be consul, I have here the
customary gown.

Fourth Citizen:
You have deserved nobly of your country, and you
have not deserved nobly.

CORIOLANUS:
Your riddle?

Fourth Citizen:
You have been a scourge to her enemies, you have
been a rod to her friends; you have not indeed loved
the common people.

CORIOLANUS:
You should consider me the more virtuous for not having been common in my love. I will, sir, flatter my sworn brother, the people, to earn a dearer estimation of them; it's a condition they consider gentle. And since the wisdom of their choice is rather to have my hat than my heart, I will practice the insinuating nod and be off to them most counterfeitly; that is, sir, I will counterfeit the bewitchment of some popular man and give it bountifully to the desirers. Therefore, I beseech you, I may be consul.

Fifth Citizen:
We hope to find you as our friend; and therefore give
you our votes heartily.

Fourth Citizen:
You have received many wounds for your country.

CORIOLANUS:
I will not confirm your knowledge by showing them. I
will make much of your votes, and so trouble you no further.

Both Citizens:
May the gods give you joy, sir, heartily!

CORIOLANUS:
Most sweet voices!
It is better to die, better to starve,
than to crave the payment which we first deserve.
Why should I stand here in this wolfish robe,
to beg of Hob and Dick, who appear here,
their needless endorsements? Custom calls me to it.
What custom wills, should we do it in all things?
The dust on ancient time would lie unswept,
and mountainous error would be too highly heaped
for truth to peer over. Rather than act the fool like this,
let the high office and the honor go
to one that would do this. I am half through;
the one part suffered, the other I will do.
Here come more voices.
Your voices! For your voices I have fought;
watched for your voices; for your voices I bear
two dozen odd wounds; I have seen and heard of
eighteen battles; for your voices I have
done many things, some less, some more. Your voices!
Indeed, I would be consul.

Sixth Citizen:
He has done nobly, and cannot go without any honest
man's voice.

Seventh Citizen:
Therefore let him be consul. May the gods give him joy,
and make him a good friend to the people!

All Citizens:
Amen, amen. God save you, noble consul!

CORIOLANUS:
Worthy voices!

MENENIUS:
You have stood your limit, and the tribunes
endow you with the people's voice. It remains
that, invested in the official robes, you
soon meet the senate.

CORIOLANUS:
Is this done?

SICINIUS:
You have discharged the custom of request.
The people accept you, and are summoned
to meet soon, for your approval.

CORIOLANUS:
Where? At the senate-house?

SICINIUS:
There, Coriolanus.

CORIOLANUS:
May I change these clothes?

SICINIUS:
You may, sir.

CORIOLANUS:
That I'll do straightaway; and, being myself again,
I will repair to the senate-house.

MENENIUS:
I'll keep you company. Will you come along?

BRUTUS:
We are staying here for the people.

SICINIUS:
Fare you well.
He has it now, and by his looks, I think,
his heart is warm.

BRUTUS:
With a proud heart, he wore his humble clothes.
Will you dismiss the people?

SICINIUS:
How now, my masters! Have you chosen this man?

First Citizen:
He has our votes, sir.

BRUTUS:
We pray to the gods he may deserve your love.

Second Citizen:
Amen, sir. In my poor, unworthy opinion,
he mocked us when he begged for our votes.

Third Citizen:
Certainly,
he mocked us outright.

First Citizen:
No, it's his way of speaking. He didn't mock us.

Second Citizen:
Not one among us, except for yourself, but says
he treated us scornfully. He should have shown us
his marks of merit, the wounds he received for his country.

SICINIUS:
Why, so he did, I am sure.

Citizens:
No, no; no man saw them.

Third Citizen:
He said he had wounds, which he could show
in private;
and with his hat, waving it in scorn like this,
'I would be consul,' he says. 'Aged custom,
but by your voices, will not so permit me;
your voices therefore.' When we granted that,
here was 'I thank you for your voices: thank you:
your most sweet voices. Now you have left
your voices,
I have no further business with you.' Wasn't this mockery?

SICINIUS:
Why were you either too ignorant to see it,
or, seeing it, of such childish friendliness
to yield your voices?

BRUTUS:
Could you not have told him,
as you were instructed, when he had no power,
but was a petty servant to the state,
he was your enemy, always spoke against
your liberties and the charters that you bear
in the body of the commonwealth; and now, arriving
at a place of potency and sway in the state,
if he should still maliciously remain
a firm foe to the common people, your voices might
be curses to yourselves? You should have said
that as his worthy deeds claimed no less
than what he stood for, so his gracious nature
would think of you for your voices and
translate his malice towards you into love,
being your friendly lord.

SICINIUS:
To have said this,
as you were fore-advised, would have touched his spirit
and tested his inclination; it would have plucked from him
either his gracious promise, which you might,
as the cause had called you up, have held him to,
or else it would have angered his surly nature,
which does not easily endure any article
tying him to anything. So, putting him to rage,
you should have taken advantage of his anger
and passed him over unelected.

BRUTUS:
Did you perceive
that he solicited you with open contempt
when he needed your love, and do you think
that his contempt will not be crushing to you
when he has the power to crush? Why, did your bodies
have no heart among you? Or did you have tongues to cry
against the rule of judgment?

SICINIUS:
Have you
ever before denied the asker? And now again,
of him that did not ask, but mock, you bestow
your sought-after tongues?

Third Citizen:
He's not confirmed; we can deny him yet.

Second Citizen:
And we will deny him.
I'll get five hundred voices of that sound.

First Citizen:
I'll get twice five hundred and their friends to join them.

BRUTUS:
Get you from here instantly, and tell those friends,
they have chosen a consul that will take from them
their liberties; make them of no more voice
than dogs that are as often beaten for barking
as they are kept to do so.

SICINIUS:
Let them assemble,
and on a safer judgment, all of you revoke
your ignorant election. Enforce his pride,
and his old hatred for you. Besides, don't forget
with what contempt he wore the humble garment,
how in his suit he scorned you. But your love,
thinking upon his services, took from you
the apprehension of his present behavior,
which most mockingly and disrespectfully he did fashion
after the inveterate hate he bears you.

BRUTUS:
Lay
a fault on us, your tribunes; that we worked,
with no impediment between, so that you had to
cast your election on him.

SICINIUS:
Say, you chose him
more according to our command than as guided
by your own true affections, and that your minds,
preoccupied with what you rather had to do
than what you should, made you against your will
to vote him consul. Lay the fault on us.

BRUTUS:
Yes, don't spare us. Say we gave you lectures on
how young he began to serve his country,
how long he continued, and what stock he comes from,
the noble house of the Marcians, from which came
that Ancus Marcius, Numa's daughter's son,
who, after great Hostilius, was king here;
of the same house Publius and Quintus were,
that brought our best water here by conduits;
and
twice being
was his great ancestor.

SICINIUS:
One thus descended,
that has besides worked well in his own person
to be set high in place, we did commend
to your remembrances. But you have found,
comparing his present bearing with his past,
that he's your fixed enemy, and revoke
your sudden approval.

BRUTUS:
Say, you never would have done it—
harp on that still—but by our urging on;
and presently, when you have gathered your number,
repair to the Capitol.

All:
We will do so. Almost all of us
repent our election.

BRUTUS:
Let them go on;
this mutiny is better put to the test
than to wait, past doubt, for a greater one.
If, as his nature is, he falls into a rage
with their refusal, both observe and respond to
the advantage of his anger.

SICINIUS:
To the Capitol, come.
We will be there before the stream of people,
and this shall seem, as it partly is, their own doing,
which we have urged onward.

CORIOLANUS:
Tullus Aufidius then had raised a new army?

LARTIUS:
He had, my lord; and that is what caused
our swifter agreement.

CORIOLANUS:
So then the Volsces stand but as at first,
ready, when the time is right, to make a raid
upon us again.

COMINIUS:
They are worn out, lord consul, so
that we shall hardly in our lifetimes see
their banners wave again.

CORIOLANUS:
Did you see Aufidius?

LARTIUS:
He came to me under safe-conduct; and he cursed
against the Volsces, for they had so vilely
yielded the town. He has retired to Antium.

CORIOLANUS:
Did he speak of me?

LARTIUS:
He did, my lord.

CORIOLANUS:
How? What?

LARTIUS:
How often he had met you, sword to sword;
that of all things on the earth he hated
your person most, that he would pawn his fortunes
to hopeless restitution, so he might
be called your vanquisher.

CORIOLANUS:
Does he live at Antium?

LARTIUS:
At Antium.

CORIOLANUS:
I wish I had a reason to seek him there,
to oppose his hatred fully. Welcome home.
Behold, these are the tribunes of the people,
the tongues of the common mouth. I despise them;
for they parade themselves in authority,
against all noble tolerance.

SICINIUS:
Go no further.

CORIOLANUS:
Ha! What is that?

BRUTUS:
It will be dangerous to go on. No further.

CORIOLANUS:
What makes this change?

MENENIUS:
What's the matter?

COMINIUS:
Has he not passed the noble and the common?

BRUTUS:
Cominius, no.

CORIOLANUS:
Have I had children's voices?

First Senator:
Tribunes, give way; he shall go to the market-place.

BRUTUS:
The people are angry with him.

SICINIUS:
Stop,
or everything will fall into a fight.

CORIOLANUS:
Are these your herd?
Must these have voices, that can give them now
and straight take them back? What are
your offices?
Since you are their mouths, why don't you rule their teeth?
Haven't you set them on me?

MENENIUS:
Be calm, be calm.

CORIOLANUS:
It is a planned thing, and it grows by a plot,
to curb the will of the nobility.
Suffer it, and live with those who cannot rule
nor ever will be ruled.

BRUTUS:
Don't call it a plot.
The people cry you mocked them, and recently,
when corn was given to them for free, you complained;
you slandered the petitioners for the people, called them
time-pleasers, flatterers, foes to nobleness.

CORIOLANUS:
Why, this was known before.

BRUTUS:
Not to them all.

CORIOLANUS:
Have you informed them since?

BRUTUS:
What! I inform them!

CORIOLANUS:
You are likely to do such business.

BRUTUS:
Not unlike,
in every way, to improve yours.

CORIOLANUS:
Why then should I be consul? By those clouds,
let me deserve as ill as you, and make me
your fellow tribune.

SICINIUS:
You show too much of that
for which the people are stirred up. If you want to pass
to where you are bound, you must inquire your way,
which you are out of, with a gentler spirit,
or never be so noble as a consul,
nor be yoked with him for tribune.

MENENIUS:
Let's be calm.

COMINIUS:
The people are being abused, set on. This paltering
does not become Rome, nor has Coriolanus
deserved this so dishonored setback, laid falsely
in the plain way of his merit.

CORIOLANUS:
Tell me about corn!
This was my speech, and I will speak it again—

MENENIUS:
Not now, not now.

First Senator:
Not in this heat, sir, now.

CORIOLANUS:
Now, as I live, I will. My nobler friends,
I crave their pardons.
For the fickle, rank-smelling many, let them
regard me as I do not flatter, and
therein behold themselves. I say again,
in soothing them, we nourish against our senate
the weed of rebellion, insolence, sedition,
which we ourselves have plowed for, sown,
and scattered,
by mingling them with us, the honored number,
who lack not virtue, no, nor power, but that
which they have given to beggars.

MENENIUS:
Well, no more.

First Senator:
No more words, we beseech you.

CORIOLANUS:
What! No more!
As for my country, I have shed my blood,
not fearing outward force, so shall my lungs
coin words until their decay against those diseases,
which we disdain should tatter us, yet sought
the very way to catch them.

BRUTUS:
You speak of the people
as if you were a god to punish, not
a man of their infirmity.

SICINIUS:
It would be well
if we let the people know it.

MENENIUS:
What, what? His anger?

CORIOLANUS:
Anger!
If I were as patient as the midnight sleep,
by Jove, that would be my mind!

SICINIUS:
It is a mind
that shall remain a poison where it is,
and not poison any further.

CORIOLANUS:
Shall remain!
Do you hear this Triton of the minnows? Do you mark
his absolute 'shall'?

COMINIUS:
It was from the law.

CORIOLANUS:
'Shall'!
O good but most unwise patricians! Why,
you grave but reckless senators, have you thus
given Hydra here the power to choose an officer,
that with his peremptory 'shall,' being but
the horn and noise of the monster, does not lack the spirit
to say he'll turn your current into a ditch,
and make your channel his? If he has power,
then veil your ignorance; if he has none, awaken
your dangerous leniency. If you are learned,
do not be like common fools; if you are not,
let them have cushions by you. You are commoners
if they are senators; and they are no less,
when, both your voices blended, the greatest taste
most favors theirs. They choose their magistrate,
and such a one as he, who puts his 'shall,'
his popular 'shall' against a graver bench
than ever frowned in Greece. By Jove himself!
It makes the consuls base, and my soul aches
to know, when two authorities are up,
neither supreme, how soon confusion
may enter between the gap of both and take
the one by the other.

COMINIUS:
Well, on to the market-place.

CORIOLANUS:
Whoever gave that counsel, to give out
the grain from the storehouse for free, as was sometimes done
in Greece—

MENENIUS:
Well, well, no more of that.

CORIOLANUS:
Though there the people had more absolute power,
I say, they nourished disobedience, fed
the ruin of the state.

BRUTUS:
Why, should the people give
their vote to one who speaks thus?

CORIOLANUS:
I'll give my reasons,
more worthy than their voices. They know the corn
was not our reward, being well assured
that they never did service for it. Being pressed to the war,
even when the heart of the state was touched,
they would not go through the gates. This kind of service
did not deserve free corn. Being in the war,
their mutinies and revolts, in which they showed
the most valor, did not speak for them. The accusation
which they have often made against the senate,
without any cause, could never be the motive
of our so generous donation. Well, what then?
How shall this blind multitude digest
the senate's courtesy? Let deeds express
what their words are likely to be: 'We requested it;
we are the greater number, and in true fear
they gave us our demands.' Thus we debase
the nature of our seats and make the rabble
call our cares fears; which will in time
break open the locks of the senate and bring in
the crows to peck the eagles.

MENENIUS:
Come, enough.

BRUTUS:
Enough, with over-measure.

CORIOLANUS:
No, take more:
What may be sworn by, both divine and human,
seal what I end with! This double worship,
where one part disdains with cause, the other
insults without any reason, where gentry, title, wisdom,
cannot conclude but by the yea and no
of general ignorance—it must omit
real necessities, and give way the while
to unstable slightness. Purpose so barred,
it follows,
nothing is done to purpose. Therefore, I beseech you—
you that will be less fearful than discreet,
that love the fundamental part of state
more than you doubt the change of it, that prefer
a noble life before a long one, and wish
to risk a body with a dangerous medicine
that's sure of death without it, at once pluck out
the multitudinous tongue; let them not lick
the sweet which is their poison. Your dishonor
mangles true judgment and deprives the state
of that integrity which should become it,
not having the power to do the good it would,
for the sake of the power which controls it.

BRUTUS:
He has said enough.

SICINIUS:
He has spoken like a traitor, and shall answer
as traitors do.

CORIOLANUS:
You wretch, may contempt overwhelm you!
What should the people do with these bald tribunes?
On whom depending, their obedience fails
to the greater bench. In a rebellion,
when what's not right, but what must be, was law,
then they were chosen. In a better hour,
let what is right be said to be right,
and throw their power in the dust.

BRUTUS:
Manifest treason!

SICINIUS:
This is a consul? No.

BRUTUS:
The aediles, ho!
Let him be arrested.

SICINIUS:
Go, call the people,
in whose name I
arrest you as a treasonous innovator,
a foe to the public good. Obey, I charge you,
and follow me to your answer.

CORIOLANUS:
Hence, old goat!

Senators, etc.:
We'll be his surety.

COMINIUS:
Aged sir, hands off.

CORIOLANUS:
Go away, you rotten thing! Or I'll shake your bones
out of your clothes.

SICINIUS:
Help, you citizens!

MENENIUS:
On both sides, more respect.

SICINIUS:
Here's the one who would take from you all your power.

BRUTUS:
Seize him, Aediles!

Citizens:
Down with him! Down with him!

Senators, etc.:
Weapons, weapons, weapons!
'Tribunes!' 'Patricians!' 'Citizens!' 'What, ho!'
'Sicinius!' 'Brutus!' 'Coriolanus!' 'Citizens!'
'Peace, peace, peace!' 'Stay, hold, peace!'

MENENIUS:
What is about to happen? I am out of breath;
confusion is near; I cannot speak. You, tribunes
to the people! Coriolanus, patience!
Speak, good Sicinius.

SICINIUS:
Hear me, people; peace!

Citizens:
Let's hear our tribune! Peace! Speak, speak, speak.

SICINIUS:
You are about to lose your liberties.
Marcius would take everything from you; Marcius,
whom you recently named for consul.

MENENIUS:
Fie, fie, fie!
This is the way to kindle, not to quench.

First Senator:
To unbuild the city and to lay everything flat.

SICINIUS:
What is the city but the people?

Citizens:
True,
the people are the city.

BRUTUS:
By the consent of all, we were established
as the people's magistrates.

Citizens:
You so remain.

MENENIUS:
And so you are likely to do.

COMINIUS:
That is the way to lay the city flat;
to bring the roof to the foundation,
and bury all, which yet is distinctly arranged,
in heaps and piles of ruin.

SICINIUS:
This deserves death.

BRUTUS:
Either let us stand by our authority,
or let us lose it. We do here pronounce,
on behalf of the people, in whose power
we were elected theirs, Marcius is worthy
of immediate death.

SICINIUS:
Therefore, lay hold of him;
bear him to the Tarpeian rock, and from there
cast him into destruction.

BRUTUS:
Aediles, seize him!

Citizens:
Yield, Marcius, yield!

MENENIUS:
Hear me for one word;
I beseech you, tribunes, hear me for just one word.

Aedile:
Peace, peace!

MENENIUS:

BRUTUS:
Sir, those cold ways,
that seem like prudent helps, are very poisonous
where the disease is violent. Lay hands upon him,
and take him to the rock.

CORIOLANUS:
No, I'll die here.
There are some among you who have seen me fighting.
Come, try for yourselves what you have seen me do.

MENENIUS:
Down with that sword! Tribunes, withdraw for a while.

BRUTUS:
Lay hands upon him.

COMINIUS:
Help Marcius, help,
you that are noble; help him, young and old!

Citizens:
Down with him, down with him!

MENENIUS:
Go, get to your house; be gone, away!
Everything will be for nothing otherwise.
