Second Senator:
Get you gone.

COMINIUS:
Stand fast;
we have as many friends as enemies.

MENENIUS:
Should it be put to that?

First Senator:
The gods forbid!
I beg you, noble friend, go home to your house;
leave us to cure this cause.

MENENIUS:
For it's a sore on us,
you cannot treat yourself. Be gone, I beseech you.

COMINIUS:
Come, sir, along with us.

CORIOLANUS:
I wish they were barbarians—as they are,
though born in Rome—not Romans—as they are not,
though born on the porch of the Capitol—

MENENIUS:
Be gone;
do not put your worthy rage into your words;
one time will owe another.

CORIOLANUS:
On fair ground,
I could beat forty of them.

COMINIUS:
I could myself
take up a pair of the best of them; yes, the
two tribunes.
But now the odds are beyond arithmetic;
and manhood is called foolishness when it stands
against a falling building. Will you go,
before the mob returns? Their rage tears
like interrupted waters and overwhelms
what they are used to bearing.

MENENIUS:
Please, be gone.
I'll try whether my old wit is in demand
with those that have but little. This must be patched
with cloth of any color.

COMINIUS:
No, come away.

A Patrician:
This man has ruined his fortune.

MENENIUS:
His nature is too noble for the world.
He would not flatter Neptune for his trident,
or Jove for his power to thunder. His heart is his mouth:
what his breast forges, his tongue must speak;
and, being angry, he forgets that he ever
heard the name of death.
Here's goodly work!

Second Patrician:
I wish they were in bed!

MENENIUS:
I wish they were in the Tiber! What the devil!
Could he not speak to them fairly?

SICINIUS:
Where is this viper
that would depopulate the city and
be every man himself?

MENENIUS:
You worthy tribunes,—

SICINIUS:
He shall be thrown down the Tarpeian rock
with rigorous hands. He has resisted the law,
and therefore the law shall scorn him any further trial
than the severity of the public power
which he so sets at nothing.

First Citizen:
He shall well know
the noble tribunes are the people's mouths,
and we are their hands.

Citizens:
He shall, for sure.

MENENIUS:
Sir, sir,--

SICINIUS:
Peace!

MENENIUS:
Do not cry havoc, where you should but hunt
with a modest warrant.

SICINIUS:
Sir, how is it that you
have helped to make this rescue?

MENENIUS:
Hear me speak.
As I know the consul's worthiness,
so I can name his faults—

SICINIUS:
Consul! What consul?

MENENIUS:
The consul Coriolanus.

BRUTUS:
He, consul!

Citizens:
No, no, no, no, no.

MENENIUS:
If, by the tribunes' leave, and yours, good people,
I may be heard, I would crave a word or two;
the which shall bring you no further harm
than so much loss of time.

SICINIUS:
Speak briefly then;
for we are determined to dispatch
this venomous traitor. To eject him from here
would be but one danger, and to keep him here
would be our certain death. Therefore, it is decreed
he dies tonight.

MENENIUS:
Now may the good gods forbid
that our renowned Rome, whose gratitude
towards her deserving children is enrolled
in Jove's own book, should, like an unnatural mother,
now eat up her own!

SICINIUS:
He's a disease that must be cut away.

MENENIUS:
Oh, he's a limb that has but a disease;
it's mortal to cut it off, but easy to cure it.
What has he done to Rome that's worthy of death?
Killing our enemies, the blood he has lost—
which, I dare say, is more than what he has left,
by many an ounce—he dropped it for his country;
and what is left, to lose it by his country,
would be to us all, who do it and suffer it,
a brand to the end of the world.

SICINIUS:
This is completely crooked.

BRUTUS:
Merely crooked. When he did love his country,
it honored him.

MENENIUS:
The service of the foot,
once gangrened, is not then respected
for what it was before.

BRUTUS:
We'll hear no more.
Pursue him to his house, and pluck him from there,
lest his infection, being of a contagious nature,
spread further.

MENENIUS:
One word more, one word.
This tiger-footed rage, when it shall find
the harm of unconsidered swiftness, will too late
tie leaden pounds to its heels. Proceed by process,
lest parties, as he is beloved, break out,
and sack great Rome with Romans.

BRUTUS:
If it were so,—

SICINIUS:
What are you talking about?
Haven't we had a taste of his obedience?
Our aediles struck? Ourselves resisted? Come.

MENENIUS:
Consider this: he has been raised in the wars
since he could draw a sword, and is poorly schooled
in polished language. He throws meal and bran together
without distinction. Give me leave,
I'll go to him, and undertake to bring him
where he shall answer, by a lawful form,
in peace, to his utmost peril.

First Senator:
Noble tribunes,
it is the humane way. The other course
will prove too bloody, and the end of it
unknown at the beginning.

SICINIUS:
Noble Menenius,
you be then as the people's officer.
Masters, lay down your weapons.

BRUTUS:
Don't go home.

SICINIUS:
Meet on the market-place. We'll attend you there.
Where, if you do not bring Marcius, we'll proceed
in our first way.

MENENIUS:
I'll bring him to you.
Let me ask for your company. He must come,
or what is worst will follow.

First Senator:
Please, let's go to him.

CORIOLANUS:
Let them puff all around my ears, present me
death on the wheel or at the heels of wild horses,
or pile ten hills on the Tarpeian rock,
so that the fall might stretch down
below the beam of sight, yet I will still
be this way to them.

A Patrician:
You are doing the nobler thing.

CORIOLANUS:
I wonder why my mother
doesn't approve of me further, who used to
call them woolen vassals, things created
to buy and sell with groats, to show bare heads
in congregations, to yawn, be still, and wonder,
when just one of my rank stood up
to speak of peace or war.
I'm talking about you.
Why did you wish I were milder? Would you have me
be false to my nature? I'd rather say I'm playing
the man I am.

VOLUMNIA:
Oh, sir, sir, sir,
I would have had you put your power on well,
before you had worn it out.

CORIOLANUS:
Let go.

VOLUMNIA:
You might have been enough of the man you are,
with less striving to be so. Your dispositions would have been
thwarted less if
you had not shown them how you were disposed
before they lacked the power to cross you.

CORIOLANUS:
Let them hang.

A Patrician:
Yes, and burn too.

MENENIUS:
Come, come, you have been too rough, a little
too rough.
You must return and fix it.

First Senator:
There's no remedy;
unless, by not doing so, our good city
cleaves in the middle and perishes.

VOLUMNIA:
Please, be counseled.
I have a heart as little apt as yours,
but yet a brain that leads my use of anger
to a better advantage.

MENENIUS:
Well said, noble woman!
Before he should thus stoop to the herd, but that
the violent fit of the time craves it as medicine
for the whole state, I would put on my armor,
which I can scarcely bear.

CORIOLANUS:
What must I do?

MENENIUS:
Return to the tribunes.

CORIOLANUS:
Well, what then? What then?

MENENIUS:
Repent what you have spoken.

CORIOLANUS:
For them! I cannot do it to the gods;
must I then do it to them?

VOLUMNIA:
You are too absolute;
though in that you can never be too noble,
but when extremities speak. I have heard you say,
honor and policy, like unseparated friends,
grow together in war. Grant that, and tell me,
in peace what does each of them lose by the other,
that they do not combine there?

CORIOLANUS:
Tush, tush!

MENENIUS:
A good question.

VOLUMNIA:
If it is honor in your wars to seem
the same as you are not, which, for your best ends,
you adopt as your policy, how is it less or worse,
that it should hold companionship in peace
with honor, as in war, since it is
in like request for both?

CORIOLANUS:
Why do you force this?

VOLUMNIA:
Because it now falls on you to speak
to the people; not by your own instruction,
nor by the matter which your heart prompts you,
but with such words that are but rooted in
your tongue, though but bastards and syllables
of no allowance to your bosom's truth.
Now, this no more dishonors you at all
than to take a town with gentle words,
which else would put you to your fortune and
the hazard of much blood.
I would dissemble with my nature where
my fortunes and my friends at stake required
I should do so in honor. I am in this,
your wife, your son, these senators, the nobles;
and you will rather show our general louts
how you can frown than spend a fawn upon them,
for the inheritance of their love and the safeguard
of what that lack might ruin.

MENENIUS:
Noble lady!
Come, go with us; speak fairly. You may solve so,
not what is dangerous now, but the loss
of what is past.

VOLUMNIA:
I beg you now, my son,
go to them, with this hat in your hand;
and thus far having stretched it—here be with them—
your knee kissing the stones—for in such business
action is eloquence, and the eyes of the ignorant
are more learned than the ears—waving your head,
which often, thus, correcting your stout heart,
is now as humble as the ripest mulberry
that will not hold the handling. Or say to them,
you are their soldier, and being raised in battles,
you do not have the soft way which, you confess,
would be fit for you to use as it would be for them to claim,
in asking for their good love, but you will frame
yourself, forsooth, hereafter theirs, as far
as you have power and person.

MENENIUS:
If this were but done,
even as she speaks, why, their hearts would be yours;
for they have pardons, when asked, as freely
as words to little purpose.

VOLUMNIA:
Please now,
go, and be ruled, although I know you would rather
follow your enemy into a fiery gulf
than flatter him in a bower. Here is Cominius.

COMINIUS:
I have been in the market-place; and, sir, it is fitting
that you make a strong party, or defend yourself
by calmness or by absence. Everything is in anger.

MENENIUS:
Only fair speech.

COMINIUS:
I think it will work, if he
can frame his spirit to it.

VOLUMNIA:
He must, and will.
Please now, say you will, and go about it.

CORIOLANUS:
Must I go show them my unadorned head?
Must I with a base tongue give my noble heart
a lie that it must bear? Well, I will do it.
Yet, if there were but this single piece of land to lose,
this mold of Marcius, they should grind it to dust
and throw it against the wind. To the market-place!
You have put me now to such a part which I never
shall perform true to life.

COMINIUS:
Come, come, we'll prompt you.

VOLUMNIA:
I beg you now, sweet son, as you have said
my praises first made you a soldier, so,
to have my praise for this, perform a part
you have not done before.

CORIOLANUS:
Well, I must do it.
Away, my disposition, and possess me
with some harlot's spirit! May my throat of war be turned,
which chimed with my drum, into a pipe
as small as a eunuch's, or the virgin voice
that lulls babies to sleep! May the smiles of knaves
pitch their tents in my cheeks, and schoolboys' tears take up
the glasses of my sight! May a beggar's tongue
make motion through my lips, and my armed knees,
which bowed but in my stirrup, bend like his
that has received an alms! I will not do it,
lest I cease to honor my own truth
and by my body's action teach my mind
a most inherent baseness.

VOLUMNIA:
At your choice, then.
To beg of you is my greater dishonor
than you of them. Let all come to ruin; let
your mother rather feel your pride than fear
your dangerous stubbornness, for I mock at death
with as big a heart as you. Do as you wish.
Your valor was mine, you sucked it from me,
but you owe your pride to yourself.

CORIOLANUS:
Please, be content.
Mother, I am going to the market-place;
chide me no more. I'll swindle their love,
con their hearts from them, and come home beloved
of all the trades in Rome. Look, I am going.
Commend me to my wife. I'll return as consul,
or never trust what my tongue can do
in the way of flattery further.

VOLUMNIA:
Do your will.

COMINIUS:
Away! The tribunes are waiting for you. Arm yourself
to answer mildly; for they are prepared
with accusations, as I hear, stronger
than those that are upon you yet.

CORIOLANUS:
The word is 'mildly.' Please, let us go.
Let them accuse me by invention, I
will answer in my honor.

MENENIUS:
Yes, but mildly.

CORIOLANUS:
Well, mildly be it then. Mildly!

BRUTUS:
On this point, charge him directly, that he seeks
tyrannical power. If he evades us there,
force him with his envy of the people,
and that the spoil gotten from the Antiates
was never distributed.
What, will he come?

Aedile:
He's coming.

BRUTUS:
Who is with him?

Aedile:
With old Menenius, and those senators
that always favored him.

SICINIUS:
Have you a catalog
of all the votes that we have procured,
set down by the poll?

Aedile:
I have; it's ready.

SICINIUS:
Have you collected them by tribes?

Aedile:
I have.

SICINIUS:
Assemble the people here immediately;
and when they hear me say, 'It shall be so
in the right and strength of the commons,' whether it be
for death, for a fine, or for banishment, then let them,
if I say 'fine,' cry 'Fine'; if 'death,' cry 'Death,'
insisting on the old prerogative
and power in the truth of the cause.

Aedile:
I shall inform them.

BRUTUS:
And when they have begun to cry at such a time,
let them not cease, but with a confused din,
enforce the immediate execution
of whatever we happen to sentence.

Aedile:
Very well.

SICINIUS:
Make them be strong and ready for this hint,
when we happen to give it to them.

BRUTUS:
Go about it.
Put him to anger straight away. He has been used
to always conquer, and to have his worth
of contradiction. Being once angered, he cannot
be reined back to temperance. Then he speaks
what's in his heart; and that is there which looks
with us to break his neck.

SICINIUS:
Well, here he comes.

MENENIUS:
Calmly, I beseech you.

CORIOLANUS:
Yes, like a stable-hand, who for the poorest piece
will bear the knave by the volume. May the honored gods
keep Rome in safety, and the chairs of justice
supplied with worthy men! Plant love among us!
Throng our large temples with the shows of peace,
and not our streets with war!

First Senator:
Amen, amen.

MENENIUS:
A noble wish.

SICINIUS:
Draw near, you people.

Aedile:
Listen to your tribunes. Audience! Peace, I say!

CORIOLANUS:
First, hear me speak.

Both Tribunes:
Well, say. Peace, ho!

CORIOLANUS:
Shall I be charged no further than this present?
Must everything be decided here?

SICINIUS:
I demand to know,
if you submit to the people's voices,
allow their officers, and are content
to suffer lawful censure for such faults
as shall be proved against you?

CORIOLANUS:
I am content.

MENENIUS:
Lo, citizens, he says he is content.
The warlike service he has done, consider; think
upon the wounds his body bears, which show
like graves in the holy churchyard.

CORIOLANUS:
Scratches with briars,
scars to provoke laughter only.

MENENIUS:
Consider further,
that when he speaks not like a citizen,
you find him like a soldier. Do not take
his rougher accents for malicious sounds,
but, as I say, such as become a soldier,
rather than to envy you.

COMINIUS:
Well, well, no more.

CORIOLANUS:
What is the matter,
that being approved as consul with a full vote,
I am so dishonored that the very hour
you take it off again?

SICINIUS:
Answer to us.

CORIOLANUS:
Say, then, it's true, I ought to.

SICINIUS:
We charge you, that you have contrived to take
from Rome all seasoned office and to wind
yourself into a tyrannical power;
for which you are a traitor to the people.

CORIOLANUS:
How! A traitor!

MENENIUS:
No, temperately; your promise.

CORIOLANUS:
May the fires in the lowest hell engulf the people!
Call me their traitor! You injurious tribune!
If twenty thousand deaths sat in your eyes,
and you clutched as many millions in your hand, and
both numbers were in your lying tongue, I would say
'You lie' to you with a voice as free
as I pray to the gods.

SICINIUS:
Do you notice this, people?

Citizens:
To the rock, to the rock with him!

SICINIUS:
Peace!
We don't need to add new matter to his charge.
What you have seen him do and heard him speak,
beating your officers, cursing yourselves,
opposing laws with strokes, and here defying
those whose great power must try him; even this,
so criminal and in such a capital kind,
deserves the most extreme death.

BRUTUS:
But since he has
served well for Rome,—

CORIOLANUS:
What are you talking about service?

BRUTUS:
I'm talking about that, because I know it.

CORIOLANUS:
You?

MENENIUS:
Is this the promise that you made your mother?

COMINIUS:
Know, I pray you,--

CORIOLANUS:
I'll go no further.
Let them pronounce the steep Tarpeian death,
vagabond exile, being left to linger
with but a grain of food a day, I would not buy
their mercy at the price of one fair word;
nor would I check my courage for what they can give,
to have it by saying 'Good morrow.'

SICINIUS:
Because he has,
as much as lies in him, from time to time
shown envy against the people, seeking means
to take away their power, as now at last
he has given hostile strokes, and that not in the presence
of dreaded justice, but on the ministers
that distribute it; in the name of the people
and in the power of us the tribunes, we,
even from this instant, banish him from our city,
in peril of being thrown
from off the Tarpeian rock, never more
to enter our Rome's gates. In the people's name,
I say it shall be so.

Citizens:
It shall be so, it shall be so; let him go away.
He's banished, and it shall be so.

COMINIUS:
Hear me, my masters, and my common friends,—

SICINIUS:
He's sentenced; no more hearing.

COMINIUS:
Let me speak.
I have been consul, and can show for Rome
her enemies' marks upon me. I do love
my country's good with a respect more tender,
more holy and profound, than my own life,
my dear wife's value, her womb's increase,
and the treasure of my loins. Then if I would
speak that,—

SICINIUS:
We know your intention. Speak what?

BRUTUS:
There's nothing more to be said, but that he is banished,
as an enemy to the people and his country.
It shall be so.

Citizens:
It shall be so, it shall be so.

CORIOLANUS:
You common cry of dogs! Whose breath I hate
as the stench of rotten swamps, whose love I value
as the dead carcasses of unburied men
that corrupt my air, I banish you;
and here remain with your uncertainty!
Let every feeble rumor shake your hearts!
May your enemies, with the nodding of their plumes,
fan you into despair! May you still have the power
to banish your defenders, until at last
your ignorance, which doesn't find out until it feels,
making no reservation for yourselves,
still your own foes, you deliver yourselves as most
abject captives to some nation
that conquered you without a fight! Despising,
for you, the city, thus I turn my back.
There is a world elsewhere.

Aedile:
The people's enemy is gone, is gone!

Citizens:
Our enemy is banished! He is gone! Hurrah! Hurrah!

SICINIUS:
Go, see him out at the gates, and follow him,
as he has followed you, with all spite;
give him the vexation he deserves. Let a guard
attend us through the city.

Citizens:
Come, come; let's see him out at the gates; come.
The gods preserve our noble tribunes! Come.

CORIOLANUS:
Come, leave your tears. A brief farewell. The beast
with many heads butts me away. No, mother,
where is your ancient courage? You were used
to say extremity was the trier of spirits;
that common chances common men could bear;
that when the sea was calm all boats alike
showed mastership in floating; fortune's blows,
when most struck home, being gently wounded, require
a noble cunning. You were used to load me
with precepts that would make invincible
the heart that learned them.

VIRGILIA:
Oh heavens! Oh heavens!

CORIOLANUS:
No, please, woman,--

VOLUMNIA:
Now may the red plague strike all trades in Rome,
and may all occupations perish!

CORIOLANUS:
What, what, what!
I shall be loved when I am missed. No, mother.
Resume that spirit, when you used to say,
if you had been the wife of Hercules,
you would have done six of his labors, and saved
your husband so much sweat. Cominius,
do not droop. Goodbye. Farewell, my wife, my mother.
I'll do well yet. You old and true Menenius,
your tears are saltier than a younger man's,
and venomous to your eyes. My former general,
I have seen you stem, and you have often beheld
heart-hardening spectacles. Tell these sad women
it is foolish to wail over inevitable strokes,
as it is to laugh at them. My mother, you know well
my hazards have always been your solace. And
believe it not lightly—though I go alone,
like a lonely dragon, whose fen
makes him feared and talked of more than seen—your son
will either exceed the common or be caught
with cautious baits and practice.

VOLUMNIA:
My first son,
where will you go? Take good Cominius
with you for a while. Decide on some course,
more than a wild exposure to every chance
that starts in the way before you.

CORIOLANUS:
Oh the gods!

COMINIUS:
I'll follow you for a month, and devise with you
where you shall rest, so that you may hear from us
and we from you. So if the time brings forth
a reason for your repeal, we will not have to send
over the vast world to seek a single man,
and lose an advantage, which always cools
in the absence of the one who needs it.

CORIOLANUS:
Fare you well.
You have years upon you, and you are too full
of the surfeits of war to go roving with one
that's yet unbruised. Just bring me out at the gate.
Come, my sweet wife, my dearest mother, and
my friends of noble touch, when I am gone,
bid me farewell, and smile. I pray you, come.
While I remain above the ground, you shall
hear from me still, and never anything of me
but what is like me formerly.

MENENIUS:
That's as worthily
as any ear can hear. Come, let's not weep.
If I could shake off but one seven years
from these old arms and legs, by the good gods,
I'd go with you every foot.

CORIOLANUS:
Give me your hand. Come.

SICINIUS:
Tell them all to go home; he's gone, and we'll go no further.
The nobility are angry, who we see have sided
with him.

BRUTUS:
Now that we have shown our power,
let us seem humbler after it is done
than when we were doing it.

SICINIUS:
Tell them to go home.
Say their great enemy is gone, and they
stand in their ancient strength.

BRUTUS:
Dismiss them home.
Here comes his mother.

SICINIUS:
Let's not meet her.

BRUTUS:
Why?

SICINIUS:
They say she's mad.

BRUTUS:
They have taken note of us. Keep on your way.

VOLUMNIA:
Oh, you are well met. May the hoarded plague of the gods
repay your love!

MENENIUS:
Peace, peace; don't be so loud.

VOLUMNIA:
If I could speak through my weeping, you would hear—
no, and you shall hear some.
Will you be gone?

VIRGILIA:

SICINIUS:
Are you human?

VOLUMNIA:
Yes, fool. Is that a shame? Just look at this fool.
Wasn't a man my father? Did you have the cunning
to banish him who struck more blows for Rome
than you have spoken words?

SICINIUS:
Oh blessed heavens!

VOLUMNIA:
More noble blows than you ever spoke wise words;
and for Rome's good. I'll tell you what; yet go.
No, but you shall stay too. I wish my son
were in Arabia, and your tribe before him,
his good sword in his hand.

SICINIUS:
What then?

VIRGILIA:
What then!
He would make an end of your posterity.

VOLUMNIA:
Bastards and all.
Good man, the wounds that he bears for Rome!

MENENIUS:
Come, come, peace.

SICINIUS:
I wish he had continued for his country
as he began, and had not untied himself
from the noble knot he made.

BRUTUS:
I wish he had.

VOLUMNIA:
'I wish he had'! It was you who incensed the rabble,
cats, that can judge his worth as well
as I can of those mysteries which heaven
will not have earth to know.

BRUTUS:
Please, let us go.

VOLUMNIA:
Now, please, sir, get you gone.
You have done a brave deed. Before you go, hear this:—
As far as the Capitol exceeds
the meanest house in Rome, so far my son—
this lady's husband here, this one, do you see—
whom you have banished, exceeds you all.

BRUTUS:
Well, well, we'll leave you.

SICINIUS:
Why do we stay to be baited
with one who has lost her wits?

VOLUMNIA:
Take my prayers with you.
I wish the gods had nothing else to do
but to confirm my curses! If I could meet them
but once a day, it would unclog my heart
of what lies heavy on it.

MENENIUS:
You have told them off.
And, by my faith, you have cause. Will you sup with me?

VOLUMNIA:
Anger is my food; I sup upon myself,
and so shall starve with feeding. Come, let's go.
Leave this faint puling and lament as I do,
in anger, like Juno. Come, come, come.

MENENIUS:
Fie, fie, fie!

Roman:
I know you well, sir, and you know
me. Your name, I think, is Adrian.

Volsce:
It is so, sir. Truly, I have forgotten you.

Roman:
I am a Roman; and my services are,
as you are, against them. Do you know me yet?

Volsce:
Nicanor? No.

Roman:
The same, sir.

Volsce:
You had more of a beard when I last saw you, but your
face is well confirmed by your tongue. What's the
news in Rome? I have a note from the Volscian state
to find you there. You have saved me a
day's journey.

Roman:
There have been strange insurrections in Rome; the
people against the senators, patricians, and nobles.

Volsce:
Has it been? Is it ended, then? Our state doesn't think
so. They are in a most warlike preparation, and
hope to come upon them in the heat of their division.

Roman:
The main blaze of it is past, but a small thing
would make it flame again. For the nobles take
the banishment of that worthy
Coriolanus so to heart, that they are in a ripe
aptness to take all power from the people and to
pluck from them their tribunes forever. This lies
glowing, I can tell you, and is almost mature for a
violent breaking out.

Volsce:
Coriolanus banished!

Roman:
Banished, sir.

Volsce:
You will be welcome with this intelligence, Nicanor.

Roman:
The day serves them well now. I have heard it
said, the fittest time to corrupt a man's wife is
when she's fallen out with her husband. Your noble
Tullus Aufidius will appear well in these wars, his
great opponent, Coriolanus, being now in no request
in his country.

Volsce:
He cannot help it. I am most fortunate, to
accidentally encounter you. You have ended my
business, and I will merrily accompany you home.

Roman:
I shall, between now and supper, tell you most
strange things from Rome; all tending to the good of
their adversaries. Have you an army ready, you say?

Volsce:
A most royal one; the centurions and their charges,
distinctly billeted, already in the entertainment,
and to be on foot at an hour's warning.

Roman:
I am joyful to hear of their readiness, and I am the
man, I think, that shall set them in present action.
So, sir, heartily well met, and most glad of your company.

Volsce:
You take my part from me, sir; I have the most cause
to be glad of yours.

Roman:
Well, let us go together.

CORIOLANUS:
A goodly city is this Antium. City,
it is I that made your widows. Many an heir
of these fair buildings, before my wars,
have I heard groan and drop. So do not recognize me,
lest your wives with spits and boys with stones
in a puny battle slay me.
Save you, sir.

Citizen:
And you.

CORIOLANUS:
Direct me, if it be your will,
where great Aufidius lies. Is he in Antium?

Citizen:
He is, and is feasting the nobles of the state
at his house this night.

CORIOLANUS:
Which is his house, I beseech you?

Citizen:
This one, here before you.

CORIOLANUS:
Thank you, sir. Farewell.
O world, your slippery turns! Friends now fast sworn,
whose double bosoms seem to wear one heart,
whose house, whose bed, whose meal, and exercise,
are still together, who are like twins, as it were, in love
inseparable, shall within this hour,
on a disagreement of a trifle, break out
into the bitterest enmity. So, the most fierce foes,
whose passions and whose plots have broken their sleep,
to take one the other, by some chance,
some trick not worth an egg, shall grow dear friends
and join their families. So with me:
my birthplace I hate, and my love is upon
this enemy town. I'll enter. If he slays me,
he does fair justice; if he gives me way,
I'll do his country service.

First Servingman:
Wine, wine, wine! What kind of service
is this! I think our fellows are asleep.

Second Servingman:
Where's Cotus? My master is calling
for him. Cotus!

CORIOLANUS:
A goodly house. The feast smells well, but I
do not look like a guest.

First Servingman:
What do you want, friend? Where are you from?
There's no place for you here. Please, go to the door.

CORIOLANUS:
I have deserved no better entertainment,
in being Coriolanus.

Second Servingman:
Where are you from, sir? Does the porter have his eyes in his
head, that he gives entrance to such companions?
Please, get out.

CORIOLANUS:
Away!

Second Servingman:
Away! Get you away.

CORIOLANUS:
Now you're being troublesome.

Second Servingman:
Are you so brave? I'll have you talked to soon.

Third Servingman:
What fellow is this?

First Servingman:
A strange one as I have ever looked on. I cannot get him
out of the house. Please, call my master to him.

Third Servingman:
What are you doing here, fellow? Please, leave
the house.

CORIOLANUS:
Let me just stand; I will not hurt your hearth.

Third Servingman:
What are you?

CORIOLANUS:
A gentleman.

Third Servingman:
A marvelously poor one.

CORIOLANUS:
True, so I am.

Third Servingman:
Please, poor gentleman, take up some other
station; there's no place for you here. Please, leave. Come.

CORIOLANUS:
Follow your function, go, and get fat on cold scraps.

Third Servingman:
What, you won't? Please, tell my master what a
strange guest he has here.

Second Servingman:
And I shall.

Third Servingman:
Where do you live?

CORIOLANUS:
Under the canopy.

Third Servingman:
Under the canopy!

CORIOLANUS:
Yes.

Third Servingman:
Where's that?

CORIOLANUS:
In the city of kites and crows.

Third Servingman:
In the city of kites and crows! What an ass he is!
Then you live with jackdaws too?

CORIOLANUS:
No, I don't serve your master.

Third Servingman:
What, sir! Are you meddling with my master?

CORIOLANUS:
Yes, it's a more honest service than to meddle with your
mistress. You prattle and prattle; serve with your
trencher, hence!

AUFIDIUS:
Where is this fellow?

Second Servingman:
Here, sir. I would have beaten him like a dog, but for
disturbing the lords within.

AUFIDIUS:
Where do you come from? What do you want? Your name?
Why don't you speak? Speak, man. What's your name?

CORIOLANUS:
If, Tullus,
you don't know me yet, and, seeing me, don't
think of me as the man I am, necessity
commands me to name myself.

AUFIDIUS:
What is your name?

CORIOLANUS:
A name unmusical to the Volscians' ears,
and harsh in sound to yours.

AUFIDIUS:
Say, what's your name?
You have a grim appearance, and your face
bears a command in it; though your clothes are torn,
you show a noble vessel. What's your name?

CORIOLANUS:
Prepare your brow to frown. Do you know me yet?

AUFIDIUS:
I don't know you. What's your name?

CORIOLANUS:
My name is Caius Marcius, who has done
to you particularly and to all the Volsces
great hurt and mischief. To that, my surname,
Coriolanus, may witness. The painful service,
the extreme dangers, and the drops of blood
shed for my thankless country are rewarded
only with that surname; a good reminder,
and a witness of the malice and displeasure
which you should bear me. Only that name remains;
the cruelty and envy of the people,
permitted by our cowardly nobles, who
have all forsaken me, has devoured the rest,
and allowed me, by the voice of slaves, to be
hooted out of Rome. Now this extremity
has brought me to your hearth; not out of hope—
mistake me not—to save my life, for if
I had feared death, of all the men in the world
I would have avoided you, but in mere spite,
to be fully rid of those who banished me,
I stand before you here. Then if you have
a heart of vengeance in you, that will avenge
your own particular wrongs and stop those injuries
of shame seen throughout your country, hurry straight away,
and make my misery serve your turn. So use it
that my vengeful services may prove
to be benefits to you, for I will fight
against my cankered country with the spleen
of all the under fiends. But if it be so
that you dare not do this, and that to try more fortunes
you are tired, then, in a word, I also am
most weary of living longer, and present
my throat to you and to your ancient malice;
which not to cut would show you but a fool,
since I have ever followed you with hate,
drawn tons of blood out of your country's breast,
and cannot live but to your shame, unless
it be to do you service.

AUFIDIUS:
Oh, Marcius, Marcius!
Every word you have spoken has weeded from my heart
a root of ancient envy. If Jupiter
should speak divine things from yonder cloud,
and say 'It is true,' I would not believe them more
than you, all noble Marcius. Let me twine
my arms about that body, against which
my grained ash spear has broken a hundred times
and scarred the moon with splinters. Here I embrace
the anvil of my sword, and I contest
as hotly and as nobly with your love
as ever in ambitious strength I did
contend against your valor. Know first,
I loved the maid I married; never did a man
sigh with a truer breath. But seeing you here,
you noble thing! My enraptured heart dances more
than when I first saw my wedded mistress
step over my threshold. Why, you are Mars! I tell you,
we have a force on foot; and I had intended
once more to hew your shield from your brawn,
or lose my arm for it. You have beaten me
twelve different times, and I have nightly since
dreamt of encounters between you and me.
We have been down together in my sleep,
unbuckling helmets, grabbing each other's throats,
and I woke up half dead with nothing. Worthy Marcius,
if we had no other quarrel with Rome but that
you are banished from there, we would muster all
from twelve to seventy, and pour war
into the bowels of ungrateful Rome,
like a bold flood overwhelming everything. Oh, come, go in,
and take our friendly senators by the hands,
who are now here, taking their leave of me,
who am prepared to go against your territories,
though not for Rome itself.

CORIOLANUS:
You bless me, gods!

AUFIDIUS:
Therefore, most absolute sir, if you want
the leadership of your own revenges, take
one half of my commission; and set down—
as you are best experienced, since you know
your country's strength and weakness—your own ways;
whether to knock against the gates of Rome,
or rudely visit them in remote parts,
to frighten them before you destroy them. But come in.
Let me commend you first to those that shall
say yes to your desires. A thousand welcomes!
And you are more a friend than you ever were an enemy;
yet, Marcius, that was much. Your hand. Most welcome!

First Servingman:
Here's a strange alteration!

Second Servingman:
By my hand, I had thought to have struck him with
a cudgel; and yet my mind told me his clothes made a
false report of him.

First Servingman:
What an arm he has! He turned me about with his
finger and his thumb, as one would set up a top.

Second Servingman:
No, I knew by his face that there was something in
him. He had, sir, a kind of face, I thought—I
cannot tell how to describe it.

First Servingman:
He had so, looking as it were—I wish I were hanged,
but I thought there was more in him than I could think.

Second Servingman:
So did I, I'll be sworn. He is simply the rarest
man in the world.

First Servingman:
I think he is. But a greater soldier than the one you know.

Second Servingman:
Who, my master?

First Servingman:
No, it doesn't matter.

Second Servingman:
He's worth six of him.

First Servingman:
No, not so either. But I take him to be the
greater soldier.

Second Servingman:
Faith, look you, one cannot tell how to say that.
For the defense of a town, our general is excellent.

First Servingman:
Yes, and for an assault too.

Third Servingman:
Oh slaves, I can tell you news—news, you rascals!

First Servingman:
What, what, what? Let's share.

Third Servingman:
I would not be a Roman, of all nations; I had as
soon be a condemned man.

First Servingman:
Why? Why?

Third Servingman:
Why, here's the one that used to thrash our general,
Caius Marcius.

First Servingman:
Why do you say 'thwack our general'?

Third Servingman:
I don't say 'thwack our general,' but he was always
good enough for him.

Second Servingman:
Come, we are fellows and friends. He was always too
hard for him; I have heard him say so himself.

First Servingman:
He was too hard for him directly, to tell the truth
of it. Before Corioli, he scored him and notched
him like a piece of meat for grilling.

Second Servingman:
If he had been cannibally inclined, he might have
broiled and eaten him too.

First Servingman:
But, more of your news?

Third Servingman:
Why, he is so made of here within, as if he were the son
and heir of Mars. He's seated at the head of the table; no
question is asked him by any of the senators, but they
stand bare-headed before him. Our general himself makes a
mistress of him; he sanctifies himself with his hand and
turns up the white of his eye to his discourse. But
the bottom of the news is that our general is cut in
the middle and is but one half of what he was
yesterday; for the other half has been given away, by the entreaty
and grant of the whole table. He'll go, he says,
and drag the porter of Rome's gates by the ears. He
will mow all down before him, and leave his passage
cleared.

Second Servingman:
And he's as likely to do it as any man I can imagine.

Third Servingman:
Do it! He will do it; for, look you, sir, he has as
many friends as enemies; which friends, sir, as it
were, dared not, look you, sir, show themselves, as
we call it, as his friends while he's in directitude.

First Servingman:
Directitude! What's that?

Third Servingman:
But when they shall see, sir, his crest up again,
and the man in a fighting mood, they will come out of their
burrows, like rabbits after rain, and revel all with
him.

First Servingman:
But when does this go forward?

Third Servingman:
Tomorrow; today; right away. You shall have the
drum struck up this afternoon. It is, as it were, a
part of their feast, and to be executed before they
wipe their lips.

Second Servingman:
Why, then we shall have a stirring world again.
This peace is nothing but to rust iron, increase
tailors, and breed ballad-makers.

First Servingman:
Let me have war, I say; it exceeds peace as far as
day does night; it's sprightly, waking, audible, and
full of action. Peace is a very apoplexy, lethargy;
dulled, deaf, sleepy, insensible; a getter of more
bastard children than war is a destroyer of men.

Second Servingman:
It's so. And as war, in some sort, may be said to
be a ravisher, so it cannot be denied but that peace is a
great maker of cuckolds.

First Servingman:
Yes, and it makes men hate one another.

Third Servingman:
The reason is that they then need each other less.
The wars for my money. I hope to see Romans as cheap
as Volscians. They are rising, they are rising.

All:
In, in, in, in!

SICINIUS:
We don't hear of him, nor do we need to fear him;
his remedies are tame in the present peace
and quietness of the people, which before
were in a wild hurry. Here we make his friends
blush that the world goes well, who would rather have,
though they themselves suffered by it, seen
rebellious numbers pestering the streets than see
our tradesmen in their shops and going
about their work in a friendly way.

BRUTUS:
We stood up to it in good time.
Is this Menenius?

SICINIUS:
It's him, it's him. Oh, he has grown most kind lately.

Both Tribunes:
Hail, sir!

MENENIUS:
Hail to you both!

SICINIUS:
Your Coriolanus
is not much missed, except by his friends.
The commonwealth stands, and so it would do,
if he were more angry at it.

MENENIUS:
All is well; and it might have been much better, if
he could have temporized.

SICINIUS:
Where is he, do you hear?

MENENIUS:
No, I hear nothing. His mother and his wife
hear nothing from him.

Citizens:
May the gods preserve you both!

SICINIUS:
Good day, our neighbors.

BRUTUS:
Good day to you all, good day to you all.

First Citizen:
Ourselves, our wives, and children, on our knees,
are bound to pray for you both.

SICINIUS:
Live, and thrive!

BRUTUS:
Farewell, kind neighbors. We wished Coriolanus
had loved you as we did.

Citizens:
Now the gods keep you!

Both Tribunes:
Farewell, farewell.

SICINIUS:
This is a happier and more seemly time
than when these fellows ran about the streets,
crying confusion.

BRUTUS:
Caius Marcius was
a worthy officer in the war; but insolent,
overcome with pride, ambitious past all thinking,
self-loving,--

SICINIUS:
And seeking one sole throne,
without assistance.

MENENIUS:
I think not so.

SICINIUS:
We should have, to all our lamentation,
if he had gone forth as consul, found it so.

BRUTUS:
The gods have well prevented it, and Rome
sits safe and still without him.

Aedile:
Worthy tribunes,
there is a slave, whom we have put in prison,
who reports that the Volsces with two separate forces
have entered the Roman territories,
and with the deepest malice of war
are destroying what lies before them.

MENENIUS:
It's Aufidius,
who, hearing of our Marcius' banishment,
thrusts forth his horns again into the world;
which were in their shell when Marcius stood for Rome,
and dared not once peep out.

SICINIUS:
Come, what are you talking about
of Marcius?

BRUTUS:
Go see this rumorer whipped. It cannot be
that the Volsces dare to break with us.

MENENIUS:
It cannot be!
We have records that it can very well,
and three examples of the like have happened
within my lifetime. But reason with the fellow,
before you punish him, where he heard this,
lest you should chance to whip your information
and beat the messenger who bids you beware
of what is to be dreaded.

SICINIUS:
Don't tell me.
I know this cannot be.

BRUTUS:
Not possible.

Messenger:
The nobles, in great earnestness, are going
all to the senate-house. Some news has come
that changes their expressions.

SICINIUS:
It's this slave—
go whip him, before the people's eyes—his raising of this rumor;
it's nothing but his report.

Messenger:
Yes, worthy sir,
the slave's report is seconded; and more,
more fearful, is delivered.

SICINIUS:
What more fearful?

Messenger:
It is spoken freely from many mouths—
how probable I do not know—that Marcius,
joined with Aufidius, is leading a force against Rome,
and vows a revenge as vast as the distance between
the youngest and oldest thing.

SICINIUS:
This is most likely!

BRUTUS:
It's only been started so that the weaker sort may wish
good Marcius home again.

SICINIUS:
That's the very trick of it.

MENENIUS:
This is unlikely.
He and Aufidius can no more be reconciled
than the most violent opposites.

Second Messenger:
You are sent for to the senate.
A fearful army, led by Caius Marcius
associated with Aufidius, is raging
upon our territories; and they have already
overrun their way, consumed with fire, and taken
what lay before them.

COMINIUS:
Oh, you have made good work!

MENENIUS:
What news? What news?

COMINIUS:
You have helped to rape your own daughters and
to melt the city's lead upon your heads,
to see your wives dishonored before your noses—

MENENIUS:
What's the news? What's the news?

COMINIUS:
Your temples burned in their cement, and
your franchises, on which you stood, confined
into an auger's hole.

MENENIUS:
Please now, your news?
You have made fair work, I fear me.—Please, your news?—
If Marcius should be joined with the Volscians,—

COMINIUS:
If!
He is their god. He leads them like a thing
made by some other deity than nature,
that shapes man better; and they follow him,
against us brats, with no less confidence
than boys pursuing summer butterflies,
or butchers killing flies.

MENENIUS:
You have done a good job,
you and your working men; you that stood so much
on the voice of your trade and
the breath of garlic-eaters!

COMINIUS:
He will shake
your Rome about your ears.

MENENIUS:
As Hercules
did shake down ripe fruit.
You have made a fine mess!

BRUTUS:
But is this true, sir?

COMINIUS:
Yes, and you'll look pale
before you find it otherwise. All the regions
are smilingly revolting; and those who resist
are mocked for their valiant ignorance,
and perish as constant fools. Who can blame him?
Your enemies and his find something in him.

MENENIUS:
We are all undone, unless
the noble man has mercy.

COMINIUS:
Who shall ask for it?
The tribunes cannot do it for shame; the people
deserve such pity from him as the wolf
does from the shepherds. For his best friends, if they
should say 'Be good to Rome,' they would be charging him even
as those who had deserved his hate,
and in that, they would appear like enemies.

MENENIUS:
It's true.
If he were putting the brand to my house
that would consume it, I would not have the face
to say, 'I beseech you, cease.' You have made a fine mess,
you and your crafts! You have crafted a fine mess!

COMINIUS:
You have brought
a trembling upon Rome, such as was never
so incapable of help.

Both Tribunes:
Don't say we brought it.

MENENIUS:
What! Was it we? We loved him, but, like beasts
and cowardly nobles, we gave way to your clusters,
who did hoot him out of the city.

COMINIUS:
But I fear
they'll roar him in again. Tullus Aufidius,
the second name among men, obeys his orders
as if he were his officer. Desperation
is all the policy, strength, and defense
that Rome can make against them.

MENENIUS:
Here come the clusters.
And is Aufidius with him? You are the ones
that made the air unwholesome, when you cast
your stinking, greasy caps in hooting at
Coriolanus' exile. Now he's coming;
and not a hair on a soldier's head
which will not prove a whip. As many fools
as you threw caps up, he will tumble down,
and pay you for your votes. It's no matter;
if he could burn us all into one coal,
we have deserved it.

Citizens:
Faith, we hear fearful news.

First Citizen:
For my own part,
when I said, "Banish him," I said, "It was a pity."

Second Citizen:
And so did I.

Third Citizen:
And so did I; and, to say the truth, so did very
many of us. What we did, we did for the best; and
though we willingly consented to his banishment, yet
it was against our will.

COMINIUS:
You are goodly things, you voices!

MENENIUS:
You have made
good work, you and your cry! Shall we go to the Capitol?

COMINIUS:
Oh, yes, what else?

SICINIUS:
Go, masters, get you home; don't be dismayed.
These are on a side that would be glad to have
this true which they so seem to fear. Go home,
and show no sign of fear.

First Citizen:
May the gods be good to us! Come, masters, let's go home.
I always said we were in the wrong when we banished
him.

Second Citizen:
So did we all. But, come, let's go home.

BRUTUS:
I do not like this news.

SICINIUS:
Neither do I.

BRUTUS:
Let's go to the Capitol. I wish half my wealth
would buy this for a lie!

SICINIUS:
Please, let us go.

AUFIDIUS:
Do they still flee to the Roman?

Lieutenant:
I do not know what witchcraft is in him, but
your soldiers treat him as the grace before a meal,
their talk at the table, and their thanks at the end;
and you are being overshadowed in this action, sir,
even by your own men.

AUFIDIUS:
I can't help it now,
unless, by using some means, I cripple the progress
of our design. He carries himself more proudly,
even towards me, than I thought he would
when I first embraced him. Yet his nature
in that is not a changeling; and I must excuse
what cannot be amended.

Lieutenant:
Yet I wish, sir,—
I mean for your own sake,—you had not
joined in commission with him; but either
you had carried out the action yourself, or else
you had left it solely to him.

AUFIDIUS:
I understand you well; and be you sure,
when he shall come to his accounting, he knows not
what I can urge against him. Although it seems,
and so he thinks, and is no less apparent
to the common eye, that he handles all things fairly,
and shows good management for the Volscian state,
fights like a dragon, and achieves victory as soon as
he draws his sword; yet he has left undone
that which shall break his neck or hazard mine,
whenever we come to our reckoning.

Lieutenant:
Sir, I beseech you, do you think he'll capture Rome?

AUFIDIUS:
All places yield to him before he sits down;
and the nobility of Rome are his.
The senators and patricians love him too.
The tribunes are no soldiers; and their people
will be as rash in the repeal, as hasty
to expel him from there. I think he'll be to Rome
as the osprey is to the fish, which takes it
by the sovereignty of nature. First he was
a noble servant to them; but he could not
carry his honors evenly. Whether it was pride,
which out of daily fortune ever taints
the happy man; whether a defect of judgment,
to fail in the disposing of those chances
of which he was the lord; or whether nature,
not to be other than one thing, not moving
from the helmet to the cushion, but commanding peace
even with the same austerity and garb
as he controlled the war; but one of these—
as he has spices of them all, not all,
for I dare so far free him—made him feared,
so hated, and so banished. But he has a merit,
to choke it in the utterance. So our virtues
lie in the interpretation of the time.
And power, to itself most commendable,
has no tomb so evident as a chair
to extol what it has done.
One fire drives out one fire; one nail, one nail;
rights by rights falter, strengths by strengths do fail.
Come, let's away. When, Caius, Rome is yours,
you are the poorest of all; then shortly you are mine.

MENENIUS:
No, I'll not go. You hear what he has said,
who was at one time his general, who loved him
in a most dear and particular way. He called me father,
but what of that? Go, you that banished him;
fall down a mile before his tent, and kneel
the way into his mercy. No, if he was coy
to hear Cominius speak, I'll stay at home.

COMINIUS:
He would not seem to know me.

MENENIUS:
Do you hear?

COMINIUS:
Yet one time he did call me by my name.
I urged our old acquaintance, and the drops
that we have bled together. Coriolanus
he would not answer to. He forbade all names;
he was a kind of nothing, titleless,
until he had forged himself a name in the fire
of burning Rome.

MENENIUS:
Why, so. You have made good work!
A pair of tribunes that have racked Rome
to make coals cheap—a noble memory!

COMINIUS:
I reminded him how royal it was to pardon
when it was least expected. He replied,
it was a bare petition of a state
to one whom they had punished.

MENENIUS:
Very well.
Could he say less?

COMINIUS:
I offered to awaken his regard
for his private friends. His answer to me was,
he could not stay to pick them out in a pile
of noisome, musty chaff. He said it was folly,
for one poor grain or two, to leave unburnt,
and still to smell the offense.

MENENIUS:
For one poor grain or two!
I am one of those; his mother, wife, his child,
and this brave fellow too, we are the grains.
You are the musty chaff; and you are smelled
above the moon. We must be burned for you.

SICINIUS:
No, please, be patient. If you refuse your aid
in this so much-needed help, yet do not
upbraid us with our distress. But, surely, if you
would be your country's pleader, your good tongue,
more than the instant army we can make,
might stop our countryman.

MENENIUS:
No, I'll not meddle.

SICINIUS:
Please, go to him.

MENENIUS:
What should I do?

BRUTUS:
Only make a trial of what your love can do
for Rome, towards Marcius.

MENENIUS:
Well, and say that Marcius
returns me, as Cominius is returned,
unheard; what then?
But as a discontented friend, grief-stricken
with his unkindness? Say it be so?

SICINIUS:
Yet your good will
must have thanks from Rome, after the measure
as you intended well.

MENENIUS:
I'll undertake it.
I think he'll hear me. Yet, to bite his lip
and hum at good Cominius, much disheartens me.
He was not taken well; he had not dined.
The veins unfilled, our blood is cold, and then
we pout upon the morning, are unapt
to give or to forgive. But when we have stuffed
these and these conveyances of our blood
with wine and food, we have suppler souls
than in our priest-like fasts. Therefore, I'll watch him
until he is in the right mood for my request,
and then I'll set upon him.

BRUTUS:
You know the very road into his kindness,
and cannot lose your way.

MENENIUS:
Good faith, I'll test him,
however it may turn out. I shall soon have knowledge
of my success.

COMINIUS:
He'll never hear him.

SICINIUS:
Not?

COMINIUS:
I tell you, he sits in gold, his eye
as red as if it would burn Rome; and his injury
is the jailer to his pity. I knelt before him;
it was very faintly he said, 'Rise'; he dismissed me
thus, with his speechless hand. What he would do,
he sent in writing after me; what he would not,
he bound with an oath to yield to his conditions.
So that all hope is in vain,
unless his noble mother and his wife,
who, as I hear, mean to solicit him
for mercy to his country. Therefore, let's go,
and with our fair entreaties, hurry them on.

First Senator:
Stay. Where are you from?

Second Senator:
Stand, and go back.

MENENIUS:
You guard like men; it's well. But, by your leave,
I am an officer of state, and I come
to speak with Coriolanus.

First Senator:
From where?

MENENIUS:
From Rome.

First Senator:
You may not pass, you must return. Our general
will no more hear from there.

Second Senator:
You'll see your Rome embraced with fire before
you'll speak with Coriolanus.

MENENIUS:
Good my friends,
if you have heard your general talk of Rome,
and of his friends there, it is a sure bet
my name has reached your ears. It is Menenius.

First Senator:
So be it; go back. The virtue of your name
is not passable here.

MENENIUS:
I tell you, fellow,
the general is my lover. I have been
the book of his good acts, from which men have read
his name unparalleled, perhaps amplified;
for I have ever verified my friends,
of whom he's chief, with all the size that truth
would suffer without lapsing. No, sometimes,
like a bowl on a subtle ground,
I have tumbled past the throw; and in his praise
have almost stamped the lie. Therefore, fellow,
I must have leave to pass.

First Senator:
Faith, sir, if you had told as many lies in his
behalf as you have uttered words in your own, you
should not pass here; no, though it were as virtuous
to lie as to live chastely. Therefore, go back.

MENENIUS:
Please, fellow, remember my name is Menenius,
always on the side of your general.

Second Senator:
However you have been his liar, as you say you
have, I am one that, telling the truth under him, must
say, you cannot pass. Therefore, go back.

MENENIUS:
Has he dined, can you tell? For I would not
speak with him until after dinner.

First Senator:
You are a Roman, are you?

MENENIUS:
I am, as your general is.

First Senator:
Then you should hate Rome, as he does. Can you,
when you have pushed out of your gates the very
defender of them, and, in a violent popular
ignorance, given your enemy your shield, think to
face his revenges with the easy groans of old
women, the virginal palms of your daughters, or with
the palsied intercession of such a decayed dotard as
you seem to be? Can you think to blow out the
intended fire your city is ready to flame in, with
such weak breath as this? No, you are deceived;
therefore, back to Rome, and prepare for your
execution. You are condemned, our general has sworn
you out of reprieve and pardon.

MENENIUS:
Sirrah, if your captain knew I were here, he would
treat me with respect.

Second Senator:
Come, my captain doesn't know you.

MENENIUS:
I mean, your general.

First Senator:
My general doesn't care for you. Back, I say, go; lest
I let out your half-pint of blood. Back—that's
the most you'll get. Back.

MENENIUS:
No, but, fellow, fellow,--

CORIOLANUS:
What's the matter?

MENENIUS:
Now, you companion, I'll say an errand for you.
You shall know now that I am in estimation; you shall
perceive that a jack-in-office cannot keep me from
my son Coriolanus. Guess, just by my entertainment
with him, if you are not in the state of
hanging, or of some death more long in
spectatorship, and crueler in suffering. Behold now
presently, and swoon for what's to come upon you.
The glorious gods sit in hourly assembly about your
particular prosperity, and love you no worse than
your old father Menenius does! Oh my son, my son!
You are preparing fire for us; look here, here's
water to quench it. I was hardly moved to come to
you; but being assured that no one but myself could move
you, I have been blown out of your gates with
sighs; and I conjure you to pardon Rome, and your
petitioning countrymen. May the good gods assuage your
wrath, and turn the dregs of it upon this scoundrel
here—this one, who, like a block, has denied my
access to you.

CORIOLANUS:
Away!

MENENIUS:
What! Away!

CORIOLANUS:
Wife, mother, child, I know not. My affairs
are in the service of others. Though I owe
my revenge to myself, my forgiveness lies
in Volscian breasts. That we have been familiar,
ungrateful forgetfulness shall poison, rather
than pity should note how much. Therefore, be gone.
My ears against your suits are stronger than
your gates against my force. Yet, because I loved you,
take this along; I wrote it for your sake
and would have torn it. Another word, Menenius,
I will not hear you speak. This man, Aufidius,
was my beloved in Rome. Yet you see!

AUFIDIUS:
You keep a constant temper.

First Senator:
Now, sir, is your name Menenius?

Second Senator:
It's a spell, you see, of much power. You know the
way home again.

First Senator:
Do you hear how we are scolded for keeping your
greatness back?

Second Senator:
What cause, do you think, I have to swoon?

MENENIUS:
I care for neither the world nor your general. For
such things as you, I can scarce think there are any,
you are so slight. He that has a will to die by
himself does not fear it from another. Let your general
do his worst. For you, be what you are, for a long time; and
may your misery increase with your age! I say to you,
as I was said to, 'Away!'

First Senator:
A noble fellow, I guarantee it.

Second Senator:
The worthy fellow is our general. He's the rock, the
oak not to be shaken by the wind.

CORIOLANUS:
We will, before the walls of Rome tomorrow,
set down our host. My partner in this action,
you must report to the Volscian lords, how plainly
I have handled this business.

AUFIDIUS:
You have respected only their ends;
you stopped your ears against
the general suit of Rome; you never admitted
a private whisper, no, not with such friends
that thought themselves sure of you.

CORIOLANUS:
This last old man,
whom with a cracked heart I have sent to Rome,
loved me above the measure of a father;
no, he deified me, indeed. Their last refuge
was to send him; for whose old love I have,
though I showed sourly to him, once more offered
the first conditions, which they did refuse
and cannot now accept; to grace him only
that thought he could do more, a very little
I have yielded to. Fresh embassies and suits,
neither from the state nor from private friends, hereafter
will I lend an ear to. Ha! What shout is this?
Shall I be tempted to break my vow
at the same time it is made? I will not.
My wife comes first; then the honored vessel
in which this body was framed, and in her hand
the grandchild of her blood. But, out, affection!
All bond and privilege of nature, break!
Let it be virtuous to be obstinate.
What is that curtsy worth? Or those doves' eyes,
which can make gods forsworn? I melt, and am not
of stronger earth than others. My mother bows,
as if Olympus to a molehill should
in supplication nod. And my young boy
has a look of intercession, which
great nature cries, 'Do not deny.' Let the Volsces
plow Rome and harrow Italy. I'll never
be such a gosling as to obey instinct, but stand,
as if a man were the author of himself
and knew no other kin.

VIRGILIA:
My lord and husband!

CORIOLANUS:
These eyes are not the same I wore in Rome.

VIRGILIA:
The sorrow that delivers us thus changed
makes you think so.

CORIOLANUS:
Like a dull actor now,
I have forgotten my part, and I am out,
even to a full disgrace. Best of my flesh,
forgive my tyranny; but do not say
for that 'Forgive our Romans.' Oh, a kiss
as long as my exile, as sweet as my revenge!
Now, by the jealous queen of heaven, that kiss
I carried from you, dear; and my true lip
has kept it virgin ever since. You gods! I prattle,
and leave the most noble mother of the world
unsaluted. Sink, my knee, in the earth;
show more impression of your deep duty
than that of common sons.

VOLUMNIA:
Oh, stand up blessed!
While, with no softer cushion than the flint,
I kneel before you, and improperly
show duty, as if I had been mistaken all this while
between the child and the parent.

CORIOLANUS:
What is this?
Your knees to me? To your corrected son?
Then let the pebbles on the hungry beach
flick the stars; then let the mutinous winds
strike the proud cedars against the fiery sun;
murdering impossibility, to make
what cannot be, a slight work.

VOLUMNIA:
You are my warrior;
I helped to frame you. Do you know this lady?

CORIOLANUS:
The noble sister of Publicola,
the moon of Rome, chaste as the icicle
that's curdled by the frost from purest snow
and hangs on Diana's temple. Dear Valeria!

VOLUMNIA:
This is a poor epitome of yours,
which, by the interpretation of full time,
may look like all of yourself.

CORIOLANUS:
May the god of soldiers,
with the consent of supreme Jove, inform
your thoughts with nobleness; that you may prove
invulnerable to shame, and stick in the wars
like a great sea-mark, withstanding every flaw,
and saving those that look at you!

VOLUMNIA:
Your knee, sirrah.

CORIOLANUS:
That's my brave boy!

VOLUMNIA:
Even he, your wife, this lady, and myself,
are suitors to you.

CORIOLANUS:
I beseech you, peace.
Or, if you would ask, remember this before:
the thing I have forsworn to grant may never
be held by your denials. Do not bid me
dismiss my soldiers, or capitulate
again with Rome's mechanics. Tell me not
wherein I seem unnatural. Do not desire
to ally my rages and revenges with
your colder reasons.

VOLUMNIA:
Oh, no more, no more!
You have said you will not grant us anything,
for we have nothing else to ask but that
which you deny already. Yet we will ask,
so that, if you fail in our request, the blame
may hang upon your hardness. Therefore, hear us.

CORIOLANUS:
Aufidius, and you Volsces, mark; for we'll
hear nothing from Rome in private. What is your request?

VOLUMNIA:
If we were to be silent and not speak, our clothing
and the state of our bodies would betray what life
we have led since your exile. Think with yourself
how more unfortunate than all living women
we have come here. Since the sight of you,
which should make our eyes flow with joy, and our hearts dance
with comfort,
constrains them to weep and shake with fear and sorrow,
making the mother, wife, and child see
the son, the husband, and the father tearing
his country's bowels out. And to us poor ones,
your enmity is most deadly. You bar us
from our prayers to the gods, which is a comfort
that all but we enjoy. For how can we,
alas, how can we pray for our country,
to which we are bound, together with your victory,
to which we are bound? Alas, either we must lose
the country, our dear nurse, or else your person,
our comfort in the country. We must find
an evident calamity, though we had
our wish, whichever side should win. For either you
must, as a foreign traitor, be led
with manacles through our streets, or else
triumphantly tread on your country's ruin,
and bear the palm for having bravely shed
your wife's and children's blood. For myself, son,
I do not intend to wait on fortune until
these wars are decided. If I cannot persuade you
rather to show a noble grace to both parts
than to seek the end of one, you shall no sooner
march to assault your country than to tread—
trust to it, you shall not—on your mother's womb,
that brought you into this world.

VIRGILIA:
Yes, and mine,
that brought you forth this boy, to keep your name
living through time.

Young MARCIUS:
He shall not tread on me;
I'll run away until I'm bigger, but then I'll fight.

CORIOLANUS:
To not be of a woman's tenderness,
requires neither a child nor a woman's face to see.
I have sat too long.

VOLUMNIA:
No, don't go from us like this.
If it were so that our request tended
to save the Romans, and thereby to destroy
the Volsces whom you serve, you might condemn us,
as poisoning your honor. No, our suit
is that you reconcile them. While the Volsces
may say, 'This mercy we have shown,' and the Romans,
'This we received,' and each on either side
give the all-hail to you and cry, 'Be blessed
for making up this peace!' You know, great son,
the end of war is uncertain, but this is certain,
that, if you conquer Rome, the benefit
which you will reap from it is such a name,
whose repetition will be followed with curses;
whose chronicle will be written thus: 'The man was noble,
but with his last attempt he wiped it out;
he destroyed his country, and his name remains
to the ensuing age abhorred.' Speak to me, son.
You have affected the fine strains of honor,
to imitate the graces of the gods;
to tear the wide cheeks of the air with thunder,
and yet to charge your sulfur with a bolt
that should but split an oak. Why don't you speak?
Do you think it is honorable for a noble man
to still remember wrongs? Daughter, you speak.
He doesn't care for your weeping. You speak, boy.
Perhaps your childishness will move him more
than our reasons can. There's no man in the world
more bound to his mother; yet here he lets me prattle
like one in the stocks. You have never in your life
shown your dear mother any courtesy,
when she, poor hen, fond of no second brood,
has clucked you to the wars and safely home,
laden with honor. Say my request is unjust,
and spurn me back. But if it is not so,
you are not honest; and the gods will plague you,
that you restrain from me the duty which
belongs to a mother's part. He turns away.
Down, ladies; let us shame him with our knees.
To his surname Coriolanus belongs more pride
than pity to our prayers. Down. An end;
this is the last. So we will go home to Rome,
and die among our neighbors. No, behold us.
This boy, that cannot tell what he would have
but kneels and holds up his hands for fellowship,
does reason our petition with more strength
than you have to deny it. Come, let us go.
This fellow had a Volscian for a mother;
his wife is in Corioli and his child
is like him by chance. Yet give us our dispatch.
I am hushed until our city is on fire,
and then I'll speak a little.

CORIOLANUS:
Oh mother, mother!
What have you done? Behold, the heavens do open,
the gods look down, and at this unnatural scene
they laugh. Oh my mother, mother! Oh!
You have won a happy victory for Rome;
but, for your son—believe it, oh, believe it—
most dangerously you have prevailed with him,
if not most mortally to him. But, let it come.
Aufidius, though I cannot make true wars,
I'll frame a convenient peace. Now, good Aufidius,
if you were in my place, would you have heard
a mother less? Or granted less, Aufidius?

AUFIDIUS:
I was moved as well.

CORIOLANUS:
I dare be sworn you were.
And, sir, it is no little thing to make
my eyes sweat with compassion. But, good sir,
what peace you'll make, advise me. For my part,
I'll not go to Rome, I'll go back with you; and I pray you,
stand by me in this cause. Oh mother! Wife!

AUFIDIUS:

CORIOLANUS:
Yes, by and by;
but we will drink together. And you shall bear
a better witness back than words, which we,
on like conditions, will have counter-sealed.
Come, enter with us. Ladies, you deserve
to have a temple built for you. All the swords
in Italy, and her confederate arms,
could not have made this peace.

MENENIUS:
Do you see that corner of the Capitol, that
corner-stone?

SICINIUS:
Why, what of that?

MENENIUS:
If it is possible for you to displace it with your
little finger, there is some hope the ladies of
Rome, especially his mother, may prevail with him.
But I say there is no hope in it. Our throats are
sentenced and are waiting for execution.

SICINIUS:
Is it possible that so short a time can alter the
condition of a man?

MENENIUS:
There is a difference between a grub and a butterfly;
yet your butterfly was a grub. This Marcius has grown
from a man to a dragon. He has wings; he's more than a
creeping thing.

SICINIUS:
He loved his mother dearly.

MENENIUS:
So he did me. And he no more remembers his mother
now than an eight-year-old horse. The tartness
of his face sours ripe grapes. When he walks, he
moves like an engine, and the ground shrinks before
his treading. He is able to pierce a breastplate with
his eye; he talks like a funeral bell, and his hum is a
battery. He sits in his state, like a thing made for
Alexander. What he bids be done is finished with
his bidding. He wants nothing of a god but eternity
and a heaven to be enthroned in.

SICINIUS:
Yes, mercy, if you report him truly.

MENENIUS:
I am painting him in character. Mark what mercy his
mother will bring from him. There is no more mercy
in him than there is milk in a male tiger. That
our poor city will find. And all this is because of
you.

SICINIUS:
May the gods be good to us!

MENENIUS:
No, in such a case the gods will not be good to
us. When we banished him, we did not respect them;
and, he returning to break our necks, they do not respect us.

Messenger:
Sir, if you want to save your life, flee to your house.
The commoners have got your fellow-tribune
and are dragging him up and down, all swearing that if
the Roman ladies do not bring comfort home,
they'll kill him by inches.

SICINIUS:
What's the news?

Second Messenger:
Good news, good news! The ladies have prevailed,
the Volscians have dislodged, and Marcius is gone.
A merrier day did never yet greet Rome,
no, not the expulsion of the Tarquins.

SICINIUS:
Friend,
are you certain this is true? Is it most certain?

Second Messenger:
As certain as I know the sun is fire.
Where have you been hiding, that you make doubt of it?
Never through an arch so hurried the blown tide,
as the comforted through the gates. Why, listen!
The trumpets, sackbuts, psalteries, and fifes,
tabors and cymbals and the shouting Romans,
make the sun dance. Listen!

MENENIUS:
This is good news.
I will go meet the ladies. This Volumnia
is worth more than consuls, senators, patricians,
a whole city full; of tribunes, such as you,
a sea and land full. You have prayed well today.
This morning, for ten thousand of your throats
I would not have given a penny. Listen, how they rejoice!

SICINIUS:
First, may the gods bless you for your news; next,
accept my thankfulness.

Second Messenger:
Sir, we all have
great cause to give great thanks.

SICINIUS:
Are they near the city?

Second Messenger:
Almost at the point of entering.

SICINIUS:
We will meet them,
and help the joy.

First Senator:
Behold our patroness, the life of Rome!
Call all your tribes together, praise the gods,
and make triumphant fires. Strew flowers before them.
Un-shout the noise that banished Marcius,
repeal him with the welcome of his mother.
Cry, 'Welcome, ladies, welcome!'

All:
Welcome, ladies, welcome!

AUFIDIUS:
Go tell the lords of the city I am here.
Deliver them this paper. Having read it,
bid them repair to the market place; where I,
even in their ears and in the commons' ears,
will vouch for the truth of it. The man I accuse
has by this entered the city ports and
intends to appear before the people, hoping
to purge himself with words. Dispatch.
Most welcome!

First Conspirator:
How is it with our general?

AUFIDIUS:
Even so
as with a man poisoned by his own alms,
and slain with his charity.

Second Conspirator:
Most noble sir,
if you still hold the same intention in which
you wished us to be parties, we'll deliver you
of your great danger.

AUFIDIUS:
Sir, I cannot tell.
We must proceed as we find the people.

Third Conspirator:
The people will remain uncertain while
there's a difference between you two. But the fall of either
makes the survivor the heir of all.

AUFIDIUS:
I know it;
and my pretext to strike at him admits
a good construction. I raised him up, and I pawned
my honor for his truth. He, being so heightened,
watered his new plants with the dews of flattery,
seducing my friends; and, to this end,
he bowed his nature, never known before
but to be rough, unswayable, and free.

Third Conspirator:
Sir, his stubbornness
when he ran for consul, which he lost
for lack of stooping—

AUFIDIUS:
That I would have spoken of.
Being banished for it, he came to my hearth,
presented his throat to my knife. I took him;
made him a joint-servant with me; gave him his way
in all his own desires; no, I let him choose
out of my files, to accomplish his projects,
my best and freshest men; served his designs
in my own person; helped to reap the fame
which he ended up taking all for himself; and took some pride
in doing myself this wrong. Until, at last,
I seemed his follower, not his partner, and
he paid me with his countenance, as if
I had been a mercenary.

First Conspirator:
So he did, my lord.
The army marveled at it, and, in the end,
when he had taken Rome and we looked
for no less spoil than glory—

AUFIDIUS:
There it was.
For which my sinews shall be stretched upon him.
At a few drops of women's tears, which are
as cheap as lies, he sold the blood and labor
of our great action. Therefore, he shall die,
and I'll renew myself in his fall. But, hark!

First Conspirator:
You entered your native town like a messenger,
and had no welcome home. But he returns,
splitting the air with noise.

Second Conspirator:
And patient fools,
whose children he has slain, their base throats tear
with giving him glory.

Third Conspirator:
Therefore, at your advantage,
before he expresses himself, or moves the people
with what he would say, let him feel your sword,
which we will second. When he lies along,
your telling of his tale shall bury
his reasons with his body.

AUFIDIUS:
Say no more.
Here come the lords.

All The Lords:
You are most welcome home.

AUFIDIUS:
I have not deserved it.
But, worthy lords, have you carefully read
what I have written to you?

Lords:
We have.

First Lord:
And we are grieved to hear it.
The faults he made before the last, I think,
might have found easy fines. But to end there
where he was to begin, and to give away
the benefit of our levies, answering us
with our own charge, making a treaty where
there was a yielding—this admits no excuse.

AUFIDIUS:
He approaches. You shall hear him.

CORIOLANUS:
Hail, lords! I have returned as your soldier,
no more infected with my country's love
than when I departed from here, but still existing
under your great command. You are to know
that I have successfully attempted and
with bloody passage led your wars even to
the gates of Rome. The spoils we have brought home
do more than counterbalance a full third part
of the charges of the action. We have made peace
with no less honor to the Antiates
than shame to the Romans. And we here deliver,
subscribed by the consuls and patricians,
together with the seal of the senate, what
we have agreed upon.

AUFIDIUS:
Do not read it, noble lords;
but tell the traitor, in the highest degree,
he has abused your powers.

CORIOLANUS:
Traitor! What's this!

AUFIDIUS:
Yes, traitor, Marcius!

CORIOLANUS:
Marcius!

AUFIDIUS:
Yes, Marcius, Caius Marcius. Do you think
I'll grace you with that robbery, your stolen name
Coriolanus in Corioli?
You lords and heads of the state, he has perfidiously
betrayed your business, and given up,
for certain drops of salt, your city Rome,
I say 'your city,' to his wife and mother;
breaking his oath and resolution like
a twist of rotten silk, never admitting
counsel of war, but at his nurse's tears
he whined and roared away your victory,
that pages blushed at him and men of heart
looked wondering at each other.

CORIOLANUS:
Do you hear this, Mars?

AUFIDIUS:
Do not name the god, you boy of tears!

CORIOLANUS:
Ha!

AUFIDIUS:
No more.

CORIOLANUS:
Immeasurable liar, you have made my heart
too big for what contains it. Boy! O slave!
Pardon me, lords, it's the first time that I ever
was forced to scold. Your judgments, my grave lords,
must give this cur the lie. And his own notion—
he who wears my stripes impressed upon him; that
must bear my beating to his grave—shall join
to thrust the lie upon him.

First Lord:
Peace, both, and hear me speak.

CORIOLANUS:
Cut me to pieces, Volscians; men and lads,
stain all your edges on me. Boy! False hound!
If you have written your annals true, it is there,
that, like an eagle in a dovecote, I
fluttered your Volscians in Corioli.
I did it alone. Boy!

AUFIDIUS:
Why, noble lords,
will you be reminded of his blind fortune,
which was your shame, by this unholy braggart,
before your own eyes and ears?

All Conspirators:
Let him die for it.

All The People:
'Tear him to pieces.' 'Do it right away.' 'He killed
my son.' 'My daughter.' 'He killed my cousin
Marcus.' 'He killed my father.'

Second Lord:
Peace, ho! No outrage! Peace!
The man is noble and his fame surrounds
this globe of the earth. His last offenses to us
shall have a judicious hearing. Stand, Aufidius,
and do not trouble the peace.

CORIOLANUS:
Oh, that I had him,
with six Aufidiuses, or more, his tribe,
to use my lawful sword!

AUFIDIUS:
Insolent villain!

All Conspirators:
Kill, kill, kill, kill, kill him!

Lords:
Hold, hold, hold, hold!

AUFIDIUS:
My noble masters, hear me speak.

First Lord:
O Tullus,—

Second Lord:
You have done a deed at which valor will weep.

Third Lord:
Don't tread on him. Masters all, be quiet;
put up your swords.

AUFIDIUS:
My lords, when you shall know—as in this rage,
provoked by him, you cannot—the great danger
which this man's life owed you, you'll rejoice
that he is thus cut off. Please it your honors
to call me to your senate, I'll deliver
myself as your loyal servant, or endure
your heaviest censure.

First Lord:
Bear his body from here,
and mourn for him. Let him be regarded
as the most noble corpse that ever a herald
did follow to his urn.

Second Lord:
His own impatience
takes from Aufidius a great part of the blame.
Let's make the best of it.

AUFIDIUS:
My rage is gone,
and I am struck with sorrow. Take him up.
Help, three of the chiefest soldiers; I'll be one.
Beat the drum, so that it speaks mournfully.
Trail your steel pikes. Though in this city he
has widowed and un-childed many a one,
which to this hour bewail the injury,
yet he shall have a noble memory. Assist.

GLOUCESTER:
Now is the winter of our discontent
made a glorious summer by this sun of York;
and all the clouds that lowered upon our house
are buried in the deep bosom of the ocean.
Now our brows are bound with victorious wreaths;
our bruised arms are hung up as monuments;
our stern alarms are changed to merry meetings,
our dreadful marches to delightful measures.
Grim-faced war has smoothed his wrinkled front;
and now, instead of mounting armored steeds
to frighten the souls of fearful adversaries,
he capers nimbly in a lady's chamber
to the lascivious pleasing of a lute.
But I, who am not shaped for sportive tricks,
nor made to court an amorous looking-glass;
I, who am rudely stamped, and lack love's majesty
to strut before a wanton, ambling nymph;
I, who am cheated of this fair proportion,
cheated of features by dissembling nature,
deformed, unfinished, sent before my time
into this breathing world, scarce half made up,
and so lamely and unfashionably
that dogs bark at me as I halt by them;
why, I, in this weak, piping time of peace,
have no delight to pass away the time,
unless to spy my shadow in the sun
and to comment on my own deformity.
And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover,
to entertain these fair, well-spoken days,
I am determined to prove a villain
and hate the idle pleasures of these days.
I have laid plots, dangerous inductions,
by drunken prophecies, libels, and dreams,
to set my brother Clarence and the king
in deadly hate against one another.
And if King Edward is as true and just
as I am subtle, false, and treacherous,
