this day Clarence should be closely locked up,
about a prophecy, which says that 'G'
of Edward's heirs will be the murderer.
Dive, thoughts, down to my soul. Here
Clarence comes.
Brother, good day. What does this armed guard mean
that waits upon your grace?

CLARENCE:
His majesty,
concerned for my person's safety, has appointed
this guard to convey me to the Tower.

GLOUCESTER:
For what reason?

CLARENCE:
Because my name is George.

GLOUCESTER:
Alas, my lord, that fault is none of yours;
he should, for that, commit your godfathers.
Oh, perhaps his majesty has some intent
that you shall be re-christened in the Tower.
But what's the matter, Clarence? May I know?

CLARENCE:
Yes, Richard, when I know; for I protest
as yet I do not. But, as I can learn,
he listens to prophecies and dreams;
and from the alphabet, he plucks the letter G,
and says a wizard told him that by G
his issue would be disinherited.
And, because my name of George begins with G,
it follows in his thought that I am he.
These, as I learn, and such like toys as these
have moved his highness to commit me now.

GLOUCESTER:
Why, this is what happens when men are ruled by women.
It's not the king that sends you to the Tower;
my Lady Grey, his wife, Clarence, it's she
that drives him to this extremity.
Was it not she and that good man of worship,
Anthony Woodville, her brother there,
that made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower,
from which this very day he is delivered?
We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.

CLARENCE:
By heaven, I think no man is secure
but the queen's kindred and the night-walking heralds
that trudge between the king and Mistress Shore.
Did you not hear what a humble supplicant
Lord Hastings was to her for his delivery?

GLOUCESTER:
Humbly complaining to her deity,
my lord chamberlain got his liberty.
I'll tell you what; I think it is our way,
if we will keep in favor with the king,
to be her men and wear her livery.
The jealous, worn-out widow and herself,
since our brother dubbed them gentlewomen,
are mighty gossips in this monarchy.

BRAKENBURY:
I beseech both your graces to pardon me;
his majesty has strictly given in charge
that no man shall have private conference,
of whatever degree, with his brother.

GLOUCESTER:
Even so; if it please your worship, Brakenbury,
you may partake of anything we say.
We speak no treason, man. We say the king
is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen
is well-advanced in years, fair, and not jealous;
we say that Shore's wife has a pretty foot,
a cherry lip, a bonny eye, a very pleasing tongue;
and that the queen's kindred are made gentle-folks.
What do you say, sir? Can you deny all this?

BRAKENBURY:
With this, my lord, I have nothing to do.

GLOUCESTER:
Nothing to do with Mistress Shore! I tell you, fellow,
he that does nothing with her, except one,
had best do it secretly, alone.

BRAKENBURY:
What one, my lord?

GLOUCESTER:
Her husband, knave. Would you betray me?

BRAKENBURY:
I beseech your grace to pardon me, and also
to forbear your conference with the noble duke.

CLARENCE:
We know your charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.

GLOUCESTER:
We are the queen's subjects, and must obey.
Brother, farewell. I will go to the king;
and whatever you will employ me in,
were it to call King Edward's widow 'sister,'
I will perform it to set you free.
In the meantime, this deep disgrace in our brotherhood
touches me deeper than you can imagine.

CLARENCE:
I know it pleases neither of us well.

GLOUCESTER:
Well, your imprisonment shall not be long.
In the meantime, have patience.

CLARENCE:
I must, perforce. Farewell.

GLOUCESTER:
Go, tread the path that you will never return from.
Simple, plain Clarence! I love you so much,
that I will shortly send your soul to heaven,
if heaven will take the present from our hands.
But who comes here? The newly-delivered Hastings?

HASTINGS:
Good time of day unto my gracious lord!

GLOUCESTER:
As much unto my good lord chamberlain!
You are well welcome to the open air.
How has your lordship endured imprisonment?

HASTINGS:
With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must.
But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks
that were the cause of my imprisonment.

GLOUCE-STER:
No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too;
for they that were your enemies are his,
and have prevailed as much on him as you.

HASTINGS:
It's a greater pity that the eagle should be caged,
while kites and buzzards prey at liberty.

GLOUCESTER:
What news is there abroad?

HASTINGS:
No news so bad abroad as this at home;
the King is sickly, weak, and melancholy,
and his physicians fear for him mightily.

GLOUCESTER:
Now, by Saint Paul, this news is bad indeed.
Oh, he has kept a bad diet for a long time,
and has over-consumed his royal person.
It is very grievous to think about.
What, is he in his bed?

HASTINGS:
He is.

GLOUCESTER:
Go you before, and I will follow you.
He cannot live, I hope; and he must not die
until George is packed with a post-horse up to heaven.
I'll go in, to urge his hatred more towards Clarence,
with lies well-steeled with weighty arguments;
and, if I do not fail in my deep intent,
Clarence has not another day to live.
Which done, may God take King Edward to his mercy,
and leave the world for me to bustle in!
For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter.
What though I killed her husband and her father?
The readiest way to make the girl amends
is to become her husband and her father,
which I will do; not so much for love
as for another secret, close intent,
which I must reach by marrying her.
But I am getting ahead of myself.
Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns.
When they are gone, then I must count my gains.

LADY ANNE:
Set down, set down your honorable load,
if honor may be shrouded in a hearse,
while I for a while dutifully lament
the untimely fall of virtuous Lancaster.
Poor key-cold figure of a holy king!
Pale ashes of the house of Lancaster!
You bloodless remnant of that royal blood!
Let it be lawful that I call upon your ghost,
to hear the lamentations of poor Anne,
wife to your Edward, to your slaughtered son,
stabbed by the very same hand that made these wounds!
Lo, in these windows that let out your life,
I pour the helpless balm of my poor eyes.
Cursed be the hand that made these fatal holes!
Cursed be the heart that had the heart to do it!
Cursed be the blood that let this blood from here!
May a more dire fate befall that hated wretch,
that makes us wretched by your death,
than I can wish to adders, spiders, toads,
or any creeping, venomous thing that lives!
If ever he has a child, may it be abortive,
prodigious, and untimely brought to light,
whose ugly and unnatural appearance
may frighten the hopeful mother at the sight;
and may that be the heir to his unhappiness!
If ever he has a wife, let her be made
as miserable by his death
as I am made by my poor lord and you!
Come, now towards Chertsey with your holy load,
taken from St. Paul's to be interred there;
and still, as you are weary of the weight,
rest you, while I lament King Henry's corpse.

GLOUCESTER:
Stay, you that bear the corpse, and set it down.

LADY ANNE:
What black magician conjures up this fiend,
to stop devoted, charitable deeds?

GLOUCESTER:
Villains, set down the corpse; or, by Saint Paul,
I'll make a corpse of him that disobeys.

Gentleman:
My lord, stand back, and let the coffin pass.

GLOUCESTER:
Unmannered dog! You stand when I command!
Advance your halberd higher than my breast,
or, by Saint Paul, I'll strike you to my foot,
and kick you, beggar, for your boldness.

LADY ANNE:
What, are you trembling? Are you all afraid?
Alas, I don't blame you; for you are mortal,
and mortal eyes cannot endure the devil.
Get away, you dreadful minister of hell!
You only had power over his mortal body,
his soul you cannot have; therefore, be gone.

GLOUCESTER:
Sweet saint, for charity, don't be so cursed.

LADY ANNE:
Foul devil, for God's sake, go away, and don't trouble us;
for you have made the happy earth your hell,
filled it with cursing cries and deep exclamations.
If you delight to view your heinous deeds,
behold this pattern of your butcheries.
Oh, gentlemen, see, see! Dead Henry's wounds
open their congealed mouths and bleed afresh!
Blush, blush, you lump of foul deformity;
for it is your presence that draws this blood
from cold and empty veins, where no blood dwells;
your deed, inhuman and unnatural,
provokes this most unnatural deluge.
O God, who made this blood, revenge his death!
O earth, who drinks this blood, revenge his death!
Either let heaven with lightning strike the
murderer dead,
or let the earth gape open wide and eat him quickly,
as you swallow up this good king's blood
which his hell-governed arm has butchered!

GLOUCESTER:
Lady, you know no rules of charity,
which gives good for bad, blessings for curses.

LADY ANNE:
Villain, you know no law of God or man.
No beast is so fierce that it doesn't know some touch of pity.

GLOUCESTER:
But I know none, and therefore I am no beast.

LADY ANNE:
Oh, wonderful, when devils tell the truth!

GLOUCESTER:
More wonderful, when angels are so angry.
Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,
of these supposed evils, to give me leave,
by circumstance, just to acquit myself.

LADY ANNE:
Vouchsafe, you foul infection of a man,
for these known evils, just to give me leave,
by circumstance, to curse your cursed self.

GLOUCESTER:
Fairer than a tongue can name you, let me have
some patient leisure to excuse myself.

LADY ANNE:
Fouler than a heart can think of you, you can make
no excuse valid, but to hang yourself.

GLOUCESTER:
By such despair, I would accuse myself.

LADY ANNE:
And, by despairing, you would be excused,
for doing worthy vengeance on yourself,
you who did unworthy slaughter on others.

GLOUCESTER:
Say that I didn't kill them?

LADY ANNE:
Why, then they are not dead.
But they are dead, you devilish slave, by you.

GLOUCESTER:
I did not kill your husband.

LADY ANNE:
Why, then he is alive.

GLOUCESTER:
No, he is dead, and slain by Edward's hand.

LADY ANNE:
You lie in your foul throat. Queen Margaret saw
your murderous sword smoking in his blood;
the which you once did bend against her breast,
but your brothers beat aside the point.

GLOUCESTER:
I was provoked by her slanderous tongue,
which laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

LADY ANNE:
You were provoked by your bloody mind,
which never dreamt of anything but butcheries.
Did you not kill this king?

GLOUCESTER:
I grant you that.

LADY ANNE:
Do you grant me that, hedgehog? Then, may God grant me too
that you may be damned for that wicked deed!
Oh, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous!

GLOUCESTER:
All the more fit for the King of heaven, who has him.

LADY ANNE:
He is in heaven, where you shall never come.

GLOUCESTER:
Let him thank me, for helping to send him there;
for he was more fit for that place than earth.

LADY ANNE:
And you are unfit for any place but hell.

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, one other place, if you will hear me name it.

LADY ANNE:
Some dungeon.

GLOUCESTER:
Your bed-chamber.

LADY ANNE:
May ill luck befall the chamber where you lie!

GLOUCESTER:
So it will, madam, until I lie with you.

LADY ANNE:
I hope so.

GLOUCESTER:
I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,
to leave this keen encounter of our wits,
and fall somewhat into a slower method,
is not the cause of the untimely deaths
of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,
as blameful as the executioner?

LADY ANNE:
You are the cause, and the most accursed effect.

GLOUCESTER:
Your beauty was the cause of that effect;
your beauty, which did haunt me in my sleep
to undertake the death of all the world,
so I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

LADY ANNE:
If I thought that, I tell you, homicide,
these nails would rend that beauty from my cheeks.

GLOUCESTER:
These eyes could never endure sweet beauty's destruction;
you would not blemish it, if I stood by.
As all the world is cheered by the sun,
so I am by that; it is my day, my life.

LADY ANNE:
May black night overshadow your day, and death your life!

GLOUCESTER:
Do not curse yourself, fair creature, you are both.

LADY ANNE:
I wish I were, to be revenged on you.

GLOUCESTER:
It is a most unnatural quarrel,
to be revenged on him that loves you.

LADY ANNE:
It is a just and reasonable quarrel,
to be revenged on him that killed my husband.

GLOUCESTER:
He that took your husband from you, lady,
did it to help you to a better husband.

LADY ANNE:
His better does not breathe upon the earth.

GLOUCESTER:
He lives that loves you better than he could.

LADY ANNE:
Name him.

GLOUCESTER:
Plantagenet.

LADY ANNE:
Why, that was he.

GLOUCESTER:
The same name, but of a better nature.

LADY ANNE:
Where is he?

GLOUCESTER:
Here.
Why do you spit at me?

LADY ANNE:
I wish it were mortal poison, for your sake!

GLOUCESTER:
Never came poison from so sweet a place.

LADY ANNE:
Never hung poison on a fouler toad.
Out of my sight! You infect my eyes.

GLOUCESTER:
Your eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

LADY ANNE:
I wish they were basilisks, to strike you dead!

GLOUCESTER:
I wish they were, that I might die at once;
for now they kill me with a living death.
Those eyes of yours have drawn salt tears from mine,
shamed their appearance with a store of childish drops.
These eyes that never shed a remorseful tear,
no, when my father York and Edward wept,
to hear the piteous moan that Rutland made
when black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;
nor when your warlike father, like a child,
told the sad story of my father's death,
and twenty times paused to sob and weep,
so that all the bystanders had wet their cheeks
like trees dashed with rain. In that sad time,
my manly eyes did scorn a humble tear;
and what these sorrows could not exhale from them,
your beauty has, and has made them blind with weeping.
I never sued to a friend nor an enemy;
my tongue could never learn a sweet, smoothing word.
But now your beauty is proposed as my fee,
my proud heart sues, and prompts my tongue to speak.
Do not teach your lips such scorn, for they were made
for kissing, lady, not for such contempt.
If your revengeful heart cannot forgive,
lo, here I lend you this sharp-pointed sword;
which, if you please to hide in this true bosom,
and let forth the soul that adores you,
I lay it naked to the deadly stroke,
and humbly beg for death upon my knee.
No, do not pause; for I did kill King Henry,
but it was your beauty that provoked me.
No, now dispatch; it was I that stabbed young Edward,
but it was your heavenly face that set me on.
Take up the sword again, or take up me.

LADY ANNE:
Arise, dissembler. Though I wish your death,
I will not be the executioner.

GLOUCESTER:
Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.

LADY ANNE:
I have already.

GLOUCESTER:
Tush, that was in your rage.
Speak it again, and, even with the word,
that hand, which, for your love, did kill your love,
shall, for your love, kill a far truer love.
You shall be an accessory to both their deaths.

LADY ANNE:
I wish I knew your heart.

GLOUCESTER:
It's figured in my tongue.

LADY ANNE:
I fear they are both false.

GLOUCESTER:
Then no man was ever true.

LADY ANNE:
Well, well, put up your sword.

GLOUCESTER:
Say, then, my peace is made.

LADY ANNE:
That you shall know hereafter.

GLOUCESTER:
But shall I live in hope?

LADY ANNE:
All men, I hope, live so.

GLOUCESTER:
Vouchsafe to wear this ring.

LADY ANNE:
To take is not to give.

GLOUCESTER:
Look, how this ring encompasses your finger.
Even so your breast encloses my poor heart;
wear both of them, for both of them are yours.
And if your poor, devoted supplicant may
but beg one favor at your gracious hand,
you will confirm his happiness forever.

LADY ANNE:
What is it?

GLOUCESTER:
That it would please you to leave these sad designs
to him that has more cause to be a mourner,
and presently go to Crosby Place;
where, after I have solemnly interred
this noble king at Chertsey monastery,
and wet his grave with my repentant tears,
I will with all convenient duty see you.
For various unknown reasons, I beseech you,
grant me this boon.

LADY ANNE:
With all my heart; and it joys me much too,
to see you have become so penitent.
Tressel and Berkeley, go along with me.

GLOUCESTER:
Bid me farewell.

LADY ANNE:
It's more than you deserve;
but since you teach me how to flatter you,
imagine I have said farewell already.

GLOUCESTER:
Sirs, take up the corpse.

GENTLEMEN:
Towards Chertsey, noble lord?

GLOUCESTER:
No, to White-Friars; there await my coming.
Was ever a woman wooed in this mood?
Was ever a woman won in this mood?
I'll have her, but I will not keep her long.
What! I, that killed her husband and his father,
to take her in her heart's extremest hate,
with curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes,
the bleeding witness of her hatred by;
having God, her conscience, and these bars
against me,
and I nothing to back my suit at all,
but the plain devil and dissembling looks,
and yet to win her, all the world to nothing!
Ha!
Has she forgotten already that brave prince,
Edward, her lord, whom I, some three months since,
stabbed in my angry mood at Tewkesbury?
A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman,
framed in the prodigality of nature,
young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, truly royal,
the spacious world cannot again afford.
And will she yet lower her eyes on me,
that cropped the golden prime of this sweet prince,
and made her a widow to a woeful bed?
On me, whose all not equals Edward's half?
On me, that halts and am misshapen thus?
My dukedom to a worthless coin,
I have been mistaken about my person all this while.
Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot,
myself to be a marvelously proper man.
I'll be at charges for a looking-glass,
and entertain some score or two of tailors,
to study fashions to adorn my body.
Since I have crept into favor with myself,
I will maintain it with some little cost.
But first I'll put that fellow in his grave;
and then return lamenting to my love.
Shine out, fair sun, until I have bought a glass,
that I may see my shadow as I pass.

RIVERS:
Have patience, madam. There's no doubt his majesty
will soon recover his accustomed health.

GREY:
In that you keep it in, it makes him worse.
Therefore, for God's sake, take good comfort,
and cheer his grace with quick and merry words.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
If he were dead, what would happen to me?

RIVERS:
No other harm but the loss of such a lord.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
The loss of such a lord includes all harm.

GREY:
The heavens have blessed you with a goodly son,
to be your comforter when he is gone.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, he is young, and his minority
is put into the trust of Richard Gloucester,
a man that loves neither me, nor any of you.

RIVERS:
Is it decided that he shall be protector?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
It is determined, but not concluded yet.
But so it must be, if the king dies.

GREY:
Here come the lords of Buckingham and Derby.

BUCKINGHAM:
Good time of day to your royal grace!

DERBY:
God make your majesty as joyful as you have been!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
The Countess Richmond, my good Lord of Derby,
will scarcely say amen to your good prayers.
Yet, Derby, notwithstanding that she's your wife,
and does not love me, be you, good lord, assured
I do not hate you for her proud arrogance.

DERBY:
I beseech you, either do not believe
the envious slanders of her false accusers,
or, if she is accused in a true report,
bear with her weakness, which, I think, proceeds
from a wayward sickness, and no grounded malice.

RIVERS:
Did you see the king today, my Lord of Derby?

DERBY:
Just now, the Duke of Buckingham and I
have come from visiting his majesty.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
What is the likelihood of his improvement, lords?

BUCKINGHAM:
Madam, good hope; his grace speaks cheerfully.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
God grant him health! Did you confer with him?

BUCKINGHAM:
Madam, we did. He desires to make peace
between the Duke of Gloucester and your brothers,
and between them and my lord chamberlain;
and he has sent to summon them to his royal presence.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I wish all were well! But that will never be.
I fear our happiness is at its peak.

GLOUCESTER:
They do me wrong, and I will not endure it.
Who are they that complain to the king,
that I, forsooth, am stern, and do not love them?
By holy Paul, they love his grace but lightly
that fill his ears with such rebellious rumors.
Because I cannot flatter and speak fair,
smile in men's faces, smooth, deceive, and cajole,
duck with French nods and apish courtesy,
I must be held a rancorous enemy.
Cannot a plain man live and think no harm,
but thus his simple truth must be abused
by silken, sly, insinuating Jacks?

RIVERS:
To whom in all this presence does your grace speak?

GLOUCESTER:
To you, who have neither honesty nor grace.
When have I injured you? When have I done you wrong?
Or you? Or you? Or any of your faction?
A plague upon you all! His royal person—
whom God preserve better than you would wish!—
cannot be quiet for scarcely a moment,
but you must trouble him with lewd complaints.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Brother of Gloucester, you misunderstand the matter.
The king, of his own royal disposition,
and not provoked by any other suitor,
aiming, perhaps, at your internal hatred,
which shows itself in your outward actions
against my kindred, brothers, and myself,
sends for you, so that he may gather
the ground of your ill-will, and so remove it.

GLOUCESTER:
I cannot tell. The world has grown so bad,
that wrens prey where eagles dare not perch.
Since every Jack became a gentleman,
there's many a gentle person made a Jack.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Come, come, we know your meaning, brother
Gloucester;
you envy my advancement and my friends'.
God grant we may never have need of you!

GLOUCESTER:
In the meantime, God grants that we have need of you.
Your brother is imprisoned by your means,
I am disgraced, and the nobility
is held in contempt; while many fair promotions
are daily given to ennoble those
that scarce, some two days since, were worth a noble.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
By Him that raised me to this careful height
from the contented happiness which I enjoyed,
I never did incite his majesty
against the Duke of Clarence, but have been
an earnest advocate to plead for him.
My lord, you do me a shameful injury,
falsely to draw me into these vile suspicions.

GLOUCESTER:
You may deny that you were not the cause
of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.

RIVERS:
She may, my lord, for—

GLOUCESTER:
She may, Lord Rivers! Why, who doesn't know that?
She may do more, sir, than just deny that.
She may help you to many fair promotions,
and then deny her aiding hand in it,
and lay those honors on your high deserts.
What may she not do? She may, yes, marry, she may—

RIVERS:
What, marry, may she?

GLOUCESTER:
What, marry, may she! Marry a king,
a bachelor, a handsome stripling too.
I know your grandmother had a worse match.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
My Lord of Gloucester, I have endured your
blunt upbraidings and your bitter scoffs for too long.
By heaven, I will acquaint his majesty
with those gross taunts I have often endured.
I would rather be a country servant-maid
than a great queen, with this condition,
to be thus taunted, scorned, and baited at.
I have small joy in being England's queen.

QUEEN MARGARET:
And may that small joy be lessened, God, I beseech you!
Your honor, state, and seat are due to me.

GLOUCESTER:
What! You threaten me with telling the king?
Tell him, and don't spare me. Look, what I have said
I will vouch for in the presence of the king.
I dare to risk being sent to the Tower.
It's time to speak; my pains are quite forgotten.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Out, devil! I remember them too well.
You killed my husband Henry in the Tower,
and Edward, my poor son, at Tewkesbury.

GLOUCESTER:
Before you were queen, yes, or your husband was king,
I was a pack-horse in his great affairs;
a weeder-out of his proud adversaries,
a liberal rewarder of his friends.
To make his blood royal, I spilt my own.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, and much better blood than his or yours.

GLOUCESTER:
In all which time you and your husband Grey
were factious for the house of Lancaster;
and, Rivers, so were you. Was not your husband
slain in Margaret's battle at Saint Alban's?
Let me remind you, if you forget,
what you have been before now, and what you are;
and also, what I have been, and what I am.

QUEEN MARGARET:
A murderous villain, and so you still are.

GLOUCESTER:
Poor Clarence forsook his father, Warwick;
yes, and forswore himself—which may Jesus pardon!—

QUEEN MARGARET:
Which God should revenge!

GLOUCESTER:
To fight on Edward's side for the crown;
and for his reward, poor lord, he is locked up.
I wish to God my heart were as hard as Edward's,
or Edward's as soft and pitiful as mine.
I am too childishly foolish for this world.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Go to hell for shame, and leave the world,
you demon! There is your kingdom.

RIVERS:
My Lord of Gloucester, in those busy days
which you bring up here to prove us enemies,
we then followed our lord, our lawful king.
So we would follow you, if you were to be our king.

GLOUCESTER:
If I should be! I would rather be a peddler.
Far be it from my heart, the thought of it!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
As little joy, my lord, as you suppose
you would enjoy, were you this country's king,
as little joy may you suppose I have,
being the queen of it.

QUEEN MARGARET:
A little joy the queen of it enjoys;
for I am she, and altogether joyless.
I can no longer hold myself patient.
Hear me, you wrangling pirates, that fall out
in sharing what you have stolen from me!
Which of you does not tremble when you look on me?
If not because I am a queen, and you bow like subjects,
then because I was deposed by you, and you quake like rebels?
Oh, gentle villain, do not turn away!

GLOUCESTER:
Foul, wrinkled witch, what are you doing in my sight?

QUEEN MARGARET:
But a repetition of what you have ruined;
that I will make before I let you go.

GLOUCESTER:
Were you not banished on pain of death?

QUEEN MARGARET:
I was; but I find more pain in banishment
than death can give me here by my abode.
A husband and a son you owe to me;
and you a kingdom; all of you owe allegiance.
The sorrow that I have, by right is yours,
and all the pleasures you usurp are mine.

GLOUCESTER:
The curse my noble father laid on you,
when you crowned his warlike brows with paper
and with your scorns drew rivers from his eyes,
and then, to dry them, gave the duke a cloth
steeped in the faultless blood of pretty Rutland—
his curses, then from the bitterness of his soul
denounced against you, have all fallen upon you;
and God, not we, has plagued your bloody deed.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
So just is God, to right the innocent.

HASTINGS:
Oh, it was the foulest deed to kill that baby,
and the most merciless that was ever heard of!

RIVERS:
Tyrants themselves wept when it was reported.

DORSET:
No man but prophesied revenge for it.

BUCKINGHAM:
Northumberland, who was present then, wept to see it.

QUEEN MARGARET:
What were you snarling about all before I came,
ready to catch each other by the throat,
and now you turn all your hatred on me?
Did York's dread curse prevail so much with heaven?
That Henry's death, my lovely Edward's death,
their kingdom's loss, my woeful banishment,
could all but answer for that peevish brat?
Can curses pierce the clouds and enter heaven?
Why, then, give way, dull clouds, to my quick curses!
If not by war, may your king die by surfeit,
as ours did by murder, to make him a king!
Edward, your son, who is now Prince of Wales,
for Edward my son, who was Prince of Wales,
may he die in his youth by like untimely violence!
You, a queen, for me that was a queen,
may you outlive your glory, like my wretched self!
Long may you live to wail your children's loss;
and see another, as I see you now,
decked in your rights, as you are stalled in mine!
Long may your happy days die before your death;
and, after many lengthened hours of grief,
die neither mother, wife, nor England's queen!
Rivers and Dorset, you were standers by,
and so were you, Lord Hastings, when my son
was stabbed with bloody daggers. God, I pray him,
that none of you may live your natural age,
but be cut off by some unexpected accident!

GLOUCESTER:
Have you done your charm, you hateful, withered hag!

QUEEN MARGARET:
And leave you out? Stay, dog, for you shall hear me.
If heaven has any grievous plague in store
exceeding those that I can wish upon you,
oh, let them keep it until your sins are ripe,
and then hurl down their indignation
on you, the troubler of the poor world's peace!
May the worm of conscience still gnaw your soul!
May your friends suspect you for traitors while you live,
and may you take deep traitors for your dearest friends!
May no sleep close that deadly eye of yours,
unless it be while some tormenting dream
affrights you with a hell of ugly devils!
You elvish-marked, abortive, rooting hog!
You that were sealed at your birth
the slave of nature and the son of hell!
You slander of your mother's heavy womb!
You loathed issue of your father's loins!
You rag of honor! You detested—

GLOUCESTER:
Margaret.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Richard!

GLOUCESTER:
Ha!

QUEEN MARGARET:
I'm not calling you.

GLOUCESTER:
Then I beg your pardon, for I had thought
that you had called me all these bitter names.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Why, so I did; but I looked for no reply.
Oh, let me make the end of my curse!

GLOUCESTER:
It's done by me, and it ends in 'Margaret.'

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Thus you have breathed your curse against yourself.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Poor painted queen, you vain flourish of my fortune!
Why do you strew sugar on that bottled spider,
whose deadly web is ensnaring you?
Fool, fool! You are sharpening a knife to kill yourself.
The time will come when you will wish for me
to help you curse that poisonous hunchbacked toad.

HASTINGS:
False-boding woman, end your frantic curse,
lest you move our patience to your harm.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Foul shame upon you! You have all moved mine.

RIVERS:
If you were well served, you would be taught your duty.

QUEEN MARGARET:
To serve me well, you should all do me duty,
teach me to be your queen, and you my subjects.
Oh, serve me well, and teach yourselves that duty!

DORSET:
Don't argue with her; she is a lunatic.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Peace, master marquess, you are impudent.
Your brand-new stamp of honor is barely current.
Oh, that your young nobility could judge
what it was to lose it, and be miserable!
They that stand high have many blasts to shake them;
and if they fall, they dash themselves to pieces.

GLOUCESTER:
Good counsel, indeed. Learn it, learn it, marquess.

DORSET:
It touches you, my lord, as much as me.

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, and much more. But I was born so high,
our nest is built in the cedar's top,
and we dally with the wind and scorn the sun.

QUEEN MARGARET:
And turns the sun to shade; alas! alas!
Witness my son, now in the shade of death;
whose bright, out-shining beams your cloudy wrath
has folded up in eternal darkness.
Your nest is built in our nest.
O God, that sees it, do not suffer it!
As it was won with blood, let it be lost so!

BUCKINGHAM:
Have done! For shame, if not for charity.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Urge neither charity nor shame to me.
You have dealt uncharitably with me,
and shamefully by you my hopes are butchered.
My charity is outrage, my life is my shame,
and in that shame still live my sorrow's rage.

BUCKINGHAM:
Have done, have done.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Oh, princely Buckingham, I'll kiss your hand,
in sign of league and friendship with you.
Now may good fortune befall you and your noble house!
Your garments are not spotted with our blood,
nor are you within the compass of my curse.

BUCKINGHAM:
Nor is anyone here; for curses never pass
the lips of those that breathe them in the air.

QUEEN MARGARET:
I will not believe but that they ascend to the sky,
and there awaken God's gentle, sleeping peace.
Oh Buckingham, take heed of that dog over there!
Look, when he fawns, he bites; and when he bites,
his venomous tooth will rankle to the death.
Have nothing to do with him, beware of him;
sin, death, and hell have set their marks on him,
and all their ministers attend on him.

GLOUCESTER:
What does she say, my Lord of Buckingham?

BUCKINGHAM:
Nothing that I respect, my gracious lord.

QUEEN MARGARET:
What, do you scorn me for my gentle counsel?
And soothe the devil that I warn you from?
Oh, but remember this another day,
when he shall split your very heart with sorrow,
and say poor Margaret was a prophetess!
May each of you live as subjects to his hate,
and he to yours, and all of you to God's!

HASTINGS:
My hair stands on end to hear her curses.

RIVERS:
And so does mine. I wonder why she's at liberty.

GLOUCESTER:
I cannot blame her. By God's holy mother,
she has had too much wrong; and I repent
my part thereof that I have done to her.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I never did her any wrong, to my knowledge.

GLOUCESTER:
But you have all the advantage of her wrong.
I was too hot to do somebody good,
that is too cold in thinking of it now.
As for Clarence, he is well repaid,
he is cooped up to be fattened for his pains.
God pardon them that are the cause of it!

RIVERS:
A virtuous and a Christian-like conclusion,
to pray for them that have done harm to us.

GLOUCESTER:
So I always do,
being well-advised.
For if I had cursed now, I would have cursed myself.

CATESBY:
Madam, his majesty is calling for you,
and for your grace; and you, my noble lords.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Catesby, we are coming. Lords, will you go with us?

RIVERS:
Madam, we will attend your grace.

GLOUCESTER:
I do the wrong, and I'm the first to start a fight.
The secret mischiefs that I set in motion,
I lay on the grievous charge of others.
Clarence, whom I have indeed put in darkness,
I weep for to many simple gulls,
namely, to Hastings, Derby, and Buckingham;
and I say it is the queen and her allies
that stir the king against my brother, the duke.
Now, they believe it; and they also urge me
to be revenged on Rivers, Vaughan, and Grey.
But then I sigh; and, with a piece of scripture,
I tell them that God bids us do good for evil.
And thus I clothe my naked villainy
with old odd ends stolen out of holy writ,
and seem a saint, when I most play the devil.
But, wait! Here come my executioners.
How now, my hardy, stout, resolved mates!
Are you now going to dispatch this deed?

First Murderer:
We are, my lord; and we have come to get the warrant
so that we may be admitted where he is.

GLOUCESTER:
Well thought of; I have it here with me.
When you have done, go to Crosby Place.
But, sirs, be sudden in the execution,
and also obdurate. Do not hear him plead;
for Clarence is well-spoken, and perhaps
he may move your hearts to pity if you listen to him.

First Murderer:
Tush!
Fear not, my lord, we will not stand to talk;
talkers are no good doers. Be assured
we come to use our hands and not our tongues.

GLOUCESTER:
Your eyes drop millstones, when fools' eyes drop tears.
I like you, lads. About your business straight away.
Go, go, dispatch.

First Murderer:
We will, my noble lord.

BRAKENBURY:
Why does your grace look so sad today?

CLARENCE:
Oh, I have had a miserable night,
so full of ugly sights, of ghastly dreams,
that, as I am a Christian faithful man,
I would not spend another such a night,
though it were to buy a world of happy days,
so full of dismal terror was the time!

BRAKENBURY:
What was your dream? I long to hear you tell it.

CLARENCE:
I thought that I had broken out of the Tower,
and was on a ship to cross to Burgundy;
and, in my company, was my brother Gloucester,
who, from my cabin, tempted me to walk
upon the hatches. From there we looked toward England,
and recalled a thousand fearful times,
during the wars of York and Lancaster,
that had befallen us. As we paced along
upon the giddy footing of the hatches,
I thought that Gloucester stumbled; and, in falling,
he struck me, who thought to steady him, overboard,
into the tumbling billows of the main.
Lord, Lord! I thought, what pain it was to drown!
What dreadful noise of waters in my ears!
What ugly sights of death within my eyes!
I thought I saw a thousand fearful wrecks;
ten thousand men that fishes had gnawed upon;
wedges of gold, great anchors, heaps of pearl,
inestimable stones, unvalued jewels,
all scattered on the bottom of the sea.
Some lay in dead men's skulls; and, in those holes
where eyes did once inhabit, there were crept,
as if in scorn of eyes, reflecting gems,
which wooed the slimy bottom of the deep,
and mocked the dead bones that lay scattered by.

BRAKENBURY:
Did you have such leisure in the time of death
to gaze upon the secrets of the deep?

CLARENCE:
I thought I had; and often did I strive
to give up the ghost, but still the envious flood
kept my soul in, and would not let it out
to seek the empty, vast, and wandering air,
but smothered it within my panting body,
which almost burst to belch it into the sea.

BRAKENBURY:
Did you not awake with this sore agony?

CLARENCE:
Oh, no, my dream was lengthened after life;
oh, then the tempest began in my soul,
which passed, I thought, the melancholy flood,
with that grim ferryman that poets write of,
to the kingdom of perpetual night.
The first that greeted my stranger soul there,
was my great father-in-law, the renowned Warwick;
who cried aloud, 'What punishment for perjury
can this dark monarchy afford false Clarence?'
And so he vanished. Then came wandering by
a shadow like an angel, with bright hair
dabbled in blood; and he shrieked out aloud,
'Clarence has come; false, fleeting, perjured Clarence,
that stabbed me in the field by Tewkesbury;
seize on him, Furies, take him to your torments!'
With that, I thought, a legion of foul fiends
surrounded me, and howled in my ears
such hideous cries, that with the very noise
I trembling awoke, and for a season after
could not believe but that I was in hell,
such a terrible impression the dream made.

BRAKENBURY:
No marvel, my lord, though it frightened you;
I promise, I am afraid to hear you tell it.

CLARENCE:
Oh, Brakenbury, I have done those things,
which now bear evidence against my soul,
for Edward's sake; and see how he repays me!
O God! If my deep prayers cannot appease you,
but you will be avenged on my misdeeds,
yet execute your wrath on me alone,
O, spare my guiltless wife and my poor children!
I pray you, gentle keeper, stay by me;
my soul is heavy, and I would fain sleep.

BRAKENBURY:
I will, my lord. God give your grace good rest!
Sorrow breaks seasons and resting hours,
makes the night morning, and the noontide night.
Princes have but their titles for their glories,
an outward honor for an inward toil;
and, for unfelt imagination,
they often feel a world of restless cares.
So that, between their titles and low names,
there's nothing different but the outward fame.

First Murderer:
Ho! Who's here?

BRAKENBURY:
In God's name, what are you, and how did you come here?

First Murderer:
I would like to speak with Clarence, and I came here on my legs.

BRAKENBURY:
Yes, are you so brief?

Second Murderer:
Oh sir, it is better to be brief than tedious. Show
him our commission; talk no more.

BRAKENBURY:
I am, in this, commanded to deliver
the noble Duke of Clarence to your hands.
I will not reason what is meant by this,
because I will be guiltless of the meaning.
Here are the keys, there sits the duke asleep.
I'll go to the king and signify to him
that I have thus resigned my charge to you.

First Murderer:
Do so, it is a point of wisdom. Fare you well.

Second Murderer:
What, shall we stab him as he sleeps?

First Murderer:
No; then he will say it was done cowardly, when he wakes.

Second Murderer:
When he wakes! Why, fool, he will never wake until
Judgment Day.

First Murderer:
Why, then he will say we stabbed him while he was sleeping.

Second Murderer:
The thought of that word 'judgment' has bred a kind
of remorse in me.

First Murderer:
What, are you afraid?

Second Murderer:
Not to kill him, having a warrant for it; but to be
damned for killing him, from which no warrant can defend us.

First Murderer:
I thought you were resolute.

Second Murderer:
So I am, to let him live.

First Murderer:
Go back to the Duke of Gloucester and tell him so.

Second Murderer:
I pray you, stay a while. I hope my holy mood
will change; it was wont to hold me but while one
would count to twenty.

First Murderer:
How do you feel now?

Second Murderer:
In faith, some certain dregs of conscience are still
within me.

First Murderer:
Remember our reward, when the deed is done.

Second Murderer:
By God, he dies! I had forgotten the reward.

First Murderer:
Where is your conscience now?

Second Murderer:
In the Duke of Gloucester's purse.

First Murderer:
So when he opens his purse to give us our reward,
your conscience flies out.

Second Murderer:
Let it go; there are few or none who will entertain it.

First Murderer:
What if it comes to you again?

Second Murderer:
I'll not meddle with it. It is a dangerous thing. It
makes a man a coward. A man cannot steal, but it
accuses him; he cannot swear, but it checks him;
he cannot lie with his neighbor's wife, but it
detects him. It's a blushing, shameful spirit that
mutinies in a man's bosom; it fills one full of
obstacles. It once made me restore a purse of gold
that I found; it beggars any man that keeps it. It
is turned out of all towns and cities for a
dangerous thing; and every man that means to live
well endeavors to trust to himself and to live
without it.

First Murderer:
By God's wounds, it is even now at my elbow, persuading me
not to kill the duke.

Second Murderer:
Take the devil in your mind, and do not relieve him. He
would insinuate with you but to make you sigh.

First Murderer:
Tut, I am strong-framed, he cannot prevail with me,
I guarantee you.

Second Murderer:
Spoken like a brave fellow that respects his
reputation. Come, shall we get to this business?

First Murderer:
Hit him over the head with the hilts of your
sword, and then we will chop him in the malmsey-butt
in the next room.

Second Murderer:
Oh, excellent device! Make a sop of him.

First Murderer:
Hark! He's stirring. Shall I strike?

Second Murderer:
No, first let's reason with him.

CLARENCE:
Where are you, keeper? Give me a cup of wine.

Second murderer:
You shall have wine enough, my lord, soon.

CLARENCE:
In God's name, what are you?

Second Murderer:
A man, as you are.

CLARENCE:
But not, as I am, royal.

Second Murderer:
Nor you, as we are, loyal.

CLARENCE:
Your voice is like thunder, but your looks are humble.

Second Murderer:
My voice is now the king's, my looks are my own.

CLARENCE:
How darkly and how deadly you speak!
Your eyes menace me. Why do you look pale?
Who sent you here? Why have you come?

Both:
To, to, to—

CLARENCE:
To murder me?

Both:
Yes, yes.

CLARENCE:
You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,
and therefore you cannot have the hearts to do it.
In what way, my friends, have I offended you?

First Murderer:
You have not offended us, but the king.

CLARENCE:
I shall be reconciled with him again.

Second Murderer:
Never, my lord; therefore prepare to die.

CLARENCE:
Are you called forth from a world of men
to kill the innocent? What is my offense?
Where is the evidence that accuses me?
What lawful inquest has given their verdict up
to the frowning judge? Or who pronounced
the bitter sentence of poor Clarence's death?
Before I am convicted by the course of law,
to threaten me with death is most unlawful.
I charge you, as you hope to have redemption
by Christ's dear blood shed for our grievous sins,
that you depart and lay no hands on me.
The deed you undertake is damnable.

First Murderer:
What we will do, we do upon command.

Second Murderer:
And he that has commanded is the king.

CLARENCE:
Erroneous vassal! The great King of kings
has, in the tables of his law, commanded
that you shall do no murder. And will you, then,
spurn at his edict and fulfill a man's?
Take heed; for he holds vengeance in his hands,
to hurl upon the heads of those that break his law.

Second Murderer:
And that same vengeance he hurls on you,
for false forswearing and for murder too.
You received the holy sacrament,
to fight in the quarrel of the house of Lancaster.

First Murderer:
And, like a traitor to the name of God,
you broke that vow; and with your treacherous blade
you ripped open the bowels of your sovereign's son.

Second Murderer:
Whom you were sworn to cherish and defend.

First Murderer:
How can you urge God's dreadful law to us,
when you have broken it in so great a degree?

CLARENCE:
Alas! For whose sake did I do that evil deed?
For Edward, for my brother, for his sake. Why, sirs,
he doesn't send you to murder me for this,
for in this sin he is as deep as I.
If God will be revenged for this deed,
oh, you know yet, he does it publicly,
don't take the quarrel from his powerful arm;
he needs no indirect nor lawless course
to cut off those that have offended him.

First Murderer:
Who made you, then, a bloody minister,
when gallant-springing brave Plantagenet,
that princely novice, was struck dead by you?

CLARENCE:
My brother's love, the devil, and my rage.

First Murderer:
Your brother's love, our duty, and your fault,
provoke us here now to slaughter you.

CLARENCE:
Oh, if you love my brother, don't hate me;
I am his brother, and I love him well.
If you are hired for a reward, go back again,
and I will send you to my brother Gloucester,
who will reward you better for my life
than Edward will for the news of my death.

Second Murderer:
You are deceived, your brother Gloucester hates you.

CLARENCE:
Oh, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear.
Go to him from me.

Both:
Yes, so we will.

CLARENCE:
Tell him, when our princely father York
blessed his three sons with his victorious arm,
and charged us from his soul to love each other,
he little thought of this divided friendship.
Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

First Murderer:
Yes, millstones; as he taught us to weep.

CLARENCE:
Oh, do not slander him, for he is kind.

First Murderer:
Right,
as snow in harvest. You are deceiving yourself.
It is he that sent us here now to slaughter you.

CLARENCE:
It cannot be; for when I parted with him,
he hugged me in his arms, and swore, with sobs,
that he would work for my delivery.

Second Murderer:
Why, so he does, now he delivers you
from this world's thraldom to the joys of heaven.

First Murderer:
Make peace with God, for you must die, my lord.

CLARENCE:
Do you have that holy feeling in your soul,
to counsel me to make my peace with God,
and yet are you so blind to your own soul,
that you will war with God by murdering me?
Ah, sirs, consider, he that set you on
to do this deed will hate you for the deed.

Second Murderer:
What shall we do?

CLARENCE:
Relent, and save your souls.

First Murderer:
Relent! It's cowardly and womanish.

CLARENCE:
Not to relent is beastly, savage, devilish.
Which of you, if you were a prince's son,
being confined from liberty, as I am now,
if two such murderers as yourselves came to you,
would not entreat for life?
My friend, I spy some pity in your looks.
Oh, if your eye is not a flatterer,
come to my side, and entreat for me,
as you would beg, if you were in my distress.
A begging prince, what beggar does not pity?

Second Murderer:
Look behind you, my lord.

First Murderer:
Take that, and that. If all this won't do,
I'll drown you in the malmsey-butt within.

Second Murderer:
A bloody deed, and desperately dispatched!
How gladly, like Pilate, I would wash my hands
of this most grievous, guilty murder.

First Murderer:
What's this! What do you mean by not helping me?
By heavens, the duke will know how slack you are!

Second Murderer:
I wish he knew that I had saved his brother!
You take the fee, and tell him what I say,
for I repent that the duke is slain.

First Murderer:
I don't. Go, you coward.
Now I must hide his body in some hole,
until the duke gives orders for his burial.
And when I have my reward, I must go away;
for this will come out, and I must not stay here.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, so. Now I have done a good day's work.
You peers, continue this united league.
I expect an embassy every day
from my Redeemer to redeem me from here;
and now in peace my soul shall part to heaven,
since I have set my friends at peace on earth.
Rivers and Hastings, take each other's hand;
do not dissemble your hatred, swear your love.

RIVERS:
By heaven, my heart is purged from grudging hate,
and with my hand I seal my true heart's love.

HASTINGS:
So may I thrive, as I truly swear the like!

KING EDWARD IV:
Take heed you don't trifle before your king;
lest he that is the supreme King of kings
confound your hidden falsehood, and award
either of you to be the other's end.

HASTINGS:
So may I prosper, as I swear perfect love!

RIVERS:
And I, as I love Hastings with my heart!

KING EDWARD IV:
Madam, you are not exempt in this,
nor your son Dorset, Buckingham, nor you;
you have been factious against one another.
Wife, love Lord Hastings, let him kiss your hand;
and what you do, do it sincerely.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Here, Hastings; I will never more remember
our former hatred, so may I and mine thrive!

KING EDWARD IV:
Dorset, embrace him; Hastings, love lord marquess.

DORSET:
This interchange of love, I here protest,
on my part shall be inviolable.

HASTINGS:
And so I swear, my lord.

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, princely Buckingham, you seal this league
with your embraces to my wife's allies,
and make me happy in your unity.

BUCKINGHAM:
Whenever Buckingham turns his hate
on you or yours, but with all dutiful love
cherishes you and yours, may God punish me
with hate in those where I expect the most love!
When I have the most need to employ a friend,
and am most assured that he is a friend,
may he be deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile
to me! This I beg of God,
when I am cold in zeal to you and yours.

KING EDWARD IV:
A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,
is this your vow to my sickly heart.
Now our brother Gloucester is missing here,
to make the perfect end of this peace.

BUCKINGHAM:
And, in good time, here comes the noble duke.

GLOUCESTER:
Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen.
And, princely peers, a happy time of day!

KING EDWARD IV:
Happy, indeed, as we have spent the day.
Brother, we have done deeds of charity;
we have made peace out of enmity, fair love out of hate,
between these swelling, wrongly-incensed peers.

GLOUCESTER:
A blessed labor, my most sovereign liege.
Among this princely heap, if any here,
by false intelligence, or wrong surmise,
holds me a foe;
if I, unwittingly, or in my rage,
have committed anything that is hard to bear
by any in this presence, I desire
to reconcile myself to his friendly peace.
It is death to me to be at enmity;
I hate it, and I desire all good men's love.
First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,
which I will purchase with my dutiful service;
of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,
if ever any grudge was lodged between us;
of you, Lord Rivers, and, Lord Grey, of you,
that without desert have frowned on me;
dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all.
I do not know that Englishman alive
with whom my soul is at any odds
more than the infant that is born tonight.
I thank my God for my humility.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
A holy day shall this be kept hereafter.
I wish to God all strifes were well compounded.
My sovereign liege, I do beseech your majesty
to take our brother Clarence into your grace.

GLOUCESTER:
Why, madam, have I offered love for this,
to be so flouted in this royal presence?
Who doesn't know that the noble duke is dead?
You do him an injury to scorn his corpse.

RIVERS:
Who doesn't know he is dead! Who knows he is?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
All-seeing heaven, what a world is this!

BUCKINGHAM:
Do I look as pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?

DORSET:
Yes, my good lord; and no one in this presence
but his red color has forsaken his cheeks.

KING EDWARD IV:
Is Clarence dead? The order was reversed.

GLOUCESTER:
But he, poor soul, died by your first order,
and that a winged Mercury did bear.
Some tardy cripple bore the countermand,
that came too late to see him buried.
God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,
nearer in bloody thoughts, but not in blood,
deserve no worse than wretched Clarence did,
and yet go free from suspicion!

DORSET:
A boon, my sovereign, for my service done!

KING EDWARD IV:
I pray you, peace. My soul is full of sorrow.

DORSET:
I will not rise, unless your highness grants.

KING EDWARD IV:
Then speak at once what it is you demand.

DORSET:
The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant's life,
who slew a righteous gentleman today,
who was lately an attendant on the Duke of Norfolk.

KING EDWARD IV:
I have a tongue to doom my brother's death,
and shall the same give pardon to a slave?
My brother killed no man; his fault was a thought,
and yet his punishment was a cruel death.
Who sued to me for him? Who, in my rage,
knelt at my feet and asked me to be advised?
Who spoke of brotherhood? Who spoke of love?
Who told me how the poor soul forsook
the mighty Warwick, and fought for me?
Who told me, in the field by Tewkesbury,
when Oxford had me down, he rescued me,
and said, 'Dear brother, live, and be a king'?
Who told me, when we both lay in the field
frozen almost to death, how he wrapped me
even in his own garments, and gave himself,
all thin and naked, to the numb, cold night?
All this from my remembrance, brutish wrath
sinfully plucked, and not a man of you
had so much grace as to put it in my mind.
But when your carters or your waiting-vassals
have done a drunken slaughter, and defaced
the precious image of our dear Redeemer,
you are straight on your knees for pardon, pardon;
and I, unjustly too, must grant it to you.
But for my brother, not a man would speak,
nor I, ungracious, speak to myself
for him, poor soul. The proudest of you all
have been beholden to him in his life;
yet none of you would once plead for his life.
O God, I fear your justice will take hold
on me, and you, and mine, and yours for this!
Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.
Oh, poor Clarence!

GLOUCESTER:
This is the fruit of rashness! Didn't you notice
how the guilty kindred of the queen
looked pale when they heard of Clarence's death?
Oh, they kept urging it on the king!
God will avenge it. But come, let's go in,
to comfort Edward with our company.

BUCKINGHAM:
We wait upon your grace.

Boy:
Tell me, good grandam, is our father dead?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
No, boy.

Boy:
Why do you wring your hands, and beat your breast,
and cry, 'O Clarence, my unhappy son!'

Girl:
Why do you look at us, and shake your head,
and call us wretches, orphans, castaways,
if our noble father is alive?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
My pretty cousins, you mistake me much;
I am lamenting the sickness of the king,
as loath to lose him, not your father's death.
It would be lost sorrow to wail for one that's lost.

Boy:
Then, grandam, you conclude that he is dead.
The king my uncle is to blame for this.
God will avenge it, whom I will implore
with daily prayers all to that effect.

Girl:
And so will I.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Peace, children, peace! The king loves you well.
Innocent and shallow, you cannot guess who caused your father's death.

Boy:
Grandam, we can; for my good uncle Gloucester
told me the king, provoked by the queen,
devised impeachments to imprison him.
And when my uncle told me so, he wept,
and hugged me in his arm, and kindly kissed my cheek;
he told me to rely on him as on my father,
and he would love me dearly as his child.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Oh, that deceit should steal such gentle shapes,
and with a virtuous mask hide foul guile!
He is my son; yes, and in that my shame;
yet from my breasts he did not draw this deceit.

Boy:
Do you think my uncle was pretending, grandam?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Yes, boy.

Boy:
I cannot think so. Hark! What noise is this?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, who shall stop me from wailing and weeping,
from chiding my fortune, and tormenting myself?
I'll join with black despair against my soul,
and become an enemy to myself.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What does this scene of rude impatience mean?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
To make an act of tragic violence:
Edward, my lord, your son, our king, is dead.
Why do the branches grow now that the root is withered?
Why do the leaves not wither now that the sap is gone?
If you will live, lament; if you will die, be brief,
so that our swift-winged souls may catch the king's;
or, like obedient subjects, follow him
to his new kingdom of perpetual rest.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Ah, I have as much interest in your sorrow
as I had title in your noble husband!
I have wept for a worthy husband's death,
and lived by looking on his images.
But now two mirrors of his princely semblance
are cracked in pieces by malignant death,
and for comfort I have but one false glass,
which grieves me when I see my shame in him.
You are a widow; yet you are a mother,
and you have the comfort of your children left to you.
But death has snatched my husband from my arms,
and plucked two crutches from my feeble limbs,
Edward and Clarence. Oh, what cause have I,
yours being but a half of my grief,
to surpass your plaints and drown your cries!

Boy:
Good aunt, you did not weep for our father's death;
how can we aid you with our kindred tears?

Girl:
Our fatherless distress was left un-moaned;
may your widow-sorrow likewise be unwept!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Give me no help in lamentation.
I am not barren to bring forth complaints.
May all springs reduce their currents to my eyes,
so that I, being governed by the watery moon,
may send forth plentiful tears to drown the world!
Oh, for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

Children:
Oh for our father, for our dear lord Clarence!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
What support had I but Edward? And he's gone.

Children:
What support had we but Clarence? And he's gone.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What stays had I but them? And they are gone.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
No widow ever had so dear a loss!

Children:
Never were orphans had so dear a loss!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
No mother ever had so dear a loss!
Alas, I am the mother of these moans!
Their woes are parceled out, mine are general.
She weeps for an Edward, and so do I;
I weep for a Clarence, but she does not.
These babies weep for Clarence, and so do I;
I weep for an Edward, but they do not.
Alas, you three, pour all your tears on me,
who is threefold distressed! I am your sorrow's nurse,
and I will pamper it with lamentations.

DORSET:
Comfort, dear mother. God is much displeased
that you take his doing with unthankfulness.
In common worldly things, it is called ungrateful,
to repay a debt with dull unwillingness,
which with a bounteous hand was kindly lent.
Much more to be thus opposite with heaven,
for it requires the royal debt it lent you.

RIVERS:
Madam, think, like a careful mother,
of the young prince your son. Send for him straight away.
Let him be crowned; in him your comfort lives.
Drown desperate sorrow in dead Edward's grave,
and plant your joys in living Edward's throne.

GLOUCESTER:
Madam, have comfort. All of us have cause
to wail the dimming of our shining star;
but no one can cure their harms by wailing them.
Madam, my mother, I beg your mercy;
I did not see your grace. Humbly on my knee
I crave your blessing.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
God bless you, and put meekness in your mind,
love, charity, obedience, and true duty!

GLOUCESTER:

BUCKINGHAM:
You cloudy princes and heart-sorrowing peers,
that bear this mutual heavy load of moan,
now cheer each other in each other's love.
Though we have spent our harvest of this king,
we are to reap the harvest of his son.
The broken rancor of your high-swollen hearts,
but lately splintered, knit, and joined together,
must gently be preserved, cherished, and kept.
It seems good to me that, with some little train,
the young prince be fetched forthwith from Ludlow
hither to London, to be crowned our king.

RIVERS:
Why with some little train, my Lord of Buckingham?

BUCKINGHAM:
Well, my lord, lest, by a multitude,
the newly-healed wound of malice should break out,
which would be so much the more dangerous
by how much the state is new and yet ungoverned.
Where every horse bears his commanding rein,
and may direct his course as he pleases,
as well the fear of harm as harm apparent,
in my opinion, ought to be prevented.

GLOUCESTER:
I hope the king made peace with all of us,
and the compact is firm and true in me.

RIVERS:
And so in me; and so, I think, in all.
Yet, since it is but new, it should not be put
to any apparent likelihood of a breach,
which might happen with a large company.
Therefore, I say with the noble Buckingham,
that it is fitting that so few should fetch the prince.

HASTINGS:
And so say I.

GLOUCESTER:
Then let it be so; and let us go to determine
who they shall be that shall go straight to Ludlow.
Madam, and you, my mother, will you go
to give your opinions in this weighty business?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
With all our hearts.

BUCKINGHAM:
My lord, whoever journeys to the Prince,
for God's sake, let not us two be behind;
for, by the way, I'll find an occasion,
as an introduction to the story we recently talked about,
to part the queen's proud kindred from the king.

GLOUCESTER:
My other self, my council's consistory,
my oracle, my prophet! My dear cousin,
I, like a child, will go by your direction.
Towards Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

First Citizen:
Neighbor, well met. Where are you going so fast?

Second Citizen:
I promise you, I hardly know myself.
Have you heard the news abroad?

First Citizen:
Yes, that the king is dead.

Second Citizen:
Bad news, by our lady; seldom comes better.
I fear, I fear it will prove a troublesome world.

Third Citizen:
Neighbors, God speed!

First Citizen:
Good morrow to you, sir.

Third Citizen:
Does this news of good King Edward's death hold true?

Second Citizen:
Yes, sir, it is too true. God help us in the meantime!

Third Citizen:
Then, masters, look to see a troublesome world.

First Citizen:
No, no; by God's good grace his son shall reign.

Third Citizen:
Woe to the land that is governed by a child!

Second Citizen:
In him, there is a hope of government,
that in his youth, with council under him,
and in his full and ripened years, himself,
no doubt, shall then and until then govern well.

First Citizen:
So stood the state when Henry the Sixth
was crowned in Paris when he was but nine months old.

Third Citizen:
Did the state stand so? No, no, good friends, God knows;
for then this land was famously enriched
with politic, grave counsel. Then the king
had virtuous uncles to protect his grace.

First Citizen:
Why, so has this one, both by his father and mother.

Third Citizen:
It would be better if they all came from the father,
or if there were none at all from the father.
For the competition now, of who shall be nearest,
will touch us all too near, if God does not prevent it.
Oh, the Duke of Gloucester is full of danger!
And the queen's sons and brothers are haughty and proud.
And if they were to be ruled, and not to rule,
this sickly land might find solace as before.

First Citizen:
Come, come, we fear the worst; all shall be well.

Third Citizen:
When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks;
when great leaves fall, the winter is at hand;
when the sun sets, who does not look for night?
Untimely storms make men expect a dearth.
All may be well; but, if God wills it so,
it is more than we deserve, or I expect.

Second Citizen:
Truly, the souls of men are full of dread.
You can hardly reason with a man
that doesn't look heavy and full of fear.

Third Citizen:
Before the times of change, it is always so.
By a divine instinct, men's minds mistrust
ensuing dangers; as we see by proof,
the waters swell before a boisterous storm.
But leave it all to God. Where are you going?

Second Citizen:
Well, we were sent for to the justices.

Third Citizen:
And so was I. I'll bear you company.

ARCHBISHOP OF YORK:
Last night, I hear, they stayed at Northampton;
at Stony-Stratford they will be tonight.
Tomorrow, or the next day, they will be here.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I long with all my heart to see the prince.
I hope he has grown much since I last saw him.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
But I hear, no; they say my son of York
has almost overtaken him in his growth.

YORK:
Yes, mother; but I would not have it so.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Why, my young cousin, it is good to grow.

YORK:
Grandam, one night, as we sat at supper,
my uncle Rivers talked about how I had grown
more than my brother. 'Yes,' said my uncle
Gloucester,
'small herbs have grace, great weeds do grow apace.'
And since then, I think, I would not grow so fast,
because sweet flowers are slow and weeds make haste.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Good faith, good faith, the saying did not hold
in him that said the same to you.
He was the most wretched thing when he was young,
so long in growing and so leisurely,
that, if this rule were true, he should be gracious.

ARCHBISHOP OF YORK:
Why, madam, so, no doubt, he is.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I hope he is; but yet let mothers doubt.

YORK:
Now, by my troth, if I had been remembered,
I could have given my uncle's grace a flout,
to touch his growth nearer than he touched mine.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
How, my pretty York? I pray you, let me hear it.

YORK:
Well, they say my uncle grew so fast
that he could gnaw a crust at two hours old.
It was a full two years before I could get a tooth.
Grandam, this would have been a biting jest.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I pray you, pretty York, who told you this?

YORK:
Grandam, his nurse.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
His nurse! Why, she was dead before you were born.

YORK:
If it wasn't she, I can't tell who told me.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
A perilous boy. Go to, you are too shrewd.

ARCHBISHOP OF YORK:
Good madam, don't be angry with the child.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Pitchers have ears.

ARCHBISHOP OF YORK:
Here comes a messenger. What news?

Messenger:
Such news, my lord, as grieves me to unfold.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
How is the prince?

Messenger:
Well, madam, and in health.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What is your news then?

Messenger:
Lord Rivers and Lord Grey are sent to Pomfret,
with them Sir Thomas Vaughan, as prisoners.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Who has committed them?

Messenger:
The mighty dukes
Gloucester and Buckingham.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
For what offense?

Messenger:
The sum of all I can, I have disclosed;
why or for what these nobles were committed
is all unknown to me, my gracious lady.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Ah me, I see the downfall of our house!
The tiger has now seized the gentle hind;
insulting tyranny begins to strut
upon the innocent and defenseless throne.
Welcome, destruction, death, and massacre!
I see, as in a map, the end of all.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Accursed and unquiet wrangling days,
how many of you have my eyes beheld!
My husband lost his life to get the crown;
and often up and down my sons were tossed,
for me to joy and weep their gain and loss.
And being seated, and domestic broils
clean over-blown, themselves, the conquerors,
make war upon themselves; blood against blood,
self against self. Oh, preposterous
and frantic outrage, end your damned spleen;
or let me die, to look on death no more!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Come, come, my boy; we will go to sanctuary.
Madam, farewell.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I'll go along with you.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
You have no cause.

ARCHBISHOP OF YORK:
My gracious lady, go;
and there bear your treasure and your goods.
For my part, I'll resign to your grace
the seal I keep. And so may it befall me
as well I care for you and all of yours!
Come, I'll conduct you to the sanctuary.

BUCKINGHAM:
Welcome, sweet prince, to London, to your chamber.

GLOUCESTER:
Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign.
The weary way has made you melancholy.

PRINCE EDWARD:
No, uncle; but our crosses on the way
have made it tedious, wearisome, and heavy.
I want more uncles here to welcome me.

GLOUCESTER:
Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years
has not yet dived into the world's deceit.
Nor can you distinguish more of a man
than of his outward show; which, God knows,
seldom or never jumps with the heart.
Those uncles which you want were dangerous;
your grace listened to their sugared words,
but didn't look on the poison of their hearts.
God keep you from them, and from such false friends!

PRINCE EDWARD:
God keep me from false friends! But they were none.

GLOUCESTER:
My lord, the mayor of London comes to greet you.

Lord Mayor:
God bless your grace with health and happy days!

PRINCE EDWARD:
I thank you, good my lord; and thank you all.
I thought my mother, and my brother York,
would have met us on the way long before this.
Fie, what a slug is Hastings, that he doesn't come
to tell us whether they will come or not!

BUCKINGHAM:
And, in good time, here comes the sweating lord.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Welcome, my lord. What, will our mother come?

HASTINGS:
On what occasion, God knows, not I,
the queen your mother, and your brother York,
have taken sanctuary. The tender prince
would have liked to come with me to meet your grace,
but was forcibly held back by his mother.

BUCKINGHAM:
Fie, what an indirect and peevish course
is this of hers! Lord cardinal, will your grace
persuade the queen to send the Duke of York
to his princely brother presently?
If she denies, Lord Hastings, go with him,
and from her jealous arms pluck him by force.

CARDINAL:
My Lord of Buckingham, if my weak oratory
can win the Duke of York from his mother,
expect him here soon. But if she is obstinate
to mild entreaties, God in heaven forbid
we should infringe the holy privilege
of blessed sanctuary! Not for all this land
would I be guilty of so deep a sin.

BUCKINGHAM:
You are too senseless—obstinate, my lord,
too ceremonious and traditional.
Weigh it but with the grossness of this age,
you do not break sanctuary in seizing him.
The benefit of it is always granted
to those whose dealings have deserved the place,
and those who have the wit to claim the place.
This prince has neither claimed it nor deserved it;
and therefore, in my opinion, cannot have it.
Then, taking him from there that is not there,
you break no privilege nor charter there.
I have often heard of sanctuary men,
but sanctuary children never until now.

CARDINAL:
My lord, you shall overrule my mind for once.
Come on, Lord Hastings, will you go with me?

HASTINGS:
I go, my lord.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Good lords, make all the speedy haste you may.
Say, uncle Gloucester, if our brother comes,
where shall we stay until our coronation?

GLOUCESTER:
Wherever seems best to your royal self.
If I may counsel you, for a day or two
your highness shall rest at the Tower.
Then wherever you please, and shall be thought most fit
for your best health and recreation.

PRINCE EDWARD:
I do not like the Tower, of any place.
Did Julius Caesar build that place, my lord?

BUCKINGHAM:
He did, my gracious lord, begin that place,
which, since then, succeeding ages have re-edified.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Is it on record, or else reported
successively from age to age, that he built it?

BUCKINGHAM:
It is on record, my gracious lord.

PRINCE EDWARD:
But say, my lord, if it were not registered,
I think the truth should live from age to age,
as if it were retold to all posterity,
even to the general, all-ending day.

GLOUCESTER:

PRINCE EDWARD:
What do you say, uncle?

GLOUCESTER:
I say, without written words, fame lives long.
Thus, like the formal vice, Iniquity,
I moralize two meanings in one word.

PRINCE EDWARD:
That Julius Caesar was a famous man;
with what his valor did enrich his wit,
his wit set down to make his valor live.
Death makes no conquest of this conqueror;
for now he lives in fame, though not in life.
I'll tell you what, my cousin Buckingham,—

BUCKINGHAM:
What, my gracious lord?

PRINCE EDWARD:
If I live until I am a man,
I'll win our ancient right in France again,
or die a soldier, as I lived a king.

GLOUCESTER:

BUCKINGHAM:
Now, in good time, here comes the Duke of York.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Richard of York! How fares our loving brother?

YORK:
Well, my dread lord; so I must call you now.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Yes, brother, to our grief, as it is yours.
He died too late that might have kept that title,
which by his death has lost much majesty.

GLOUCESTER:
How fares our cousin, noble Lord of York?

YORK:
I thank you, gentle uncle. Oh, my lord,
you said that idle weeds grow fast.
The prince my brother has outgrown me far.

GLOUCESTER:
He has, my lord.

YORK:
And therefore is he idle?

GLOUCESTER:
Oh, my fair cousin, I must not say so.

YORK:
Then he is more beholden to you than I.

GLOUCESTER:
He may command me as my sovereign;
but you have power over me as a kinsman.

YORK:
I pray you, uncle, give me this dagger.

GLOUCESTER:
My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.

PRINCE EDWARD:
A beggar, brother?

YORK:
Of my kind uncle, that I know will give;
and being but a toy, which is no grief to give.

GLOUCESTER:
I'll give my cousin a greater gift than that.

YORK:
A greater gift! Oh, that's the sword to go with it.

GLOUCESTER:
A gentle cousin, if it were light enough.

YORK:
Oh, then, I see, you will part with only light gifts;
in weightier things you'll say no to a beggar.

GLOUCESTER:
It is too heavy for your grace to wear.

YORK:
I would consider it light, were it heavier.

GLOUCESTER:
What, would you have my weapon, little lord?

YORK:
I would, so that I might thank you as you call me.

GLOUCESTER:
How?

YORK:
Little.

PRINCE EDWARD:
My Lord of York will always be cross in his talk.
Uncle, your grace knows how to bear with him.

YORK:
You mean, to carry me, not to put up with me.
Uncle, my brother is mocking both you and me.
Because I am little, like an ape,
he thinks that you should carry me on your shoulders.

BUCKINGHAM:
With what a sharp-witted mind he reasons!
To mitigate the scorn he gives his uncle,
he prettily and aptly taunts himself.
So cunning and so young is wonderful.

GLOUCESTER:
My lord, would it please you to pass along?
Myself and my good cousin Buckingham
will go to your mother, to entreat of her
to meet you at the Tower and welcome you.

YORK:
What, are you going to the Tower, my lord?

PRINCE EDWARD:
My lord protector insists on it.

YORK:
I shall not sleep in quiet at the Tower.

GLOUCESTER:
Why, what should you fear?

YORK:
Well, my uncle Clarence's angry ghost.
My grandmother told me he was murdered there.

PRINCE EDWARD:
I fear no dead uncles.

GLOUCESTER:
Nor any that are alive, I hope.

PRINCE EDWARD:
If they are alive, I hope I need not fear.
But come, my lord; and with a heavy heart,
thinking of them, I go to the Tower.

BUCKINGHAM:
Do you think, my lord, this little prating York
was not incited by his subtle mother
to taunt and scorn you so offensively?

GLOUCESTER:
No doubt, no doubt. Oh, he's a perilous boy;
bold, quick, ingenious, forward, capable.
He is all his mother's, from head to toe.

BUCKINGHAM:
Well, let them rest. Come here, Catesby.
You are sworn as deeply to effect what we intend
as closely to conceal what we impart.
You know our reasons, which we urged on the way.
What do you think? Is it not an easy matter
to make William Lord Hastings of our mind,
for the installation of this noble duke
in the royal seat of this famous isle?

CATESBY:
He loves the prince so much for his father's sake,
that he will not be won over to anything against him.

BUCKINGHAM:
What do you think, then, of Stanley? What will he do?

CATESBY:
He will do everything as Hastings does.

BUCKINGHAM:
Well, then, no more but this: go, gentle Catesby,
and, as if from a distance, sound out Lord Hastings,
how he is affected to our purpose;
and summon him tomorrow to the Tower,
to sit about the coronation.
If you find him tractable to us,
encourage him, and show him all our reasons.
If he is leaden, icy-cold, unwilling,
be you so too; and so break off your talk,
and give us notice of his inclination.
For we tomorrow hold divided councils,
in which you shall be highly employed.

GLOUCESTER:
Commend me to Lord William. Tell him, Catesby,
his ancient knot of dangerous adversaries
will be let blood at Pomfret Castle tomorrow;
and bid my friend, for joy of this good news,
give Mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.

BUCKINGHAM:
Good Catesby, go, and carry out this business soundly.

CATESBY:
My good lords both, with all the care I can.

GLOUCESTER:
Shall we hear from you, Catesby, before we sleep?

CATESBY:
You shall, my lord.

GLOUCESTER:
At Crosby Place, you shall find us both.

BUCKINGHAM:
Now, my lord, what shall we do if we perceive
that Lord Hastings will not yield to our plots?

GLOUCESTER:
Chop off his head, man; we will do something.
And, look, when I am king, you claim from me
the earldom of Hereford, and the movables
of which the king my brother was possessed.

BUCKINGHAM:
I'll claim that promise at your grace's hands.

GLOUCESTER:
And look to have it yielded with all willingness.
Come, let us sup early, so that afterwards
we may digest our plots in some form.

Messenger:
What, ho! my lord!

HASTINGS:

Messenger:
A messenger from the Lord Stanley.

HASTINGS:
What time is it?

Messenger:
Upon the stroke of four.

HASTINGS:
Can't your master sleep these tedious nights?

Messenger:
So it would seem by what I have to say.
First, he commends himself to your noble lordship.

HASTINGS:
And then?

Messenger:
And then he sends you word
that he dreamt tonight the boar had destroyed his helmet.
Besides, he says there are two councils being held,
and that what may be decided at one
may make you and him regret it at the other.
Therefore, he sends to know your lordship's pleasure,
if you will presently take a horse with him,
and with all speed post with him toward the north,
to shun the danger that his soul divines.

HASTINGS:
Go, fellow, go, return to your lord;
tell him not to fear the separate councils.
His honor and myself are at one,
and at the other is my servant Catesby,
where nothing can proceed that touches us
of which I shall not have intelligence.
Tell him his fears are shallow, lacking proof.
And as for his dreams, I wonder that he is so foolish
to trust the mockery of unquiet slumbers.
To flee the boar before the boar pursues,
would be to incite the boar to follow us
and make pursuit where he meant no chase.
Go, tell your master to rise and come to me,
and we will both go together to the Tower,
where, he shall see, the boar will treat us kindly.

Messenger:
My gracious lord, I'll tell him what you say.

CATESBY:
Many good morrows to my noble lord!

HASTINGS:
Good morrow, Catesby. You are stirring early.
What news, what news, in this our tottering state?

CATESBY:
It is a reeling world, indeed, my lord;
and I believe it will never stand upright
until Richard wears the garland of the realm.

HASTINGS:
What! Wear the garland! Do you mean the crown?

CATESBY:
Yes, my good lord.

HASTINGS:
I'll have this crown of mine cut from my shoulders
before I will see the crown so foully misplaced.
But can you guess that he is aiming for it?

CATESBY:
Yes, on my life; and he hopes to find you forward
on his side for the gain of it.
And for that reason he sends you this good news,
that this very same day your enemies,
the kindred of the queen, must die at Pomfret.

HASTINGS:
Indeed, I am no mourner for that news,
because they have always been my enemies.
But, that I'll give my voice on Richard's side,
to bar my master's heirs in true descent,
God knows I will not do it, to the death.

CATESBY:
God keep your lordship in that gracious mind!

HASTINGS:
But I shall laugh at this a year from now,
that those who brought me into my master's hate,
I live to look upon their tragedy.
I tell you, Catesby—

CATESBY:
What, my lord?

HASTINGS:
Before a fortnight makes me older,
I'll send some packing that don't even think about it yet.

CATESBY:
It's a vile thing to die, my gracious lord,
when men are unprepared and don't expect it.

HASTINGS:
Oh, monstrous, monstrous! And so it happens
with Rivers, Vaughan, and Grey. And so it will do
