with some other men, who think themselves as safe
as you and I; who, as you know, are dear
to princely Richard and to Buckingham.

CATESBY:
The princes both hold you in high account;
for they consider his head to be on the bridge.

HASTINGS:
I know they do; and I have well deserved it.
Come on, come on; where is your boar-spear, man?
Do you fear the boar, and go so unprepared?

STANLEY:
My lord, good morrow; good morrow, Catesby.
You may jest on, but, by the holy rood,
I do not like these several councils, I.

HASTINGS:
My lord,
I hold my life as dear as you do yours;
and never in my life, I do protest,
was it more precious to me than it is now.
Do you think, but that I know our state is secure,
I would be so triumphant as I am?

STANLEY:
The lords at Pomfret, when they rode from London,
were jocund, and supposed their state was sure,
and they indeed had no cause to mistrust.
But yet, you see how soon the day overcast.
This sudden stab of rancor I misdoubt.
I pray God, I say, I prove a needless coward!
What, shall we go toward the Tower? The day is spent.

HASTINGS:
Come, come, I'm with you. Do you know what, my lord?
Today the lords you speak of are beheaded.

LORD STANLEY:
They, for their truth, might better wear their heads
than some that have accused them wear their hats.
But come, my lord, let us go away.

HASTINGS:
Go on before; I'll talk with this good fellow.
How now, sirrah! How goes the world with you?

Pursuivant:
The better that your lordship pleases to ask.

HASTINGS:
I tell you, man, it is better with me now
than when I met you last where we now meet.
Then I was going as a prisoner to the Tower,
by the suggestion of the queen's allies.
But now, I tell you—keep it to yourself—
this day those enemies are put to death,
and I am in a better state than I ever was.

Pursuivant:
May God hold it, to your honor's good content!

HASTINGS:
Thank you, fellow. There, drink that for me.

Pursuivant:
God save your lordship!

Priest:
Well met, my lord; I am glad to see your honor.

HASTINGS:
I thank you, good Sir John, with all my heart.
I am in your debt for your last exercise;
come the next Sabbath, and I will pay you.

BUCKINGHAM:
What, talking with a priest, lord chamberlain?
Your friends at Pomfret, they need the priest;
your honor has no confessing to do.

HASTINGS:
Good faith, and when I met this holy man,
those men you talk of came into my mind.
What, are you going toward the Tower?

BUCKINGHAM:
I am, my lord; but I shall not stay long.
I shall return before your lordship leaves there.

HASTINGS:
It's likely enough, for I am staying for dinner there.

BUCKINGHAM:

HASTINGS:
I'll wait upon your lordship.

RATCLIFF:
Come, bring forth the prisoners.

RIVERS:
Sir Richard Ratcliff, let me tell you this:
today you shall see a subject die
for truth, for duty, and for loyalty.

GREY:
God keep the prince from all of you!
You are a knot of damned blood-suckers!

VAUGHAN:
You who are alive shall cry woe for this hereafter.

RATCLIFF:
Dispatch; the limit of your lives is out.

RIVERS:
O Pomfret, Pomfret! O you bloody prison,
fatal and ominous to noble peers!
Within the guilty enclosure of your walls,
Richard the Second was hacked to death here;
and, for more slander to your dismal seat,
we give you our guiltless blood to drink.

GREY:
Now Margaret's curse has fallen upon our heads,
for standing by when Richard stabbed her son.

RIVERS:
Then she cursed Hastings, then she cursed Buckingham,
then she cursed Richard. Oh, remember, God,
to hear her prayers for them, as now for us.
And for my sister and her princely sons,
be satisfied, dear God, with our true blood,
which, as you know, must be unjustly spilt.

RATCLIFF:
Make haste; the hour of death is at hand.

RIVERS:
Come, Grey, come, Vaughan, let us all embrace,
and take our leave, until we meet in heaven.

HASTINGS:
My lords, at once: the reason we are met
is to decide on the coronation.
In God's name, speak: when is the royal day?

BUCKINGHAM:
Are all things fitting for that royal time?

DERBY:
It is, and it only needs a date to be named.

BISHOP OF ELY:
Tomorrow, then, I judge to be a happy day.

BUCKINGHAM:
Who knows the lord protector's mind in this?
Who is most intimate with the royal duke?

BISHOP OF ELY:
Your grace, we think, should soonest know his mind.

BUCKINGHAM:
Who, I, my lord? We know each other's faces,
but as for our hearts, he knows no more of mine,
than I of yours;
nor I any more of his, than you of mine.
Lord Hastings, you and he are close in love.

HASTINGS:
I thank his grace, I know he loves me well;
but, as for his purpose in the coronation,
I have not sounded him out, nor has he delivered
his gracious pleasure in any way therein.
But you, my noble lords, may name the time;
and in the duke's behalf I'll give my voice,
which, I presume, he'll take in a gentle way.

BISHOP OF ELY:
Now, in good time, here comes the duke himself.

GLOUCESTER:
My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow.
I have been a long sleeper; but, I hope,
my absence does not neglect any great designs,
which by my presence might have been concluded.

BUCKINGHAM:
If you had not come on your cue, my lord,
William Lord Hastings would have pronounced your part—
I mean, your vote—for the crowning of the king.

GLOUCESTER:
Than my Lord Hastings, no man might be bolder;
his lordship knows me well, and loves me well.

HASTINGS:
I thank your grace.

GLOUCESTER:
My lord of Ely!

BISHOP OF ELY:
My lord?

GLOUCESTER:
When I was last in Holborn,
I saw good strawberries in your garden there.
I beseech you, send for some of them.

BISHOP OF ELY:
Yes, and I will, my lord, with all my heart.

GLOUCESTER:
Cousin of Buckingham, a word with you.
Catesby has sounded out Hastings on our business,
and finds the testy gentleman so hot,
that he will lose his head before he gives his consent
that his master's son, as he worshipfully terms it,
shall lose the royalty of England's throne.

BUCKINGHAM:
Withdraw from here, my lord, I'll follow you.

DERBY:
We have not yet set down this day of triumph.
Tomorrow, in my opinion, is too sudden;
for I myself am not so well provided
as I would otherwise be, if the day were prolonged.

BISHOP OF ELY:
Where is my lord protector? I have sent for these
strawberries.

HASTINGS:
His grace looks cheerful and smooth today;
there's some conceit or other that pleases him well,
when he bids good morrow with such a spirit.
I think there's never a man in Christendom
that can less hide his love or hate than he;
for by his face you shall know his heart straight away.

DERBY:
What of his heart do you perceive in his face
by any likelihood he showed today?

HASTINGS:
Well, that he is not offended with any man here;
for, if he were, he would have shown it in his looks.

DERBY:
I pray God he is not, I say.

GLOUCESTER:
I pray you all, tell me what they deserve
that conspire my death with devilish plots
of damned witchcraft, and that have prevailed
upon my body with their hellish charms?

HASTINGS:
The tender love I bear your grace, my lord,
makes me most forward in this noble presence
to doom the offenders, whoever they may be.
I say, my lord, they have deserved death.

GLOUCESTER:
Then let your eyes be the witness of this evil.
See how I am bewitched; behold my arm
is, like a blasted sapling, withered up.
And this is Edward's wife, that monstrous witch,
consorted with that harlot strumpet Shore,
that by their witchcraft have thus marked me.

HASTINGS:
If they have done this thing, my gracious lord—

GLOUCESTER:
If! You protector of this damned strumpet—
are you telling me of 'ifs'? You are a traitor.
Off with his head! Now, by Saint Paul I swear,
I will not dine until I see it done.
Lovel and Ratcliff, see that it is done.
The rest, that love me, rise and follow me.

HASTINGS:
Woe, woe for England! Not a bit for me;
for I, too foolish, might have prevented this.
Stanley dreamt the boar destroyed his helmet;
but I disdained it, and scorned to flee.
Three times today my foot-cloth horse stumbled,
and startled, when he looked upon the Tower,
as if he were loath to bear me to the slaughter-house.
Oh, now I want the priest that spoke to me.
I now repent that I told the pursuivant,
as if I were triumphing over my enemies,
how they at Pomfret were bloodily butchered,
and I myself was secure in grace and favor.
Oh Margaret, Margaret, now your heavy curse
has lighted on poor Hastings' wretched head!

RATCLIFF:
Dispatch, my lord; the duke would like to be at dinner.
Make a short confession; he longs to see your head.

HASTINGS:
O momentary grace of mortal men,
which we hunt for more than the grace of God!
Who builds his hopes in the air of your good looks,
lives like a drunken sailor on a mast,
ready, with every nod, to tumble down
into the fatal bowels of the deep.

LOVEL:
Come, come, dispatch; it's useless to exclaim.

HASTINGS:
Oh, bloody Richard! Miserable England!
I prophesy the most fearful time for you
that any wretched age has ever looked upon.
Come, lead me to the block; bear him my head.
They smile at me who will shortly be dead.

GLOUCESTER:
Come, cousin, can you quake, and change your color,
murder your breath in the middle of a word,
and then begin again, and stop again,
as if you were distraught and mad with terror?

BUCKINGHAM:
Tut, I can counterfeit the deep tragedian;
speak and look back, and pry on every side,
tremble and start at the wagging of a straw,
intending deep suspicion. Ghastly looks
are at my service, like enforced smiles;
and both are ready in their offices,
at any time, to grace my stratagems.
But what, is Catesby gone?

GLOUCESTER:
He is; and, see, he brings the mayor along.

BUCKINGHAM:
Lord mayor,—

GLOUCESTER:
Look to the drawbridge there!

BUCKINGHAM:
Hark! A drum.

GLOUCESTER:
Catesby, look over the walls.

BUCKINGHAM:
Lord mayor, the reason we have sent—

GLOUCESTER:
Look back, defend yourself, here are enemies.

BUCKINGHAM:
God and our innocence defend and guard us!

GLOUCESTER:
Be patient, they are friends, Ratcliff and Lovel.

LOVEL:
Here is the head of that ignoble traitor,
the dangerous and unsuspected Hastings.

GLOUCESTER:
I loved the man so dearly that I must weep.
I took him for the plainest, harmless creature
that breathed upon this earth as a Christian;
I made him my book in which my soul recorded
the history of all her secret thoughts.
So smoothly did he daub his vice with a show of virtue,
that, omitting his apparent, open guilt,
I mean, his conversation with Shore's wife,
he lived free from all taint of suspicion.

BUCKINGHAM:
Well, well, he was the most covert, sheltered traitor
that ever lived.
Would you imagine, or almost believe,
if it were not that, by great preservation,
we live to tell it to you, that the subtle traitor
had plotted this day, in the council-house,
to murder me and my good Lord of Gloucester?

Lord Mayor:
What, had he so?

GLOUCESTER:
What, do you think we are Turks or infidels?
Or that we would, against the form of law,
proceed thus rashly to the villain's death,
but that the extreme peril of the case,
the peace of England, and our persons' safety,
forced us to this execution?

Lord Mayor:
Now, may good fortune befall you! He deserved his death;
and you, my good lords, both have well proceeded,
to warn false traitors from the like attempts.
I never expected better from his hands,
after he once fell in with Mistress Shore.

GLOUCESTER:
Yet we had not determined he should die,
until your lordship came to see his death;
which now the loving haste of these our friends,
somewhat against our meaning, has prevented.
Because, my lord, we would have had you heard
the traitor speak, and timidly confess
the manner and the purpose of his treason;
so that you might have well signified the same
to the citizens, who might happen to
misconstrue us in him and wail his death.

Lord Mayor:
But, my good lord, your grace's word shall serve,
as well as if I had seen and heard him speak.
And do not doubt, right noble princes both,
but I'll acquaint our dutiful citizens
with all your just proceedings in this cause.

GLOUCESTER:
And to that end we wished your lordship here,
to avoid the carping censures of the world.

BUCKINGHAM:
But since you have come too late for our intentions,
yet witness what you hear we did intend.
And so, my good lord mayor, we bid farewell.

GLOUCE-STER:
Go, after, after, cousin Buckingham.
The mayor is hurrying to Guildhall.
There, at your most opportune moment,
infer the bastardy of Edward's children.
Tell them how Edward put a citizen to death,
only for saying he would make his son
heir to the crown; meaning, indeed, his house,
which, by the sign of it, was called so.
Moreover, urge his hateful luxury
and bestial appetite in his changing lusts,
which stretched to their servants, daughters, and wives,
even where his lustful eye or savage heart,
without control, chose to make his prey.
No, for a need, thus far come near my person:
tell them, when my mother was with child
of that insatiable Edward, the noble York,
my princely father, was then at war in France.
And, by just computation of the time,
he found that the issue was not his,
which was well-apparent in his features,
being nothing like the noble duke my father.
But touch this sparingly, as if from a distance,
because you know, my lord, my mother is alive.

BUCKINGHAM:
Fear not, my lord, I'll play the orator
as if the golden fee for which I plead
were for myself. And so, my lord, adieu.

GLOUCESTER:
If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle;
where you shall find me well accompanied
with reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.

BUCKINGHAM:
I go. And around three or four o'clock,
look for the news that the Guildhall affords.

GLOUCESTER:
Go, Lovel, with all speed to Doctor Shaw;
go to Friar Penker; bid them both
meet me within this hour at Baynard's Castle.
Now I will go in, to take some private order,
to draw the brats of Clarence out of sight;
and to give notice, that no person
at any time should have recourse to the princes.

Scrivener:
This is the indictment of the good Lord Hastings,
which is fairly engrossed in a set hand,
so that it may be read over in St. Paul's this day.
And mark how well the sequel hangs together:
I spent eleven hours to write it over,
for last night it was brought to me by Catesby;
the precedent was just as long in the making.
And yet within these five hours Lord Hastings was alive,
untainted, unexamined, free, at liberty.
Here's a good world in the meantime! Why, who's so gross,
that doesn't see this palpable device?
Yet who's so blind, but says he doesn't see it?
Bad is the world; and all will come to nothing,
when such bad dealings must be seen in thought.

GLOUCESTER:
How now, my lord, what do the citizens say?

BUCKINGHAM:
Now, by the holy mother of our Lord,
the citizens are silent and do not speak a word.

GLOUCESTER:
Did you touch on the bastardy of Edward's children?

BUCKINGHAM:
I did; with his contract with Lady Lucy,
and his contract by deputy in France;
the insatiable greediness of his desires,
and his enforcement of the city wives;
his tyranny for trifles; his own bastardy,
as being conceived, your father then in France,
his resemblance, being not like the duke;
withal I did infer your features,
being the right idea of your father,
both in your form and nobleness of mind;
laid open all your victories in Scotland,
your discipline in war, wisdom in peace,
your bounty, virtue, fair humility.
Indeed, I left nothing fitting for the purpose
untouched, or slightly handled, in my discourse.
And when my oratory came to an end,
I bid them that did love their country's good
cry, 'God save Richard, England's royal king!'

GLOUCESTER:
Ah! And did they do so?

BUCKINGHAM:
No, so God help me, they did not speak a word;
but, like dumb statues or breathing stones,
they gazed at each other, and looked deathly pale.
When I saw this, I reprimanded them,
and asked the mayor what this willful silence meant.
His answer was, the people were not accustomed
to be spoken to but by the recorder.
Then he was urged to tell my tale again,
'Thus says the duke, thus has the duke inferred';
but he spoke nothing in warrant from himself.
When he had finished, some followers of my own,
at the lower end of the hall, hurled up their caps,
and some ten voices cried, 'God save King Richard!'
And thus I took advantage of those few,
'Thanks, gentle citizens and friends,' I said;
'this general applause and loving shout
argues your wisdom and your love for Richard.'
And right there I broke off, and came away.

GLOUCE-STER:
What tongueless blocks were they! Wouldn't they speak?

BUCKINGHAM:
No, by my troth, my lord.

GLOUCESTER:
Will not the mayor then and his brethren come?

BUCKINGHAM:
The mayor is here at hand. Feign some fear;
don't be spoken with, but by a mighty suit.
And see that you get a prayer-book in your hand,
and stand between two churchmen, good my lord;
for on that ground I'll build a holy discourse.
And don't be easily won over to our request.
Play the maid's part, still answer no, and take it.

GLOUCESTER:
I'm going. And if you plead as well for them
as I can say no to you for myself,
no doubt we'll bring it to a happy issue.

BUCKINGHAM:
Go, go, up to the leads; the lord mayor is knocking.
Welcome, my lord; I am waiting here.
I think the duke will not be spoken to.
Here comes his servant. How now, Catesby,
what does he say?

CATESBY:
My lord, he entreats your grace
to visit him tomorrow or the next day.
He is within, with two right reverend fathers,
divinely bent on meditation;
and he would not be moved by any worldly suit,
to draw him from his holy exercise.

BUCKINGHAM:
Return, good Catesby, to your lord again;
tell him, myself, the mayor and citizens,
in deep designs and matters of great moment,
no less important than our general good,
have come to have some conference with his grace.

CATESBY:
I'll tell him what you say, my lord.

BUCKINGHAM:
Ah, ha, my lord, this prince is not an Edward!
He is not lounging on a lewd day-bed,
but on his knees at meditation;
not dallying with a pair of courtesans,
but meditating with two deep divines;
not sleeping, to engross his idle body,
but praying, to enrich his watchful soul.
Happy would England be, if this gracious prince
would take upon himself the sovereignty of it.
But, surely, I fear, we shall never win him to it.

Lord Mayor:
Well, God forbid his grace should say no to us!

BUCKINGHAM:
I fear he will.
How now, Catesby, what says your lord?

CATESBY:
My lord,
he wonders to what end you have assembled
such troops of citizens to speak with him,
his grace not being warned of it before.
My lord, he fears you mean no good to him.

BUCKINGHAM:
I am sorry that my noble cousin should
suspect me, that I mean no good to him.
By heaven, I come in perfect love to him;
and so once more return and tell his grace.
When holy and devout religious men
are at their beads, it's hard to draw them away,
so sweet is zealous contemplation.

Lord Mayor:
See, where he stands between two clergymen!

BUCKINGHAM:
Two props of virtue for a Christian prince,
to keep him from the fall of vanity.
And, see, a book of prayer in his hand,
true ornaments to know a holy man.
Famous Plantagenet, most gracious prince,
lend favorable ears to our request;
and pardon us the interruption
of your devotion and right Christian zeal.

GLOUCESTER:
My lord, there needs no such apology.
I rather do beseech you to pardon me,
who, earnest in the service of my God,
neglect the visitation of my friends.
But, leaving this, what is your grace's pleasure?

BUCKINGHAM:
Even that, I hope, which pleases God above,
and all good men of this ungoverned isle.

GLOUCESTER:
I suspect I have done some offense
that seems disgraceful in the city's eyes,
and that you have come to reprimand my ignorance.

BUCKINGHAM:
You have, my lord. Would it might please your grace,
at our entreaties, to amend that fault!

GLOUCESTER:
Otherwise, why do I breathe in a Christian land?

BUCKINGHAM:
Then know, it is your fault that you resign
the supreme seat, the majestic throne,
the sceptered office of your ancestors,
your state of fortune and your right of birth,
the lineal glory of your royal house,
to the corruption of a blemished stock.
While, in the mildness of your sleepy thoughts,
which here we awaken for our country's good,
this noble isle lacks her proper limbs;
her face is defaced with scars of infamy,
her royal stock is grafted with ignoble plants,
and almost shouldered in the swallowing gulf
of blind forgetfulness and dark oblivion.
To cure which, we heartily solicit
your gracious self to take upon you the charge
and kingly government of this your land,
not as protector, steward, substitute,
or a lowly factor for another's gain;
but as successively from blood to blood,
your right of birth, your empire, your own.
For this, in company with the citizens,
your very worshipful and loving friends,
and by their vehement instigation,
in this just suit I come to move your grace.

GLOUCESTER:
I don't know whether to depart in silence,
or to speak bitterly in your reproof.
It best befits my degree or your condition.
If I don't answer, you might perhaps think
that my tongue-tied ambition, not replying, has yielded
to bear the golden yoke of sovereignty,
which you would fondly impose on me here.
If I reprove you for this suit of yours,
so seasoned with your faithful love for me,
then, on the other hand, I have checked my friends.
Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first,
and then, in speaking, not to incur the last,
I definitively answer you thus.
Your love deserves my thanks; but my unworthiness
shuns your high request.
First, if all obstacles were cut away,
and my path were even to the crown,
as my ripe inheritance and right by birth,
yet my poverty of spirit is so great,
and my defects are so many and so mighty,
that I would rather hide myself from my greatness,
being a bark that can brook no mighty sea,
than to be hidden in my greatness,
and be smothered in the vapor of my glory.
But, God be thanked, there's no need of me,
and I much need to help you, if need were.
The royal tree has left us royal fruit,
which, mellowed by the stealing hours of time,
will well become the seat of majesty,
and make us, no doubt, happy by his reign.
On him I lay what you would lay on me,
the right and fortune of his happy stars;
which God forbid that I should wring from him!

BUCKINGHAM:
My lord, this argues conscience in your grace;
but the considerations of it are nice and trivial,
all circumstances well considered.
You say that Edward is your brother's son.
So we say too, but not by Edward's wife;
for first he was contracted to Lady Lucy—
your mother lives as a witness to that vow—
and afterward, by a substitute, betrothed
to Bona, sister to the King of France.
Both of these were put aside for a poor petitioner,
a care-crazed mother of many children,
a beauty-waning and distressed widow,
even in the afternoon of her best days,
made a prize and purchase of his lustful eye,
seduced the pitch and height of all his thoughts
to a base declension and loathsome bigamy.
By her, in his unlawful bed, he got
this Edward, whom our manners term the prince.
I could expostulate more bitterly,
except that, for reverence to some who are alive,
I give a sparing limit to my tongue.
Then, good my lord, take to your royal self
this proffered benefit of dignity;
if not to bless us and the land withal,
yet to draw forth your noble ancestry
from the corruption of abusing times,
to a lineal, truly-derived course.

Lord Mayor:
Do, good my lord, your citizens entreat you.

BUCKINGHAM:
Refuse not, mighty lord, this proffered love.

CATESBY:
Oh, make them joyful, grant their lawful suit!

GLOUCESTER:
Alas, why would you heap these cares on me?
I am unfit for state and majesty.
I beseech you, don't take it amiss;
I cannot and I will not yield to you.

BUCKINGHAM:
If you refuse it—as, in love and zeal,
loath to depose the child, your brother's son,
as well we know your tenderness of heart
and gentle, kind, effeminate remorse,
which we have noted in you to your kin,
and equally indeed to all estates—
yet whether you accept our suit or not,
your brother's son shall never reign as our king.
But we will plant some other on the throne,
to the disgrace and downfall of your house.
And in this resolution, we leave you here.
Come, citizens. By God's wounds! I'll entreat no more.

GLOUCESTER:
Oh, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham.

CATESBY:
Call them again, my lord, and accept their suit.

ANOTHER:
Do, good my lord, lest all the land rue it.

GLOUCESTER:
Would you force me into a world of care?
Well, call them again. I am not made of stone,
but penetrable to your kind entreaties,
albeit against my conscience and my soul.
Cousin of Buckingham, and you sage, grave men,
since you will buckle fortune on my back,
to bear her burden, whether I will or not,
I must have patience to endure the load.
But if black scandal or foul-faced reproach
attend the sequel of your imposition,
your mere enforcement shall acquit me
from all the impure blots and stains thereof;
for God knows, and you may partly see,
how far I am from the desire of it.

Lord Mayor:
God bless your grace! We see it, and we will say it.

GLOUCESTER:
In saying so, you shall but say the truth.

BUCKINGHAM:
Then I salute you with this kingly title:
Long live Richard, England's royal king!

Lord Mayor:
Amen.

BUCKINGHAM:
Will it please you to be crowned tomorrow?

GLOUCESTER:
Even when you please, since you will have it so.

BUCKINGHAM:
Tomorrow, then, we will attend your grace.
And so, most joyfully, we take our leave.

GLOUCESTER:
Come, let us go to our holy task again.
Farewell, good cousin; farewell, gentle friends.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Who is this that meets us here? My niece Plantagenet,
led by the hand of her kind aunt of Gloucester?
Now, for my life, she's wandering to the Tower,
out of pure heart's love, to greet the tender princes.
Daughter, well met.

LADY ANNE:
God give both your graces
a happy and a joyful time of day!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
As much to you, good sister! Where are you going?

LADY ANNE:
No farther than the Tower; and, as I guess,
upon the same devotion as yourselves,
to congratulate the gentle princes there.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Kind sister, thanks. We'll enter all together.
And, in good time, here comes the lieutenant.
Master lieutenant, please, by your leave,
how is the prince, and my young son of York?

BRAKENBURY:
Right well, dear madam. With your patience,
I may not allow you to visit them;
the king has strictly charged the contrary.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
The king! Why, who's that?

BRAKENBURY:
I beg your mercy; I mean the lord protector.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
May the Lord protect him from that kingly title!
Has he set bounds between their love and me?
I am their mother; who should keep me from them?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I am their father's mother; I will see them.

LADY ANNE:
Their aunt I am in law, in love their mother.
Then bring me to their sights; I'll bear your blame
and take your office from you, at my peril.

BRAKENBURY:
No, madam, no; I may not leave it so.
I am bound by oath, and therefore pardon me.

LORD STANLEY:
Let me but meet you, ladies, one hour from now,
and I'll salute your grace of York as mother,
and reverend onlooker of two fair queens.
Come, madam, you must go straight to Westminster,
there to be crowned Richard's royal queen.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, cut my lace in sunder, so that my pent-up heart
may have some room to beat, or else I will swoon
with this dead-killing news!

LADY ANNE:
Despiteful news! Oh, unpleasing news!

DORSET:
Be of good cheer. Mother, how is your grace?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, Dorset, don't speak to me, get away from here!
Death and destruction dog you at the heels;
your mother's name is ominous to children.
If you want to outstrip death, go across the seas,
and live with Richmond, out of the reach of hell.
Go, hurry, hurry from this slaughter-house,
lest you increase the number of the dead;
and make me die the slave of Margaret's curse,
not a mother, wife, nor England's counted queen.

LORD STANLEY:
Your counsel, madam, is full of wise care.
Take all the swift advantage of the hours.
You shall have letters from me to my son
to meet you on the way, and welcome you.
Do not be taken tardy by unwise delay.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
O ill-dispersing wind of misery!
O my accursed womb, the bed of death!
A cockatrice you have hatched to the world,
whose unavoidable eye is murderous.

LORD STANLEY:
Come, madam, come; I was sent in all haste.

LADY ANNE:
And I in all unwillingness will go.
I wish to God that the inclusive circlet
of golden metal that must round my brow
were red-hot steel, to sear me to the brain!
Let me be anointed with deadly venom,
and die, before men can say, 'God save the queen!'

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Go, go, poor soul, I do not envy your glory.
To feed my humor, wish yourself no harm.

LADY ANNE:
No! Why? When he that is my husband now
came to me, as I followed Henry's corpse,
when the blood was scarce well washed from his hands
which issued from my other angel husband
and that dead saint which I then followed weeping;
oh, when, I say, I looked on Richard's face,
this was my wish: 'Be thou,' I said, 'accursed,
for making me, so young, so old a widow!
And, when you wed, let sorrow haunt your bed;
and may your wife—if any be so mad—
be as miserable by your life
as you have made me by my dear lord's death!'
Lo, before I can repeat this curse again,
even in so short a space, my woman's heart
grossly grew captive to his honeyed words
and proved the subject of my own soul's curse,
which ever since has kept my eyes from rest;
for never yet one hour in his bed
have I enjoyed the golden dew of sleep,
but have been awakened by his timorous dreams.
Besides, he hates me for my father Warwick;
and will, no doubt, shortly be rid of me.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Poor heart, adieu! I pity your complaining.

LADY ANNE:
No more than from my soul I mourn for yours.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Farewell, you woeful welcomer of glory!

LADY ANNE:
Adieu, poor soul, that takes your leave of it!

DUCHESS OF YORK:

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Stay, yet look back with me to the Tower.
Pity, you ancient stones, those tender babies
whom envy has immured within your walls!
Rough cradle for such little pretty ones!
Rude, ragged nurse, old, sullen playfellow
for tender princes, use my babies well!
So foolish sorrow bids your stones farewell.

KING RICHARD III:
Stand all apart. Cousin of Buckingham!

BUCKINGHAM:
My gracious sovereign?

KING RICHARD III:
Give me your hand.
Thus high, by your advice
and your assistance, is King Richard seated;
but shall we wear these honors for a day?
Or shall they last, and shall we rejoice in them?

BUCKINGHAM:
May they live still and last forever!

KING RICHARD III:
Oh, Buckingham, now I'm playing the touchstone,
to test if you are current gold indeed.
Young Edward lives. Now think what I would say.

BUCKINGHAM:
Say on, my loving lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Why, Buckingham, I say, I would be king.

BUCKINGHAM:
Why, so you are, my thrice renowned liege.

KING RICHARD III:
Ha! Am I king? It is so. But Edward lives.

BUCKINGHAM:
True, noble prince.

KING RICHARD III:
Oh, bitter consequence,
that Edward should still live! 'True, noble prince!'
Cousin, you were not used to being so dull.
Shall I be plain? I wish the bastards were dead,
and I would have it suddenly performed.
What do you say? Speak suddenly; be brief.

BUCKINGHAM:
Your grace may do your pleasure.

KING RICHARD III:
Tut, tut, you are all ice, your kindness freezes.
Say, do I have your consent that they shall die?

BUCKINGHAM:
Give me some breath, some little pause, my lord,
before I positively decide on this.
I will resolve your grace immediately.

CATESBY:

KING RICHARD III:
I will converse with iron-witted fools
and reckless boys. None are for me
that look into me with considerate eyes.
High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect.
Boy!

Page:
My lord?

KING RICHARD III:
Do you not know anyone whom corrupting gold
would tempt to a secret exploit of death?

Page:
My lord, I know a discontented gentleman,
whose humble means do not match his haughty mind.
Gold would be as good as twenty orators,
and will, no doubt, tempt him to anything.

KING RICHARD III:
What is his name?

Page:
His name, my lord, is Tyrrel.

KING RICHARD III:
I partly know the man. Go, call him here.
The deep-revolving, witty Buckingham
shall no more be my neighbor in counsel.
Has he so long held out with me, untired,
and does he stop now for breath?
How now! What news with you?

STANLEY:
My lord, I hear the Marquis Dorset has fled
to Richmond, in those parts beyond the sea
where he resides.

KING RICHARD III:
Catesby!

CATESBY:
My lord?

KING RICHARD III:
Rumor it abroad
that Anne, my wife, is sick and likely to die.
I will take order for her to be kept in confinement.
Inquire for some mean-born gentleman,
whom I will marry straight to Clarence's daughter.
The boy is foolish, and I do not fear him.
Look, how you are dreaming! I say again, give out
that Anne my wife is sick and likely to die.
About it; for it is very important to me,
to stop all hopes whose growth may damage me.
I must be married to my brother's daughter,
or else my kingdom stands on brittle glass.
Murder her brothers, and then marry her!
An uncertain way of gain! But I am in
so far in blood that sin will lead to more sin.
Tear-falling pity does not dwell in this eye.
Is your name Tyrrel?

TYRREL:
James Tyrrel, and your most obedient subject.

KING RICHARD III:
Are you, indeed?

TYRREL:
Test me, my gracious sovereign.

KING RICHARD III:
Do you dare to resolve to kill a friend of mine?

TYRREL:
Yes, my lord;
but I would rather kill two enemies.

KING RICHARD III:
Why, there you have it. Two deep enemies,
foes to my rest and my sweet sleep's disturbers,
are they that I would have you deal with.
Tyrrel, I mean those bastards in the Tower.

TYRREL:
Let me have open means to come to them,
and soon I'll rid you from the fear of them.

KING RICHARD III:
You sing sweet music. Hark, come here, Tyrrel.
Go, by this token. Rise, and lend your ear.
There is no more but so. Say it is done,
and I will love you, and prefer you too.

TYRREL:
It's done, my gracious lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Shall we hear from you, Tyrrel, before we sleep?

TYRREL:
You shall, my Lord.

BUCKINGHAM:
My Lord, I have considered in my mind
the late demand that you did sound me in.

KING RICHARD III:
Well, let that pass. Dorset has fled to Richmond.

BUCKINGHAM:
I hear that news, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Stanley, he is your wife's son. Well, look to it.

BUCKINGHAM:
My lord, I claim your gift, my due by promise,
for which your honor and your faith are pawned;
the earldom of Hereford and the movables
which you promised I should possess.

KING RICHARD III:
Stanley, look to your wife; if she conveys
letters to Richmond, you shall answer for it.

BUCKINGHAM:
What does your highness say to my just demand?

KING RICHARD III:
As I remember, Henry the Sixth
did prophesy that Richmond would be king,
when Richmond was a little peevish boy.
A king, perhaps, perhaps—

BUCKINGHAM:
My lord!

KING RICHARD III:
How is it that the prophet could not at that time
have told me, since I was by, that I should kill him?

BUCKINGHAM:
My lord, your promise for the earldom—

KING RICHARD III:
Richmond! When I was last at Exeter,
the mayor in courtesy showed me the castle,
and called it Rougemont. At which name I started,
because a bard of Ireland told me once
I should not live long after I saw Richmond.

BUCKINGHAM:
My Lord!

KING RICHARD III:
Yes, what time is it?

BUCKINGHAM:
I am thus bold to put your grace in mind
of what you promised me.

KING RICHARD III:
Well, but what's o'clock?

BUCKINGHAM:
Upon the stroke of ten.

KING RICHARD III:
Well, let it strike.

BUCKINGHAM:
Why let it strike?

KING RICHARD III:
Because, like a jack-in-the-box, you keep the stroke
between your begging and my meditation.
I am not in the giving mood today.

BUCKINGHAM:
Why, then tell me whether you will or not.

KING RICHARD III:
Tut, tut,
you are troubling me; I am not in the mood.

BUCKINGHAM:
Is it even so? Does he reward my true service
with such deep contempt? Did I make him king for this?
Oh, let me think on Hastings, and be gone
to Brecknock, while my fearful head is still on!

TYRREL:
The tyrannical and bloody deed is done,
the most arch of piteous massacres
that this land was ever guilty of.
Dighton and Forrest, whom I suborned
to do this ruthless piece of butchery,
although they were hardened villains, bloody dogs,
melting with tenderness and kind compassion,
wept like two children in their deaths' sad stories.
'Lo, thus,' said Dighton, 'lay those tender babes.'
'Thus, thus,' said Forrest, 'girdling one another
within their innocent alabaster arms.
Their lips were four red roses on a stalk,
which in their summer beauty kissed each other.
A book of prayers lay on their pillow;
which once,' said Forrest, 'almost changed my mind;
but oh! The devil'—there the villain stopped
while Dighton thus told on: 'We smothered
the most replenished sweet work of nature,
that from the prime creation she ever framed.'
Thus both are gone with conscience and remorse;
they could not speak; and so I left them both,
to bring this news to the bloody king.
And here he comes.
All hail, my sovereign liege!

KING RICHARD III:
Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in your news?

TYRREL:
If having done the thing you gave in charge
begets your happiness, be happy then,
for it is done, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
But did you see them dead?

TYRREL:
I did, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
And buried, gentle Tyrrel?

TYRREL:
The chaplain of the Tower has buried them;
but how or in what place I do not know.

KING RICHARD III:
Come to me, Tyrrel, soon after supper,
and you shall tell me the process of their death.
In the meantime, just think how I may do you good,
and be the inheritor of your desire.
Farewell till soon.
The son of Clarence I have penned up close;
his daughter I have meanly matched in marriage;
the sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom,
and Anne my wife has bid the world good night.
Now, because I know the Breton Richmond aims
at young Elizabeth, my brother's daughter,
and, by that knot, looks proudly over the crown,
to her I go, a jolly, thriving wooer.

CATESBY:
My lord!

KING RICHARD III:
Good news or bad, that you come in so bluntly?

CATESBY:
Bad news, my lord. Ely has fled to Richmond;
and Buckingham, backed by the hardy Welshmen,
is in the field, and his power still increases.

KING RICHARD III:
Ely with Richmond troubles me more nearly
than Buckingham and his rashly-levied army.
Come, I have heard that fearful commenting
is a leaden servitor to dull delay;
delay leads to impotent and snail-paced beggary.
Then let fiery expedition be my wing,
Jove's Mercury, and a herald for a king!
Come, muster men. My counsel is my shield;
we must be brief when traitors brave the field.

QUEEN MARGARET:
So, now prosperity begins to mellow
and drop into the rotten mouth of death.
Here in these confines I have slyly lurked,
to watch the waning of my adversaries.
A dire induction I am witness to,
and I will go to France, hoping the consequence
will prove as bitter, black, and tragical.
Withdraw, wretched Margaret. Who comes here?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Ah, my young princes! Ah, my tender babies!
My un-blown flowers, new-appearing sweets!
If your gentle souls still fly in the air
and are not fixed in perpetual doom,
hover about me with your airy wings
and hear your mother's lamentation!

QUEEN MARGARET:
Hover about her; say that right for right
has dimmed your infant morning to an aged night.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
So many miseries have crazed my voice,
that my woe-wearied tongue is mute and dumb.
Edward Plantagenet, why are you dead?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Plantagenet requites Plantagenet.
Edward for Edward pays a dying debt.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Will you, O God, flee from such gentle lambs,
and throw them into the entrails of the wolf?
When did you sleep when such a deed was done?

QUEEN MARGARET:
When holy Harry died, and my sweet son.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Blind sight, dead life, poor mortal living ghost,
woe's scene, world's shame, grave's due by a usurped life,
brief abstract and record of tedious days,
rest your unrest on England's lawful earth,
unlawfully made drunk with innocents' blood!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, that you would as well afford a grave
as you can yield a melancholy seat!
Then I would hide my bones, not rest them here.
Oh, who has any cause to mourn but I?

QUEEN MARGARET:
If ancient sorrow is most reverend,
give mine the benefit of seniority,
and let my woes frown from the upper hand.
If sorrow can admit society,
tell over your woes again by viewing mine.
I had an Edward, until a Richard killed him;
I had a Harry, until a Richard killed him.
You had an Edward, until a Richard killed him;
you had a Richard, until a Richard killed him.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I had a Richard too, and you killed him;
I had a Rutland too, you helped to kill him.

QUEEN MARGARET:
You had a Clarence too, and Richard killed him.
From out of the kennel of your womb has crept
a hell-hound that hunts us all to death.
That dog, that had his teeth before his eyes,
to worry lambs and lap their gentle blood,
that foul defacer of God's handiwork,
that excellent grand tyrant of the earth,
that reigns in the galled eyes of weeping souls,
your womb let loose, to chase us to our graves.
O upright, just, and true-disposing God,
how I thank you, that this carnal cur
preys on the issue of his mother's body,
and makes her a companion with others' moan!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
O Harry's wife, do not triumph in my woes!
God is my witness, I have wept for yours.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Bear with me; I am hungry for revenge,
and now I sate myself with beholding it.
Your Edward, he is dead, that stabbed my Edward;
your other Edward is dead, to requite my Edward;
young York is but an addition, because both of them
do not match the high perfection of my loss.
Your Clarence is dead that killed my Edward;
and the beholders of this tragic play,
the adulterous Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, and Grey,
are untimely smothered in their dusky graves.
Richard still lives, hell's black intelligencer,
only reserved as their factor, to buy souls
and send them there. But at hand, at hand,
ensues his piteous and unpitied end.
Earth gapes, hell burns, fiends roar, saints pray,
to have him suddenly conveyed away.
Cancel his bond of life, dear God, I pray,
that I may live to say, 'The dog is dead!'

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, you did prophesy the time would come
that I should wish for you to help me curse
that bottled spider, that foul hunchbacked toad!

QUEEN MARGARET:
I called you then a vain flourish of my fortune;
I called you then a poor shadow, a painted queen;
the presentation of but what I was;
the flattering index of a direful pageant;
one heaved up high, to be hurled down below;
a mother only mocked with two sweet babes;
a dream of what you were, a breath, a bubble,
a sign of dignity, a garish flag,
to be the aim of every dangerous shot,
a queen in jest, only to fill the scene.
Where is your husband now? Where are your brothers?
Where are your children? In what do you find joy?
Who sues to you and cries, 'God save the queen'?
Where are the bending peers that flattered you?
Where are the thronging troops that followed you?
Decline all this, and see what you are now.
For a happy wife, a most distressed widow;
for a joyful mother, one that wails the name;
for a queen, a very wretch crowned with care;
for one being sued to, one that humbly sues;
for one that scorned me, now scorned by me;
for one being feared by all, now fearing one;
for one commanding all, obeyed by none.
Thus has the course of justice wheeled about,
and left you but a very prey to time;
having no more but the thought of what you were,
to torture you the more, being what you are.
You usurped my place, and do you not
usurp the just proportion of my sorrow?
Now your proud neck bears half my burdened yoke,
from which even here I slip my weary neck,
and leave the burden of it all on you.
Farewell, York's wife, and queen of sad misfortune.
These English woes will make me smile in France.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh, you who are well skilled in curses, stay a while,
and teach me how to curse my enemies!

QUEEN MARGARET:
Forbear to sleep at night, and fast during the day;
compare dead happiness with living woe;
think that your babies were fairer than they were,
and that he that killed them was fouler than he is.
Bettering your loss makes the bad cause worse.
Revolving this will teach you how to curse.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
My words are dull; oh, quicken them with yours!

QUEEN MARGARET:
Your woes will make them sharp, and pierce like mine.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Why should calamity be full of words?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Windy attorneys to their client woes,
airy successors of intestate joys,
poor breathing orators of miseries!
Let them have scope. Though what they impart
doesn't help at all, yet it does ease the heart.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
If so, then don't be tongue-tied. Go with me,
and in the breath of bitter words, let's smother
my damned son, who smothered your two sweet sons.
I hear his drum. Be copious in your exclamations.

KING RICHARD III:
Who is intercepting my expedition?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Oh, she that might have intercepted you,
by strangling you in her accursed womb
from all the slaughters, wretch, that you have done!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Do you hide that forehead with a golden crown,
where should be engraved, if right were right,
the slaughter of the prince that owned that crown,
and the dire death of my two sons and brothers?
Tell me, you villain slave, where are my children?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
You toad, you toad, where is your brother Clarence?
And little Ned Plantagenet, his son?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Where is kind Hastings, Rivers, Vaughan, and Grey?

KING RICHARD III:
A flourish, trumpets! Strike the alarm, drums!
Let not the heavens hear these tell-tale women
rail on the Lord's anointed. Strike, I say!
Either be patient, and entreat me fairly,
or with the clamorous report of war
thus will I drown your exclamations.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Are you my son?

KING RICHARD III:
Yes, I thank God, my father, and yourself.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Then patiently hear my impatience.

KING RICHARD III:
Madam, I have a touch of your condition,
which cannot brook the accent of reproof.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Oh, let me speak!

KING RICHARD III:
Do then, but I'll not hear.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I will be mild and gentle in my speech.

KING RICHARD III:
And brief, good mother; for I am in a haste.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Are you so hasty? I have waited for you,
God knows, in anguish, pain, and agony.

KING RICHARD III:
And did I not come at last to comfort you?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
No, by the holy rood, you know it well,
you came on earth to make the earth my hell.
A grievous burden was your birth to me;
tetchy and wayward was your infancy;
your school-days were frightful, desperate, wild, and furious,
your prime of manhood was daring, bold, and venturous,
your age confirmed, proud, subdued, bloody,
treacherous,
more mild, but yet more harmful, kind in hatred.
What comfortable hour can you name,
that ever graced me in your company?

KING RICHARD III:
Faith, none, but Humphrey Hour, that called
your grace to breakfast once out of my company.
If I am so disgraceful in your sight,
let me march on, and not offend your grace.
Strike the drum.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I beg you, hear me speak.

KING RICHARD III:
You speak too bitterly.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Hear me a word;
for I shall never speak to you again.

KING RICHARD III:
So.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Either you will die, by God's just ordinance,
before you return a conqueror from this war,
or I with grief and extreme age shall perish
and never look upon your face again.
Therefore, take with you my most heavy curse;
which, in the day of battle, will tire you more
than all the complete armor that you wear!
My prayers will fight on the adverse party;
and there the little souls of Edward's children
will whisper to the spirits of your enemies
and promise them success and victory.
Bloody you are, and bloody will be your end;
shame serves your life and attends your death.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Though I have far more cause, yet much less spirit to curse
abides in me. I say amen to all.

KING RICHARD III:
Stay, madam; I must speak a word with you.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I have no more sons of the royal blood
for you to murder. As for my daughters, Richard,
they shall be praying nuns, not weeping queens;
and therefore, do not aim to take their lives.

KING RICHARD III:
You have a daughter named Elizabeth,
virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
And must she die for this? Oh, let her live,
and I'll corrupt her manners, stain her beauty;
slander myself as false to Edward's bed;
throw over her the veil of infamy.
So she may live unscathed by bleeding slaughter,
I will confess she was not Edward's daughter.

KING RICHARD III:
Do not wrong her birth, she is of royal blood.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
To save her life, I'll say she is not so.

KING RICHARD III:
Herlife is only safest in her birth.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
And only in that safety did her brothers die.

KING RICHARD III:
Lo, at their births, good stars were opposite.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
No, in their lives, bad friends were contrary.

KING RICHARD III:
All that is unavoidable is the doom of destiny.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
True, when avoided grace makes destiny.
My babies were destined for a fairer death,
if grace had blessed you with a fairer life.

KING RICHARD III:
You speak as if I had slain my cousins.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Cousins, indeed; and by their uncle cheated
of comfort, kingdom, kindred, freedom, and life.
Whosever hand lanced their tender hearts,
your head, all indirectly, gave the direction.
No doubt the murderous knife was dull and blunt
until it was whetted on your stone-hard heart,
to revel in the entrails of my lambs.
But that the constant use of grief makes wild grief tame,
my tongue would not name my boys to your ears
until my nails were anchored in your eyes;
and I, in such a desperate bay of death,
like a poor bark, bereft of sails and tackling,
would rush all to pieces on your rocky bosom.

KING RICHARD III:
Madam, so may I thrive in my enterprise
and the dangerous success of bloody wars,
as I intend more good to you and yours,
than you or yours were ever wronged by me!

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
What good is covered with the face of heaven,
to be discovered, that can do me good?

KING RICHARD III:
The advancement of your children, gentle lady.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Up to some scaffold, there to lose their heads?

KING RICHARD III:
No, to the dignity and height of honor,
the high imperial type of this earth's glory.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Flatter my sorrows with a report of it;
tell me what state, what dignity, what honor,
can you devise for any child of mine?

KING RICHARD III:
Even all I have; yes, and myself and all,
I will endow a child of yours with;
so that in the Lethe of your angry soul
you may drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs
which you suppose I have done to you.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Be brief, lest the process of your kindness
last longer in the telling than the date of your kindness.

KING RICHARD III:
Then know, that from my soul I love your daughter.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
My daughter's mother thinks so with her soul.

KING RICHARD III:
What do you think?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
That you love my daughter from your soul;
so from your soul's love you loved her brothers;
and from my heart's love I thank you for it.

KING RICHARD III:
Don't be so hasty to misunderstand my meaning.
I mean, that with my soul I love your daughter,
and I mean to make her queen of England.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Say then, who do you mean shall be her king?

KING RICHARD III:
Even he that makes her queen. Who else should it be?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
What, you?

KING RICHARD III:
I, even I. What do you think of it, madam?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
How can you woo her?

KING RICHARD III:
That I would learn from you,
as one that is best acquainted with her humor.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
And will you learn from me?

KING RICHARD III:
Madam, with all my heart.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Send to her, by the man that killed her brothers,
a pair of bleeding hearts; on them engrave
Edward and York. Then perhaps she will weep.
Therefore, present to her—as Margaret once
did to your father, steeped in Rutland's blood—
a handkerchief; which, say to her, did drain
the purple sap from her sweet brother's body,
and bid her dry her weeping eyes with it.
If this inducement does not force her to love,
send her a story of your noble acts;
tell her you made away with her uncle Clarence,
her uncle Rivers; yes, and, for her sake,
made quick conveyance with her good aunt Anne.

KING RICHARD III:
Come, come, you're mocking me; this is not the way
to win your daughter.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
There is no other way,
unless you could put on some other shape,
and not be Richard that has done all this.

KING RICHARD III:
Say that I did all this for love of her.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
No, then indeed she cannot choose but hate you,
having bought love with such a bloody spoil.

KING RICHARD III:
Look, what is done cannot be amended now.
Men shall deal unadvisedly sometimes,
which after hours give leisure to repent.
If I did take the kingdom from your sons,
to make amends, I'll give it to your daughter.
If I have killed the issue of your womb,
to quicken your increase, I will beget
my issue of your blood upon your daughter.
A grandmother's name is little less in love
than the doting title of a mother;
they are as children but one step below,
even of your mettle, of your very blood;
of one pain, save for a night of groans
endured by her, for whom you bid like sorrow.
Your children were a vexation to your youth,
but mine shall be a comfort to your age.
The loss you have is but a son being king,
and by that loss your daughter is made queen.
I cannot make you the amends I would,
therefore accept such kindness as I can.
Your son Dorset, that with a fearful soul
leads discontented steps in foreign soil,
this fair alliance will quickly call him home
to high promotions and great dignity.
The king, that calls your beautiful daughter wife,
shall familiarly call your Dorset brother.
Again you shall be mother to a king,
and all the ruins of distressful times
repaired with double riches of content.
What! We have many goodly days to see.
The liquid drops of tears that you have shed
shall come again, transformed to orient pearl,
advantaging their loan with interest
of ten times double the gain of happiness.
Go, then my mother, to your daughter go.
Make bold her bashful years with your experience;
prepare her ears to hear a wooer's tale.
Put in her tender heart the aspiring flame
of golden sovereignty; acquaint the princess
with the sweet, silent hours of marriage joys.
And when this arm of mine has chastised
the petty rebel, dull-brained Buckingham,
bound with triumphant garlands I will come
and lead your daughter to a conqueror's bed;
to whom I will retail my conquest won,
and she shall be the sole victress, Caesar's Caesar.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
What would be best for me to say? That her father's brother
would be her lord? Or shall I say, her uncle?
Or, he that slew her brothers and her uncles?
Under what title shall I woo for you,
that God, the law, my honor, and her love,
can make seem pleasing to her tender years?

KING RICHARD III:
Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Which she shall purchase with still-lasting war.

KING RICHARD III:
Say that the king, who may command, entreats.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
That at her hands which the King of kings forbids.

KING RICHARD III:
Say, she shall be a high and mighty queen.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
To wail the tide, as her mother does.

KING RICHARD III:
Say, I will love her everlastingly.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
But how long shall that title 'ever' last?

KING RICHARD III:
Sweetly in force until her fair life's end.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
But how long, fairly, shall her sweet life last?

KING RICHARD III:
As long as heaven and nature lengthen it.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
As long as hell and Richard like of it.

KING RICHARD III:
Say, I, her sovereign, am her subject love.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
But she, your subject, loathes such sovereignty.

KING RICHARD III:
Be eloquent on my behalf to her.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
An honest tale speeds best when plainly told.

KING RICHARD III:
Then in plain terms tell her my loving tale.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Plain and not honest is too harsh a style.

KING RICHARD III:
Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Oh no, my reasons are too deep and dead;
too deep and dead, poor infants, in their grave.

KING RICHARD III:
Don't harp on that string, madam; that is past.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I shall harp on it still until my heart-strings break.

KING RICHARD III:
Now, by my George, my garter, and my crown—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Profaned, dishonored, and the third usurped.

KING RICHARD III:
I swear—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
By nothing; for this is no oath.
The George, profaned, has lost his holy honor;
the garter, blemished, has pawned its knightly virtue;
the crown, usurped, has disgraced its kingly glory.
If you will swear by something to be believed,
swear then by something that you have not wronged.

KING RICHARD III:
Now, by the world—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
It is full of your foul wrongs.

KING RICHARD III:
My father's death—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Your life has dishonored that.

KING RICHARD III:
Then, by myself—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Yourself you misuse.

KING RICHARD III:
Why then, by God—

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
God's wrong is the most of all.
If you had feared to break an oath by Him,
the unity the king your brother made
would not have been broken, nor my brother slain.
If you had feared to break an oath by Him,
the imperial metal, now circling your brow,
would have graced the tender temples of my child,
and both the princes would have been breathing here,
which now, two tender playfellows to dust,
your broken faith has made a prey for worms.
What can you swear by now?

KING RICHARD III:
The time to come.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
That you have wronged in the time overpast;
for I myself have many tears to wash
hereafter, for time past wronged by you.
The children live, whose parents you have
slaughtered,
ungoverned youth, to wail it in their age.
The parents live, whose children you have butchered,
old, withered plants, to wail it with their age.
Do not swear by the time to come; for you have
misused it before it was used, by the time misused in the past.

KING RICHARD III:
As I intend to prosper and repent,
so may I thrive in my dangerous attempt
of hostile arms! May I confound myself!
May heaven and fortune bar me from happy hours!
May day not yield me its light, nor night its rest!
May all planets of good luck be opposite
to my proceedings, if, with a pure heart's love,
immaculate devotion, and holy thoughts,
I do not tender your beautiful, princely daughter!
In her consists my happiness and yours;
without her, death, desolation, ruin, and decay
follow this land and me,
you, herself, and many a Christian soul.
It cannot be avoided but by this;
it will not be avoided but by this.
Therefore, good mother—I must call you so—
be the attorney of my love to her.
Plead what I will be, not what I have been;
not my deserts, but what I will deserve.
Urge the necessity and state of the times,
and do not be childishly foolish in great designs.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Shall I be tempted by the devil thus?

KING RICHARD III:
Yes, if the devil tempts you to do good.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Shall I forget myself to be myself?

KING RICHARD III:
Yes, if your remembrance of yourself wrongs you.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
But you did kill my children.

KING RICHARD III:
But in your daughter's womb I will bury them,
where in that nest of spicery they shall breed
selves of themselves, to your comfort.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Shall I go win my daughter to your will?

KING RICHARD III:
And be a happy mother by the deed.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I'm going. Write to me very shortly,
and you shall understand her mind from me.

KING RICHARD III:
Bear her my true love's kiss; and so, farewell.
Relenting fool, and shallow, changing woman!
How now! What news?

RATCLIFF:
My gracious sovereign, on the western coast
a powerful navy is riding; to the shore
throng many doubtful, hollow-hearted friends,
unarmed, and unresolved to beat them back.
It's thought that Richmond is their admiral;
and there they wait, expecting but the aid
of Buckingham to welcome them ashore.

KING RICHARD III:
Some light-footed friend, post to the Duke of Norfolk.
Ratcliff, yourself, or Catesby; where is he?

CATESBY:
Here, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Fly to the duke.
Go to Salisbury.
When you get there—
Dull, unmindful villain,
why are you standing still, and not going to the duke?

CATESBY:
First, mighty sovereign, let me know your mind,
what I shall deliver to him from your grace.

KING RICHARD III:
Oh, true, good Catesby. Bid him levy straight
the greatest strength and power he can make,
and meet me presently at Salisbury.

CATESBY:
I go.

RATCLIFF:
What is it your highness's pleasure I should do at
Salisbury?

KING RICHARD III:
Why, what would you do there before I go?

RATCLIFF:
Your highness told me I should post ahead.

KING RICHARD III:
My mind has changed, sir, my mind has changed.
How now, what news with you?

STANLEY:
None good, my lord, to please you with the hearing;
nor none so bad, but it may well be told.

KING RICHARD III:
Well now, a riddle! Neither good nor bad!
Why do you run so many miles about,
when you could tell your tale a nearer way?
Once more, what news?

STANLEY:
Richmond is on the seas.

KING RICHARD III:
There let him sink, and may the seas be on him!
White-livered runaway, what is he doing there?

STANLEY:
I don't know, mighty sovereign, but by guess.

KING RICHARD III:
Well, sir, as you guess, as you guess?

STANLEY:
Stirred up by Dorset, Buckingham, and Ely,
he is making for England, to claim the crown there.

KING RICHARD III:
Is the chair empty? Is the sword un-swayed?
Is the king dead? The empire un-possessed?
What heir of York is there alive but us?
And who is England's king but great York's heir?
Then, tell me, what is he doing upon the sea?

STANLEY:
Unless for that, my liege, I cannot guess.

KING RICHARD III:
Unless he comes for that to be your liege,
you cannot guess why the Welshman comes.
You will revolt, and flee to him, I fear.

STANLEY:
No, mighty liege; therefore, do not mistrust me.

KING RICHARD III:
Where is your power, then, to beat him back?
Where are your tenants and your followers?
Are they not now upon the western shore,
safely conducting the rebels from their ships?

STANLEY:
No, my good lord, my friends are in the north.

KING RICHARD III:
Cold friends to Richard. What are they doing in the north,
when they should be serving their sovereign in the west?

STANLEY:
They have not been commanded, mighty sovereign.
If it please your majesty to give me leave,
I'll muster up my friends, and meet your grace
where and at what time your majesty shall please.

KING RICHARD III:
Yes, yes, you would be gone to join with Richmond.
I will not trust you, sir.

STANLEY:
Most mighty sovereign,
you have no cause to hold my friendship in doubt.
I never was nor ever will be false.

KING RICHARD III:
Well,
go and muster your men; but, hear you, leave behind
your son, George Stanley. Make sure your faith is firm,
or else his head's assurance is but frail.

STANLEY:
So deal with him as I prove true to you.

Messenger:
My gracious sovereign, now in Devonshire,
as I am well advertised by friends,
Sir Edward Courtney, and the haughty prelate
the Bishop of Exeter, his brother there,
with many more confederates, are in arms.

Second Messenger:
My liege, in Kent the Guildfords are in arms;
and every hour more competitors
flock to their aid, and their power still increases.

Third Messenger:
My lord, the army of the Duke of Buckingham—

KING RICHARD III:
Out on you, owls! Nothing but songs of death?
Take that, until you bring me better news.

Third Messenger:
The news I have to tell your majesty
is, that by sudden floods and the fall of waters,
Buckingham's army is dispersed and scattered;
and he himself has wandered away alone,
no man knows where.

KING RICHARD III:
I beg your mercy.
There is my purse to cure that blow of yours.
Has any well-advised friend proclaimed
a reward to him that brings the traitor in?

Third Messenger:
Such a proclamation has been made, my liege.

Fourth Messenger:
Sir Thomas Lovel and Lord Marquis Dorset,
it is said, my liege, are in arms in Yorkshire.
Yet this good comfort I bring to your grace,
the Breton navy is dispersed by a tempest.
Richmond, in Yorkshire, sent out a boat
to the shore, to ask those on the banks
if they were his assistants, yes or no;
who answered him, they came from Buckingham
on his side. He, mistrusting them,
hoisted sail and made away for Brittany.

KING RICHARD III:
March on, march on, since we are up in arms;
if not to fight with foreign enemies,
yet to beat down these rebels here at home.

CATESBY:
My liege, the Duke of Buckingham is taken;
that is the best news. That the Earl of Richmond
has landed at Milford with a mighty power,
is colder news, yet it must be told.

KING RICHARD III:
Away towards Salisbury! While we reason here,
a royal battle might be won and lost.
Someone take order for Buckingham to be brought
to Salisbury; the rest march on with me.

DERBY:
Sir Christopher, tell Richmond this from me:
that in the sty of this most bloody boar,
my son George Stanley is cooped up in hold.
If I revolt, off goes young George's head;
the fear of that withholds my present aid.
But, tell me, where is princely Richmond now?

CHRISTOPHER:
At Pembroke, or at Haverfordwest, in Wales.

DERBY:
What men of name are resorting to him?

CHRISTOPHER:
Sir Walter Herbert, a renowned soldier;
Sir Gilbert Talbot, Sir William Stanley;
Oxford, the redoubted Pembroke, Sir James Blunt,
and Rhys ap Thomas with a valiant crew;
and many more of noble fame and worth.
And they are bending their course towards London,
if they are not fought with along the way.

DERBY:
Return to your lord; commend me to him.
Tell him the queen has heartily consented
that he shall marry Elizabeth her daughter.
These letters will resolve him of my mind. Farewell.

BUCKINGHAM:
Will not King Richard let me speak with him?

Sheriff:
No, my good lord; therefore be patient.

BUCKINGHAM:
Hastings, and Edward's children, Rivers, Grey,
Holy King Henry, and your fair son Edward,
Vaughan, and all that have miscarried
by underhand, corrupted, foul injustice,
if your moody, discontented souls
do through the clouds behold this present hour,
even for revenge mock my destruction!
This is All-Souls' day, fellows, is it not?

Sheriff:
It is, my lord.

BUCKINGHAM:
Why, then All-Souls' day is my body's doomsday.
This is the day that, in King Edward's time,
I wished might fall on me, when I was found
false to his children or his wife's allies.
This is the day in which I wished to fall
by the false faith of him I trusted most.
This, this All-Souls' day to my fearful soul
is the determined respite of my wrongs.
That high All-Seer that I dallied with
has turned my feigned prayer on my head
and given in earnest what I begged in jest.
Thus he forces the swords of wicked men
to turn their own points on their masters' bosoms.
Now Margaret's curse has fallen upon my head;
'When he,' she said, 'shall split your heart with sorrow,
remember Margaret was a prophetess.'
Come, sirs, convey me to the block of shame;
wrong has but wrong, and blame the due of blame.

RICHMOND:
Fellows in arms, and my most loving friends,
bruised underneath the yoke of tyranny,
thus far into the bowels of the land
we have marched on without impediment;
and here we receive from our father Stanley
lines of fair comfort and encouragement.
The wretched, bloody, and usurping boar,
that spoiled your summer fields and fruitful vines,
swills your warm blood like wash, and makes his trough
in your emboweled bosoms, this foul swine
lies now even in the center of this isle,
near to the town of Leicester, as we learn.
From Tamworth to there is but one day's march.
In God's name, cheerly on, courageous friends,
to reap the harvest of perpetual peace
by this one bloody trial of sharp war.

OXFORD:
Every man's conscience is a thousand swords,
to fight against that bloody homicide.

HERBERT:
I have no doubt that his friends will flee to us.

BLUNT:
He has no friends but those who are friends for fear,
who in his greatest need will shrink from him.

RICHMOND:
All for our advantage. Then, in God's name, march.
True hope is swift, and flies with a swallow's wings;
it makes kings gods, and meaner creatures kings.

KING RICHARD III:
Here we will pitch our tents, right here in Bosworth field.
My Lord of Surrey, why do you look so sad?

SURREY:
My heart is ten times lighter than my looks.

KING RICHARD III:
My Lord of Norfolk,--

NORFOLK:
Here, most gracious liege.

KING RICHARD III:
Norfolk, we must have some fighting, eh? Mustn't we?

NORFOLK:
We must both give and take, my gracious lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Up with my tent there! Here I will lie tonight;
but where tomorrow? Well, it's all the same for that.
Who has seen the number of the enemy?

NORFOLK:
Six or seven thousand is their utmost power.

KING RICHARD III:
Why, our battalion is three times that number.
Besides, the king's name is a tower of strength,
which they on the adverse side lack.
Up with my tent there! Valiant gentlemen,
let us survey the advantage of the field.
Call for some men of sound direction.
Let's not lack discipline, and make no delay,
for, lords, tomorrow is a busy day.

RICHMOND:
The weary sun has made a golden set,
and by the bright track of his fiery chariot,
he gives a signal of a goodly day tomorrow.
Sir William Brandon, you shall bear my standard.
Give me some ink and paper in my tent.
I'll draw the form and model of our battle,
limit each leader to his several charge,
and part our small strength in just proportion.
My Lord of Oxford, you, Sir William Brandon,
and you, Sir Walter Herbert, stay with me.
The Earl of Pembroke keeps his regiment.
Good Captain Blunt, bear my good night to him,
and by the second hour in the morning
desire the earl to see me in my tent.
Yet one thing more, good Blunt, before you go,
where is Lord Stanley quartered, do you know?

BLUNT:
Unless I have mistaken his colors much,
which I am well assured I have not done,
his regiment lies half a mile at least
south from the mighty power of the king.

RICHMOND:
If it is possible without peril,
good Captain Blunt, bear my good-night to him,
and give him this most needful scroll from me.

BLUNT:
Upon my life, my lord, I'll undertake it;
and so, God give you quiet rest tonight!

RICHMOND:
Good night, good Captain Blunt. Come, gentlemen,
let us consult upon tomorrow's business
in our tent; the air is raw and cold.

KING RICHARD III:
What time is it?

CATESBY:
It's supper-time, my lord;
it's nine o'clock.

KING RICHARD III:
I will not sup tonight.
Give me some ink and paper.
What, is my helmet easier than it was?
And all my armor laid out in my tent?

CATESBY:
It is, my liege; and all things are in readiness.

KING RICHARD III:
Good Norfolk, go to your post;
use careful watch, choose trusty sentinels.

NORFOLK:
I go, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Stir with the lark tomorrow, gentle Norfolk.

NORFOLK:
I guarantee it, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Catesby!

CATESBY:
My lord?

KING RICHARD III:
Send out a pursuivant at arms
to Stanley's regiment; tell him to bring his force
before sunrise, lest his son George fall
into the blind cave of eternal night.
Fill me a bowl of wine. Give me a watch.
Saddle white Surrey for the field tomorrow.
Look that my staves are sound, and not too heavy.
Ratcliff!

RATCLIFF:
My lord?

KING RICHARD III:
Did you see the melancholy Lord Northumberland?

RATCLIFF:
Thomas the Earl of Surrey, and himself,
much about dusk, from troop to troop
went through the army, cheering up the soldiers.

KING RICHARD III:
So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine.
I do not have that alacrity of spirit,
nor cheer of mind, that I was wont to have.
Set it down. Is the ink and paper ready?

RATCLIFF:
It is, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Tell my guard to watch; leave me.
Ratcliff, about the middle of the night, come to my tent
and help me to arm myself. Leave me, I say.

DERBY:
May fortune and victory sit on your helm!

RICHMOND:
May all the comfort that the dark night can afford
be to your person, noble father-in-law!
Tell me, how is our loving mother?

DERBY:
I, by proxy, bless you from your mother,
who prays continually for Richmond's good.
So much for that. The silent hours steal on,
and flaky darkness breaks in the east.
In brief—for so the season bids us be—
prepare your battle early in the morning,
and put your fortune to the test
of bloody strokes and mortal-staring war.
I, as I may—that which I would, I cannot—
with the best advantage, will deceive the time,
and aid you in this doubtful shock of arms.
But on your side, I may not be too forward,
lest, being seen, your brother, the tender George,
be executed in his father's sight.
Farewell. The leisure and the fearful time
cut off the ceremonious vows of love
and the ample interchange of sweet discourse,
which so long-sundered friends should dwell upon.
God give us leisure for these rites of love!
Once more, adieu. Be valiant, and speed well!

RICHMOND:
Good lords, conduct him to his regiment.
I'll strive, with troubled thoughts, to take a nap,
lest leaden slumber weigh me down tomorrow,
when I should mount with wings of victory.
Once more, good night, kind lords and gentlemen.
O You, whose captain I account myself,
look on my forces with a gracious eye;
put in their hands your bruising irons of wrath,
that they may crush down with a heavy fall
the usurping helmets of our adversaries!
Make us your ministers of chastisement,
that we may praise you in the victory!
To you I commend my watchful soul,
before I let fall the windows of my eyes.
Sleeping and waking, O, defend me still!

Ghost of Prince Edward:

Ghost of King Henry VI:

Ghost of CLARENCE:

Ghost of RIVERS:

Ghost of GREY:

Ghost of VAUGHAN:

All:

Ghost of HASTINGS:

Ghosts of young Princes:

Ghost of LADY ANNE:

Ghost of BUCKINGHAM:

KING RICHARD III:
Give me another horse. Bind up my wounds.
Have mercy, Jesus!—Wait! I was just dreaming.
Oh, coward conscience, how you afflict me!
The lights are burning blue. It is now dead midnight.
Cold, fearful drops stand on my trembling flesh.
What do I fear? Myself? There's no one else by.
Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I.
Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am.
Then flee. What, from myself? Great reason why:
lest I take revenge. What, myself upon myself?
Alas. I love myself. For what reason? For any good
that I myself have done to myself?
Oh, no! Alas, I rather hate myself
for the hateful deeds I have committed myself!
I am a villain. Yet I lie. I am not.
Fool, speak well of yourself. Fool, do not flatter.
My conscience has a thousand different tongues,
and every tongue brings in a different tale,
and every tale condemns me for a villain.
Perjury, perjury, in the highest degree;
murder, stern murder, in the direst degree;
all several sins, all used in each degree,
throng to the bar, all crying, 'Guilty! Guilty!'
I shall despair. There is no creature that loves me;
and if I die, no soul shall pity me.
No, why should they, since I myself
find no pity in myself for myself?
I thought the souls of all that I had murdered
came to my tent; and every one did threaten
tomorrow's vengeance on the head of Richard.

RATCLIFF:
My lord!

KING RICHARD III:
Zounds! Who is there?

RATCLIFF:
Ratcliff, my lord; it's I. The early village cock
has twice done salutation to the morn;
your friends are up, and are buckling on their armor.

KING RICHARD III:
Oh, Ratcliff, I have dreamed a fearful dream!
What do you think, will our friends all prove true?

RATCLIFF:
No doubt, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Oh, Ratcliff, I fear, I fear—

RATCLIFF:
No, good my lord, don't be afraid of shadows.

KING RICHARD III:
By the apostle Paul, shadows tonight
have struck more terror to the soul of Richard
than can the substance of ten thousand soldiers
armed in proof, and led by shallow Richmond.
It is not yet near day. Come, go with me;
under our tents I'll play the eavesdropper,
to see if any mean to shrink from me.

LORDS:
Good morrow, Richmond!

RICHMOND:
I beg your pardon, lords and watchful gentlemen,
that you have caught a tardy sluggard here.

LORDS:
How have you slept, my lord?

RICHMOND:
The sweetest sleep, and the fairest-boding dreams
that ever entered a drowsy head,
I have had since your departure, my lords.
I thought that the souls of those whose bodies Richard murdered,
came to my tent, and cried for victory.
I promise you, my soul is very jocund
in the remembrance of so fair a dream.
How far into the morning is it, lords?

LORDS:
Upon the stroke of four.

RICHMOND:
Why, then it's time to arm and give direction.
More than I have said, loving countrymen,
the leisure and enforcement of the time
forbids me to dwell upon. Yet remember this,
God and our good cause fight on our side;
the prayers of holy saints and wronged souls,
like high-reared bulwarks, stand before our faces.
Except for Richard, those whom we fight against
would rather have us win than him they follow.
For what is he they follow? Truly, gentlemen,
a bloody tyrant and a homicide;
one raised in blood, and one established in blood;
one that made means to come by what he has,
and slaughtered those that were the means to help him;
a base, foul stone, made precious by the foil
of England's chair, where he is falsely set;
one that has ever been God's enemy.
Then, if you fight against God's enemy,
God will in justice protect you as his soldiers.
If you sweat to put a tyrant down,
you will sleep in peace, the tyrant being slain.
If you fight against your country's foes,
your country's fat shall pay your pains the hire.
If you fight in safeguard of your wives,
your wives shall welcome home the conquerors.
If you free your children from the sword,
your children's children will repay you in your old age.
Then, in the name of God and all these rights,
advance your standards, draw your willing swords.
For me, the ransom of my bold attempt
shall be this cold corpse on the earth's cold face;
but if I thrive, the gain of my attempt
the least of you shall share his part of it.
Sound drums and trumpets boldly and cheerfully;
God and Saint George! Richmond and victory!

KING RICHARD III:
What did Northumberland say about Richmond?

RATCLIFF:
That he was never trained up in arms.

KING RICHARD III:
He said the truth. And what did Surrey say then?

RATCLIFF:
He smiled and said, 'The better for our purpose.'

KING RICHARD III:
He was in the right; and so indeed it is.
Ten o'clock there. Give me a calendar.
Who saw the sun today?

RATCLIFF:
Not I, my lord.

KING RICHARD III:
Then he disdains to shine; for by the book
he should have braved the east an hour ago.
It will be a black day for somebody. Ratcliff!

RATCLIFF:
My lord?

KING RICHARD III:
The sun will not be seen today;
the sky does frown and lour upon our army.
I wish these dewy tears were from the ground.
Not shine today! Why, what is that to me
more than to Richmond? For the same heaven
that frowns on me looks sadly upon him.

NORFOLK:
Arm, arm, my lord; the foe is boasting in the field.

KING RICHARD III:
Come, bustle, bustle; get my horse ready.
Call up Lord Stanley, bid him bring his power.
I will lead my soldiers out to the plain,
and thus my battle shall be ordered:
my vanguard shall be drawn out in a long line,
consisting equally of horse and foot;
our archers shall be placed in the middle.
John Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Earl of Surrey,
shall have the leading of this foot and horse.
They thus directed, we will follow
