in the main battle, whose power on either side
shall be well-winged with our chiefest horse.
This, and Saint George to boot! What do you think, Norfolk?

NORFOLK:
A good direction, warlike sovereign.
I found this on my tent this morning.

KING RICHARD III:

Messenger:
My lord, he denies to come.

KING RICHARD III:
Off with his son George's head!

NORFOLK:
My lord, the enemy has passed the marsh.
After the battle, let George Stanley die.

KING RICHARD III:
A thousand hearts are great within my bosom.
Advance our standards, set upon our foes.
Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George,
inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons!
Upon them! Victory sits on our helms.

CATESBY:
Rescue, my Lord of Norfolk, rescue, rescue!
The king is performing more wonders than a man,
daring to face every danger.
His horse is slain, and he fights all on foot,
seeking for Richmond in the throat of death.
Rescue, fair lord, or else the day is lost!

KING RICHARD III:
A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!

CATESBY:
Withdraw, my lord; I'll help you to a horse.

KING RICHARD III:
Slave, I have set my life upon a cast,
and I will stand the hazard of the die.
I think there are six Richmonds in the field;
I have slain five today instead of him.
A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!

RICHMOND:
God and your arms be praised, victorious friends,
the day is ours, the bloody dog is dead.

DERBY:
Courageous Richmond, you have acquitted yourself well.
Lo, here, this long-usurped royalty
from the dead temples of this bloody wretch
I have plucked off, to grace your brows withal.
Wear it, enjoy it, and make much of it.

RICHMOND:
Great God of heaven, say Amen to all!
But, tell me, is young George Stanley living?

DERBY:
He is, my lord, and safe in Leicester town;
where, if it please you, we may now withdraw.

RICHMOND:
What men of name are slain on either side?

DERBY:
John Duke of Norfolk, Walter Lord Ferrers,
Sir Robert Brakenbury, and Sir William Brandon.

RICHMOND:
Inter their bodies as befits their births.
Proclaim a pardon to the soldiers who have fled
that will return to us in submission.
And then, as we have taken the sacrament,
we will unite the white rose and the red.
May heaven smile upon this fair conjunction,
that has long frowned upon their enmity!
What traitor hears me, and does not say amen?
England has long been mad, and scarred herself;
the brother blindly shed the brother's blood,
the father rashly slaughtered his own son,
the son, compelled, has been a butcher to the sire.
All this divided York and Lancaster,
divided in their dire division.
Oh, now, let Richmond and Elizabeth,
the true successors of each royal house,
by God's fair ordinance, conjoin together!
And may their heirs, God, if it is your will,
enrich the time to come with smooth-faced peace,
with smiling plenty and fair prosperous days!
Abate the edge of traitors, gracious Lord,
that would reduce these bloody days again,
and make poor England weep in streams of blood!
Let them not live to taste this land's increase
that would with treason wound this fair land's peace!
Now civil wounds are stopped, peace lives again.
That she may long live here, God say amen!

KING RICHARD II:
Old John of Gaunt, time-honored Lancaster,
have you, according to your oath and bond,
brought here Henry Hereford, your bold son,
here to make good the boisterous late appeal,
which then our leisure would not let us hear,
against the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
I have, my liege.

KING RICHARD II:
Tell me, moreover, have you sounded him out,
if he appeals the duke on ancient malice,
or worthily, as a good subject should,
on some known ground of treachery in him?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
As near as I could sift him on that argument,
it's for some apparent danger he's seen in him,
aimed at your highness, not an ingrained malice.

KING RICHARD II:
Then call them to our presence; face to face,
and frowning brow to brow, we ourselves will hear
the accuser and the accused speak freely.
They are both high-stomached and full of anger,
in a rage as deaf as the sea, as hasty as fire.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
May many years of happy days befall
my gracious sovereign, my most loving liege!

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
May each day be better than the last,
until the heavens, envying earth's good fortune,
add an immortal title to your crown!

KING RICHARD II:
We thank you both. Yet one of you but flatters us,
as well appears by the cause you have come for;
namely, to accuse each other of high treason.
Cousin of Hereford, what do you object
against the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
First, may heaven be the record of my speech!
In the devotion of a subject's love,
tendering the precious safety of my prince,
and free from other misbegotten hate,
I come as an appellant to this princely presence.
Now, Thomas Mowbray, I turn to you,
and mark my greeting well; for what I speak
my body shall make good upon this earth,
or my divine soul will answer for it in heaven.
You are a traitor and a miscreant,
too good to be so and too bad to live,
since the more fair and crystal the sky is,
the uglier the clouds that fly in it seem.
Once more, to aggravate the note even more,
I stuff your throat with a foul traitor's name;
and I wish, if it pleases my sovereign, before I move,
that my right-drawn sword may prove what my tongue speaks.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
Let not my cold words here accuse my zeal.
It is not the trial of a woman's war,
the bitter clamor of two eager tongues,
that can arbitrate this cause between us two.
The blood is hot that must be cooled for this.
Yet I cannot boast of such tame patience
as to be hushed and say nothing at all.
First, the fair reverence of your highness curbs me
from giving reins and spurs to my free speech;
which else would post until it had returned
these terms of treason doubled down his throat.
Setting aside his high blood's royalty,
and let him be no kinsman to my liege,
I defy him, and I spit at him;
I call him a slanderous coward and a villain.
Which to maintain, I would allow him odds,
and meet him, were I tied to run on foot
even to the frozen ridges of the Alps,
or any other uninhabitable ground,
wherever an Englishman dared to set his foot.
In the meantime, let this defend my loyalty,
by all my hopes, he lies most falsely.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Pale, trembling coward, there I throw my gauntlet,
disclaiming here the kinship of the king,
and I lay aside my high blood's royalty,
which fear, not reverence, makes you except.
If guilty dread has left you so much strength
as to take up my honor's pawn, then stoop.
By that and all the rites of knighthood else,
I will make good against you, arm to arm,
what I have spoken, or whatever worse you can devise.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
I take it up; and by that sword I swear
which gently laid my knighthood on my shoulder,
I'll answer you in any fair degree,
or chivalrous design of knightly trial.
And when I mount, may I not light alive,
if I am a traitor or fight unjustly!

KING RICHARD II:
What does our cousin lay to Mowbray's charge?
It must be great that can make us have
so much as a thought of ill in him.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Look, what I speak, my life shall prove it true;
that Mowbray has received eight thousand nobles
in the name of loans for your highness' soldiers,
which he has detained for lewd employments,
like a false traitor and an injurious villain.
Besides, I say and will prove in battle,
either here or elsewhere to the furthest verge
that was ever surveyed by an English eye,
that all the treasons for these eighteen years
plotted and contrived in this land
originate from false Mowbray.
Further, I say and will maintain
upon his bad life to make all this good,
that he did plot the Duke of Gloucester's death,
suggested his soon-believing adversaries,
and consequently, like a traitorous coward,
drained out his innocent soul through streams of blood.
Which blood, like sacrificing Abel's, cries,
even from the tongueless caverns of the earth,
to me for justice and rough chastisement;
and, by the glorious worth of my descent,
this arm shall do it, or this life be spent.

KING RICHARD II:
How high a pitch his resolution soars!
Thomas of Norfolk, what do you say to this?

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
Oh, let my sovereign turn away his face
and bid his ears be deaf for a little while,
until I have told this slander of his blood,
how God and good men hate so foul a liar.

KING RICHARD II:
Mowbray, our eyes and ears are impartial.
If he were my brother, no, my kingdom's heir,
as he is but my father's brother's son,
now, by my scepter's awe, I make a vow,
such close kinship to our sacred blood
would give him no privilege, nor would it partialize
the un-stooping firmness of my upright soul.
He is our subject, Mowbray; so are you.
I allow you free and fearless speech.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
Then, Bolingbroke, as low as to your heart,
through the false passage of your throat, you lie.
Three parts of that receipt I had for Calais
I disbursed duly to his highness' soldiers;
the other part I reserved by consent,
because my sovereign liege was in my debt
upon the remainder of a dear account,
since I last went to France to fetch his queen.
Now swallow down that lie. As for Gloucester's death,
I did not kill him; but to my own disgrace
I neglected my sworn duty in that case.
For you, my noble Lord of Lancaster,
the honorable father to my foe,
I once laid an ambush for your life,
a trespass that vexes my grieved soul.
But before I last received the sacrament,
I confessed it, and exactly begged
your grace's pardon, and I hope I had it.
This is my fault. As for the rest of the accusations,
it issues from the rancor of a villain,
a recreant and most degenerate traitor,
which I will boldly defend in myself;
and I will interchangeably hurl down my gauntlet
upon this overweening traitor's foot,
to prove myself a loyal gentleman
even in the best blood chambered in his bosom.
In haste of which, I most heartily pray
your highness to assign our trial day.

KING RICHARD II:
Wrath-kindled gentlemen, be ruled by me;
let's purge this anger without letting blood.
This we prescribe, though we are no physicians;
deep malice makes too deep an incision.
Forget, forgive; conclude and be agreed;
our doctors say this is no month to bleed.
Good uncle, let this end where it began;
we'll calm the Duke of Norfolk, you your son.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
To be a peacemaker will suit my age.
Throw down, my son, the Duke of Norfolk's gauntlet.

KING RICHARD II:
And, Norfolk, throw down his.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
When, Harry, when?
Obedience bids me not to bid again.

KING RICHARD II:
Norfolk, throw it down, we command you; there is no help for it.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
I throw myself, dread sovereign, at your foot.
You shall command my life, but not my shame.
The one my duty owes; but my fair name,
despite of death that lives upon my grave,
you shall not have to use for dark dishonor.
I am disgraced, impeached, and baffled here,
pierced to the soul with slander's venomous spear,
which no balm can cure but the heart-blood of him
who breathed this poison.

KING RICHARD II:
Rage must be withstood.
Give me his gauntlet. Lions make leopards tame.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
Yes, but they don't change his spots. Take but my shame,
and I will resign my gauntlet. My dear, dear lord,
the purest treasure mortal times afford
is a spotless reputation. Without that,
men are but gilded loam or painted clay.
A jewel in a ten-times-barred-up chest
is a bold spirit in a loyal breast.
My honor is my life; both grow in one.
Take honor from me, and my life is done.
Then, dear my liege, let me try my honor;
in that I live and for that I will die.

KING RICHARD II:
Cousin, throw up your gauntlet; you begin.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Oh, may God defend my soul from such a deep sin!
Shall I seem crestfallen in my father's sight?
Or with pale, beggar-fear impeach my height
before this out-dared dastard? Before my tongue
shall wound my honor with such a feeble wrong,
or sound so base a parley, my teeth shall tear
the slavish motive of recanting fear,
and spit it bleeding in his high disgrace,
where shame does harbor, even in Mowbray's face.

KING RICHARD II:
We were not born to sue, but to command;
which since we cannot do to make you friends,
be ready, as your lives shall answer for it,
at Coventry, upon Saint Lambert's day.
There your swords and lances shall arbitrate
the swelling difference of your settled hate.
Since we cannot reconcile you, we shall see
justice decide the victor's chivalry.
Lord marshal, command our officers at arms
to be ready to direct these home alarms.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Alas, the part I had in Woodstock's blood
does solicit me more than your exclamations,
to stir against the butchers of his life!
But since correction lies in those hands
which made the fault that we cannot correct,
let us put our quarrel to the will of heaven;
who, when they see the hours are ripe on earth,
will rain hot vengeance on the offenders' heads.

DUCHESS:
Does brotherhood find no sharper spur in you?
Does love in your old blood have no living fire?
Edward's seven sons, of which you are one,
were like seven vials of his sacred blood,
or seven fair branches springing from one root.
Some of those seven have dried by nature's course,
some of those branches have been cut by the Fates;
but Thomas, my dear lord, my life, my Gloucester,
one vial full of Edward's sacred blood,
one flourishing branch of his most royal root,
is cracked, and all the precious liquor is spilt,
is hacked down, and his summer leaves are all faded,
by envy's hand and murder's bloody axe.
Ah, Gaunt, his blood was yours! That bed, that womb,
that metal, that self-mold, that fashioned you
made him a man; and though you live and breathe,
yet you are slain in him. You consent
in some large measure to your father's death,
in that you see your wretched brother die,
who was the model of your father's life.
Do not call it patience, Gaunt; it is despair.
In suffering your brother to be slaughtered like this,
you show the naked pathway to your life,
teaching stern murder how to butcher you.
What we call patience in mean men
is pale, cold cowardice in noble breasts.
What shall I say? To safeguard your own life,
the best way is to avenge my Gloucester's death.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
God's is the quarrel; for God's substitute,
his deputy anointed in His sight,
has caused his death. If wrongfully,
let heaven revenge; for I may never lift
an angry arm against His minister.

DUCHESS:
Where then, alas, may I complain myself?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
To God, the widow's champion and defense.

DUCHESS:
Why, then, I will. Farewell, old Gaunt.
You are going to Coventry, to behold
our cousin Hereford and the fierce Mowbray fight.
Oh, may my husband's wrongs sit on Hereford's spear,
so that it may enter the butcher Mowbray's breast!
Or, if misfortune misses the first career,
may Mowbray's sins be so heavy in his bosom,
that they may break his foaming courser's back,
and throw the rider headlong in the lists,
a recreant villain to my cousin Hereford!
Farewell, old Gaunt. Your sometimes brother's wife,
with her companion grief, must end her life.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Sister, farewell; I must go to Coventry.
May as much good stay with you as goes with me!

DUCHESS:
Yet one word more. Grief rebounds where it falls,
not with empty hollowness, but with weight.
I take my leave before I have begun,
for sorrow doesn't end when it seems done.
Commend me to your brother, Edmund York.
Lo, this is all—no, don't depart so soon;
though this is all, don't go so quickly.
I shall remember more. Bid him—ah, what?—
with all good speed, visit me at Plashy.
Alas, and what will good old York see there
but empty lodgings and unfurnished walls,
unpeopled offices, untrodden stones?
And what will he hear there for a welcome but my groans?
Therefore, commend me; let him not come there,
to seek out sorrow that dwells everywhere.
Desolate, desolate, I will go from here and die.
My weeping eye takes its last leave of you.

Lord Marshal:
My Lord Aumerle, is Harry Hereford armed?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Yes, at all points; and he is eager to enter.

Lord Marshal:
The Duke of Norfolk, spiritedly and boldly,
is just waiting for the summons of the appellant's trumpet.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Why, then, the champions are prepared, and are waiting
for nothing but his majesty's approach.

KING RICHARD II:
Marshal, demand of that champion over there
the cause of his arrival here in arms.
Ask him his name and proceed in an orderly fashion
to swear him in on the justice of his cause.

Lord Marshal:
In God's name and the king's, say who you are
and why you have come thus knightly clad in arms,
against what man you come, and what your quarrel is.
Speak truly, on your knighthood and your oath;
and so may heaven and your valor defend you!

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
My name is Thomas Mowbray, Duke of Norfolk;
who have come here, engaged by my oath—
which God forbid a knight should violate!—
both to defend my loyalty and truth
to God, my king, and my succeeding issue,
against the Duke of Hereford who accuses me.
And, by the grace of God and this my arm,
to prove him, in defending myself,
a traitor to my God, my king, and me.
And as I truly fight, may heaven defend me!

KING RICHARD II:
Marshal, ask that knight in arms over there,
both who he is and why he has come here
thus plated in the habiliments of war,
and formally, according to our law,
depose him on the justice of his cause.

Lord Marshal:
What is your name? And why have you come here,
before King Richard in his royal lists?
Against whom have you come? And what is your quarrel?
Speak like a true knight, so may heaven defend you!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I am Harry of Hereford, Lancaster, and Derby,
who stand ready here in arms,
to prove, by God's grace and my body's valor,
in these lists, on Thomas Mowbray, Duke of Norfolk,
that he is a traitor, foul and dangerous,
to the God of heaven, to King Richard, and to me;
and as I truly fight, may heaven defend me!

Lord Marshal:
On pain of death, no person should be so bold
or daring-hardy as to touch the lists,
except the marshal and such officers
appointed to direct these fair designs.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Lord Marshal, let me kiss my sovereign's hand,
and bow my knee before his majesty.
For Mowbray and myself are like two men
that vow a long and weary pilgrimage;
then let us take a ceremonious leave
and loving farewell of our several friends.

Lord Marshal:
The appellant, in all duty, greets your highness,
and craves to kiss your hand and take his leave.

KING RICHARD II:
We will descend and fold him in our arms.
Cousin of Hereford, as your cause is right,
so may your fortune be in this royal fight!
Farewell, my blood; which if you shed today,
we may lament, but not revenge you when you're dead.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Oh, let no noble eye profane a tear
for me, if I am gored with Mowbray's spear.
As confident as the falcon's flight
against a bird, so do I fight with Mowbray.
My loving lord, I take my leave of you;
of you, my noble cousin, Lord Aumerle;
not sick, although I have to do with death,
but lusty, young, and cheerfully drawing breath.
Lo, as at English feasts, so I re-greet
the daintiest last, to make the end most sweet.
Oh, you, the earthly author of my blood,
whose youthful spirit, regenerated in me,
lifts me up with a twofold vigor
to reach for victory above my head,
add proof to my armor with your prayers;
and with your blessings, steel my lance's point,
that it may enter Mowbray's waxen coat,
and furbish new the name of John of Gaunt,
even in the lusty behavior of his son.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
May God make you prosperous in your good cause!
Be as swift as lightning in the execution;
and may your blows, doubly redoubled,
fall like amazing thunder on the helmet
of your adverse, pernicious enemy.
Rouse up your youthful blood, be valiant and live.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
May my innocence and Saint George thrive!

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
However God or fortune may cast my lot,
there lives or dies, true to King Richard's throne,
a loyal, just, and upright gentleman.
Never did a captive with a freer heart
cast off his chains of bondage and embrace
his golden, uncontrolled enfranchisement,
more than my dancing soul does celebrate
this feast of battle with my adversary.
Most mighty liege, and my companion peers,
take from my mouth the wish of happy years.
As gentle and as jocund as to jest
I go to fight. Truth has a quiet breast.

KING RICHARD II:
Farewell, my lord. I can see securely
virtue with valor couched in your eye.
Order the trial, marshal, and begin.

Lord Marshal:
Harry of Hereford, Lancaster and Derby,
receive your lance; and may God defend the right!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Strong as a tower in hope, I cry amen.

Lord Marshal:
Go and bear this lance to Thomas, Duke of Norfolk.

First Herald:
Harry of Hereford, Lancaster and Derby,
stands here for God, his sovereign, and himself,
on pain of being found false and recreant,
to prove the Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Mowbray,
a traitor to his God, his king, and him;
and he dares him to come forward to the fight.

Second Herald:
Here stands Thomas Mowbray, Duke of Norfolk,
on pain of being found false and recreant,
both to defend himself and to prove
Henry of Hereford, Lancaster, and Derby,
disloyal to God, his sovereign, and to him;
courageously and with a free desire
waiting but the signal to begin.

Lord Marshal:
Sound, trumpets; and come forward, combatants.
Stay, the king has thrown his staff down.

KING RICHARD II:
Let them lay by their helmets and their spears,
and both return back to their chairs again.
Withdraw with us, and let the trumpets sound
while we inform these dukes of our decree.
Draw near,
and listen to what we have decided with our council.
Because our kingdom's earth should not be soiled
with the dear blood which it has fostered;
and because our eyes hate the dire sight
of civil wounds plowed up with a neighbor's sword;
and because we think the eagle-winged pride
of sky-aspiring and ambitious thoughts,
with rival-hating envy, has set you
to wake our peace, which in our country's cradle
draws the sweet infant breath of gentle sleep;
which, so roused up with boisterous, untuned drums,
with the harsh, resounding trumpets' dreadful bray,
and the grating shock of wrathful iron arms,
might from our quiet confines frighten fair peace
and make us wade even in our kindred's blood,
therefore, we banish you from our territories.
You, cousin Hereford, upon pain of life,
until twice five summers have enriched our fields
shall not re-greet our fair dominions,
but tread the stranger paths of banishment.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Your will be done. This must be my comfort:
the sun that warms you here will shine on me;
and those golden beams lent to you here
will point on me and gild my banishment.

KING RICHARD II:
Norfolk, for you remains a heavier doom,
which I pronounce with some unwillingness.
The sly, slow hours shall not determine
the dateless limit of your dear exile;
the hopeless word of 'never to return'
I breathe against you, upon pain of life.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
A heavy sentence, my most sovereign liege,
and all unexpected from your highness's mouth.
A dearer merit, not so deep a wound
as to be cast forth into the common air,
I have deserved at your highness's hands.
The language I have learned these forty years,
my native English, I must now forego.
And now the use of my tongue is no more to me
than an unstrung viol or a harp,
or like a cunning instrument cased up,
or, being open, put into the hands
of one that knows no touch to tune the harmony.
Within my mouth, you have jailed my tongue,
doubly portcullised with my teeth and lips;
and dull, unfeeling, barren ignorance
is made my jailer to attend on me.
I am too old to fawn upon a nurse,
too far in years to be a pupil now.
What is your sentence then but speechless death,
which robs my tongue from breathing its native breath?

KING RICHARD II:
It does you no good to be compassionate.
After our sentence, complaining comes too late.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
Then thus I turn me from my country's light,
to dwell in the solemn shades of endless night.

KING RICHARD II:
Return again, and take an oath with you.
Lay your banished hands on our royal sword;
swear by the duty that you owe to God—
our part in it we banish with yourselves—
to keep the oath that we administer.
You never shall, so help you truth and God!
embrace each other's love in banishment;
nor ever look upon each other's face;
nor ever write, re-greet, nor reconcile
this lowering tempest of your home-bred hate;
nor ever by a planned purpose meet
to plot, contrive, or complot any ill
against us, our state, our subjects, or our land.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I swear.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
And I, to keep all this.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Norfolk, as far as I'm concerned, you're my enemy.
By this time, if the king had permitted us,
one of our souls would have wandered in the air,
banished from this frail sepulchre of our flesh,
as now our flesh is banished from this land.
Confess your treasons before you flee the realm;
since you have far to go, don't bear along
the clogging burden of a guilty soul.

THOMAS MOWBRAY:
No, Bolingbroke. If ever I were a traitor,
may my name be blotted from the book of life,
and may I be banished from heaven as I am from here!
But what you are, God, you, and I know;
and all too soon, I fear, the king shall regret it.
Farewell, my liege. Now I can stray no way;
except back to England, all the world is my way.

KING RICHARD II:
Uncle, even in the glasses of your eyes
I see your grieved heart. Your sad appearance
has plucked four years away
from the number of his banished years.
After six frozen winters,
he may return with a welcome home from banishment.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
How long a time lies in one little word!
Four lagging winters and four wanton springs
end in a word. Such is the breath of kings.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
I thank my liege, that in regard of me
he shortens my son's exile by four years.
But I will reap little advantage by it;
for, before the six years that he has to spend
can change their moons and bring their times about,
my oil-dried lamp and time-wasted light
shall be extinguished with age and endless night;
my inch of taper will be burnt and done,
and blindfolded death will not let me see my son.

KING RICHARD II:
Why, uncle, you have many years to live.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
But not a minute, king, that you can give.
You can shorten my days with sullen sorrow,
and pluck nights from me, but not lend a morrow;
you can help time to furrow me with age,
but stop no wrinkle in his pilgrimage.
Your word is current with him for my death,
but once I'm dead, your kingdom cannot buy my breath.

KING RICHARD II:
Your son is banished upon good advice,
to which your tongue gave a partial verdict.
Why do you then frown at our justice?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Things that taste sweet prove sour in digestion.
You urged me as a judge; but I would have rather
you had bid me argue like a father.
Oh, if it had been a stranger, not my child,
I would have been more mild in smoothing his fault.
I sought to avoid a partial slander,
and in the sentence, I destroyed my own life.
Alas, I looked for some of you to say,
'I was too strict to send my own away';
but you gave leave to my unwilling tongue
to do myself this wrong against my will.

KING RICHARD II:
Cousin, farewell; and, uncle, bid him so.
We banish him for six years, and he shall go.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Cousin, farewell. What our presence must not know,
let paper show from where you remain.

Lord Marshal:
My lord, I take no leave; for I will ride,
as far as land will let me, by your side.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Oh, to what purpose do you hoard your words,
that you return no greeting to your friends?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I have too few to take my leave of you,
when the tongue's office should be prodigal
to breathe the abundant sorrow of the heart.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Your grief is but your absence for a time.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Joy is absent, so grief is present for that time.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
What are six winters? They are quickly gone.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
To men in joy; but grief makes one hour ten.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Call it a journey that you take for pleasure.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My heart will sigh when I miscall it so,
which finds it to be an enforced pilgrimage.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
The sullen passage of your weary steps,
esteem as a foil in which you are to set
the precious jewel of your home return.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
No, rather, every tedious stride I make
will but remind me of what a great part of the world
I wander from the jewels that I love.
Must I not serve a long apprenticeship
to foreign passages, and in the end,
having my freedom, boast of nothing else
but that I was a journeyman to grief?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
All places that the eye of heaven visits
are to a wise man ports and happy havens.
Teach your necessity to reason thus;
there is no virtue like necessity.
Do not think the king banished you,
but that you banished the king. Woe sits heavier,
where it perceives it is but faintly borne.
Go, say I sent you forth to purchase honor,
and not that the king exiled you; or suppose
a devouring pestilence hangs in our air
and you are flying to a fresher clime.
Look, whatever your soul holds dear, imagine it
to lie that way you go, not from where you come.
Suppose the singing birds are musicians,
the grass where you tread is the presence strewed,
the flowers are fair ladies, and your steps are no more
than a delightful measure or a dance;
for snarling sorrow has less power to bite
the man that mocks at it and sets it light.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Oh, who can hold a fire in his hand
by thinking of the frosty Caucasus?
Or satisfy the hungry edge of appetite
by the bare imagination of a feast?
Or wallow naked in December snow
by thinking of fantastic summer's heat?
Oh, no! The apprehension of the good
gives but the greater feeling to the worse.
Fell sorrow's tooth never rankles more
than when he bites, but doesn't lance the sore.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Come, come, my son, I'll bring you on your way.
If I had your youth and your cause, I would not stay.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Then, England's ground, farewell; sweet soil, adieu;
my mother, and my nurse, that still bears me!
Wherever I wander, I can boast of this,
though banished, I am yet a true-born Englishman.

KING RICHARD II:
We did observe. Cousin Aumerle,
how far did you bring high Hereford on his way?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
I brought high Hereford, if you call him so,
only to the next highway, and there I left him.

KING RICHARD II:
And tell me, what store of parting tears were shed?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Faith, none for me; except the north-east wind,
which then blew bitterly against our faces,
awoke the sleeping rheum, and so by chance
did grace our hollow parting with a tear.

KING RICHARD II:
What did our cousin say when you parted with him?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
'Farewell.'
And, because my heart disdained that my tongue
should so profane the word, that taught me the craft
to counterfeit the oppression of such grief
that my words seemed buried in my sorrow's grave.
Indeed, if the word 'farewell' could have lengthened the hours
and added years to his short banishment,
he would have had a volume of farewells;
but since it would not, he had none from me.

KING RICHARD II:
He is our cousin, cousin; but it is doubtful,
when time shall call him home from banishment,
whether our kinsman will come to see his friends.
Myself and Bushy, Bagot here and Green,
observed his courtship of the common people;
how he did seem to dive into their hearts
with humble and familiar courtesy,
what reverence he did throw away on slaves,
wooing poor craftsmen with the craft of smiles
and the patient endurance of his fortune,
as if he were to banish their affections with him.
He takes off his hat to an oyster-wench;
a pair of draymen bid God speed him well
and had the tribute of his supple knee,
with 'Thanks, my countrymen, my loving friends';
as if our England were his in reversion,
and he our subjects' next degree in hope.

GREEN:
Well, he is gone; and with him go these thoughts.
Now for the rebels which stand out in Ireland,
expedient management must be made, my liege,
before further leisure gives them further means
for their advantage and your highness's loss.

KING RICHARD II:
We will go in person to this war.
And, because our coffers, with too great a court
and liberal largess, have grown somewhat light,
we are forced to farm out our royal realm;
the revenue of which shall furnish us
for our affairs in hand. If that comes short,
our substitutes at home shall have blank charters;
to which, when they shall know what men are rich,
they shall subscribe them for large sums of gold
and send them after to supply our wants;
for we will make for Ireland presently.
Bushy, what news?

BUSHY:
Old John of Gaunt is grievously sick, my lord,
suddenly taken; and has sent post-haste
to entreat your majesty to visit him.

KING RICHARD II:
Where is he?

BUSHY:
At Ely House.

KING RICHARD II:
Now, God, put it in the physician's mind
to help him to his grave immediately!
The lining of his coffers shall make coats
to deck our soldiers for these Irish wars.
Come, gentlemen, let's all go visit him.
I pray to God we make haste and come too late!

All:
Amen.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Will the king come, that I may breathe my last
in wholesome counsel to his un-stayed youth?

DUKE OF YORK:
Don't vex yourself, nor strive with your breath;
for all counsel comes in vain to his ear.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Oh, but they say the tongues of dying men
enforce attention like deep harmony.
Where words are scarce, they are seldom spent in vain,
for they breathe truth that breathe their words in pain.
He that must say no more is listened to more
than they whom youth and ease have taught to flatter;
men's ends are more marked than their lives before.
The setting sun, and music at the close,
as the last taste of sweets, is sweetest last,
written in remembrance more than things long past.
Though Richard would not hear my life's counsel,
my death's sad tale may yet un-deafen his ear.

DUKE OF YORK:
No; it is stopped with other flattering sounds,
as praises, of whose taste the wise are fond,
lascivious meters, to whose venomous sound
the open ear of youth always listens;
reports of fashions in proud Italy,
whose manners our tardy, apish nation still
limps after in base imitation.
Where does the world thrust forth a vanity—
so it be new, there's no respect how vile—
that is not quickly buzzed into his ears?
Then all too late comes counsel to be heard,
where will mutinies with wit's regard.
Do not direct him whose way he will choose himself.
It is breath you lack, and that breath you will lose.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
I think I am a newly inspired prophet,
and thus, expiring, I foretell of him:
his rash, fierce blaze of riot cannot last,
for violent fires soon burn themselves out;
small showers last long, but sudden storms are short;
he tires early that spurs too fast early;
with eager feeding, food chokes the feeder.
Light vanity, an insatiable cormorant,
consuming means, soon preys upon itself.
This royal throne of kings, this sceptered isle,
this earth of majesty, this seat of Mars,
this other Eden, a demi-paradise,
this fortress built by Nature for herself
against infection and the hand of war,
this happy breed of men, this little world,
this precious stone set in the silver sea,
which serves it as a wall,
or as a moat defensive to a house,
against the envy of less happier lands,
this blessed plot, this earth, this realm, this England,
this nurse, this teeming womb of royal kings,
feared for their breed and famous for their birth,
renowned for their deeds as far from home,
for Christian service and true chivalry,
as is the sepulchre in stubborn Jewry,
of the world's ransom, blessed Mary's Son,
this land of such dear souls, this dear, dear land,
dear for her reputation throughout the world,
is now leased out, I die pronouncing it,
like a tenement or a worthless farm.
England, bound in with the triumphant sea,
whose rocky shore beats back the envious siege
of watery Neptune, is now bound in with shame,
with inky blots and rotten parchment bonds.
That England, that was wont to conquer others,
has made a shameful conquest of itself.
Ah, if only the scandal would vanish with my life,
how happy then would be my ensuing death!

DUKE OF YORK:
The king has come. Deal mildly with his youth,
for young hot colts, when enraged, rage all the more.

QUEEN:
How is our noble uncle, Lancaster?

KING RICHARD II:
What comfort, man? How is it with aged Gaunt?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Oh, how that name befits my composition!
Old Gaunt indeed, and gaunt in being old.
Within me, grief has kept a tedious fast;
and who abstains from meat that is not gaunt?
For sleeping England I have long watched;
watching breeds leanness, and leanness is all gaunt.
The pleasure that some fathers feed upon
is my strict fast; I mean, my children's looks.
And in that fasting, you have made me gaunt.
Gaunt I am for the grave, as gaunt as a grave,
whose hollow womb inherits nothing but bones.

KING RICHARD II:
Can sick men play so nicely with their names?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
No, misery makes sport to mock itself.
Since you seek to kill my name in me,
I mock my name, great king, to flatter you.

KING RICHARD II:
Should dying men flatter those that live?

JOHN OF GAUNT:
No, no, men living flatter those that die.

KING RICHARD II:
You, now dying, say you are flattering me.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Oh, no! You are dying, though I am the sicker one.

KING RICHARD II:
I am in health, I breathe, and I see you ill.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Now He that made me knows I see you ill;
ill in myself to see, and in seeing you ill.
Your death-bed is no less than your land,
where you lie sick in reputation.
And you, too careless a patient as you are,
commit your anointed body to the cure
of those physicians that first wounded you.
A thousand flatterers sit within your crown,
whose compass is no bigger than your head;
and yet, caged in so small a space,
the waste is no less than your land.
Oh, if your grandsire, with a prophet's eye,
had seen how his son's son would destroy his sons,
he would have laid your shame out of your reach,
deposing you before you were possessed,
which you are possessed now to depose yourself.
Why, cousin, if you were the regent of the world,
it would be a shame to lease this land;
but for your world, enjoying only this land,
is it not more than a shame to shame it so?
You are now the landlord of England, not the king.
Your state of law is a bondslave to the law; and you—

KING RICHARD II:
A lunatic, lean-witted fool,
presuming on a fever's privilege,
you dare with your frozen admonition
make our cheek pale, chasing the royal blood
with fury from his native residence.
Now, by my seat's right royal majesty,
if you were not the brother to great Edward's son,
this tongue that runs so roundly in your head
would run your head from your unreverent shoulders.

JOHN OF GAUNT:
Oh, do not spare me, my brother Edward's son,
for I was his father Edward's son.
That blood already, like the pelican,
you have tapped out and drunkenly caroused.
My brother Gloucester, a plain, well-meaning soul,
whom may good fortune befall in heaven among happy souls!
May be a precedent and a good witness
that you do not respect the spilling of Edward's blood.
Join with the present sickness that I have;
and may your unkindness be like crooked age,
to crop at once a too long-withered flower.
Live in your shame, but may shame not die with you!
May these words hereafter be your tormentors!
Convey me to my bed, then to my grave.
May they love to live that have love and honor.

KING RICHARD II:
And let them die that have age and sullens;
for you have both, and both become the grave.

DUKE OF YORK:
I beseech your majesty, impute his words
to the wayward sickliness and age in him.
He loves you, on my life, and holds you as dear
as Harry Duke of Hereford, if he were here.

KING RICHARD II:
Right, you say true. As Hereford's love, so his;
as theirs, so mine; and let all be as it is.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
My liege, old Gaunt commends himself to your majesty.

KING RICHARD II:
What does he say?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
No, nothing; all is said.
His tongue is now a stringless instrument;
words, life, and all, old Lancaster has spent.

DUKE OF YORK:
May York be the next that must be bankrupt so!
Though death is poor, it ends a mortal woe.

KING RICHARD II:
The ripest fruit falls first, and so does he;
his time is spent, our pilgrimage must be.
So much for that. Now for our Irish wars.
We must supplant those rough, shaggy-headed kerns,
which live like venom where no venom else
but only they have the privilege to live.
And because these great affairs require some expense,
for our assistance we do seize to ourselves
the plate, corn, revenues, and movables,
of which our uncle Gaunt was possessed.

DUKE OF YORK:
How long shall I be patient? Ah, how long
shall tender duty make me suffer wrong?
Not Gloucester's death, nor Hereford's banishment,
not Gaunt's rebukes, nor England's private wrongs,
nor the prevention of poor Bolingbroke
about his marriage, nor my own disgrace,
have ever made me sour my patient cheek,
or bend one wrinkle on my sovereign's face.
I am the last of noble Edward's sons,
of whom your father, the Prince of Wales, was the first.
In war, no lion ever raged more fiercely,
in peace, no gentle lamb was ever more mild,
than that young and princely gentleman.
You have his face, for he looked just so,
accomplished with the number of your hours;
but when he frowned, it was against the French
and not against his friends. His noble hand
willed what he spent, and he did not spend that
which his triumphant father's hand had won;
his hands were guilty of no kindred blood,
but were bloody with the enemies of his kin.
Oh, Richard! York is too far gone with grief,
or else he would never compare between.

KING RICHARD II:
Why, uncle, what's the matter?

DUKE OF YORK:
Oh, my liege,
pardon me, if you please; if not, I, being pleased
not to be pardoned, am content withal.
Do you seek to seize and grip into your hands
the royalties and rights of the banished Hereford?
Is not Gaunt dead, and does not Hereford live?
Was not Gaunt just, and is not Harry true?
Did not the one deserve to have an heir?
Is not his heir a well-deserving son?
Take Hereford's rights away, and you take from Time
his charters and his customary rights;
let not tomorrow then ensue today;
be not yourself; for how are you a king
but by fair sequence and succession?
Now, before God—God forbid I say true!—
if you wrongfully seize Hereford's rights,
call in the letters patent that he has
by his attorneys-general to sue
his livery, and deny his offered homage,
you will bring a thousand dangers on your head,
you will lose a thousand well-disposed hearts,
and prick my tender patience to those thoughts
which honor and allegiance cannot think.

KING RICHARD II:
Think what you will, we seize into our hands
his plate, his goods, his money, and his lands.

DUKE OF YORK:
I will not be by while this happens. My liege, farewell.
What will come of this, no one can tell;
but by bad courses it may be understood
that their outcomes can never be good.

KING RICHARD II:
Go, Bushy, to the Earl of Wiltshire straight away.
Bid him repair to us at Ely House
to see to this business. Tomorrow next
we will go to Ireland; and it is time, I think.
And we create, in our absence,
our uncle York lord governor of England;
for he is just and has always loved us well.
Come on, our queen. Tomorrow we must part;
be merry, for our time of stay is short.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Well, lords, the Duke of Lancaster is dead.

LORD ROSS:
And living too; for now his son is duke.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
Barely in title, not in revenue.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Richly in both, if justice had her right.

LORD ROSS:
My heart is heavy, but it must break with silence,
before it can be unburdened with a liberal tongue.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
No, speak your mind; and let him never speak more
that speaks your words again to do you harm!

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
Does what you would speak of concern the Duke of Hereford?
If so, out with it boldly, man;
my ear is quick to hear of good towards him.

LORD ROSS:
No good at all that I can do for him;
unless you call it good to pity him,
bereft and stripped of his patrimony.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Now, before God, it's a shame such wrongs are borne
in him, a royal prince, and many more
of noble blood in this declining land.
The king is not himself, but is basely led
by flatterers; and whatever they will inform,
merely in hate, against any of us all,
that the king will severely prosecute
against us, our lives, our children, and our heirs.

LORD ROSS:
He has plundered the commons with grievous taxes,
and has completely lost their hearts. He has fined the nobles
for ancient quarrels, and has completely lost their hearts.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
And daily new exactions are devised,
such as blanks, benevolences, and I don't know what.
But what, in God's name, becomes of all this?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Wars have not wasted it, for he has not warred,
but has basely yielded upon compromise
that which his noble ancestors achieved with blows.
He has spent more in peace than they did in wars.

LORD ROSS:
The Earl of Wiltshire has the realm in farm.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
The king has gone bankrupt, like a broken man.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Reproach and dissolution hang over him.

LORD ROSS:
He has no money for these Irish wars,
despite his burdensome taxes,
but by robbing the banished duke.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
His noble kinsman! A most degenerate king!
But, lords, we hear this fearful tempest sing,
yet see no shelter to avoid the storm;
we see the wind sit sore upon our sails,
and yet we do not strike them, but securely perish.

LORD ROSS:
We see the very wreck that we must suffer;
and the danger is unavoidable now,
for suffering so the causes of our wreck.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Not so; even through the hollow eyes of death
I spy life peering; but I dare not say
how near the tidings of our comfort are.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
No, let us share your thoughts, as you do ours.

LORD ROSS:
Be confident to speak, Northumberland.
We three are but yourself; and, speaking so,
your words are but as thoughts; therefore, be bold.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Then thus: I have received intelligence from Port le Blanc, a bay
in Brittany,
that Harry Duke of Hereford, Rainold Lord Cobham,
who recently broke from the Duke of Exeter,
his brother, the late Archbishop of Canterbury,
Sir Thomas Erpingham, Sir John Ramston,
Sir John Norbery, Sir Robert Waterton, and Francis Quoint,
all of these well-furnished by the Duke of Bretagne
with eight tall ships, and three thousand men of war,
are making their way here with all due speed
and shortly mean to touch our northern shore.
Perhaps they would have been here already, but they are waiting for
the first departure of the king for Ireland.
If then we shall shake off our slavish yoke,
imp out our drooping country's broken wing,
redeem from a broken pawn the blemished crown,
wipe off the dust that hides our scepter's gilt,
and make high majesty look like itself,
away with me in post to Ravenspurgh.
But if you faint, as if fearing to do so,
stay and be secret, and I myself will go.

LORD ROSS:
To horse, to horse! Urge doubts to those that fear.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
Hold out my horse, and I will be there first.

BUSHY:
Madam, your majesty is too sad.
You promised, when you parted with the king,
to lay aside life-harming heaviness
and entertain a cheerful disposition.

QUEEN:
To please the king, I did; to please myself
I cannot do it. Yet I know no cause
why I should welcome such a guest as grief,
except for bidding farewell to so sweet a guest
as my sweet Richard. Yet again, I think,
some unborn sorrow, ripe in fortune's womb,
is coming towards me, and my inward soul
with nothing trembles. At something it grieves,
more than with parting from my lord the king.

BUSHY:
Each substance of a grief has twenty shadows,
which looks like grief itself, but is not so.
For sorrow's eye, glazed with blinding tears,
divides one whole thing into many objects;
like perspectives, which, when looked at correctly,
show nothing but confusion, but when looked at awry,
distinguish a form. So your sweet majesty,
looking awry upon your lord's departure,
finds shapes of grief, more than himself, to wail;
which, when looked on as it is, is nothing but shadows
of what it is not. Then, thrice-gracious queen,
do not weep more than for your lord's departure; more is not seen.
Or if it is, it is with false sorrow's eye,
which weeps for imaginary things as if they were true.

QUEEN:
It may be so; but yet my inward soul
persuades me it is otherwise. However it may be,
I cannot but be sad; so heavy sad
that, though I think on no thought, it
makes me with heavy nothing faint and shrink.

BUSHY:
It's nothing but a thought, my gracious lady.

QUEEN:
It is nothing less. A thought is always derived
from some pre-existing grief. Mine is not so,
for nothing has begotten my something of grief;
or something has the nothing that I grieve.
It is in the future that I possess it;
but what it is, that is not yet known; what
I cannot name; it is a nameless woe, I know.

GREEN:
God save your majesty! And well met, gentlemen.
I hope the king has not yet sailed for Ireland.

QUEEN:
Why do you hope so? It's better to hope he has;
for his designs require haste, and his haste requires good hope.
Then why do you hope he has not sailed?

GREEN:
So that he, our hope, might have brought back his power,
and driven an enemy's hope into despair,
who has strongly set foot in this land.
The banished Bolingbroke has returned,
and with upraised arms is safely arrived
at Ravenspurgh.

QUEEN:
Now God in heaven forbid!

GREEN:
Ah, madam, it is too true. And what is worse,
the Lord Northumberland, his son young Henry Percy,
the Lords of Ross, Beaumond, and Willoughby,
with all their powerful friends, have fled to him.

BUSHY:
Why have you not proclaimed Northumberland
and all the rest of the revolted faction as traitors?

GREEN:
We have, whereupon the Earl of Worcester
has broken his staff, resigned his stewardship,
and all the household servants have fled with him
to Bolingbroke.

QUEEN:
So, Green, you are the midwife to my woe,
and Bolingbroke is my sorrow's dismal heir.
Now my soul has brought forth her prodigy,
and I, a gasping, newly-delivered mother,
have joined woe to woe, sorrow to sorrow.

BUSHY:
Do not despair, madam.

QUEEN:
Who shall stop me?
I will despair, and be at enmity
with deceiving hope. He is a flatterer,
a parasite, a keeper back of death,
who would gently dissolve the bands of life,
which false hope lingers in extremity.

GREEN:
Here comes the Duke of York.

QUEEN:
With signs of war about his aged neck.
Oh, his looks are full of careful business!
Uncle, for God's sake, speak comfortable words.

DUKE OF YORK:
If I were to do so, I would be lying about my thoughts.
Comfort is in heaven, and we are on the earth,
where nothing lives but crosses, cares, and grief.
Your husband, he has gone to save far off,
while others come to make him lose at home.
Here I am left to support his land,
I, who, weak with age, cannot support myself.
Now comes the sick hour that his surfeit made;
now he shall try his friends that flattered him.

Servant:
My lord, your son was gone before I came.

DUKE OF YORK:
He was? Why, so! Let everything go as it will!
The nobles have fled, the common people are cold,
and I fear they will revolt to Hereford's side.
Sirrah, go to Plashy, to my sister Gloucester;
bid her send me a thousand pounds immediately.
Hold, take my ring.

Servant:
My lord, I had forgotten to tell your lordship,
today, as I came by, I called there;
but I shall grieve you to report the rest.

DUKE OF YORK:
What is it, knave?

Servant:
An hour before I came, the duchess died.

DUKE OF YORK:
God have mercy! What a tide of woes
comes rushing on this woeful land at once!
I do not know what to do. I wish to God,
if only my untruth had not provoked him to it,
the king had cut off my head with my brother's.
What, are there no posts dispatched for Ireland?
How shall we get money for these wars?
Come, sister—cousin, I would say—please, pardon me.
Go, fellow, get home, provide some carts
and bring away the armor that is there.
Gentlemen, will you go and muster men?
If I know how or which way to order these affairs,
thus thrust disorderly into my hands,
never believe me. Both are my kinsmen.
One is my sovereign, whom both my oath
and duty bid me defend; the other, again,
is my kinsman, whom the king has wronged,
whom conscience and my kindred bid me to right.
Well, we must do something. Come, cousin, I'll
dispose of you.
Gentlemen, go, muster up your men,
and meet me presently at Berkeley.
I should go to Plashy too;
but time will not permit. All is uneven,
and everything is left at sixes and sevens.

BUSHY:
The wind is fair for news to go to Ireland,
but none returns. For us to levy a force
proportional to the enemy
is all impossible.

GREEN:
Besides, our nearness to the king in love
is near the hate of those who do not love the king.

BAGOT:
And that's the wavering commons. For their love
lies in their purses, and whoever empties them
by so much fills their hearts with deadly hate.

BUSHY:
In which the king is generally condemned.

BAGOT:
If judgment lies in them, then so do we,
because we have always been near the king.

GREEN:
Well, I will go straight to Bristol Castle for refuge.
The Earl of Wiltshire is already there.

BUSHY:
I will go there with you; for the hateful commons
will perform little service for us,
except like dogs to tear us all to pieces.
Will you go along with us?

BAGOT:
No; I will go to Ireland to his majesty.
Farewell. If my heart's premonitions are not in vain,
we three here are those that will never meet again.

BUSHY:
That's as York thrives to beat back Bolingbroke.

GREEN:
Alas, poor duke! The task he undertakes
is like numbering sands and drinking oceans dry.
Where one on his side fights, thousands will fly.
Farewell at once, for once, for all, and ever.

BUSHY:
Well, we may meet again.

BAGOT:
I fear we never will.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
How far is it, my lord, to Berkeley now?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Believe me, noble lord,
I am a stranger here in Gloucestershire.
These high, wild hills and rough, uneven ways
draw out our miles and make them wearisome.
And yet your fair discourse has been like sugar,
making the hard way sweet and delectable.
But I remember what a weary way
from Ravenspurgh to Cotswold will be found
in Ross and Willoughby, wanting your company,
which, I protest, has very much beguiled
the tediousness and process of my travel.
But theirs is sweetened with the hope to have
the present benefit which I possess;
and the hope of joy is little less in joy
than the hope enjoyed. By this, the weary lords
shall make their way seem short, as mine has done
by the sight of what I have, your noble company.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My company is of much less value
than your good words. But who comes here?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
It is my son, young Harry Percy,
sent from my brother Worcester, from wherever.
Harry, how is your uncle?

HENRY PERCY:
I had thought, my lord, to have learned of his health from you.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Why, isn't he with the queen?

HENRY PERCY:
No, my good Lord; he has forsaken the court,
broken his staff of office and dispersed
the household of the king.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
What was his reason?
He was not so resolved when we last spoke together.

HENRY PERCY:
Because your lordship was proclaimed a traitor.
But he, my lord, has gone to Ravenspurgh,
to offer his service to the Duke of Hereford,
and he sent me over by Berkeley, to discover
what power the Duke of York had levied there;
then with directions to repair to Ravenspurgh.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Have you forgotten the Duke of Hereford, boy?

HENRY PERCY:
No, my good lord, for that is not forgotten
which I never did remember. To my knowledge,
I have never in my life looked on him.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Then learn to know him now; this is the duke.

HENRY PERCY:
My gracious lord, I offer you my service,
such as it is, being tender, raw, and young,
which elder days shall ripen and confirm
to more approved service and desert.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I thank you, gentle Percy; and be sure
I count myself in nothing else so happy
as in a soul remembering my good friends;
and, as my fortune ripens with your love,
it shall still be your true love's recompense.
My heart makes this covenant, and my hand thus seals it.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
How far is it to Berkeley? And what stir
is good old York keeping there with his men of war?

HENRY PERCY:
There stands the castle, by that tuft of trees,
manned with three hundred men, as I have heard;
and in it are the Lords of York, Berkeley, and Seymour;
no one else of name and noble estimate.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Here come the Lords of Ross and Willoughby,
bloody with spurring, fiery-red with haste.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Welcome, my lords. I know your love pursues
a banished traitor. All my treasury
is yet but unfelt thanks, which, more enriched,
shall be your love and labor's recompense.

LORD ROSS:
Your presence makes us rich, most noble lord.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
And it far surpasses our labor to attain it.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Evermore thanks, the exchequer of the poor;
which, until my infant fortune comes of age,
stands for my bounty. But who comes here?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
It is my Lord of Berkeley, as I guess.

LORD BERKELEY:
My Lord of Hereford, my message is to you.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My lord, my answer is—to Lancaster;
and I have come to seek that name in England;
and I must find that title in your tongue,
before I make a reply to anything you say.

LORD BERKELEY:
Don't misunderstand me, my lord; it's not my intention
to erase a single title of your honor.
To you, my lord, I come, whatever lord you may be,
from the most gracious regent of this land,
the Duke of York, to know what prompts you on
to take advantage of the absent time
and frighten our native peace with self-born arms.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I shall not need to transport my words by you;
here comes his grace in person. My noble uncle!

DUKE OF YORK:
Show me your humble heart, and not your knee,
whose duty is deceitful and false.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My gracious uncle—

DUKE OF YORK:
Tut, tut!
Don't grace me with 'grace,' nor uncle me with 'uncle.'
I am no traitor's uncle; and that word 'grace'
in an ungracious mouth is but profane.
Why have those banished and forbidden legs
dared to once touch the dust of England's ground?
But then, more 'why?' Why have they dared to march
so many miles upon her peaceful bosom,
frightening her pale-faced villages with war
and the ostentation of despised arms?
Do you come because the anointed king is away?
Why, foolish boy, the king is left behind,
and in my loyal bosom lies his power.
If I were now the lord of such hot youth
as when the brave Gaunt, your father, and myself
rescued the Black Prince, that young Mars of men,
from the ranks of many thousand French,
oh, then how quickly would this arm of mine,
now a prisoner to palsy, chastise you
and administer correction to your fault!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My gracious uncle, let me know my fault.
On what condition does it stand and wherein?

DUKE OF YORK:
Even in the condition of the worst degree,
in gross rebellion and detested treason.
You are a banished man, and you have come here
before the expiration of your time,
in braving arms against your sovereign.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
As I was banished, I was banished as Hereford;
but as I come, I come for Lancaster.
And, noble uncle, I beseech your grace
to look on my wrongs with an indifferent eye.
You are my father, for I think in you
I see old Gaunt alive. Oh, then, my father,
will you permit that I shall stand condemned
as a wandering vagabond; my rights and royalties
plucked from my arms by force and given away
to upstart unthrifts? Why was I born?
If my cousin the king is King of England,
it must be granted that I am Duke of Lancaster.
You have a son, Aumerle, my noble cousin;
if you had died first, and he had been thus trodden down,
he would have found his uncle Gaunt a father,
to rouse his wrongs and chase them to the bay.
I am denied to sue for my inheritance here,
and yet my letters patent give me leave.
My father's goods are all distrained and sold,
and these and all are all amiss employed.
What would you have me do? I am a subject,
and I challenge the law. Attorneys are denied me;
and therefore, I personally lay my claim
to my inheritance of free descent.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
The noble duke has been too much abused.

LORD ROSS:
It is your grace's duty to do him right.

LORD WILLOUGHBY:
Base men are made great by his endowments.

DUKE OF YORK:
My lords of England, let me tell you this:
I have had a feeling for my cousin's wrongs
and I have labored all I could to do him right;
but to come in this way, in braving arms,
to be his own carver and cut out his way,
to find out right with wrong, it may not be;
and you that abet him in this way
cherish rebellion and are all rebels.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
The noble duke has sworn his coming is
only for his own; and for the right of that
we have all strongly sworn to give him aid;
and may he never see joy that breaks that oath!

DUKE OF YORK:
Well, well, I see the outcome of these arms.
I cannot mend it, I must needs confess,
because my power is weak and all is ill left.
But if I could, by Him that gave me life,
I would arrest you all and make you stoop
to the sovereign mercy of the king;
but since I cannot, be it known to you
I remain neutral. So, fare you well;
unless you please to enter the castle
and rest there for this night.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
An offer, uncle, that we will accept.
But we must win your grace to go with us
to Bristol Castle, which they say is held
by Bushy, Bagot, and their accomplices,
the caterpillars of the commonwealth,
which I have sworn to weed and pluck away.

DUKE OF YORK:
It may be that I will go with you, but I will pause for now;
for I am loath to break our country's laws.
You are welcome to me as neither friends nor foes.
Things past redress are now with me past care.

Captain:
My lord of Salisbury, we have stayed ten days,
and have hardly kept our countrymen together,
and yet we hear no news from the king;
therefore we will disperse ourselves. Farewell.

EARL OF SALISBURY:
Stay yet another day, you trusty Welshman.
The king reposes all his confidence in you.

Captain:
It is thought the king is dead; we will not stay.
The bay-trees in our country are all withered
and meteors frighten the fixed stars of heaven;
the pale-faced moon looks bloody on the earth
and lean-looked prophets whisper of fearful change;
rich men look sad and ruffians dance and leap,
the one in fear of losing what they enjoy,
the other to enjoy by rage and war.
These signs forerun the death or fall of kings.
Farewell. Our countrymen have gone and fled,
as well assured that Richard their king is dead.

EARL OF SALISBURY:
Ah, Richard, with the eyes of a heavy mind
I see your glory like a shooting star
fall to the base earth from the firmament.
Your sun sets weeping in the lowly west,
witnessing storms to come, woe, and unrest.
Your friends have fled to wait upon your foes,
and all fortune goes against your good.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Bring forth these men.
Bushy and Green, I will not vex your souls—
since your souls must presently part from your bodies—
with too much urging of your pernicious lives,
for it would be no charity. Yet, to wash your blood
from my hands, here in the view of men
I will unfold some causes of your deaths.
You have misled a prince, a royal king,
a happy gentleman in blood and features,
made unhappy and disfigured by you.
You have, in a way, with your sinful hours
made a divorce between his queen and him,
broken the possession of a royal bed
and stained the beauty of a fair queen's cheeks
with tears drawn from her eyes by your foul wrongs.
I, a prince by the fortune of my birth,
near to the king in blood, and near in love
until you made him misinterpret me,
have stooped my neck under your injuries,
and sighed my English breath in foreign clouds,
eating the bitter bread of banishment;
while you have fed upon my lands,
dis-parked my parks and felled my forest woods,
from my own windows torn my household coat,
erased my emblem, leaving me no sign,
save men's opinions and my living blood,
to show the world I am a gentleman.
This and much more, much more than twice all this,
condemns you to death. See them delivered over
to execution and the hand of death.

BUSHY:
The stroke of death is more welcome to me
than Bolingbroke is to England. Lords, farewell.

GREEN:
My comfort is that heaven will take our souls
and plague injustice with the pains of hell.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My Lord Northumberland, see them dispatched.
Uncle, you say the queen is at your house;
for God's sake, let her be treated fairly.
Tell her I send her my kind regards;
take special care that my greetings are delivered.

DUKE OF YORK:
A gentleman of mine I have dispatched
with letters of your love to her at large.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Thanks, gentle uncle. Come, lords, let's go.
To fight with Glendower and his accomplices.
A while to work, and then a holiday.

KING RICHARD II:
Do they call this Barkloughly Castle at hand?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Yes, my lord. How does your grace like the air,
after your recent tossing on the breaking seas?

KING RICHARD II:
I must like it well. I weep for joy
to stand upon my kingdom once again.
Dear earth, I salute you with my hand,
though rebels wound you with their horses' hooves.
As a long-parted mother with her child
plays fondly with her tears and smiles in meeting,
so, weeping and smiling, I greet you, my earth,
and do you favors with my royal hands.
Do not feed my sovereign's foe, my gentle earth,
nor comfort his ravenous sense with your sweets;
but let your spiders, that suck up your venom,
and heavy-gaited toads lie in their way,
doing annoyance to the treacherous feet
which with usurping steps do trample you.
Yield stinging nettles to my enemies;
and when they from your bosom pluck a flower,
guard it, I pray you, with a lurking adder
whose double tongue may with a mortal touch
throw death upon my sovereign's enemies.
Do not mock my senseless conjuration, lords.
This earth shall have a feeling and these stones
will prove to be armed soldiers, before her native king
shall falter under the arms of foul rebellion.

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
Fear not, my lord. The Power that made you king
has the power to keep you king in spite of all.
The means that heaven yields must be embraced,
and not neglected; otherwise, if heaven would,
and we will not, we refuse heaven's offer,
the proffered means of succor and redress.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
He means, my lord, that we are too remiss;
while Bolingbroke, through our security,
grows strong and great in substance and in power.

KING RICHARD II:
Discomfortable cousin! Do you not know
that when the searching eye of heaven is hidden,
behind the globe, that lights the lower world,
then thieves and robbers range abroad unseen
in murders and in outrage, boldly here;
but when from under this terrestrial ball
he fires the proud tops of the eastern pines
and darts his light through every guilty hole,
then murders, treasons, and detested sins,
the cloak of night being plucked from off their backs,
stand bare and naked, trembling at themselves?
So when this thief, this traitor, Bolingbroke,
who all this while has reveled in the night
while we were wandering with the antipodes,
shall see us rising in our throne, the east,
his treasons will sit blushing in his face,
not able to endure the sight of day,
but self-affrighted, he will tremble at his sin.
Not all the water in the rough, rude sea
can wash the balm off an anointed king;
the breath of worldly men cannot depose
the deputy elected by the Lord.
For every man that Bolingbroke has pressed
to lift shrewd steel against our golden crown,
God for his Richard has in heavenly pay
a glorious angel. Then, if angels fight,
weak men must fall, for heaven still guards the right.
Welcome, my lord. How far off is your power?

EARL OF SALISBURY:
Neither near nor farther off, my gracious lord,
than this weak arm. Discomfort guides my tongue
and bids me speak of nothing but despair.
One day too late, I fear me, noble lord,
has clouded all your happy days on earth.
Oh, call back yesterday, bid time return,
and you shall have twelve thousand fighting men!
Today, today, unhappy day, too late,
overthrows your joys, friends, fortune, and your state.
For all the Welshmen, hearing you were dead,
have gone to Bolingbroke, dispersed and fled.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Comfort, my liege; why do you look so pale?

KING RICHARD II:
Just now, the blood of twenty thousand men
did triumph in my face, and they have fled;
and, until so much blood comes there again,
do I not have a reason to look pale and dead?
All souls that will be safe, flee from my side,
for time has set a blot upon my pride.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Comfort, my liege; remember who you are.

KING RICHARD II:
I had forgotten myself. Am I not the king?
Awake, you coward majesty! You are sleeping.
Is not the king's name worth twenty thousand names?
Arm, arm, my name! A puny subject strikes
at your great glory. Do not look to the ground,
you favorites of a king. Are we not high?
Let our thoughts be high. I know my uncle York
has enough power to serve our turn. But who comes here?

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
May more health and happiness befall my liege
than my care-tuned tongue can deliver to him!

KING RICHARD II:
My ear is open and my heart is prepared;
the worst you can unfold is worldly loss.
Say, is my kingdom lost? Why, it was my care,
and what loss is it to be rid of care?
Does Bolingbroke strive to be as great as we?
He shall not be greater. If he serves God,
we'll serve Him too and be his fellow so.
Do our subjects revolt? That we cannot mend;
they break their faith to God as well as to us.
Cry woe, destruction, ruin, and decay.
The worst is death, and death will have his day.

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
I am glad that your highness is so armed
to bear the news of calamity.
Like an unseasonable, stormy day,
which makes the silver rivers drown their shores,
as if the world were all dissolved to tears,
so high above his limits swells the rage
of Bolingbroke, covering your fearful land
with hard, bright steel, and hearts harder than steel.
White-beards have armed their thin and hairless scalps
against your majesty; boys, with women's voices,
strive to speak big and clap their female joints
in stiff, unwieldy arms against your crown.
The very beadsmen learn to bend their bows
of double-fatal yew against your state;
yes, even the distaff-women manage rusty bills
against your seat. Both young and old rebel,
and everything goes worse than I have the power to tell.

KING RICHARD II:
Too well, too well you tell a tale so ill.
Where is the Earl of Wiltshire? Where is Bagot?
What has become of Bushy? Where is Green?
That they have let the dangerous enemy
measure our confines with such peaceful steps?
If we prevail, their heads shall pay for it.
I warrant they have made peace with Bolingbroke.

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
They have made peace with him indeed, my lord.

KING RICHARD II:
O villains, vipers, damned without redemption!
Dogs, easily won to fawn on any man!
Snakes, warmed in my heart-blood, that sting my heart!
Three Judases, each one thrice worse than Judas!
Would they make peace? May terrible hell make war
upon their spotted souls for this offense!

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
Sweet love, I see, changing its property,
turns to the sourest and most deadly hate.
Un-curse their souls again; their peace is made
with heads, and not with hands; those whom you curse
have felt the worst of death's destroying wound
and lie full low, graved in the hollow ground.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Are Bushy, Green, and the Earl of Wiltshire dead?

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
Yes, all of them lost their heads at Bristol.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Where is the duke my father with his power?

KING RICHARD II:
No matter where; let no man speak of comfort.
Let's talk of graves, of worms, and epitaphs;
let's make dust our paper and with rainy eyes
write sorrow on the bosom of the earth.
Let's choose executors and talk of wills.
And yet not so, for what can we bequeath
except our deposed bodies to the ground?
Our lands, our lives, and all are Bolingbroke's,
and we can call nothing our own but death
and that small model of the barren earth
which serves as paste and cover to our bones.
For God's sake, let us sit upon the ground
and tell sad stories of the death of kings;
how some have been deposed; some slain in war,
some haunted by the ghosts they have deposed;
some poisoned by their wives; some sleeping killed;
all murdered. For within the hollow crown
that rounds the mortal temples of a king
keeps Death his court, and there the antic sits,
scoffing at his state and grinning at his pomp,
allowing him a breath, a little scene,
to monarchize, be feared, and kill with looks,
infusing him with self and vain conceit,
as if this flesh which walls about our life
were brass impregnable. And, humored thus,
Death comes at last and with a little pin
bores through his castle wall, and farewell king!
Cover your heads and do not mock flesh and blood
with solemn reverence. Throw away respect,
tradition, form, and ceremonious duty,
for you have but mistaken me all this while.
I live with bread like you, feel want,
taste grief, need friends. Subjected thus,
how can you say to me, 'I am a king'?

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
My lord, wise men never sit and wail their woes,
but immediately prevent the ways to wail.
To fear the foe, since fear oppresses strength,
gives in your weakness strength to your foe,
and so your follies fight against yourself.
Fear and be slain; no worse can come from fighting.
And to fight and die is death destroying death;
where fearing dying pays death a servile breath.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
My father has a power; inquire of him
and learn to make a body of a limb.

KING RICHARD II:
You scold me well. Proud Bolingbroke, I come
to exchange blows with you for our day of doom.
This ague fit of fear is over-blown;
it is an easy task to win our own.
Say, Scroop, where is our uncle with his power?
Speak sweetly, man, although your looks are sour.

SIR STEPHEN SCROOP:
Men judge by the complexion of the sky
the state and inclination of the day.
So you may, by my dull and heavy eye,
my tongue has but a heavier tale to say.
I play the torturer, by small and small
to lengthen out the worst that must be spoken.
Your uncle York has joined with Bolingbroke,
and all your northern castles have been yielded up,
and all your southern gentlemen are in arms
on his side.

KING RICHARD II:
You have said enough.
Shame on you, cousin, who led me out
of that sweet way I was in to despair!
What do you say now? What comfort do we have now?
By heaven, I'll hate him everlastingly
that bids me be of comfort any more.
Go to Flint Castle; there I'll pine away.
A king, woe's slave, shall kingly woe obey.
That power I have, discharge; and let them go
to till the land that has some hope to grow,
for I have none. Let no man speak again
to alter this, for counsel is but in vain.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
My liege, one word.

KING RICHARD II:
He does me double wrong
that wounds me with the flatteries of his tongue.
Discharge my followers; let them go away,
from Richard's night to Bolingbroke's fair day.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
So, by this intelligence, we learn
that the Welshmen are dispersed, and Salisbury
is gone to meet the king, who recently landed
with some few private friends on this coast.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
The news is very fair and good, my lord.
Richard has hidden his head not far from here.

DUKE OF YORK:
It would befit the Lord Northumberland
to say 'King Richard.' Alas, the heavy day
when such a sacred king should hide his head.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Your grace is mistaken; only to be brief
I left his title out.

DUKE OF YORK:
There was a time,
if you had been so brief with him, he would
have been so brief with you, as to shorten you,
for taking his head, by the length of your whole head.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Do not misunderstand, uncle, further than you should.

DUKE OF YORK:
Do not take, good cousin, more than you should,
lest you mistake that the heavens are over our heads.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I know it, uncle, and I do not oppose myself
against their will. But who comes here?
Welcome, Harry. What, will not this castle yield?

HENRY PERCY:
The castle is royally manned, my lord,
against your entrance.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Royally!
Why, does it contain no king?

HENRY PERCY:
Yes, my good lord,
it does contain a king. King Richard lies
within the limits of that lime and stone.
And with him are the Lord Aumerle, Lord Salisbury,
Sir Stephen Scroop, besides a clergyman
of holy reverence; who, I cannot learn.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Oh, it is likely the Bishop of Carlisle.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Noble lords,
go to the rude ribs of that ancient castle;
through a brazen trumpet send the breath of parley
into his ruined ears, and thus deliver:
Henry Bolingbroke
on both his knees does kiss King Richard's hand
and sends allegiance and true faith of heart
to his most royal person, having come here
even at his feet to lay my arms and power,
provided that my banishment is repealed
and my lands are restored again freely.
If not, I'll use the advantage of my power
and lay the summer's dust with showers of blood
rained from the wounds of slaughtered Englishmen.
How far off from the mind of Bolingbroke
it is that such a crimson tempest should drench
the fresh green lap of fair King Richard's land,
my stooping duty shall tenderly show.
Go, signify as much, while here we march
upon the grassy carpet of this plain.
Let's march without the noise of a threatening drum,
so that from this castle's tattered battlements
our fair appointments may be well perused.
I think King Richard and I should meet
with no less terror than the elements
of fire and water, when their thundering shock
at meeting tears the cloudy cheeks of heaven.
Let him be the fire, I'll be the yielding water.
Let the rage be his, while on the earth I rain
my waters; on the earth, and not on him.
March on, and mark how King Richard looks.
See, see, King Richard himself appears,
as does the blushing, discontented sun
from out the fiery portal of the east,
when he perceives the envious clouds are bent
to dim his glory and to stain the track
of his bright passage to the west.

DUKE OF YORK:
Yet he looks like a king. Behold, his eye,
as bright as the eagle's, shines forth
with controlling majesty. Alas, alas, for woe,
that any harm should stain so fair a show!

KING RICHARD II:
We are amazed; and we have stood this long
to watch the fearful bending of your knee,
because we thought we were your lawful king.
And if we are, how dare your joints forget
to pay their awful duty to our presence?
If we are not, show us the hand of God
that has dismissed us from our stewardship;
for we know well, no hand of blood and bone
can grip the sacred handle of our scepter,
unless he profanes, steals, or usurps.
And though you think that all, as you have done,
have torn their souls by turning them from us,
and we are barren and bereft of friends;
yet know, my master, God omnipotent,
is mustering in his clouds on our behalf
armies of pestilence; and they shall strike
your children yet unborn and unbegot,
that lift your vassal hands against my head
and threaten the glory of my precious crown.
Tell Bolingbroke—for there I think he stands—
that every stride he makes upon my land
is dangerous treason. He has come to open
the purple testament of bleeding war;
but before the crown he looks for can live in peace,
ten thousand bloody crowns of mothers' sons
shall ill become the flower of England's face,
change the complexion of her maiden-pale peace
