to scarlet indignation, and bedew
her pastures' grass with faithful English blood.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
May the king of heaven forbid that our lord the king
should be so rushed upon with civil and uncivil arms!
Your thrice-noble cousin,
Harry Bolingbroke, humbly kisses your hand;
and by the honorable tomb he swears,
that stands upon your royal grandsire's bones,
and by the royalties of both your bloods,
currents that spring from one most gracious head,
and by the buried hand of warlike Gaunt,
and by his own worth and honor,
comprising all that may be sworn or said,
his coming here has no further scope
than for his lineal royalties and to beg
for immediate enfranchisement on his knees.
Which, once granted on your royal part,
he will commend his glittering arms to rust,
his barbed steeds to stables, and his heart
to the faithful service of your majesty.
This he swears, as he is a prince, is just;
and, as I am a gentleman, I believe him.

KING RICHARD II:
Northumberland, say thus the king returns:
his noble cousin is right welcome here;
and all the number of his fair demands
shall be accomplished without contradiction.
With all the gracious utterance you have,
speak kind commendations to his gentle hearing.
We do debase ourselves, cousin, do we not,
to look so poorly and to speak so fair?
Shall we call back Northumberland, and send
defiance to the traitor, and so die?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
No, good my lord; let's fight with gentle words
until time lends us friends and friends their helpful swords.

KING RICHARD II:
Oh God, oh God! that this tongue of mine,
that laid the sentence of dread banishment
on that proud man, should take it off again
with words of comfort! Oh, that I were as great
as my grief, or lesser than my name!
Or that I could forget what I have been,
or not remember what I must be now!
Are you swelling, proud heart? I'll give you scope to beat,
since foes have scope to beat both you and me.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Northumberland is coming back from Bolingbroke.

KING RICHARD II:
What must the king do now? Must he submit?
The king shall do it. Must he be deposed?
The king shall be contented. Must he lose
the name of king? In God's name, let it go.
I'll give my jewels for a set of beads,
my gorgeous palace for a hermitage,
my gay apparel for an almsman's gown,
my figured goblets for a dish of wood,
my scepter for a palmer's walking staff,
my subjects for a pair of carved saints,
and my large kingdom for a little grave,
a little, little grave, an obscure grave;
or I'll be buried in the king's highway,
some way of common trade, where subjects' feet
may hourly trample on their sovereign's head;
for on my heart they tread now while I live;
and once I'm buried, why not upon my head?
Aumerle, you are weeping, my tender-hearted cousin!
We'll make foul weather with despised tears;
our sighs and they shall lodge the summer corn,
and make a dearth in this revolting land.
Or shall we play the wantons with our woes,
and make some pretty match with shedding tears?
As thus, to drop them still upon one place,
until they have fretted us a pair of graves
within the earth; and, therein laid—there lies
two kinsmen who dug their graves with weeping eyes.
Wouldn't this ill do well? Well, well, I see
I'm just talking idly, and you are laughing at me.
Most mighty prince, my Lord Northumberland,
what says King Bolingbroke? Will his majesty
give Richard leave to live until Richard dies?
You make a leg, and Bolingbroke says yes.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
My lord, he is waiting in the base court
to speak with you; may it please you to come down.

KING RICHARD II:
Down, down I come; like the glittering Phaethon,
lacking the control of unruly horses.
In the base court? The base court, where kings grow base,
to come at traitors' calls and do them grace.
In the base court? Come down? Down, court!
Down, king!
For night-owls shriek where mounting larks
should sing.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
What does his majesty say?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Sorrow and grief of heart
make him speak foolishly, like a frantic man.
Yet he has come.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Stand all apart,
and show fair duty to his majesty.
My gracious lord,—

KING RICHARD II:
Fair cousin, you are debasing your princely knee
to make the base earth proud with kissing it.
I would rather my heart might feel your love
than my unpleased eye see your courtesy.
Up, cousin, up; your heart is up, I know,
at least this high, although your knee is low.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
My gracious lord, I come only for what is mine.

KING RICHARD II:
What is yours is yours, and I am yours, and all.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
So far be it mine, my most redoubted lord,
as my true service shall deserve your love.

KING RICHARD II:
You deserve well. They well deserve to have,
that know the strongest and surest way to get.
Uncle, give me your hands. No, dry your eyes;
tears show their love, but they lack their remedies.
Cousin, I am too young to be your father,
though you are old enough to be my heir.
What you will have, I'll give, and willingly too;
for we must do what force will have us do.
Let's go towards London, cousin, is it so?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Yes, my good lord.

KING RICHARD II:
Then I must not say no.

QUEEN:
What sport shall we devise here in this garden,
to drive away the heavy thought of care?

Lady:
Madam, we'll play at bowls.

QUEEN:
It will make me think the world is full of rubs,
and that my fortune is rubbing against the bias.

Lady:
Madam, we'll dance.

QUEEN:
My legs can keep no measure in delight,
when my poor heart keeps no measure in grief.
Therefore, no dancing, girl; some other sport.

Lady:
Madam, we'll tell tales.

QUEEN:
Of sorrow or of joy?

Lady:
Of either, madam.

QUEEN:
Of neither, girl.
For of joy, being altogether wanting,
it reminds me more of sorrow;
or if of grief, being altogether had,
it adds more sorrow to my want of joy.
For what I have I need not repeat;
and what I want it is useless to complain of.

Lady:
Madam, I'll sing.

QUEEN:
It is well that you have a reason.
But you would please me better, if you would weep.

Lady:
I could weep, madam, if it would do you good.

QUEEN:
And I could sing, if weeping would do me good,
and never borrow any tear from you.
But stay, here come the gardeners.
Let's step into the shadow of these trees.
My wretchedness to a row of pins,
they'll talk of state; for everyone does so
against a change. Woe is preceded by woe.

Gardener:
Go, bind up those dangling apricots,
which, like unruly children, make their father
stoop with the oppression of their prodigal weight.
Give some support to the bending twigs.
Go, and like an executioner,
cut off the heads of the too-fast-growing sprays,
that look too lofty in our commonwealth.
All must be even in our government.
While you are thus employed, I will go and root away
the noisome weeds, which without profit suck
the soil's fertility from the wholesome flowers.

Servant:
Why should we, within the compass of a fence,
keep law and form and due proportion,
showing, as in a model, our firm estate,
when our sea-walled garden, the whole land,
is full of weeds, her fairest flowers choked up,
her fruit-trees all uprooted, her hedges ruined,
her knots disordered, and her wholesome herbs
swarming with caterpillars?

Gardener:
Hold your peace.
He that has suffered this disordered spring
has now himself met with the fall of the leaf.
The weeds which his broad-spreading leaves did shelter,
that seemed in eating him to hold him up,
are plucked up, root and all, by Bolingbroke,
I mean the Earl of Wiltshire, Bushy, and Green.

Servant:
What, are they dead?

Gardener:
They are; and Bolingbroke
has seized the wasteful king. Oh, what a pity it is
that he had not so trimmed and dressed his land
as we do this garden! We, at the time of year,
do wound the bark, the skin of our fruit-trees,
lest, being over-proud in sap and blood,
it should confound itself with too much riches.
If he had done so to great and growing men,
they might have lived to bear, and he to taste
their fruits of duty. We lop away superfluous branches,
so that bearing boughs may live.
If he had done so, he himself would have borne the crown,
which the waste of idle hours has quite thrown down.

Servant:
What, do you think then the king will be deposed?

Gardener:
He is already depressed, and it is doubtful
that he will be deposed. Letters came last night
to a dear friend of the good Duke of York's,
that tell black news.

QUEEN:
Oh, I am pressed to death for want of speaking!
You, old Adam's likeness, set to dress this garden,
how dare your harsh, rude tongue sound this unpleasing news?
What Eve, what serpent, has suggested you
to make a second fall of cursed man?
Why do you say King Richard is deposed?
Do you dare, you little better thing than earth,
to divine his downfall? Say, where, when, and how,
did you come by this ill news? Speak, you wretch.

Gardener:
Pardon me, madam. I have little joy
to breathe this news; yet what I say is true.
King Richard, he is in the mighty hold
of Bolingbroke. Their fortunes are both weighed.
In your lord's scale is nothing but himself,
and some few vanities that make him light;
but in the balance of great Bolingbroke,
besides himself, are all the English peers,
and with that odds he weighs King Richard down.
Post to London, and you will find it so;
I speak no more than everyone knows.

QUEEN:
Nimble misfortune, that is so light of foot,
does not your message belong to me,
and am I the last that knows it? Oh, you think
to serve me last, so that I may longest keep
your sorrow in my breast. Come, ladies, go,
to meet at London London's king in woe.
What, was I born to this, that my sad look
should grace the triumph of great Bolingbroke?
Gardener, for telling me this news of woe,
I pray God the plants you graft may never grow.

GARDENER:
Poor queen! So that your state might be no worse,
I would my skill were subject to your curse.
Here she shed a tear; here in this place
I'll set a bank of rue, the sour herb of grace.
Rue, even for ruth, shall shortly be seen here,
in the remembrance of a weeping queen.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Call forth Bagot.
Now, Bagot, speak your mind freely;
what you know of the noble Gloucester's death,
who worked it with the king, and who performed
the bloody office of his untimely end.

BAGOT:
Then set Lord Aumerle before my face.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Cousin, stand forth, and look upon that man.

BAGOT:
My Lord Aumerle, I know your daring tongue
scorns to unsay what it has once delivered.
In that dead time when Gloucester's death was plotted,
I heard you say, 'Is not my arm long enough,
that it reaches from the restful English court
as far as Calais, to my uncle's head?'
Among much other talk, at that very time,
I heard you say that you would rather refuse
the offer of a hundred thousand crowns
than Bolingbroke's return to England;
adding, withal, how blessed this land would be
in your cousin's death.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Princes and noble lords,
what answer shall I make to this base man?
Shall I so much dishonor my fair stars,
as to give him chastisement on equal terms?
Either I must, or have my honor soiled
with the taint of his slanderous lips.
There is my gauntlet, the manual seal of death,
that marks you out for hell. I say, you lie,
and I will maintain that what you have said is false
in your heart-blood, though it is all too base
to stain the temper of my knightly sword.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Bagot, forbear; you shall not take it up.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Excepting one, I wish he were the best
in all this presence that has moved me so.

LORD FITZWATER:
If your valor stands on sympathy,
there is my gauntlet, Aumerle, in pledge to yours.
By that fair sun which shows me where you stand,
I heard you say, and you spoke it vauntingly,
that you were the cause of noble Gloucester's death.
If you deny it twenty times, you lie;
and I will turn your falsehood to your heart,
where it was forged, with my rapier's point.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
You dare not, coward, live to see that day.

LORD FITZWATER:
Now, by my soul, I wish it were this hour.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Fitzwater, you are damned to hell for this.

HENRY PERCY:
Aumerle, you lie; his honor is as true
in this appeal as you are all unjust.
And that you are so, there I throw my gauntlet,
to prove it on you to the extremest point
of mortal breathing. Seize it, if you dare.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
And if I do not, may my hands rot off
and never brandish a more revengeful steel
over the glittering helmet of my foe!

Lord:
I challenge you to the same, forsworn Aumerle;
and I spur you on with as many lies
as may be hollered in your treacherous ear
from sun to sun. There is my honor's pawn;
engage it to the trial, if you dare.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Who else will challenge me? By heaven, I'll take on all of you.
I have a thousand spirits in one breast,
to answer twenty thousand such as you.

DUKE OF SURREY:
My Lord Fitzwater, I remember well
the very time Aumerle and you did talk.

LORD FITZWATER:
It's very true. You were in my presence then;
and you can witness with me that this is true.

DUKE OF SURREY:
As false, by heaven, as heaven itself is true.

LORD FITZWATER:
Surrey, you lie.

DUKE OF SURREY:
Dishonorable boy!
That lie shall lie so heavy on my sword,
that it shall render vengeance and revenge
until you, the lie-giver, and that lie do lie
in the earth as quiet as your father's skull.
In proof of which, there is my honor's pawn;
engage it to the trial, if you dare.

LORD FITZWATER:
How foolishly you spur a forward horse!
If I dare eat, or drink, or breathe, or live,
I dare meet Surrey in a wilderness,
and spit upon him, while I say he lies,
and lies, and lies. There is my bond of faith,
to tie you to my strong correction.
As I intend to thrive in this new world,
Aumerle is guilty of my true appeal.
Besides, I heard the banished Norfolk say
that you, Aumerle, did send two of your men
to execute the noble duke at Calais.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Some honest Christian, trust me with a gauntlet.
That Norfolk lies, here I throw down this,
if he may be repealed, to try his honor.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
These differences shall all rest under pledge
until Norfolk is recalled. He shall be recalled,
and, though he is my enemy, he will be restored again
to all his lands and lordships. When he has returned,
we will enforce his trial against Aumerle.

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
That honorable day shall never be seen.
Many a time has the banished Norfolk fought
for Jesus Christ in the glorious Christian field,
streaming the ensign of the Christian cross
against black pagans, Turks, and Saracens.
And, wearied with the works of war, he retired
to Italy; and there at Venice gave
his body to that pleasant country's earth,
and his pure soul to his captain Christ,
under whose colors he had fought so long.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Why, bishop, is Norfolk dead?

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
As surely as I live, my lord.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
May sweet peace conduct his sweet soul to the bosom
of good old Abraham! Lords appellants,
your differences shall all rest under gage
until we assign you to your days of trial.

DUKE OF YORK:
Great Duke of Lancaster, I come to you
from the plume-plucked Richard, who with a willing soul
adopts you as his heir, and yields his high scepter
to the possession of your royal hand.
Ascend his throne, descending now from him;
and long live Henry, the fourth of that name!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
In God's name, I'll ascend the regal throne.

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
Marry, God forbid!
Worst in this royal presence may I speak,
yet it best befits me to speak the truth.
Would to God that anyone in this noble presence
were noble enough to be an upright judge
of noble Richard! Then true nobility would
teach him forbearance from so foul a wrong.
What subject can give sentence on his king?
And who sits here that is not Richard's subject?
Thieves are not judged unless they are by to hear,
although their apparent guilt is seen in them;
and shall the figure of God's majesty,
his captain, steward, deputy-elect,
anointed, crowned, and planted for many years,
be judged by a subject and an inferior's breath,
and he himself not present? Oh, God forbid it,
that in a Christian climate, refined souls
should show so heinous, black, and obscene a deed!
I speak to subjects, and a subject speaks,
stirred up by God, thus boldly for his king.
My Lord of Hereford here, whom you call king,
is a foul traitor to proud Hereford's king.
And if you crown him, let me prophesy:
the blood of the English shall manure the ground,
and future ages will groan for this foul act;
peace shall go to sleep with Turks and infidels,
and in this seat of peace, tumultuous wars
shall confound kin with kin and kind with kind;
disorder, horror, fear, and mutiny
shall inhabit here, and this land will be called
the field of Golgotha and dead men's skulls.
Oh, if you raise this house against this house,
it will prove the most woeful division
that ever fell upon this cursed earth.
Prevent it, resist it, let it not be so,
lest your children's children cry against you with woe!

NORTHUMBERLAND:
You have argued well, sir; and, for your pains,
we arrest you here for capital treason.
My Lord of Westminster, it will be your charge
to keep him safely until his day of trial.
May it please you, lords, to grant the commons' suit.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Fetch Richard here, so that in common view
he may surrender; so we shall proceed
without suspicion.

DUKE OF YORK:
I will be his guide.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Lords, you that are under our arrest here,
procure your sureties for your days of answer.
We are little beholden to your love,
and we looked for little from your helping hands.

KING RICHARD II:
Alas, why am I sent for to a king,
before I have shaken off the regal thoughts
with which I reigned? I have hardly yet learned
to insinuate, flatter, bow, and bend my limbs.
Give sorrow leave for a while to teach me
this submission. Yet I well remember
the favors of these men. Were they not mine?
Did they not sometimes cry, 'All hail!' to me?
So Judas did to Christ. But he, in twelve,
found truth in all but one. I, in twelve thousand, found none.
God save the king! Will no man say amen?
Am I both priest and clerk? Well then, amen.
God save the king! Although I am not he;
and yet, amen, if heaven does think him me.
To do what service am I sent for here?

DUKE OF YORK:
To do that office of your own good will
which your tired majesty did offer you,
the resignation of your state and crown
to Henry Bolingbroke.

KING RICHARD II:
Give me the crown. Here, cousin, seize the crown;
here, cousin.
On this side my hand, and on that side yours.
Now this golden crown is like a deep well
that owes two buckets, filling one another,
the emptier one ever dancing in the air,
the other down, unseen and full of water.
That bucket down and full of tears am I,
drinking my griefs, while you mount up on high.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I thought you were willing to resign.

KING RICHARD II:
I am willing to resign my crown, but my griefs are still mine.
You may depose my glories and my state,
but not my griefs; I am still the king of those.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
You give me part of your cares with your crown.

KING RICHARD II:
Your cares, when they are set up, do not pluck my cares down.
My care is the loss of care, done by old care;
your care is the gain of care, won by new care.
The cares I give, I still have, though they are given away;
they tend to the crown, yet they still stay with me.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Are you content to resign the crown?

KING RICHARD II:
Yes, no; no, yes; for I must be nothing;
therefore no, for I resign to you.
Now mark me, how I will undo myself.
I give this heavy weight from off my head,
and this unwieldy scepter from my hand,
the pride of kingly sway from out my heart;
with my own tears I wash away my balm,
with my own hands I give away my crown,
with my own tongue I deny my sacred state,
with my own breath I release all the rites of duty.
All pomp and majesty I do forswear;
my manors, rents, and revenues I forgo;
my acts, decrees, and statutes I deny.
God pardon all oaths that are broken to me!
God keep all vows unbroken that are sworn to you!
Make me, that have nothing, be grieved with nothing,
and you, with all pleased, that have achieved all!
Long may you live in Richard's seat to sit,
and soon may Richard lie in an earthly pit!
God save King Harry, says un-kinged Richard,
and send him many years of sunshine days!
What more remains?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
No more, but that you read
these accusations and these grievous crimes
committed by your person and your followers
against the state and profit of this land;
so that, by confessing them, the souls of men
may deem that you are worthily deposed.

KING RICHARD II:
Must I do so? And must I unravel
my woven-up folly? Gentle Northumberland,
if your offenses were on record,
would it not shame you in so fair a troop
to read a lecture of them? If you would,
there you would find one heinous article,
containing the deposing of a king
and the cracking of the strong warrant of an oath,
marked with a blot, damned in the book of heaven.
No, all of you that stand and look on,
while my wretchedness baits myself,
though some of you with Pilate wash your hands
showing an outward pity; yet you Pilates
have here delivered me to my sour cross,
and water cannot wash away your sin.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
My lord, dispatch; read over these articles.

KING RICHARD II:
My eyes are full of tears, I cannot see;
and yet salt water does not blind them so much
but that they can see a group of traitors here.
No, if I turn my eyes upon myself,
I find myself a traitor with the rest;
for I have given here my soul's consent
to un-deck the pompous body of a king;
I have made glory base and sovereignty a slave,
proud majesty a subject, and state a peasant.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
My lord,—

KING RICHARD II:
No lord of yours, you haughty, insulting man,
nor any man's lord; I have no name, no title,
no, not even the name that was given to me at the font,
but it is usurped. Alas, the heavy day,
that I have worn out so many winters,
and now I do not know what name to call myself!
Oh, that I were a mockery king of snow,
standing before the sun of Bolingbroke,
to melt myself away in water-drops!
Good king, great king, and yet not greatly good,
if my word is still sterling in England,
let it command a mirror to be brought here straight,
so that it may show me what a face I have,
since it is bankrupt of its majesty.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Go, some of you, and fetch a looking-glass.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Read over this paper while the glass is coming.

KING RICHARD II:
Fiend, you torment me before I come to hell!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Urge it no more, my Lord Northumberland.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
The commons will not then be satisfied.

KING RICHARD II:
They shall be satisfied. I'll read enough,
when I see the very book indeed
where all my sins are written, and that's myself.
Give me the glass, and in it I will read.
No deeper wrinkles yet? Has sorrow struck
so many blows upon this face of mine,
and made no deeper wounds? Oh, flattering glass,
like my followers in prosperity,
you are beguiling me! Was this the face
that every day under his household roof
kept ten thousand men? Was this the face
that, like the sun, made beholders wink?
Was this the face that faced so many follies,
and was at last out-faced by Bolingbroke?
A brittle glory shines in this face.
As brittle as the glory is the face;
for there it is, cracked in a hundred shivers.
Mark, silent king, the moral of this sport,
how soon my sorrow has destroyed my face.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
The shadow of your sorrow has destroyed
the shadow of your face.

KING RICHARD II:
Say that again.
The shadow of my sorrow! Ha! Let's see.
It's very true, my grief lies all within;
and these external manners of laments
are merely shadows to the unseen grief
that swells with silence in the tortured soul;
there lies the substance. And I thank you, king,
for your great bounty, that not only gives
me cause to wail but teaches me the way
how to lament the cause. I'll beg one boon,
and then be gone and trouble you no more.
Shall I obtain it?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Name it, fair cousin.

KING RICHARD II:
'Fair cousin'? I am greater than a king.
For when I was a king, my flatterers
were then but subjects; being now a subject,
I have a king here to be my flatterer.
Being so great, I have no need to beg.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Yet ask.

KING RICHARD II:
And shall I have?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
You shall.

KING RICHARD II:
Then give me leave to go.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Where to?

KING RICHARD II:
Wherever you will, as long as I am out of your sight.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Go, some of you, and convey him to the Tower.

KING RICHARD II:
Oh, good! Convey? You are all conveyors,
that rise so nimbly by a true king's fall.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
On Wednesday next, we will solemnly set down
our coronation. Lords, prepare yourselves.

Abbot:
We have beheld a woeful pageant here.

BISHOP OF CARLISLE:
The woe is yet to come; the children yet unborn
shall feel this day as sharp to them as a thorn.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
You holy clergymen, is there no plot
to rid the realm of this pernicious blot?

Abbot:
My lord,
before I freely speak my mind in this,
you shall not only take the sacrament
to bury my intents, but also to effect
whatever I shall happen to devise.
I see your brows are full of discontent,
your hearts of sorrow, and your eyes of tears.
Come home with me to supper; and I'll lay
a plot that shall show us all a merry day.

QUEEN:
This way the king will come; this is the way
to Julius Caesar's ill-erected tower,
to whose flint bosom my condemned lord
is doomed a prisoner by proud Bolingbroke.
Here let us rest, if this rebellious earth
has any resting place for her true king's queen.
But wait, but see, or rather do not see,
my fair rose wither. Yet look up, behold,
that you in pity may dissolve to dew,
and wash him fresh again with true-love tears.
Ah, you, the model where old Troy did stand,
you map of honor, you King Richard's tomb,
and not King Richard; you most beautiful inn,
why should hard-favored grief be lodged in you,
when triumph has become an alehouse guest?

KING RICHARD II:
Don't join with grief, fair woman, don't do so,
to make my end too sudden. Learn, good soul,
to think of our former state as a happy dream;
from which, when we are awakened, the truth of what we are
shows us but this: I am a sworn brother, sweet,
to grim Necessity, and he and I
will keep a league until death. Hurry to France
and cloister yourself in some religious house.
Our holy lives must win a new world's crown,
which our profane hours here have stricken down.

QUEEN:
What, is my Richard both in shape and mind
transformed and weakened? Has Bolingbroke deposed
your intellect? Has he been in your heart?
The dying lion thrusts out his paw,
and wounds the earth, if nothing else, with rage
to be overpowered. And will you, like a pupil,
take your correction mildly, kiss the rod,
and fawn on rage with base humility,
you who are a lion and a king of beasts?

KING RICHARD II:
A king of beasts, indeed. If I were anything but a beast,
I would have still been a happy king of men.
Good sometime queen, prepare to go to France.
Think I am dead, and that even here you are taking,
as from my death-bed, your last living leave.
In winter's tedious nights, sit by the fire
with good old folks and let them tell you tales
of woeful ages long ago.
And before you bid good night, to relieve their grief,
tell the lamentable tale of me
and send the hearers weeping to their beds.
For why, the senseless brands will sympathize
with the heavy accent of your moving tongue
and in compassion weep the fire out;
and some will mourn in ashes, some coal-black,
for the deposing of a rightful king.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
My lord, Bolingbroke's mind is changed.
You must go to Pomfret, not to the Tower.
And, madam, there is an order taken for you;
with all swift speed you must go to France.

KING RICHARD II:
Northumberland, you are the ladder with which
the mounting Bolingbroke ascends my throne.
The time shall not be many hours older
than it is before foul sin, gathering a head,
shall break into corruption. You shall think,
though he divides the realm and gives you half,
it is too little, for helping him to all;
and he shall think that you, who know the way
to plant unrightful kings, will know again,
being ever so little urged, another way
to pluck him headlong from the usurped throne.
The love of wicked men converts to fear;
that fear to hate, and hate turns one or both
to a worthy danger and a deserved death.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
May my guilt be on my head, and that's the end of it.
Take your leave and part, for you must part immediately.

KING RICHARD II:
Doubly divorced! Bad men, you violate
a twofold marriage, between my crown and me,
and then between me and my married wife.
Let me un-kiss the oath between you and me;
and yet not so, for it was made with a kiss.
Part us, Northumberland. I go toward the north,
where shivering cold and sickness pine the climate;
my wife to France, from where, set forth in pomp,
she came adorned here like sweet May,
and is sent back like Hallowmas or the shortest day.

QUEEN:
And must we be divided? Must we part?

KING RICHARD II:
Yes, hand from hand, my love, and heart from heart.

QUEEN:
Banish us both and send the king with me.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
That would be some love, but little policy.

QUEEN:
Then wherever he goes, there let me go.

KING RICHARD II:
So two, weeping together, make one woe.
You weep for me in France, I for you here;
it is better to be far off than near, and never to be near.
Go, count your way with sighs; I will count mine with groans.

QUEEN:
So the longest way shall have the longest moans.

KING RICHARD II:
I'll groan twice for one step, the way being short,
and I'll piece out the way with a heavy heart.
Come, come, in wooing sorrow let's be brief,
since, in marrying it, there is such length in grief.
One kiss shall stop our mouths, and we shall part dumbly;
thus I give you mine, and thus I take your heart.

QUEEN:
Give me my own again; it would not be a good part
to take it upon me to keep and kill your heart.
So, now I have my own again, be gone,
so that I might strive to kill it with a groan.

KING RICHARD II:
We make woe wanton with this fond delay.
Once more, adieu; the rest let sorrow say.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
My lord, you told me you would tell me the rest,
when weeping made you break off the story,
of our two cousins coming into London.

DUKE OF YORK:
Where did I leave off?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
At that sad stop, my lord,
where rude, misgoverned hands from the tops of windows
threw dust and rubbish on King Richard's head.

DUKE OF YORK:
Then, as I said, the duke, great Bolingbroke,
mounted upon a hot and fiery steed
which his aspiring rider seemed to know,
kept on his course with a slow but stately pace,
while all tongues cried, 'God save you,
Bolingbroke!'
You would have thought the very windows spoke,
so many greedy looks of young and old
through casements darted their desiring eyes
upon his face, and that all the walls
with painted imagery had said at once,
'Jesus preserve you! Welcome, Bolingbroke!'
While he, turning from one side to the other,
bareheaded, lower than his proud steed's neck,
spoke to them thus: 'I thank you, countrymen.'
And thus still doing, he passed along.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Alas, poor Richard! Where did he ride in the meantime?

DUKE OF YORK:
As in a theater, the eyes of men,
after a well-graced actor leaves the stage,
are idly bent on him that enters next,
thinking his prattle to be tedious;
even so, or with much more contempt, men's eyes
did scowl on gentle Richard. No man cried, 'God save him!'
No joyful tongue gave him his welcome home.
But dust was thrown upon his sacred head,
which he shook off with such gentle sorrow,
his face still combating with tears and smiles,
the badges of his grief and patience,
that if God, for some strong purpose, had not steeled
the hearts of men, they must have perforce melted
and barbarism itself would have pitied him.
But heaven has a hand in these events,
to whose high will we bind our calm contents.
To Bolingbroke we are sworn subjects now,
whose state and honor I for ever allow.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Here comes my son Aumerle.

DUKE OF YORK:
Aumerle that was;
but that is lost for being Richard's friend,
and, madam, you must call him Rutland now.
I am in parliament, a pledge for his truth
and lasting fealty to the new-made king.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Welcome, my son. Who are the violets now
that strew the green lap of the new-come spring?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Madam, I don't know, nor do I greatly care.
God knows I would as soon be none as one.

DUKE OF YORK:
Well, carry yourself well in this new spring of time,
lest you be cropped before you come to your prime.
What news from Oxford? Are those jousts and triumphs still on?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
For all I know, my lord, they are.

DUKE OF YORK:
You will be there, I know.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
If God does not prevent it, I plan to.

DUKE OF YORK:
What seal is that, that hangs outside your bosom?
Yes, you look pale. Let me see the writing.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
My lord, it is nothing.

DUKE OF YORK:
It doesn't matter, then, who sees it;
I will be satisfied. Let me see the writing.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
I do beseech your grace to pardon me.
It is a matter of small consequence,
which for some reasons I would not have seen.

DUKE OF YORK:
Which for some reasons, sir, I mean to see.
I fear, I fear—

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What should you fear?
It's nothing but some bond that he has entered into
for gay apparel for the triumph day.

DUKE OF YORK:
Bound to himself! What is he doing with a bond
that he is bound to? Wife, you are a fool.
Boy, let me see the writing.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
I beseech you, pardon me; I may not show it.

DUKE OF YORK:
I will be satisfied; let me see it, I say.
Treason! Foul treason! Villain! Traitor! Slave!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What is the matter, my lord?

DUKE OF YORK:
Ho! Who is within there?
Saddle my horse.
God have mercy, what treachery is here!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Why, what is it, my lord?

DUKE OF YORK:
Give me my boots, I say; saddle my horse.
Now, by my honor, by my life, by my troth,
I will accuse the villain.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
What is the matter?

DUKE OF YORK:
Peace, foolish woman.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I will not be at peace. What is the matter, Aumerle?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Good mother, be content; it is no more
than what my poor life must answer for.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Your life must answer!

DUKE OF YORK:
Bring me my boots! I will go to the king.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Strike him, Aumerle. Poor boy, you are amazed.
Go away, villain! Never come in my sight again.

DUKE OF YORK:
Give me my boots, I say.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Why, York, what will you do?
Will you not hide the trespass of your own?
Do we have more sons? Or are we likely to have?
Is not my teeming date drunk up with time?
And will you pluck my fair son from my age,
and rob me of a happy mother's name?
Is he not like you? Is he not your own?

DUKE OF YORK:
You foolish, mad woman,
will you conceal this dark conspiracy?
A dozen of them have taken the sacrament here,
and interchangeably set down their hands,
to kill the king at Oxford.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
He shall be none of them;
we'll keep him here. Then what is that to him?

DUKE OF YORK:
Away, foolish woman! If he were twenty times my son,
I would still accuse him.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
If you had groaned for him
as I have done, you would be more pitiful.
But now I know your mind; you suspect
that I have been disloyal to your bed,
and that he is a bastard, not your son.
Sweet York, sweet husband, do not be of that mind.
He is as like you as a man can be,
not like me, or any of my kin,
and yet I love him.

DUKE OF YORK:
Make way, unruly woman!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
After him, Aumerle! Mount his horse;
spur on, and get before him to the king,
and beg your pardon before he accuses you.
I'll not be long behind. Though I am old,
I have no doubt I can ride as fast as York.
And I will never rise up from the ground
until Bolingbroke has pardoned you. Away, be gone!

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Can no man tell me of my unthrifty son?
It's been a full three months since I last saw him.
If any plague hangs over us, it's he.
I wish to God, my lords, he might be found.
Inquire at London, among the taverns there,
for there, they say, he daily frequents,
with unrestrained, loose companions,
even such, they say, as stand in narrow lanes,
and beat our watch, and rob our passengers;
which he, a young, wanton, and effeminate boy,
takes upon himself as a point of honor to support,
so dissolute a crew.

HENRY PERCY:
My lord, some two days since I saw the prince,
and told him of those triumphs held at Oxford.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
And what did the gallant say?

HENRY PERCY:
His answer was, he would go to the stews,
and from the commonest creature pluck a glove,
and wear it as a favor; and with that
he would unhorse the lustiest challenger.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
As dissolute as he is desperate; yet through both
I see some sparks of better hope, which elder years
may happily bring forth. But who comes here?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Where is the king?

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
What does our cousin mean, that he stares and looks
so wildly?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
God save your grace! I beseech your majesty,
to have some conference with your grace alone.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Withdraw yourselves, and leave us here alone.
What is the matter with our cousin now?

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
May my knees forever grow to the earth,
my tongue cleave to the roof of my mouth,
unless I receive a pardon before I rise or speak.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Was this fault intended or committed?
If it was the first, however heinous it may be,
to win your after-love, I pardon you.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Then give me leave that I may turn the key,
so that no man may enter until my tale is done.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Have your desire.

DUKE OF YORK:

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Villain, I'll make you safe.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Stay your revengeful hand; you have no cause to fear.

DUKE OF YORK:

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
What is the matter, uncle? Speak;
recover your breath. Tell us how near the danger is,
so that we may arm ourselves to encounter it.

DUKE OF YORK:
Read this writing here, and you will know
the treason that my haste forbids me to show.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
Remember, as you read, your promise past.
I repent. Do not read my name there.
My heart is not confederate with my hand.

DUKE OF YORK:
It was, villain, before your hand set it down.
I tore it from the traitor's bosom, king;
fear, and not love, begets his penitence.
Forget to pity him, lest your pity prove
a serpent that will sting you to the heart.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Oh, heinous, strong, and bold conspiracy!
Oh, loyal father of a treacherous son!
You are a sheer, immaculate, and silver fountain,
from which this stream, through muddy passages,
has held its current and defiled itself!
Your overflow of good converts to bad,
and your abundant goodness shall excuse
this deadly blot in your digressing son.

DUKE OF YORK:
So my virtue will be his vice's bawd;
and he will spend my honor with his shame,
as thriftless sons do their scraping fathers' gold.
My honor lives when his dishonor dies,
or my shamed life lies in his dishonor.
You kill me in his life; by giving him breath,
the traitor lives, and the true man is put to death.

DUCHESS OF YORK:

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
What shrill-voiced supplicant makes this eager cry?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
A woman, and your aunt, great king; it's I.
Speak with me, pity me, open the door.
A beggar begs that never begged before.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Our scene has changed from a serious thing,
and is now changed to 'The Beggar and the King.'
My dangerous cousin, let your mother in.
I know she has come to pray for your foul sin.

DUKE OF YORK:
If you pardon, whoever prays,
more sins for this forgiveness may prosper.
This festered joint cut off, the rest will rest sound;
this left alone will confound all the rest.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
O king, do not believe this hard-hearted man!
Love that does not love itself can love no other.

DUKE OF YORK:
You frantic woman, what are you doing here?
Shall your old breasts once more raise a traitor?

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Sweet York, be patient. Hear me, gentle liege.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Rise up, good aunt.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Not yet, I beseech you.
I will forever walk upon my knees,
and never see the day that the happy see,
until you give joy; until you bid me joy,
by pardoning Rutland, my transgressing boy.

DUKE OF AUMERLE:
I bend my knee to my mother's prayers.

DUKE OF YORK:
Against them both, my true joints are bent.
May you thrive ill, if you grant any grace!

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Does he plead in earnest? Look upon his face;
his eyes drop no tears, his prayers are in jest;
his words come from his mouth, ours from our breast.
He prays but faintly and would be denied;
we pray with heart and soul and all beside.
His weary joints would gladly rise, I know;
our knees shall kneel until they grow to the ground.
His prayers are full of false hypocrisy;
ours of true zeal and deep integrity.
Our prayers out-pray his; then let them have
that mercy which true prayer ought to have.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Good aunt, stand up.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
No, do not say, 'Stand up.'
Say, 'pardon' first, and afterwards 'stand up.'
And if I were your nurse, to teach your tongue,
'pardon' would be the first word of your speech.
I never longed to hear a word until now.
Say 'pardon,' king; let pity teach you how.
The word is short, but not so short as sweet;
no word like 'pardon' is so fitting for kings' mouths.

DUKE OF YORK:
Speak it in French, king; say, 'pardonne-moi.'

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Do you teach pardon to destroy pardon?
Ah, my sour husband, my hard-hearted lord,
that sets the word itself against the word!
Speak 'pardon' as it is current in our land;
we do not understand the chopping French.
Your eye begins to speak; set your tongue there;
or in your piteous heart, plant your ear;
so that hearing how our plaints and prayers pierce,
pity may move you to rehearse 'pardon.'

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Good aunt, stand up.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
I do not sue to stand;
pardon is all the suit I have in hand.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
I pardon him, as God shall pardon me.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Oh, happy advantage of a kneeling knee!
Yet I am sick for fear. Speak it again;
saying 'pardon' twice does not make two pardons,
but makes one pardon strong.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
With all my heart,
I pardon him.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
You are a god on earth.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
But as for our trusty brother-in-law and the abbot,
with all the rest of that consorted crew,
destruction shall straight away dog them at the heels.
Good uncle, help to order several forces
to Oxford, or wherever these traitors are.
They shall not live within this world, I swear,
but I will have them, if I once know where.
Uncle, farewell. And, cousin too, adieu.
Your mother has prayed well, and may you prove true.

DUCHESS OF YORK:
Come, my old son. I pray God makes you new.

EXTON:
Did you not notice the king, what words he spoke,
'Have I no friend who will rid me of this living fear?'
Was it not so?

Servant:
Those were his very words.

EXTON:
'Have I no friend?' he said. He spoke it twice,
and urged it twice together, did he not?

Servant:
He did.

EXTON:
And speaking it, he looked wistfully at me,
as if to say, 'I wish you were the man
that would divorce this terror from my heart,'
meaning the king at Pomfret. Come, let's go.
I am the king's friend, and I will rid him of his foe.

KING RICHARD II:
I have been studying how I may compare
this prison where I live to the world.
And because the world is populous
and here there is not a creature but myself,
I cannot do it; yet I'll hammer it out.
My brain I'll prove the female to my soul,
my soul the father; and these two will beget
a generation of still-breeding thoughts,
and these same thoughts will people this little world,
in humors like the people of this world,
for no thought is contented. The better sort,
as thoughts of divine things, are intermixed
with scruples and set the word itself
against the word,
as thus, 'Come, little ones,' and then again,
'It is as hard to come as for a camel
to thread the postern of a small needle's eye.'
Thoughts tending to ambition, they plot
unlikely wonders; how these vain, weak nails
may tear a passage through the flinty ribs
of this hard world, my ragged prison walls,
and, because they cannot, they die in their own pride.
Thoughts tending to contentment flatter themselves
that they are not the first of fortune's slaves,
nor shall they be the last; like silly beggars
who, sitting in the stocks, take refuge in their shame,
that many have and others must sit there;
and in this thought they find a kind of ease,
bearing their own misfortunes on the back
of such as have endured the like before.
Thus I play many people in one person,
and none of them are contented. Sometimes I am a king;
then treasons make me wish I were a beggar,
and so I am. Then crushing poverty
persuades me I was better when I was a king;
then I am a king again. And by and by,
I think that I am un-kinged by Bolingbroke,
and straight I am nothing. But whatever I may be,
neither I nor any man that is but a man
will be pleased with anything, until he is eased
by being nothing. Do I hear music?
Ha, ha! Keep time. How sour sweet music is,
when time is broken and no proportion is kept!
So it is in the music of men's lives.
And here I have the daintiness of ear
to check a broken time in a disordered string;
but for the concord of my state and time,
I did not have an ear to hear my true time broken.
I wasted time, and now time wastes me;
for now time has made me his numbering clock.
My thoughts are minutes; and with sighs they jar
their watches on to my eyes, the outward watch,
to which my finger, like a dial's point,
is pointing still, in cleansing them from tears.
Now sir, the sound that tells what hour it is
are clamorous groans, which strike upon my heart,
which is the bell. So sighs and tears and groans
show minutes, times, and hours. But my time
runs posting on in Bolingbroke's proud joy,
while I stand fooling here, his jack-o'-the-clock.
This music makes me mad; let it sound no more;
for though it has helped madmen to their wits,
in me it seems it will make wise men mad.
Yet a blessing on his heart that gives it to me!
For it is a sign of love; and love for Richard
is a strange brooch in this all-hating world.

Groom:
Hail, royal prince!

KING RICHARD II:
Thanks, noble peer;
the cheapest of us is ten groats too dear.
What are you? And how did you come here,
where no man ever comes but that sad dog
that brings me food to make misfortune live?

Groom:
I was a poor groom of your stable, king,
when you were king; who, traveling towards York,
with much ado at length have gotten leave
to look upon my sometimes royal master's face.
Oh, how it yearned my heart when I beheld
in the streets of London, on that coronation-day,
when Bolingbroke rode on roan Barbary,
that horse that you have so often ridden,
that horse that I have so carefully groomed!

KING RICHARD II:
Did he ride on Barbary? Tell me, gentle friend,
how did he go under him?

Groom:
So proudly as if he disdained the ground.

KING RICHARD II:
So proud that Bolingbroke was on his back!
That jade has eaten bread from my royal hand;
this hand has made him proud by clapping him.
Would he not stumble? Would he not fall down,
since pride must have a fall, and break the neck
of that proud man that did usurp his back?
Forgiveness, horse! Why do I rail on you,
since you, created to be awed by man,
were born to bear? I was not made a horse;
and yet I bear a burden like an ass,
spurred, galled, and tired by jouncing Bolingbroke.

Keeper:
Fellow, give place; there is no longer a stay here.

KING RICHARD II:
If you love me, it is time you were away.

Groom:
What my tongue dares not, that my heart shall say.

Keeper:
My lord, will it please you to begin?

KING RICHARD II:
Taste of it first, as you are wont to do.

Keeper:
My lord, I dare not. Sir Pierce of Exton, who
recently came from the king, commands the contrary.

KING RICHARD II:
The devil take Henry of Lancaster and you!
Patience is stale, and I am weary of it.

Keeper:
Help, help, help!

KING RICHARD II:
What now! What does death mean in this rude assault?
Villain, your own hand yields your death's instrument.
Go, and fill another room in hell.
That hand shall burn in never-quenching fire
that staggers my person thus. Exton, your fierce hand
has stained the king's own land with the king's blood.
Mount, mount, my soul! Your seat is up on high;
while my gross flesh sinks downward, here to die.

EXTON:
As full of valor as of royal blood.
I have spilled both. Oh, I wish the deed were good!
For now the devil, that told me I did well,
says that this deed is chronicled in hell.
I'll bear this dead king to the living king.
Take the rest from here, and give them burial here.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Kind uncle York, the latest news we hear
is that the rebels have consumed our town of Cirencester in Gloucestershire with fire;
but whether they have been taken or slain, we do not hear.
Welcome, my lord. What is the news?

NORTHUMBERLAND:
First, I wish all happiness to your sacred state.
The next news is, I have sent to London
the heads of Oxford, Salisbury, Blunt, and Kent.
The manner of their taking may appear
at large in this paper here.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
We thank you, gentle Percy, for your pains;
and to your worth we will add right worthy gains.

LORD FITZWATER:
My lord, I have sent from Oxford to London
the heads of Brocas and Sir Bennet Seely,
two of the dangerous, consorted traitors
that sought your dire overthrow at Oxford.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Your pains, Fitzwater, shall not be forgotten;
your merit is right noble, I know well.

HENRY PERCY:
The grand conspirator, the Abbot of Westminster,
with a clog of conscience and sour melancholy,
has yielded up his body to the grave;
but here is Carlisle living, to abide
your kingly doom and the sentence of his pride.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Carlisle, this is your doom:
choose out some secret place, some reverend room,
more than you have, and with it enjoy your life;
so that you live in peace, and die free from strife.
For though you have ever been my enemy,
I have seen high sparks of honor in you.

EXTON:
Great king, within this coffin I present
your buried fear. Herein lies all breathless
the mightiest of your greatest enemies,
Richard of Bordeaux, brought here by me.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
Exton, I do not thank you; for you have wrought
a deed of slander with your fatal hand
upon my head and all this famous land.

EXTON:
From your own mouth, my lord, I did this deed.

HENRY BOLINGBROKE:
They do not love poison that need poison,
nor do I love you. Though I did wish him dead,
I hate the murderer, but I love him murdered.
Take the guilt of your conscience for your labor,
but neither my good word nor my princely favor.
Go wander through the shades of night with Cain,
and never show your head by day or light.
Lords, I protest, my soul is full of woe,
that blood should sprinkle me to make me grow.
Come, mourn with me for what I do lament,
and put on sullen black immediately.
I'll make a voyage to the Holy Land,
to wash this blood off from my guilty hand.
March sadly after; grace my mournings here;
in weeping after this untimely bier.


SAMPSON:
Gregory, on my word, we won't carry coals.

GREGORY:
No, for then we would be colliers.

SAMPSON:
I mean, if we are angry, we'll draw our swords.

GREGORY:
Yes, as long as you live, draw your neck out of the noose.

SAMPSON:
I strike quickly, when I'm moved.

GREGORY:
But you are not quickly moved to strike.

SAMPSON:
A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

GREGORY:
To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand.
Therefore, if you are moved, you run away.

SAMPSON:
A dog of that house will move me to stand. I will
take the wall from any man or maid of Montague's.

GREGORY:
That shows you are a weak slave; for the weakest goes
to the wall.

SAMPSON:
True; and therefore women, being the weaker vessels,
are always thrust to the wall. Therefore I will push
Montague's men from the wall, and thrust his maids
to the wall.

GREGORY:
The quarrel is between our masters and us, their men.

SAMPSON:
It's all the same. I will show myself a tyrant. When I
have fought with the men, I will be cruel with the
maids, and cut off their heads.

GREGORY:
The heads of the maids?

SAMPSON:
Yes, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads;
take it in whatever sense you will.

GREGORY:
They must take it in the sense that they feel it.

SAMPSON:
They will feel me while I am able to stand. And
it's known that I am a pretty piece of flesh.

GREGORY:
It's a good thing you're not a fish; if you were, you
would have been a poor John. Draw your tool! Here come
two from the house of the Montagues.

SAMPSON:
My naked weapon is out. Quarrel, and I will back you.

GREGORY:
What! Turn your back and run?

SAMPSON:
Don't fear me.

GREGORY:
No, indeed; I fear you!

SAMPSON:
Let us take the law on our side; let them begin.

GREGORY:
I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it as
they wish.

SAMPSON:
No, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at them;
which is a disgrace to them, if they bear it.

ABRAHAM:
Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

SAMPSON:
I do bite my thumb, sir.

ABRAHAM:
Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

SAMPSON:

GREGORY:
No.

SAMPSON:
No, sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir, but I
do bite my thumb, sir.

GREGORY:
Are you looking for a fight, sir?

ABRAHAM:
Quarrel, sir! No, sir.

SAMPSON:
If you do, sir, I am for you. I serve as good a man as you.

ABRAHAM:
No better.

SAMPSON:
Well, sir.

GREGORY:
Say 'better.' Here comes one of my master's kinsmen.

SAMPSON:
Yes, better, sir.

ABRAHAM:
You lie.

SAMPSON:
Draw, if you are men. Gregory, remember your swashing blow.

BENVOLIO:
Part, fools!
Put up your swords; you don't know what you are doing.

TYBALT:
What, are you drawn among these heartless servants?
Turn, Benvolio, and look upon your death.

BENVOLIO:
I am only trying to keep the peace. Put up your sword,
or use it to part these men with me.

TYBALT:
What, you've drawn your sword and you talk of peace! I hate the word,
as I hate hell, all Montagues, and you.
Have at you, coward!

First Citizen:
Clubs, bills, and partisans! Strike! Beat them down!
Down with the Capulets! Down with the Montagues!

CAPULET:
What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!

LADY CAPULET:
A crutch, a crutch! Why are you calling for a sword?

CAPULET:
My sword, I say! Old Montague has come,
and is flourishing his blade in spite of me.

MONTAGUE:
You villain Capulet!—Don't hold me, let me go.

LADY MONTAGUE:
You shall not stir a foot to seek a foe.

PRINCE:
Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace,
profaners of this neighbor-stained steel—
will they not hear? What, ho! You men, you beasts,
that quench the fire of your pernicious rage
with purple fountains issuing from your veins,
on pain of torture, from those bloody hands,
throw your mistempered weapons to the ground,
and hear the sentence of your moved prince.
Three civil brawls, bred of an airy word,
by you, old Capulet, and Montague,
have thrice disturbed the quiet of our streets,
and made Verona's ancient citizens
cast by their grave, beseeming ornaments,
to wield old partisans, in hands as old,
cankered with peace, to part your cankered hate.
If you ever disturb our streets again,
your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace.
For this time, all the rest of you, depart away.
You, Capulet, shall go along with me.
And, Montague, you come this afternoon,
to know our further pleasure in this case,
to old Free-town, our common judgment-place.
Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.

MONTAGUE:
Who started this ancient quarrel anew?
Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?

BENVOLIO:
Here were the servants of your adversary,
and yours, fighting closely before I approached.
I drew my sword to part them. In that instant,
the fiery Tybalt came, with his sword prepared,
which, as he breathed defiance to my ears,
he swung about his head and cut the winds,
who, not hurt at all, hissed at him in scorn.
While we were exchanging thrusts and blows,
more and more came and fought on both sides,
until the prince came, who parted both sides.

LADY MONTAGUE:
Oh, where is Romeo? Did you see him today?
I'm very glad he was not at this fight.

BENVOLIO:
Madam, an hour before the worshipped sun
peered forth from the golden window of the east,
a troubled mind drove me to walk abroad;
where, underneath the grove of sycamore
that roots westward from the city's side,
I saw your son walking so early.
I went towards him, but he was aware of me
and stole into the cover of the wood.
I, measuring his affections by my own,
that are most busy when they are most alone,
pursued my own humor, not his,
and gladly shunned who gladly fled from me.

MONTAGUE:
Many a morning has he been seen there,
with tears augmenting the fresh morning dew,
adding to the clouds more clouds with his deep sighs.
But as soon as the all-cheering sun
should in the furthest east begin to draw
the shady curtains from Aurora's bed,
away from the light my heavy son steals home,
and pens himself in his private chamber,
shuts up his windows, locks out the daylight,
and makes himself an artificial night.
This humor must prove to be black and portentous,
unless good counsel may remove the cause.

BENVOLIO:
My noble uncle, do you know the cause?

MONTAGUE:
I neither know it nor can I learn it from him.

BENVOLIO:
Have you questioned him by any means?

MONTAGUE:
Both by myself and many other friends.
But he, his own affections' counselor,
is to himself—I will not say how true—
but to himself so secret and so close,
so far from sounding and discovery,
as is the bud bit with an envious worm,
before he can spread his sweet leaves to the air,
or dedicate his beauty to the sun.
If we could but learn from where his sorrows grow,
we would as willingly give a cure as we would know.

BENVOLIO:
See, where he comes. If you please, step aside;
I'll know his grievance, or be much denied.

MONTAGUE:
I hope you will be so happy by your stay,
to hear a true confession. Come, madam, let's go.

BENVOLIO:
Good morning, cousin.

ROMEO:
Is the day so young?

BENVOLIO:
It's just struck nine.

ROMEO:
Ah me! Sad hours seem long.
Was that my father that went from here so fast?

BENVOLIO:
It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo's hours?

ROMEO:
Not having that, which, when I have it, makes them short.

BENVOLIO:
In love?

ROMEO:
Out—

BENVOLIO:
Of love?

ROMEO:
Out of her favor, where I am in love.

BENVOLIO:
Alas, that love, so gentle in its appearance,
should be so tyrannical and rough in proof!

ROMEO:
Alas, that love, whose view is still muffled,
should, without eyes, see pathways to his will!
Where shall we dine? Oh me! What fight was here?
Yet don't tell me, for I have heard it all.
Here's much to do with hate, but more with love.
Why, then, O brawling love! O loving hate!
O anything, created first from nothing!
O heavy lightness! Serious vanity!
Misshapen chaos of well-seeming forms!
Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire,
sick health!
Still-waking sleep, that is not what it is!
This love I feel, that feel no love in this.
Are you not laughing?

BENVOLIO:
No, cousin, I'd rather weep.

ROMEO:
Good heart, at what?

BENVOLIO:
At your good heart's oppression.

ROMEO:
Why, such is love's transgression.
My own griefs lie heavy in my breast,
which you will propagate, to have it pressed
with more of yours. This love that you have shown
adds more grief to too much of my own.
Love is a smoke raised with the fume of sighs;
when it is purged, it is a fire sparkling in lovers' eyes;
when it is vexed, it is a sea nourished with lovers' tears.
What else is it? A most discreet madness,
a choking gall and a preserving sweet.
Farewell, my cousin.

BENVOLIO:
Wait! I will go with you;
if you leave me so, you do me wrong.

ROMEO:
Tut, I have lost myself; I am not here.
This is not Romeo, he's somewhere else.

BENVOLIO:
Tell me in sadness, who is it that you love?

ROMEO:
What, shall I groan and tell you?

BENVOLIO:
Groan! Why, no.
But sadly tell me who.

ROMEO:
Bid a sick man in sadness make his will.
Ah, a word ill-urged to one that is so ill!
In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.

BENVOLIO:
I aimed so near, when I supposed you loved.

ROMEO:
A right good marksman! And she is fair, the one I love.

BENVOLIO:
A right fair mark, fair cousin, is soonest hit.

ROMEO:
Well, in that hit you miss. She'll not be hit
with Cupid's arrow; she has Diana's wit;
and, in strong proof of chastity well-armed,
from love's weak, childish bow she lives unharmed.
She will not stay the siege of loving terms,
nor bide the encounter of assailing eyes,
nor open her lap to saint-seducing gold.
Oh, she is rich in beauty, only poor,
that when she dies, her beauty dies with her store.

BENVOLIO:
Then she has sworn that she will always live chaste?

ROMEO:
She has, and in that sparing she makes a huge waste,
for beauty, starved with her severity,
cuts beauty off from all posterity.
She is too fair, too wise, wisely too fair,
to merit bliss by making me despair.
She has forsworn to love, and in that vow
I live dead, who live to tell it now.

BENVOLIO:
Be ruled by me, forget to think of her.

ROMEO:
Oh, teach me how I should forget to think.

BENVOLIO:
By giving liberty to your eyes;
examine other beauties.

ROMEO:
That is the way
to call hers exquisite, more in question.
These happy masks that kiss fair ladies' brows,
being black, remind us that they hide the fair.
He that is stricken blind cannot forget
the precious treasure of his eyesight lost.
Show me a mistress that is passing fair,
what does her beauty serve, but as a note
where I may read who passed that passing fair?
Farewell. You cannot teach me to forget.

BENVOLIO:
I'll pay that doctrine, or else die in debt.

CAPULET:
But Montague is bound as well as I,
in a like penalty; and it's not hard, I think,
for men as old as we are to keep the peace.

PARIS:
You are both of honorable reckoning;
and it's a pity you have lived at odds for so long.
But now, my lord, what do you say to my suit?

CAPULET:
But I am just repeating what I have said before:
my child is still a stranger in the world;
she has not seen the change of fourteen years.
Let two more summers wither in their pride,
before we may think her ripe to be a bride.

PARIS:
Younger than she are happy mothers made.

CAPULET:
And those so early made are too soon marred.
The earth has swallowed all my hopes but she,
she is the hopeful lady of my earth.
But woo her, gentle Paris, get her heart,
my will to her consent is but a part;
if she agrees, within her scope of choice
lies my consent and fair according voice.
This night I am holding an old, accustomed feast,
to which I have invited many a guest,
such as I love; and you, among the store,
one more, most welcome, makes my number more.
At my poor house, look to behold this night
earth-treading stars that make the dark heaven light.
Such comfort as lusty young men feel
when well-appareled April on the heel
of limping winter treads, even such delight
among fresh female buds you shall this night
inherit at my house; hear all, see all,
and like her most whose merit shall be most.
Which, on more view, of many, mine being one,
may stand in number, though in reckoning none.
Come, go with me.
Go, sirrah, trudge about
through fair Verona; find those persons out
whose names are written there, and say to them,
my house and welcome await their pleasure.

Servant:
Find them out whose names are written here! It is
written, that the shoemaker should meddle with his
yard, and the tailor with his last, the fisher with
his pencil, and the painter with his nets; but I am
sent to find those persons whose names are here
written, and can never find what names the writing
person has written here. I must go to the learned.—In good time.

BENVOLIO:
Tut, man, one fire burns out another's burning,
one pain is lessened by another's anguish;
turn giddy, and be helped by turning backward;
one desperate grief is cured by another's languish.
Take some new infection to your eye,
and the rank poison of the old will die.

ROMEO:
Your plantain-leaf is excellent for that.

BENVOLIO:
For what, I pray you?

ROMEO:
For your broken shin.

BENVOLIO:
Why, Romeo, are you mad?

ROMEO:
Not mad, but bound more than a mad-man is;
shut up in prison, kept without my food,
whipped and tormented and—good day, good fellow.

Servant:
Good day to you. I ask, sir, can you read?

ROMEO:
Yes, my own fortune in my misery.

Servant:
Perhaps you have learned it without a book. But, I
ask, can you read anything you see?

ROMEO:
Yes, if I know the letters and the language.

Servant:
You say honestly. Rest you merry!

ROMEO:
Stay, fellow; I can read.
'Signor Martino and his wife and daughters;
County Anselme and his beautiful sisters; the lady
widow of Vitravio; Signor Placentio and his lovely
nieces; Mercutio and his brother Valentine; my
uncle Capulet, his wife and daughters; my fair niece
Rosaline; Livia; Signor Valentio and his cousin
Tybalt, Lucio and the lively Helena.' A fair
assembly. Where should they come?

Servant:
Up.

ROMEO:
Where to?

Servant:
To supper; to our house.

ROMEO:
Whose house?

Servant:
My master's.

ROMEO:
Indeed, I should have asked you that before.

Servant:
Now I'll tell you without asking. My master is the
great, rich Capulet; and if you are not of the house
of Montagues, I pray, come and have a cup of wine.
Rest you merry!

BENVOLIO:
At this same ancient feast of Capulet's
sups the fair Rosaline whom you so love,
with all the admired beauties of Verona.
Go there; and, with an untainted eye,
compare her face with some that I shall show,
and I will make you think your swan a crow.

ROMEO:
When the devout religion of my eye
maintains such falsehood, then may my tears turn to fires;
and these, who, though often drowned, could never die,
transparent heretics, be burnt for liars!
One fairer than my love! The all-seeing sun
never saw her match since the world first began.

BENVOLIO:
Tut, you saw her as fair, with no one else being by,
she was poised with herself in either eye.
But in that crystal scale let there be weighed
your lady's love against some other maid
that I will show you shining at this feast,
and she shall scarcely show well that now shows best.

ROMEO:
I'll go along, not to see such a sight,
but to rejoice in the splendor of my own.

LADY CAPULET:
Nurse, where's my daughter? Call her to me.

Nurse:
Now, by my maidenhead, at twelve years old,
I told her to come. What, lamb! What, ladybird!
God forbid! Where is this girl? What, Juliet!

JULIET:
What now! Who calls?

Nurse:
Your mother.

JULIET:
Madam, I am here.
What is your will?

LADY CAPULET:
This is the matter:—Nurse, leave us for a while,
we must talk in secret.—Nurse, come back again;
I have remembered, you shall hear our counsel.
You know my daughter is of a pretty age.

Nurse:
Faith, I can tell her age to the hour.

LADY CAPULET:
She's not fourteen.

Nurse:
I'll bet fourteen of my teeth—
and yet, though I say it to my teeth, I have but four—
she is not fourteen. How long is it now
to Lammas-tide?

LADY CAPULET:
A fortnight and a few odd days.

Nurse:
Even or odd, of all the days in the year,
on Lammas-eve at night she will be fourteen.
Susan and she—God rest all Christian souls!—
were of the same age. Well, Susan is with God;
she was too good for me. But, as I said,
on Lammas-eve at night she will be fourteen;
that she will, indeed; I remember it well.
It's been eleven years since the earthquake now;
and she was weaned—I'll never forget it—
of all the days of the year, on that day.
For I had then laid wormwood on my breast,
sitting in the sun under the dove-house wall;
my lord and you were then at Mantua.
No, I do have a brain. But, as I said,
when it did taste the wormwood on the nipple
of my breast and felt it bitter, the pretty fool,
to see it get angry and fall out with the breast!
The dove-house shook. There was no need, I think,
to tell me to go.
And since that time, it has been eleven years;
for then she could stand alone; no, by the rood,
she could have run and waddled all about;
for even the day before, she broke her brow.
And then my husband—God be with his soul!—
he was a merry man—took up the child.
'Yes,' he said, 'do you fall upon your face?
You will fall backward when you have more wit;
won't you, Jule?' And, by my holy dame,
the pretty wretch left off crying and said 'Yes.'
To see, now, how a jest shall come about!
I warrant, if I should live a thousand years,
I would never forget it. 'Won't you, Jule?' he said;
and, the pretty fool, it stopped and said 'Yes.'

LADY CAPULET:
Enough of this; I pray you, hold your peace.

Nurse:
Yes, madam. Yet I cannot choose but laugh,
to think it should stop crying and say 'Yes.'
And yet, I warrant, it had on its brow
a bump as big as a young cockerel's stone;
a perilous knock; and it cried bitterly.
'Yes,' said my husband, 'do you fall upon your face?
You will fall backward when you come of age;
won't you, Jule?' It stopped and said 'Yes.'

JULIET:
And you stop too, I pray you, nurse, say I.

Nurse:
Peace, I have done. God mark you to his grace!
You were the prettiest baby that I ever nursed.
If I might live to see you married once,
I would have my wish.

LADY CAPULET:
Marry, that 'marry' is the very theme
I came to talk about. Tell me, daughter Juliet,
how does your disposition stand to be married?

JULIET:
It is an honor that I do not dream of.

Nurse:
An honor! If I were not your only nurse,
I would say you had sucked wisdom from your teat.

LADY CAPULET:
Well, think of marriage now. Younger than you,
here in Verona, ladies of esteem,
are already made mothers. By my count,
I was your mother at about these years
that you are now a maid. Thus then in brief:
the valiant Paris seeks you for his love.

Nurse:
A man, young lady! Lady, such a man
as all the world—why, he's a man of wax.

LADY CAPULET:
Verona's summer has no such flower.

Nurse:
No, he's a flower; in faith, a very flower.

LADY CAPULET:
What do you say? Can you love the gentleman?
This night you shall behold him at our feast;
read over the volume of young Paris' face,
and find delight written there with beauty's pen;
examine every married feature,
and see how one another lends content.
And what is obscured in this fair volume lies
written in the margin of his eyes.
This precious book of love, this unbound lover,
to beautify him, only lacks a cover.
The fish lives in the sea, and it is much pride
for the fair without to hide the fair within.
That book in many's eyes shares the glory,
that in gold clasps locks in the golden story;
so you shall share all that he possesses,
by having him, and making yourself no less.

Nurse:
No less! No, bigger; women grow by men.

LADY CAPULET:
Speak briefly, can you like Paris's love?

JULIET:
I'll look to like, if looking makes me like.
But I will not let my eye dart any deeper
than your consent gives it strength to fly.

Servant:
Madam, the guests have come, supper is served up, you
are called, my young lady is asked for, the nurse is cursed in
the pantry, and everything is in extremity. I must
go to wait; I beseech you, follow straight away.

LADY CAPULET:
We are following you.
Juliet, the county is waiting.

Nurse:
Go, girl, seek happy nights for happy days.

ROMEO:
What, shall this speech be spoken for our excuse?
Or shall we go on without an apology?

BENVOLIO:
The time for such prolixity is past.
We'll have no Cupid hoodwinked with a scarf,
bearing a Tartar's painted bow of lath,
scaring the ladies like a crow-keeper;
nor any without-book prologue, faintly spoken
after the prompter, for our entrance.
But let them measure us by what they will;
we'll measure them a measure, and be gone.

ROMEO:
Give me a torch. I am not for this ambling;
being but heavy, I will bear the light.

MERCUTIO:
No, gentle Romeo, we must have you dance.

ROMEO:
Not I, believe me. You have dancing shoes
with nimble soles. I have a soul of lead
that stakes me to the ground so I cannot move.

MERCUTIO:
You are a lover; borrow Cupid's wings,
and soar with them above a common bound.

ROMEO:
I am too sore pierced with his shaft
to soar with his light feathers, and so bound,
I cannot bound a pitch above dull woe.
Under love's heavy burden do I sink.

MERCUTIO:
And, to sink in it, you would burden love;
too great an oppression for a tender thing.

ROMEO:
Is love a tender thing? It is too rough,
too rude, too boisterous, and it pricks like a thorn.

MERCUTIO:
If love is rough with you, be rough with love;
prick love for pricking, and you will beat love down.
Give me a case to put my face in.
A visor for a visor! What do I care
what curious eye notes my deformities?
Here are the beetle brows that will blush for me.

BENVOLIO:
Come, let's knock and enter; and no sooner in,
but every man should betake himself to his legs.

ROMEO:
A torch for me. Let wantons, light of heart,
tickle the senseless rushes with their heels,
for I am provided with a grandsire's phrase;
I'll be a candle-holder, and look on.
The game was never so fair, and I am done.

MERCUTIO:
Tut, dun's the mouse, the constable's own word.
If you are dun, we'll draw you from the mire
of this sir-reverence love, in which you are stuck
up to the ears. Come, we are burning daylight, ho!

ROMEO:
No, that's not so.

MERCUTIO:
I mean, sir, in delay
we waste our lights in vain, like lamps by day.
Take our good meaning, for our judgment sits
five times in that before once in our five wits.

ROMEO:
And we mean well in going to this mask;
but it is not wise to go.

MERCUTIO:
Why, may one ask?

ROMEO:
I dreamed a dream tonight.

MERCUTIO:
And so did I.

ROMEO:
Well, what was yours?

MERCUTIO:
That dreamers often lie.

ROMEO:
In bed asleep, while they do dream things true.

MERCUTIO:
Oh, then, I see Queen Mab has been with you.
She is the fairies' midwife, and she comes
in a shape no bigger than an agate-stone
on the forefinger of an alderman,
drawn by a team of little atoms
across men's noses as they lie asleep.
Her wagon-spokes are made of long spiders' legs,
the cover is of the wings of grasshoppers,
the traces are of the smallest spider's web,
the collars are of the moonshine's watery beams,
her whip is of a cricket's bone, the lash of film,
her wagoner is a small, gray-coated gnat,
not so big as a round little worm
pricked from the lazy finger of a maid.
Her chariot is an empty hazel-nut
made by the joiner squirrel or an old grub,
the fairies' coachmakers from time immemorial.
And in this state she gallops night by night
through lovers' brains, and then they dream of love;
over courtiers' knees, that dream on courtesies straight away,
over lawyers' fingers, who straight away dream on fees,
over ladies' lips, who straight away dream of kisses,
which often the angry Mab plagues with blisters,
because their breaths are tainted with sweetmeats.
Sometimes she gallops over a courtier's nose,
and then he dreams of smelling out a suit;
and sometimes she comes with a tithe-pig's tail
tickling a parson's nose as he lies asleep,
then he dreams of another benefice.
Sometimes she drives over a soldier's neck,
and then he dreams of cutting foreign throats,
of breaches, ambushes, Spanish blades,
of healths five fathoms deep; and then anon
drums in his ear, at which he starts and wakes,
and being thus frightened, he swears a prayer or two
and sleeps again. This is that very Mab
that plaits the manes of horses in the night,
and bakes the elf-locks in foul, sluttish hair,
which once untangled, bodes much misfortune.
This is the hag, when maids lie on their backs,
that presses them and teaches them first to bear,
making them women of good carriage.
This is she—

ROMEO:
Peace, peace, Mercutio, peace!
You are talking about nothing.

MERCUTIO:
True, I talk of dreams,
which are the children of an idle brain,
begotten of nothing but vain fantasy,
which is as thin of substance as the air
and more inconstant than the wind, who woos
even now the frozen bosom of the north,
and, being angered, puffs away from there,
turning his face to the dew-dropping south.

BENVOLIO:
This wind, you talk of, blows us from ourselves;
supper is done, and we shall come too late.

ROMEO:
I fear, too early. For my mind misgives
some consequence yet hanging in the stars
shall bitterly begin its fearful date
with this night's revels and expire the term
of a despised life closed in my breast
by some vile forfeit of untimely death.
But He, that has the steerage of my course,
direct my sail! On, lusty gentlemen.

BENVOLIO:
Strike, drum.

First Servant:
Where's Potpan, that he doesn't help to take away? He
should shift a trencher? He should scrape a trencher!

Second Servant:
When good manners shall lie all in one or two men's
hands, and they unwashed too, it's a foul thing.

First Servant:
Away with the joint-stools, remove the
court-cupboard, and look to the plate. Good you, save
me a piece of marchpane; and, as you love me, let
the porter let in Susan Grindstone and Nell.
Antony, and Potpan!

Second Servant:
Yes, boy, ready.

First Servant:
You are looked for and called for, asked for and
sought for, in the great chamber.

Second Servant:
We cannot be here and there too. Cheer up, boys; be
brisk for a while, and the longer liver take all.

CAPULET:
Welcome, gentlemen! Ladies that have their toes
un-plagued with corns will have a bout with you.
Ah ha, my mistresses! Which of you all
will now deny to dance? She that makes a show of being dainty,
she, I'll swear, has corns. Am I close now?
Welcome, gentlemen! I have seen the day
that I have worn a visor and could tell
a whispering tale in a fair lady's ear,
such as would please. It's gone, it's gone, it's gone.
You are welcome, gentlemen! Come, musicians, play.
A hall, a hall! Give room! And foot it, girls.
More light, you knaves; and turn the tables up,
and quench the fire, the room has grown too hot.
Ah, sirrah, this unlooked-for sport comes well.
No, sit, no, sit, good cousin Capulet;
for you and I are past our dancing days.
How long is it now since you and I
were last in a mask?

Second Capulet:
By our lady, thirty years.

CAPULET:
What, man! It's not so much, it's not so much.
It's been since the wedding of Lucentio,
come Pentecost as quickly as it will,
some twenty-five years; and then we masked.

Second Capulet:
It's more, it's more, his son is older, sir;
his son is thirty.

CAPULET:
Will you tell me that?
His son was but a ward two years ago.

ROMEO:

Servant:
I don't know, sir.

ROMEO:
Oh, she teaches the torches to burn bright!
It seems she hangs upon the cheek of night
like a rich jewel in an Ethiopian's ear;
beauty too rich for use, for earth too dear!
So a snowy dove, trooping with crows,
shows as that lady over her fellows shows.
When the measure is done, I'll watch her place of stand,
and, touching hers, make my rude hand blessed.
Did my heart love until now? Forswear it, sight!
For I never saw true beauty until this night.

TYBALT:
This, by his voice, should be a Montague.
Fetch me my rapier, boy. What, does the slave dare
to come here, covered with an antic face,
to sneer and scorn at our solemnity?
Now, by the stock and honor of my kin,
to strike him dead, I hold it not a sin.

CAPULET:
Why, how now, kinsman! Why are you storming so?

TYBALT:
Uncle, this is a Montague, our foe,
a villain that has come here in spite,
to scorn our solemnity this night.

CAPULET:
Is it young Romeo?

TYBALT:
It's he, that villain Romeo.

CAPULET:
Content yourself, gentle cousin, let him alone;
he carries himself like a portly gentleman;
and, to tell the truth, Verona brags of him
to be a virtuous and well-governed youth.
I would not for the wealth of all the town
do him any disrespect here in my house.
Therefore be patient, take no note of him.
