It is my will, which if you respect,
show a fair presence and put off these frowns,
and this ill-beseeming semblance for a feast.

TYBALT:
It is fitting, when such a villain is a guest.
I'll not endure him.

CAPULET:
He shall be endured.
What, goodman boy! I say, he shall. Go to;
am I the master here, or you? Go to.
You'll not endure him! God shall mend my soul!
You'll make a mutiny among my guests!
You'll set everything in an uproar! You'll be the man!

TYBALT:
Why, uncle, it's a shame.

CAPULET:
Go to, go to;
you are a saucy boy. Is it so, indeed?
This trick may chance to harm you, I know what.
You must contradict me! Indeed, it's time.
Well said, my hearts! You are a coxcomb; go.
Be quiet, or—more light, more light! For shame!
I'll make you quiet. What, cheer up, my hearts!

TYBALT:
Patience, by force, meeting with willful anger,
makes my flesh tremble in their different greeting.
I will withdraw, but this intrusion shall,
now seeming sweet, convert to bitter gall.

ROMEO:

JULIET:
Good pilgrim, you do wrong your hand too much,
which shows mannerly devotion in this;
for saints have hands that pilgrims' hands do touch,
and palm to palm is a holy palmer's kiss.

ROMEO:
Do not saints have lips, and holy palmers too?

JULIET:
Yes, pilgrim, lips that they must use in prayer.

ROMEO:
Oh, then, dear saint, let lips do what hands do;
they pray, grant it, lest faith turn to despair.

JULIET:
Saints do not move, though they grant for prayer's sake.

ROMEO:
Then don't move, while I take the effect of my prayer.
Thus from my lips, by yours, my sin is purged.

JULIET:
Then my lips have the sin that they have taken.

ROMEO:
Sin from your lips? O trespass sweetly urged!
Give me my sin again.

JULIET:
You kiss by the book.

Nurse:
Madam, your mother craves a word with you.

ROMEO:
What is her mother?

Nurse:
Well, bachelor,
her mother is the lady of the house,
and a good lady, and a wise and virtuous one.
I nursed her daughter, that you talked with;
I tell you, he that can lay hold of her
shall have the money.

ROMEO:
Is she a Capulet?
O dear account! My life is my foe's debt.

BENVOLIO:
Away, be gone; the sport is at its best.

ROMEO:
Yes, so I fear; the more is my unrest.

CAPULET:
No, gentlemen, don't prepare to leave;
we have a trifling, foolish banquet to come.
Is it even so? Why, then, I thank you all.
I thank you, honest gentlemen; good night.
More torches here! Come on then, let's go to bed.
Ah, sirrah, by my faith, it is getting late.
I'll go to my rest.

JULIET:
Come here, nurse. Who is that gentleman?

Nurse:
The son and heir of old Tiberio.

JULIET:
Who's he that is now going out of the door?

Nurse:
Well, that, I think, is young Petrucio.

JULIET:
Who's he that follows there, that would not dance?

Nurse:
I don't know.

JULIET:
Go and ask his name. If he is married,
my grave is likely to be my wedding bed.

Nurse:
His name is Romeo, and he's a Montague;
the only son of your great enemy.

JULIET:
My only love has sprung from my only hate!
I saw him too early without knowing him, and I learned who he was too late!
It is a prodigious birth of love to me,
that I must love a loathed enemy.

Nurse:
What's this? What's this?

JULIET:
A rhyme I learned just now
from one I danced with.

Nurse:
Soon, soon!
Come, let's go away; the strangers are all gone.

Chorus:
Now old desire lies on its death-bed,
and young affection gapes to be its heir;
that fair one for which love groaned and would die,
matched with tender Juliet, is now not fair.
Now Romeo is beloved and loves again,
both bewitched by the charm of looks,
but he must complain to his supposed foe,
and she must steal love's sweet bait from fearful hooks.
Being held a foe, he may not have access
to breathe such vows as lovers use to swear;
and she, as much in love, has much less means
to meet her new-beloved anywhere.
But passion lends them power, and time lends them means, to meet,
tempering extremities with extreme sweetness.

ROMEO:
Can I go forward when my heart is here?
Turn back, dull earth, and find your center out.

BENVOLIO:
Romeo! My cousin Romeo!

MERCUTIO:
He is wise;
and, on my life, he has stolen home to bed.

BENVOLIO:
He ran this way, and leaped this orchard wall.
Call, good Mercutio.

MERCUTIO:
No, I'll conjure too.
Romeo! Humors! Madman! Passion! Lover!
Appear in the likeness of a sigh.
Speak but one rhyme, and I will be satisfied;
cry but 'Ay me!' pronounce but 'love' and 'dove';
speak to my gossip Venus one fair word,
one nickname for her purblind son and heir,
young Adam Cupid, he that shot so trim,
when King Cophetua loved the beggar-maid!
He does not hear, he does not stir, he does not move;
the ape is dead, and I must conjure him.
I conjure you by Rosaline's bright eyes,
by her high forehead and her scarlet lip,
by her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh,
and the domains that lie adjacent there,
that in your likeness you appear to us!

BENVOLIO:
And if he hears you, you will anger him.

MERCUTIO:
This cannot anger him. It would anger him
to raise a spirit in his mistress's circle
of some strange nature, letting it stand there
until she had laid it and conjured it down;
that would be some spite. My invocation
is fair and honest, and in his mistress's name
I conjure only to raise him up.

BENVOLIO:
Come, he has hidden himself among these trees,
to be in company with the humorous night.
Blind is his love and it best befits the dark.

MERCUTIO:
If love is blind, love cannot hit the mark.
Now he will sit under a medlar tree,
and wish his mistress were that kind of fruit
as maids call medlars, when they laugh alone.
Romeo, if only she were, oh, if only she were
an open et cetera, and you a poperin pear!
Romeo, good night. I'll go to my truckle-bed;
this field-bed is too cold for me to sleep.
Come, shall we go?

BENVOLIO:
Go, then; for it is in vain
to seek him here who does not mean to be found.

ROMEO:
He jests at scars that never felt a wound.
But, soft! What light breaks through that window?
It is the east, and Juliet is the sun.
Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,
who is already sick and pale with grief,
that you, her maid, are far more fair than she.
Do not be her maid, since she is envious;
her vestal livery is but sick and green
and none but fools do wear it; cast it off.
It is my lady, oh, it is my love!
Oh, that she knew she were!
She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that?
Her eye discourses; I will answer it.
I am too bold, it is not to me she speaks.
Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,
having some business, do entreat her eyes
to twinkle in their spheres till they return.
What if her eyes were there, they in her head?
The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars,
as daylight does a lamp; her eyes in heaven
would stream so bright through the airy region
that birds would sing and think it were not night.
See, how she leans her cheek upon her hand!
Oh, that I were a glove upon that hand,
that I might touch that cheek!

JULIET:
Ah me!

ROMEO:
She speaks.
Oh, speak again, bright angel! For you are
as glorious to this night, being over my head,
as is a winged messenger of heaven
to the white-upturned, wondering eyes
of mortals that fall back to gaze on him
when he bestrides the lazy-pacing clouds
and sails upon the bosom of the air.

JULIET:
Oh Romeo, Romeo! Why are you Romeo?
Deny your father and refuse your name;
or, if you will not, just be sworn my love,
and I'll no longer be a Capulet.

ROMEO:

JULIET:
It is but your name that is my enemy;
you are yourself, though not a Montague.
What is a Montague? It is neither a hand, nor a foot,
nor an arm, nor a face, nor any other part
belonging to a man. Oh, be some other name!
What's in a name? That which we call a rose
by any other name would smell as sweet;
so Romeo would, if he were not called Romeo,
retain that dear perfection which he owes
without that title. Romeo, cast off your name,
and for that name, which is no part of you,
take all of myself.

ROMEO:
I take you at your word.
Just call me 'love,' and I'll be newly baptized;
from now on I will never be Romeo.

JULIET:
What man are you that, thus hidden in the night,
so stumbles upon my counsel?

ROMEO:
By a name,
I don't know how to tell you who I am.
My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself,
because it is an enemy to you;
if I had it written, I would tear the word.

JULIET:
My ears have not yet drunk a hundred words
of that tongue's utterance, yet I know the sound.
Are you not Romeo and a Montague?

ROMEO:
Neither, fair saint, if you dislike either.

JULIET:
How did you come here, tell me, and why?
The orchard walls are high and hard to climb,
and the place means death, considering who you are,
if any of my kinsmen find you here.

ROMEO:
I flew over these walls with love's light wings;
for stony limits cannot hold love out,
and what love can do, that love dares to attempt;
therefore your kinsmen are no hindrance to me.

JULIET:
If they see you, they will murder you.

ROMEO:
Alas, there is more peril in your eye
than in twenty of their swords. Just look sweet at me,
and I am proof against their enmity.

JULIET:
I would not for the world have them see you here.

ROMEO:
I have night's cloak to hide me from their sight;
and if you do not love me, let them find me here.
My life would be better ended by their hate,
than death prolonged, wanting of your love.

JULIET:
By whose direction did you find this place?

ROMEO:
By love, who first prompted me to inquire;
he lent me counsel and I lent him eyes.
I am no pilot; yet, if you were as far
as that vast shore washed by the farthest sea,
I would adventure for such merchandise.

JULIET:
You know the mask of night is on my face,
or else a maiden blush would paint my cheek
for what you have heard me speak tonight.
I would fain dwell on form, fain, fain deny
what I have spoken. But farewell, compliment!
Do you love me? I know you will say 'Yes,'
and I will take your word. Yet if you swear,
you may prove false; at lovers' perjuries,
they say, Jove laughs. Oh, gentle Romeo,
if you do love, pronounce it faithfully.
Or if you think I am too quickly won,
I'll frown and be perverse and say no to you,
so that you will woo; but otherwise, not for the world.
In truth, fair Montague, I am too fond,
and therefore you may think my behavior is light.
But trust me, gentleman, I'll prove more true
than those that have more cunning to be strange.
I should have been more strange, I must confess,
but that you overheard, before I was aware,
my true love's passion. Therefore, pardon me,
and do not impute this yielding to light love,
which the dark night has so discovered.

ROMEO:
Lady, by that blessed moon I swear,
that tips all these fruit-tree tops with silver—

JULIET:
Oh, don't swear by the moon, the inconstant moon,
that changes monthly in her circled orb,
lest your love prove likewise variable.

ROMEO:
What shall I swear by?

JULIET:
Do not swear at all;
or, if you will, swear by your gracious self,
which is the god of my idolatry,
and I'll believe you.

ROMEO:
If my heart's dear love—

JULIET:
Well, do not swear. Although I find joy in you,
I have no joy of this contract tonight.
It is too rash, too unadvised, too sudden;
too like the lightning, which ceases to be
before one can say, 'It lightens.' Sweet, good night!
This bud of love, by summer's ripening breath,
may prove a beautiful flower when next we meet.
Good night, good night! May sweet repose and rest
come to your heart as that within my breast!

ROMEO:
Oh, will you leave me so unsatisfied?

JULIET:
What satisfaction can you have tonight?

ROMEO:
The exchange of your love's faithful vow for mine.

JULIET:
I gave you mine before you requested it,
and yet I wish it were to give again.

ROMEO:
Would you withdraw it? For what purpose, love?

JULIET:
But to be frank, and to give it to you again.
And yet I wish only for the thing I have.
My bounty is as boundless as the sea,
my love as deep; the more I give to you,
the more I have, for both are infinite.
I hear some noise within; dear love, adieu!
Anon, good nurse! Sweet Montague, be true.
Stay but a little, I will come again.

ROMEO:
Oh, blessed, blessed night! I am afraid,
being in the night, that all this is but a dream,
too flattering-sweet to be substantial.

JULIET:
Three words, dear Romeo, and good night indeed.
If your intention of love is honorable,
and your purpose is marriage, send me word tomorrow,
by one that I'll procure to come to you,
where and what time you will perform the rite;
and I'll lay all my fortunes at your foot
and follow you, my lord, throughout the world.

Nurse:

JULIET:
I'm coming, soon.—But if you don't mean well,
I beseech you—

Nurse:

JULIET:
By and by, I'm coming—
to cease your suit, and leave me to my grief.
Tomorrow I will send.

ROMEO:
So may my soul thrive—

JULIET:
A thousand times good night!

ROMEO:
A thousand times the worse, to want your light.
Love goes toward love, as schoolboys from
their books,
but love from love, toward school with heavy looks.

JULIET:
Hist! Romeo, hist! Oh, for a falconer's voice,
to lure this tassel-gentle back again!
Bondage is hoarse, and may not speak aloud;
else would I tear the cave where Echo lies,
and make her airy tongue more hoarse than mine,
with the repetition of my Romeo's name.

ROMEO:
It is my soul that calls upon my name.
How silver-sweet sound lovers' tongues by night,
like the softest music to attending ears!

JULIET:
Romeo!

ROMEO:
My dear?

JULIET:
At what time tomorrow
shall I send to you?

ROMEO:
At the hour of nine.

JULIET:
I will not fail. It feels like twenty years until then.
I have forgotten why I called you back.

ROMEO:
Let me stand here until you remember it.

JULIET:
I shall forget, just to have you still stand there,
remembering how I love your company.

ROMEO:
And I'll still stay, to have you still forget,
forgetting any other home but this.

JULIET:
It is almost morning; I would have you gone.
And yet, no further than a wanton's bird,
who lets it hop a little from her hand,
like a poor prisoner in his twisted chains,
and with a silk thread plucks it back again,
so loving-jealous of his liberty.

ROMEO:
I wish I were your bird.

JULIET:
Sweet, so would I.
Yet I would kill you with much cherishing.
Good night, good night! Parting is such
sweet sorrow,
that I shall say good night until it is morrow.

ROMEO:
May sleep dwell upon your eyes, and peace in your breast!
I wish I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest!
From here, I will go to my ghostly father's cell,
to ask for his help, and to tell him of my dear happiness.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
The grey-eyed morning smiles on the frowning night,
checking the eastern clouds with streaks of light,
and flecked darkness, like a drunkard, reels
from day's path and Titan's fiery wheels.
Now, before the sun advances his burning eye,
to cheer the day and dry the night's dank dew,
I must up-fill this osier cage of ours
with baleful weeds and precious-juiced flowers.
The earth, which is nature's mother, is her tomb;
what is her burying grave is her womb,
and from her womb, children of diverse kind
we find sucking on her natural bosom,
many excellent for many virtues,
none but for some, and yet all different.
Oh, much is the powerful grace that lies
in herbs, plants, stones, and their true qualities.
For nothing is so vile that lives on the earth
but that it gives some special good to the earth,
nor anything so good but that, when strained from its fair use,
it revolts from its true birth, stumbling on abuse.
Virtue itself turns to vice, being misapplied;
and vice is sometimes dignified by action.
Within the infant rind of this small flower,
poison has its residence and medicine its power.
For this, being smelled, with that part cheers each part;
being tasted, it slays all senses with the heart.
Two such opposed kings are always encamped
in man as well as in herbs, grace and rude will;
and where the worse is predominant,
full soon the canker of death eats up that plant.

ROMEO:
Good morning, father.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
God bless you!
What early tongue salutes me so sweetly?
Young son, it argues a distempered head
to bid good morrow to your bed so soon.
Care keeps his watch in every old man's eye,
and where care lodges, sleep will never lie;
but where unbruised youth with an unstuffed brain
doth couch his limbs, there golden sleep doth reign.
Therefore, your earliness assures me
you are aroused by some distemperature;
or if not so, then here I hit it right,
our Romeo has not been in bed tonight.

ROMEO:
That last is true; the sweeter rest was mine.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
God pardon sin! Were you with Rosaline?

ROMEO:
With Rosaline, my ghostly father? No;
I have forgotten that name, and that name's woe.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
That's my good son. But where have you been, then?

ROMEO:
I'll tell you, before you ask me again.
I have been feasting with my enemy,
where on a sudden one has wounded me,
that is wounded by me. Both our remedies
lie within your help and holy physic.
I bear no hatred, blessed man, for, lo,
my intercession likewise helps my foe.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Be plain, good son, and homely in your drift;
a riddling confession finds but a riddling shrift.

ROMEO:
Then plainly know my heart's dear love is set
on the fair daughter of rich Capulet.
As mine on hers, so hers is set on mine;
and all is combined, save what you must combine
by holy marriage. When and where and how
we met, we wooed, and made an exchange of vows,
I'll tell you as we pass; but this I pray,
that you consent to marry us today.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Holy Saint Francis, what a change is here!
Is Rosaline, whom you loved so dearly,
so soon forsaken? Young men's love then lies
not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes.
Jesu Maria, what a deal of brine
has washed your sallow cheeks for Rosaline!
How much salt water was thrown away in waste,
to season love, that of it does not taste!
The sun has not yet cleared your sighs from heaven,
your old groans still ring in my ancient ears;
lo, here upon your cheek the stain does sit
of an old tear that is not washed off yet.
If you were ever yourself and these woes were yours,
you and these woes were all for Rosaline.
And are you changed? Pronounce this sentence then,
women may fall, when there's no strength in men.

ROMEO:
You often scolded me for loving Rosaline.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
For doting, not for loving, my pupil.

ROMEO:
And you told me to bury my love.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Not in a grave,
to lay one in, and have another out.

ROMEO:
I pray you, do not chide. She whom I love now
allows grace for grace and love for love;
the other did not so.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Oh, she knew well
your love was recited by rote and you could not spell.
But come, young waverer, come, go with me,
in one respect I'll be your assistant;
for this alliance may prove so happy,
as to turn your households' rancor to pure love.

ROMEO:
Oh, let us go; I am in a sudden haste.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Wisely and slowly; they that run fast stumble.

MERCUTIO:
Where the devil should this Romeo be?
Did he not come home tonight?

BENVOLIO:
Not to his father's; I spoke with his man.

MERCUTIO:
Ah, that same pale, hard-hearted wench, that Rosaline,
torments him so, that he will surely run mad.

BENVOLIO:
Tybalt, the kinsman of old Capulet,
has sent a letter to his father's house.

MERCUTIO:
A challenge, on my life.

BENVOLIO:
Romeo will answer it.

MERCUTIO:
Any man that can write may answer a letter.

BENVOLIO:
No, he will answer the letter's master, how he
dares, being dared.

MERCUTIO:
Alas, poor Romeo! He is already dead; stabbed with a
white wench's black eye; shot through the ear with a
love-song; the very pin of his heart cleft with the
blind bow-boy's butt-shaft. And is he a man to
encounter Tybalt?

BENVOLIO:
Why, what is Tybalt?

MERCUTIO:
He's more than the prince of cats, I can tell you. Oh, he is
the courageous captain of compliments. He fights as
you sing prick-song, keeps time, distance, and
proportion; he rests me his minim rest, one, two, and
the third in your bosom. He's the very butcher of a silk
button, a duelist, a duelist; a gentleman of the
very first house, of the first and second cause.
Ah, the immortal passado! The punto reverso! The
hai!

BENVOLIO:
The what?

MERCUTIO:
A pox on such antic, lisping, affecting
fantasticoes; these new tuners of accents! 'By Jesu,
a very good blade! A very tall man! A very good
whore!' Why, is not this a lamentable thing,
grandsire, that we should be thus afflicted with
these strange flies, these fashion-mongers, these
'pardon-me's,' who stand so much on the new form,
that they cannot sit at ease on the old bench? Oh, their
bones, their bones!

BENVOLIO:
Here comes Romeo, here comes Romeo.

MERCUTIO:
Without his roe, like a dried herring. Flesh, flesh,
how are you fishified! Now he is for the numbers
that Petrarch flowed in. Laura to his lady was but a
kitchen-wench; indeed, she had a better love to
be-rhyme her; Dido a dowdy; Cleopatra a gypsy;
Helen and Hero hildings and harlots; Thisbe a grey
eye or so, but not to the purpose. Signor
Romeo, good day! There's a French salutation
to your French slop. You gave us the counterfeit
fairly last night.

ROMEO:
Good morrow to you both. What counterfeit did I give you?

MERCUTIO:
The slip, sir, the slip; can you not understand?

ROMEO:
Pardon, good Mercutio, my business was great; and in
such a case as mine, a man may strain courtesy.

MERCUTIO:
That's as much as to say, such a case as yours
constrains a man to bow in the hams.

ROMEO:
Meaning, to curtsy.

MERCUTIO:
You have most kindly hit it.

ROMEO:
A most courteous exposition.

MERCUTIO:
No, I am the very pink of courtesy.

ROMEO:
Pink for flower.

MERCUTIO:
Right.

ROMEO:
Why, then my pump is well-flowered.

MERCUTIO:
Well said. Follow me this jest now until you have
worn out your pump, so that when the single sole of it
is worn, the jest may remain after the wearing, solely singular.

ROMEO:
Oh, single-soled jest, solely singular for its
singleness.

MERCUTIO:
Come between us, good Benvolio; my wits are fainting.

ROMEO:
Switch and spurs, switch and spurs; or I'll cry a match.

MERCUTIO:
No, if your wits run the wild-goose chase, I'm
done, for you have more of the wild-goose in one of
your wits than, I'm sure, I have in my whole five.
Was I with you there for the goose?

ROMEO:
You were never with me for anything when you weren't
there for the goose.

MERCUTIO:
I will bite you by the ear for that jest.

ROMEO:
No, good goose, don't bite.

MERCUTIO:
Your wit is a very bitter apple; it is a most
sharp sauce.

ROMEO:
And is it not well served with a sweet goose?

MERCUTIO:
Oh, here's a wit of kidskin, that stretches from an
inch narrow to an ell broad!

ROMEO:
I stretch it out for that word 'broad,' which, added
to the goose, proves you to be a broad goose far and wide.

MERCUTIO:
Why, isn't this better now than groaning for love?
Now you are sociable, now you are Romeo; now you are
what you are, by art as well as by nature.
For this drivelling love is like a great natural,
that runs lolling up and down to hide his bauble in a hole.

BENVOLIO:
Stop there, stop there.

MERCUTIO:
You want me to stop in my tale against the grain.

BENVOLIO:
You would have otherwise made your tale long.

MERCUTIO:
Oh, you are deceived; I would have made it short.
For I had come to the whole depth of my tale, and
I meant, indeed, to occupy the argument no longer.

ROMEO:
Here's a goodly sight!

MERCUTIO:
A sail, a sail!

BENVOLIO:
Two, two; a shirt and a smock.

Nurse:
Peter!

PETER:
Anon!

Nurse:
My fan, Peter.

MERCUTIO:
Good Peter, to hide her face; for her fan is the
fairer face.

Nurse:
Good morning, gentlemen.

MERCUTIO:
Good afternoon, fair gentlewoman.

Nurse:
Is it a good afternoon?

MERCUTIO:
It's no less, I tell you, for the bawdy hand of the
dial is now upon the prick of noon.

Nurse:
Out upon you! What a man you are!

ROMEO:
One, gentlewoman, that God has made for himself to
mar.

Nurse:
By my troth, it is well said; 'for himself to mar,'
said he? Gentlemen, can any of you tell me where I
may find the young Romeo?

ROMEO:
I can tell you; but young Romeo will be older when
you have found him than he was when you sought him.
I am the youngest of that name, for lack of a worse.

Nurse:
You say well.

MERCUTIO:
Yes, is the worst well? Very well taken, in faith;
wisely, wisely.

Nurse:
If you are he, sir, I desire some confidence with
you.

BENVOLIO:
She will invite him to some supper.

MERCUTIO:
A bawd, a bawd, a bawd! So ho!

ROMEO:
What have you found?

MERCUTIO:
No hare, sir; unless a hare, sir, in a Lenten pie,
that is somewhat stale and hoar before it is spent.
An old hare hoar,
and an old hare hoar,
is very good meat in Lent.
But a hare that is hoar
is too much for a score,
when it hoars before it is spent.
Romeo, will you come to your father's? We'll go
to dinner there.

ROMEO:
I will follow you.

MERCUTIO:
Farewell, ancient lady; farewell,
'lady, lady, lady.'

Nurse:
Well, farewell! I ask you, sir, what saucy
merchant was this, that was so full of his roguery?

ROMEO:
A gentleman, nurse, that loves to hear himself talk,
and will speak more in a minute than he will stand
to in a month.

Nurse:
If he speaks anything against me, I'll take him
down, even if he were lustier than he is, and twenty such
Jacks; and if I cannot, I'll find those that shall.
Scurvy knave! I am not one of his flirtatious girls; I am
not one of his loose companions. And must you stand by
too, and let every knave use me at his pleasure?

PETER:
I saw no man use you for his pleasure; if I had, my weapon
would have been out quickly, I assure you. I dare
draw as soon as another man, if I see an occasion in a
good quarrel, and the law is on my side.

Nurse:
Now, before God, I am so vexed, that every part of
me is quivering. Scurvy knave! Please, sir, a word.
And as I told you, my young lady told me to inquire for you;
what she told me to say, I will keep to myself.
But first let me tell you, if you should lead her into
a fool's paradise, as they say, it would be a very gross
kind of behavior, as they say. For the gentlewoman
is young; and, therefore, if you should deal doubly
with her, truly it would be an ill thing to be offered
to any gentlewoman, and very weak dealing.

ROMEO:
Nurse, commend me to your lady and mistress. I
protest to you—

Nurse:
Good heart, and, in faith, I will tell her as much.
Lord, Lord, she will be a joyful woman.

ROMEO:
What will you tell her, nurse? You are not listening to me.

Nurse:
I will tell her, sir, that you do protest; which, as
I take it, is a gentlemanlike offer.

ROMEO:
Tell her to devise
some means to come to confession this afternoon;
and there she shall, at Friar Laurence's cell,
be confessed and married. Here is something for your pains.

Nurse:
No truly, sir; not a penny.

ROMEO:
Go to; I say you shall.

Nurse:
This afternoon, sir? Well, she shall be there.

ROMEO:
And stay, good nurse, behind the abbey wall.
Within this hour my man shall be with you
and bring you cords made like a tackled stair;
which to the high top-gallant of my joy
must be my convoy in the secret night.
Farewell; be trusty, and I'll reward your pains.
Farewell; commend me to your mistress.

Nurse:
Now may God in heaven bless you! Listen, sir.

ROMEO:
What do you say, my dear nurse?

Nurse:
Is your man discreet? Have you never heard it said,
two may keep a secret, if one is put away?

ROMEO:
I guarantee you, my man is as true as steel.

NURSE:
Well, sir; my mistress is the sweetest lady—Lord,
Lord! When she was a little prattling thing—Oh, there
is a nobleman in town, one Paris, that would gladly
lay a knife aboard; but she, good soul, would as soon
see a toad, a very toad, as see him. I anger her
sometimes and tell her that Paris is the more proper
man; but, I'll warrant you, when I say so, she looks
as pale as any clout in the whole world. Don't
rosemary and Romeo both begin with a letter?

ROMEO:
Yes, nurse; what of that? Both with an R.

Nurse:
Ah, mocker! That's the dog's name; R is for
the—no; I know it begins with some other
letter—and she has the prettiest sayings of
it, of you and rosemary, that it would do you good
to hear it.

ROMEO:
Commend me to your lady.

Nurse:
Yes, a thousand times.
Peter!

PETER:
Anon!

Nurse:
Peter, take my fan, and go before and apace.

JULIET:
The clock struck nine when I sent the nurse;
in half an hour she promised to return.
Perhaps she cannot meet him. That's not so.
Oh, she is lame! Love's heralds should be thoughts,
which glide ten times faster than the sun's beams,
driving back shadows over lowering hills.
Therefore, nimble-winged doves draw love,
and therefore, the wind-swift Cupid has wings.
Now the sun is on the highest hill
of this day's journey, and from nine till twelve
is three long hours, yet she has not come.
If she had affections and warm, youthful blood,
she would be as swift in motion as a ball;
my words would bandy her to my sweet love,
and his to me.
But old folks, many feign as if they were dead;
unwieldy, slow, heavy, and pale as lead.
Oh God, she's coming!
Oh, honey nurse, what news?
Have you met with him? Send your man away.

Nurse:
Peter, stay at the gate.

JULIET:
Now, good sweet nurse—O Lord, why do you look so sad?
Though the news may be sad, yet tell it merrily;
if it is good, you are shaming the music of sweet news
by playing it to me with so sour a face.

Nurse:
I am weary, give me leave for a while.
Fie, how my bones ache! What a jaunt I have had!

JULIET:
I wish you had my bones, and I had your news.
No, come, I beg you, speak; good, good nurse, speak.

Nurse:
Jesus, what a hurry! Can't you stay for a while?
Don't you see that I am out of breath?

JULIET:
How are you out of breath, when you have the breath
to say to me that you are out of breath?
The excuse that you make in this delay
is longer than the tale you are excusing.
Is your news good, or bad? Answer to that;
say either, and I'll wait for the details.
Let me be satisfied, is it good or bad?

Nurse:
Well, you have made a simple choice; you don't know
how to choose a man. Romeo! No, not he; though his
face is better than any man's, yet his leg excels
all men's; and as for a hand, and a foot, and a body,
though they are not to be talked about, yet they are
beyond compare. He is not the flower of courtesy,
but, I'll warrant him, as gentle as a lamb. Go your
ways, wench; serve God. What, have you dined at home?

JULIET:
No, no. But all this I knew before.
What does he say about our marriage? What about that?

Nurse:
Lord, how my head aches! What a head I have!
It beats as if it would fall into twenty pieces.
My back on the other side—oh, my back, my back!
A plague on your heart for sending me about,
to catch my death by jaunting up and down!

JULIET:
In faith, I am sorry that you are not well.
Sweet, sweet, sweet nurse, tell me, what does my love say?

Nurse:
Your love says, like an honest gentleman, and a
courteous, and a kind, and a handsome, and, I
warrant, a virtuous one—where is your mother?

JULIET:
Where is my mother! Why, she is within;
where should she be? How oddly you reply!
'Your love says, like an honest gentleman,
where is your mother?'

Nurse:
Oh, God's lady dear!
Are you so hot? Well, come up, I think;
is this the poultice for my aching bones?
From now on, do your messages yourself.

JULIET:
Here's such a fuss! Come, what does Romeo say?

Nurse:
Have you gotten leave to go to confession today?

JULIET:
I have.

Nurse:
Then hurry from here to Friar Laurence's cell;
a husband is waiting there to make you a wife.
Now the wanton blood is coming up in your cheeks,
they'll be scarlet in a moment at any news.
Hurry to church; I must go another way,
to fetch a ladder, by which your love
must climb a bird's nest soon when it is dark.
I am the drudge and I toil in your delight,
but you shall bear the burden soon at night.
Go; I'll go to dinner. Hurry to the cell.

JULIET:
Hie to high fortune! Honest nurse, farewell.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
So may the heavens smile upon this holy act,
that after-hours with sorrow do not chide us!

ROMEO:
Amen, amen! But let whatever sorrow can come,
it cannot countervail the exchange of joy
that one short minute gives me in her sight.
Just close our hands with holy words,
then let love-devouring death do what he dares;
it is enough that I may but call her mine.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
These violent delights have violent ends,
and in their triumph they die, like fire and powder,
which, as they kiss, consume. The sweetest honey
is loathsome in its own deliciousness
and in the taste confounds the appetite.
Therefore, love moderately; long love does so;
too swift arrives as tardy as too slow.
Here comes the lady. Oh, so light a foot
will never wear out the everlasting flint.
A lover may bestride the gossamer
that idles in the wanton summer air,
and yet not fall; so light is vanity.

JULIET:
Good evening to my ghostly confessor.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Romeo shall thank you, daughter, for us both.

JULIET:
As much to him, or else his thanks are too much.

ROMEO:
Ah, Juliet, if the measure of your joy
is heaped like mine, and your skill is more
to blazon it, then sweeten with your breath
this neighbor air, and let rich music's tongue
unfold the imagined happiness that both
receive in each other by this dear encounter.

JULIET:
Conceit, more rich in matter than in words,
brags of its substance, not of its ornament.
They are but beggars that can count their worth;
but my true love has grown to such an excess
that I cannot sum up the sum of half my wealth.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Come, come with me, and we will make short work;
for, by your leave, you shall not stay alone
until the holy church incorporates two into one.

BENVOLIO:
I pray you, good Mercutio, let's go inside.
The day is hot, the Capulets are abroad,
and, if we meet, we will not escape a brawl;
for now, these hot days, the mad blood is stirring.

MERCUTIO:
You are like one of those fellows that when he
enters the confines of a tavern, he claps his sword
upon the table and says, 'God send me no need of
thee!' and by the operation of the second cup, he draws
it on the drawer, when indeed there is no need.

BENVOLIO:
Am I like such a fellow?

MERCUTIO:
Come, come, you are as hot a Jack in your mood as
any in Italy, and as soon moved to be moody, and as
soon moody to be moved.

BENVOLIO:
And what to?

MERCUTIO:
No, if there were two such, we should have none
shortly, for one would kill the other. You! Why,
you will quarrel with a man that has a hair more,
or a hair less, in his beard, than you have. You
will quarrel with a man for cracking nuts, having no
other reason but because you have hazel eyes. What
eye but such an eye would spy out such a quarrel?
Your head is as full of quarrels as an egg is full of
meat, and yet your head has been beaten as addled as
an egg for quarreling. You have quarreled with a
man for coughing in the street, because he has
wakened your dog that has been lying asleep in the sun.
Did you not fall out with a tailor for wearing
his new doublet before Easter? With another, for
tying his new shoes with old ribbon? And yet you
will tutor me against quarreling!

BENVOLIO:
If I were as apt to quarrel as you are, any man
should buy the absolute ownership of my life for an hour and a quarter.

MERCUTIO:
The fee-simple! O simple!

BENVOLIO:
By my head, here come the Capulets.

MERCUTIO:
By my heel, I don't care.

TYBALT:
Follow me close, for I will speak to them.
Gentlemen, good afternoon. A word with one of you.

MERCUTIO:
And but one word with one of us? Couple it with
something; make it a word and a blow.

TYBALT:
You will find me apt enough for that, sir, if you
will give me the occasion.

MERCUTIO:
Could you not take some occasion without it being given?

TYBALT:
Mercutio, you consort with Romeo—

MERCUTIO:
Consort! What, do you make us minstrels? If
you make minstrels of us, look to hear nothing but
discords. Here's my fiddlestick; here's what shall
make you dance. Zounds, consort!

BENVOLIO:
We are talking here in the public haunt of men.
Either withdraw to some private place,
and reason coldly about your grievances,
or else depart; here all eyes are gazing on us.

MERCUTIO:
Men's eyes were made to look, and let them gaze;
I will not budge for any man's pleasure, I.

TYBALT:
Well, peace be with you, sir. Here comes my man.

MERCUTIO:
But I'll be hanged, sir, if he wears your livery.
Indeed, go before him to the field, and he'll be your follower;
your worship in that sense may call him 'man.'

TYBALT:
Romeo, the hate I bear you can afford
no better term than this—you are a villain.

ROMEO:
Tybalt, the reason that I have to love you
doth much excuse the rage that pertains
to such a greeting. I am no villain;
therefore, farewell. I see you don't know me.

TYBALT:
Boy, this shall not excuse the injuries
that you have done me; therefore, turn and draw.

ROMEO:
I do protest, I have never injured you,
but I love you better than you can devise,
until you shall know the reason of my love.
And so, good Capulet—which name I hold
as dearly as my own—be satisfied.

MERCUTIO:
Oh, calm, dishonorable, vile submission!
Alla stoccata carries it away.
Tybalt, you rat-catcher, will you walk?

TYBALT:
What do you want with me?

MERCUTIO:
Good king of cats, nothing but one of your nine
lives; that I mean to make bold with, and as you
shall use me hereafter, I will dry-beat the rest of the
eight. Will you pluck your sword out of its pitcher
by the ears? Make haste, lest mine be about your
ears before it is out.

TYBALT:
I am for you.

ROMEO:
Gentle Mercutio, put your rapier up.

MERCUTIO:
Come, sir, your passado.

ROMEO:
Draw, Benvolio; beat down their weapons.
Gentlemen, for shame, forbear this outrage!
Tybalt, Mercutio, the prince has expressly
forbidden fighting in the streets of Verona.
Hold, Tybalt! Good Mercutio!

MERCUTIO:
I am hurt.
A plague on both your houses! I am done for.
Is he gone, and has nothing?

BENVOLIO:
What, are you hurt?

MERCUTIO:
Yes, yes, a scratch, a scratch; indeed, it's enough.
Where is my page? Go, villain, fetch a surgeon.

ROMEO:
Courage, man; the hurt cannot be much.

MERCUTIO:
No, it's not as deep as a well, nor as wide as a
church-door; but it's enough, it will serve. Ask for
me tomorrow, and you shall find me a grave man. I
am peppered, I warrant, for this world. A plague on
both your houses! Zounds, a dog, a rat, a mouse, a
cat, to scratch a man to death! A braggart, a
rogue, a villain, that fights by the book of
arithmetic! Why the devil did you come between us? I
was hurt under your arm.

ROMEO:
I thought it was all for the best.

MERCUTIO:
Help me into some house, Benvolio,
or I shall faint. A plague on both your houses!
They have made worms' meat of me. I have it,
and soundly too. Your houses!

ROMEO:
This gentleman, the prince's near ally,
my very friend, has gotten his mortal hurt
on my behalf; my reputation is stained
with Tybalt's slander—Tybalt, who for an hour
has been my kinsman! O sweet Juliet,
your beauty has made me effeminate
and in my temper has softened valor's steel!

BENVOLIO:
O Romeo, Romeo, brave Mercutio is dead!
That gallant spirit has aspired to the clouds,
which too untimely here did scorn the earth.

ROMEO:
This day's black fate depends on more days;
this but begins the woe, others must end.

BENVOLIO:
Here comes the furious Tybalt back again.

ROMEO:
Alive, in triumph! And Mercutio slain!
Away to heaven, respective lenity,
and fire-eyed fury be my conduct now!
Now, Tybalt, take the 'villain' back again,
that you gave me late; for Mercutio's soul
is but a little way above our heads,
staying for yours to keep him company.
Either you, or I, or both, must go with him.

TYBALT:
You, wretched boy, that did consort him here,
shall go with him from here.

ROMEO:
This shall decide that.

BENVOLIO:
Romeo, away, be gone!
The citizens are up, and Tybalt is slain.
Don't stand amazed. The prince will sentence you to death,
if you are taken. Hence, be gone, away!

ROMEO:
Oh, I am fortune's fool!

BENVOLIO:
Why are you staying?

First Citizen:
Which way did he run that killed Mercutio?
Tybalt, that murderer, which way did he run?

BENVOLIO:
There lies that Tybalt.

First Citizen:
Up, sir, go with me;
I charge you in the prince's name, obey.

PRINCE:
Where are the vile beginners of this fight?

BENVOLIO:
Oh, noble prince, I can discover all
the unlucky management of this fatal brawl.
There lies the man, slain by young Romeo,
that killed your kinsman, brave Mercutio.

LADY CAPULET:
Tybalt, my cousin! Oh, my brother's child!
O prince! O cousin! Husband! Oh, the blood is spilt
O my dear kinsman! Prince, as you are true,
for our blood, shed the blood of a Montague.
O cousin, cousin!

PRINCE:
Benvolio, who began this bloody fight?

BENVOLIO:
Tybalt, slain here, whom Romeo's hand did slay;
Romeo, who spoke to him fairly, bade him consider
how delicate the quarrel was, and urged, withal,
your high displeasure. All this was uttered
with a gentle breath, a calm look, and humbly bowed knees,
but it could not make a truce with the unruly spleen
of Tybalt, deaf to peace, but that he tilts
with piercing steel at bold Mercutio's breast,
who, just as hot, turns a deadly point to a point,
and, with a martial scorn, with one hand beats
cold death aside, and with the other sends
it back to Tybalt, whose dexterity
retorts it. Romeo cries aloud,
'Hold, friends! Friends, part!' and, swifter than
his tongue,
his agile arm beats down their fatal points,
and rushes between them. Underneath his arm,
an envious thrust from Tybalt hit the life
of stout Mercutio, and then Tybalt fled;
but by and by he comes back to Romeo,
who had but newly entertained revenge,
and to it they go like lightning, for, before I
could draw to part them, stout Tybalt was slain.
And, as he fell, Romeo turned and fled.
This is the truth, or let Benvolio die.

LADY CAPULET:
He is a kinsman to the Montague;
affection makes him false; he does not speak the truth.
Some twenty of them fought in this black strife,
and all those twenty could only kill one life.
I beg for justice, which you, prince, must give;
Romeo killed Tybalt, so Romeo must not live.

PRINCE:
Romeo killed him, and he killed Mercutio;
who now owes the price of his dear blood?

MONTAGUE:
Not Romeo, prince, he was Mercutio's friend;
his fault only concludes what the law should end,
the life of Tybalt.

PRINCE:
And for that offense,
we immediately exile him from here.
I have an interest in your hate's proceeding,
my blood for your rude brawls lies bleeding;
but I'll fine you with so strong a fine
that you shall all repent the loss of mine.
I will be deaf to pleading and excuses;
neither tears nor prayers shall buy out these abuses.
Therefore, use none. Let Romeo go from here in haste,
or else, when he's found, that hour will be his last.
Bear this body from here and attend our will.
Mercy but murders, by pardoning those that kill.

JULIET:
Gallop apace, you fiery-footed steeds,
towards Phoebus's lodging. Such a wagoner
as Phaethon would whip you to the west,
and bring in cloudy night immediately.
Spread your close curtain, love-performing night,
so that the runaway's eyes may wink, and Romeo
may leap to these arms, untalked of and unseen.
Lovers can see to do their amorous rites
by their own beauties; or, if love is blind,
it best agrees with night. Come, civil night,
you sober-suited matron, all in black,
and teach me how to lose a winning match,
played for a pair of stainless maidenhoods.
Hood my untamed blood, which is beating in my cheeks,
with your black mantle; until strange love, grown bold,
thinks that true love acted with simple modesty.
Come, night; come, Romeo; come, you day in night;
for you will lie upon the wings of night
whiter than new snow on a raven's back.
Come, gentle night, come, loving, black-browed night,
give me my Romeo; and, when he shall die,
take him and cut him out in little stars,
and he will make the face of heaven so fine
that all the world will be in love with night
and pay no worship to the garish sun.
Oh, I have bought the mansion of a love,
but I have not possessed it, and, though I am sold,
I have not yet enjoyed it. So tedious is this day
as is the night before some festival
to an impatient child that has new robes
and may not wear them. Oh, here comes my nurse,
and she brings news; and every tongue that speaks
but Romeo's name speaks heavenly eloquence.
Now, nurse, what news? What do you have there? The cords
that Romeo told you to fetch?

Nurse:
Yes, yes, the cords.

JULIET:
Ah me! What news? Why are you wringing your hands?

Nurse:
Ah, well-a-day! He's dead, he's dead, he's dead!
We are undone, lady, we are undone!
Alack the day! He's gone, he's killed, he's dead!

JULIET:
Can heaven be so envious?

Nurse:
Romeo can,
though heaven cannot. Oh, Romeo, Romeo!
Who ever would have thought it? Romeo!

JULIET:
What devil are you, that you torment me thus?
This torture should be roared in dismal hell.
Has Romeo killed himself? Say but 'yes,'
and that bare vowel 'I' shall poison more
than the death-darting eye of a cockatrice.
I am not I, if there is such an 'I';
or if those eyes are shut, that make you answer 'yes.'
If he is slain, say 'yes'; or if not, 'no.'
Brief sounds will determine my well-being or my woe.

Nurse:
I saw the wound, I saw it with my own eyes—
God save the mark!—here on his manly breast.
A piteous corpse, a bloody, piteous corpse;
pale, pale as ashes, all bedaubed in blood,
all in gore-blood; I swooned at the sight.

JULIET:
Oh, break, my heart! Poor bankrupt, break at once!
To prison, eyes, never look on liberty!
Vile earth, to earth resign; end motion here;
and you and Romeo press one heavy bier!

Nurse:
Oh Tybalt, Tybalt, the best friend I had!
Oh courteous Tybalt! Honest gentleman!
That I should ever live to see you dead!

JULIET:
What storm is this that blows so contrary?
Is Romeo slaughtered, and is Tybalt dead?
My dear-loved cousin, and my dearer lord?
Then, dreadful trumpet, sound the general doom!
For who is living, if those two are gone?

Nurse:
Tybalt is gone, and Romeo is banished;
Romeo that killed him, he is banished.

JULIET:
O God! Did Romeo's hand shed Tybalt's blood?

Nurse:
It did, it did; alas the day, it did!

JULIET:
Oh, a serpent's heart, hidden with a flowering face!
Did a dragon ever keep so fair a cave?
A beautiful tyrant! An angelic fiend!
A dove-feathered raven! A wolfish-ravening lamb!
A despised substance of the most divine show!
The exact opposite of what you justly seem,
a damned saint, an honorable villain!
Oh nature, what did you have to do in hell,
when you placed the spirit of a fiend
in the moral paradise of such sweet flesh?
Was there ever a book containing such vile matter
so fairly bound? Oh, that deceit should dwell
in such a gorgeous palace!

Nurse:
There's no trust,
no faith, no honesty in men; all are perjured,
all forsworn, all naught, all dissemblers.
Ah, where's my man? Give me some aqua vitae.
These griefs, these woes, these sorrows make me old.
Shame come to Romeo!

JULIET:
May your tongue be blistered
for such a wish! He was not born to shame.
Upon his brow, shame is ashamed to sit;
for it is a throne where honor may be crowned
the sole monarch of the universal earth.
Oh, what a beast I was to scold him!

Nurse:
Will you speak well of him that killed your cousin?

JULIET:
Shall I speak ill of him that is my husband?
Ah, my poor lord, what tongue shall smooth your name,
when I, your three-hour wife, have mangled it?
But, why, villain, did you kill my cousin?
That villain cousin would have killed my husband.
Back, foolish tears, back to your native spring;
your tributary drops belong to woe,
which you, mistaking, offer up to joy.
My husband lives, whom Tybalt would have slain;
and Tybalt is dead, who would have slain my husband.
All this is comfort; why then do I weep?
There was some word, worse than Tybalt's death,
that murdered me. I would gladly forget it;
but, oh, it presses to my memory,
like damned guilty deeds to sinners' minds.
'Tybalt is dead, and Romeo—banished.'
That 'banished,' that one word 'banished,'
has slain ten thousand Tybalts. Tybalt's death
was woe enough, if it had ended there.
Or, if sour woe delights in fellowship
and will needs be ranked with other griefs,
why did it not follow, when she said 'Tybalt's dead,'
your father, or your mother, no, or both,
which modern lamentations might have moved?
But with a rear-guard following Tybalt's death,
'Romeo is banished,' to speak that word,
is father, mother, Tybalt, Romeo, Juliet,
all slain, all dead. 'Romeo is banished!'
There is no end, no limit, measure, bound,
in that word's death; no words can sound that woe.
Where are my father and my mother, nurse?

Nurse:
Weeping and wailing over Tybalt's corpse.
Will you go to them? I will bring you there.

JULIET:
They may wash his wounds with tears; mine shall be spent,
when theirs are dry, for Romeo's banishment.
Take up those cords. Poor ropes, you are beguiled,
both you and I; for Romeo is exiled.
He made you for a highway to my bed;
but I, a maid, will die a maiden-widow.
Come, cords, come, nurse; I'll go to my wedding-bed;
and death, not Romeo, will take my maidenhead!

Nurse:
Hurry to your chamber. I'll find Romeo
to comfort you. I know well where he is.
Listen, your Romeo will be here at night.
I'll go to him; he is hidden at Laurence's cell.

JULIET:
Oh, find him! Give this ring to my true knight,
and bid him come to take his last farewell.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Romeo, come forth; come forth, you fearful man.
Affliction is in love with your parts,
and you are wedded to calamity.

ROMEO:
Father, what news? What is the prince's doom?
What sorrow craves acquaintance at my hand,
that I do not yet know?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
My dear son is too familiar
with such sour company.
I bring you news of the prince's doom.

ROMEO:
What less than doomsday is the prince's doom?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
A gentler judgment vanished from his lips,
not death of the body, but banishment of the body.

ROMEO:
Ha, banishment! Be merciful, say 'death';
for exile has more terror in its look,
much more than death. Do not say 'banishment.'

FRIAR LAURENCE:
You are banished from Verona.
Be patient, for the world is broad and wide.

ROMEO:
There is no world outside of Verona's walls,
but purgatory, torture, hell itself.
To be banished from here is to be banished from the world,
and the world's exile is death. Then to be banished,
is death misnamed. By calling death banishment,
you are cutting my head off with a golden axe,
and smiling upon the stroke that murders me.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Oh deadly sin! Oh rude unthankfulness!
Your fault our law calls death; but the kind prince,
taking your part, has brushed aside the law,
and turned that black word 'death' to 'banishment.'
This is dear mercy, and you do not see it.

ROMEO:
It is torture, and not mercy. Heaven is here,
where Juliet lives; and every cat and dog
and little mouse, every unworthy thing,
lives here in heaven and may look on her;
but Romeo may not. More validity,
more honorable state, more courtship lives
in carrion-flies than in Romeo. They may seize
on the white wonder of dear Juliet's hand
and steal an immortal blessing from her lips,
who, even in pure and vestal modesty,
still blush, as if thinking their own kisses are a sin;
but Romeo may not; he is banished.
Flies may do this, but I from this must fly.
They are free men, but I am banished.
And do you still say that exile is not death?
Did you have no mixed poison, no sharp-ground knife,
no sudden means of death, though ever so mean,
but 'banished' to kill me?—'Banished'?
Oh friar, the damned use that word in hell;
howlings attend it. How do you have the heart,
being a divine, a ghostly confessor,
a sin-absolver, and my professed friend,
to mangle me with that word 'banished'?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
You foolish madman, just hear me speak a word.

ROMEO:
Oh, you will speak again of banishment.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
I'll give you armor to keep off that word:
adversity's sweet milk, philosophy,
to comfort you, though you are banished.

ROMEO:
Yet 'banished'? Hang up philosophy!
Unless philosophy can make a Juliet,
displant a town, or reverse a prince's doom,
it doesn't help, it doesn't prevail. Talk no more.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Oh, then I see that madmen have no ears.

ROMEO:
How should they, when wise men have no eyes?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Let me dispute with you about your estate.

ROMEO:
You cannot speak of what you do not feel.
If you were as young as I, Juliet your love,
married but an hour, Tybalt murdered,
doting like me and banished like me,
then you might speak, then you might tear your hair,
and fall upon the ground, as I do now,
taking the measure of an unmade grave.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Arise; someone is knocking. Good Romeo, hide yourself.

ROMEO:
Not I; unless the breath of heart-sick groans,
mist-like, enfolds me from the search of eyes.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Hark, how they knock! Who's there? Romeo, arise;
you will be taken. Stay a while! Stand up;
run to my study. By and by! God's will,
what simpleness is this! I come, I come!
Who knocks so hard? Where do you come from? What's your will?

Nurse:

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Welcome, then.

Nurse:
Oh holy friar, oh, tell me, holy friar,
where is my lady's lord, where's Romeo?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
There on the ground, made drunk with his own tears.

Nurse:
Oh, he is even in my mistress's case,
just in her case! Oh, woeful sympathy!
Piteous predicament! Even so she lies,
blubbering and weeping, weeping and blubbering.
Stand up, stand up; stand, if you are a man.
For Juliet's sake, for her sake, rise and stand;
why should you fall into so deep an 'O'?

ROMEO:
Nurse!

Nurse:
Ah sir! Ah sir! Well, death is the end of all.

ROMEO:
Did you speak of Juliet? How is she?
Does she not think of me as an old murderer,
now that I have stained the childhood of our joy
with blood removed but little from her own?
Where is she? And how is she? And what does
my concealed lady say to our canceled love?

Nurse:
Oh, she says nothing, sir, but weeps and weeps;
and now she falls on her bed; and then she starts up,
and calls Tybalt's name; and then she cries for Romeo,
and then she falls down again.

ROMEO:
As if that name,
shot from the deadly level of a gun,
did murder her; as that name's cursed hand
murdered her kinsman. Oh, tell me, friar, tell me,
in what vile part of this anatomy
does my name lodge? Tell me, so that I may sack
the hateful mansion.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Hold your desperate hand.
Are you a man? Your form cries out that you are.
Your tears are womanish; your wild acts denote
the unreasonable fury of a beast.
An unseemly woman in a seeming man!
Or an ill-beseeming beast in seeming both!
You have amazed me. By my holy order,
I thought your disposition was better tempered.
Have you slain Tybalt? Will you slay yourself?
And stay your lady too that lives in you,
by doing damned hate upon yourself?
Why do you rail on your birth, the heaven, and the earth?
Since birth, and heaven, and earth, all three meet
in you at once; which you would at once lose.
Fie, fie, you shame your shape, your love, your wit;
which, like a usurer, you abound in all,
and use none in that true use indeed
which should bedeck your shape, your love, your wit.
Your noble shape is but a form of wax,
digressing from the valor of a man;
your dear love sworn is but hollow perjury,
killing that love which you have vowed to cherish;
your wit, that ornament to shape and love,
is misshapen in the conduct of them both,
like powder in a skill-less soldier's flask,
is set on fire by your own ignorance,
and you are dismembered with your own defense.
What, rouse yourself, man! Your Juliet is alive,
for whose dear sake you were but lately dead;
there you are happy. Tybalt would kill you,
but you killed Tybalt; there you are happy too.
The law that threatened death becomes your friend
and turns it to exile; there you are happy.
A pack of blessings light upon your back;
happiness courts you in her best array;
but, like a misbehaved and sullen wench,
you pout upon your fortune and your love.
Take heed, take heed, for such die miserable.
Go, get to your love, as was decreed,
ascend her chamber, from here, and comfort her.
But see that you do not stay until the watch is set,
for then you cannot pass to Mantua;
where you shall live, until we can find a time
to blaze your marriage, reconcile your friends,
beg pardon of the prince, and call you back
with twenty hundred thousand times more joy
than you went forth in lamentation.
Go before, nurse. Commend me to your lady;
and bid her hasten all the house to bed,
which heavy sorrow makes them apt to do.
Romeo is coming.

Nurse:
Oh Lord, I could have stayed here all night
to hear good counsel. Oh, what learning is!
My lord, I'll tell my lady you will come.

ROMEO:
Do so, and bid my sweet prepare to chide.

Nurse:
Here, sir, a ring she told me to give you, sir.
Hurry, make haste, for it is getting very late.

ROMEO:
How well my comfort is revived by this!

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Go hence; good night; and here stands all your state.
Either be gone before the watch is set,
or by the break of day, disguised from here.
Sojourn in Mantua; I'll find your man,
and he shall signify from time to time
every good thing that happens to you here.
Give me your hand; it's late. Farewell; good night.

ROMEO:
But that a joy past joy calls out to me,
it would be a grief, to part with you so briefly. Farewell.

CAPULET:
Things have turned out, sir, so unluckily,
that we have had no time to move our daughter.
Look you, she loved her kinsman Tybalt dearly,
and so did I. Well, we were born to die.
It's very late, she'll not come down tonight.
I promise you, but for your company,
I would have been in bed an hour ago.

PARIS:
These times of woe afford no time to woo.
Madam, good night. Commend me to your daughter.

LADY CAPULET:
I will, and I'll know her mind early tomorrow;
tonight she is shut up with her heaviness.

CAPULET:
Sir Paris, I will make a desperate offer
of my child's love. I think she will be ruled
in all respects by me; no, more, I have no doubt of it.
Wife, go to her before you go to bed;
acquaint her here of my son Paris's love;
and bid her, mark me, on Wednesday next—
but, wait! What day is this?

PARIS:
Monday, my lord.

CAPULET:
Monday! Ha, ha! Well, Wednesday is too soon,
let it be on Thursday. On Thursday, tell her,
she shall be married to this noble earl.
Will you be ready? Do you like this haste?
We'll keep no great ado—a friend or two;
for, listen, Tybalt being slain so recently,
it may be thought we held him carelessly,
being our kinsman, if we revel much.
Therefore, we'll have about half a dozen friends,
and that's the end of it. But what do you say to Thursday?

PARIS:
My lord, I wish that Thursday were tomorrow.

CAPULET:
Well, get you gone. On Thursday be it, then.
Go to Juliet before you go to bed,
prepare her, wife, for this wedding-day.
Farewell, my lord. Light to my chamber, ho!
Before me! It is so very, very late,
that we may call it early by and by.
Good night.

JULIET:
Will you be gone? It is not yet near day.
It was the nightingale, and not the lark,
that pierced the fearful hollow of your ear;
she sings nightly on that pomegranate-tree.
Believe me, love, it was the nightingale.

ROMEO:
It was the lark, the herald of the morning,
no nightingale. Look, love, what envious streaks
do lace the severing clouds in the east yonder.
Night's candles are burnt out, and jocund day
stands on tiptoe on the misty mountain tops.
I must be gone and live, or stay and die.

JULIET:
That light is not daylight, I know it, I.
It is some meteor that the sun exhales,
to be a torch-bearer to you this night,
and to light you on your way to Mantua.
Therefore, stay yet; you need not be gone.

ROMEO:
Let me be taken, let me be put to death;
I am content, so you will have it so.
I'll say that grey is not the morning's eye,
it's but the pale reflection of Cynthia's brow;
nor is that the lark, whose notes do beat
the vaulted heaven so high above our heads.
I have more desire to stay than will to go.
Come, death, and welcome! Juliet wills it so.
How is it, my soul? Let's talk; it is not day.

JULIET:
It is, it is! Hurry away, be gone, away!
It is the lark that sings so out of tune,
straining harsh discords and unpleasing sharps.
Some say the lark makes sweet division;
this does not so, for she divides us.
Some say the lark and the loathed toad change eyes,
oh, now I wish they had changed voices too!
Since arm from arm that voice does frighten us,
hunting you away with a hunt's-up to the day,
oh, now be gone; it grows more and more light.

ROMEO:
More light and light; more dark and dark our woes!

Nurse:
Madam!

JULIET:
Nurse?

Nurse:
Your lady mother is coming to your chamber.
The day has broken; be wary, look about.

JULIET:
Then, window, let day in, and let life out.

ROMEO:
Farewell, farewell! One kiss, and I'll descend.

JULIET:
Are you gone so? Love, lord, yes, husband, friend!
I must hear from you every day in the hour,
for in a minute there are many days.
Oh, by this count I shall be much in years
before I see my Romeo again!

ROMEO:
Farewell!
I will not omit any opportunity
that may convey my greetings, love, to you.

JULIET:
Oh, do you think we shall ever meet again?

ROMEO:
I do not doubt it; and all these woes shall serve
for sweet discourses in our time to come.

JULIET:
Oh God, I have an ill-divining soul!
I think I see you, now you are below,
as one dead in the bottom of a tomb.
Either my eyesight is failing, or you look pale.

ROMEO:
And trust me, love, in my eye so do you.
Dry sorrow drinks our blood. Adieu, adieu!

JULIET:
O fortune, fortune! All men call you fickle.
If you are fickle, what do you do with him
that is renowned for faith? Be fickle, fortune;
for then, I hope, you will not keep him long,
but send him back.

LADY CAPULET:

JULIET:
Who is it that calls? Is it my lady mother?
Is she not down so late, or up so early?
What unaccustomed cause brings her here?

LADY CAPULET:
Why, how now, Juliet!

JULIET:
Madam, I am not well.

LADY CAPULET:
Are you still weeping for your cousin's death?
What, will you wash him from his grave with tears?
And if you could, you could not make him live;
therefore, be done. Some grief shows much of love;
but much of grief still shows some want of wit.

JULIET:
Yet let me weep for such a feeling loss.

LADY CAPULET:
So you will feel the loss, but not the friend
for whom you weep.

JULIET:
Feeling so the loss,
I cannot choose but ever weep for the friend.

LADY CAPULET:
Well, girl, you are not weeping so much for his death,
as for the fact that the villain who slaughtered him is still alive.

JULIET:
What villain, madam?

LADY CAPULET:
That same villain, Romeo.

JULIET:

LADY CAPULET:
That is, because the traitorous murderer lives.

JULIET:
Yes, madam, out of the reach of these my hands.
I wish no one but I could avenge my cousin's death!

LADY CAPULET:
We will have vengeance for it, don't you worry.
Then weep no more. I'll send to one in Mantua,
where that same banished runaway lives,
who will give him such an unaccustomed drink,
that he will soon keep Tybalt company.
And then, I hope, you will be satisfied.

JULIET:
Indeed, I shall never be satisfied
with Romeo, until I behold him—dead—
is my poor heart, so vexed for a kinsman.
Madam, if you could but find a man
to bear a poison, I would temper it;
so that Romeo should, upon receipt of it,
soon sleep in quiet. Oh, how my heart abhors
to hear him named, and not be able to come to him,
to wreak the love I bore my cousin
upon his body that slaughtered him!

LADY CAPULET:
You find the means, and I'll find such a man.
But now I'll tell you joyful news, girl.

JULIET:
And joy comes well in such a needy time.
What are they, I beseech your ladyship?

LADY CAPULET:
Well, well, you have a careful father, child;
one who, to put you from your heaviness,
has sorted out a sudden day of joy,
that you don't expect nor I didn't look for.

JULIET:
Madam, in happy time, what day is that?

LADY CAPULET:
Well, my child, early next Thursday morning,
the gallant, young, and noble gentleman,
the County Paris, at Saint Peter's Church,
shall happily make you a joyful bride there.

JULIET:
Now, by Saint Peter's Church and Peter too,
he shall not make me a joyful bride there.
I wonder at this haste; that I must wed
before he, that should be my husband, comes to woo.
I pray you, tell my lord and father, madam,
I will not marry yet; and, when I do, I swear,
it shall be Romeo, whom you know I hate,
rather than Paris. These are news indeed!

LADY CAPULET:
Here comes your father; tell him so yourself,
and see how he will take it at your hands.

CAPULET:
When the sun sets, the air drizzles dew;
but for the sunset of my brother's son,
it rains downright.
What now! A conduit, girl? What, still in tears?
Evermore showering? In one little body
you counterfeit a bark, a sea, a wind;
for your eyes, which I may call the sea,
do ebb and flow with tears; the bark is your body,
sailing in this salt flood; the winds are your sighs;
which, raging with your tears, and they with them,
without a sudden calm, will overturn
your tempest-tossed body. What now, wife!
Have you delivered our decree to her?

LADY CAPULET:
Yes, sir; but she will have none of it, she gives you thanks.
I wish the fool were married to her grave!

CAPULET:
Wait! Take me with you, take me with you, wife.
What! She will have none? Does she not give us thanks?
Is she not proud? Does she not count herself blessed,
unworthy as she is, that we have wrought
so worthy a gentleman to be her bridegroom?

JULIET:
Not proud that you have, but thankful that you have.
Proud I can never be of what I hate;
but thankful even for hate, that is meant as love.

CAPULET:
What now, what now, you chop-logic! What is this?
'Proud,' and 'I thank you,' and 'I thank you not';
and yet 'not proud,' mistress minion, you,
thank me no thankings, nor proud me no prouds,
but get your fine joints ready for next Thursday,
to go with Paris to Saint Peter's Church,
or I will drag you on a hurdle there.
Out, you green-sickness carrion! Out, you baggage!
You tallow-face!

LADY CAPULET:
Fie, fie! What, are you mad?

JULIET:
Good father, I beseech you on my knees,
hear me with patience, but to speak a word.

CAPULET:
Hang you, young baggage! Disobedient wretch!
I tell you what: get you to church on Thursday,
or never after look me in the face.
Speak not, reply not, do not answer me;
my fingers itch. Wife, we scarce thought ourselves blessed
that God had lent us but this only child;
but now I see this one is one too many,
and that we have a curse in having her.
Out on her, hussy!

Nurse:
God in heaven bless her!
You are to blame, my lord, to scold her so.

CAPULET:
And why, my lady wisdom? Hold your tongue,
good prudence; go and gossip with your friends.

Nurse:
I speak no treason.

CAPULET:
Oh, God give you good evening.

Nurse:
May not one speak?

CAPULET:
Peace, you mumbling fool!
Utter your gravity over a gossip's bowl;
for here we do not need it.

LADY CAPULET:
You are too hot.

CAPULET:
God's bread! It makes me mad.
Day, night, hour, tide, time, work, play,
alone, in company, my care has always been
to have her matched. And having now provided
a gentleman of noble parentage,
of fair estates, youthful, and nobly trained,
stuffed, as they say, with honorable parts,
proportioned as one's thought would wish a man;
and then to have a wretched, puling fool,
a whining puppet, in her fortune's tender,
to answer, 'I'll not wed; I cannot love,
I am too young; I pray you, pardon me.'
But, as you will not wed, I'll pardon you.
Graze where you will, you shall not house with me.
Look to it, think on it, I do not use to jest.
Thursday is near; lay your hand on your heart, advise.
If you are mine, I'll give you to my friend;
and if you are not, hang, beg, starve, die in
the streets,
for, by my soul, I'll never acknowledge you,
nor shall what is mine ever do you any good.
Trust to it, think about it; I will not be forsworn.

JULIET:
Is there no pity sitting in the clouds,
that sees into the bottom of my grief?
Oh, sweet my mother, do not cast me away!
Delay this marriage for a month, a week;
or, if you do not, make the bridal bed
in that dim monument where Tybalt lies.

LADY CAPULET:
Don't talk to me, for I won't speak a word.
Do as you will, for I have done with you.

JULIET:
Oh God!—Oh nurse, how can this be prevented?
My husband is on earth, my faith is in heaven;
how shall that faith return again to earth,
unless that husband sends it to me from heaven
by leaving the earth? Comfort me, counsel me.
Alas, alas, that heaven should practice stratagems
upon so soft a subject as myself!
What do you say? Do you not have a word of joy?
Some comfort, nurse.

Nurse:
Faith, here it is.
Romeo is banished; and it's all the world to nothing,
that he dares never come back to challenge you;
or, if he does, it must be by stealth.
Then, since the case so stands as it does now,
I think it's best you married with the county.
Oh, he's a lovely gentleman!
Romeo is a dishcloth compared to him. An eagle, madam,
does not have so green, so quick, so fair an eye
as Paris has. I curse my very heart,
I think you are happy in this second match,
for it excels your first. Or if it did not,
your first is dead; or it would be as good if he were,
as living here and you have no use of him.

JULIET:
Do you speak from your heart?

Nurse:
And from my soul too;
or else may both of them be cursed.

JULIET:
Amen!

Nurse:
What?

JULIET:
Well, you have comforted me marvelously much.
Go in, and tell my lady I am gone,
having displeased my father, to Laurence's cell,
to make confession and to be absolved.

Nurse:
Indeed, I will; and this is wisely done.

JULIET:
Ancient damnation! Oh, most wicked fiend!
Is it more of a sin to wish me thus forsworn,
or to dispraise my lord with that same tongue
which she has praised him with above compare
so many thousand times? Go, counselor;
you and my bosom shall henceforth be two.
I'll go to the friar, to know his remedy.
If all else fails, I myself have the power to die.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
On Thursday, sir? The time is very short.

PARIS:
My father Capulet will have it so;
and I am not slow to slacken his haste.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
You say you do not know the lady's mind.
The course is uneven, I do not like it.

PARIS:
She is weeping immoderately for Tybalt's death,
and therefore I have talked little of love;
for Venus does not smile in a house of tears.
Now, sir, her father considers it dangerous
that she gives her sorrow so much sway,
and in his wisdom, he hastens our marriage,
to stop the inundation of her tears;
which, being too much minded by herself alone,
may be put from her by society.
Now you know the reason for this haste.

FRIAR LAURENCE:

PARIS:
Happily met, my lady and my wife!

JULIET:
That may be, sir, when I may be a wife.

PARIS:
That may be, must be, love, on Thursday next.

JULIET:
What must be, shall be.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
That's a certain text.

PARIS:
Have you come to make confession to this father?

JULIET:
To answer that, I should confess to you.

PARIS:
Do not deny to him that you love me.

JULIET:
I will confess to you that I love him.

PARIS:
So you will, I am sure, that you love me.

JULIET:
If I do so, it will be of more price,
being spoken behind your back, than to your face.

PARIS:
Poor soul, your face is much abused with tears.

JULIET:
The tears have gotten a small victory by that,
for it was bad enough before their spite.

PARIS:
You wrong it more than tears with that report.

JULIET:
That is no slander, sir, which is a truth;
and what I spoke, I spoke it to my face.

PARIS:
Your face is mine, and you have slandered it.

JULIET:
It may be so, for it is not my own.
Are you at leisure, holy father, now;
or shall I come to you at evening mass?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
My leisure serves me, pensive daughter, now.
My lord, we must entreat for some time alone.

PARIS:
God forbid I should disturb your devotion!
Juliet, on Thursday early I will rouse you.
Until then, adieu; and keep this holy kiss.

JULIET:
Oh, shut the door! And when you have done so,
come and weep with me; past hope, past cure, past help!

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Ah, Juliet, I already know your grief;
it strains me past the compass of my wits.
I hear you must, and nothing may postpone it,
on Thursday next be married to this county.

JULIET:
Don't tell me, friar, that you hear of this,
unless you tell me how I may prevent it.
If, in your wisdom, you can give no help,
just call my resolution wise,
and with this knife I'll help it presently.
God joined my heart and Romeo's, you our hands;
and before this hand, sealed by you to Romeo,
shall be the label to another deed,
or my true heart, with treacherous revolt,
turns to another, this shall slay them both.
Therefore, out of your long-experienced time,
give me some present counsel, or, behold,
between my extremes and me this bloody knife
shall play the umpire, arbitrating that
which the commission of your years and art
could bring to no issue of true honor.
Don't be so long in speaking; I long to die,
if what you speak does not speak of a remedy.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Hold, daughter. I spy a kind of hope,
which craves as desperate an execution
as that is desperate which we would prevent.
If, rather than marry County Paris,
you have the strength of will to kill yourself,
then it is likely you will undertake
a thing like death to drive away this shame,
that you would cope with death itself to escape from it.
And, if you dare, I'll give you a remedy.

JULIET:
Oh, bid me leap, rather than marry Paris,
from off the battlements of yonder tower;
or walk in thievish ways; or bid me lurk
where serpents are; chain me with roaring bears;
or shut me nightly in a charnel-house,
completely covered with dead men's rattling bones,
with reeking shanks and yellow, chapless skulls;
or bid me go into a newly-made grave
and hide me with a dead man in his shroud;
things that, to hear them told, have made me tremble;
and I will do it without fear or doubt,
to live an unstained wife to my sweet love.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Hold, then; go home, be merry, give consent
to marry Paris. Wednesday is tomorrow.
Tomorrow night, see that you lie alone;
do not let your nurse lie with you in your chamber.
Take this vial, being then in bed,
and drink off this distilled liquor.
When you do, a cold and drowsy humor will run
through all your veins, for no pulse
shall keep its native progress, but will cease.
No warmth, no breath, shall testify that you are alive;
the roses in your lips and cheeks shall fade
to pale ashes, and your eyes' windows will fall,
like death, when he shuts up the day of life.
Each part, deprived of supple government,
shall, stiff and stark and cold, appear like death.
And in this borrowed likeness of shrunken death
you shall continue for two and forty hours,
and then awake as from a pleasant sleep.
Now, when the bridegroom in the morning comes
to rouse you from your bed, there you are, dead.
Then, as the manner of our country is,
in your best robes, uncovered on the bier,
you shall be borne to that same ancient vault
where all the kindred of the Capulets lie.
In the meantime, before you shall awake,
Romeo shall know our plan by my letters,
and he shall come here. And he and I
will watch your waking, and that very night
Romeo shall bear you from here to Mantua.
And this shall free you from this present shame;
if no inconstant toy, nor womanish fear,
abates your valor in the acting of it.

JULIET:
Give it to me, give it to me! Oh, don't tell me about fear!

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Hold; get you gone, be strong and prosperous
in this resolve. I'll send a friar with speed
to Mantua, with my letters to your lord.

JULIET:
Love, give me strength! And strength shall help afford.
Farewell, dear father!

CAPULET:
Invite as many guests as are written here.
Sirrah, go hire me twenty cunning cooks.

Second Servant:
You shall have none that are bad, sir; for I'll test if they
can lick their fingers.

CAPULET:
How can you test them so?

Second Servant:
Well, sir, it's a bad cook that cannot lick his
own fingers. Therefore, he that cannot lick his
fingers does not go with me.

CAPULET:
Go, be gone.
We shall be very unprepared for this occasion.
What, has my daughter gone to Friar Laurence?

Nurse:
Yes, indeed.

CAPULET:
Well, he may chance to do some good on her.
She is a peevish, self-willed hussy.

Nurse:
See where she comes from confession with a merry look.

CAPULET:
How now, my headstrong girl! Where have you been gadding?

JULIET:
Where I have learned to repent the sin
of disobedient opposition
to you and your commands, and I am enjoined
by holy Laurence to fall prostrate here,
and beg your pardon. Pardon, I beseech you!
Henceforth I am ever ruled by you.

CAPULET:
Send for the county; go and tell him of this.
I'll have this knot tied up tomorrow morning.

JULIET:
I met the youthful lord at Laurence's cell,
and I gave him what became love I might,
not stepping over the bounds of modesty.

CAPULET:
Why, I'm glad of it. This is well. Stand up.
This is as it should be. Let me see the county;
yes, indeed, go, I say, and fetch him here.
Now, before God! This reverend holy friar,
our whole city is much indebted to him.

JULIET:
Nurse, will you go with me into my closet,
to help me sort such needful ornaments
as you think fit to furnish me tomorrow?

LADY CAPULET:
No, not until Thursday; there is time enough.

CAPULET:
Go, nurse, go with her. We'll go to church tomorrow.

LADY CAPULET:
We shall be short in our provisions.
It is now near night.

CAPULET:
Tush, I will stir about,
and all things shall be well, I guarantee you, wife.
Go to Juliet, help to deck her up;
I'll not go to bed tonight; let me alone;
I'll play the housewife for this once. What, ho!
They are all gone. Well, I will go myself
to County Paris, to prepare him
for tomorrow. My heart is wondrously light,
since this same wayward girl is so reclaimed.

JULIET:
Yes, those clothes are the best. But, gentle nurse,
I pray you, leave me to myself tonight,
for I have need of many prayers
to move the heavens to smile upon my state,
which, as you well know, is crossed, and full of sin.

LADY CAPULET:
What, are you busy, ho? Do you need my help?

JULIET:
No, madam; we have culled such necessaries
as are befitting for our state tomorrow.
So, if you please, let me now be left alone,
and let the nurse this night sit up with you;
for, I am sure, you all have your hands full,
in this so sudden business.

LADY CAPULET:
Good night.
Get to bed, and rest; for you have need.

JULIET:
Farewell! God knows when we shall meet again.
I have a faint, cold fear that thrills through my veins,
that almost freezes up the heat of life.
I'll call them back again to comfort me.
Nurse! What should she do here?
I must act my dismal scene alone.
Come, vial.
What if this mixture does not work at all?
Shall I be married then tomorrow morning?
No, no. This shall forbid it. Lie there.
What if it is a poison, which the friar
has subtly administered to have me dead,
lest in this marriage he should be dishonored,
because he married me before to Romeo?
I fear it is. And yet, I think, it should not be,
for he has always been tried as a holy man.
What if, when I am laid in the tomb,
I wake before the time that Romeo
comes to redeem me? There's a fearful point!
Shall I not, then, be stifled in the vault,
to whose foul mouth no wholesome air breathes in,
and there die strangled before my Romeo comes?
Or, if I live, is it not very likely,
the horrible conceit of death and night,
together with the terror of the place—
as in a vault, an ancient receptacle,
where, for these many hundred years, the bones
of all my buried ancestors are packed;
where bloody Tybalt, yet but new in the earth,
lies festering in his shroud; where, as they say,
at some hours in the night, spirits resort;—
Alas, alas, is it not likely that I,
so early waking, what with loathsome smells,
and shrieks like mandrakes torn out of the earth,
