that living mortals, hearing them, run mad?
Oh, if I wake, shall I not be distraught,
environed with all these hideous fears?
And madly play with my forefathers' joints?
And pluck the mangled Tybalt from his shroud?
And, in this rage, with some great kinsman's bone,
as with a club, dash out my desperate brains?
Oh, look! I think I see my cousin's ghost
seeking out Romeo, that did spit his body
upon a rapier's point. Stay, Tybalt, stay!
Romeo, I come! This I drink to you.

LADY CAPULET:
Hold, take these keys, and fetch more spices, nurse.

Nurse:
They are calling for dates and quinces in the pastry.

CAPULET:
Come, stir, stir, stir! The second cock has crowed,
the curfew-bell has rung, it's three o'clock.
Look to the baked meats, good Angelica.
Spare no expense.

Nurse:
Go, you cot-quean, go,
get you to bed; faith, you'll be sick tomorrow
for this night's watching.

CAPULET:
No, not a bit. What! I have watched before now
all night for a lesser cause, and never been sick.

LADY CAPULET:
Yes, you have been a mouse-hunter in your time;
but I will watch you from such watching now.

CAPULET:
A jealous hood, a jealous hood!
Now, fellow,
what's there?

First Servant:
Things for the cook, sir; but I don't know what.

CAPULET:
Make haste, make haste.
Sirrah, fetch drier logs.
Call Peter, he will show you where they are.

Second Servant:
I have a head, sir, that will find out logs,
and never trouble Peter for the matter.

CAPULET:
Well, and well said; a merry whoreson, ha!
You shall be a logger-head. Good faith, it's day.
The county will be here with music straight away,
for so he said he would. I hear him near.
Nurse! Wife! What, ho! What, nurse, I say!
Go and waken Juliet, go and trim her up;
I'll go and chat with Paris. Hurry, make haste,
make haste; the bridegroom has come already.
Make haste, I say.

Nurse:
Mistress! What, mistress! Juliet! Asleep, I guarantee her, she is.
Why, lamb! Why, lady! Fie, you slug-a-bed!
Why, love, I say! Madam! Sweet-heart! Why, bride!
What, not a word? You are taking your pennyworths now;
sleep for a week; for the next night, I guarantee,
the County Paris has set up his rest,
that you shall rest but little. God forgive me,
marry, and amen, how soundly she is asleep!
I must needs wake her. Madam, madam, madam!
Yes, let the county take you in your bed;
he'll frighten you up, in faith. Will it not be?
What, dressed! And in your clothes! And down again!
I must needs wake you; Lady! Lady! Lady!
Alas, alas! Help, help! My lady's dead!
Oh, well-a-day, that ever I was born!
Some aqua vitae, ho! My lord! My lady!

LADY CAPULET:
What noise is here?

Nurse:
Oh, lamentable day!

LADY CAPULET:
What is the matter?

Nurse:
Look, look! Oh, heavy day!

LADY CAPULET:
Oh me, oh me! My child, my only life,
revive, look up, or I will die with you!
Help, help! Call for help.

CAPULET:
For shame, bring Juliet forth; her lord has come.

Nurse:
She's dead, deceased, she's dead; alas the day!

LADY CAPULET:
Alas the day, she's dead, she's dead, she's dead!

CAPULET:
Ha! Let me see her. Out, alas! She's cold.
Her blood has settled, and her joints are stiff;
life and these lips have long been separated.
Death lies on her like an untimely frost
upon the sweetest flower of all the field.

Nurse:
Oh, lamentable day!

LADY CAPULET:
Oh, woeful time!

CAPULET:
Death, that has taken her from here to make me wail,
ties up my tongue, and will not let me speak.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Come, is the bride ready to go to church?

CAPULET:
Ready to go, but never to return.
O son! The night before your wedding-day,
Death has lain with your wife. There she lies,
a flower as she was, deflowered by him.
Death is my son-in-law, Death is my heir;
he has wedded my daughter. I will die,
and leave him all; life, living, all is Death's.

PARIS:
Have I thought long to see this morning's face,
and does it give me such a sight as this?

LADY CAPULET:
Accursed, unhappy, wretched, hateful day!
The most miserable hour that time ever saw
in the lasting labor of his pilgrimage!
But one, poor one, one poor and loving child,
but one thing to rejoice and find solace in,
and cruel death has snatched it from my sight!

Nurse:
Oh woe! Oh woeful, woeful, woeful day!
Most lamentable day, most woeful day,
that ever, ever, I did yet behold!
Oh day! Oh day! Oh day! Oh hateful day!
Never was a day as black as this seen.
Oh woeful day, oh woeful day!

PARIS:
Beguiled, divorced, wronged, spited, slain!
Most detestable death, by you beguiled,
by cruel, cruel you quite overthrown!
Oh love! Oh life! Not life, but love in death!

CAPULET:
Despised, distressed, hated, martyred, killed!
Uncomfortable time, why did you come now
to murder, murder our solemnity?
O child! O child! My soul, and not my child!
You are dead! Alas! My child is dead;
and with my child my joys are buried.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Peace, ho, for shame! The cure for confusion does not live
in these confusions. Heaven and yourself
had a part in this fair maid; now heaven has all,
and all the better it is for the maid.
Your part in her you could not keep from death,
but heaven keeps its part in eternal life.
The most you sought was her promotion;
for it was your heaven that she should be advanced.
And do you weep now, seeing that she is advanced
above the clouds, as high as heaven itself?
Oh, in this love, you love your child so ill,
that you run mad, seeing that she is well.
She is not well-married that lives married long;
but she is best married that dies married young.
Dry up your tears, and stick your rosemary
on this fair corpse; and, as the custom is,
in all her best array, bear her to church.
For though fond nature bids us all lament,
yet nature's tears are reason's merriment.

CAPULET:
All the things that we had ordained for the festival,
turn from their office to a black funeral;
our instruments to melancholy bells,
our wedding cheer to a sad burial feast,
our solemn hymns to sullen dirges change,
our bridal flowers serve for a buried corpse,
and all things change them to the contrary.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Sir, you go in; and, madam, go with him;
and go, Sir Paris; everyone prepare
to follow this fair corpse to her grave.
The heavens are lowering upon you for some ill;
do not move them any more by crossing their high will.

First Musician:
Faith, we may put up our pipes, and be gone.

Nurse:
Honest goodfellows, ah, put them up, put them up;
for, as you well know, this is a pitiful case.

First Musician:
Yes, by my troth, the case may be amended.

PETER:
Musicians, oh, musicians, 'Heart's ease, Heart's
ease.' Oh, if you want me to live, play 'Heart's ease.'

First Musician:
Why 'Heart's ease'?

PETER:
Oh, musicians, because my heart itself plays 'My
heart is full of woe.' Oh, play me some merry tune,
to comfort me.

First Musician:
Not a dump, we won't. It's no time to play now.

PETER:
You won't, then?

First Musician:
No.

PETER:
Then I will give it to you soundly.

First Musician:
What will you give us?

PETER:
No money, on my faith, but the jest;
I will give you the minstrel.

First Musician:
Then I will give you the serving-creature.

PETER:
Then I will lay the serving-creature's dagger on
your head. I will not carry any crotchets. I'll re you,
I'll fa you; do you understand me?

First Musician:
If you re us and fa us, you are noting us.

Second Musician:
Please, put up your dagger, and put out your wit.

PETER:
Then have at you with my wit! I will dry-beat you
with an iron wit, and put up my iron dagger. Answer
me like men:
'When griping grief the heart does wound,
and doleful dumps the mind oppress,
then music with her silver sound'—
why 'silver sound'? Why 'music with her silver
sound'? What do you say, Simon Catling?

Musician:
Well, sir, because silver has a sweet sound.

PETER:
Pretty! What do you say, Hugh Rebeck?

Second Musician:
I say 'silver sound,' because musicians sound for silver.

PETER:
Pretty too! What do you say, James Soundpost?

Third Musician:
Faith, I don't know what to say.

PETER:
Oh, I beg your mercy; you are the singer. I will say
for you. It is 'music with her silver sound,'
because musicians have no gold for sounding.
'Then music with her silver sound
with speedy help does lend redress.'

First Musician:
What a pestilent knave this is!

Second Musician:
Hang him, Jack! Come, we'll go in here; wait for the
mourners, and stay for dinner.

ROMEO:
If I may trust the flattering truth of sleep,
my dreams presage some joyful news at hand.
My bosom's lord sits lightly in his throne;
and all this day an unaccustomed spirit
lifts me above the ground with cheerful thoughts.
I dreamt my lady came and found me dead—
a strange dream, that gives a dead man leave
to think!—
and breathed such life with kisses in my lips,
that I revived, and was an emperor.
Ah me! How sweet is love itself when it is possessed,
when but love's shadows are so rich in joy!
News from Verona!—How now, Balthasar!
Do you not bring me letters from the friar?
How is my lady? Is my father well?
How is my Juliet? I ask that again;
for nothing can be ill, if she is well.

BALTHASAR:
Then she is well, and nothing can be ill.
Her body sleeps in Capel's monument,
and her immortal part lives with the angels.
I saw her laid low in her kindred's vault,
and I immediately took post to tell you.
Oh, pardon me for bringing this ill news,
since you did leave it for my office, sir.

ROMEO:
Is it even so? Then I defy you, stars!
You know my lodging. Get me ink and paper,
and hire post-horses; I will go from here tonight.

BALTHASAR:
I beseech you, sir, have patience.
Your looks are pale and wild, and they import
some misadventure.

ROMEO:
Tush, you are deceived.
Leave me, and do the thing I bid you do.
Do you have no letters for me from the friar?

BALTHASAR:
No, my good lord.

ROMEO:
No matter. Get you gone,
and hire those horses; I'll be with you straight.
Well, Juliet, I will lie with you tonight.
Let's see for the means. Oh, mischief, you are swift
to enter the thoughts of desperate men!
I do remember an apothecary—
and he dwells hereabouts—whom I recently noted
in tattered weeds, with overwhelming brows,
culling of simples; his looks were meager,
sharp misery had worn him to the bones.
And in his needy shop a tortoise hung,
a stuffed alligator, and other skins
of ill-shaped fishes; and about his shelves
a beggarly account of empty boxes,
green earthen pots, bladders, and musty seeds,
remnants of packthread and old cakes of roses,
were thinly scattered, to make up a show.
Noting this poverty, to myself I said,
'And if a man did need a poison now,
whose sale is present death in Mantua,
here lives a wretched wretch who would sell it to him.'
Oh, this same thought did but forerun my need;
and this same needy man must sell it to me.
As I remember, this should be the house.
Being a holiday, the beggar's shop is shut.
What, ho! Apothecary!

Apothecary:
Who calls so loud?

ROMEO:
Come here, man. I see that you are poor.
Hold, there are forty ducats. Let me have
a dram of poison, such soon-speeding gear
as will disperse itself through all the veins
so that the life-weary taker may fall dead
and that the trunk may be discharged of breath
as violently as hasty powder, when fired,
does hurry from the fatal cannon's womb.

Apothecary:
I have such mortal drugs; but Mantua's law
is death to any he that utters them.

ROMEO:
Are you so bare and full of wretchedness,
and you fear to die? Famine is in your cheeks,
need and oppression starve in your eyes,
contempt and beggary hang upon your back.
The world is not your friend, nor the world's law;
the world affords no law to make you rich;
then do not be poor, but break it, and take this.

Apothecary:
My poverty, but not my will, consents.

ROMEO:
I pay your poverty, and not your will.

Apothecary:
Put this in any liquid thing you will,
and drink it off; and, if you had the strength
of twenty men, it would dispatch you straight.

ROMEO:
There is your gold, a worse poison to men's souls,
doing more murders in this loathsome world,
than these poor compounds that you may not sell.
I sell you poison; you have sold me none.
Farewell. Buy food, and get yourself some flesh.
Come, cordial and not poison, go with me
to Juliet's grave; for there I must use you.

FRIAR JOHN:
Holy Franciscan friar! Brother, ho!

FRIAR LAURENCE:
This should be the voice of Friar John.
Welcome from Mantua. What does Romeo say?
Or, if his mind is written, give me his letter.

FRIAR JOHN:
Going to find a bare-foot brother out,
one of our order, to associate with me,
here in this city visiting the sick,
and finding him, the searchers of the town,
suspecting that we were both in a house
where the infectious pestilence did reign,
sealed up the doors, and would not let us out;
so that my speed to Mantua was stayed there.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Who carried my letter, then, to Romeo?

FRIAR JOHN:
I could not send it—here it is again—
nor get a messenger to bring it to you,
they were so fearful of infection.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Unhappy fortune! By my brotherhood,
the letter was not trivial but full of charge
of dear importance, and neglecting it
may do much danger. Friar John, go hence;
get me an iron crow, and bring it straight
to my cell.

FRIAR JOHN:
Brother, I'll go and bring it to you.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Now I must go to the monument alone;
within three hours, fair Juliet will wake.
She will scold me much that Romeo
has had no notice of these accidents;
but I will write again to Mantua,
and keep her at my cell until Romeo comes;
a poor living corpse, closed in a dead man's tomb!

PARIS:
Give me your torch, boy. Go, and stand aloof.
Yet put it out, for I would not be seen.
Under those yew-trees, lie all along,
holding your ear close to the hollow ground;
so that no foot shall tread upon the churchyard,
being loose, and unfirm, from the digging up of graves,
but you shall hear it. Whistle then to me,
as a signal that you hear something approach.
Give me those flowers. Do as I bid you, go.

PAGE:

PARIS:
Sweet flower, with flowers I strew your bridal bed—
oh woe! Your canopy is dust and stones;—
which with sweet water I will dew nightly,
or, lacking that, with tears distilled by moans.
The obsequies that I will keep for you
nightly shall be to strew your grave and weep.
The boy gives a warning that something is approaching.
What cursed foot wanders this way tonight,
to cross my obsequies and the rite of true love?
What, with a torch! Muffle me, night, for a while.

ROMEO:
Give me that mattock and the wrenching iron.
Hold, take this letter; early in the morning
see that you deliver it to my lord and father.
Give me the light. Upon your life, I charge you,
whatever you hear or see, stand all aloof,
and do not interrupt me in my course.
Why I descend into this bed of death,
is partly to behold my lady's face;
but chiefly to take from her dead finger
a precious ring, a ring that I must use
in a dear employment. Therefore, hence, be gone.
But if you, being jealous, do return to pry
into what I shall further intend to do,
by heaven, I will tear you joint by joint
and strew this hungry churchyard with your limbs.
The time and my intents are savage-wild,
more fierce and more inexorable by far
than empty tigers or the roaring sea.

BALTHASAR:
I will be gone, sir, and not trouble you.

ROMEO:
So you will show me friendship. Take that.
Live, and be prosperous. And farewell, good fellow.

BALTHASAR:

ROMEO:
You detestable maw, you womb of death,
gorged with the dearest morsel of the earth,
thus I force your rotten jaws to open,
and, in despite, I'll cram you with more food!

PARIS:
This is that banished, haughty Montague,
that murdered my love's cousin, with which grief,
it is supposed, the fair creature died;
and he has come here to do some villainous shame
to the dead bodies. I will apprehend him.
Stop your unhallowed toil, vile Montague!
Can vengeance be pursued further than death?
Condemned villain, I do apprehend you.
Obey, and go with me; for you must die.

ROMEO:
I must indeed; and therefore I came here.
Good gentle youth, do not tempt a desperate man;
flee from here, and leave me. Think upon these who are gone;
let them frighten you. I beseech you, youth,
do not put another sin upon my head,
by urging me to fury. Oh, be gone!
By heaven, I love you better than myself;
for I have come here armed against myself.
Do not stay, be gone; live, and hereafter say,
a madman's mercy bade you run away.

PARIS:
I defy your incantations,
and I apprehend you for a felon here.

ROMEO:
Will you provoke me? Then have at you, boy!

PAGE:
Oh Lord, they are fighting! I will go call the watch.

PARIS:
Oh, I am slain!
If you are merciful,
open the tomb, and lay me with Juliet.

ROMEO:
In faith, I will. Let me peruse this face.
Mercutio's kinsman, noble County Paris!
What did my man say, when my tossed soul
did not attend to him as we rode? I think
he told me Paris was to have married Juliet.
Did he not say so? Or did I dream it so?
Or am I mad, hearing him talk of Juliet,
to think it was so? Oh, give me your hand,
one written with me in sour misfortune's book!
I'll bury you in a triumphant grave;
a grave? Oh no! A lantern, slaughtered youth,
for here lies Juliet, and her beauty makes
this vault a feasting presence full of light.
Death, lie there, interred by a dead man.
How often, when men are at the point of death,
have they been merry! Which their keepers call
a lightning before death. Oh, how may I
call this a lightning? Oh, my love! My wife!
Death, that has sucked the honey of your breath,
has had no power yet upon your beauty.
You are not conquered; beauty's ensign is yet
crimson in your lips and in your cheeks,
and death's pale flag is not advanced there.
Tybalt, are you lying there in your bloody sheet?
Oh, what more favor can I do to you,
than with that hand that cut your youth in two,
to sunder his that was your enemy?
Forgive me, cousin! Ah, dear Juliet,
why are you yet so fair? Shall I believe
that unsubstantial death is amorous,
and that the lean, abhorred monster keeps
you here in the dark to be his paramour?
For fear of that, I will still stay with you;
and never from this palace of dim night
depart again. Here, here will I remain
with the worms that are your chamber-maids. Oh, here
will I set up my everlasting rest,
and shake the yoke of inauspicious stars
from this world-wearied flesh. Eyes, look your last!
Arms, take your last embrace! And, lips, oh you,
the doors of breath, seal with a righteous kiss
a dateless bargain to engrossing death!
Come, bitter conduct, come, unsavory guide!
You desperate pilot, now at once run on
the dashing rocks your sea-sick, weary bark!
Here's to my love!
O true apothecary!
Your drugs are quick. Thus with a kiss I die.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Saint Francis be my speed! How often tonight
have my old feet stumbled at graves! Who's there?

BALTHASAR:
Here's one, a friend, and one that knows you well.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
May bliss be upon you! Tell me, my good friend,
what torch is that, that vainly lends its light
to grubs and eyeless skulls? As I discern,
it is burning in the Capel's monument.

BALTHASAR:
It does so, holy sir; and there is my master,
one that you love.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Who is it?

BALTHASAR:
Romeo.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
How long has he been there?

BALTHASAR:
A full half an hour.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Go with me to the vault.

BALTHASAR:
I dare not, sir.
My master doesn't know that I am gone from here;
and he fearfully threatened me with death,
if I stayed to look on his intents.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Stay, then; I'll go alone. Fear comes upon me.
Oh, I much fear some ill, unlucky thing.

BALTHASAR:
As I slept under this yew-tree here,
I dreamt my master and another fought,
and that my master killed him.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
Romeo!
Alas, alas, what blood is this, which stains
the stony entrance of this sepulchre?
What do these masterless and gory swords mean,
lying discolored by this place of peace?
Romeo! Oh, pale! Who else? What, Paris too?
And steeped in blood? Ah, what an unkind hour
is guilty of this lamentable chance!
The lady stirs.

JULIET:
Oh, comfortable friar! Where is my lord?
I remember well where I should be,
and there I am. Where is my Romeo?

FRIAR LAURENCE:
I hear some noise. Lady, come from that nest
of death, contagion, and unnatural sleep.
A greater power than we can contradict
has thwarted our intents. Come, come away.
Your husband lies dead in your bosom there;
and Paris too. Come, I'll dispose of you
among a sisterhood of holy nuns.
Do not stay to question, for the watch is coming.
Come, go, good Juliet,
I dare no longer stay.

JULIET:
Go, get away from here, for I will not go away.
What's here? A cup, closed in my true love's hand?
Poison, I see, has been his untimely end.
Oh, churl! You drank it all, and left no friendly drop
to help me after? I will kiss your lips;
perhaps some poison yet hangs on them,
to make me die with a restorative.
Your lips are warm.

First Watchman:

JULIET:
Yes, noise? Then I'll be brief. Oh, happy dagger!
This is your sheath;
there, rust, and let me die.

PAGE:
This is the place; there, where the torch is burning.

First Watchman:
The ground is bloody; search around the churchyard.
Go, some of you, and whoever you find, arrest them.
A pitiful sight! Here lies the county slain,
and Juliet bleeding, warm, and newly dead,
who has been buried here these two days.
Go, tell the prince. Run to the Capulets.
Raise up the Montagues. Some others search.
We see the ground where these woes lie;
but the true ground of all these piteous woes
we cannot discover without circumstance.

Second Watchman:
Here's Romeo's man; we found him in the churchyard.

First Watchman:
Hold him in safety, until the prince comes here.

Third Watchman:
Here is a friar, that trembles, sighs, and weeps.
We took this mattock and this spade from him,
as he was coming from this side of the churchyard.

First Watchman:
A great suspicion. Stay the friar too.

PRINCE:
What misadventure is up so early,
that it calls our person from our morning's rest?

CAPULET:
What could it be, that they are shrieking abroad so?

LADY CAPULET:
The people in the street are crying 'Romeo,'
some 'Juliet,' and some 'Paris'; and they all run,
with open outcry toward our monument.

PRINCE:
What fear is this which startles our ears?

First Watchman:
Sovereign, here lies the County Paris slain;
and Romeo dead; and Juliet, dead before,
warm and newly killed.

PRINCE:
Search, seek, and know how this foul murder happened.

First Watchman:
Here is a friar, and the slaughtered Romeo's man;
with instruments upon them, fit to open
these dead men's tombs.

CAPULET:
Oh heavens! Oh wife, look how our daughter is bleeding!
This dagger has been mistaken—for, look, his house
is empty on the back of Montague,—
and it is missheathed in my daughter's bosom!

LADY CAPULET:
Oh me! This sight of death is like a bell,
that warns my old age to a sepulchre.

PRINCE:
Come, Montague; for you are up early,
to see your son and heir down even earlier.

MONTAGUE:
Alas, my liege, my wife is dead tonight;
grief from my son's exile has stopped her breath.
What further woe conspires against my age?

PRINCE:
Look, and you shall see.

MONTAGUE:
Oh, you untaught! What manners is this?
To press before your father to a grave?

PRINCE:
Seal up the mouth of outrage for a while,
until we can clear up these ambiguities,
and know their spring, their head, their
true descent;
and then I will be the general of your woes,
and lead you even to death. In the meantime, forbear,
and let mischance be a slave to patience.
Bring forth the parties of suspicion.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
I am the greatest, yet able to do the least,
and most suspected, as the time and place
make against me in this direful murder.
And here I stand, both to impeach and to purge
myself, condemned and excused.

PRINCE:
Then say at once what you know in this.

FRIAR LAURENCE:
I will be brief, for my short date of breath
is not as long as a tedious tale.
Romeo, there dead, was husband to that Juliet;
and she, there dead, was that Romeo's faithful wife.
I married them; and their stolen marriage-day
was Tybalt's doomsday, whose untimely death
banished the new-made bridegroom from the city,
for whom, and not for Tybalt, Juliet pined.
You, to remove that siege of grief from her,
betrothed and would have married her by force
to County Paris. Then she comes to me,
and, with wild looks, bid me devise some means
to rid her from this second marriage,
or in my cell she would kill herself.
Then I gave her, so tutored by my art,
a sleeping potion; which so took effect
as I intended, for it wrought on her
the form of death. In the meantime, I wrote to Romeo,
that he should come here on this dire night,
to help to take her from her borrowed grave,
being the time the potion's force should cease.
But he who bore my letter, Friar John,
was stayed by accident, and last night
returned my letter back. Then all alone,
at the prefixed hour of her waking,
I came to take her from her kindred's vault;
meaning to keep her closely at my cell,
until I could conveniently send to Romeo.
But when I came, some minute before the time
of her awaking, here untimely lay
the noble Paris and true Romeo dead.
She wakes; and I entreated her to come forth,
and bear this work of heaven with patience.
But then a noise did scare me from the tomb;
and she, too desperate, would not go with me,
but, as it seems, did violence on herself.
All this I know; and to the marriage
her nurse is privy. And, if anything in this
miscarried by my fault, let my old life
be sacrificed, some hour before its time,
unto the rigor of the severest law.

PRINCE:
We have always known you to be a holy man.
Where's Romeo's man? What can he say in this?

BALTHASAR:
I brought my master news of Juliet's death;
and then in post he came from Mantua
to this same place, to this same monument.
This letter he early bid me give his father,
and threatened me with death, going in the vault,
if I did not depart and leave him there.

PRINCE:
Give me the letter; I will look at it.
Where is the county's page, that raised the watch?
Sirrah, what was your master doing in this place?

PAGE:
He came with flowers to strew his lady's grave;
and he told me to stand aloof, and so I did.
Soon, someone came with a light to open the tomb;
and by and by my master drew on him;
and then I ran away to call the watch.

PRINCE:
This letter makes good the friar's words,
their course of love, the news of her death.
And here he writes that he did buy a poison
from a poor apothecary, and with it,
he came to this vault to die, and lie with Juliet.
Where are these enemies? Capulet! Montague!
See, what a scourge is laid upon your hate,
that heaven finds means to kill your joys with love.
And I, for winking at your discords too,
have lost a pair of kinsmen. All are punished.

CAPULET:
Oh, brother Montague, give me your hand.
This is my daughter's dowry, for no more
can I demand.

MONTAGUE:
But I can give you more,
for I will raise her statue in pure gold;
so that while Verona is known by that name,
no figure shall be set at such a rate
as that of true and faithful Juliet.

CAPULET:
Romeo's shall lie as rich by his lady's;
poor sacrifices of our enmity!

PRINCE:
A glooming peace this morning brings with it;
the sun, for sorrow, will not show his head.
Go from here, to have more talk of these sad things;
some shall be pardoned, and some shall be punished.
For never was a story of more woe
than this of Juliet and her Romeo.

WARWICK:
I wonder how the king escaped our hands.

YORK:
While we were pursuing the horsemen of the north,
he slyly stole away and left his men.
At which, the great Lord of Northumberland,
whose warlike ears could never brook a retreat,
cheered up the drooping army; and he himself,
Lord Clifford and Lord Stafford, all abreast,
charged our main battle's front, and breaking in,
were slain by the swords of common soldiers.

EDWARD:
Lord Stafford's father, the Duke of Buckingham,
is either slain or wounded dangerously;
I cleft his helmet with a downright blow.
That this is true, father, behold his blood.

MONTAGUE:
And, brother, here's the Earl of Wiltshire's blood,
whom I encountered as the battles joined.

RICHARD:
You speak for me and tell them what I did.

YORK:
Richard has deserved the best of all my sons.
But are you dead, my Lord of Somerset?

NORFOLK:
Such hope have all the line of John of Gaunt!

RICHARD:
Thus I hope to shake King Henry's head.

WARWICK:
And so do I. Victorious Prince of York,
before I see you seated on that throne
which the house of Lancaster now usurps,
I vow by heaven these eyes shall never close.
This is the palace of the fearful king,
and this is the regal seat. Possess it, York;
for this is yours and not King Henry's heirs'.

YORK:
Assist me, then, sweet Warwick, and I will;
for we have broken in here by force.

NORFOLK:
We'll all assist you; he that flees shall die.

YORK:
Thanks, gentle Norfolk. Stay by me, my lords;
and, soldiers, stay and lodge by me this night.

WARWICK:
And when the king comes, offer no violence,
unless he seeks to thrust you out by force.

YORK:
The queen holds her parliament here today,
but she little thinks we shall be of her council.
By words or blows, let us win our right here.

RICHARD:
Armed as we are, let's stay within this house.

WARWICK:
This shall be called the bloody parliament,
unless Plantagenet, Duke of York, is king,
and bashful Henry is deposed, whose cowardice
has made us bywords to our enemies.

YORK:
Then do not leave me, my lords; be resolute.
I mean to take possession of my right.

WARWICK:
Neither the king, nor he that loves him best,
the proudest he that holds up Lancaster,
dares stir a wing, if Warwick shakes his bells.
I'll plant Plantagenet, and whoever dares, may root him up.
Resolve yourself, Richard; claim the English crown.

KING HENRY VI:
My lords, look where the sturdy rebel sits,
even in the chair of state. It seems he means,
backed by the power of Warwick, that false peer,
to aspire to the crown and reign as king.
Earl of Northumberland, he killed your father.
And yours, Lord Clifford; and you both have vowed revenge
on him, his sons, his favorites, and his friends.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
If I am not, may the heavens be revenged on me!

CLIFFORD:
The hope of it makes Clifford mourn in steel.

WESTMORELAND:
What, shall we suffer this? Let's pluck him down.
My heart burns for anger; I cannot bear it.

KING HENRY VI:
Be patient, gentle Earl of Westmoreland.

CLIFFORD:
Patience is for cowards, such as he.
He would not dare to sit there, if your father were alive.
My gracious lord, here in the parliament,
let us assail the family of York.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
You have spoken well, cousin. Let it be so.

KING HENRY VI:
Ah, don't you know the city favors them,
and they have troops of soldiers at their command?

EXETER:
But when the duke is slain, they'll quickly fly.

KING HENRY VI:
Far be the thought of this from Henry's heart,
to make a shambles of the parliament-house!
Cousin of Exeter, frowns, words, and threats
shall be the war that Henry means to use.
You factious Duke of York, descend from my throne,
and kneel for grace and mercy at my feet;
I am your sovereign.

YORK:
I am yours.

EXETER:
For shame, come down. He made you Duke of York.

YORK:
It was my inheritance, as the earldom was.

EXETER:
Your father was a traitor to the crown.

WARWICK:
Exeter, you are a traitor to the crown
in following this usurping Henry.

CLIFFORD:
Whom should he follow but his natural king?

WARWICK:
True, Clifford; and that's Richard, Duke of York.

KING HENRY VI:
And shall I stand, while you sit on my throne?

YORK:
It must and shall be so. Content yourself.

WARWICK:
Be Duke of Lancaster; let him be king.

WESTMORELAND:
He is both king and Duke of Lancaster;
and that the Lord of Westmoreland shall maintain.

WARWICK:
And Warwick shall disprove it. You forget
that we are the ones who chased you from the field
and killed your fathers, and with our colors spread,
we marched through the city to the palace gates.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Yes, Warwick, I remember it to my grief;
and, by his soul, you and your house shall regret it.

WESTMORELAND:
Plantagenet, of you and these your sons,
your kinsman and your friends, I'll have more lives
than there were drops of blood in my father's veins.

CLIFFORD:
Urge it no more; lest that, instead of words,
I send you, Warwick, such a messenger
as shall revenge his death before I stir.

WARWICK:
Poor Clifford! How I scorn his worthless threats!

YORK:
Will you let us show our title to the crown?
If not, our swords shall plead it in the field.

KING HENRY VI:
What title do you have, traitor, to the crown?
Your father was, as you are, the Duke of York;
your grandfather, Roger Mortimer, was the Earl of March.
I am the son of Henry the Fifth,
who made the Dauphin and the French to stoop
and seized upon their towns and provinces.

WARWICK:
Don't talk of France, since you have lost it all.

KING HENRY VI:
The lord protector lost it, and not I.
When I was crowned, I was but nine months old.

RICHARD:
You are old enough now, and yet, I think, you are losing.
Father, tear the crown from the usurper's head.

EDWARD:
Sweet father, do so; set it on your head.

MONTAGUE:
Good brother, as you love and honor arms,
let's fight it out and not stand here arguing.

RICHARD:
Sound the drums and trumpets, and the king will flee.

YORK:
Sons, peace!

KING HENRY VI:
Peace, you! And give King Henry leave to speak.

WARWICK:
Plantagenet shall speak first. Hear him, lords;
and you be silent and attentive too,
for he that interrupts him shall not live.

KING HENRY VI:
Do you think I will leave my kingly throne,
where my grandsire and my father sat?
No. First, war shall unpeople this my realm;
yes, and their colors, often borne in France,
and now in England to our heart's great sorrow,
shall be my winding-sheet. Why do you faint, lords?
My title is good, and far better than his.

WARWICK:
Prove it, Henry, and you shall be king.

KING HENRY VI:
Henry the Fourth got the crown by conquest.

YORK:
It was by rebellion against his king.

KING HENRY VI:

YORK:
What then?

KING HENRY VI:
If he may, then I am the lawful king;
for Richard, in the view of many lords,
resigned the crown to Henry the Fourth,
whose heir my father was, and I am his.

YORK:
He rose against him, being his sovereign,
and made him resign his crown by force.

WARWICK:
Suppose, my lords, he did it unconstrained.
Do you think it would be prejudicial to his crown?

EXETER:
No; for he could not so resign his crown
but that the next heir should succeed and reign.

KING HENRY VI:
Are you against us, Duke of Exeter?

EXETER:
His is the right, and therefore pardon me.

YORK:
Why are you whispering, my lords, and not answering?

EXETER:
My conscience tells me he is the lawful king.

KING HENRY VI:

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Plantagenet, for all the claim you lay,
do not think that Henry will be so deposed.

WARWICK:
Deposed he shall be, in spite of all.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
You are deceived. It is not your southern power,
of Essex, Norfolk, Suffolk, nor of Kent,
which makes you thus presumptuous and proud,
that can set the duke up in spite of me.

CLIFFORD:
King Henry, whether your title is right or wrong,
Lord Clifford vows to fight in your defense.
May that ground gape open and swallow me alive,
where I shall kneel to him that killed my father!

KING HENRY VI:
Oh, Clifford, how your words revive my heart!

YORK:
Henry of Lancaster, resign your crown.
What are you muttering, or what are you conspiring, lords?

WARWICK:
Do right to this princely Duke of York,
or I will fill the house with armed men,
and over the chair of state, where he now sits,
I will write up his title with usurping blood.

KING HENRY VI:
My Lord of Warwick, hear me but one word.
Let me reign as king for this lifetime of mine.

YORK:
Confirm the crown to me and to my heirs,
and you shall reign in quiet while you live.

KING HENRY VI:
I am content. Richard Plantagenet,
enjoy the kingdom after my death.

CLIFFORD:
What wrong is this to the prince your son!

WARWICK:
What good is this to England and to himself!

WESTMORELAND:
Base, fearful, and despairing Henry!

CLIFFORD:
How have you injured both yourself and us!

WESTMORELAND:
I cannot stay to hear these articles.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Neither can I.

CLIFFORD:
Come, cousin, let us tell the queen this news.

WESTMORELAND:
Farewell, faint-hearted and degenerate king,
in whose cold blood no spark of honor resides.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
May you be a prey to the house of York,
and die in chains for this unmanly deed!

CLIFFORD:
In dreadful war, may you be overcome,
or live in peace, abandoned and despised!

WARWICK:
Turn this way, Henry, and don't pay attention to them.

EXETER:
They seek revenge and therefore will not yield.

KING HENRY VI:
Ah, Exeter!

WARWICK:
Why should you sigh, my lord?

KING HENRY VI:
Not for myself, Lord Warwick, but for my son,
whom I shall unnaturally disinherit.
But be it as it may. I hereby entail
the crown to you and to your heirs forever;
on the condition that you take an oath here
to cease this civil war, and, while I live,
to honor me as your king and sovereign,
and neither by treason nor hostility
to seek to put me down and reign yourself.

YORK:
This oath I willingly take and will perform.

WARWICK:
Long live King Henry! Plantagenet, embrace him.

KING HENRY VI:
And long may you and these your forward sons live!

YORK:
Now York and Lancaster are reconciled.

EXETER:
Accursed be he that seeks to make them foes!

YORK:
Farewell, my gracious lord; I'll go to my castle.

WARWICK:
And I'll keep London with my soldiers.

NORFOLK:
And I to Norfolk with my followers.

MONTAGUE:
And I to the sea from where I came.

KING HENRY VI:
And I, with grief and sorrow, to the court.

EXETER:
Here comes the queen, whose looks betray her anger.
I'll steal away.

KING HENRY VI:
Exeter, so will I.

QUEEN MARGARET:
No, don't go from me; I will follow you.

KING HENRY VI:
Be patient, gentle queen, and I will stay.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Who can be patient in such extremes?
Ah, wretched man! I wish I had died a maid
and never seen you, never borne you a son,
seeing you have proved to be so unnatural a father.
Has he deserved to lose his birthright thus?
If you had but loved him half as well as I,
or felt the pain that I once felt for him,
or nourished him as I did with my blood,
you would have left your dearest heart-blood there,
rather than have that savage duke as your heir
and disinherit your only son.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Father, you cannot disinherit me.
If you are king, why should not I succeed?

KING HENRY VI:
Pardon me, Margaret; pardon me, sweet son.
The Earl of Warwick and the duke forced me.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Forced you! Are you a king, and will you be forced?
I am ashamed to hear you speak. Ah, timorous wretch!
You have undone yourself, your son, and me;
and you have given the house of York such a head start
that you shall reign but by their sufferance.
To entail him and his heirs to the crown,
what is it, but to make your sepulchre
and creep into it far before your time?
Warwick is chancellor and the lord of Calais;
Stern Falconbridge commands the narrow seas;
the duke is made protector of the realm;
and yet shall you be safe? Such safety is found
by the trembling lamb surrounded by wolves.
If I had been there, I who am a silly woman,
the soldiers would have tossed me on their pikes
before I would have granted that act.
But you prefer your life before your honor.
And seeing that you do, I hereby divorce myself
both from your table, Henry, and your bed,
until that act of parliament is repealed
whereby my son is disinherited.
The northern lords that have forsworn your colors
will follow mine, if once they see them spread;
and spread they shall be, to your foul disgrace
and the utter ruin of the house of York.
Thus I leave you. Come, son, let's go;
our army is ready; come, we'll go after them.

KING HENRY VI:
Stay, gentle Margaret, and hear me speak.

QUEEN MARGARET:
You have spoken too much already. Get you gone.

KING HENRY VI:
Gentle son Edward, will you stay with me?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, to be murdered by his enemies.

PRINCE EDWARD:
When I return with victory from the field,
I'll see your grace. Until then, I'll follow her.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Come, son, away; we may not linger thus.

KING HENRY VI:
Poor queen! How love for me and for her son
has made her break out into terms of rage!
May she be revenged on that hateful duke,
whose haughty spirit, winged with desire,
will cost me my crown, and like an empty eagle
will tire of the flesh of me and of my son!
The loss of those three lords torments my heart.
I'll write to them and entreat them fairly.
Come, cousin, you shall be the messenger.

EXETER:
And I, I hope, shall reconcile them all.
3 KING HENRY VI

RICHARD:
Brother, though I am the youngest, give me leave.

EDWARD:
No, I can better play the orator.

MONTAGUE:
But I have strong and forcible reasons.

YORK:
Why, what's this, sons and brother! At a strife?
What is your quarrel? How did it begin?

EDWARD:
No quarrel, but a slight contention.

YORK:
About what?

RICHARD:
About what concerns your grace and us;
the crown of England, father, which is yours.

YORK:
Mine, boy? Not until King Henry is dead.

RICHARD:
Your right does not depend on his life or death.

EDWARD:
Now you are the heir, so enjoy it now.
By giving the house of Lancaster leave to breathe,
it will outrun you, father, in the end.

YORK:
I took an oath that he should reign quietly.

EDWARD:
But for a kingdom, any oath may be broken.
I would break a thousand oaths to reign for one year.

RICHARD:
No; God forbid your grace should be forsworn.

YORK:
I shall be, if I claim by open war.

RICHARD:
I'll prove the contrary, if you'll hear me speak.

YORK:
You cannot, son; it is impossible.

RICHARD:
An oath is of no moment, if it is not taken
before a true and lawful magistrate,
that has authority over him that swears.
Henry had none, but did usurp the place;
then, seeing it was he that made you depose,
your oath, my lord, is vain and frivolous.
Therefore, to arms! And, father, just think
how sweet a thing it is to wear a crown;
within whose circuit is Elysium
and all that poets feign of bliss and joy.
Why do we linger thus? I cannot rest
until the white rose that I wear is dyed
even in the lukewarm blood of Henry's heart.

YORK:
Richard, enough; I will be king, or die.
Brother, you shall go to London presently,
and urge Warwick to this enterprise.
You, Richard, shall go to the Duke of Norfolk,
and tell him privately of our intent.
You, Edward, shall go to my Lord Cobham,
with whom the Kentishmen will willingly rise.
In them I trust; for they are soldiers,
witty, courteous, liberal, and full of spirit.
While you are thus employed, what more remains,
but that I seek an occasion to rise,
and yet the king is not privy to my drift,
nor any of the house of Lancaster?
But, stay! What news? Why have you come in such a hurry?

Messenger:
The queen, with all the northern earls and lords,
intends to besiege you here in your castle.
She is close by with twenty thousand men;
and therefore, fortify your hold, my lord.

YORK:
Yes, with my sword. What! Do you think we fear them?
Edward and Richard, you shall stay with me;
my brother Montague shall post to London.
Let noble Warwick, Cobham, and the rest,
whom we have left as protectors of the king,
with powerful policy, strengthen themselves,
and do not trust simple Henry or his oaths.

MONTAGUE:
Brother, I go; I'll win them over, don't fear it.
And thus, most humbly, I take my leave.
Sir John and Sir Hugh Mortimer, my uncles,
you have come to Sandal at a happy hour;
the army of the queen means to besiege us.

JOHN MORTIMER:
She will not need to; we'll meet her in the field.

YORK:
What, with five thousand men?

RICHARD:
Yes, with five hundred, father, if need be.
A woman's general; what should we fear?

EDWARD:
I hear their drums. Let's set our men in order,
and go out and challenge them to battle straight away.

YORK:
Five men to twenty! Though the odds are great,
I do not doubt, uncle, of our victory.
I have won many a battle in France,
when the enemy has been ten to one.
Why should I not now have the same success?
3 KING HENRY VI

RUTLAND:
Ah, where shall I flee to escape their hands?
Ah, tutor, look where bloody Clifford comes!

CLIFFORD:
Chaplain, away! Your priesthood saves your life.
As for the brat of this accursed duke,
whose father killed my father, he shall die.

Tutor:
And I, my lord, will bear him company.

CLIFFORD:
Soldiers, away with him!

Tutor:
Ah, Clifford, do not murder this innocent child,
lest you be hated by both God and man!

CLIFFORD:
What now! Is he dead already? Or is it fear
that makes him close his eyes? I'll open them.

RUTLAND:
So the pent-up lion looks over the wretch
that trembles under his devouring paws;
and so he walks, insulting over his prey,
and so he comes, to rend his limbs asunder.
Ah, gentle Clifford, kill me with your sword,
and not with such a cruel, threatening look.
Sweet Clifford, hear me speak before I die.
I am too mean a subject for your wrath.
Be revenged on men, and let me live.

CLIFFORD:
You speak in vain, poor boy; my father's blood
has stopped the passage where your words should enter.

RUTLAND:
Then let my father's blood open it again.
He is a man, and, Clifford, you should cope with him.

CLIFFORD:
If your brothers were here, their lives and yours
would not be sufficient revenge for me;
no, if I dug up your forefathers' graves
and hung their rotten coffins up in chains,
it could not slake my anger, nor ease my heart.
The sight of anyone from the house of York
is like a fury to torment my soul;
and until I root out their accursed line
and leave not one alive, I live in hell.
Therefore—

RUTLAND:
Oh, let me pray before I take my death!
To you I pray; sweet Clifford, pity me!

CLIFFORD:
Such pity as my rapier's point affords.

RUTLAND:
I never did you harm. Why will you kill me?

CLIFFORD:
Your father has.

RUTLAND:
But that was before I was born.
You have one son; for his sake pity me,
lest in revenge, since God is just,
he be as miserably slain as I.
Ah, let me live in prison all my days;
and when I give occasion of offense,
then let me die, for now you have no cause.

CLIFFORD:
No cause!
Your father killed my father; therefore, die.

RUTLAND:
May the gods grant that this be the height of your praise!

CLIFFORD:
Plantagenet! I come, Plantagenet!
And this, your son's blood, cleaving to my blade,
shall rust upon my weapon, until your blood,
congealed with this, makes me wipe off both.
3 KING HENRY VI

YORK:
The queen's army has taken the field.
My uncles were both slain while rescuing me;
and all my followers, before the eager foe,
turn back and flee, like ships before the wind
or lambs pursued by hunger-starved wolves.
My sons, God knows what has happened to them.
But this I know, they have behaved themselves
like men born to renown, by life or death.
Three times Richard made a lane for me,
and three times he cried, 'Courage, father! Fight it out!'
And just as often, Edward came to my side,
with a purple sword, painted to the hilt
in the blood of those who had encountered him.
And when the hardiest warriors did retire,
Richard cried, 'Charge! And give no foot of ground!'
And he cried, 'A crown, or else a glorious tomb!
A scepter, or an earthly sepulchre!'
With this, we charged again. But, alas!
We failed again; as I have seen a swan
with fruitless labor swim against the tide
and spend her strength with over-matching waves.
Ah, listen! The fatal followers are pursuing;
and I am faint and cannot flee their fury.
And if I were strong, I would not shun their fury.
The sands that make up my life are numbered;
here I must stay, and here my life must end.
Come, bloody Clifford, rough Northumberland,
I dare your unquenchable fury to more rage.
I am your target, and I will abide your shot.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Yield to our mercy, proud Plantagenet.

CLIFFORD:
Yes, to such mercy as his ruthless arm,
with downright payment, showed to my father.
Now Phaethon has tumbled from his chariot,
and made an evening at the noontide prick.

YORK:
My ashes, like the phoenix, may bring forth
a bird that will take revenge upon you all.
And in that hope, I throw my eyes to heaven,
scorning whatever you can afflict me with.
Why don't you come? What! Multitudes, and you are afraid?

CLIFFORD:
So cowards fight when they can flee no further;
so doves peck at the falcon's piercing talons;
so desperate thieves, all hopeless of their lives,
breathe out invectives against the officers.

YORK:
Oh, Clifford, just think for a moment,
and in your thoughts, go over my former time;
and, if you can without blushing, look at this face,
and bite your tongue, that slanders him with cowardice,
whose frown has made you faint and flee before this!

CLIFFORD:
I will not bandy words with you word for word,
but I will buckle with you with blows, twice two for one.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Hold, valiant Clifford! For a thousand reasons,
I would prolong the traitor's life for a while.
Wrath makes him deaf. Speak, Northumberland.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Hold, Clifford! Don't honor him so much
as to prick your finger, even to wound his heart.
What valor would it be, when a dog grins,
for one to thrust his hand between its teeth,
when he could kick it away with his foot?
It is a prize of war to take all advantages;
and ten to one is no impeachment of valor.

CLIFFORD:
Yes, yes, so the woodcock strives with the gin.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
So does the rabbit struggle in the net.

YORK:
So thieves triumph over their conquered booty;
so true men yield, with robbers so overmatched.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
What would your grace have done to him now?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Brave warriors, Clifford and Northumberland,
come, make him stand upon this molehill here,
that reached at mountains with outstretched arms,
yet parted but the shadow with his hand.
What! Was it you that would be England's king?
Was it you that reveled in our parliament,
and made a sermon of your high descent?
Where are your mess of sons to back you now?
The wanton Edward, and the lusty George?
And where's that valiant, crook-backed prodigy,
Dicky your boy, that with his grumbling voice
was wont to cheer his dad in mutinies?
Or, with the rest, where is your darling Rutland?
Look, York. I stained this napkin with the blood
that valiant Clifford, with his rapier's point,
made issue from the bosom of the boy.
And if your eyes can water for his death,
I give you this to dry your cheeks with.
Alas, poor York! But that I hate you deadly,
I should lament your miserable state.
I pray you, grieve, to make me merry, York.
What, has your fiery heart so parched your entrails
that not a tear can fall for Rutland's death?
Why are you patient, man? You should be mad;
and I, to make you mad, do mock you thus.
Stamp, rave, and fret, so that I may sing and dance.
You would be paid, I see, to make me sport.
York cannot speak, unless he wears a crown.
A crown for York! And, lords, bow low to him.
Hold his hands, while I do set it on.
Yes, indeed, sir, now he looks like a king!
Yes, this is he that took King Henry's chair,
and this is he who was his adopted heir.
But how is it that great Plantagenet
is crowned so soon, and has broken his solemn oath?
As I recall, you were not to be king
until our King Henry had shaken hands with death.
And will you pale your head in Henry's glory,
and rob his temples of the diadem,
now in his life, against your holy oath?
Oh, it's a fault too, too unpardonable!
Off with the crown, and with the crown, his head;
and, while we breathe, take time to kill him.

CLIFFORD:
That is my office, for my father's sake.

QUEEN MARGARET:
No, stay; let's hear the prayers he makes.

YORK:
She-wolf of France, but worse than the wolves of France,
whose tongue is more poisonous than an adder's tooth!
How ill-fitting it is in your sex
to triumph, like an Amazonian tramp,
upon the woes of those whom fortune captivates!
But that your face is, like a mask, unchanging,
made impudent with the use of evil deeds,
I would try, proud queen, to make you blush.
To tell you where you came from, of whom you are derived,
would be shame enough to shame you, if you were not shameless.
Your father bears the title of King of Naples,
of both the Sicilies and Jerusalem,
yet he is not as wealthy as an English yeoman.
Has that poor monarch taught you to insult?
It is not necessary, nor does it benefit you, proud queen,
unless the adage must be verified,
that beggars on horseback ride their horses to death.
It is beauty that often makes women proud;
but, God knows, your share of it is small.
It is virtue that makes them most admired;
the contrary makes you wondered at.
It is government that makes them seem divine;
the lack of it makes you abominable.
You are as opposite to every good thing
as the Antipodes are to us,
or as the south is to the north.
Oh, a tiger's heart wrapped in a woman's hide!
How could you drain the life-blood of the child,
to bid the father wipe his eyes withal,
and yet be seen to bear a woman's face?
Women are soft, mild, pitiful, and flexible;
you are stern, obdurate, flinty, rough, and remorseless.
Do you bid me to rage? Why, now you have your wish.
Would you have me weep? Why, now you have your will.
For a raging wind blows up incessant showers,
and when the rage allays, the rain begins.
These tears are my sweet Rutland's obsequies,
and every drop cries for vengeance for his death,
against you, fell Clifford, and you, false
Frenchwoman.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
I swear, his passion moves me so
that I can hardly keep my eyes from tears.

YORK:
That face of his, the hungry cannibals
would not have touched, would not have stained with blood.
But you are more inhuman, more inexorable,
oh, ten times more, than the tigers of Hyrcania.
See, ruthless queen, a hapless father's tears.
This cloth you dipped in the blood of my sweet boy,
and I with tears do wash the blood away.
Keep the napkin, and go and boast of this.
And if you tell the heavy story right,
upon my soul, the hearers will shed tears;
yes, even my foes will shed fast-falling tears,
and say, 'Alas, it was a piteous deed!'
There, take the crown, and with the crown, my curse;
and in your need, may such comfort come to you
as I now reap at your too cruel hand!
Hard-hearted Clifford, take me from the world.
My soul to heaven, my blood upon your heads!

NORTHUMBERLAND:
If he had been the slaughter-man of all my kin,
I could not, for my life, but weep with him,
to see how deeply sorrow grips his soul.

QUEEN MARGARET:
What, are you ripe for weeping, my Lord Northumberland?
Just think about the wrong he did us all,
and that will quickly dry your melting tears.

CLIFFORD:
Here's for my oath, here's for my father's death.

QUEEN MARGARET:
And here's to right our gentle-hearted king.

YORK:
Open Your gate of mercy, gracious God!
My soul flies through these wounds to seek You out.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Off with his head, and set it on the gates of York;
so York may overlook the town of York.
3 KING HENRY VI

EDWARD:
I wonder how our princely father escaped,
or whether he escaped away or not
from Clifford's and Northumberland's pursuit.
If he had been taken, we would have heard the news;
if he had been slain, we would have heard the news;
or if he had escaped, I think we would have heard
the happy news of his good escape.
How is my brother? Why is he so sad?

RICHARD:
I cannot be joyful, until I am resolved
where our right valiant father has gone.
I saw him in the battle, ranging about;
and I watched him as he singled out Clifford.
I thought he bore himself in the thickest troop
as a lion does in a herd of cattle;
or as a bear, surrounded by dogs,
who, having pinched a few and made them cry,
the rest stand all aloof, and bark at him.
So it was with our father and his enemies;
so his enemies fled from my warlike father.
I think it is prize enough to be his son.
See how the morning opens her golden gates,
and takes her farewell of the glorious sun!
How well it resembles the prime of youth,
trimmed like a young man prancing to his love!

EDWARD:
Are my eyes dazzling me, or do I see three suns?

RICHARD:
Three glorious suns, each one a perfect sun;
not separated by the racking clouds,
but severed in a pale, clear-shining sky.
See, see! They join, embrace, and seem to kiss,
as if they were vowing some inviolable league.
Now they are but one lamp, one light, one sun.
In this, the heaven figures some event.

EDWARD:
It's wondrous strange, the like of which has never been heard of.
I think it summons us, brother, to the field,
that we, the sons of the brave Plantagenet,
each one already blazing by our merits,
should nevertheless join our lights together
and outshine the earth as this does the world.
Whatever it bodes, from now on I will bear
upon my shield three fair-shining suns.

RICHARD:
No, bear three daughters. With your leave I say it,
you love the breeder better than the male.
But what are you, whose heavy looks foretell
some dreadful story hanging on your tongue?

Messenger:
Ah, one that was a woeful onlooker
when the noble Duke of York was slain,
your princely father and my loving lord!

EDWARD:
Oh, speak no more, for I have heard too much.

RICHARD:
Tell me how he died, for I will hear it all.

Messenger:
He was surrounded by many foes,
and he stood against them, as the hope of Troy
stood against the Greeks that would have entered Troy.
But Hercules himself must yield to odds;
and many strokes, though with a little axe,
hew down and fell the hardest-timbered oak.
By many hands your father was subdued;
but only slaughtered by the ireful arm
of unrelenting Clifford and the queen,
who crowned the gracious duke in high despite,
laughed in his face; and when he wept with grief,
the ruthless queen gave him a napkin steeped in the harmless blood
of sweet young Rutland, slain by the rough Clifford,
to dry his cheeks with.
And after many scorns, many foul taunts,
they took his head, and on the gates of York
they set it; and there it remains,
the saddest spectacle that I ever saw.

EDWARD:
Sweet Duke of York, our prop to lean upon,
now that you are gone, we have no staff, no stay.
Oh Clifford, boisterous Clifford! You have slain
the flower of Europe for his chivalry;
and treacherously you have vanquished him,
for hand to hand he would have vanquished you.
Now my soul's palace has become a prison.
Ah, would she break from here, so that this body of mine
might be closed up in rest in the ground!
For from now on, I shall never have joy again,
never, oh never shall I see more joy!

RICHARD:
I cannot weep; for all my body's moisture
scarce serves to quench my furnace-burning heart.
Nor can my tongue unload my heart's great burden;
for the very same wind that I should speak with
is kindling coals that fire all my breast,
and burns me up with flames that tears would quench.
To weep is to make the depth of grief less.
Tears are for babies then; blows and revenge are for me.
Richard, I bear your name; I'll avenge your death,
or die renowned by attempting it.

EDWARD:
His name, that valiant duke, has been left with you;
his dukedom and his chair are left with me.

RICHARD:
No, if you are that princely eagle's bird,
show your descent by gazing against the sun.
For chair and dukedom, throne and kingdom, say;
either that is yours, or else you were not his.

WARWICK:
How now, fair lords! What fare? What news abroad?

RICHARD:
Great Lord of Warwick, if we were to recount
our sorrowful news, and at each word's deliverance
stab daggers in our flesh until all were told,
the words would add more anguish than the wounds.
Oh, valiant lord, the Duke of York is slain!

EDWARD:
Oh Warwick, Warwick! That Plantagenet,
who held you three as dearly as his soul's redemption,
has been put to death by the stern Lord Clifford.

WARWICK:
Ten days ago, I drowned this news in tears;
and now, to add more measure to your woes,
I come to tell you of things that have befallen since then.
After the bloody fray fought at Wakefield,
where your brave father breathed his last gasp,
tidings, as swiftly as the posts could run,
were brought to me of your loss and his departure.
I, then in London, keeper of the king,
mustered my soldiers, gathered flocks of friends,
and very well appointed, as I thought,
marched toward Saint Alban's to intercept the queen,
bearing the king along with me on my behalf;
for by my scouts I was advertised
that she was coming with a full intent
to dash our late decree in parliament
concerning King Henry's oath and your succession.
To make a long story short, we met at Saint Alban's.
Our armies joined, and both sides fought fiercely.
But whether it was the coldness of the king,
who looked very gently on his warlike queen,
that robbed my soldiers of their heated spleen;
or whether it was the report of her success;
or more than the common fear of Clifford's rigor,
who thunders to his captives with blood and death,
I cannot judge. But to conclude with the truth,
their weapons, like lightning, came and went;
our soldiers', like the night-owl's lazy flight,
or like an idle thresher with a flail,
fell gently down, as if they were striking their friends.
I cheered them up with the justice of our cause,
with the promise of high pay and great rewards.
But all in vain; they had no heart to fight,
and we in them had no hope of winning the day;
so that we fled; the king to the queen;
Lord George your brother, Norfolk, and myself,
in haste, post-haste, have come to join with you.
For in the marches here we heard you were,
making another head to fight again.

EDWARD:
Where is the Duke of Norfolk, gentle Warwick?
And when did George come from Burgundy to England?

WARWICK:
Some six miles off, the duke is with the soldiers;
and as for your brother, he was recently sent
from your kind aunt, the Duchess of Burgundy,
with aid of soldiers for this needful war.

RICHARD:
It was against the odds, it seems, when valiant Warwick fled.
I have often heard his praises in pursuit,
but never until now his scandal of retreat.

WARWICK:
Nor now my scandal, Richard, do you hear;
for you shall know this strong right hand of mine
can pluck the diadem from faint Henry's head,
and wring the awful scepter from his fist,
if he were as famous and as bold in war
as he is famed for mildness, peace, and prayer.

RICHARD:
I know it well, Lord Warwick; do not blame me.
It is the love I bear your glories that makes me speak.
But in this troublesome time, what is to be done?
Shall we go and throw away our coats of steel,
and wrap our bodies in black mourning gowns,
numbering our Ave-Marias with our beads?
Or shall we on the helmets of our foes
tell our devotion with revengeful arms?
If for the last, say 'yes,' and to it, lords.

WARWICK:
Why, for that reason Warwick came to seek you out;
and for that reason my brother Montague comes.
Attend me, lords. The proud, insulting queen,
with Clifford and the haughty Northumberland,
and many more proud birds of their feather,
have wrought the easy-melting king like wax.
He swore consent to your succession,
his oath enrolled in the parliament;
and now the whole crew has gone to London,
to frustrate both his oath and whatever else
may work against the house of Lancaster.
Their power, I think, is thirty thousand strong.
Now, if the help of Norfolk and myself,
with all the friends that you, brave Earl of March,
can procure amongst the loving Welshmen,
will but amount to twenty-five thousand,
why, then! We will march to London with all speed,
and once again bestride our foaming steeds,
and once again cry, 'Charge upon our foes!'
But never once again turn back and fly.

RICHARD:
Yes, now I think I hear great Warwick speak.
May he never live to see a sunshine day,
that cries 'Retire,' if Warwick bids him stay.

EDWARD:
Lord Warwick, I will lean on your shoulder;
and when you fail—as God forbid the hour!—
Edward must fall, which peril heaven forfend!

WARWICK:
No longer Earl of March, but Duke of York.
The next degree is England's royal throne;
for you shall be proclaimed King of England
in every borough as we pass along;
and he that does not throw up his cap for joy
shall forfeit his head for the fault.
King Edward, valiant Richard, Montague,
let us no longer stay, dreaming of renown,
but sound the trumpets, and get about our task.

RICHARD:
Then, Clifford, if your heart were as hard as steel,
as you have shown it to be flinty by your deeds,
I will come to pierce it, or to give you mine.

EDWARD:
Then strike up the drums. God and Saint George for us!

WARWICK:
How now! What news?

Messenger:
The Duke of Norfolk sends you word by me,
the queen is coming with a powerful host;
and he craves your company for speedy counsel.

WARWICK:
Why then it works out, brave warriors, let's go.
3 KING HENRY VI

QUEEN MARGARET:
Welcome, my lord, to this brave town of York.
Yonder is the head of that arch-enemy
that sought to be encompassed with your crown.
Does not the object cheer your heart, my lord?

KING HENRY VI:
Yes, as the rocks cheer those that fear their wreck.
To see this sight, it irks my very soul.
Withhold revenge, dear God! It is not my fault,
nor have I wittingly infringed my vow.

CLIFFORD:
My gracious liege, this too much lenity
and harmful pity must be laid aside.
To whom do lions cast their gentle looks?
Not to the beast that would usurp their den.
Whose hand is it that the forest bear licks?
Not his that spoils her young before her face.
Who escapes the lurking serpent's mortal sting?
Not he that sets his foot upon her back.
The smallest worm will turn when it is trodden on,
and doves will peck in safeguard of their brood.
Ambitious York is aiming at your crown,
while you are smiling as he knits his angry brows.
He, though but a duke, would have his son be a king,
and would raise his issue, like a loving sire;
you, being a king, blessed with a goodly son,
did consent to disinherit him,
which argued you a most unloving father.
Unreasonable creatures feed their young;
and though a man's face is fearful to their eyes,
yet, in protection of their tender ones,
who has not seen them, even with those wings
which they have sometimes used with fearful flight,
make war with him that climbed to their nest,
offering their own lives in their young's defense?
For shame, my liege, make them your precedent!
Would it not be a pity that this goodly boy
should lose his birthright by his father's fault,
and long hereafter say to his child,
'What my great-grandfather and his grandsire got,
my careless father fondly gave away'?
Ah, what a shame this would be! Look on the boy;
and let his manly face, which promises
a successful fortune, steel your melting heart
to hold your own and leave your own with him.

KING HENRY VI:
Clifford has played the orator very well,
inferring arguments of mighty force.
But, Clifford, tell me, have you never heard
that things ill-gotten have always had bad success?
And was it always happy for that son
whose father went to hell for his hoarding?
I'll leave my son my virtuous deeds behind;
and I wish my father had left me no more!
For all the rest is held at such a rate
as brings a thousand-fold more care to keep
than any jot of pleasure in possession.
Ah, cousin York! I wish your best friends knew
how it grieves me that your head is here!

QUEEN MARGARET:
My lord, cheer up your spirits. Our foes are near,
and this soft courage makes your followers faint.
You promised knighthood to our forward son.
Unsheathe your sword, and dub him presently.
Edward, kneel down.

KING HENRY VI:
Edward Plantagenet, arise a knight;
and learn this lesson, draw your sword in right.

PRINCE:
My gracious father, by your kingly leave,
I'll draw it as the apparent heir to the crown,
and in that quarrel I'll use it to the death.

CLIFFORD:
Why, that is spoken like a promising prince.

Messenger:
Royal commanders, be in readiness.
For with a band of thirty thousand men
comes Warwick, backing the Duke of York;
and in the towns, as they march along,
he proclaims him king, and many flee to him.
Arrange your battle, for they are at hand.

CLIFFORD:
I wish your highness would depart the field.
The queen has the best success when you are absent.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, good my lord, and leave us to our fortune.

KING HENRY VI:
Why, that's my fortune too; therefore, I'll stay.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
Let it be with resolution then to fight.

PRINCE EDWARD:
My royal father, cheer these noble lords
and hearten those that fight in your defense.
Unsheathe your sword, good father; cry 'Saint George!'

EDWARD:
Now, perjured Henry! Will you kneel for grace,
and set your diadem upon my head;
or bide the mortal fortune of the field?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Go, scold your minions, proud, insulting boy!
Does it become you to be so bold in terms
before your sovereign and your lawful king?

EDWARD:
I am his king, and he should bow his knee;
I was adopted as his heir by his consent.
Since when, his oath has been broken; for, as I hear,
you, who are king, though he wears the crown,
have caused him, by a new act of parliament,
to blot me out, and put his own son in.

CLIFFORD:
And with good reason too.
Who should succeed the father but the son?

RICHARD:
Are you there, butcher? Oh, I cannot speak!

CLIFFORD:
Yes, crook-back, here I stand to answer you,
or any of the proudest of your sort.

RICHARD:
Was it you that killed young Rutland, was it not?

CLIFFORD:
Yes, and old York, and I'm still not satisfied.

RICHARD:
For God's sake, lords, give the signal to the fight.

WARWICK:
What do you say, Henry, will you yield the crown?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Why, how now, long-tongued Warwick! Do you dare to speak?
When you and I last met at Saint Alban's,
your legs did you better service than your hands.

WARWICK:
Then it was my turn to flee, and now it is yours.

CLIFFORD:
You said so much before, and yet you fled.

WARWICK:
It was not your valor, Clifford, that drove me from there.

NORTHUMBERLAND:
No, nor your manhood that dared to make you stay.

RICHARD:
Northumberland, I hold you in reverence.
Break off the parley; for I can scarcely refrain
the execution of my big-swollen heart
upon that Clifford, that cruel child-killer.

CLIFFORD:
I killed your father, do you call him a child?

RICHARD:
Yes, like a dastard and a treacherous coward,
as you killed our tender brother Rutland.
But before sunset, I'll make you curse the deed.

KING HENRY VI:
Have done with words, my lords, and hear me speak.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Defy them then, or else hold your lips closed.

KING HENRY VI:
I beg you, give no limits to my tongue.
I am a king, and I am privileged to speak.

CLIFFORD:
My liege, the wound that brought about this meeting here
cannot be cured by words; therefore, be still.

RICHARD:
Then, executioner, unsheathe your sword.
By him that made us all, I am resolved
that Clifford's manhood lies upon his tongue.

EDWARD:
Say, Henry, shall I have my right, or no?
A thousand men have broken their fasts today,
that will never dine unless you yield the crown.

WARWICK:
If you deny, their blood will be on your head;
for York in justice is putting on his armor.

PRINCE EDWARD:
If what Warwick says is right, is right,
then there is no wrong, but everything is right.

RICHARD:
Whoever got you, there your mother stands;
for, I know well, you have your mother's tongue.

QUEEN MARGARET:
But you are neither like your sire nor your dam;
but like a foul, misshapen stigmatic,
marked by the destinies to be avoided,
like venomous toads, or lizards' dreadful stings.

RICHARD:
Iron of Naples, hidden with English gilt,
whose father bears the title of a king—
as if a channel should be called the sea—
are you not ashamed, knowing where you come from,
to let your tongue reveal your base-born heart?

EDWARD:
A wisp of straw would be worth a thousand crowns,
to make this shameless harlot know herself.
Helen of Greece was far fairer than you,
although your husband may be Menelaus;
and Agamemnon's brother was never wronged
by that false woman, as this king is by you.
His father reveled in the heart of France,
and tamed the king, and made the dauphin stoop;
and if he had matched according to his state,
he might have kept that glory to this day.
But when he took a beggar to his bed,
and graced your poor sire with his bridal-day,
even then that sunshine brewed a shower for him,
that washed his father's fortunes out of France,
and heaped sedition on his crown at home.
For what has caused this tumult but your pride?
If you had been meek, our title would have still slept;
and we, in pity of the gentle king,
would have slipped our claim until another age.

GEORGE:
But when we saw our sunshine made your spring,
and that your summer bred us no increase,
we set the axe to your usurping root;
and though the edge has hit ourselves somewhat,
yet, know this, since we have begun to strike,
we'll never leave until we have hewn you down,
or bathed your growing with our heated bloods.

EDWARD:
And, in this resolution, I defy you;
not willing to have any longer conference,
since you deny the gentle king to speak.
Sound trumpets! Let our bloody colors wave!
And either victory, or else a grave.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Stay, Edward.

EDWARD:
No, wrangling woman, we'll no longer stay.
These words will cost ten thousand lives this day.
3 KING HENRY VI

WARWICK:
Exhausted with toil, like runners after a race,
I'll lay me down for a little while to breathe;
for strokes received, and many blows repaid,
have robbed my strong-knit sinews of their strength,
and in spite of everything, I must rest for a while.

EDWARD:
Smile, gentle heaven! Or strike, ungentle death!
For this world frowns, and Edward's sun is clouded.

WARWICK:
How now, my lord! What's happened? What hope of good?

GEORGE:
Our fortune is loss, our hope but sad despair;
our ranks are broken, and ruin follows us.
What counsel do you give? Where shall we flee?

EDWARD:
Flight is useless, they follow us with wings;
and we are weak and cannot escape pursuit.

RICHARD:
Ah, Warwick, why have you withdrawn yourself?
Your brother's blood the thirsty earth has drunk,
broached with the steely point of Clifford's lance;
and in the very pangs of death he cried,
like a dismal clamor heard from far,
'Warwick, revenge! Brother, revenge my death!'
So, underneath the bellies of their steeds,
that stained their fetlocks in his smoking blood,
the noble gentleman gave up the ghost.

WARWICK:
Then let the earth be drunken with our blood.
I'll kill my horse, because I will not flee.
Why do we stand here like soft-hearted women,
wailing our losses, while the foe does rage;
and look on, as if the tragedy
were played in jest by counterfeiting actors?
Here on my knee I vow to God above,
I'll never pause again, never stand still,
until either death has closed these eyes of mine
or fortune has given me the measure of revenge.

EDWARD:
O Warwick, I do bend my knee with yours;
and in this vow, I chain my soul to yours!
And, before my knee rises from the earth's cold face,
I throw my hands, my eyes, my heart to you,
you setter-up and plucker-down of kings,
beseeching you, if it is your will
that this body must be prey to my foes,
yet that your brazen gates of heaven may open,
and give sweet passage to my sinful soul!
Now, lords, take your leave until we meet again,
wherever it may be, in heaven or on earth.

RICHARD:
Brother, give me your hand; and, gentle Warwick,
let me embrace you in my weary arms.
I, that never wept, now melt with woe
that winter should cut off our springtime so.

WARWICK:
Away, away! Once more, sweet lords, farewell.

GEORGE:
Yet let us all go together to our troops,
and give leave to those to flee that will not stay;
and call them pillars that will stand by us;
and, if we thrive, promise them such rewards
as victors wear at the Olympian games.
This may plant courage in their quailing breasts;
for there is still hope of life and victory.
Let's delay no longer, let's go from here with all speed.
3 KING HENRY VI

RICHARD:
Now, Clifford, I have singled you out alone.
Suppose this arm is for the Duke of York,
and this for Rutland; both are bound to revenge,
even if you were surrounded by a brazen wall.

CLIFFORD:
Now, Richard, I am with you here alone.
This is the hand that stabbed your father, York;
and this is the hand that killed your brother, Rutland;
and here is the heart that triumphs in their deaths
and cheers these hands that killed your father and brother
to do the same to you;
and so, have at you!

RICHARD:
No, Warwick, single out some other chase;
for I myself will hunt this wolf to death.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING HENRY VI:
This battle is like the morning's war,
when dying clouds contend with growing light,
at a time when the shepherd, blowing on his nails,
can neither call it perfect day nor night.
Now it sways this way, like a mighty sea
forced by the tide to combat with the wind;
now it sways that way, like the same sea
forced to retire by the fury of the wind.
Sometimes the flood prevails, and then the wind;
now one is better, then another is best;
both are tugging to be victors, breast to breast,
yet neither is a conqueror nor conquered.
So is the equal of this fell war.
Here on this molehill, I will sit me down.
To whom God wills, there be the victory!
For Margaret my queen, and Clifford too,
have chided me from the battle; swearing both
that they prosper best of all when I am away.
I wish I were dead! If it were God's good will;
for what is in this world but grief and woe?
O God! I think it would be a happy life,
to be no better than a homely swain;
to sit upon a hill, as I do now,
to carve out dials quaintly, point by point,
thereby to see the minutes how they run,
how many make the hour full complete;
how many hours bring about the day;
how many days will finish up the year;
how many years a mortal man may live.
When this is known, then to divide the times:
so many hours I must tend my flock;
so many hours I must take my rest;
so many hours I must contemplate;
so many hours I must sport myself;
so many days my ewes have been with young;
so many weeks before the poor fools will give birth;
so many years before I shall shear the fleece.
So minutes, hours, days, months, and years,
passed over to the end they were created,
would bring white hairs to a quiet grave.
Ah, what a life this would be! How sweet! How lovely!
Does not the hawthorn-bush give a sweeter shade
to shepherds looking on their silly sheep,
than a rich embroidered canopy does
to kings that fear their subjects' treachery?
Oh, yes, it does; a thousand-fold it does.
And to conclude, the shepherd's homely curds,
his cold, thin drink out of his leather bottle,
his accustomed sleep under a fresh tree's shade,
all of which he enjoys securely and sweetly,
is far beyond a prince's delicacies,
his viands sparkling in a golden cup,
his body couched in a curious bed,
when care, mistrust, and treason wait on him.

Son:
The wind that profits nobody blows ill.
This man, whom I killed hand to hand in fight,
may be possessed with some store of crowns;
and I, that perhaps take them from him now,
may yet before night yield both my life and them
to some other man, as this dead man does to me.
Who's this? O God! It is my father's face,
whom I have unwittingly killed in this conflict.
Oh, heavy times, begetting such events!
From London, I was pressed forth by the king;
my father, being the Earl of Warwick's man,
came on the part of York, pressed by his master;
and I, who received my life at his hands,
have by my hands bereaved him of his life.
Pardon me, God, I did not know what I was doing!
And pardon, father, for I did not know you!
My tears shall wipe away these bloody marks;
and no more words until they have flowed their fill.

KING HENRY VI:
Oh, piteous spectacle! Oh, bloody times!
While lions war and battle for their dens,
poor, harmless lambs abide their enmity.
Weep, wretched man, I'll aid you tear for tear;
and let our hearts and eyes, like civil war,
be blind with tears, and break, overcharged with grief.

Father:
You that so stoutly have resisted me,
