give me your gold, if you have any gold.
For I have bought it with a hundred blows.
But let me see. Is this our foeman's face?
Ah, no, no, no, it is my only son!
Ah, boy, if any life is left in you,
throw up your eye! See, see what showers arise,
blown with the windy tempest of my heart,
upon your words, that kill my eye and heart!
Oh, pity, God, this miserable age!
What stratagems, how fell, how butcherly,
erroneous, mutinous, and unnatural,
this deadly quarrel daily does beget!
Oh boy, your father gave you life too soon,
and has bereft you of your life too late!

KING HENRY VI:
Woe above woe! Grief more than common grief!
Oh, that my death would stop these ruthless deeds!
Oh, pity, pity, gentle heaven, pity!
The red rose and the white are on his face,
the fatal colors of our striving houses.
The one his purple blood right well resembles;
the other, his pale cheeks, I think, presents.
Let one rose wither, and let the other flourish;
if you contend, a thousand lives must wither.

Son:
How will my mother, for a father's death,
take on with me and never be satisfied!

Father:
How will my wife, for the slaughter of my son,
shed seas of tears and never be satisfied!

KING HENRY VI:
How will the country, for these woeful chances,
misthink the king and not be satisfied!

Son:
Was ever a son so mourned a father's death?

Father:
Was ever a father so bemoaned his son?

KING HENRY VI:
Was ever a king so grieved for his subjects' woe?
Your sorrow is much; mine is ten times as much.

Son:
I'll carry you from here, where I may weep my fill.

Father:
These arms of mine shall be your winding-sheet;
my heart, sweet boy, shall be your sepulcher,
for from my heart your image will never go;
my sighing breast shall be your funeral bell.
And so obsequious will your father be,
even for the loss of you, having no more,
as Priam was for all his valiant sons.
I'll carry you from here; and let them fight that will,
for I have murdered where I should not kill.

KING HENRY VI:
Sad-hearted men, much overcome with care,
here sits a king more woeful than you are.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Fly, father, fly! For all your friends have fled,
and Warwick is raging like a chafed bull.
Away! For death is holding us in pursuit.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Mount your horse, my lord; post to Berwick with all speed.
Edward and Richard, like a pair of greyhounds
having the fearful, flying hare in sight,
with fiery eyes sparkling for very wrath,
and bloody steel grasped in their ireful hands,
are at our backs; and therefore, let's go with all speed.

EXETER:
Away! For vengeance comes along with them.
No, don't stay to expostulate, make speed;
or else come after. I'll go on ahead.

KING HENRY VI:
No, take me with you, good sweet Exeter.
Not that I fear to stay, but I love to go
where the queen intends. Forward; away!
3 KING HENRY VI

CLIFFORD:
Here my candle burns out; yes, here it dies,
which, while it lasted, gave King Henry light.
Oh, Lancaster, I fear your overthrow
more than my body's parting with my soul!
My love and fear glued many friends to you;
and, now that I fall, your tough mixture melts.
Impairing Henry, strengthening the mis-proud York,
the common people swarm like summer flies;
and where do the gnats fly but to the sun?
And who shines now but Henry's enemies?
Oh Phoebus, if you had never given your consent
that Phaethon should check your fiery steeds,
your burning chariot would never have scorched the earth!
And, Henry, if you had ruled as kings should do,
or as your father and his father did,
giving no ground to the house of York,
they would never then have sprung up like summer flies;
I and ten thousand in this luckless realm
would have left no mourning widows for our death;
and you this day would have kept your chair in peace.
For what cherishes weeds but gentle air?
And what makes robbers bold but too much lenity?
Complaints are useless, and my wounds are incurable;
there is no way to flee, nor strength to hold out in flight.
The foe is merciless, and will not pity;
for at their hands I have deserved no pity.
The air has gotten into my deadly wounds,
and much loss of blood is making me faint.
Come, York and Richard, Warwick and the rest;
I stabbed your fathers' bosoms, split my breast.

EDWARD:
Now let's breathe, lords. Good fortune bids us pause,
and smooth the frowns of war with peaceful looks.
Some troops are pursuing the bloody-minded queen,
who led the calm Henry, though he was a king,
as a sail, filled with a fretting gust,
commands a large merchant ship to stem the waves.
But do you think, lords, that Clifford fled with them?

WARWICK:
No, it's impossible he should escape,
for, though I speak the words before his face,
your brother Richard marked him for the grave.
And wherever he is, he's surely dead.

EDWARD:
Whose soul is that which is taking its heavy leave?

RICHARD:
A deadly groan, like the departing of life and death.

EDWARD:
See who it is. And, now that the battle is ended,
if it is a friend or foe, let him be gently used.

RICHARD:
Revoke that doom of mercy, for it is Clifford;
who, not content that he lopped off the branch
in hewing down Rutland when his leaves put forth,
but set his murdering knife to the root
from which that tender spray did sweetly spring,
I mean our princely father, the Duke of York.

WARWICK:
From off the gates of York, fetch down the head,
your father's head, which Clifford placed there.
Instead of it, let this supply the room.
Measure for measure must be answered.

EDWARD:
Bring forth that fatal screech-owl to our house,
that sang nothing but death to us and ours.
Now death shall stop his dismal, threatening sound,
and his ill-boding tongue shall speak no more.

WARWICK:
I think his understanding is gone.
Speak, Clifford, do you know who is speaking to you?
Dark, cloudy death overshadows his beams of life,
and he neither sees nor hears what we say.

RICHARD:
Oh, I wish he did! And so perhaps he does.
It's just his policy to counterfeit,
because he would avoid such bitter taunts
which he gave our father at the time of his death.

GEORGE:
If you think so, vex him with eager words.

RICHARD:
Clifford, ask for mercy and get no grace.

EDWARD:
Clifford, repent in bootless penitence.

WARWICK:
Clifford, devise excuses for your faults.

GEORGE:
While we devise cruel tortures for your faults.

RICHARD:
You loved York, and I am the son of York.

EDWARD:
You pitied Rutland; I will pity you.

GEORGE:
Where's Captain Margaret, to defend you now?

WARWICK:
They are mocking you, Clifford. Swear as you used to.

RICHARD:
What, not an oath? No, then the world is in a bad state
when Clifford cannot spare his friends an oath.
I know by that he's dead; and, by my soul,
if this right hand would buy two hours of life,
so that I, in all despite, might rail at him,
this hand would chop it off, and with the
issuing blood,
I would stifle the villain whose unquenched thirst
York and young Rutland could not satisfy.

WARWICK:
Yes, but he's dead. Off with the traitor's head,
and raise it in the place where your father's stands.
And now to London with a triumphant march,
there to be crowned England's royal king.
From there, Warwick will cross the sea to France,
and ask for the Lady Bona to be your queen.
So you shall unite both these lands together;
and, having France as your friend, you shall not dread
the scattered foe that hopes to rise again;
for though they cannot sting greatly to hurt,
yet you should expect them to buzz to offend your ears.
First I will see the coronation;
and then to Brittany I'll cross the sea,
to effect this marriage, if it pleases my lord.

EDWARD:
Even as you wish, sweet Warwick, let it be;
for on your shoulder I build my seat,
and I will never undertake anything
in which your counsel and consent are wanting.
Richard, I will create you Duke of Gloucester,
and George, of Clarence. Warwick, like ourselves,
shall do and undo as he pleases best.

RICHARD:
Let me be Duke of Clarence, and George of Gloucester;
for Gloucester's dukedom is too ominous.

WARWICK:
Tut, that's a foolish observation.
Richard, be Duke of Gloucester. Now to London,
to see these honors in possession.
3 KING HENRY VI

First Keeper:
We'll hide ourselves under this thick-grown brake;
for soon the deer will come through this clearing;
and in this covert we will make our stand,
culling the principal of all the deer.

Second Keeper:
I'll stay above the hill, so we can both shoot.

First Keeper:
That cannot be; the noise of your crossbow
will scare the herd, and so my shot will be lost.
Here we'll both stand, and aim for the best.
And, so the time won't seem tedious,
I'll tell you what happened to me one day
in this very place where we now plan to stand.

Second Keeper:
Here comes a man; let's wait until he has passed.

KING HENRY VI:
I have stolen from Scotland, out of pure love,
to greet my own land with my wishful sight.
No, Harry, Harry, this is no land of yours;
your place is filled, your scepter is wrung from you,
your anointing balm is washed off.
No bending knee will call you Caesar now,
no humble suitors will press to speak for what is right,
no, not a man comes for redress from you;
for how can I help them, and not myself?

First Keeper:
Yes, here's a deer whose skin is a keeper's fee.
This is the former king; let's seize upon him.

KING HENRY VI:
Let me embrace you, sour adversity,
for wise men say it is the wisest course.

Second Keeper:
Why are we waiting? Let's lay our hands on him.

First Keeper:
Forbear for a while; we'll hear a little more.

KING HENRY VI:
My queen and son have gone to France for aid;
and, as I hear, the great, commanding Warwick
has gone there, to ask for the French king's sister
as a wife for Edward. If this news is true,
poor queen and son, your labor is but lost;
for Warwick is a subtle orator,
and Lewis is a prince soon won with moving words.
By this account, then, Margaret may win him;
for she is a woman to be pitied much.
Her sighs will make a battery in his breast;
her tears will pierce into a marble heart;
the tiger will be mild while she does mourn;
and Nero will be tainted with remorse,
to hear and see her plaints, her briny tears.
Yes, but she has come to beg, Warwick to give;
she, on his left side, craving aid for Henry,
he, on his right, asking for a wife for Edward.
She weeps, and says her Henry is deposed;
he smiles, and says his Edward is installed;
that she, poor wretch, can speak no more for grief;
while Warwick tells his title, smooths the wrong,
infers arguments of mighty strength,
and in conclusion wins the king from her,
with the promise of his sister, and whatever else,
to strengthen and support King Edward's place.
Oh Margaret, thus it will be; and you, poor soul,
are then forsaken, as you went forlorn!

Second Keeper:
Say, what are you that talk of kings and queens?

KING HENRY VI:
More than I seem, and less than I was born to.
A man at least, for I should not be less;
and men may talk of kings, so why not I?

Second Keeper:
Yes, but you talk as if you were a king.

KING HENRY VI:
Why, so I am, in my mind; and that's enough.

Second Keeper:
But, if you are a king, where is your crown?

KING HENRY VI:
My crown is in my heart, not on my head;
it is not decked with diamonds and Indian stones,
nor is it to be seen. My crown is called content;
it is a crown that kings seldom enjoy.

Second Keeper:
Well, if you are a king crowned with content,
your crown, your content, and you must be contented
to go along with us; for as we think,
you are the king that King Edward has deposed;
and we, his subjects sworn in all allegiance,
will apprehend you as his enemy.

KING HENRY VI:
But did you never swear, and then break an oath?

Second Keeper:
No, never such an oath; nor will I now.

KING HENRY VI:
Where did you live when I was King of England?

Second Keeper:
Here in this country, where we now remain.

KING HENRY VI:
I was anointed king at nine months old;
my father and my grandfather were kings,
and you were sworn true subjects to me.
And tell me, then, have you not broken your oaths?

First Keeper:
No;
for we were subjects only while you were king.

KING HENRY VI:
Why, am I dead? Do I not breathe as a man?
Ah, simple men, you do not know what you swear!
Look, as I blow this feather from my face,
and as the air blows it to me again,
obeying with my wind when I do blow,
and yielding to another when it blows,
always commanded by the greater gust;
such is the lightness of you common men.
But do not break your oaths; for of that sin
my mild entreaty shall not make you guilty.
Go where you will, the king shall be commanded;
and if you are kings, command, and I'll obey.

First Keeper:
We are true subjects to the king, King Edward.

KING HENRY VI:
So you would be again to Henry,
if he were seated as King Edward is.

First Keeper:
We charge you, in God's name, and the king's,
to go with us to the officers.

KING HENRY VI:
In God's name, lead; may your king's name be obeyed.
And what God wills, that let your king perform;
and what he wills, I humbly yield to.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
Brother of Gloucester, at Saint Alban's field,
this lady's husband, Sir Richard Grey, was slain,
and his lands were then seized by the conqueror.
Her suit is now to repossess those lands,
which we, in justice, cannot well deny,
because the worthy gentleman lost his life
in the quarrel of the house of York.

GLOUCESTER:
Your highness will do well to grant her suit;
it would be a dishonor to deny it to her.

KING EDWARD IV:
It would be no less; but I'll make a pause yet.

GLOUCESTER:

CLARENCE:

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
Widow, we will consider your suit,
and come some other time to know our mind.

LADY GREY:
Right gracious lord, I cannot brook delay.
May it please your highness to resolve me now;
and whatever your pleasure is, shall satisfy me.

GLOUCESTER:

CLARENCE:

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
How many children do you have, widow? Tell me.

CLARENCE:

GLOUCESTER:

LADY GREY:
Three, my most gracious lord.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
It would be a pity if they were to lose their father's lands.

LADY GREY:
Be pitiful, dread lord, and grant it then.

KING EDWARD IV:
Lords, give us leave. I'll test this widow's wit.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
Now tell me, madam, do you love your children?

LADY GREY:
Yes, as dearly as I love myself.

KING EDWARD IV:
And would you not do much to do them good?

LADY GREY:
To do them good, I would sustain some harm.

KING EDWARD IV:
Then get your husband's lands, to do them good.

LADY GREY:
Therefore I came to your majesty.

KING EDWARD IV:
I'll tell you how these lands are to be gotten.

LADY GREY:
So you shall bind me to your highness's service.

KING EDWARD IV:
What service will you do for me, if I give them to you?

LADY GREY:
Whatever you command, it is within my power to do.

KING EDWARD IV:
But you will take exceptions to my boon.

LADY GREY:
No, gracious lord, unless I cannot do it.

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, but you can do what I mean to ask.

LADY GREY:
Why, then I will do what your grace commands.

GLOUCESTER:

CLARENCE:

LADY GREY:
Why does my lord stop? Shall I not hear my task?

KING EDWARD IV:
An easy task; it is but to love a king.

LADY GREY:
That's soon performed, because I am a subject.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, then, I freely give you your husband's lands.

LADY GREY:
I take my leave with many thousand thanks.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
But stay, it's the fruits of love I mean.

LADY GREY:
The fruits of love I mean, my loving liege.

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, but, I fear, in another sense.
What love, do you think, I am suing so much to get?

LADY GREY:
My love until death, my humble thanks, my prayers;
that love which virtue begs and virtue grants.

KING EDWARD IV:
No, by my troth, I did not mean such love.

LADY GREY:
Why, then you don't mean what I thought you did.

KING EDWARD IV:
But now you can partly perceive my mind.

LADY GREY:
My mind will never grant what I perceive
your highness aims at, if I am aiming correctly.

KING EDWARD IV:
To tell you plainly, I aim to lie with you.

LADY GREY:
To tell you plainly, I would rather lie in prison.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, then you shall not have your husband's lands.

LADY GREY:
Why, then my honesty shall be my dower;
for by that loss I will not purchase them.

KING EDWARD IV:
In that, you are wronging your children mightily.

LADY GREY:
Herein your highness wrongs both them and me.
But, mighty lord, this merry inclination
does not accord with the sadness of my suit.
Please dismiss me, either with a 'yes' or a 'no.'

KING EDWARD IV:
'Yes,' if you will say 'yes' to my request;
'no' if you say 'no' to my demand.

LADY GREY:
Then, no, my lord. My suit is at an end.

GLOUCESTER:

CLARENCE:

KING EDWARD IV:

LADY GREY:
It is better said than done, my gracious lord.
I am a subject fit to be jested with,
but far unfit to be a sovereign.

KING EDWARD IV:
Sweet widow, by my state I swear to you,
I speak no more than what my soul intends;
and that is, to enjoy you for my love.

LADY GREY:
And that is more than I will yield to.
I know I am too mean to be your queen,
and yet too good to be your concubine.

KING EDWARD IV:
You are quibbling, widow. I did mean, my queen.

LADY GREY:
It will grieve your grace that my sons should call you father.

KING EDWARD IV:
No more than when my daughters call you mother.
You are a widow, and you have some children;
and, by God's mother, I, being but a bachelor,
have some others. Why, it's a happy thing
to be the father of many sons.
Answer no more, for you shall be my queen.

GLOUCESTER:

CLARENCE:

KING EDWARD IV:
Brothers, you are wondering what we two have been talking about.

GLOUCESTER:
The widow does not like it, for she looks very sad.

KING EDWARD IV:
You'll think it strange if I should marry her.

CLARENCE:
To whom, my lord?

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, Clarence, to myself.

GLOUCESTER:
That would be a ten days' wonder at the least.

CLARENCE:
That's a day longer than a wonder lasts.

GLOUCESTER:
By so much is the wonder in extremes.

KING EDWARD IV:
Well, jest on, brothers. I can tell you both
her suit is granted for her husband's lands.

Nobleman:
My gracious lord, your foe Henry has been taken,
and brought as your prisoner to your palace gate.

KING EDWARD IV:
See that he is conveyed to the Tower.
And let us go, brothers, to the man that took him,
to question him about his apprehension.
Widow, you go along. Lords, use her honorably.

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, Edward will use women honorably.
I wish he were wasted, marrow, bones, and all,
so that from his loins no hopeful branch may spring,
to cross me from the golden time I look for!
And yet, between my soul's desire and me—
the lustful Edward's title being buried—
are Clarence, Henry, and his son young Edward,
and all the unexpected issue of their bodies,
to take their places, before I can place myself.
A cold premeditation for my purpose!
Why, then, I am but dreaming of sovereignty;
like one that stands upon a promontory,
and spies a far-off shore where he would tread,
wishing his foot were equal with his eye,
and chides the sea that separates him from there,
saying, he'll lade it dry to have his way.
So do I wish for the crown, being so far off;
and so I chide the means that keep me from it;
and so I say, I'll cut the causes off,
flattering myself with impossibilities.
My eye is too quick, my heart is too overweening,
unless my hand and strength could equal them.
Well, say there is no kingdom then for Richard;
what other pleasure can the world afford?
I'll make my heaven in a lady's lap,
and deck my body in gay ornaments,
and witch sweet ladies with my words and looks.
Oh, a miserable thought! And more unlikely
than to accomplish twenty golden crowns!
Why, love forswore me in my mother's womb.
And, so that I should not deal in her soft laws,
she did corrupt frail nature with some bribe,
to shrink my arm up like a withered shrub;
to make an envious mountain on my back,
where deformity sits to mock my body;
to shape my legs of an unequal size;
to disproportion me in every part,
like a chaos, or an unlicked bear-whelp
that carries no impression like the dam.
And am I then a man to be beloved?
Oh, what a monstrous fault, to harbor such a thought!
Then, since this earth affords no joy to me,
but to command, to check, to overbear such
as are of a better person than myself,
I'll make my heaven to dream upon the crown,
and, while I live, to account this world but hell,
until my misshapen trunk that bears this head
is roundly impaled with a glorious crown.
And yet I do not know how to get the crown,
for many lives stand between me and home.
And I—like one lost in a thorny wood,
that rends the thorns and is rent by the thorns,
seeking a way and straying from the way;
not knowing how to find the open air,
but toiling desperately to find it out—
torment myself to catch the English crown.
And from that torment I will free myself,
or hew my way out with a bloody axe.
Why, I can smile, and murder while I smile,
and cry 'Content' to that which grieves my heart,
and wet my cheeks with artificial tears,
and frame my face to all occasions.
I'll drown more sailors than the mermaid shall;
I'll slay more gazers than the basilisk;
I'll play the orator as well as Nestor,
deceive more slyly than Ulysses could,
and, like a Sinon, take another Troy.
I can add colors to the chameleon,
change shapes with Proteus for advantages,
and set the murderous Machiavelli to school.
Can I do this, and not get a crown?
Tut, if it were farther off, I'd pluck it down.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING LEWIS XI:
Fair Queen of England, worthy Margaret,
sit down with us. It ill befits your state
and birth, that you should stand while Lewis sits.

QUEEN MARGARET:
No, mighty King of France. Now Margaret
must strike her sail and learn for a while to serve
where kings command. I was, I must confess,
Great Albion's queen in former golden days.
But now misfortune has trodden my title down,
and with dishonor laid me on the ground;
where I must take a seat like my fortune,
and conform myself to my humble seat.

KING LEWIS XI:
Why, say, fair queen, from where does this deep despair spring?

QUEEN MARGARET:
From such a cause as fills my eyes with tears
and stops my tongue, while my heart is drowned in cares.

KING LEWIS XI:
Whatever it may be, be you still like yourself,
and sit by our side.
Do not yield your neck
to fortune's yoke, but let your dauntless mind
still ride in triumph over all misfortune.
Be plain, Queen Margaret, and tell your grief;
it shall be eased, if France can yield relief.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Those gracious words revive my drooping thoughts
and give my tongue-tied sorrows leave to speak.
Now, therefore, let it be known to the noble Lewis,
that Henry, the sole possessor of my love,
has become a banished man from a king,
and is forced to live in Scotland, a forlorn man;
while the proud and ambitious Edward, Duke of York,
usurps the regal title and the seat
of England's true-anointed, lawful king.
This is the cause that I, poor Margaret,
with this my son, Prince Edward, Henry's heir,
have come to crave your just and lawful aid;
and if you fail us, all our hope is gone.
Scotland has the will to help, but cannot help;
our people and our peers are both misled,
our treasures are seized, our soldiers are put to flight,
and, as you see, we ourselves are in a heavy plight.

KING LEWIS XI:
Renowned queen, with patience, calm the storm,
while we think of a means to break it off.

QUEEN MARGARET:
The more we stay, the stronger our foe grows.

KING LEWIS XI:
The more I stay, the more I'll help you.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Oh, but impatience waits on true sorrow.
And see where the breeder of my sorrow comes!

KING LEWIS XI:
Who is he that approaches boldly to our presence?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Our Earl of Warwick, Edward's greatest friend.

KING LEWIS XI:
Welcome, brave Warwick! What brings you to France?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, now a second storm begins to rise;
for this is he that moves both wind and tide.

WARWICK:
From the worthy Edward, King of Albion,
my lord and sovereign, and your vowed friend,
I come, in kindness and unfeigned love,
first, to do greetings to your royal person;
and then to crave a league of amity;
and lastly, to confirm that amity
with a nuptial knot, if you vouchsafe to grant
that virtuous Lady Bona, your fair sister,
to England's king in lawful marriage.

QUEEN MARGARET:

WARWICK:

QUEEN MARGARET:
King Lewis and Lady Bona, hear me speak,
before you answer Warwick. His demand
springs not from Edward's well-meant, honest love,
but from deceit bred by necessity;
for how can tyrants safely govern at home,
unless they purchase a great alliance abroad?
To prove him a tyrant, this reason may suffice,
that Henry still lives. But if he were dead,
yet here Prince Edward stands, King Henry's son.
Look, therefore, Lewis, that by this league and marriage
you do not draw danger and dishonor upon yourself;
for though usurpers sway the rule for a while,
yet the heavens are just, and time suppresses wrongs.

WARWICK:
Injurious Margaret!

PRINCE EDWARD:
And why not queen?

WARWICK:
Because your father Henry did usurp;
and you are no more a prince than she is a queen.

OXFORD:
Then Warwick disannuls great John of Gaunt,
who subdued the greatest part of Spain;
and, after John of Gaunt, Henry the Fourth,
whose wisdom was a mirror to the wisest;
and, after that wise prince, Henry the Fifth,
who by his prowess conquered all of France.
From these, our Henry lineally descends.

WARWICK:
Oxford, how does it happen that, in this smooth discourse,
you did not tell how Henry the Sixth has lost
all that which Henry the Fifth had gotten?
I think these peers of France should smile at that.
But for the rest, you tell a pedigree
of sixty-two years; a silly time
to make a prescription for a kingdom's worth.

OXFORD:
Why, Warwick, can you speak against your liege,
whom you obeyed for thirty-six years,
and not betray your treason with a blush?

WARWICK:
Can Oxford, that always defended the right,
now buckler falsehood with a pedigree?
For shame! Leave Henry, and call Edward king.

OXFORD:
Call him my king by whose injurious doom
my elder brother, the Lord Aubrey Vere,
was put to death? And more than that, my father,
even in the downfall of his mellowed years,
when nature brought him to the door of death?
No, Warwick, no; while life upholds this arm,
this arm upholds the house of Lancaster.

WARWICK:
And I the house of York.

KING LEWIS XI:
Queen Margaret, Prince Edward, and Oxford,
vouchsafe, at our request, to stand aside,
while I have further conference with Warwick.

QUEEN MARGARET:
May the heavens grant that Warwick's words do not bewitch him!

KING LEWIS XI:
Now Warwick, tell me, even upon your conscience,
is Edward your true king? For I would be loath
to link with him that was not lawfully chosen.

WARWICK:
On that I pawn my credit and my honor.

KING LEWIS XI:
But is he gracious in the people's eyes?

WARWICK:
All the more because Henry was unfortunate.

KING LEWIS XI:
Then further, all dissembling set aside,
tell me, in truth, the measure of his love
for our sister Bona.

WARWICK:
It seems to be such
as may befit a monarch like himself.
I myself have often heard him say and swear
that this his love was an eternal plant,
of which the root was fixed in virtue's ground,
the leaves and fruit maintained with beauty's sun,
exempt from envy, but not from disdain,
unless the Lady Bona relieves his pain.

KING LEWIS XI:
Now, sister, let us hear your firm resolve.

BONA:
Your grant, or your denial, shall be mine.
Yet I confess that often before this day,
when I have heard your king's desert recounted,
my ear has tempted my judgment to desire.

KING LEWIS XI:
Then, Warwick, thus: our sister shall be Edward's;
and now, forthwith, articles shall be drawn
concerning the jointure that your king must make,
which, with her dowry, shall be counterpoised.
Draw near, Queen Margaret, and be a witness
that Bona shall be the wife of the English king.

PRINCE EDWARD:
To Edward, but not to the English king.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Deceitful Warwick! It was your device
by this alliance to make my suit void.
Before your coming, Lewis was Henry's friend.

KING LEWIS XI:
And he is still a friend to him and Margaret.
But if your title to the crown is weak,
as may appear by Edward's good success,
then it is but reasonable that I be released
from giving the aid which I recently promised.
Yet you shall have all the kindness at my hand
that your estate requires and mine can yield.

WARWICK:
Henry now lives in Scotland at his ease,
where, having nothing, he can lose nothing.
And as for yourself, our former queen,
you have a father who is able to maintain you;
and it would be better if you troubled him than France.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Peace, you impudent and shameless Warwick, peace,
you proud setter-up and puller-down of kings!
I will not go from here, until, with my talk and tears,
both full of truth, I make King Lewis behold
your sly conveyance and your lord's false love;
for both of you are birds of the same feather.

KING LEWIS XI:
Warwick, this is some post to us or to you.

Post:

OXFORD:
I like it well that our fair queen and mistress
smiles at her news, while Warwick frowns at his.

PRINCE EDWARD:
No, mark how Lewis stamps, as if he were nettled.
I hope all's for the best.

KING LEWIS XI:
Warwick, what is your news? And yours, fair queen?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Mine, such as fill my heart with unhoped-for joys.

WARWICK:
Mine, full of sorrow and heart's discontent.

KING LEWIS XI:
What! Has your king married the Lady Grey!
And now, to soothe your forgery and his,
he sends me a paper to persuade me to have patience?
Is this the alliance that he seeks with France?
Does he dare to presume to scorn us in this manner?

QUEEN MARGARET:
I told your majesty as much before.
This proves Edward's love and Warwick's honesty.

WARWICK:
King Lewis, I hereby protest, in the sight of heaven,
and by the hope I have of heavenly bliss,
that I am clear from this misdeed of Edward's,
who is no more my king, for he dishonors me,
but most of all himself, if he could see his shame.
Did I forget that my father came to an untimely death
by the house of York?
Did I let the abuse done to my niece pass?
Did I impale him with the regal crown?
Did I put Henry from his native right?
And am I rewarded at last with shame?
Shame on himself! For my desert is honor.
And to repair my honor lost for him,
I here renounce him and return to Henry.
My noble queen, let former grudges pass,
and from now on, I am your true servant.
I will revenge his wrong to Lady Bona,
and replant Henry in his former state.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Warwick, these words have turned my hate to love;
and I forgive and quite forget old faults,
and I am joyful that you are becoming King Henry's friend.

WARWICK:
So much his friend, yes, his unfeigned friend,
that, if King Lewis will vouchsafe to furnish us
with some few bands of chosen soldiers,
I'll undertake to land them on our coast
and force the tyrant from his seat by war.
It is not his new-made bride that will help him.
And as for Clarence, as my letters tell me,
he's very likely to fall from him now,
for matching more for wanton lust than for honor,
or than for the strength and safety of our country.

BONA:
Dear brother, how shall Bona be revenged
but by your help to this distressed queen?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Renowned prince, how shall poor Henry live,
unless you rescue him from foul despair?

BONA:
My quarrel and this English queen's are one.

WARWICK:
And mine, fair lady Bona, joins with yours.

KING LEWIS XI:
And mine with hers, and yours, and Margaret's.
Therefore, at last I am firmly resolved
that you shall have aid.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Let me give humble thanks for all at once.

KING LEWIS XI:
Then, England's messenger, return in post,
and tell false Edward, your supposed king,
that Lewis of France is sending over masquers
to revel with him and his new bride.
You see what has passed; go and fear your king withal.

BONA:
Tell him, in the hope that he'll become a widower shortly,
I'll wear the willow garland for his sake.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Tell him, my mourning weeds are laid aside,
and I am ready to put on armor.

WARWICK:
Tell him from me that he has done me wrong,
and therefore I'll uncrown him before long.
There's your reward. Be gone.

KING LEWIS XI:
But, Warwick,
you and Oxford, with five thousand men,
shall cross the seas, and challenge false Edward to battle;
and, as the occasion serves, this noble queen
and prince shall follow with a fresh supply.
Yet, before you go, answer me one doubt,
what pledge do we have of your firm loyalty?

WARWICK:
This shall assure my constant loyalty,
that if our queen and this young prince agree,
I'll join my eldest daughter and my joy
to him forthwith in holy wedlock bands.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, I agree, and I thank you for your motion.
Son Edward, she is fair and virtuous,
therefore, do not delay, give your hand to Warwick;
and, with your hand, your irrevocable faith,
that only Warwick's daughter shall be yours.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Yes, I accept her, for she well deserves it;
and here, to pledge my vow, I give my hand.

KING LEWIS XI:
Why are we waiting now? These soldiers shall be levied,
and you, Lord Bourbon, our high admiral,
shall waft them over with our royal fleet.
I long until Edward falls by the mischance of war,
for mocking marriage with a dame of France.

WARWICK:
I came from Edward as an ambassador,
but I return as his sworn and mortal foe.
The matter of marriage was the charge he gave me,
but dreadful war shall answer his demand.
Did he have no one else to make a fool of but me?
Then no one but I shall turn his jest to sorrow.
I was the chief that raised him to the crown,
and I'll be the chief to bring him down again.
Not that I pity Henry's misery,
but I seek revenge on Edward's mockery.
3 KING HENRY VI

GLOUCESTER:
Now tell me, brother Clarence, what do you think
of this new marriage with the Lady Grey?
Has not our brother made a worthy choice?

CLARENCE:
Alas, you know, it's far from here to France;
how could he stay until Warwick made his return?

SOMERSET:
My lords, forbear this talk; here comes the king.

GLOUCESTER:
And his well-chosen bride.

CLARENCE:
I intend to tell him plainly what I think.

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, brother of Clarence, how do you like our choice,
that you stand pensive, as if you were half-discontented?

CLARENCE:
As well as Lewis of France, or the Earl of Warwick,
who are so weak of courage and in judgment
that they'll take no offense at our abuse.

KING EDWARD IV:
Suppose they take offense without a cause,
they are but Lewis and Warwick. I am Edward,
your king and Warwick's, and I must have my will.

GLOUCESTER:
And you shall have your will, because you are our king.
Yet a hasty marriage seldom proves well.

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, brother Richard, are you offended too?

GLOUCESTER:
Not I.
No, God forbid that I should wish them to be separated,
whom God has joined together; yes, and it would be a pity
to separate them that are so well-yoked together.

KING EDWARD IV:
Setting your scorns and your dislike aside,
tell me some reason why the Lady Grey
should not become my wife and England's queen.
And you too, Somerset and Montague,
speak freely what you think.

CLARENCE:
Then this is my opinion: that King Lewis
will become your enemy, for mocking him
about the marriage of the Lady Bona.

GLOUCESTER:
And Warwick, doing what you gave him in charge,
is now dishonored by this new marriage.

KING EDWARD IV:
What if both Lewis and Warwick are appeased
by such an invention as I can devise?

MONTAGUE:
Yet, to have joined with France in such an alliance
would have more strengthened this our commonwealth
against foreign storms than any home-bred marriage.

HASTINGS:
Why, does Montague not know that England
is safe by itself, if it is true within itself?

MONTAGUE:
But it is safer when it is backed by France.

HASTINGS:
It is better to use France than to trust France.
Let us be backed with God and with the seas,
which He has given for an impregnable fence,
and with their help, we can defend ourselves alone;
in them and in ourselves our safety lies.

CLARENCE:
For this one speech, Lord Hastings well deserves
to have the heir of the Lord Hungerford.

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, what of that? It was my will and grant;
and for this once my will shall stand as law.

GLOUCESTER:
And yet I think your grace has not done well,
to give the heir and daughter of Lord Scales
to the brother of your loving bride;
she would have better fitted me or Clarence.
But in your bride you bury brotherhood.

CLARENCE:
Or else you would not have bestowed the heir
of the Lord Bonville on your new wife's son,
and leave your brothers to go and find their fortunes elsewhere.

KING EDWARD IV:
Alas, poor Clarence! Is it for a wife
that you are discontented? I will provide you with one.

CLARENCE:
In choosing for yourself, you showed your judgment,
which being shallow, you give me leave
to play the broker on my own behalf;
and to that end I shortly intend to leave you.

KING EDWARD IV:
Leave me, or stay, Edward will be king,
and he will not be tied to his brother's will.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
My lords, before it pleased his majesty
to raise my state to the title of a queen,
do me but right, and you must all confess
that I was not of ignoble descent;
and meaner people than myself have had a similar fortune.
But as this title honors me and mine,
so your dislike, to whom I would be pleasing,
clouds my joys with danger and with sorrow.

KING EDWARD IV:
My love, forbear to fawn upon their frowns.
What danger or what sorrow can befall you,
so long as Edward is your constant friend,
and their true sovereign, whom they must obey?
No, whom they shall obey, and love you too,
unless they seek for hatred at my hands;
which if they do, yet I will keep you safe,
and they shall feel the vengeance of my wrath.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, messenger, what letters or what news
from France?

Post:
My sovereign liege, no letters; and few words,
but such as I, without your special pardon,
dare not relate.

KING EDWARD IV:
Go to, we pardon you. Therefore, in brief,
tell me their words as near as you can guess them.
What answer does King Lewis make to our letters?

Post:
At my departure, these were his very words:
'Go and tell false Edward, your supposed king,
that Lewis of France is sending over masquers
to revel with him and his new bride.'

KING EDWARD IV:
Is Lewis so brave? Perhaps he thinks I'm Henry.
But what did Lady Bona say to my marriage?

Post:
These were her words, uttered with mad disdain:
'Tell him, in the hope that he'll become a widower shortly,
I'll wear the willow garland for his sake.'

KING EDWARD IV:
I don't blame her, she could say little less;
she had the wrong. But what did Henry's queen say?
For I have heard that she was there in her place.

Post:
'Tell him,' she said, 'my mourning weeds are done,
and I am ready to put on armor.'

KING EDWARD IV:
Perhaps she intends to play the Amazon.
But what did Warwick say to these injuries?

Post:
He, more incensed against your majesty
than all the rest, discharged me with these words:
'Tell him from me that he has done me wrong,
and therefore I'll uncrown him before long.'

KING EDWARD IV:
Ha! Did the traitor dare to breathe out such proud words?
Well, I will arm myself, being thus forewarned.
They shall have wars and pay for their presumption.
But say, is Warwick friends with Margaret?

Post:
Yes, gracious sovereign; they are so linked in
friendship
that young Prince Edward is marrying Warwick's daughter.

CLARENCE:
Perhaps the elder; Clarence will have the younger.
Now, brother king, farewell, and sit you fast,
for I will go from here to Warwick's other daughter;
so that, though I lack a kingdom, yet in marriage
I may not prove inferior to yourself.
You that love me and Warwick, follow me.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
Clarence and Somerset have both gone to Warwick!
Yet I am armed against the worst that can happen;
and haste is needful in this desperate case.
Pembroke and Stafford, you on our behalf,
go and levy men, and prepare for war;
they have already landed, or will quickly be landed.
I myself will follow you straight away.
But, before I go, Hastings and Montague,
resolve my doubt. You two, of all the rest,
are near to Warwick by blood and by alliance.
Tell me if you love Warwick more than me?
If it is so, then both of you depart to him;
I would rather have you as foes than as hollow friends.
But if you intend to hold your true obedience,
give me some assurance with a friendly vow,
so that I may never have you in suspicion.

MONTAGUE:
So may God help Montague as he proves true!

HASTINGS:
And Hastings as he favors Edward's cause!

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, brother Richard, will you stand by us?

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, in despite of all that shall withstand you.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, so! Then I am sure of victory.
Now therefore let us go from here; and lose no hour,
until we meet Warwick with his foreign power.
3 KING HENRY VI

WARWICK:
Trust me, my lord, all has gone well so far;
the common people are swarming to us in numbers.
But see where Somerset and Clarence are coming!
Speak suddenly, my lords, are we all friends?

CLARENCE:
Do not fear that, my lord.

WARWICK:
Then, gentle Clarence, welcome to Warwick;
and welcome, Somerset. I hold it cowardice
to be mistrustful where a noble heart
has pawned an open hand in a sign of love;
otherwise, I might think that Clarence, Edward's brother,
was but a feigned friend to our proceedings.
But welcome, sweet Clarence; my daughter shall be yours.
And now what remains but that, in the cover of night,
your brother being carelessly encamped,
his soldiers lurking in the towns about,
and attended by only a simple guard,
we may surprise and take him at our pleasure?
Our scouts have found the adventure to be very easy.
Just as Ulysses and the stout Diomedes
with sleight and manhood stole to Rhesus's tents,
and brought from there the Thracian fatal steeds,
so we, well covered with the night's black mantle,
may at unawares beat down Edward's guard
and seize him; I do not say, slaughter him,
for I only intend to surprise him.
You that will follow me in this attempt,
applaud the name of Henry with your leader.
Why, then, let's go on our way in a silent sort.
For Warwick and his friends, God and Saint George!
3 KING HENRY VI

First Watchman:
Come on, my masters, each man take his stand.
The king is now set down to sleep.

Second Watchman:
What, will he not go to bed?

First Watchman:
Why, no; for he has made a solemn vow
never to lie down and take his natural rest
until Warwick or himself is quite suppressed.

Second Watchman:
Tomorrow then, it is likely, will be the day,
if Warwick is as near as men report.

Third Watchman:
But say, I pray, what nobleman is that
that is resting here with the king in his tent?

First Watchman:
It's the Lord Hastings, the king's chiefest friend.

Third Watchman:
Oh, is it so? But why does the king command
that his chief followers lodge in towns about him,
while he himself keeps in the cold field?

Second Watchman:
It is the more honorable, because it is more dangerous.

Third Watchman:
Yes, but give me worship and quietness;
I like it better than a dangerous honor.
If Warwick knew in what state he stands,
it is to be doubted he would waken him.

First Watchman:
Unless our halberds shut up his passage.

Second Watchman:
Yes, why else do we guard his royal tent,
but to defend his person from night-foes?

WARWICK:
This is his tent; and see where his guard stands.
Courage, my masters! It's honor now or never!
But follow me, and Edward shall be ours.

First Watchman:
Who goes there?

Second Watchman:
Stay, or you die!

SOMERSET:
Who are they that are fleeing there?

WARWICK:
Richard and Hastings. Let them go; here is the duke.

KING EDWARD IV:
The duke! Why, Warwick, when we parted,
you called me king.

WARWICK:
Yes, but the case is altered.
When you disgraced me in my embassy,
then I degraded you from being king,
and I have come now to create you Duke of York.
Alas! How should you govern any kingdom,
you that do not know how to use ambassadors,
nor how to be contented with one wife,
nor how to use your brothers in a brotherly way,
nor how to study for the people's welfare,
nor how to shroud yourself from enemies?

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, brother of Clarence, are you here too?
No, then I see that Edward must needs go down.
Yet, Warwick, in despite of all misfortune,
of you yourself and all your accomplices,
Edward will always bear himself as king.
Though fortune's malice may overthrow my state,
my mind exceeds the compass of her wheel.

WARWICK:
Then, for his mind, let Edward be England's king.
But Henry now shall wear the English crown,
and be the true king indeed, you but the shadow.
My Lord of Somerset, at my request,
see that Duke Edward is forthwith conveyed
to my brother, the Archbishop of York.
When I have fought with Pembroke and his fellows,
I'll follow you, and tell you what answer
Lewis and the Lady Bona send to him.
Now, for a while, farewell, good Duke of York.

KING EDWARD IV:
What fates impose, that men must needs abide;
it is useless to resist both wind and tide.

OXFORD:
What now remains, my lords, for us to do
but to march to London with our soldiers?

WARWICK:
Yes, that's the first thing that we have to do;
to free King Henry from imprisonment
and see him seated on the regal throne.
3 KING HENRY VI

RIVERS:
Madam, what is the reason for this sudden change?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Why, brother Rivers, have you not yet learned
what late misfortune has befallen King Edward?

RIVERS:
What! The loss of some pitched battle against Warwick?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
No, but the loss of his own royal person.

RIVERS:
Then is my sovereign slain?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Yes, almost slain, for he has been taken prisoner,
either betrayed by the falsehood of his guard
or surprised by his foe unawares.
And, as I further have come to understand,
he has been newly committed to the Bishop of York,
the fell Warwick's brother, and by that, our foe.

RIVERS:
This news I must confess is full of grief;
yet, gracious madam, bear it as you may.
Warwick may lose, who has now won the day.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Until then, fair hope must hinder life's decay.
And I would rather wean myself from despair
for the love of Edward's offspring in my womb.
This is what makes me bridle my passion
and bear with mildness my misfortune's cross;
yes, yes, for this I draw in many a tear
and stop the rising of blood-sucking sighs,
lest with my sighs or tears I blast or drown
King Edward's fruit, the true heir to the English crown.

RIVERS:
But, madam, where has Warwick gone then?

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
I am informed that he is coming towards London,
to set the crown once more on Henry's head.
Guess the rest; King Edward's friends must go down.
But, to prevent the tyrant's violence—
for do not trust him that has once broken faith—
I'll go from here forthwith to the sanctuary,
to save at least the heir of Edward's right.
There I shall rest, secure from force and fraud.
Come, therefore, let us flee while we may flee.
If Warwick takes us, we are sure to die.
3 KING HENRY VI

GLOUCESTER:
Now, my Lord Hastings and Sir William Stanley,
stop wondering why I drew you here,
into this chiefest thicket of the park.
This is the case: you know our king, my brother,
is a prisoner to the bishop here, at whose hands
he has good usage and great liberty,
and, often but attended by a weak guard,
he comes hunting this way to disport himself.
I have informed him by secret means
that if about this hour he makes his way
under the color of his usual game,
he shall find his friends here with horses and men
to set him free from his captivity.

Huntsman:
This way, my lord; for this way lies the game.

KING EDWARD IV:
No, this way, man. See where the huntsmen stand.
Now, brother of Gloucester, Lord Hastings, and the rest,
are you standing so close to steal the bishop's deer?

GLOUCESTER:
Brother, the time and case require haste.
Your horse stands ready at the park corner.

KING EDWARD IV:
But where shall we go then?

HASTINGS:
To Lynn, my lord,
and from there, we'll take a ship to Flanders.

GLOUCESTER:
Well guessed, believe me; for that was my meaning.

KING EDWARD IV:
Stanley, I will repay your forwardness.

GLOUCESTER:
But why are we staying? This is no time to talk.

KING EDWARD IV:
Huntsman, what do you say? Will you go along?

Huntsman:
It's better to do so than to stay and be hanged.

GLOUCESTER:
Come then, away; let's have no more ado.

KING EDWARD IV:
Bishop, farewell. Shield yourself from Warwick's frown;
and pray that I may repossess the crown.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING HENRY VI:
Master lieutenant, now that God and my friends
have shaken Edward from the regal seat,
and turned my captive state to liberty,
my fear to hope, my sorrows to joys,
at my enlargement what are your due fees?

Lieutenant:
Subjects may challenge nothing of their sovereigns;
but if a humble prayer may prevail,
I then crave pardon of your majesty.

KING HENRY VI:
For what, lieutenant? For using me well?
No, be sure I'll repay your kindness well,
for it made my imprisonment a pleasure;
yes, such a pleasure as caged birds
conceive when, after many moody thoughts,
at last by notes of household harmony,
they quite forget their loss of liberty.
But, Warwick, after God, you set me free,
and chiefly therefore I thank God and you;
he was the author, you were the instrument.
Therefore, so that I may conquer fortune's spite
by living low, where fortune cannot hurt me,
and so that the people of this blessed land
may not be punished with my thwarting stars,
Warwick, although my head still wears the crown,
I hereby resign my government to you,
for you are fortunate in all your deeds.

WARWICK:
Your grace has always been famed for being virtuous;
and now you may seem as wise as you are virtuous,
by spying and avoiding fortune's malice,
for few men rightly temper with the stars.
Yet in this one thing let me blame your grace,
for choosing me when Clarence is in your place.

CLARENCE:
No, Warwick, you are worthy of the sway,
to whom the heavens in your nativity
adjudged an olive branch and a laurel crown,
as likely to be blessed in peace and war;
and therefore I yield you my free consent.

WARWICK:
And I choose Clarence only for protector.

KING HENRY VI:
Warwick and Clarence, give me both your hands.
Now join your hands, and with your hands your hearts,
so that no dissension may hinder the government.
I make you both protectors of this land,
while I myself will lead a private life
and in devotion spend my latter days,
to rebuke sin and to praise my Creator.

WARWICK:
What does Clarence answer to his sovereign's will?

CLARENCE:
That he consents, if Warwick yields his consent;
for on your fortune I repose myself.

WARWICK:
Why, then, though I am loath, yet I must be content.
We'll be yoked together, like a double shadow
to Henry's body, and we'll supply his place;
I mean, in bearing the weight of government,
while he enjoys the honor and his ease.
And, Clarence, now then it is more than needful
that Edward be pronounced a traitor forthwith,
and all his lands and goods be confiscated.

CLARENCE:
What else? And that the succession be determined.

WARWICK:
Yes, in that Clarence shall not want his part.

KING HENRY VI:
But, with the first of all your chief affairs,
let me entreat, for I command no more,
that Margaret your queen and my son Edward
be sent for, to return from France with speed;
for, until I see them here, by doubtful fear
my joy of liberty is half eclipsed.

CLARENCE:
It shall be done, my sovereign, with all speed.

KING HENRY VI:
My Lord of Somerset, what youth is that,
of whom you seem to have so tender a care?

SOMERSET:
My liege, it is young Henry, the Earl of Richmond.

KING HENRY VI:
Come here, England's hope.
If secret powers
suggest but truth to my divining thoughts,
this pretty lad will prove our country's bliss.
His looks are full of peaceful majesty,
his head is framed by nature to wear a crown,
his hand to wield a scepter, and himself
is likely in time to bless a regal throne.
Make much of him, my lords, for this is he
that must help you more than you are hurt by me.

WARWICK:
What news, my friend?

Post:
That Edward has escaped from your brother,
and has fled, as he has heard since, to Burgundy.

WARWICK:
Unsavoury news! But how did he escape?

Post:
He was conveyed by Richard, Duke of Gloucester,
and the Lord Hastings, who attended him
in a secret ambush on the forest side
and rescued him from the bishop's huntsmen;
for hunting was his daily exercise.

WARWICK:
My brother was too careless of his charge.
But let us go from here, my sovereign, to provide
a salve for any sore that may arise.

SOMERSET:
My lord, I do not like this flight of Edward's;
for doubtless Burgundy will give him help,
and we shall have more wars before long.
As Henry's late presaging prophecy
did gladden my heart with the hope of this young Richmond,
so my heart misgives me, in these conflicts,
what may befall him, to his harm and ours.
Therefore, Lord Oxford, to prevent the worst,
we will send him from here to Brittany forthwith,
until the storms of civil enmity are past.

OXFORD:
Yes, for if Edward repossesses the crown,
it's likely that Richmond, with the rest, shall go down.

SOMERSET:
It shall be so; he shall go to Brittany.
Come, therefore, let's get about it speedily.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, brother Richard, Lord Hastings, and the rest,
yet thus far fortune is making amends to us,
and says that once more I shall interchange
my waned state for Henry's regal crown.
We have passed well and now re-passed the seas
and brought the desired help from Burgundy.
What then remains, now that we have arrived
from Ravenspurgh haven before the gates of York,
but that we enter, as into our dukedom?

GLOUCESTER:
The gates are made fast! Brother, I don't like this;
for many men that stumble at the threshold
are well foretold that danger lurks within.

KING EDWARD IV:
Tush, man, abodements must not frighten us now.
By fair or foul means, we must enter in,
for our friends will repair to us here.

HASTINGS:
My liege, I'll knock once more to summon them.

Mayor:
My lords, we were forewarned of your coming,
and we shut the gates for the safety of ourselves;
for now we owe allegiance to Henry.

KING EDWARD IV:
But, master mayor, if Henry is your king,
yet Edward at the least is Duke of York.

Mayor:
True, my good lord; I know you for no less.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why, and I challenge nothing but my dukedom,
as I am well content with that alone.

GLOUCESTER:

HASTINGS:
Why, master mayor, why are you in doubt?
Open the gates; we are King Henry's friends.

Mayor:
Yes, you say so? The gates shall then be opened.

GLOUCESTER:
A wise, stout captain, and soon persuaded!

HASTINGS:
The good old man would gladly have all be well,
as long as it's not because of him. But once we're in,
I have no doubt that we shall soon persuade
both him and all his brothers to reason.

KING EDWARD IV:
So, master mayor. These gates must not be shut
except at night or in time of war.
What! Do not fear, man, but give me the keys;
for Edward will defend the town and you,
and all those friends that deign to follow me.

GLOUCESTER:
Brother, this is Sir John Montgomery,
our trusty friend, unless I am deceived.

KING EDWARD IV:
Welcome, Sir John! But why have you come in arms?

MONTAGUE:
To help King Edward in his time of storm,
as every loyal subject ought to do.

KING EDWARD IV:
Thanks, good Montgomery; but we now forget
our title to the crown and only claim
our dukedom until God is pleased to send the rest.

MONTAGUE:
Then fare you well, for I will go from here again.
I came to serve a king and not a duke.
Drummer, strike up, and let us march away.

KING EDWARD IV:
No, stay, Sir John, for a while, and we'll debate
by what safe means the crown may be recovered.

MONTAGUE:
What are you talking about debating? In a few words,
if you won't proclaim yourself our king here,
I'll leave you to your fortune and be gone
to keep back those who come to help you.
Why should we fight, if you pretend to have no title?

GLOUCESTER:
Why, brother, why do you stand on such nice points?

KING EDWARD IV:
When we grow stronger, then we'll make our claim.
Until then, it is wise to conceal our meaning.

HASTINGS:
Away with scrupulous wit! Now arms must rule.

GLOUCESTER:
And fearless minds climb soonest to crowns.
Brother, we will proclaim you out of hand.
The rumor of it will bring you many friends.

KING EDWARD IV:
Then let it be as you will; for it is my right,
and Henry but usurps the diadem.

MONTAGUE:
Yes, now my sovereign speaks like himself;
and now I will be Edward's champion.

HASTINGS:
Sound the trumpet; Edward shall be proclaimed here.
Come, fellow-soldier, you make the proclamation.

Soldier:
Edward the Fourth, by the grace of God, king of
England and France, and lord of Ireland, etc.

MONTAGUE:
And whoever gainsays King Edward's right,
by this I challenge him to a single fight.

All:
Long live Edward the Fourth!

KING EDWARD IV:
Thanks, brave Montgomery; and thanks to you all.
If fortune serves me, I'll repay this kindness.
Now, for this night, let's harbor here in York;
and when the morning sun shall raise his chariot
above the border of this horizon,
we'll go forward towards Warwick and his mates;
for I know well that Henry is no soldier.
Ah, froward Clarence! How ill it befits you
to flatter Henry and forsake your brother!
Yet, as we may, we'll meet both you and Warwick.
Come on, brave soldiers. Do not doubt the day,
and, that once gotten, do not doubt of large pay.
3 KING HENRY VI

WARWICK:
What is your counsel, lords? Edward from Belgia,
with hasty Germans and blunt Hollanders,
has passed safely through the narrow seas,
and with his troops is marching amain to London;
and many giddy people are flocking to him.

KING HENRY VI:
Let's levy men, and beat him back again.

CLARENCE:
A little fire is quickly trodden out;
which, if it is suffered, rivers cannot quench.

WARWICK:
In Warwickshire, I have true-hearted friends,
not mutinous in peace, yet bold in war;
those I will muster up. And you, son Clarence,
shall stir up in Suffolk, Norfolk, and in Kent,
the knights and gentlemen to come with you.
You, brother Montague, in Buckingham,
Northampton, and in Leicestershire, shall find
men well-inclined to hear what you command.
And you, brave Oxford, wondrously well-beloved,
in Oxfordshire shall muster up your friends.
My sovereign, with the loving citizens,
like to his island girt in with the ocean,
or modest Diana circled with her nymphs,
shall rest in London until we come to him.
Fair lords, take your leave and do not stand to reply.
Farewell, my sovereign.

KING HENRY VI:
Farewell, my Hector, and my Troy's true hope.

CLARENCE:
In a sign of truth, I kiss your highness's hand.

KING HENRY VI:
Well-minded Clarence, may you be fortunate!

MONTAGUE:
Comfort, my lord; and so I take my leave.

OXFORD:
And thus I seal my truth, and bid adieu.

KING HENRY VI:
Sweet Oxford, and my loving Montague,
and all at once, once more a happy farewell.

WARWICK:
Farewell, sweet lords. Let's meet at Coventry.

KING HENRY VI:
I will rest here at the palace for a while.
Cousin of Exeter, what does your lordship think?
I think the power that Edward has in the field
should not be able to encounter mine.

EXETER:
The doubt is that he will seduce the rest.

KING HENRY VI:
That's not my fear; my worth has gotten me fame.
I have not stopped my ears to their demands,
nor have I put off their suits with slow delays;
my pity has been a balm to heal their wounds,
my mildness has allayed their swelling griefs,
my mercy has dried their water-flowing tears;
I have not been desirous of their wealth,
nor have I much oppressed them with great subsidies,
nor have I been forward of revenge, though they have erred much.
Then why should they love Edward more than me?
No, Exeter, these graces challenge grace.
And when the lion fawns upon the lamb,
the lamb will never cease to follow him.

EXETER:
Hark, hark, my lord! What shouts are these?

KING EDWARD IV:
Seize the shame-faced Henry, and take him away;
and once again, proclaim us King of England.
You are the fount that makes small brooks to flow.
Now your spring stops; my sea shall suck them dry,
and swell so much the higher by their ebb.
Take him to the Tower; let him not speak.
And, lords, towards Coventry let us bend our course,
where the peremptory Warwick now remains.
The sun shines hot; and, if we use delay,
cold, biting winter will mar our hoped-for hay.

GLOUCESTER:
Let's go away early, before his forces join,
and take the great-grown traitor unawares.
Brave warriors, march quickly towards Coventry.
3 KING HENRY VI

WARWICK:
Where is the post that came from valiant Oxford?
How far from here is your lord, my honest fellow?

First Messenger:
By now, he is at Dunsmore, marching this way.

WARWICK:
How far off is our brother Montague?
Where is the post that came from Montague?

Second Messenger:
By now, he is at Daintry, with a powerful troop.

WARWICK:
Say, Somerville, what does my loving son say?
And, by your guess, how near is Clarence now?

SOMERSET:
I left him at Southam with his forces,
and I expect him here in about two hours.

WARWICK:
Then Clarence is at hand, I hear his drum.

SOMERSET:
It is not his, my lord; Southam lies here.
The drum your honor hears is marching from Warwick.

WARWICK:
Who could that be? It is likely, unexpected friends.

SOMERSET:
They are at hand, and you shall quickly know.

KING EDWARD IV:
Go, trumpet, to the walls, and sound a parley.

GLOUCESTER:
See how the surly Warwick mans the wall!

WARWICK:
Oh, unbidden spite! Has the sportful Edward come?
Where did our scouts sleep, or how were they seduced,
that we could hear no news of his repair?

KING EDWARD IV:
Now, Warwick, will you open the city gates,
speak gentle words, and humbly bend your knee,
call Edward king, and beg for mercy at his hands?
And he shall pardon you for these outrages.

WARWICK:
No, rather, will you draw your forces from here,
confess who set you up and who plucked you down,
call Warwick your patron and be penitent?
And you shall still remain the Duke of York.

GLOUCESTER:
I thought, at least, he would have said 'the king';
or did he make the jest against his will?

WARWICK:
Is not a dukedom, sir, a goodly gift?

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, by my faith, for a poor earl to give.
I'll do you service for so good a gift.

WARWICK:
It was I that gave the kingdom to your brother.

KING EDWARD IV:
Why then it is mine, if it is only by Warwick's gift.

WARWICK:
You are no Atlas for so great a weight.
And, weakling, Warwick takes his gift again;
and Henry is my king, Warwick is his subject.

KING EDWARD IV:
But Warwick's king is Edward's prisoner.
And, gallant Warwick, just answer this:
what is the body when the head is off?

GLOUCESTER:
Alas, that Warwick had no more foresight,
but, while he thought to steal the single ten,
the king was slyly taken from the deck!
You left poor Henry at the Bishop's palace,
and, ten to one, you'll meet him in the Tower.

EDWARD:
It is even so; yet you are Warwick still.

GLOUCESTER:
Come, Warwick, take the time; kneel down, kneel down.
No, when? Strike now, or else the iron will cool.

WARWICK:
I would rather chop this hand off at a blow,
and with the other fling it at your face,
than bear so low a sail, to strike to you.

KING EDWARD IV:
Sail how you can, have the wind and tide as your friend,
this hand, fast wound about your coal-black hair,
shall, while your head is warm and newly cut off,
write this sentence in the dust with your blood,
'Wind-changing Warwick now can change no more.'

WARWICK:
Oh, cheerful colors! See where Oxford comes!

OXFORD:
Oxford, Oxford, for Lancaster!

GLOUCESTER:
The gates are open, let us enter too.

KING EDWARD IV:
So other foes may set upon our backs.
Let us stand in good array; for they, no doubt,
will issue out again and bid us battle.
If not, the city being of but small defense,
we'll quickly rouse the traitors in the same.

WARWICK:
Oh, welcome, Oxford! For we want your help.

MONTAGUE:
Montague, Montague, for Lancaster!

GLOUCESTER:
You and your brother both shall pay for this treason
even with the dearest blood your bodies bear.

KING EDWARD IV:
The harder the match, the greater the victory.
My mind foresees happy gain and conquest.

SOMERSET:
Somerset, Somerset, for Lancaster!

GLOUCESTER:
Two of your name, both Dukes of Somerset,
have sold their lives to the house of York;
and you shall be the third if this sword holds.

WARWICK:
And lo, where George of Clarence sweeps along,
of force enough to challenge his brother to battle;
with whom an upright zeal for what is right prevails
more than the nature of a brother's love!
Come, Clarence, come; you will, if Warwick calls.

CLARENCE:
Father of Warwick, do you know what this means?
Look here, I throw my infamy at you.
I will not ruin my father's house,
who gave his blood to lime the stones together,
and set up Lancaster. Why, do you think, Warwick,
that Clarence is so harsh, so blunt, so unnatural,
as to bend the fatal instruments of war
against his brother and his lawful king?
Perhaps you will object to my holy oath.
To keep that oath would be more impious
than Jephthah's, when he sacrificed his daughter.
I am so sorry for my trespass made
that, to deserve well at my brother's hands,
I here proclaim myself your mortal foe,
with the resolution, wherever I meet you—
as I will meet you, if you stir abroad—
to plague you for your foul misleading of me.
And so, proud-hearted Warwick, I defy you,
and to my brother I turn my blushing cheeks.
Pardon me, Edward, I will make amends.
And, Richard, do not frown upon my faults,
for I will henceforth be no more inconstant.

KING EDWARD IV:
Now you are more welcome, and ten times more beloved,
than if you had never deserved our hate.

GLOUCESTER:
Welcome, good Clarence; this is brotherly.

WARWICK:
Oh, passing traitor, perjured and unjust!

KING EDWARD IV:
What, Warwick, will you leave the town and fight?
Or shall we beat the stones about your ears?

WARWICK:
Alas, I am not cooped up here for defense!
I will go away towards Barnet presently,
and challenge you to battle, Edward, if you dare.

KING EDWARD IV:
Yes, Warwick, Edward dares, and he leads the way.
Lords, to the field; Saint George and victory!
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
So, lie there. Die, you, and die our fear;
for Warwick was a bug that we all feared.
Now, Montague, sit fast; I am looking for you,
so that Warwick's bones may keep your company.

WARWICK:
Ah, who is near? Come to me, friend or foe,
and tell me who is the victor, York or Warwick?
Why do I ask that? My mangled body shows,
my blood, my lack of strength, my sick heart shows,
that I must yield my body to the earth
and, by my fall, the conquest to my foe.
Thus the cedar yields to the axe's edge,
whose arms gave shelter to the princely eagle,
under whose shade the ramping lion slept,
whose top-branch over-peered Jove's spreading tree
and kept low shrubs from the winter's powerful wind.
These eyes, that are now dimmed with death's black veil,
have been as piercing as the midday sun,
to search the secret treasons of the world.
The wrinkles in my brows, now filled with blood,
were often likened to kingly sepulchers;
for who lived as a king, but I could dig his grave?
And who dared to dig mine when Warwick bent his brow?
Lo, now my glory is smeared in dust and blood!
My parks, my walks, my manors that I had,
even now forsake me, and of all my lands
nothing is left to me but my body's length.
Why, what is pomp, rule, reign, but earth and dust?
And, live we how we can, yet we must die.

SOMERSET:
Ah, Warwick, Warwick! If you were as we are,
we might recover all our loss again.
The queen has brought a powerful army from France.
We just heard the news. Ah, if only you could fly!

WARWICK:
Why, then I would not flee. Ah, Montague,
if you are there, sweet brother, take my hand,
and with your lips keep my soul in for a while!
You do not love me; for, brother, if you did,
your tears would wash this cold, congealed blood
that glues my lips and will not let me speak.
Come quickly, Montague, or I am dead.

SOMERSET:
Ah, Warwick! Montague has breathed his last;
and to his last gasp he cried out for Warwick,
and said, 'Commend me to my valiant brother.'
And more he would have said, and more he spoke,
which sounded like a clamor in a vault,
that could not be distinguished; but at last
I could well hear, delivered with a groan,
'Oh, farewell, Warwick!'

WARWICK:
May his soul rest in peace! Fly, lords, and save yourselves;
for Warwick bids you all farewell, to meet in heaven.

OXFORD:
Away, away, to meet the queen's great power!
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
Thus far, our fortune keeps an upward course,
and we are graced with wreaths of victory.
But, in the midst of this bright, shining day,
I spy a black, suspicious, threatening cloud,
that will encounter our glorious sun,
before he attains his easeful western bed.
I mean, my lords, those powers that the queen
has raised in Gallia have arrived on our coast
and, as we hear, are marching on to fight with us.

CLARENCE:
A little gale will soon disperse that cloud
and blow it back to the source from which it came.
The very beams will dry those vapors up,
for every cloud does not engender a storm.

GLOUCESTER:
The queen's forces are valued at thirty thousand strong,
and Somerset, with Oxford, has fled to her.
If she has time to breathe, be well assured
her faction will be as strong as ours.

KING EDWARD IV:
We are informed by our loving friends
that they are holding their course toward Tewkesbury.
We, having now the best at Barnet field,
will go there straight away, for willingness makes the way easy;
and, as we march, our strength will be augmented
in every county as we go along.
Strike up the drum; cry 'Courage!' and away.
3 KING HENRY VI

QUEEN MARGARET:
Great lords, wise men never sit and wail their loss,
but cheerfully seek how to redress their harms.
What though the mast is now blown overboard,
the cable is broken, the holding-anchor is lost,
and half our sailors are swallowed in the flood?
Yet our pilot still lives. Is it fitting that he
should leave the helm and, like a fearful lad,
with tearful eyes add water to the sea
and give more strength to that which has too much,
while, in his moan, the ship splits on the rock,
which industry and courage might have saved?
Ah, what a shame! Ah, what a fault this would be!
Say Warwick was our anchor; what of that?
And Montague our topmast; what of him?
Our slaughtered friends the tackles; what of these?
Why, is not Oxford here another anchor?
And Somerset another goodly mast?
The friends of France our shrouds and tacklings?
And, though unskillful, why not Ned and I
for once be allowed the skillful pilot's charge?
We will not leave the helm to sit and weep,
but will keep our course, though the rough wind says no,
from shelves and rocks that threaten us with shipwreck.
It is as good to scold the waves as to speak to them fairly.
And what is Edward but a ruthless sea?
What is Clarence but a quicksand of deceit?
And what is Richard but a ragged, fatal rock?
All these are the enemies of our poor bark.
Say you can swim; alas, it is but for a while!
Tread on the sand; why, there you will quickly sink.
Bestride the rock; the tide will wash you off,
or else you will famish; that's a threefold death.
I speak this, lords, to let you understand,
in case some one of you would flee from us,
that there is no hoped-for mercy with the brothers
more than with ruthless waves, with sands and rocks.
Why, then, courage! What cannot be avoided,
it would be childish weakness to lament or fear.

PRINCE EDWARD:
I think a woman of this valiant spirit
should, if a coward heard her speak these words,
infuse his breast with magnanimity
and make him, naked, foil a man at arms.
I do not speak this as doubting anyone here,
for if I but suspected a fearful man,
he would have leave to go away betimes,
lest in our need he might infect another
and make him of a like spirit to himself.
If any such are here—as God forbid!—
let him depart before we need his help.

OXFORD:
Women and children of so high a courage,
and warriors faint! Why, it would be a perpetual shame.
Oh, brave young prince! Your famous grandfather
lives again in you. Long may you live
to bear his image and renew his glories!

SOMERSET:
And he that will not fight for such a hope,
let him go home to bed, and like the owl by day,
if he arises, be mocked and wondered at.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Thanks, gentle Somerset; sweet Oxford, thanks.

PRINCE EDWARD:
And take his thanks who yet has nothing else.

Messenger:
Prepare yourselves, lords, for Edward is at hand,
ready to fight; therefore, be resolute.

OXFORD:
I thought no less. It is his policy
to haste thus fast, to find us unprovided.

SOMERSET:
But he's deceived; we are in readiness.

QUEEN MARGARET:
This cheers my heart, to see your forwardness.

OXFORD:
We will pitch our battle here; we will not budge from here.

KING EDWARD IV:
Brave followers, yonder stands the thorny wood,
which, by the heavens' assistance and your strength,
must be hewn up by the roots before night.
I need not add more fuel to your fire,
for I know well you are blazing to burn them out.
Give the signal to the fight, and to it, lords!

QUEEN MARGARET:
Lords, knights, and gentlemen, what I should say
my tears gainsay; for every word I speak,
you see, I drink the water of my eyes.
Therefore, no more but this: Henry, your sovereign,
is a prisoner to the foe; his state is usurped,
his realm is a slaughter-house, his subjects are slain,
his statutes are cancelled, and his treasure is spent;
and yonder is the wolf that makes this spoil.
You fight in justice; then, in God's name, lords,
be valiant and give the signal to the fight.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
Now here is a period of tumultuous broils.
Away with Oxford to Hammes Castle straight.
For Somerset, off with his guilty head.
Go, take them away; I will not hear them speak.

OXFORD:
For my part, I'll not trouble you with words.

SOMERSET:
Nor I, but I will stoop with patience to my fortune.

QUEEN MARGARET:
So we part sadly in this troublesome world,
to meet with joy in sweet Jerusalem.

KING EDWARD IV:
Has a proclamation been made, that whoever finds Edward
shall have a high reward, and he his life?

GLOUCESTER:
It has. And look, where youthful Edward comes!

KING EDWARD IV:
Bring forth the gallant, let us hear him speak.
What! Can so young a thorn begin to prick?
Edward, what satisfaction can you make
for bearing arms, for stirring up my subjects,
and all the trouble you have put me to?

PRINCE EDWARD:
Speak like a subject, proud, ambitious York!
Suppose that I am now my father's mouth;
resign your chair, and where I stand, you kneel,
while I propose the very same words to you,
which you, a traitor, would have me answer to.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Ah, that your father had been so resolved!

GLOUCESTER:
So that you might still have worn the petticoat,
and never have stolen the breech from Lancaster.

PRINCE EDWARD:
Let Aesop tell fables on a winter's night;
his currish riddles do not sort with this place.

GLOUCESTER:
By heaven, brat, I'll plague you for that word.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, you were born to be a plague to men.

GLOUCESTER:
For God's sake, take away this captive scold.

PRINCE EDWARD:
No, take away this scolding crookback rather.

KING EDWARD IV:
Peace, willful boy, or I will charm your tongue.

CLARENCE:
Untutored lad, you are too impertinent.

PRINCE EDWARD:
I know my duty; you are all undutiful.
Lascivious Edward, and you, perjured George,
and you, misshapen Dick, I tell you all,
I am your better, traitors as you are.
And you usurp my father's right and mine.

KING EDWARD IV:
Take that, you likeness of this railer here.

GLOUCESTER:
Are you sprawling? Take that, to end your agony.

CLARENCE:
And there's for twitting me with perjury.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Oh, kill me too!

GLOUCESTER:
Yes, and I shall.

KING EDWARD IV:
Hold, Richard, hold; for we have done too much.

GLOUCESTER:
Why should she live, to fill the world with words?

KING EDWARD IV:
What, does she swoon? Use means for her recovery.

GLOUCESTER:
Clarence, excuse me to the king, my brother;
I'll go to London on a serious matter.
Before you come there, be sure to hear some news.

CLARENCE:
What? What?

GLOUCESTER:
The Tower, the Tower.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Oh Ned, sweet Ned! Speak to your mother, boy!
Can't you speak? Oh traitors! Murderers!
They that stabbed Caesar shed no blood at all,
did not offend, nor were not worthy of blame,
if this foul deed were by to equal it.
He was a man; this, in respect, was a child.
And men never spend their fury on a child.
What's worse than a murderer, that I may name it?
No, no, my heart will burst if I speak.
And I will speak, so that my heart may burst.
Butchers and villains! Bloody cannibals!
How sweet a plant have you untimely cropped!
You have no children, butchers! If you had,
the thought of them would have stirred up remorse.
But if you ever chance to have a child,
look in his youth to have him so cut off
as, death-men, you have rid this sweet young prince!

KING EDWARD IV:
Away with her; go, take her from here by force.

QUEEN MARGARET:
No, never take me from here, dispatch me here.
Here sheathe your sword, I'll pardon you for my death.
What, will you not? Then, Clarence, you do it.

CLARENCE:
By heaven, I will not do you so much ease.

QUEEN MARGARET:
Good Clarence, do; sweet Clarence, do you do it.

CLARENCE:
Did you not hear me swear I would not do it?

QUEEN MARGARET:
Yes, but you are used to forswearing yourself.
It was a sin before, but now it is charity.
What, will you not? Where is that devil's butcher,
hard-favored Richard? Richard, where are you?
You are not here. Murder is your alms-deed;
you never turn back petitioners for blood.

KING EDWARD IV:
Away, I say; I charge you, bear her hence.

QUEEN MARGARET:
So may it come to you and yours, as it has come to this Prince!

KING EDWARD IV:
Where has Richard gone?

CLARENCE:
To London, all in haste; and, as I guess,
to make a bloody supper in the Tower.

KING EDWARD IV:
He's sudden, if a thing comes into his head.
Now let us march from here. Discharge the common sort
with pay and thanks, and let's go away to London
and see how our gentle queen is faring.
By now, I hope, she has a son for me.
3 KING HENRY VI

GLOUCESTER:
Good day, my lord. What, are you so hard at your book?

KING HENRY VI:
Yes, my good lord—my lord, I should say rather;
it's a sin to flatter; 'good' was little better.
'Good Gloucester' and 'good devil' were alike,
and both preposterous; therefore, not 'good lord.'

GLOUCESTER:
Sirrah, leave us to ourselves; we must confer.

KING HENRY VI:
So the reckless shepherd flies from the wolf;
so first the harmless sheep yields its fleece
and next its throat to the butcher's knife.
What scene of death does Roscius have to act now?

GLOUCESTER:
Suspicion always haunts the guilty mind;
the thief fears every bush is an officer.

KING HENRY VI:
The bird that has been limed in a bush,
with trembling wings, mistrusts every bush;
and I, the hapless male to one sweet bird,
have now the fatal object in my eye
where my poor young was limed, was caught, and killed.

GLOUCESTER:
Why, what a peevish fool was that of Crete,
that taught his son the office of a fowl!
And yet, for all his wings, the fool was drowned.

KING HENRY VI:
I, Daedalus; my poor boy, Icarus;
your father, Minos, that denied our course;
the sun that seared the wings of my sweet boy,
your brother Edward, and yourself the sea,
whose envious gulf did swallow up his life.
Ah, kill me with your weapon, not with words!
My breast can better brook your dagger's point
than my ears can that tragic history.
But why have you come? Is it for my life?

GLOUCESTER:
Do you think I am an executioner?

KING HENRY VI:
