A persecutor, I am sure, you are.
If murdering innocents is executing,
why, then you are an executioner.

GLOUCESTER:
I killed your son for his presumption.

KING HENRY VI:
If you had been killed when you first presumed,
you would not have lived to kill a son of mine.
And thus I prophesy, that many a thousand,
who now mistrust no part of my fear,
and many an old man's sigh and many a widow's,
and many an orphan's water-standing eye—
men for their sons, wives for their husbands,
and orphans for their parents' untimely death—
shall rue the hour that you were ever born.
The owl shrieked at your birth—an evil sign;
the night-crow cried, portending a luckless time;
dogs howled, and a hideous tempest shook down trees;
the raven perched on the chimney's top,
and chattering magpies sang in dismal discords.
Your mother felt more than a mother's pain,
and yet brought forth less than a mother's hope,
to wit, an undigested and deformed lump,
not like the fruit of such a goodly tree.
You had teeth in your head when you were born,
to signify you came to bite the world.
And, if the rest is true that I have heard,
you came—

GLOUCESTER:
I'll hear no more. Die, you prophet, in your speech.
For this, among the rest, was I ordained.

KING HENRY VI:
Yes, and for much more slaughter after this.
God forgive my sins, and pardon you!

GLOUCESTER:
What, will the aspiring blood of Lancaster
sink into the ground? I thought it would have mounted.
See how my sword weeps for the poor king's death!
Oh, may such purple tears be always shed
from those that wish the downfall of our house!
If any spark of life is yet remaining,
down, down to hell; and say I sent you there.
I, that have neither pity, love, nor fear.
Indeed, it's true what Henry told me of;
for I have often heard my mother say
I came into the world with my legs forward.
Did I not have a reason, do you think, to make haste,
and seek their ruin that usurped our right?
The midwife wondered and the women cried,
'Oh, Jesus bless us, he is born with teeth!'
And so I was; which plainly signified
that I should snarl and bite and play the dog.
Then, since the heavens have shaped my body so,
let hell make my mind crooked to answer it.
I have no brother, I am like no brother;
and this word 'love,' which graybeards call divine,
may be resident in men that are like one another,
but not in me. I am myself alone.
Clarence, beware; you are keeping me from the light.
But I will sort out a dark day for you;
for I will buzz abroad such prophecies
that Edward shall be fearful of his life,
and then, to purge his fear, I'll be your death.
King Henry and the prince his son are gone.
Clarence, your turn is next, and then the rest,
counting myself but bad until I am the best.
I'll throw your body in another room
and triumph, Henry, on your day of doom.
3 KING HENRY VI

KING EDWARD IV:
Once more we sit on England's royal throne,
re-purchased with the blood of our enemies.
What valiant foemen, like autumn's corn,
have we mowed down, in the tops of all their pride!
Three Dukes of Somerset, threefold renowned
for being hardy and undoubted champions;
two Cliffords, the father and the son,
and two Northumberlands; two braver men
never spurred their horses at the trumpet's sound;
with them, the two brave bears, Warwick and Montague,
that in their chains fettered the kingly lion
and made the forest tremble when they roared.
Thus we have swept suspicion from our seat
and made our footstool of security.
Come here, Bess, and let me kiss my boy.
Young Ned, for you, your uncles and myself
have watched in our armors through the winter's night,
and have gone all on foot in the summer's scalding heat,
so that you might repossess the crown in peace;
and you shall reap the gain of our labors.

GLOUCESTER:

KING EDWARD IV:
Clarence and Gloucester, love my lovely queen;
and kiss your princely nephew, both of you.

CLARENCE:
The duty that I owe to your majesty
I seal upon the lips of this sweet babe.

QUEEN ELIZABETH:
Thanks, noble Clarence; worthy brother, thanks.

GLOUCESTER:
And, that I love the tree from which you sprang,
witness the loving kiss I give the fruit.

KING EDWARD IV:
Now I am seated as my soul delights,
having my country's peace and my brothers' loves.

CLARENCE:
What will your grace have done with Margaret?
Reignier, her father, to the king of France
has pawned the Sicilies and Jerusalem,
and they have sent it here for her ransom.

KING EDWARD IV:
Away with her, and waft her from here to France.
And now what remains but that we spend the time
with stately triumphs and mirthful comic shows,
such as befits the pleasure of the court?
Sound drums and trumpets! Farewell, sour annoyance!
For here, I hope, begins our lasting joy.

ARCHIDAMUS:
If you shall chance, Camillo, to visit Bohemia, on
a similar occasion to the one on which my services are now on
foot, you shall see, as I have said, a great
difference between our Bohemia and your Sicilia.

CAMILLO:
I think, this coming summer, the King of Sicilia
intends to pay Bohemia the visitation which he justly owes him.

ARCHIDAMUS:
In which our entertainment will shame us, but we will be
justified in our love; for indeed—

CAMILLO:
I beseech you,—

ARCHIDAMUS:
Verily, I speak it in the freedom of my knowledge:
we cannot with such magnificence—in so rare—I know
not what to say. We will give you sleepy drinks,
so that your senses, unintelligent of our insufficiency,
may, though they cannot praise us, as little accuse
us.

CAMILLO:
You pay a great deal too much for what is given freely.

ARCHIDAMUS:
Believe me, I speak as my understanding instructs me
and as my honesty puts it into words.

CAMILLO:
Sicilia cannot show himself over-kind to Bohemia.
They were trained together in their childhoods; and
there rooted between them then such an affection,
which cannot choose but branch out now. Since their
more mature dignities and royal necessities made a
separation of their society, their encounters,
though not personal, have been royally represented
with an interchange of gifts, letters, and loving
embassies; so that they have seemed to be together,
though absent, they shook hands, as if over a vast distance, and
embraced, as it were, from the ends of opposed
winds. May the heavens continue their love!

ARCHIDAMUS:
I think there is not in the world either malice or
matter to alter it. You have an unspeakable
comfort in your young prince Mamillius. He is a
gentleman of the greatest promise that ever came
into my notice.

CAMILLO:
I very well agree with you in the hopes for him. He
is a gallant child; one that indeed is a tonic to the
subject, and makes old hearts fresh. They that went on
crutches before he was born now desire their life to
see him a man.

ARCHIDAMUS:
Would they otherwise be content to die?

CAMILLO:
Yes; if there were no other excuse why they should
desire to live.

ARCHIDAMUS:
If the king had no son, they would desire to live
on crutches until he had one.

POLIXENES:
Nine changes of the watery star have been
the shepherd's note since we left our throne
without a burden. A time as long again
would be filled up, my brother, with our thanks;
and yet we should, for perpetuity,
go from here in debt. And therefore, like a cipher,
yet standing in a rich place, I multiply
with one 'We thank you' many thousands more
that go before it.

LEONTES:
Stay your thanks for a while,
and pay them when you part.

POLIXENES:
Sir, that's tomorrow.
I am questioned by my fears, of what may happen
or breed upon our absence; that may blow
no harsh winds at home, to make us say,
'This is put forth too truly.' Besides, I have stayed
to tire your royalty.

LEONTES:
We are tougher, brother,
than you can put us to.

POLIXENES:
I'll stay no longer.

LEONTES:
One week longer.

POLIXENES:
Truly, tomorrow.

LEONTES:
We'll split the time between us then; and in that,
I'll have no gainsaying.

POLIXENES:
Don't press me, I beseech you, so.
There is no tongue that moves, none, none in the world,
that could win me over as soon as yours. So it should now,
if there were a necessity in your request, although
it were needful that I denied it. My affairs
do even drag me homeward. To hinder them
would be a whip to me in your love; my stay
would be a charge and trouble to you. To save both,
farewell, our brother.

LEONTES:
Are you tongue-tied, our queen?
You speak.

HERMIONE:
I had thought, sir, to have held my peace until
you had drawn oaths from him not to stay. You, sir,
charge him too coldly. Tell him, you are sure
all is well in Bohemia; this satisfaction
the bygone day proclaimed. Say this to him,
he's beaten from his best defense.

LEONTES:
Well said, Hermione.

HERMIONE:
To tell him he longs to see his son, would be strong.
But let him say so then, and let him go;
but let him swear so, and he shall not stay,
we'll beat him away with distaffs.
Yet of your royal presence, I'll venture
to borrow a week. When you are at Bohemia,
you take my lord, I'll give him my commission
to let him stay there a month behind the appointed
time for his parting. Yet, good deed, Leontes,
I love you not a tick of the clock behind
what any lady-she loves her lord. You'll stay?

POLIXENES:
No, madam.

HERMIONE:
No, but you will?

POLIXENES:
I may not, truly.

HERMIONE:
Truly!
You are putting me off with feeble vows; but I,
though you would seek to un-sphere the
stars with oaths,
I would still say, 'Sir, no going.' Truly,
you shall not go. A lady's 'truly' is
as potent as a lord's. Will you go yet?
Force me to keep you as a prisoner,
not like a guest; so you shall pay your fees
when you depart, and save your thanks. What do you say?
My prisoner? Or my guest? By your dread 'truly,'
you shall be one of them.

POLIXENES:
Your guest, then, madam.
To be your prisoner would imply offending;
which is less easy for me to commit
than for you to punish.

HERMIONE:
Not your jailer, then,
but your kind hostess. Come, I'll question you
about my lord's tricks and yours when you were boys.
You were pretty little lords then?

POLIXENES:
We were, fair queen,
two lads that thought there was no more behind
but such a day tomorrow as today,
and to be a boy eternal.

HERMIONE:
Was not my lord
the more mischievous of the two?

POLIXENES:
We were like twinned lambs that did frisk in the sun,
and bleat at one another. What we changed
was innocence for innocence; we knew not
the doctrine of ill-doing, nor did we dream
that any did. If we had pursued that life,
and our weak spirits had never been reared higher
with stronger blood, we would have answered heaven
boldly 'not guilty'; the imposition
of hereditary sin cleared.

HERMIONE:
By this we gather
you have tripped since.

POLIXENES:
Oh, my most sacred lady!
Temptations have since then been born to us; for
in those unfledged days, my wife was a girl;
your precious self had then not crossed the eyes
of my young play-fellow.

HERMIONE:
Grace to boot!
Don't make any conclusion of this, lest you say
your queen and I are devils. Yet go on;
the offenses we have made you do, we'll answer for,
if you first sinned with us and if you
continued in your fault with us and didn't slip
with any but with us.

LEONTES:
Is he won over yet?

HERMIONE:
He'll stay, my lord.

LEONTES:
At my request, he would not.
Hermione, my dearest, you never spoke
to a better purpose.

HERMIONE:
Never?

LEONTES:
Never, but once.

HERMIONE:
What! Have I said well twice? When was it before?
I pray you tell me; cram me with praise, and make me
as fat as tame things. One good deed, dying without being spoken of,
slaughters a thousand that were waiting on it.
Our praises are our wages. You may ride me
a thousand furlongs with one soft kiss, before
we beat an acre with a spur. But to the goal:
my last good deed was to entreat his stay.
What was my first? It has an elder sister,
or I am mistaken. Oh, I wish her name were Grace!
But I spoke to the purpose once before. When?
No, let me have it; I long for it.

LEONTES:
Why, that was when
three crabbed months had soured themselves to death,
before I could make you open your white hand
and clap yourself my love. Then you did utter,
'I am yours forever.'

HERMIONE:
It is grace indeed.
Why, look you now, I have spoken to the purpose twice.
The one forever earned me a royal husband;
the other for some while, a friend.

LEONTES:

MAMILLIUS:
Yes, my good lord.

LEONTES:
By Jove!
Why, that's my boy. What, have you
smudged your nose?
They say it's a copy of mine. Come, captain,
we must be neat; not neat, but cleanly, captain.
And yet the steer, the heifer, and the calf
are all called neat.—Still tapping
on his palm!—What's up, you wanton calf!
Are you my calf?

MAMILLIUS:
Yes, if you will, my lord.

LEONTES:
You lack a rough patch and the horns that I have,
to be exactly like me. Yet they say we are
almost as alike as eggs; women say so,
who will say anything. But if they were as false
as over-dyed blacks, as wind, as waters, as false
as dice are to be wished for by one that fixes
no boundary between his and mine, yet it would be true
to say this boy is like me. Come, sir page,
look at me with your welkin eye. Sweet villain!
Most dearest! My own flesh and blood! Can your mother?—Could it be?—
Affection! Your intention stabs the center.
You make things possible that are not held to be so,
you communicate with dreams;—how can this be?—
with what's unreal you are co-active,
and you follow nothing. Then it is very credible
that you may co-join with something; and you do,
and that beyond commission, and I find it,
and that to the infection of my brains
and the hardening of my brows.

POLIXENES:
What does Sicilia mean?

HERMIONE:
He seems somewhat unsettled.

POLIXENES:
What, my lord!
What cheer? How is it with you, best brother?

HERMIONE:
You look as if you held a brow of much distraction.
Are you moved, my lord?

LEONTES:
No, in good earnest.
How sometimes nature will betray its folly,
its tenderness, and make itself a pastime
to harder bosoms! Looking on the lines
of my boy's face, I thought I recoiled
twenty-three years, and saw myself un-breeched,
in my green velvet coat, my dagger muzzled,
lest it should bite its master, and so prove,
as ornaments often do, too dangerous.
How like, I thought, I then was to this kernel,
this squash, this gentleman. My honest friend,
will you take eggs for money?

MAMILLIUS:
No, my lord, I'll fight.

LEONTES:
You will! Why, may happy man be his share! My brother,
are you as fond of your young prince as we
seem to be of ours?

POLIXENES:
If I am at home, sir,
he is all my exercise, my mirth, my matter,
now my sworn friend and then my enemy,
my parasite, my soldier, statesman, all.
He makes a July's day as short as a December,
and with his varying childness, he cures in me
thoughts that would thicken my blood.

LEONTES:
So stands this squire
officed with me. We two will walk, my lord,
and leave you to your graver steps. Hermione,
show how much you love us in our brother's welcome;
let what is dear in Sicily be cheap.
Next to yourself and my young rover, he is
apparent to my heart.

HERMIONE:
If you would seek us,
we are yours in the garden. Shall we attend you there?

LEONTES:
Dispose yourselves to your own inclinations. You'll be found,
if you are beneath the sky.
I am angling now,
though you don't perceive me, how I give line.
Go to, go to!
How she holds up her nose, her bill to him!
And arms herself with the boldness of a wife
to her allowing husband!
Gone already!
An inch-thick, knee-deep, over head and
ears a forked one!
Go, play, boy, play. Your mother is playing, and I
am playing too, but so disgraced a part, whose issue
will hiss me to my grave. Contempt and clamor
will be my knell. Go, play, boy, play.
There have been,
or I am much deceived, cuckolds before now;
and many a man there is, even at this present,
now while I speak this, holds his wife by the arm,
that little thinks she has been sluiced in his absence
and his pond fished by his next neighbor, by
Sir Smile, his neighbor. No, there's comfort in it
while other men have gates and those gates are opened,
like mine, against their will. Should all despair
that have revolted wives, the tenth of mankind
would hang themselves. There is no physic for it;
it is a bawdy planet, that will strike
where it is predominant; and it is powerful, think it,
from east, west, north, and south. Let it be concluded,
there is no barricade for a belly; know it;
it will let in and out the enemy
with bag and baggage. Many thousands of us
have the disease, and do not feel it. How now, boy!

MAMILLIUS:
They say I look like you.

LEONTES:
Why, that's some comfort. What, Camillo there?

CAMILLO:
Yes, my good lord.

LEONTES:
Go and play, Mamillius; you are an honest man.
Camillo, this great sir will yet stay longer.

CAMILLO:
You had much ado to make his anchor hold.
When you cast it out, it still came home.

LEONTES:
Did you notice it?

CAMILLO:
He would not stay at your petitions. He made
his business more important.

LEONTES:
Did you perceive it?
They are here with me already, whispering, rounding,
'Sicilia is a so-and-so.' It's far gone,
when I shall taste it last. How did it happen, Camillo,
that he stayed?

CAMILLO:
At the good queen's entreaty.

LEONTES:
Let it be at the queen's. 'Good' should be pertinent,
but, so it is, it is not. Was this taken
by any understanding mind but yours?
For your conceit is soaking, and will draw in
more than the common blocks. Is it not noted,
but by the finer natures? By some several
of extraordinary head-piece? Are the lower messes
perhaps purblind to this business? Say.

CAMILLO:
Business, my lord! I think most people understand
that Bohemia is staying here longer.

LEONTES:
Ha!

CAMILLO:
He is staying here longer.

LEONTES:
Yes, but why?

CAMILLO:
To satisfy your highness and the entreaties
of our most gracious mistress.

LEONTES:
Satisfy!
The entreaties of your mistress! Satisfy!
Let that suffice. I have trusted you, Camillo,
with all the things nearest to my heart, as well as
my chamber-councils, in which, like a priest, you
have cleansed my bosom, and I departed from you
as your reformed penitent. But we have been
deceived in your integrity, deceived
in that which seems so.

CAMILLO:
Let it be forbidden, my lord!

LEONTES:
To dwell on it, you are not honest, or,
if you are inclined that way, you are a coward,
which hamstrings honesty behind, restraining
it from the required course; or else you must be counted
a servant grafted in my serious trust
and therein negligent; or else a fool
that sees a game played home, the rich stake drawn,
and takes it all for a jest.

CAMILLO:
My gracious lord,
I may be negligent, foolish, and fearful;
in every one of these no man is free,
but that his negligence, his folly, and his fear,
among the infinite doings of the world,
sometime puts forth. In your affairs, my lord,
if ever I were willfully negligent,
it was my folly; if I industriously
played the fool, it was my negligence,
not weighing well the end; if I was ever fearful
to do a thing, where I doubted the issue,
where the execution cried out
against the non-performance, it was a fear
which often infects the wisest. These, my lord,
are such allowed infirmities that honesty
is never free of. But, I beseech your grace,
be plainer with me; let me know my trespass
by its own visage. If I then deny it,
it is none of mine.

LEONTES:
Haven't you seen, Camillo—
but that's past doubt, you have, or your eye-glass
is thicker than a cuckold's horn—or heard—
for to a vision so apparent, rumor
cannot be mute—or thought—for cogitation
does not reside in that man that does not think—
that my wife is slippery? If you will confess,
or else be impudently negative,
to have neither eyes nor ears nor thought, then say
my wife is a hobby-horse, and deserves a name
as rank as any flax-wench that puts out
before her troth-plight. Say it and justify it.

CAMILLO:
I would not be a stander-by to hear
my sovereign mistress so clouded, without
my present vengeance taken. I curse my heart,
you never spoke what became you less
than this; which to reiterate would be a sin
as deep as that, though true.

LEONTES:
Is whispering nothing?
Is leaning cheek to cheek? Is meeting noses?
Kissing with the inside lip? Stopping the career
of laughing with a sigh?—an infallible note
of breaking honesty—horsing foot on foot?
Skulking in corners? Wishing the clocks were more swift?
Hours, minutes? Noon, midnight? And all eyes
blind with the pin and web but theirs, theirs only,
that would be wicked unseen? Is this nothing?
Why, then the world and all that's in it is nothing;
the covering sky is nothing; Bohemia is nothing;
my wife is nothing; nor do these nothings have anything,
if this is nothing.

CAMILLO:
Good my lord, be cured
of this diseased opinion, and in time;
for it is most dangerous.

LEONTES:
Say it is, it's true.

CAMILLO:
No, no, my lord.

LEONTES:
It is; you lie, you lie.
I say you lie, Camillo, and I hate you,
I pronounce you a gross lout, a mindless slave,
or else a hovering temporizer, that
can with your eyes at once see good and evil,
inclining to them both. If my wife's liver were
as infected as her life, she would not live
for the running of one glass.

CAMILLO:
Who is infecting her?

LEONTES:
Why, he that wears her like a medal, hanging
about his neck, Bohemia. Who, if I
had true servants about me, that had eyes
to see my honor as well as their own profits,
their own particular thrifts, they would do that
which should undo more doing. Yes, and you,
his cupbearer—whom I from a meaner form
have benched and raised to worship, who can see
plainly as heaven sees earth and earth sees heaven,
how I am galled—might bespice a cup,
to give my enemy a lasting wink;
which draught would be a cordial to me.

CAMILLO:
Sir, my lord,
I could do this, and that with no rash potion,
but with a lingering dram that would not work
maliciously like poison. But I cannot
believe this crack to be in my dread mistress,
she being so sovereignly honorable.
I have loved you—

LEONTES:
Make that your question, and go rot!
Do you think I am so muddy, so unsettled,
to appoint myself in this vexation, to sully
the purity and whiteness of my sheets,
which to preserve is sleep, which being spotted
is goads, thorns, nettles, and the tails of wasps,
to give scandal to the blood of the prince my son,
who I do think is mine and whom I love as mine,
without a ripe moving to it? Would I do this?
Could a man so blench?

CAMILLO:
I must believe you, sir.
I do; and I will fetch off Bohemia for it;
provided that, when he's removed, your highness
will take again your queen as yours at first,
even for your son's sake; and thereby, for sealing
the injury of tongues in courts and kingdoms
known and allied to yours.

LEONTES:
You are advising me
even as I have set down my own course.
I'll give no blemish to her honor, none.

CAMILLO:
My lord,
go then; and with a countenance as clear
as friendship wears at feasts, keep with Bohemia
and with your queen. I am his cupbearer.
If he has a wholesome beverage from me,
do not account me as your servant.

LEONTES:
This is all.
Do it and you have one half of my heart;
don't do it, and you split your own.

CAMILLO:
I'll do it, my lord.

LEONTES:
I will seem friendly, as you have advised me.

CAMILLO:
Oh, miserable lady! But, for me,
what case am I in? I must be the poisoner
of good Polixenes; and my ground for doing it
is the obedience to a master, one
who, in rebellion with himself, will have
all that are his to be so too. To do this deed,
promotion follows. If I could find an example
of thousands that had struck anointed kings
and flourished after, I would not do it; but since
neither brass nor stone nor parchment bears a single one,
let villainy itself forswear it. I must
forsake the court. To do it, or not, is certain
to be a break-neck for me. Happy star, reign now!
Here comes Bohemia.

POLIXENES:
This is strange. I think
my favor here is beginning to warp. Not speaking?
Good day, Camillo.

CAMILLO:
Hail, most royal sir!

POLIXENES:
What is the news in the court?

CAMILLO:
None that is rare, my lord.

POLIXENES:
The king has such a countenance on him
as if he had lost some province and a region
loved as he loves himself. Even now I met him
with the customary compliment; when he,
wafting his eyes to the contrary and falling
a lip of much contempt, he speeds away from me and
so leaves me to consider what is breeding
that changes his manners thus.

CAMILLO:
I dare not know, my lord.

POLIXENES:
What! You dare not! You do not. Do you know, and you dare not?
Be intelligent to me. It's thereabouts;
for, to yourself, what you do know, you must,
and you cannot say, you dare not. Good Camillo,
your changed complexions are a mirror to me
which shows me mine changed too; for I must be
a party in this alteration, finding
myself thus altered with it.

CAMILLO:
There is a sickness
which puts some of us in distemper, but
I cannot name the disease; and it is caught
from you that are yet well.

POLIXENES:
What! Caught from me!
Don't make me sighted like the basilisk.
I have looked on thousands, who have fared the better
for my regard, but I have killed none so. Camillo—
as you are certainly a gentleman, and thereto
clerk-like and experienced, which no less adorns
our gentry than our parents' noble names,
in whose success we are gentle—I beseech you,
if you know anything which it would behoove my knowledge
to be informed of, do not imprison it
in ignorant concealment.

CAMILLO:
I may not answer.

POLIXENES:
A sickness caught from me, and yet I am well!
I must be answered. Do you hear, Camillo,
I conjure you, by all the parts of man
which honor does acknowledge, of which the least
is not this suit of mine, that you declare
what incidency of harm you do guess
is creeping toward me; how far off, how near;
which way to be prevented, if it can be;
if not, how best to bear it.

CAMILLO:
Sir, I will tell you;
since I am charged in honor and by him
that I think is honorable. Therefore, mark my counsel,
which must be followed as swiftly
as I mean to utter it, or both yourself and I
will cry 'lost,' and so good night!

POLIXENES:
Go on, good Camillo.

CAMILLO:
I have been appointed to murder you.

POLIXENES:
By whom, Camillo?

CAMILLO:
By the king.

POLIXENES:
For what?

CAMILLO:
He thinks, no, with all confidence he swears,
as if he had seen it or been an instrument
to entice you to it, that you have touched his queen
in a forbidden way.

POLIXENES:
Oh, then may my best blood turn
to an infected jelly and may my name
be yoked with his that did betray the Best!
Then may my freshest reputation turn to
a savor that may strike the dullest nostril
wherever I arrive, and may my approach be shunned,
no, hated too, worse than the greatest infection
that was ever heard or read of!

CAMILLO:
Swear his thought over
by each particular star in heaven and
by all their influences, you may as well
forbid the sea to obey the moon
as either by oath remove or by counsel shake
the fabric of his folly, whose foundation
is piled upon his faith and will continue
for the standing of his body.

POLIXENES:
How should this grow?

CAMILLO:
I do not know, but I am sure it is safer to
avoid what has grown than to question how it was born.
If, therefore, you dare trust my honesty,
that lies enclosed in this trunk which you
shall bear along as a pawn, go away tonight!
I will whisper to your followers about the business,
and I will, by twos and threes, at several posterns
clear them out of the city. For myself, I will put
my fortunes at your service, which are lost here
by this discovery. Do not be uncertain;
for, by the honor of my parents, I
have uttered the truth. If you seek to prove it,
I dare not stand by; nor shall you be safer
than one condemned by the king's own mouth, with his
execution sworn thereon.

POLIXENES:
I do believe you.
I saw his heart in his face. Give me your hand.
Be my pilot and your places shall
still be my neighbors. My ships are ready and
my people expected my departure from here
two days ago. This jealousy
is for a precious creature. As she is rare,
it must be great, and as his person is mighty,
it must be violent, and as he conceives
he is dishonored by a man who ever
professed to him, why, his revenges must
in that be made more bitter. Fear overshadows me.
May a good expedition be my friend, and may it comfort
the gracious queen, who is part of his theme, but nothing
of his ill-taken suspicion! Come, Camillo;
I will respect you as a father if
you bear my life off from here. Let us avoid this.

CAMILLO:
It is within my authority to command
the keys of all the posterns. Please your highness
to take the urgent hour. Come, sir, away.

HERMIONE:
Take the boy to you. He is troubling me so much,
it's past enduring.

First Lady:
Come, my gracious lord,
shall I be your playfellow?

MAMILLIUS:
No, I'll have none of you.

First Lady:
Why, my sweet lord?

MAMILLIUS:
You'll kiss me hard and speak to me as if
I were still a baby. I love you better.

Second Lady:
And why so, my lord?

MAMILLIUS:
Not because
your brows are blacker; yet black brows, they say,
become some women best, so that there is not
too much hair there, but in a semicircle
or a half-moon made with a pen.

Second Lady:
Who taught you this?

MAMILLIUS:
I learned it from women's faces. Please now,
what color are your eyebrows?

First Lady:
Blue, my lord.

MAMILLIUS:
No, that's a joke. I have seen a lady's nose
that has been blue, but not her eyebrows.

First Lady:
Listen.
The queen your mother is getting big. We shall
present our services to a fine new prince
one of these days; and then you would want to play with us,
if we would have you.

Second Lady:
She has spread out lately
into a goodly bulk. May a good time encounter her!

HERMIONE:
What wisdom is stirring amongst you? Come, sir, now
I am for you again. Please, sit by us,
and tell us a tale.

MAMILLIUS:
Shall it be merry or sad?

HERMIONE:
As merry as you will.

MAMILLIUS:
A sad tale is best for winter. I have one
of sprites and goblins.

HERMIONE:
Let's have that, good sir.
Come on, sit down. Come on, and do your best
to frighten me with your sprites; you're powerful at it.

MAMILLIUS:
There was a man—

HERMIONE:
No, come, sit down; then go on.

MAMILLIUS:
He lived by a churchyard. I will tell it softly;
those crickets over there shall not hear it.

HERMIONE:
Come on, then,
and tell it in my ear.

LEONTES:
Was he met there? His train? Camillo with him?

First Lord:
I met them behind the tuft of pines; I never
saw men scour so on their way. I watched them
even to their ships.

LEONTES:
How blessed I am
in my just censure, in my true opinion!
Alas, for lesser knowledge! How accursed
in being so blessed! There may be a spider
steeped in the cup, and one may drink, depart,
and yet partake of no venom, for his knowledge
is not infected. But if one presents
the abhorred ingredient to his eye, and makes known
how he has drunk, he cracks his gorge, his sides,
with violent hefts. I have drunk,
and seen the spider.
Camillo was his help in this, his pander.
There is a plot against my life, my crown;
all that is mistrusted is true. That false villain
whom I employed was pre-employed by him.
He has discovered my design, and I
remain a pinched thing; yes, a very trick
for them to play at will. How did the posterns
open so easily?

First Lord:
By his great authority;
which has often prevailed no less so
on your command.

LEONTES:
I know it too well.
Give me the boy. I am glad you did not nurse him.
Though he does bear some signs of me, yet you
have too much blood in him.

HERMIONE:
What is this? A sport?

LEONTES:
Take the boy away from here; he shall not come near her.
Away with him! And let her sport herself
with that she is pregnant with; for it is Polixenes
that has made you swell thus.

HERMIONE:
But I would say he had not,
and I'll be sworn you would believe my saying,
however you lean to the contrary.

LEONTES:
You, my lords,
look at her, mark her well. Just be about
to say, 'She is a goodly lady,' and
the justice of your hearts will add to that,
'It's a pity she's not honest and honorable.'
Praise her but for this, her outward form,
which, on my faith, deserves high speech, and straight away
the shrug, the 'hum' or 'ha,' these petty brands
that calumny does use—oh, I am out—
that mercy does, for calumny will sear
virtue itself. These shrugs, these 'hums' and 'ha's,'
when you have said, 'She's goodly,' come between
before you can say, 'She's honest.' But let it be known,
from him that has the most cause to grieve that it should be,
she's an adulteress.

HERMIONE:
If a villain were to say so,
the most complete villain in the world,
he would be that much more of a villain. You, my lord,
are but mistaken.

LEONTES:
You have mistaken, my lady,
Polixenes for Leontes. Oh, you thing!
Which I'll not call a creature of your place,
lest barbarism, making me the precedent,
should use a like language to all degrees
and leave out mannerly distinction
between the prince and the beggar. I have said
she's an adulteress; I have said with whom.
More, she's a traitor, and Camillo is
a confederate with her, and one that knows
what she should be ashamed to know herself
but with her most vile principal, that she is
a bed-swerver, even as bad as those
that common people give the boldest titles to, yes, and is privy
to this, their late escape.

HERMIONE:
No, by my life,
I am privy to none of this. How this will grieve you,
when you come to a clearer knowledge, that
you have thus published me! Gentle my lord,
you can hardly right me thoroughly then by saying
you were mistaken.

LEONTES:
No; if I am mistaken
in those foundations which I build upon,
the center is not big enough to bear
a school-boy's top. Away with her! To prison!
He who shall speak for her is as guilty
as if he had spoken.

HERMIONE:
There's some ill planet reigning.
I must be patient until the heavens look
with a more favorable aspect. Good my lords,
I am not prone to weeping, as our sex
commonly is; the lack of which vain dew
perhaps shall dry your pities. But I have
that honorable grief lodged here which burns
worse than tears can drown. I beseech you all, my lords,
with thoughts so qualified as your charities
shall best instruct you, measure me; and so
the king's will shall be performed!

LEONTES:
Shall I be heard?

HERMIONE:
Who is it that goes with me? I beseech your highness,
that my women may be with me; for you see
my plight requires it. Do not weep, good fools;
there is no cause. When you shall know your mistress
has deserved prison, then abound in tears
as I come out. This action I now go on
is for my better grace. Adieu, my lord.
I never wished to see you sorry; now
I trust I shall. My women, come; you have leave.

LEONTES:
Go, do our bidding; hence!

First Lord:
I beseech your highness, call the queen again.

ANTIGONUS:
Be certain what you do, sir, lest your justice
prove to be violence; in which three great ones will suffer,
yourself, your queen, and your son.

First Lord:
For her, my lord,
I would dare to lay down my life, and I will do it, sir,
if you please to accept it, that the queen is spotless
in the eyes of heaven and to you; I mean,
in this which you accuse her of.

ANTIGONUS:
If it proves
she's otherwise, I'll keep my stables where
I lodge my wife; I'll go in couples with her;
and when I feel and see her, I will trust her no farther;
for every inch of woman in the world,
yes, every dram of woman's flesh is false, if she is.

LEONTES:
Hold your peace.

First Lord:
Good my lord,—

ANTIGONUS:
It is for you we speak, not for ourselves.
You are being abused and by some instigator
that will be damned for it; I wish I knew the villain,
I would land-damn him. If she is honor-flawed,
I have three daughters; the eldest is eleven,
the second and the third are nine and some five;
if this proves true, they'll pay for it. By my honor,
I'll geld them all; they shall not see fourteen,
to bring forth false generations. They are co-heirs;
and I would rather castrate myself than that they
should not produce fair issue.

LEONTES:
Cease; no more.
You smell this business with a sense as cold
as a dead man's nose. But I do see it and feel it
as you feel doing this; and I see withal
the instruments that feel.

ANTIGONUS:
If it is so,
we need no grave to bury honesty.
There's not a grain of it to sweeten the face
of the whole dungy earth.

LEONTES:
What! Do I lack credit?

First Lord:
I would rather you lacked it than I, my lord,
upon this ground; and it would content me more
to have her honor true than your suspicion,
however you might be blamed for it.

LEONTES:
Why, what need do we have
to commune with you about this, but rather to follow
our forceful instigation? Our prerogative
does not call for your counsels, but our natural goodness
imparts this; which if you, either stupefied
or seeming so in skill, cannot or will not
relish a truth like us, inform yourselves
that we need no more of your advice. The matter,
the loss, the gain, the ordering of it, is all
properly ours.

ANTIGONUS:
And I wish, my liege,
you had only tried it in your silent judgment,
without more overture.

LEONTES:
How could that be?
Either you are most ignorant because of your age,
or you were born a fool. Camillo's flight,
added to their familiarity,
which was as gross as ever touched conjecture,
that only lacked sight, and nothing for approbation
but only seeing, all other circumstances
made up to the deed, pushes on this proceeding.
Yet, for a greater confirmation,
for in an act of this importance it would be
most piteous to be wild, I have dispatched in post
to sacred Delphos, to Apollo's temple,
Cleomenes and Dion, whom you know
to be of stuffed sufficiency. Now from the oracle
they will bring all; whose spiritual counsel, when I have it,
shall stop or spur me. Have I done well?

First Lord:
Well done, my lord.

LEONTES:
Though I am satisfied and need no more
than what I know, yet the oracle shall
give rest to the minds of others, such as he
whose ignorant credulity will not
come up to the truth. So we have thought it good
that she should be confined from our free person,
lest the treachery of the two who have fled from here
be left for her to perform. Come, follow us;
we are to speak in public; for this business
will raise us all.

ANTIGONUS:

PAULINA:
Call the keeper of the prison to him;
let him know who I am.
Good lady,
no court in Europe is too good for you;
what are you doing then in prison?
Now, good sir,
you know me, do you not?

Gaoler:
For a worthy lady,
and one whom I much honor.

PAULINA:
Please then,
conduct me to the queen.

Gaoler:
I may not, madam.
To the contrary, I have an express commandment.

PAULINA:
Here's a fuss,
to lock up honesty and honor from
the access of gentle visitors!
Is it lawful, please,
to see her women? Any of them? Emilia?

Gaoler:
If you please, madam,
to put aside these your attendants, I
shall bring Emilia forth.

PAULINA:
I pray you now, call her.
Withdraw yourselves.

Gaoler:
And, madam,
I must be present at your conference.

PAULINA:
Well, so be it, please.
Here's such a fuss to make no stain a stain
as passes coloring.
Dear gentlewoman,
how is our gracious lady?

EMILIA:
As well as one so great and so forlorn
may hold together. On her frights and griefs,
which no tender lady has borne greater,
she has delivered somewhat before her time.

PAULINA:
A boy?

EMILIA:
A daughter, and a goodly baby,
lusty and likely to live. The queen receives
much comfort in it; she says, 'My poor prisoner,
I am as innocent as you.'

PAULINA:
I dare be sworn.
These dangerous, unsafe fits of the king,
a plague on them!
He must be told of it, and he shall. The office
is best suited for a woman; I'll take it upon myself.
If I prove honey-mouthed, let my tongue blister
and never be the trumpet to my red-faced anger
any more. Please, Emilia,
commend my best obedience to the queen.
If she dares to trust me with her little baby,
I'll show it to the king and undertake to be
her advocate to the loudest. We do not know
how he may soften at the sight of the child.
The silence often of pure innocence
persuades when speaking fails.

EMILIA:
Most worthy madam,
your honor and your goodness are so evident
that your free undertaking cannot miss
a thriving issue. There is no lady living
so meet for this great errand. If it pleases your ladyship
to visit the next room, I'll presently
acquaint the queen of your most noble offer;
who just today was hammering on this design,
but dared not tempt a minister of honor,
lest she should be denied.

PAULINA:
Tell her, Emilia.
I'll use the tongue I have. If wit flows from it
as boldness does from my bosom, let it not be doubted
that I shall do good.

EMILIA:
Now may you be blessed for it!
I'll go to the queen. If you please,
come a little nearer.

Gaoler:
Madam, if it pleases the queen to send the baby,
I don't know what I shall incur by letting it pass,
having no warrant.

PAULINA:
You need not fear it, sir.
This child was a prisoner to the womb and is,
by the law and process of great nature,
freed and enfranchised from there, not a party to
the anger of the king nor guilty of,
if there is any, the trespass of the queen.

Gaoler:
I do believe it.

PAULINA:
Do not you fear. Upon my honor,
I will stand between you and danger.

LEONTES:
Neither night nor day, no rest. It is but weakness
to bear the matter thus; mere weakness. If
the cause were not in being—part of the cause,
she the adulteress; for the harlot king
is quite beyond my arm, out of the range
and level of my brain, plot-proof; but she
I can hook to me. Say that she were gone,
given to the fire, a moiety of my rest
might come to me again. Who's there?

First Servant:
My lord?

LEONTES:
How is the boy?

First Servant:
He took a good rest tonight;
it is hoped his sickness has been discharged.

LEONTES:
To see his nobleness!
Conceiving the dishonor of his mother,
he straightway declined, drooped, and took it deeply,
fastened and fixed the shame of it on himself,
threw off his spirit, his appetite, his sleep,
and downright languished. Leave me alone. Go,
see how he is faring.
Fie, fie! No thought of him.
The thought of my revenges in that way
recoils upon me. He is too mighty in himself,
and in his parties, his alliance; let him be
until a time may serve. For present vengeance,
take it on her. Camillo and Polixenes
laugh at me, and make their pastime at my sorrow.
They would not laugh if I could reach them, nor
shall she within my power.

First Lord:
You must not enter.

PAULINA:
No, rather, good my lords, be my second.
Do you fear his tyrannical passion more, alas,
than the queen's life? A gracious, innocent soul,
more free than he is jealous.

ANTIGONUS:
That's enough.

Second Servant:
Madam, he has not slept tonight; he has commanded
that no one should come to him.

PAULINA:
Not so hot, good sir.
I have come to bring him sleep. It is such as you,
that creep like shadows by him and sigh
at each of his needless heavings, such as you
that nourish the cause of his waking. I
have come with words as medicinal as they are true,
as honest as either, to purge him of that humor
that keeps him from sleep.

LEONTES:
What noise is there, ho?

PAULINA:
No noise, my lord; but a needful conference
about some gossips for your highness.

LEONTES:
How!
Away with that audacious lady! Antigonus,
I charged you that she should not come about me.
I knew she would.

ANTIGONUS:
I told her so, my lord,
on your displeasure's peril and on mine,
that she should not visit you.

LEONTES:
What, can you not rule her?

PAULINA:
From all dishonesty, he can. In this,
unless he takes the course that you have done,
and commits me for committing honor, trust it,
he shall not rule me.

ANTIGONUS:
Well now, you hear.
When she will take the rein, I let her run;
but she'll not stumble.

PAULINA:
Good my liege, I have come;
and, I beseech you, hear me, who professes
myself to be your loyal servant, your physician,
your most obedient counselor, yet that dare
less to appear so in comforting your evils,
than such as most seem yours. I say, I come
from your good queen.

LEONTES:
Good queen!

PAULINA:
Good queen, my lord,
good queen; I say good queen;
and I would make her good by combat, if I were
a man, the worst about you.

LEONTES:
Force her away.

PAULINA:
Let him that makes but trifles of his eyes
first touch me. On my own accord, I'll go;
but first, I'll do my errand. The good queen,
for she is good, has brought you a daughter;
here it is; she commends it to your blessing.

LEONTES:
Out!
A man-like witch! Take her away, out of the door!
A most intelligencing bawd!

PAULINA:
Not so.
I am as ignorant in that as you
in so entitling me, and no less honest
than you are mad; which is enough, I'll warrant,
as this world goes, to pass for honest.

LEONTES:
Traitors!
Will you not push her out? Give her the bastard.
You dotard! You are woman-tired, un-roosted
by your dame Partlet here. Take up the bastard;
take it up, I say; give it to your crone.

PAULINA:
May your hands be forever
unvenerable, if you
take up the princess by that forced baseness
which he has put upon it!

LEONTES:
He dreads his wife.

PAULINA:
So I wish you did; then it would be past all doubt
that you would call your children yours.

LEONTES:
A nest of traitors!

ANTIGONUS:
I am none, by this good light.

PAULINA:
Nor I, nor any
but one that's here, and that's himself, for he
betrays to slander the sacred honor of himself, his queen,
his hopeful son, and his baby;
whose sting is sharper than the sword's;
and will not—
for, as the case now stands, it is a curse
he cannot be compelled to it—once remove
the root of his opinion, which is as rotten
as ever an oak or stone was sound.

LEONTES:
A scold
of boundless tongue, who has recently beaten her husband
and now baits me! This brat is none of mine;
it is the issue of Polixenes.
Take it away, and together with its mother,
commit them to the fire!

PAULINA:
It is yours;
and, if we might lay the old proverb to your charge,
it is so like you, it is the worse. Behold, my lords,
although the print is little, the whole matter
and copy of the father, the eye, nose, lip,
the trick of his frown, his forehead, no, the valley,
the pretty dimples of his chin and cheek,
his smiles,
the very mold and frame of his hand, nail, and finger.
And you, good goddess Nature, which has made it
so like to him that got it, if you have
the ordering of the mind too, among all colors
put no yellow in it, lest she suspect, as he does,
her children are not her husband's!

LEONTES:
A gross hag!
And, worthless fellow, you are worthy to be hanged,
that will not stop her tongue.

ANTIGONUS:
Hang all the husbands
that cannot do that feat, you'll leave yourself
hardly one subject.

LEONTES:
Once more, take her away.

PAULINA:
A most unworthy and unnatural lord
can do no more.

LEONTES:
I'll have you burnt.

PAULINA:
I don't care.
It is a heretic that makes the fire,
not she who burns in it. I'll not call you a tyrant;
but this most cruel usage of your queen,
not being able to produce more accusation
than your own weak-hinged fancy, somewhat savors
of tyranny and will make you ignoble,
yes, and scandalous to the world.

LEONTES:
On your allegiance,
out of the chamber with her! If I were a tyrant,
where would her life be? She would not dare to call me so,
if she knew me to be one. Away with her!

PAULINA:
I pray you, do not push me; I'll be gone.
Look after your baby, my lord; it's yours.
May Jove send her
a better guiding spirit! What need are these hands for?
You, that are so tender over his follies,
will never do him good, not one of you.
So, so. Farewell; we are gone.

LEONTES:
You, traitor, have set your wife on to this.
My child? Away with it! Even you, that have
a heart so tender over it, take it from here
and see it instantly consumed with fire;
even you and none but you. Take it up straight.
Within this hour, bring me word that it is done,
and by good testimony, or I'll seize your life,
with whatever else you call yours. If you refuse
and will encounter my wrath, say so;
I will dash out the bastard's brains with these my own hands.
Go, take it to the fire;
for you set your wife on.

ANTIGONUS:
I did not, sir.
These lords, my noble fellows, if they please,
can clear me of it.

Lords:
We can, my royal liege.
He is not guilty of her coming here.

LEONTES:
You are all liars.

First Lord:
I beseech your highness, give us better credit.
We have always served you truly, and I beseech you
to so esteem of us, and on our knees we beg,
as recompense for our dear services
past and to come, that you change this purpose,
which, being so horrible, so bloody, must
lead on to some foul issue. We all kneel.

LEONTES:
I am a feather for every wind that blows.
Shall I live on to see this bastard kneel
and call me father? It would be better to burn it now
than to curse it then. But let it be; let it live.
It shall not either. You, sir, come here;
you that have been so tenderly officious
with Lady Margery, your midwife there,
to save this bastard's life—for it is a bastard,
as sure as this beard is gray—what will you venture
to save this brat's life?

ANTIGONUS:
Anything, my lord,
that my ability may undergo
and that nobleness may impose. At least this much:
I'll pawn the little blood which I have left
to save the innocent. Anything possible.

LEONTES:
It shall be possible. Swear by this sword
that you will perform my bidding.

ANTIGONUS:
I will, my lord.

LEONTES:
Mark and perform it, do you see! For the failure
of any point in it shall not only be
death to yourself but to your lewd-tongued wife,
whom for this time we pardon. We enjoin you,
as you are a liegeman to us, that you carry
this female bastard from here and that you take it
to some remote and desert place quite out
of our dominions, and that you leave it there,
without more mercy, to its own protection
and the favor of the climate. As by a strange fortune
it came to us, I do, in justice, charge you,
on the peril of your soul and the torture of your body,
that you commend it strangely to some place
where chance may nurse or end it. Take it up.

ANTIGONUS:
I swear to do this, though a present death
would have been more merciful. Come on, poor babe.
May some powerful spirit instruct the kites and ravens
to be your nurses! Wolves and bears, they say,
casting their savageness aside, have done
like offices of pity. Sir, may you be prosperous
in more than this deed requires! And may a blessing
fight against this cruelty on your side,
poor thing, condemned to loss!

LEONTES:
No, I'll not raise
another's issue.

Servant:
Please your highness, posts
from those you sent to the oracle have come
an hour since. Cleomenes and Dion,
being well arrived from Delphos, have both landed,
and are hastening to the court.

First Lord:
If it please you, sir, their speed
has been beyond account.

LEONTES:
They have been absent for twenty-three days.
It's good speed; it foretells that
the great Apollo will suddenly have
the truth of this appear. Prepare yourselves, lords;
summon a session, so that we may arraign
our most disloyal lady, for, as she has
been publicly accused, so shall she have
a just and open trial. While she lives,
my heart will be a burden to me. Leave me,
and think upon my bidding.

CLEOMENES:
The climate is delicate, the air most sweet,
the isle is fertile, and the temple much surpasses
the common praise it bears.

DION:
I shall report,
for it caught me most, the celestial habits,
I think I should call them so, and the reverence
of the grave wearers. Oh, the sacrifice!
How ceremonious, solemn, and unearthly
it was in the offering!

CLEOMENES:
But of all, the burst
and the ear-deafening voice of the oracle,
kin to Jove's thunder, so surprised my sense,
that I was nothing.

DION:
If the outcome of the journey
proves as successful to the queen—oh, may it be so!—
as it has been to us, rare, pleasant, and speedy,
the time is worth the use of it.

CLEOMENES:
Great Apollo,
turn all to the best! These proclamations,
so forcing faults upon Hermione,
I little like.

DION:
The violent carriage of it
will either clear or end the business. When the oracle,
thus sealed up by Apollo's great divine seal,
shall discover the contents, something rare
even then will rush to knowledge. Go, fresh horses!
And may the issue be gracious!

LEONTES:
This session, to our great grief we pronounce,
even pushes against our heart. The party being tried is
the daughter of a king, our wife, and one
too much beloved by us. Let us be cleared
of being tyrannical, since we so openly
proceed in justice, which shall have its due course,
even to the guilt or the purgation.
Produce the prisoner.

Officer:
It is his highness's pleasure that the queen
appear in person here in court. Silence!

LEONTES:
Read the indictment.

Officer:

HERMIONE:
Since what I am to say must be but that
which contradicts my accusation and
the testimony on my part is no other
but what comes from myself, it will scarcely benefit me
to say 'not guilty.' My integrity,
being counted as falsehood, shall, as I express it,
be so received. But thus: if divine powers
behold our human actions, as they do,
I do not doubt then but that innocence shall make
false accusation blush and tyranny
tremble at patience. You, my lord, best know,
who will least seem to do so, that my past life
has been as continent, as chaste, as true,
as I am now unhappy; which is more
than history can pattern, though it were devised
and played to take spectators. For behold me,
a fellow of the royal bed, who owes
a moiety of the throne, a great king's daughter,
the mother to a hopeful prince, standing here
to prate and talk for my life and honor before
whoever pleases to come and hear. For life, I prize it
as I weigh grief, which I would spare. For honor,
it is a derivative from me to mine,
and that is the only thing I stand for. I appeal
to your own conscience, sir. Before Polixenes
came to your court, how I was in your grace,
how I merited to be so; since he came,
with what uncurrent encounter have I
strained to appear thus? If one jot beyond
the bound of honor, or in act or will
that way inclining, may the hearts be hardened
of all that hear me, and may my nearest of kin
cry 'fie' upon my grave!

LEONTES:
I have never yet heard
that any of these bolder vices wanted
less impudence to gainsay what they did
than to perform it first.

HERMIONE:
That's true enough;
though it's a saying, sir, not due to me.

LEONTES:
You will not own it.

HERMIONE:
More than being the mistress of
that which comes to me in the name of a fault, I must not
acknowledge at all. As for Polixenes,
with whom I am accused, I do confess
I loved him as, in honor, he required,
with such a kind of love as might become
a lady like me, with a love even such,
so and no other, as you yourself commanded.
Which not to have done, I think, would have been in me
both disobedience and ingratitude
to you and to your friend, whose love had spoken,
ever since it could speak, from an infant, freely
that it was yours. Now, as for conspiracy,
I do not know how it tastes; though it is dished
for me to try how. All I know of it
is that Camillo was an honest man;
and why he left your court, the gods themselves,
knowing no more than I, are ignorant.

LEONTES:
You knew of his departure, as you know
what you have undertaken to do in his absence.

HERMIONE:
Sir,
you speak a language that I do not understand.
My life stands in the level of your dreams,
which I will lay down.

LEONTES:
Your actions are my dreams;
you had a bastard by Polixenes,
and I only dreamed it. As you were past all shame—
those of your kind are so—so you are past all truth.
To deny this concerns more than it avails; for as
your brat has been cast out, like itself,
with no father owning it—which is, indeed,
more criminal in you than in it—so you
shall feel our justice, in whose easiest passage
you should look for no less than death.

HERMIONE:
Sir, spare your threats.
The bug with which you would frighten me, I seek.
To me, life can be no commodity.
The crown and comfort of my life, your favor,
I do consider lost; for I do feel it is gone,
but I do not know how it went. My second joy
and the first-fruits of my body, from his presence
I am barred, like one who is infectious. My third comfort,
starred most unluckily, is from my breast,
the innocent milk in its most innocent mouth,
hauled out to be murdered. I myself am on every post
proclaimed a strumpet. With immodest hatred,
the child-bed privilege is denied me, which belongs
to women of all fashion; lastly, I have been hurried
here to this place, in the open air, before
I have regained my strength. Now, my liege,
tell me what blessings I have here alive,
that I should fear to die? Therefore, proceed.
But yet hear this: do not mistake me; no life,
I do not prize it a straw, but for my honor,
which I would free. If I shall be condemned
upon surmises, with all proofs sleeping else
but what your jealousies awaken, I tell you
it is rigor and not law. All your honors,
I do refer myself to the oracle.
May Apollo be my judge!

First Lord:
This, your request,
is altogether just. Therefore bring forth,
and in Apollo's name, his oracle.

HERMIONE:
The Emperor of Russia was my father.
Oh, that he were alive, and here beholding
his daughter's trial! That he did but see
the flatness of my misery, yet with eyes
of pity, not of revenge!

Officer:
You shall swear here upon this sword of justice,
that you, Cleomenes and Dion, have
both been at Delphos, and from there have brought
the sealed-up oracle, delivered by the hand
of great Apollo's priest; and that, since then,
you have not dared to break the holy seal
nor read the secrets in it.

CLEOMENES:
All this we swear.

LEONTES:
Break up the seals and read.

Officer:

Lords:
Now blessed be the great Apollo!

HERMIONE:
Praised!

LEONTES:
Have you read the truth?

Officer:
Yes, my lord; even so
as it is set down here.

LEONTES:
There is no truth at all in the oracle.
The session shall proceed. This is mere falsehood.

Servant:
My lord the king, the king!

LEONTES:
What is the business?

Servant:
Oh sir, I shall be hated for reporting it!
The prince your son, with mere conceit and fear
of the queen's speed, is gone.

LEONTES:
What! Gone!

Servant:
He is dead.

LEONTES:
Apollo is angry; and the heavens themselves
are striking at my injustice.
What's happening there!

PAULINA:
This news is mortal to the queen. Look down
and see what death is doing.

LEONTES:
Take her away from here.
Her heart is but overcharged; she will recover.
I have believed my own suspicion too much.
I beseech you, tenderly apply to her
some remedies for her life.
Apollo, pardon
my great profaneness against your oracle!
I'll reconcile myself to Polixenes,
I'll woo my queen again, I'll recall the good Camillo,
whom I proclaim a man of truth, of mercy;
for, being transported by my jealousies
to bloody thoughts and to revenge, I chose
Camillo for the minister to poison
my friend Polixenes; which would have been done,
but that the good mind of Camillo delayed
my swift command, though I with death and with
a reward did threaten and encourage him,
not doing it and it being done. He, most humane
and filled with honor, to my kingly guest
unclasped my practice, quit his fortunes here,
which you knew were great, and to the hazard
of all uncertainties himself commended,
no richer than his honor. How he glisters
through my rust! And how his pity
does make my deeds the blacker!

PAULINA:
Woe the while!
Oh, cut my lace, lest my heart, cracking it,
breaks too.

First Lord:
What fit is this, good lady?

PAULINA:
What studied torments, tyrant, do you have for me?
What wheels? Racks? Fires? What flaying? Boiling?
In lead or oils? What old or newer torture
must I receive, whose every word deserves
to taste of your most worst? Your tyranny,
working together with your jealousies,
fancies too weak for boys, too green and idle
for girls of nine, oh, think what they have done
and then run mad indeed, stark mad! For all
your by-gone fooleries were but spices of it.
That you betrayed Polixenes, it was nothing;
that did but show you, of a fool, to be inconstant
and damnably ungrateful. Nor was it much,
that you would have poisoned good Camillo's honor,
to have him kill a king. Poor trespasses,
more monstrous standing by, of which I reckon
the casting forth to crows your baby-daughter
to be either none or little; though a devil
would have shed water out of fire before he had done it.
Nor is it directly laid to you, the death
of the young prince, whose honorable thoughts,
thoughts high for one so tender, cleft the heart
that could conceive a gross and foolish father
had blemished his gracious mother. This is not, no,
laid to your answer. But the last—oh, lords,
when I have said, cry 'woe!'—the queen, the queen,
the sweetest, dearest creature is dead,
and vengeance for it
has not dropped down yet.

First Lord:
May the higher powers forbid it!

PAULINA:
I say she's dead; I'll swear it. If neither word nor oath
prevails, go and see. If you can bring
any color or luster to her lip, her eye,
any heat outwardly or breath within, I'll serve you
as I would do the gods. But, oh you tyrant!
Do not repent these things, for they are heavier
than all your woes can stir. Therefore, betake yourself
to nothing but despair. A thousand knees
for ten thousand years together, naked, fasting,
upon a barren mountain and in still winter
in a perpetual storm, could not move the gods
to look that way you were.

LEONTES:
Go on, go on.
You cannot speak too much; I have deserved
all tongues to talk their bitterest.

First Lord:
Say no more.
However the business goes, you have made a fault
in the boldness of your speech.

PAULINA:
I am sorry for it.
All the faults I make, when I come to know them,
I do repent. Alas! I have shown too much
the rashness of a woman. He is touched
to the noble heart. What's gone and what's past help
should be past grief. Do not receive affliction
at my petition; I beseech you, rather
let me be punished, for having reminded you
of what you should forget. Now, good my liege,
sir, royal sir, forgive a foolish woman.
The love I bore your queen—lo, I'm a fool again!—
I'll speak of her no more, nor of your children;
I'll not remind you of my own lord,
who is lost too. Take your patience to you,
and I'll say nothing.

LEONTES:
You spoke but well
when you spoke the most truth; which I receive much better
than to be pitied by you. Please, bring me
to the dead bodies of my queen and son.
One grave shall be for both. Upon them shall
the causes of their death appear, to
our perpetual shame. Once a day I'll visit
the chapel where they lie, and the tears I shed there
shall be my recreation. As long as nature
will bear up with this exercise, so long
I daily vow to use it. Come and lead me
to these sorrows.

ANTIGONUS:
You are certain then, that our ship has touched upon
the deserts of Bohemia?

Mariner:
Yes, my lord. And I fear
we have landed at an ill time. The skies look grimly
and threaten present blusters. In my conscience,
the heavens are angry with what we have in hand
and are frowning upon us.

ANTIGONUS:
May their sacred wills be done! Go, get aboard;
look to your ship. I'll not be long before
I call upon you.

Mariner:
Make your best haste, and don't go
too far into the land. It's likely to be loud weather;
besides, this place is famous for the creatures
of prey that live here.

ANTIGONUS:
Go away.
I'll follow instantly.

Mariner:
I am glad at heart
to be so rid of the business.

ANTIGONUS:
Come, poor baby.
I have heard, but not believed, that the spirits of the dead
may walk again. If such a thing is true, your mother
appeared to me last night, for never was a dream
so like a waking. A creature came to me,
sometimes her head on one side, sometimes on another;
I never saw a vessel of such sorrow,
so filled and so becoming. In pure white robes,
like very sanctity, she did approach
my cabin where I lay; she bowed three times before me,
and gasping to begin some speech, her eyes
became two spouts. The fury spent, she then
broke this from her: 'Good Antigonus,
since fate, against your better disposition,
has made you the person to throw out
my poor baby, according to your oath,
there are places remote enough in Bohemia,
there weep and leave it crying; and, because the baby
is counted lost forever, Perdita,
I pray you, call it. For this ungentle business
put upon you by my lord, you shall never see
your wife Paulina more.' And so, with shrieks,
she melted into the air. Much frightened,
I did in time collect myself and thought
this was so and no slumber. Dreams are toys.
Yet for this once, yes, superstitiously,
I will be squared by this. I do believe
Hermione has suffered death, and that
Apollo would, this being indeed the issue
of King Polixenes, have it laid here,
either for life or death, upon the earth
of its right father. Blossom, speed you well!
There you lie, and there your character; there these;
which may, if fortune pleases, both breed you, pretty,
and still remain yours. The storm begins; poor wretch,
that for your mother's fault are thus exposed
to loss and what may follow! I cannot weep,
but my heart bleeds; and I am most accursed
to be enjoined by oath to this. Farewell!
The day frowns more and more. You're likely to have
a lullaby too rough. I never saw
the heavens so dim by day. A savage clamor!
I may well get aboard! This is the chase.
I am gone for ever.

Shepherd:
I wish there were no age between sixteen and
twenty-three, or that youth would sleep out the
rest; for there is nothing in between but
getting wenches with child, wronging the ancientry,
stealing, and fighting—listen now! Would anyone but
these boiled brains of nineteen and twenty-two
hunt in this weather? They have scared away two of my
best sheep, which I fear the wolf will find sooner
than the master. If I have them anywhere, it's by
the seaside, browsing on ivy. Good luck, if it's your
will! What have we here! Mercy on us, a child! A very
pretty child! A boy or a girl, I wonder? A
pretty one; a very pretty one. Surely, some escapee.
Though I am not bookish, yet I can read
"waiting-gentlewoman" in the "escapee." This has been
some stair-work, some trunk-work, some
behind-door-work. They were warmer that got this
than the poor thing is here. I'll take it up for
pity. Yet I'll wait until my son comes; he was just
calling out. Whoa, ho, hoa!

Clown:
Hilloa, loa!

Shepherd:
What, are you so near? If you want to see something to talk
about when you are dead and rotten, come here. What
ails you, man?

Clown:
I have seen two such sights, by sea and by land!
But I am not to say it is a sea, for it is now the
sky. Between the firmament and it, you cannot thrust
a bodkin's point.

Shepherd:
Why, boy, how is it?

Clown:
I wish you could just see how it chafes, how it rages,
how it takes up the shore! But that's not the
point. Oh, the most piteous cry of the poor souls!
Sometimes to see them, and then not to see them; now the
ship is boring the moon with her main-mast, and then anon
it is swallowed with yeast and froth, as you would thrust a
cork into a hogshead. And then for the
land-service, to see how the bear tore out his
shoulder-bone; how he cried to me for help and said
his name was Antigonus, a nobleman. But to make an
end of the ship, to see how the sea flap-dragoned
it. But, first, how the poor souls roared, and the
sea mocked them; and how the poor gentleman roared
and the bear mocked him, both roaring louder than
the sea or the weather.

Shepherd:
In the name of mercy, when was this, boy?

Clown:
Now, now. I haven't blinked since I saw these
sights. The men are not yet cold under water, nor
is the bear half-dined on the gentleman. He's at it
now.

Shepherd:
I wish I had been there, to have helped the old man!

Clown:
I wish you had been by the ship's side, to have
helped her. There your charity would have lacked footing.

Shepherd:
Heavy matters! Heavy matters! But look here,
boy. Now bless yourself. You met with things
dying, I with things newborn. Here's a sight for
you; look here, a bearing-cloth for a squire's
child! Look here; take it up, take it up, boy;
open it. So, let's see. I was told I would be
rich by the fairies. This is some changeling.
Open it. What's within, boy?

Clown:
You're a made old man. If the sins of your youth
are forgiven you, you're well to live. Gold! All gold!

Shepherd:
This is fairy gold, boy, and it will prove to be so. Up
with it, keep it close. Home, home, the next way.
We are lucky, boy; and to be so still requires
nothing but secrecy. Let my sheep go. Come, good
boy, the next way home.

Clown:
You go the next way with your findings. I'll go see
if the bear has gone from the gentleman and how much
he has eaten. They are never angry except when they
are hungry. If there is any of him left, I'll bury
it.

Shepherd:
That's a good deed. If you can discern by what
is left of him what he is, fetch me to the
sight of him.

Clown:
Indeed, I will; and you shall help to put him in the ground.

Shepherd:
It's a lucky day, boy, and we'll do good deeds on it.

Time:
I, who please some and try all, both the joy and terror
of good and bad, who make and unfold error,
now take upon me, in the name of Time,
to use my wings. Do not impute it as a crime
to me or my swift passage, that I slide
over sixteen years and leave the growth untried
of that wide gap, since it is in my power
to overthrow law and in one self-born hour
to plant and overwhelm custom. Let me pass
as the same I am, before the most ancient order was
or what is now received. I witness to
the times that brought them in; so shall I do
to the freshest things now reigning and make stale
the glistering of this present, as my tale
now seems to it. With your patience allowing this,
I turn my glass and give my scene such growing
as if you had slept between. Leontes is leaving,
the effects of his fond jealousies so grieving
that he shuts himself up. Imagine me,
gentle spectators, that I may now be
in fair Bohemia, and remember well,
I mentioned a son of the king, whom I now name
Florizel to you; and with such speed I pace
to speak of Perdita, now grown in grace
equal with wondering. What ensues from her
I do not list to prophesy; but let Time's news
be known when it is brought forth.
A shepherd's daughter,
and what adheres to her, which follows after,
is the argument of Time. Of this, allow,
if you have ever spent time worse before now;
if never, yet Time himself says
he wishes earnestly you never may.

POLIXENES:
I pray you, good Camillo, don't be so importunate.
It's a sickness to deny you anything; a death to
grant this.

CAMILLO:
It has been fifteen years since I saw my country. Though
I have for the most part been abroad, I
desire to lay my bones there. Besides, the penitent
king, my master, has sent for me; to whose feeling
sorrows I might be some comfort, or I am overconfident to
think so, which is another spur to my departure.

POLIXENES:
As you love me, Camillo, don't wipe out the rest of
your services by leaving me now. The need I have of
you, your own goodness has made; it's better not to
have had you than to want you thus. You, having
made me businesses which no one without you can
sufficiently manage, must either stay to execute
them yourself or take away with you the very
services you have done; which if I have not enough
considered, as I cannot too much, to be more
thankful to you shall be my study, and my profit
in it the heaping of friendships. Of that fatal
country, Sicilia, please speak no more; the very
naming of it punishes me with the remembrance of that
penitent, as you call him, and reconciled king,
my brother; whose loss of his most precious queen
and children are even now to be freshly lamented.
Say to me, when did you see the Prince Florizel, my
son? Kings are no less unhappy, their issue not
being gracious, than they are in losing them when
they have approved their virtues.

CAMILLO:
Sir, it has been three days since I saw the prince. What
his happier affairs may be, are to me unknown. But I
have noticed, with concern, that he has of late much retired
from court and is less frequent in his princely
exercises than he has formerly appeared.

POLIXENES:
I have considered that much, Camillo, and with some
care; so far that I have eyes under my service which
look upon his removedness; from whom I have this
intelligence, that he is seldom away from the house of a
most homely shepherd; a man, they say, that from
very nothing, and beyond the imagination of his
neighbors, has grown into an unspeakable estate.

CAMILLO:
I have heard, sir, of such a man, who has a
daughter of most rare note. The report of her has
extended more than can be thought to begin from such a cottage.

POLIXENES:
That's also part of my intelligence; but, I
fear, it is the bait that is luring our son there. You
shall accompany us to the place; where we will, not
appearing as we are, have some questions with the
shepherd; from whose simplicity I think it will not be
uneasy to get the cause of my son's resort there.
Please, be my present partner in this business, and
lay aside the thoughts of Sicilia.

CAMILLO:
I willingly obey your command.

POLIXENES:
My best Camillo! We must disguise ourselves.

AUTOLYCUS:
When daffodils begin to peer,
with a hey! The doxy over the dale,
why, then comes in the sweet of the year;
for the red blood reigns in the winter's pale.
The white sheet bleaching on the hedge,
with a hey! The sweet birds, oh, how they sing!
It sets my thieving tooth on edge;
for a quart of ale is a dish for a king.
The lark, that sings "tirra-lyra,"
with a hey! With a hey! The thrush and the jay,
are summer songs for me and my aunts,
while we lie tumbling in the hay.
I have served Prince Florizel and in my time
wore three-pile velvet; but now I am out of service.
But shall I go mourn for that, my dear?
The pale moon shines by night,
and when I wander here and there,
I then do most go right.
If tinkers may have leave to live,
and bear the sow-skin budget,
then my account I may well give,
and in the stocks avouch it.
My trade is in sheets; when the kite builds, look to
lesser linen. My father named me Autolycus; who,
being, as I am, born under Mercury, was likewise
a snapper-up of unconsidered trifles. With dice and
drab I purchased this outfit, and my revenue is
the silly cheat. Gallows and knocks are too powerful
on the highway; beating and hanging are terrors to
me. As for the life to come, I sleep out the thought
of it. A prize! A prize!

Clown:
Let me see. Every eleven wethers produce a tod of wool; every tod
yields a pound and an odd shilling; fifteen hundred
are shorn. What does the wool come to?

AUTOLYCUS:

Clown:
I can't do it without counters. Let me see; what am
I to buy for our sheep-shearing feast? Three pounds
of sugar, five pounds of currants, rice—what will
this sister of mine do with rice? But my father
has made her mistress of the feast, and she is laying it
on. She has made me twenty-four nosegays for
the shearers, all of them three-man-song men, and very good
ones; but they are most of them means and bases; but
one puritan among them, and he sings psalms to
horn-pipes. I must have saffron to color the warden
pies; mace; dates?—none, that's out of my note;
seven nutmegs; a race or two of ginger, but that I
may beg; four pounds of prunes, and as many of
raisins of the sun.

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh, that I was ever born!

Clown:
In the name of me—

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh, help me, help me! Just pluck off these rags; and
then, death, death!

Clown:
Alas, poor soul! You have more need of rags to lay
on you, rather than to have these off.

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh sir, the loathsomeness of them offends me more
than the stripes I have received, which are mighty
ones and millions.

Clown:
Alas, poor man! A million beatings can amount to a
great matter.

AUTOLYCUS:
I have been robbed, sir, and beaten; my money and apparel
have been taken from me, and these detestable things have been put upon
me.

Clown:
What, by a horseman, or a footman?

AUTOLYCUS:
A footman, sweet sir, a footman.

Clown:
Indeed, he should be a footman by the garments he
has left with you. If this is a horseman's coat,
it has seen very hot service. Lend me your hand,
I'll help you. Come, lend me your hand.

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh, good sir, tenderly, oh!

Clown:
Alas, poor soul!

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh, good sir, softly, good sir! I fear, sir, my
shoulder-blade is out.

Clown:
How now! Can you stand?

AUTOLYCUS:

Clown:
Do you lack any money? I have a little money for you.

AUTOLYCUS:
No, good sweet sir; no, I beseech you, sir. I have
a kinsman not more than three-quarters of a mile from here,
to whom I was going; I shall have money there, or
anything I want. Offer me no money, I pray you;
that kills my heart.

Clown:
What kind of fellow was he that robbed you?

AUTOLYCUS:
A fellow, sir, that I have known to go about with
troll-my-dames; I knew him once as a servant of the
prince. I cannot tell, good sir, for which of his
virtues it was, but he was certainly whipped out of the court.

Clown:
His vices, you would say; there is no virtue whipped
out of the court. They cherish it to make it stay
there; and yet it will no more but abide.

AUTOLYCUS:
Vices, I would say, sir. I know this man well. He
has since been an ape-bearer; then a
process-server, a bailiff; then he compassed a
motion of the Prodigal Son, and married a tinker's
wife within a mile of where my land and living lies;
and, having flown over many knavish professions, he
settled only in being a rogue. Some call him Autolycus.

Clown:
Out upon him! A thief, for my life, a thief. He haunts
wakes, fairs, and bear-baitings.

AUTOLYCUS:
Very true, sir; he, sir, he; that's the rogue that
put me into this apparel.

Clown:
Not a more cowardly rogue in all of Bohemia. If you had
but looked big and spit at him, he would have run.

AUTOLYCUS:
I must confess to you, sir, that I am no fighter. I am
false of heart in that way; and that he knew, I warrant
him.

Clown:
How are you now?

AUTOLYCUS:
Sweet sir, much better than I was; I can stand and
walk. I will even take my leave of you, and pace
softly towards my kinsman's.

Clown:
Shall I bring you on the way?

AUTOLYCUS:
No, good-faced sir; no, sweet sir.

Clown:
Then fare you well. I must go and buy spices for our
sheep-shearing.

AUTOLYCUS:
May you prosper, sweet sir!
Your purse is not hot enough to purchase your spice.
I'll be with you at your sheep-shearing too. If I
don't make this cheat bring out another and the
shearers prove to be sheep, let me be unrolled and my name
put in the book of virtue!
Jog on, jog on, the foot-path way,
and merrily take the stile.
A merry heart goes all the day,
your sad one tires in a mile.

FLORIZEL:
These unusual weeds of yours, to each part of you,
give a life. You are not a shepherdess, but Flora
peering in the front of April. This sheep-shearing of yours
is like a meeting of the petty gods,
and you are the queen of it.

PERDITA:
Sir, my gracious lord,
it does not become me to chide at your extremes.
Oh, pardon me, that I name them! Your high self,
the gracious mark of the land, you have obscured
with a swain's wearing, and I, a poor lowly maid,
am pranked up most goddess-like. But that our feasts
in every mess have folly and the feeders
digest it with a custom, I would blush
to see you so attired, sworn, I think,
to show myself a glass.

FLORIZEL:
I bless the time
when my good falcon made her flight across
your father's ground.

PERDITA:
Now may Jove afford you cause!
To me, the difference forges dread. Your greatness
has not been used to fear. Even now I tremble
to think your father, by some accident,
should pass this way as you did. Oh, the Fates!
How would he look, to see his work so noble
vilely bound up? What would he say? Or how
should I, in these my borrowed flaunts, behold
the sternness of his presence?

FLORIZEL:
Apprehend
nothing but jollity. The gods themselves,
humbling their deities to love, have taken
the shapes of beasts upon them. Jupiter
