became a bull, and bellowed; the green Neptune
a ram, and bleated; and the fire-robed god,
golden Apollo, a poor, humble swain,
as I seem now. Their transformations
were never for a piece of beauty rarer,
nor in a way so chaste, since my desires
do not run before my honor, nor do my lusts
burn hotter than my faith.

PERDITA:
Oh, but, sir,
your resolution cannot hold, when it is
opposed, as it must be, by the power of the king.
One of these two must be necessities,
which will then speak, that you must
change this purpose,
or I my life.

FLORIZEL:
You dearest Perdita,
with these forced thoughts, I beg you, do not darken
the mirth of the feast. Either I'll be yours, my fair,
or not my father's. For I cannot be
my own, nor anything to anyone, if
I am not yours. To this I am most constant,
though destiny may say no. Be merry, gentle;
strangle such thoughts as these with anything
that you behold the while. Your guests are coming.
Lift up your countenance, as if it were the day
of celebration of that nuptial which
we two have sworn shall come.

PERDITA:
Oh, lady Fortune,
may you stand auspicious!

FLORIZEL:
See, your guests are approaching.
Address yourself to entertain them sprightly,
and let's be red with mirth.

Shepherd:
Fie, daughter! When my old wife lived, on
this day she was the pantler, the butler, and the cook,
both the dame and the servant; she welcomed all, and served all;
she would sing her song and dance her turn; now here,
at the upper end of the table, now in the middle;
on his shoulder, and his; her face was on fire
with labor and the thing she took to quench it,
she would sip with each one. You are retired,
as if you were a feasted one and not
the hostess of the meeting. Please, bid
these unknown friends to our welcome; for it is
a way to make us better friends, more known.
Come, quench your blushes and present yourself
as what you are, the mistress of the feast. Come on,
and bid us welcome to your sheep-shearing,
as your good flock shall prosper.

PERDITA:

POLIXENES:
Shepherdess,
you are a fair one—you fit our ages well
with flowers of winter.

PERDITA:
Sir, the year is growing ancient,
not yet on summer's death, nor on the birth
of trembling winter. The fairest
flowers of the season
are our carnations and streaked gillyvors,
which some call nature's bastards. Our rustic garden is
barren of that kind; and I do not care
to get slips of them.

POLIXENES:
Why, gentle maiden,
do you neglect them?

PERDITA:
Because I have heard it said
there is an art which, in their piedness, shares
with great, creating nature.

POLIXENES:
Say there is;
yet nature is not made better by any means
but those that nature makes. So, over that art
which you say adds to nature, is an art
that nature makes. You see, sweet maid, we marry
a gentler scion to the wildest stock,
and make a bark of a baser kind conceive
by the bud of a nobler race. This is an art
which mends nature, or rather changes it, but
the art itself is nature.

PERDITA:
So it is.

POLIXENES:
Then make your garden rich in gillyvors,
and do not call them bastards.

PERDITA:
I will not put
the dibble in the earth to set one slip of them;
no more than if I were painted, I would wish
this youth to say it was well, and only for that reason
desire to breed by me. Here are flowers for you;
hot lavender, mints, savory, marjoram;
the marigold, that goes to bed with the sun
and rises weeping with him. These are flowers
of middle summer, and I think they are given
to men of middle age. You're very welcome.

CAMILLO:
I would stop grazing, if I were of your flock,
and only live by gazing.

PERDITA:
Out, alas!
You would be so lean, that the blasts of January
would blow you through and through.
Now, my fairest friend,
I wish I had some flowers of the spring that might
become your time of day; and yours, and yours,
that still wear upon your virgin branches
your maidenheads growing. Oh Proserpina,
for the flowers now, that in your fright you let fall
from Dis's wagon! Daffodils,
that come before the swallow dares, and take
the winds of March with beauty; violets dim,
but sweeter than the lids of Juno's eyes
or Cytherea's breath; pale primroses
that die unmarried, before they can behold
bright Phoebus in his strength—a malady
most incident to maids; bold oxlips and
the crown imperial; lilies of all kinds,
the flower-de-luce being one! Oh, I lack these,
to make garlands of for you, and for my sweet friend,
to strew him over and over!

FLORIZEL:
What, like a corpse?

PERDITA:
No, like a bank for love to lie and play on;
not like a corpse; or if, not to be buried,
but quick and in my arms. Come, take your flowers.
I think I am playing as I have seen them do
in Whitsun pastorals. Surely this robe of mine
does change my disposition.

FLORIZEL:
What you do
still betters what is done. When you speak, sweet,
I would have you do it forever. When you sing,
I would have you buy and sell so, so give alms,
and pray so; and, for the ordering of your affairs,
to sing them too. When you dance, I wish you were
a wave of the sea, so that you might ever do
nothing but that; move still, still so,
and have no other function. Each of your doings,
so singular in each particular,
crowns what you are doing in the present deed,
so that all your acts are queens.

PERDITA:
Oh, Doricles,
your praises are too large. But that your youth,
and the true blood which peeps fairly through it,
do plainly give you out as an unstained shepherd,
with wisdom I might fear, my Doricles,
that you were wooing me the false way.

FLORIZEL:
I think you have
as little skill to fear as I have a purpose
to put you to it. But come; our dance, I pray.
Your hand, my Perdita. So turtles pair,
that never mean to part.

PERDITA:
I'll swear for them.

POLIXENES:
This is the prettiest low-born lass that ever
ran on the green-sward. Nothing she does or seems
but smacks of something greater than herself,
too noble for this place.

CAMILLO:
He is telling her something
that makes her blood look out. In good sooth, she is
the queen of curds and cream.

Clown:
Come on, strike up!

DORCAS:
Mopsa must be your mistress. Marry, garlic,
to mend her kissing with!

MOPSA:
Now, in good time!

Clown:
Not a word, not a word; we stand on our manners.
Come, strike up!

POLIXENES:
Pray, good shepherd, what fair swain is this
that dances with your daughter?

Shepherd:
They call him Doricles; and he boasts
of having a worthy feeding. But I have it
on his own report, and I believe it;
he looks like the truth. He says he loves my daughter.
I think so too; for the moon has never gazed
upon the water as he will stand and read,
as it were, my daughter's eyes. And, to be plain,
I think there is not half a kiss to choose
who loves the other best.

POLIXENES:
She dances featly.

Shepherd:
So she does everything; though I report it,
that should be silent. If young Doricles
does light upon her, she shall bring him that
which he does not dream of.

Servant:
Oh master, if you only heard the peddler at the
door, you would never dance again after a tabor and
pipe; no, the bagpipe could not move you. He sings
several tunes faster than you'll count money; he
utters them as if he had eaten ballads, and all men's
ears grew to his tunes.

Clown:
He could never have come at a better time; he shall come in. I
love a ballad but all too well, if it is a doleful
matter merrily set down, or a very pleasant thing
indeed and sung lamentably.

Servant:
He has songs for men or women, of all sizes; no
milliner can so fit his customers with gloves. He
has the prettiest love-songs for maids; so without
bawdry, which is strange; with such delicate
burdens of 'dildos' and 'fadings,' 'jump her and thump
her'; and where some stretch-mouthed rascal would,
as it were, mean mischief and break a foul gap into
the matter, he makes the maid answer, 'Whoop, do me
no harm, good man'; he puts him off, slights him, with
'Whoop, do me no harm, good man.'

POLIXENES:
This is a brave fellow.

Clown:
Believe me, you are talking about an admirable, conceited
fellow. Does he have any unbraided wares?

Servant:
He has ribbons of all the colors in the rainbow;
points more than all the lawyers in Bohemia can
learnedly handle, though they come to him by the
gross; inkles, caddisses, cambrics, lawns. Why, he
sings them over as if they were gods or goddesses; you
would think a smock were a she-angel, he so chants
to the sleeve-hand and the work about the square on it.

Clown:
Please bring him in, and let him approach singing.

PERDITA:
Forewarn him that he use no scurrilous words in his tunes.

Clown:
You have some of these peddlers, that have more in them
than you'd think, sister.

PERDITA:
Yes, good brother, or go about to think.

AUTOLYCUS:
Lawn as white as driven snow;
cypress black as a crow ever was;
gloves as sweet as damask roses;
masks for faces and for noses;
bugle bracelet, necklace amber,
perfume for a lady's chamber;
golden quoifs and stomachers,
for my lads to give their dears.
Pins and poking-sticks of steel,
what maids lack from head to heel.
Come buy of me, come; come buy, come buy;
buy lads, or else your lasses will cry. Come buy.

Clown:
If I were not in love with Mopsa, you would take
no money from me; but being enthralled as I am, it
will also be the bondage of certain ribbons and gloves.

MOPSA:
I was promised them for the feast; but they are not
coming too late now.

DORCAS:
He has promised you more than that, or there are liars.

MOPSA:
He has paid you all he promised you; maybe he has
paid you more, which will shame you to give him again.

Clown:
Are there no manners left among maids? Will they
wear their plackets where they should wear their
faces? Is there not a milking-time, when you are
going to bed, or a kiln-hole, to whistle off these
secrets, but you must be tittle-tattling before all
our guests? It's well they are whispering. Clamor
your tongues, and not a word more.

MOPSA:
I am done. Come, you promised me a tawdry-lace
and a pair of sweet gloves.

Clown:
Haven't I told you how I was tricked on the way
and lost all my money?

AUTOLYCUS:
And indeed, sir, there are swindlers abroad;
therefore it behooves men to be wary.

Clown:
Don't you worry, man, you'll lose nothing here.

AUTOLYCUS:
I hope so, sir; for I have many parcels of value about me.

Clown:
What do you have here? Ballads?

MOPSA:
Please now, buy some. I love a ballad in print, on my
life, for then we are sure they are true.

AUTOLYCUS:
Here's one to a very doleful tune, about how a usurer's
wife was brought to bed of twenty money-bags at one
birth, and how she longed to eat adders' heads and
toads that had been carbonadoed.

MOPSA:
Is it true, do you think?

AUTOLYCUS:
Very true, and but a month old.

DORCAS:
Bless me from marrying a usurer!

AUTOLYCUS:
Here's the midwife's name to it, one Mistress
Tale-porter, and five or six honest wives that were
present. Why should I carry lies abroad?

MOPSA:
Please now, buy it.

Clown:
Come on, lay it by. And let's first see more
ballads; we'll buy the other things later.

AUTOLYCUS:
Here's another ballad about a fish, that appeared on
the coast on Wednesday the eightieth of April,
forty thousand fathoms above water, and sang this
ballad against the hard hearts of maids. It was
thought she was a woman and was turned into a cold
fish because she would not exchange flesh with one that
loved her. The ballad is very pitiful and as true.

DORCAS:
Is it true too, do you think?

AUTOLYCUS:
I have five justices' hands on it, and more witnesses than
my pack will hold.

Clown:
Lay it by too. Another.

AUTOLYCUS:
This is a merry ballad, but a very pretty one.

MOPSA:
Let's have some merry ones.

AUTOLYCUS:
Why, this is a very merry one and it goes to
the tune of 'Two maids wooing a man.' There's
scarce a maid westward but she sings it; it's in
request, I can tell you.

MOPSA:
We can both sing it. If you'll sing a part, you
shall hear; it's in three parts.

DORCAS:
We had the tune of it a month ago.

AUTOLYCUS:
I can bear my part; you must know it's my
occupation. I'll have at it with you.

AUTOLYCUS:
Get you away from here, for I must go
where it is not fitting for you to know.

DORCAS:
Where to?

MOPSA:
Oh, where to?

DORCAS:
Where to?

MOPSA:
It becomes your oath full well,
for you to tell me your secrets.

DORCAS:
Me too, let me go there.

MOPSA:
Or you are going to the orange or the mill.

DORCAS:
If to either, you do ill.

AUTOLYCUS:
Neither.

DORCAS:
What, neither?

AUTOLYCUS:
Neither.

DORCAS:
You have sworn to be my love.

MOPSA:
You have sworn it more to me.
Then where are you going? Say, where?

Clown:
We'll have this song out later by ourselves. My
father and the gentlemen are in a sad talk, and we'll
not trouble them. Come, bring your pack after
me. Wenches, I'll buy for you both. Peddler, let's
have the first choice. Follow me, girls.

AUTOLYCUS:
And you shall pay well for them.
Will you buy any tape,
or lace for your cape,
my dainty duck, my dear-a?
Any silk, any thread,
any toys for your head,
of the newest and finest, finest wear-a?
Come to the peddler;
money's a meddler,
that does utter all men's ware-a.

Servant:
Master, there are three carters, three shepherds,
three neat-herds, and three swine-herds, who have made
themselves all into men of hair. They call themselves
Saltiers, and they have a dance which the wenches
say is a hodgepodge of gambols, because they are
not in it. But they themselves are of the mind that, if it
is not too rough for some that know little but
bowling, it will please plentifully.

Shepherd:
Away! We'll have none of it. There has been too much
homely foolery already. I know, sir, we are wearying you.

POLIXENES:
You weary those that refresh us. Please, let's see
these four groups of three herdsmen.

Servant:
One group of three of them, by their own report, sir, has
danced before the king; and not the worst of the
three but can jump twelve and a half feet by the square.

Shepherd:
Leave your prating. Since these good men are
pleased, let them come in; but quickly now.

Servant:
Why, they are waiting at the door, sir.

POLIXENES:
Oh, father, you'll know more about that hereafter.
Is it not too far gone? It's time to part them.
He's simple and tells a lot.
How now, fair shepherd!
Your heart is full of something that is taking
your mind from feasting. In truth, when I was young
and handled love as you do, I was accustomed
to load my girl with knick-knacks. I would have ransacked
the pedlar's silken treasury and poured it
for her acceptance. You have let him go
and have not bought anything from him. If your lass
should misinterpret and call this
your lack of love or bounty, you would be hard-pressed
for a reply, at least if you care
about happily holding her.

FLORIZEL:
Old sir, I know
she does not prize such trifles as these are.
The gifts she looks for from me are packed and locked
up in my heart; which I have given her already,
but have not delivered. Oh, hear me breathe my life
before this ancient sir, who, it should seem,
has sometime loved! I take your hand, this hand,
as soft as a dove's down and as white as it,
or an Ethiopian's tooth, or the fanned
snow that is bolted
by the northern blasts twice over.

POLIXENES:
What follows this?
How prettily the young swain seems to wash
the hand that was fair before! I have put you out.
But to your protestation; let me hear
what you profess.

FLORIZEL:
Do so, and be a witness to it.

POLIXENES:
And this neighbor of mine too?

FLORIZEL:
And he, and more
than he, and men, the earth, the heavens, and all.
That, if I were crowned the most imperial monarch,
and the most worthy of it, if I were the fairest youth
that ever made an eye swerve, if I had the force and knowledge
more than was ever man's, I would not prize them
without her love; for her, I would employ them all;
I would commend them and condemn them to her service
or to their own perdition.

POLIXENES:
Fairly offered.

CAMILLO:
This shows a sound affection.

Shepherd:
But, my daughter,
do you say the same to him?

PERDITA:
I cannot speak
so well, not nearly so well; no, nor do I mean any better.
By the pattern of my own thoughts, I cut out
the purity of his.

Shepherd:
Take hands, it's a bargain!
And, unknown friends, you shall bear witness to it.
I give my daughter to him, and I will make
her portion equal to his.

FLORIZEL:
Oh, that must be
in the virtue of your daughter. One being dead,
I shall have more than you can dream of yet;
enough then for your wonder. But, come on,
let us be contracted before these witnesses.

Shepherd:
Come, your hand;
and, daughter, yours.

POLIXENES:
Wait, swain, for a while, I beseech you.
Do you have a father?

FLORIZEL:
I have. But what of him?

POLIXENES:
Does he know of this?

FLORIZEL:
He neither does nor shall.

POLIXENES:
I think a father
is, at the nuptial of his son, a guest
that best becomes the table. Please, once more,
is not your father grown incapable
of reasonable affairs? Is he not stupid
with age and altering rheums? Can he speak? Hear?
Know one man from another? Dispute his own estate?
Is he not bed-ridden? And again, does he do nothing
but what he did when he was childish?

FLORIZEL:
No, good sir;
he is in good health and has more strength indeed
than most have at his age.

POLIXENES:
By my white beard,
you do him, if this is so, a wrong
that is somewhat un-filial. It is reasonable that my son
should choose a wife for himself, but it is just as reasonable
that the father, whose joy is nothing else
but fair posterity, should have some say
in such a business.

FLORIZEL:
I yield all this;
but for some other reasons, my grave sir,
which it is not fitting for you to know, I will not acquaint
my father of this business.

POLIXENES:
Let him know about it.

FLORIZEL:
He shall not.

POLIXENES:
Please, let him.

FLORIZEL:
No, he must not.

Shepherd:
Let him, my son. He will not need to grieve
at knowing of your choice.

FLORIZEL:
Come, come, he must not.
Mark our contract.

POLIXENES:
Mark your divorce, young sir,
whom I dare not call son; you are too base
to be acknowledged. You, a scepter's heir,
that thus affects a sheep-hook! You old traitor,
I am sorry that by hanging you I can
but shorten your life by one week. And you, fresh piece
of excellent witchcraft, who must of force know
the royal fool you are dealing with—

Shepherd:
Oh, my heart!

POLIXENES:
I'll have your beauty scratched with briars, and made
more homely than your state. For you, fond boy,
if I may ever know that you do but sigh
that you shall see this knack no more, as I never
mean you shall, we'll bar you from the succession;
we'll not hold you as our blood, no, not our kin,
farther than Deucalion off. Mark my words.
Follow us to the court. You churl, for this time,
though you are full of our displeasure, yet we free you
from the dead blow of it. And you, enchantment—
worthy enough for a herdsman; yes, him too,
that makes himself, but for our honor in it,
unworthy of you—if you ever henceforth
open these rural latches to his entrance,
or embrace his body more with your embraces,
I will devise a death as cruel for you
as you are tender to it.

PERDITA:
Even here, undone!
I was not much afraid; for once or twice
I was about to speak and tell him plainly,
the very same sun that shines upon his court
does not hide his face from our cottage but
looks on us both alike. Will it please you, sir, to be gone?
I told you what would come of this. I beseech you,
take care of your own state. This dream of mine—
being now awake, I'll play the queen no further,
but I will milk my ewes and weep.

CAMILLO:
Why, what's wrong, father?
Speak before you die.

Shepherd:
I cannot speak, nor think,
nor dare to know that which I know. Oh sir!
You have undone a man of eighty-three,
that thought to fill his grave in quiet, yes,
to die upon the bed my father died,
to lie close by his honest bones. But now
some hangman must put on my shroud and lay me
where no priest shovels in dust. Oh, cursed wretch,
that knew this was the prince,
and would adventure
to mingle faith with him! Undone! Undone!
If I might die within this hour, I have lived
to die when I desire.

FLORIZEL:
Why do you look at me so?
I am but sorry, not afraid; delayed,
but nothing altered. What I was, I am;
I am straining more for being pulled back, not following
my leash unwillingly.

CAMILLO:
Gracious my lord,
you know your father's temper. At this time
he will allow no speech, which I guess
you do not intend to give him; and as hardly
will he endure your sight as yet, I fear.
Then, until the fury of his highness settles,
do not come before him.

FLORIZEL:
I don't plan to.
I think, Camillo?

CAMILLO:
Even he, my lord.

PERDITA:
How often have I told you it would be like this!
How often I said, my dignity would last
only until it was known!

FLORIZEL:
It cannot fail but by
the violation of my faith; and then
let nature crush the sides of the earth together
and mar the seeds within! Lift up your looks.
From my succession, wipe me, father; I
am the heir to my affection.

CAMILLO:
Be advised.

FLORIZEL:
I am, and by my fancy. If my reason
will be obedient to it, I have reason;
if not, my senses, better pleased with madness,
bid it welcome.

CAMILLO:
This is desperate, sir.

FLORIZEL:
So call it. But it does fulfill my vow;
I must think it is honesty. Camillo,
not for Bohemia, nor for the pomp that may
be gleaned there, for all the sun sees or
the close earth wombs or the profound sea hides
in unknown fathoms, will I break my oath
to this my fair beloved. Therefore, I pray you,
as you have ever been my father's honored friend,
when he shall miss me—as, in faith, I do not mean
to see him any more—cast your good counsels
upon his passion; let myself and fortune
tug for the time to come. This you may know
and so deliver: I am put to sea
with her whom I cannot hold on shore here;
and, most opportune to our need, I have
a vessel that rides fast by, but is not prepared
for this design. What course I mean to hold
shall not benefit your knowledge, nor
concern me to report.

CAMILLO:
Oh my lord!
I wish your spirit were easier for advice,
or stronger for your need.

FLORIZEL:
Listen, Perdita.
I'll hear you by and by.

CAMILLO:
He's immovable,
resolved for flight. Now I would be happy, if
I could frame his going to serve my turn,
save him from danger, do him love and honor,
purchase the sight again of dear Sicilia
and that unhappy king, my master, whom
I so much thirst to see.

FLORIZEL:
Now, good Camillo;
I am so fraught with curious business that
I am leaving out ceremony.

CAMILLO:
Sir, I think
you have heard of my poor services, in the love
that I have borne your father?

FLORIZEL:
You have deserved very nobly. It is my father's music
to speak of your deeds, not a little of his care
to have them recompensed as they are thought of.

CAMILLO:
Well, my lord,
if you may please to think I love the king
and through him what is nearest to him, which is
your gracious self, just embrace my direction.
If your more ponderous and settled project
may suffer alteration, on my honor,
I'll point you where you shall have such a reception
as shall become your highness; where you may
enjoy your mistress, from whom, I see,
there's no disjunction to be made, but by—
as heavens forbid!—your ruin; marry her,
and, with my best endeavors in your absence,
I will strive to qualify your discontenting father
and bring him up to liking.

FLORIZEL:
How, Camillo,
may this, almost a miracle, be done?
So that I may call you something more than a man
and after that trust you.

CAMILLO:
Have you thought on
a place where you'll go?

FLORIZEL:
Not any yet.
But as the un-thought-on accident is guilty
of what we wildly do, so we profess
ourselves to be the slaves of chance and flies
of every wind that blows.

CAMILLO:
Then listen to me.
This follows, if you will not change your purpose
but undergo this flight: make for Sicilia,
and there present yourself and your fair princess,
for so I see she must be, before Leontes.
She shall be dressed as it becomes
the partner of your bed. I think I see
Leontes opening his free arms and weeping
his welcomes forth; he asks you, the son, for forgiveness,
as if in the father's person; he kisses the hands
of your fresh princess; over and over he divides himself
between his unkindness and his kindness; the one
he chides to hell and bids the other grow
faster than thought or time.

FLORIZEL:
Worthy Camillo,
what reason for my visitation shall I
hold up before him?

CAMILLO:
You were sent by the king your father
to greet him and to give him comfort. Sir,
the manner of your bearing towards him, with
what you shall deliver as from your father,
things known between us three, I'll write down for you.
This shall point you out at every sitting
what you must say; so that he will not perceive
but that you have your father's bosom there
and speak his very heart.

FLORIZEL:
I am bound to you.
There is some substance in this.

CAMILLO:
A cause more promising
than a wild dedication of yourselves
to unpathed waters, undreamed-of shores, most certain
to miseries enough; no hope to help you,
but as you shake off one to take another;
nothing so certain as your anchors, who
do their best office, if they can but stay you
where you'll be loath to be. Besides, you know
prosperity is the very bond of love,
whose fresh complexion and whose heart together
affliction alters.

PERDITA:
One of these is true.
I think affliction may subdue the cheek,
but not take in the mind.

CAMILLO:
Yes, do you say so?
There shall not be born another such
at your father's house for these
seven years.

FLORIZEL:
My good Camillo,
she is as forward in her breeding as
she is in the rear of our birth.

CAMILLO:
I cannot say it's a pity
she lacks instructions, for she seems a mistress
to most that teach.

PERDITA:
Your pardon, sir; for this
I'll blush you thanks.

FLORIZEL:
My prettiest Perdita!
But oh, the thorns we stand upon! Camillo,
preserver of my father, and now of me,
the medicine of our house, how shall we do?
We are not furnished like Bohemia's son,
nor shall we appear in Sicilia.

CAMILLO:
My lord,
fear none of this. I think you know my fortunes
all lie there. It shall be my care
to have you royally appointed, as if
the scene you are playing were mine. For instance, sir,
so that you may know you shall not want, one word.

AUTOLYCUS:
Ha, ha! What a fool Honesty is! And Trust, his
sworn brother, is a very simple gentleman! I have sold
all my trinkets; not a counterfeit stone, not a
ribbon, glass, pomander, brooch, table-book, ballad,
knife, tape, glove, shoe-tie, bracelet, or horn-ring,
to keep my pack from fasting. They throng to see who
should buy first, as if my trinkets had been
hallowed and had brought a benediction to the buyer.
By which means I saw whose purse was best in
picture; and what I saw, to my good use, I
remembered. My clown, who wants but something to
be a reasonable man, grew so in love with the
wenches' song, that he would not stir his little toes
until he had both the tune and the words; which so drew the
rest of the herd to me that all their other senses
were stuck in their ears. You might have pinched a placket, it
was senseless; it was nothing to geld a codpiece of a
purse; I could have filed keys off that hung in
chains. There was no hearing, no feeling, but my sir's song,
and admiring the nothing of it. So that in this
time of lethargy, I picked and cut most of their
festival purses; and if the old man had not come in
with a whoo-bub against his daughter and the king's
son and scared my choughs from the chaff, I would not have
left a purse alive in the whole army.

CAMILLO:
No, but my letters, by this means being there
as soon as you arrive, shall clear that doubt.

FLORIZEL:
And those that you'll procure from King Leontes—

CAMILLO:
It shall satisfy your father.

PERDITA:
Happy are you!
All that you speak shows fair.

CAMILLO:
Who do we have here?
We'll make an instrument of this, and omit
nothing that may give us aid.

AUTOLYCUS:
If they have overheard me now, why, then it's hanging.

CAMILLO:
How now, good fellow! Why are you shaking so? Don't be
afraid, man; there's no harm intended for you here.

AUTOLYCUS:
I am a poor fellow, sir.

CAMILLO:
Why, be so still; there's nobody here who will steal that from
you. Yet, for the outside of your poverty, we must
make an exchange; therefore, undress yourself instantly,
—you must think there's a necessity in it,—and
change garments with this gentleman. Though the
pennyworth on his side is the worst, yet hold on,
there's some boot.

AUTOLYCUS:
I am a poor fellow, sir.
I know you well enough.

CAMILLO:
No, please, dispatch. The gentleman is half
flayed already.

AUTOLYCUS:
Are you in earnest, sir?
I smell the trick of it.

FLORIZEL:
Dispatch, I beg you.

AUTOLYCUS:
Indeed, I have had a down payment, but I cannot with
conscience take it.

CAMILLO:
Unbuckle, unbuckle.
Fortunate mistress—may my prophecy
come home to you!—you must retire
into some covert. Take your sweetheart's hat
and pluck it over your brows, muffle your face,
dismantle yourself, and, as you can, dislike
the truth of your own seeming; so that you may—
for I do fear eyes over—to the shipboard
get away unseen.

PERDITA:
I see the play is so arranged
that I must bear a part.

CAMILLO:
There is no remedy.
Have you finished there?

FLORIZEL:
If I were to meet my father now,
he would not call me his son.

CAMILLO:
No, you shall have no hat.
Come, lady, come. Farewell, my friend.

AUTOLYCUS:
Adieu, sir.

FLORIZEL:
Oh Perdita, what have we both forgotten!
Please, a word.

CAMILLO:

FLORIZEL:
May fortune speed us!
Thus we set on, Camillo, to the seaside.

CAMILLO:
The swifter the speed, the better.

AUTOLYCUS:
I understand the business, I hear it. To have an
open ear, a quick eye, and a nimble hand, is
necessary for a cut-purse; a good nose is requisite
also, to smell out work for the other senses. I see
this is the time that the unjust man thrives.
What an exchange this would have been without any boot! What
a boot is here with this exchange! Surely the gods are
conniving with us this year, and we may do anything
extempore. The prince himself is about a piece of
iniquity, stealing away from his father with his
clog at his heels. If I thought it were a piece of
honesty to acquaint the king with it, I would not
do it. I hold it the more knavery to conceal it;
and in that I am constant to my profession.
Aside, aside; here is more matter for a hot brain.
Every lane's end, every shop, church, session,
and hanging, yields a careful man work.

Clown:
See, see; what a man you are now!
There is no other way but to tell the king
that she's a changeling and none of your flesh and blood.

Shepherd:
No, but hear me.

Clown:
No, but hear me.

Shepherd:
Go to, then.

Clown:
She being none of your flesh and blood, your flesh
and blood has not offended the king; and so your
flesh and blood is not to be punished by him. Show
those things you found about her, those secret
things, all but what she has with her. This being
done, let the law go whistle. I guarantee it.

Shepherd:
I will tell the king everything, every word, yes, and his
son's pranks too; who, I may say, is no honest man,
neither to his father nor to me, to go about to make
me the king's brother-in-law.

Clown:
Indeed, brother-in-law was the farthest you
could have been from him, and then your blood would have been
dearer by I know how much an ounce.

AUTOLYCUS:

Shepherd:
Well, let us go to the king. There is that in this
bundle that will make him scratch his beard.

AUTOLYCUS:

Clown:
I pray heartily that he is at the palace.

AUTOLYCUS:

Shepherd:
To the palace, if it pleases your worship.

AUTOLYCUS:
Your affairs there, what, with whom, the condition
of that bundle, the place of your dwelling, your
names, your ages, of what having, breeding, and any
thing that is fitting to be known, discover.

Clown:
We are but plain fellows, sir.

AUTOLYCUS:
That's a lie; you are rough and hairy. Let me have no
lying. It becomes no one but tradesmen, and they
often give us soldiers the lie. But we pay them for
it with stamped coin, not stabbing steel; therefore
they do not give us the lie.

Clown:
Your worship had almost given us one, if you
had not caught yourself with the manner.

Shepherd:
Are you a courtier, if it pleases you, sir?

AUTOLYCUS:
Whether it pleases me or not, I am a courtier. Don't
you see the air of the court in these clothes?
Doesn't my gait have in it the measure of the court?
Doesn't your nose receive court-odor from me? Don't I
reflect on your baseness with court-contempt? Do you think,
because I insinuate, or tease from you your
business, that I am therefore no courtier? I am a courtier
from head to toe; and one that will either push on or pull
back your business there. Whereupon I command you to
open your affair.

Shepherd:
My business, sir, is to the king.

AUTOLYCUS:
What advocate do you have to him?

Shepherd:
I don't know, if it pleases you.

Clown:
'Advocate' is the court-word for a pheasant. Say you
have none.

Shepherd:
None, sir; I have no pheasant, neither cock nor hen.

AUTOLYCUS:
How blessed are we that are not simple men!
Yet nature might have made me as these are,
therefore I will not disdain.

Clown:
This cannot be but a great courtier.

Shepherd:
His garments are rich, but he doesn't wear
them handsomely.

Clown:
He seems to be more noble for being fantastical.
A great man, I'll warrant; I know by the picking
of his teeth.

AUTOLYCUS:
The bundle there? What's in the bundle?
Why that box?

Shepherd:
Sir, there are such secrets in this bundle and box,
which no one must know but the king; and which he
shall know within this hour, if I may come to
speak with him.

AUTOLYCUS:
Age, you have lost your labor.

Shepherd:
Why, sir?

AUTOLYCUS:
The king is not at the palace; he has gone aboard a
new ship to purge his melancholy and to air himself. For,
if you are capable of serious things, you must
know the king is full of grief.

Shepherd:
So it is said, sir; about his son, that should have
married a shepherd's daughter.

AUTOLYCUS:
If that shepherd is not in chains, let him flee.
The curses he will receive, the tortures he will
feel, will break the back of a man, the heart of a monster.

Clown:
Do you think so, sir?

AUTOLYCUS:
Not he alone shall suffer what wit can make heavy
and vengeance bitter; but those that are related to
him, though removed fifty times, shall all come
under the hangman. Which, though it is a great pity,
yet it is necessary. An old sheep-whistling rogue, a
ram-tender, to offer to have his daughter come into
grace! Some say he shall be stoned; but that death
is too soft for him, I say. To draw our throne into a
sheep-cote! All deaths are too few, the sharpest too easy.

Clown:
Does the old man have a son, sir, do you hear, if it
pleases you, sir?

AUTOLYCUS:
He has a son, who shall be flayed alive; then
anointed over with honey, set on the head of a
wasp's nest; then he shall stand until he is three-quarters
and a dram dead; then he will be recovered again with
aqua-vitae or some other hot infusion; then, raw as
he is, and in the hottest day that prognostication
proclaims, he shall be set against a brick-wall, the
sun looking with a southward eye upon him, where he
is to behold him with flies blown to death. But what
are we talking about these traitorly rascals, whose miseries
are to be smiled at, their offenses being so
capital? Tell me, for you seem to be honest, plain
men, what you have for the king. Being something
gently considered, I'll bring you where he is
aboard, I will tender your persons to his presence,
I will whisper to him on your behalf; and if it is in any man
besides the king to effect your suits, here is the man
that shall do it.

Clown:
He seems to be of great authority. Close with him,
give him gold; and though authority is a stubborn
bear, yet it is often led by the nose with gold. Show
the inside of your purse to the outside of his hand,
and no more ado. Remember, 'stoned,' and 'flayed alive.'

Shepherd:
If it pleases you, sir, to undertake the business for
us, here is the gold I have. I'll make it as much
more and leave this young man in pawn until I bring it to you.

AUTOLYCUS:
After I have done what I promised?

Shepherd:
Yes, sir.

AUTOLYCUS:
Well, give me the half. Are you a party in this business?

Clown:
In some sort, sir. But though my case is a pitiful
one, I hope I shall not be flayed out of it.

AUTOLYCUS:
Oh, that's the case of the shepherd's son. Hang him,
he'll be made an example.

Clown:
Comfort, good comfort! We must go to the king and show
our strange sights. He must know it is none of your
daughter nor my sister; we are gone else. Sir, I
will give you as much as this old man does when the
business is performed, and remain, as he says, your
pawn until it is brought to you.

AUTOLYCUS:
I will trust you. Walk before me toward the seaside;
go on the right hand. I will but look upon the
hedge and follow you.

Clown:
We are blessed in this man, as I may say, even blessed.

Shepherd:
Let's go before as he bids us. He was provided to do us good.

AUTOLYCUS:
If I had a mind to be honest, I see that Fortune would
not suffer me. She drops booties in my mouth. I am
courted now with a double occasion, gold and a means
to do the prince my master good; which, who knows how
that may turn back to my advancement? I will bring
these two moles, these blind ones, aboard him. If he
thinks it is fit to put them ashore again and that the
complaint they have to the king concerns him
nothing, let him call me a rogue for being so far
officious; for I am proof against that title and
what shame else belongs to it. I will present them to him.
There may be something in it.

CLEOMENES:
Sir, you have done enough, and have performed
a saint-like sorrow. You could make no fault
which you have not redeemed; indeed, you have paid down
more penitence than you have done trespass. At last,
do as the heavens have done, forget your evil;
with them, forgive yourself.

LEONTES:
While I remember
her and her virtues, I cannot forget
my blemishes in them, and so I still think of
the wrong I did myself; which was so much,
that it has made my kingdom heirless and
has destroyed the sweetest companion that a man ever
bred his hopes out of.

PAULINA:
True, too true, my lord.
If, one by one, you were to wed all the women in the world,
or from all that are, you were to take something good,
to make a perfect woman, she that you killed
would be unparalleled.

LEONTES:
I think so. Killed!
She I killed! I did so. But you strike me
sorely, to say I did; it is as bitter
upon your tongue as in my thought. Now, good now,
say so but seldom.

CLEOMENES:
Not at all, good lady.
You might have spoken a thousand things that would
have done the time more benefit and graced
your kindness better.

PAULINA:
You are one of those
who would have him wed again.

DION:
If you would not so,
you do not pity the state, nor the remembrance
of his most sovereign name; you consider little
what dangers, by his highness's lack of issue,
may drop upon his kingdom and devour
uncertain onlookers. What would be more holy
than to rejoice that the former queen is well?
What holier than, for royalty's repair,
for present comfort and for future good,
to bless the bed of majesty again
with a sweet fellow to it?

PAULINA:
There is no one worthy,
respecting her that's gone. Besides, the gods
will have fulfilled their secret purposes;
for has not the divine Apollo said,
is it not the tenor of his oracle,
that King Leontes shall not have an heir
until his lost child is found? Which that it shall,
is as monstrous to our human reason
as for my Antigonus to break his grave
and come again to me; who, on my life,
did perish with the infant. It is your counsel
that my lord should be contrary to the heavens,
and oppose against their wills.
Care not for issue;
the crown will find an heir. Great Alexander
left his to the worthiest; so his successor
was likely to be the best.

LEONTES:
Good Paulina,
who has the memory of Hermione,
I know, in honor, oh, that I had ever
squared myself to your counsel! Then, even now,
I might have looked upon my queen's full eyes,
and have taken treasure from her lips—

PAULINA:
And left them
more rich for what they yielded.

LEONTES:
You speak the truth.
No more such wives; therefore, no wife. One worse,
and better used, would make her sainted spirit
possess her corpse again, and on this stage,
where we are offenders now, appear soul-vexed,
and begin, 'Why to me?'

PAULINA:
If she had such power,
she would have just cause.

LEONTES:
She had; and she would incite me
to murder the one I married.

PAULINA:
I should do so.
If I were the ghost that walked, I would bid you mark
her eye, and tell me for what dull part in it
you chose her; then I would shriek, so that even your ears
would split to hear me; and the words that followed
would be 'Remember mine.'

LEONTES:
Stars, stars,
and all other eyes are dead coals! Do not fear a wife;
I'll have no wife, Paulina.

PAULINA:
Will you swear
never to marry but by my free leave?

LEONTES:
Never, Paulina; so may my spirit be blessed!

PAULINA:
Then, good my lords, bear witness to his oath.

CLEOMENES:
You tempt him over-much.

PAULINA:
Unless another,
as like Hermione as her picture is,
confronts his eye.

CLEOMENES:
Good madam,—

PAULINA:
I am done.
Yet, if my lord will marry—if you will, sir,
and there is no remedy, but you will—give me the office
to choose you a queen. She shall not be so young
as your former one was; but she shall be such
that, if your first queen's ghost walked,
it would take joy
to see her in your arms.

LEONTES:
My true Paulina,
we shall not marry until you bid us.

PAULINA:
That
shall be when your first queen is alive again;
never until then.

Gentleman:
One who gives himself out to be Prince Florizel,
son of Polixenes, with his princess, she
the fairest I have yet beheld, desires access
to your high presence.

LEONTES:
What about him? He does not come
like his father's greatness. His approach,
so out of circumstance and sudden, tells us
it is not a planned visitation, but one forced
by need and accident. What train?

Gentleman:
But few,
and those but mean.

LEONTES:
His princess, you say, is with him?

Gentleman:
Yes, the most peerless piece of earth, I think,
that the sun has ever shone brightly on.

PAULINA:
Oh, Hermione,
as every present time boasts itself
above a better one that is gone, so must your grave
give way to what is seen now! Sir, you yourself
have said and written so, but your writing now
is colder than that theme, 'She had not been,
nor was not to be equaled';—thus your verse
flowed with her beauty once. It has shrewdly ebbed,
to say you have seen a better.

Gentleman:
Pardon me, madam.
The one I have almost forgotten—your pardon—
the other, when she has obtained your eye,
will have your tongue too. This is a creature,
if she were to begin a sect, might quench the zeal
of all other professors, and make proselytes
of whomever she but bid to follow.

PAULINA:
What! Not women?

Gentleman:
Women will love her, for she is a woman
more worth than any man; men, for she is
the rarest of all women.

LEONTES:
Go, Cleomenes;
yourself, assisted by your honored friends,
bring them to our embracement. Still, it is strange
that he should thus steal upon us.

PAULINA:
If our prince,
that jewel of children, had seen this hour, he would have been a good pair
with this lord. There was not a full month
between their births.

LEONTES:
Please, no more; cease. You know
he dies to me again when he is talked of. Surely,
when I shall see this gentleman, your speeches
will bring me to consider that which may
unfurnish me of reason. They have come.
Your mother was most true to wedlock, prince;
for she did print your royal father off,
conceiving you. If I were but twenty-one,
your father's image is so hit in you,
his very air, that I should call you brother,
as I did him, and speak of something wildly
performed by us before. Most dearly welcome!
And your fair princess—goddess!—Oh, alas!
I lost a couple, that between heaven and earth
might have thus stood, begetting wonder as
you, gracious couple, do. And then I lost—
all my own folly—the society,
and the amity too, of your brave father, whom,
though I am bearing misery, I desire my life
once more to look on him.

FLORIZEL:
By his command
I have touched Sicilia here and from him
I give you all the greetings that a king, as a friend,
can send to his brother. And, but for the infirmity
which waits upon worn times and has somewhat seized
his wished-for ability, he would have himself
measured the lands and waters between your throne and his
to look upon you; whom he loves—
he told me to say so—more than all the scepters
and those that bear them living.

LEONTES:
Oh, my brother,
good gentleman! The wrongs I have done you stir
afresh within me, and these offices of yours,
so rarely kind, are like interpreters
of my behind-hand slackness. Welcome here,
as is the spring to the earth. And has he too
exposed this paragon to the fearful usage,
at least ungentle, of the dreadful Neptune,
to greet a man not worth her pains, much less
the adventure of her person?

FLORIZEL:
Good my lord,
she came from Libya.

LEONTES:
Where the warlike Smalus,
that noble, honored lord, is feared and loved?

FLORIZEL:
Most royal sir, from there; from him, whose daughter
his tears proclaimed to be his, upon parting with her. From there,
with a prosperous south wind as our friend, we have crossed,
to execute the charge my father gave me
for visiting your highness. My best train
I have dismissed from your Sicilian shores;
they are bound for Bohemia, to signify
not only my success in Libya, sir,
but my arrival and my wife's in safety
here where we are.

LEONTES:
May the blessed gods
purge all infection from our air while you
are in this climate! You have a holy father,
a graceful gentleman; against whose person,
so sacred as it is, I have sinned.
For which the heavens, taking angry note,
have left me issueless; and your father is blessed,
as he merits it from heaven, with you,
worthy of his goodness. What might I have been,
if I could now have looked on a son and daughter,
such goodly things as you!

Lord:
Most noble sir,
that which I shall report will bear no credit,
if the proof were not so near. Please you, great sir,
Bohemia greets you from himself by me;
he desires you to arrest his son, who has—
his dignity and duty both cast off—
fled from his father, from his hopes, and with
a shepherd's daughter.

LEONTES:
Where is Bohemia? Speak.

Lord:
Here in your city; I have just come from him.
I speak with amazement; and it becomes
my marvel and my message. To your court,
while he was hastening, in the chase, it seems,
of this fair couple, he met on the way
the father of this seeming lady and
her brother, having both quit their country
with this young prince.

FLORIZEL:
Camillo has betrayed me;
whose honor and honesty until now
have endured all weathers.

Lord:
Lay it so to his charge.
He's with the king your father.

LEONTES:
Who? Camillo?

Lord:
Camillo, sir; I spoke with him; he now
has these poor men in question. I never saw
wretches so quake. They kneel, they kiss the earth;
they forswear themselves as often as they speak.
Bohemia stops his ears, and threatens them
with various deaths in death.

PERDITA:
Oh, my poor father!
The heavens have set spies upon us, and will not have
our contract celebrated.

LEONTES:
Are you married?

FLORIZEL:
We are not, sir, nor are we likely to be;
the stars, I see, will kiss the valleys first.
The odds for high and low are alike.

LEONTES:
My lord,
is this the daughter of a king?

FLORIZEL:
She is,
once she is my wife.

LEONTES:
That 'once' I see by your good father's speed
will come on very slowly. I am sorry,
most sorry, that you have broken from his liking
where you were tied in duty, and I am as sorry
that your choice is not as rich in worth as it is in beauty,
so that you might well enjoy her.

FLORIZEL:
Dear, look up.
Though Fortune, a visible enemy,
should chase us with my father, she has no power
to change our love. I beseech you, sir,
remember when you owed no more to time
than I do now. With the thought of such affections,
step forth as my advocate; at your request,
my father will grant precious things as trifles.

LEONTES:
If he would do so, I would beg for your precious mistress,
which he considers but a trifle.

PAULINA:
Sir, my liege,
your eye has too much youth in it. Not a month
before your queen died, she was more worth such gazes
than what you look on now.

LEONTES:
I thought of her,
even in these looks I made.
But your petition
is yet unanswered. I will go to your father.
If your honor is not overthrown by your desires,
I am a friend to them and to you. Upon which errand
I now go toward him; therefore, follow me
and mark what way I make. Come, good my lord.

AUTOLYCUS:
I beseech you, sir, were you present at this relation?

First Gentleman:
I was by at the opening of the bundle, and I heard the old
shepherd deliver the manner in which he found it.
Whereupon, after a little amazement, we were all
commanded out of the chamber; only this I thought I
heard the shepherd say, that he found the child.

AUTOLYCUS:
I would most gladly know the outcome of it.

First Gentleman:
I am giving a broken delivery of the business; but the
changes I perceived in the king and Camillo were
very notes of admiration. They seemed almost, with
staring at one another, to tear the cases of their
eyes; there was speech in their dumbness, language
in their very gesture; they looked as if they had heard
of a world ransomed, or one destroyed. A notable
passion of wonder appeared in them; but the wisest
beholder, that knew no more but what he saw, could not
say if the importance were joy or sorrow; but in the
extremity of the one, it must needs be.
Here comes a gentleman that perhaps knows more.
The news, Rogero?

Second Gentleman:
Nothing but bonfires. The oracle is fulfilled; the
king's daughter is found. Such a deal of wonder has
broken out within this hour that ballad-makers
cannot be able to express it.
Here comes the Lady Paulina's steward; he can
deliver you more. How goes it now, sir? This news
which is called true is so like an old tale, that
the verity of it is in strong suspicion. Has the king
found his heir?

Third Gentleman:
Most true, if ever truth were pregnant by
circumstance. What you hear, you'll swear you
see, there is such unity in the proofs. The mantle
of Queen Hermione, her jewel about the neck of it,
the letters of Antigonus found with it which they
know to be his character, the majesty of the
creature in resemblance of the mother, the affection
of nobleness which nature shows above her breeding,
and many other evidences proclaim her with all
certainty to be the king's daughter. Did you see
the meeting of the two kings?

Second Gentleman:
No.

Third Gentleman:
Then you have lost a sight, which was to be seen, but
cannot be spoken of. There you might have beheld one
joy crown another, so and in such a manner that it
seemed sorrow wept to take leave of them, for their
joy waded in tears. There was casting up of eyes,
holding up of hands, with countenances of such
distraction that they were to be known by their garments,
not by their faces. Our king, being ready to leap out of
himself for joy of his found daughter, as if that
joy were now become a loss, cries, 'Oh, your mother,
your mother!' then he asks Bohemia for forgiveness; then he
embraces his son-in-law; then again he worries his
daughter with clipping her; now he thanks the old
shepherd, who stands by like a weather-beaten
conduit of many kings' reigns. I never heard of such
another encounter, which lames report to follow it
and undoes description to do it.

Second Gentleman:
What, I ask you, became of Antigonus, who carried
the child from here?

Third Gentleman:
It's like an old tale still, which will have something to
rehearse, though credit is asleep and not an ear
is open. He was torn to pieces by a bear. This
the shepherd's son avouches; who has not only his
innocence, which seems much, to justify him, but also a
handkerchief and rings of his that Paulina knows.

First Gentleman:
What became of his ship and his followers?

Third Gentleman:
They were wrecked at the same instant of their master's death and
in the view of the shepherd. So that all the
instruments which aided in exposing the child were
lost even when it was found. But oh, the noble
combat that was fought between joy and sorrow in
Paulina! She had one eye declined for the loss of
her husband, and another elevated because the oracle was
fulfilled. She lifted the princess from the earth,
and so she locks her in an embrace, as if she would pin
her to her heart so that she might no more be in danger
of losing her.

First Gentleman:
The dignity of this act was worth the audience of
kings and princes; for it was acted by such.

Third Gentleman:
One of the prettiest touches of all, and that which
angled for my eyes, caught the water though not
the fish, was when, at the relation of the queen's
death, with the manner of how she came to it, bravely
confessed and lamented by the king, how
attentiveness wounded his daughter; until, from one
sign of sorrow to another, she did, with an 'Alas,'
I would fain say, bleed tears, for I am sure my
heart wept blood. Whoever was most marble there changed
color; some swooned, all sorrowed. If all the world
could have seen it, the woe would have been universal.

First Gentleman:
Have they returned to the court?

Third Gentleman:
No. The princess, hearing of her mother's statue,
which is in the keeping of Paulina—a piece many
years in the making and now newly performed by that rare
Italian master, Julio Romano, who, if he himself had
eternity and could put breath into his work, would
beguile Nature of her custom, so perfectly is he her
ape. He has made Hermione so close to the real Hermione
that they say one would speak to her and stand in hope of an
answer. They have gone there with all the greediness of affection,
and there they intend to sup.

Second Gentleman:
I thought she had some great matter in hand there;
for she has privately visited that removed
house twice or thrice a day, ever since the death of Hermione.
Shall we go there and with our company add to
the rejoicing?

First Gentleman:
Who would be away from there that has the benefit of access?
With every wink of an eye, some new grace will be born.
Our absence makes us unthrifty to our knowledge.
Let's go along.

AUTOLYCUS:
Now, if I didn't have the dash of my former life in me,
preferment would drop on my head. I brought the old
man and his son aboard the prince. I told him I heard
them talk of a bundle and I don't know what. But he
at that time, being over-fond of the shepherd's daughter,
as he then took her to be, who began to be very
sea-sick, and himself little better, the extremity of the
weather continuing, this mystery remained
undiscovered. But it's all the same to me; for if I had
been the finder of this secret, it would not
have been relished among my other discredits.
Here come those I have done good to against my will,
and who are already appearing in the blossoms of their fortune.

Shepherd:
Come, boy; I am past having more children, but your sons and
daughters will all be gentlemen born.

Clown:
You are well met, sir. You refused to fight with me
the other day, because I was not a gentleman born.
Do you see these clothes? Say you do not see them and
think me still not a gentleman born. You were best to say
these robes are not gentlemen born. Give me the
lie, do, and try whether I am not now a gentleman born.

AUTOLYCUS:
I know you are now, sir, a gentleman born.

Clown:
Yes, and I have been so for any time these four hours.

Shepherd:
And so have I, boy.

Clown:
So you have. But I was a gentleman born before my
father; for the king's son took me by the hand, and
called me brother; and then the two kings called my
father brother; and then the prince my brother and
the princess my sister called my father father; and
so we wept, and there were the first gentleman-like
tears that we ever shed.

Shepherd:
We may live, son, to shed many more.

Clown:
Yes; or else it would be hard luck, being in so
preposterous an estate as we are.

AUTOLYCUS:
I humbly beseech you, sir, to pardon me for all the
faults I have committed to your worship and to give
me your good report to the prince my master.

Shepherd:
Please, son, do; for we must be gentle, now that we are
gentlemen.

Clown:
Will you amend your life?

AUTOLYCUS:
Yes, if it pleases your good worship.

Clown:
Give me your hand. I will swear to the prince that you
are as honest a true fellow as any in Bohemia.

Shepherd:
You may say it, but not swear it.

Clown:
Not swear it, now that I am a gentleman? Let boors and
franklins say it, I'll swear it.

Shepherd:
What if it is false, son?

Clown:
If it is ever so false, a true gentleman may swear
it on behalf of his friend. And I'll swear to
the prince that you are a tall fellow of your hands and
that you will not be drunk; but I know you are no
tall fellow of your hands and that you will be
drunk. But I'll swear it, and I wish you would
be a tall fellow of your hands.

AUTOLYCUS:
I will prove so, sir, to the best of my ability.

Clown:
Yes, by any means, prove to be a tall fellow. If I do not
wonder how you dare to venture to be drunk, not
being a tall fellow, do not trust me. Hark! The kings
and the princes, our kindred, are going to see the
queen's picture. Come, follow us. We'll be your
good masters.

LEONTES:
Oh, grave and good Paulina, the great comfort
that I have had from you!

PAULINA:
What, sovereign sir,
I did not do well, but I meant well. All my services
you have paid back in full. But that you have vouchsafed,
with your crowned brother and these your contracted
heirs of your kingdoms, to visit my poor house,
it is a surplus of your grace, which my
life may never last to answer for.

LEONTES:
Oh Paulina,
we honor you with trouble. But we came
to see the statue of our queen. We have
passed through your gallery, not without much content
in many singularities; but we did not see
that which my daughter came to look upon,
the statue of her mother.

PAULINA:
As she lived peerless,
so her dead likeness, I do well believe,
excels whatever you have yet looked upon
or the hand of man has done. Therefore I keep it
lonely, apart. But here it is. Prepare
to see the life as lively mocked as ever
still sleep mocked death. Behold, and say it is well.
I like your silence, it the more shows off
your wonder. But yet speak; first, you, my liege.
Does it not come somewhat near?

LEONTES:
Her natural posture!
Chide me, dear stone, so that I may say indeed
that you are Hermione; or rather, you are she
in your not chiding, for she was as tender
as infancy and grace. But yet, Paulina,
Hermione was not so much wrinkled, nothing
so aged as this seems.

POLIXENES:
Oh, not by much.

PAULINA:
So much the more is our carver's excellence;
which lets some sixteen years go by and makes her
as she would have lived now.

LEONTES:
As she might have done now,
so much to my good comfort, as it is
now piercing to my soul. Oh, thus she stood,
even with such a life of majesty, a warm life,
as it now coldly stands, when I first wooed her!
I am ashamed. Does not the stone rebuke me
for being more stone than it? Oh, royal piece,
there's magic in your majesty, which has
conjured my evils to remembrance and
from your admiring daughter has taken her spirits,
so that she stands like stone with you.

PERDITA:
And give me leave,
and do not say it is superstition, that
I kneel and then implore her blessing. Lady,
dear queen, that ended when I but began,
give me that hand of yours to kiss.

PAULINA:
Oh, patience!
The statue has just been fixed, the color is not dry.

CAMILLO:
My lord, your sorrow was laid on too sore,
which sixteen winters cannot blow away,
and so many summers cannot dry. Scarcely any joy
did ever live so long; no sorrow
but killed itself much sooner.

POLIXENES:
Dear my brother,
let him that was the cause of this have the power
to take off as much grief from you as he
will piece up in himself.

PAULINA:
Indeed, my lord,
if I had thought the sight of my poor image
would have wrought you thus—for the stone is mine—
I would not have shown it.

LEONTES:
Do not draw the curtain.

PAULINA:
You shall no longer gaze on it, lest your fancy
may think soon that it moves.

LEONTES:
Let it be, let it be.
I wish I were dead, but that, I think, already—
who was he that made it? See, my lord,
would you not deem it breathed? And that those veins
did verily bear blood?

POLIXENES:
It is masterfully done.
The very life seems warm upon her lip.

LEONTES:
The fixture of her eye has motion in it,
as we are mocked with art.

PAULINA:
I'll draw the curtain.
My lord is almost so far transported that
he'll think soon that it lives.

LEONTES:
Oh, sweet Paulina,
make me think so for twenty years together!
No settled senses of the world can match
the pleasure of that madness. Let it alone.

PAULINA:
I am sorry, sir, that I have thus far stirred you. But
I could afflict you further.

LEONTES:
Do, Paulina;
for this affliction has a taste as sweet
as any cordial comfort. Still, I think,
there is an air that comes from her. What fine chisel
could ever yet cut breath? Let no man mock me,
for I will kiss her.

PAULINA:
Good my lord, forbear.
The ruddiness upon her lip is wet;
you'll mar it if you kiss it, and you'll stain your own
with oily painting. Shall I draw the curtain?

LEONTES:
No, not for these twenty years.

PERDITA:
For so long I could
stand by, a looker-on.

PAULINA:
Either forbear,
quit this chapel immediately, or resolve
for more amazement. If you can behold it,
I'll make the statue move indeed, descend
and take you by the hand; but then you'll think—
which I protest against—that I am assisted
by wicked powers.

LEONTES:
Whatever you can make her do,
I am content to look on. Whatever she has to speak,
I am content to hear; for it is as easy
to make her speak as to make her move.

PAULINA:
It is required
that you awake your faith. Then all of you stand still;
on: those that think it is an unlawful business
that I am about, let them depart.

LEONTES:
Proceed.
No foot shall stir.

PAULINA:
Music, awake her; strike!
It's time; descend; be stone no more; approach;
strike all that look upon you with marvel. Come,
I'll fill your grave up. Stir, no, come away,
bequeath to death your numbness, for from him
dear life redeems you. You perceive she stirs.
Do not start; her actions shall be as holy as
you hear my spell is lawful. Do not shun her
until you see her die again; for then
you will kill her double. No, present your hand.
When she was young, you wooed her; now in her age
has she become the suitor?

LEONTES:
Oh, she's warm!
If this is magic, let it be an art
as lawful as eating.

POLIXENES:
She embraces him.

CAMILLO:
She is hanging about his neck.
If she pertains to life, let her speak too.

POLIXENES:
Yes, and make it manifest where she has lived,
or how she was stolen from the dead.

PAULINA:
That she is living,
if it were but told to you, would be hooted at
like an old tale. But it appears she lives,
though she does not yet speak. Mark a little while.
Please you to interpose, fair madam. Kneel
and pray for your mother's blessing. Turn, good lady;
our Perdita is found.

HERMIONE:
You gods, look down
and from your sacred vials pour your graces
upon my daughter's head! Tell me, my own,
where have you been preserved? Where have you lived? How did you find
your father's court? For you shall hear that I,
knowing by Paulina that the oracle
gave hope that you were in being, have preserved
myself to see the issue.

PAULINA:
There's time enough for that;
lest they desire upon this push to trouble
your joys with a like relation. Go together,
you precious winners all; and share your exultation
with every one. I, an old turtle,
will wing myself to some withered bough and there
my mate, that is never to be found again,
I will lament until I am lost.

LEONTES:
Oh, peace, Paulina!
You should take a husband by my consent,
as I by yours a wife. This is a match,
and it is made between us by vows. You have found mine;
but how, is to be questioned; for I saw her,
as I thought, dead, and I have in vain said many
a prayer upon her grave. I'll not seek far—
for I partly know his mind—to find you
an honorable husband. Come, Camillo,
and take her by the hand, whose worth and honesty
is richly noted and here justified
by us, a pair of kings. Let's go from this place.
What! Look upon my brother. Both your pardons,
that I ever put my ill suspicion between
your holy looks. This is your son-in-law,
and son to the king, who, with the heavens directing,
is betrothed to your daughter. Good Paulina,
lead us from here, where we may leisurely
each one demand an answer to his part
performed in this wide gap of time since we were first
separated. Lead away hastily.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Escalus.

ESCALUS:
My lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
To unfold the properties of government,
would seem in me to affect speech and discourse;
since I am put to know that your own science
exceeds, in that, the lists of all advice
that my strength can give you. Then no more remains,
but that to your sufficiency, as your worth is able,
and let them work. The nature of our people,
our city's institutions, and the terms
for common justice, you are as pregnant in
as art and practice has enriched any
that we remember. There is our commission,
from which we would not have you warp. Call hither,
I say, bid Angelo to come before us.
What figure of us do you think he will bear?
For you must know, we have with a special soul
elected him to supply our absence,
lent him our terror, dressed him with our love,
and given his deputation all the organs
of our own power. What do you think of it?

ESCALUS:
If any in Vienna is of worth
to undergo such ample grace and honor,
it is Lord Angelo.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Look, here he comes.

ANGELO:
Always obedient to your grace's will,
I have come to know your pleasure.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Angelo,
there is a kind of character in your life,
that to the observer, your history
fully unfolds. Yourself and your belongings
are not your own so properly as to waste
yourself upon your virtues, and they upon you.
Heaven does with us as we do with torches,
we do not light them for themselves; for if our virtues
did not go forth from us, it would be all the same
as if we did not have them. Spirits are not finely touched
but for fine issues, nor does Nature ever lend
the smallest scruple of her excellence
but, like a thrifty goddess, she determines
herself the glory of a creditor,
both thanks and use. But I do bend my speech
to one that can advertise my part in him;
hold therefore, Angelo:
In our remove, be you at full ourself;
mortality and mercy in Vienna
live in your tongue and heart. Old Escalus,
though first in question, is your secondary.
Take your commission.

ANGELO:
Now, good my lord,
let there be some more tests made of my metal,
before so noble and so great a figure
is stamped upon it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No more evasion.
We have, with a leavened and prepared choice,
proceeded to you; therefore, take your honors.
Our haste from here is of so quick a condition
that it prefers itself and leaves unquestioned
matters of needful value. We shall write to you,
as time and our concerns shall require,
how it goes with us, and we look to know
what befalls you here. So, fare you well;
to the hopeful execution I leave you
of your commissions.

ANGELO:
Yet give me leave, my lord,
that we may bring you something on the way.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
My haste may not admit it;
nor do you need, on my honor, to have to do
with any scruple. Your scope is as my own,
so as to enforce or qualify the laws
as seems good to your soul. Give me your hand.
I'll go away privately. I love the people,
but I do not like to stage myself to their eyes.
Though it does well, I do not relish well
their loud applause and vehement "Aves";
nor do I think the man of safe discretion
that does affect it. Once more, fare you well.

ANGELO:
May the heavens give safety to your purposes!

ESCALUS:
May you be led forth and brought back in happiness!

DUKE:
I thank you. Fare you well.

ESCALUS:
I shall desire you, sir, to give me leave
to have free speech with you; and it concerns me
to look into the bottom of my place.
I have a power, but of what strength and nature
I am not yet instructed.

ANGELO:
It's the same with me. Let's withdraw together,
and we may soon have our satisfaction
concerning that point.

ESCALUS:
I'll wait upon your honor.

LUCIO:
If the duke and the other dukes don't come to a
composition with the King of Hungary, why then all
the dukes will fall upon the king.

First Gentleman:
Heaven grant us its peace, but not the King of
Hungary's!

Second Gentleman:
Amen.

LUCIO:
You conclude like the sanctimonious pirate, who
went to sea with the Ten Commandments, but scraped
one out of the table.

Second Gentleman:
'You shall not steal'?

LUCIO:
Yes, that he erased.

First Gentleman:
Why, it was a commandment to command the captain and
all the rest from their functions. They put forth
to steal. There's not a soldier among us all, that, in
the thanksgiving before a meal, does relish the petition
well that prays for peace.

Second Gentleman:
I have never heard any soldier dislike it.

LUCIO:
I believe you; for I think you were never where
grace was said.

Second Gentleman:
No? A dozen times at least.

First Gentleman:
What, in meter?

LUCIO:
In any proportion or in any language.

First Gentleman:
I think, or in any religion.

LUCIO:
Yes, why not? Grace is grace, despite all
controversy. As, for example, you yourself are a
wicked villain, despite all grace.

First Gentleman:
Well, there was but a pair of shears between us.

LUCIO:
I grant you; as there may be between the lists and the
velvet. You are the list.

First Gentleman:
And you are the velvet. You are good velvet; you are
a three-piled piece, I guarantee you. I would as soon
be a list of an English kersey as be piled, as you
are piled, for a French velvet. Am I speaking
feelingly now?

LUCIO:
I think you are; and, indeed, with a most painful
feeling of your speech. I will, out of your own
confession, learn to begin your health; but, while I
live, I will forget to drink after you.

First Gentleman:
I think I have done myself wrong, haven't I?

Second Gentleman:
Yes, that you have, whether you are tainted or free.

LUCIO:
Behold, behold, where Madam Mitigation comes! I
have purchased as many diseases under her roof as come to—

Second Gentleman:
To what, I pray?

LUCIO:
Judge.

Second Gentleman:
To three thousand dollars a year.

First Gentleman:
Yes, and more.

LUCIO:
A French crown more.

First Gentleman:
You are always figuring diseases in me; but you
are full of error; I am sound.

LUCIO:
No, not as one would say, healthy; but so sound as
things that are hollow. Your bones are hollow;
impiety has made a feast of you.

First Gentleman:
How now! Which of your hips has the most profound sciatica?

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Well, well; there's one yonder who was arrested and carried
to prison that was worth five thousand of you all.

Second Gentleman:
Who is that, I pray you?

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Well, sir, that's Claudio, Signor Claudio.

First Gentleman:
Claudio to prison? It's not so.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
No, but I know it's so. I saw him arrested, I saw
him carried away; and, what is more, within these
three days his head is to be chopped off.

LUCIO:
But, after all this fooling, I would not have it so.
Are you sure of this?

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
I am too sure of it. And it is for getting Madam
Julietta with child.

LUCIO:
Believe me, this may be true. He promised to meet me two
hours ago, and he was always precise in
promise-keeping.

Second Gentleman:
Besides, you know, it draws somewhat near to the
speech we had for such a purpose.

First Gentleman:
But, most of all, it agrees with the proclamation.

LUCIO:
Away! Let's go and learn the truth of it.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Thus, what with the war, what with the sweat, what
with the gallows, and what with poverty, I am
custom-shrunk.
How now! What's the news with you?

POMPEY:
That man over there is being taken to prison.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Well; what has he done?

POMPEY:
A woman.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
But what's his offense?

POMPEY:
Groping for trout in a peculiar river.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
What, is there a maid with a child by him?

POMPEY:
No, but there's a woman with a maid by him. You have
not heard of the proclamation, have you?

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
What proclamation, man?

POMPEY:
All houses in the suburbs of Vienna must be pulled down.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
And what will become of those in the city?

POMPEY:
They will stand for seed. They would have gone down too,
but a wise burgher put in for them.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
But shall all our houses of resort in the suburbs be
pulled down?

POMPEY:
To the ground, mistress.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Why, here's a change indeed in the commonwealth!
What shall become of me?

POMPEY:
Come; don't you worry. Good counselors don't lack
clients. Though you change your place, you don't need to
change your trade; I'll still be your tapster.
Courage! Pity will be taken on you. You that
have worn your eyes almost out in the service, you
will be considered.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
What's to be done here, Thomas Tapster? Let's withdraw.

POMPEY:
Here comes Signior Claudio, led by the provost to
prison; and there's Madam Juliet.

CLAUDIO:
Fellow, why are you showing me to the world like this?
Take me to prison, where I am committed.

Provost:
I'm not doing it with any evil disposition,
but by a special charge from Lord Angelo.

CLAUDIO:
Thus can the demigod Authority
make us pay down for our offense by weight
the words of heaven; on whom it will, it will;
on whom it will not, so; yet it is still just.

LUCIO:
Why, how now, Claudio! From where does this restraint come?

CLAUDIO:
From too much liberty, my Lucio, liberty.
As surfeit is the father of much fasting,
so every scope, by its immoderate use,
turns to restraint. Our natures do pursue,
like rats that raven down their proper bane,
a thirsty evil; and when we drink, we die.

LUCIO:
If I could speak so wisely under an arrest, I would
send for certain of my creditors. And yet, to say
the truth, I would as soon have the foppery of freedom
as the morality of imprisonment. What's your
offense, Claudio?

CLAUDIO:
To speak of it would be to offend again.

LUCIO:
What, is it murder?

CLAUDIO:
No.

LUCIO:
Lechery?

CLAUDIO:
Call it so.

Provost:
Away, sir! You must go.

CLAUDIO:
One word, good friend. Lucio, a word with you.

LUCIO:
A hundred, if they'll do you any good.
Is lechery so looked after?

CLAUDIO:
This is how it stands with me: upon a true contract,
I got possession of Julietta's bed.
You know the lady; she is my fast wife,
except that we lack the denunciation
of outward order. This we did not do,
only for the propagation of a dower
remaining in the coffer of her friends,
from whom we thought it fitting to hide our love
until time had made them for us. But it happens that
the stealth of our most mutual entertainment
is written on Juliet with a character too gross.

LUCIO:
With child, perhaps?

CLAUDIO:
Unhappily, even so.
And the new deputy, now for the duke—
whether it is the fault and glimpse of newness,
or whether the body politic is
a horse on which the governor rides,
who, newly in the seat, so that it may know
he can command, lets it feel the spur straight away;
whether the tyranny is in his place,
or in his eminence that fills it up,
I stagger in—but this new governor
awakens in me all the enrolled penalties
which have, like unscoured armor, hung by the wall
for so long that nineteen zodiacs have gone round
and none of them have been worn; and, for a name,
he now puts the drowsy and neglected act
freshly on me. It is surely for a name.

LUCIO:
I guarantee it is. And your head stands so ticklishly on
your shoulders that a milkmaid, if she is in love,
may sigh it off. Send after the duke and appeal to
him.

CLAUDIO:
I have done so, but he is not to be found.
I pray you, Lucio, do me this kind service.
This day my sister should enter the cloister
and there receive her approbation.
Acquaint her with the danger of my state.
Implore her, in my voice, that she make friends
with the strict deputy; bid her to try him herself.
I have great hope in that; for in her youth
there is a prone and speechless dialect,
such as moves men. Besides, she has a prosperous art
when she will play with reason and discourse,
and she can persuade well.

LUCIO:
I hope she may; as well for the encouragement of the
like, which would otherwise stand under a grievous
imposition, as for the enjoying of your life, which I
would be sorry should be thus foolishly lost at a
game of tick-tack. I'll go to her.

CLAUDIO:
I thank you, good friend Lucio.

LUCIO:
Within two hours.

CLAUDIO:
Come, officer, away!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No, holy father; throw away that thought;
do not believe that the dribbling dart of love
can pierce a complete bosom. Why I desire you
to give me secret harbor, has a purpose
more grave and wrinkled than the aims and ends
of burning youth.

FRIAR THOMAS:
May your grace speak of it?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
My holy sir, no one knows better than you
how I have always loved the removed life
and held it at an idle price to haunt assemblies
where youth, and cost, and witless bravery are kept.
I have delivered to Lord Angelo,
a man of stricture and firm abstinence,
my absolute power and place here in Vienna,
and he supposes that I have traveled to Poland;
for so I have spread it in the common ear,
and so it is received. Now, pious sir,
you will demand of me why I do this?

FRIAR THOMAS:
Gladly, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
We have strict statutes and most biting laws,
the needful bits and curbs to headstrong weeds,
which for these nineteen years we have let slip;
even like an overgrown lion in a cave,
that does not go out to prey. Now, like fond fathers,
having bound up the threatening twigs of birch,
only to stick them in their children's sight
for terror, not to use, in time the rod
becomes more mocked than feared; so our decrees,
dead to infliction, are dead to themselves;
and liberty plucks justice by the nose;
the baby beats the nurse, and quite athwart
goes all decorum.

FRIAR THOMAS:
It rested in your grace
to unloose this tied-up justice when you pleased.
And it would have seemed more dreadful in you
than in Lord Angelo.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I fear, too dreadful.
Since it was my fault to give the people scope,
it would be my tyranny to strike and gall them
for what I bid them do. For we bid this be done,
when evil deeds have their permissive pass
and not the punishment. Therefore, indeed, my father,
I have imposed the office on Angelo;
who may, in the ambush of my name, strike home,
and yet my nature never in the fight
to do so in slander. And to behold his sway,
I will, as it were a brother of your order,
visit both prince and people. Therefore, I pray you,
supply me with the habit and instruct me
how I may formally in person bear myself
like a true friar. I will render you more reasons for this action
at our more leisure;
only, this one: Lord Angelo is precise;
he stands at a guard with envy; he scarcely confesses
that his blood flows, or that his appetite
is more for bread than for stone. Hence we shall see,
if power changes purpose, what our seemers are.

ISABELLA:
And do you nuns have no further privileges?

FRANCISCA:
Are these not large enough?

ISABELLA:
Yes, truly; I don't speak as if I desire more,
but rather I wish for a more strict restraint
upon the sisterhood, the votarists of Saint Clare.

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
Who is that that is calling?

FRANCISCA:
It is a man's voice. Gentle Isabella,
turn the key, and find out his business from him;
you may, I may not; you are not yet sworn.
