When you have taken your vows, you must not speak with men
except in the presence of the prioress.
Then, if you speak, you must not show your face,
or, if you show your face, you must not speak.
He is calling again; I pray you, answer him.

ISABELLA:
Peace and prosperity! Who is it that calls?

LUCIO:
Hail, virgin, if you are one, as those cheek-roses
proclaim you are no less! Can you so help me
as to bring me to the sight of Isabella,
a novice of this place and the fair sister
to her unhappy brother Claudio?

ISABELLA:
Why 'her unhappy brother'? Let me ask,
all the more because I must now let you know
that I am that Isabella and his sister.

LUCIO:
Gentle and fair, your brother kindly greets you.
Not to be weary with you, he's in prison.

ISABELLA:
Woe is me! For what?

LUCIO:
For that which, if I myself might be his judge,
he should receive his punishment in thanks.
He has gotten his friend with child.

ISABELLA:
Sir, do not make me your story.

LUCIO:
It is true.
I would not—though it is my familiar sin
to seem like the lapwing with maids and to jest,
with my tongue far from my heart—play with all virgins so.
I hold you as a thing enskied and sainted,
an immortal spirit by your renouncement,
and to be talked with in sincerity,
as with a saint.

ISABELLA:
You are blaspheming the good by mocking me.

LUCIO:
Do not believe it. In fewness and in truth, it is thus:
your brother and his lover have embraced.
As those that feed grow full, as the blossoming time
that brings the bare fallow from the seedness
to a teeming harvest, even so her plentiful womb
expresses his full tilth and husbandry.

ISABELLA:
Someone with a child by him? My cousin Juliet?

LUCIO:
Is she your cousin?

ISABELLA:
Adoptively; as school-maids change their names
by a vain though apt affection.

LUCIO:
She it is.

ISABELLA:
Oh, let him marry her.

LUCIO:
This is the point.
The duke has gone very strangely from here;
he held out to many gentlemen, myself being one,
the hand and hope of action. But we have learned
from those that know the very nerves of the state,
that his announced intentions were an infinite distance
from his true-meant design. In his place,
and with the full line of his authority,
Lord Angelo governs; a man whose blood
is very snow-broth; one who never feels
the wanton stings and motions of the sense,
but does rebate and blunt his natural edge
with the profits of the mind, study, and fasting.
He—to give fear to use and liberty,
which have for a long time run by the hideous law,
like mice by lions—has picked out an act,
under whose heavy sense your brother's life
falls into forfeit. He arrests him on it,
and follows the rigor of the statute closely,
to make him an example. All hope is gone,
unless you have the grace by your fair prayer
to soften Angelo. And that's the pith of my business
between you and your poor brother.

ISABELLA:
Does he so seek his life?

LUCIO:
He has already sentenced him;
and, as I hear, the provost has
a warrant for his execution.

ISABELLA:
Alas! What poor ability is in me
to do him good?

LUCIO:
Try the power you have.

ISABELLA:
My power? Alas, I doubt—

LUCIO:
Our doubts are traitors
and they make us lose the good we often might win
by fearing to attempt. Go to Lord Angelo,
and let him learn to know, when maidens sue,
men give like gods; but when they weep and kneel,
all their petitions are as freely theirs
as they themselves would owe them.

ISABELLA:
I'll see what I can do.

LUCIO:
But do it speedily.

ISABELLA:
I will go about it straight away;
I will stay no longer than to give my mother
notice of my affair. I humbly thank you.
Commend me to my brother. Soon at night
I'll send him certain word of my success.

LUCIO:
I take my leave of you.

ISABELLA:
Good sir, adieu.

ANGELO:
We must not make a scarecrow of the law,
setting it up to scare the birds of prey,
and let it keep one shape, until custom makes it
their perch and not their terror.

ESCALUS:
Yes, but yet
let us be keen, and rather cut a little,
than to fall, and bruise to death. Alas, this gentleman
whom I would save, had a most noble father!
Just let your honor know,
whom I believe to be most straight in virtue,
that, in the working of your own affections,
if time had coincided with place, or place with wishing,
or if the resolute acting of your blood
could have attained the effect of your own purpose,
whether you would not have sometime in your life
erred in this point for which you now censure him,
and pulled the law upon you.

ANGELO:
It is one thing to be tempted, Escalus,
and another thing to fall. I do not deny,
the jury, passing on the prisoner's life,
may have a thief or two among the sworn twelve
who are guiltier than the one they are trying. What is made open to justice,
justice seizes. What do the laws know
that thieves pass judgment on other thieves? It is very pregnant,
that the jewel we find, we stoop and take,
because we see it; but what we do not see,
we tread upon, and never think of it.
You may not so extenuate his offense
because I have had such faults; but rather tell me,
when I, who censure him, do so offend,
let my own judgment pattern out my death,
and let nothing come in partial. Sir, he must die.

ESCALUS:
Let it be as your wisdom will.

ANGELO:
Where is the provost?

Provost:
Here, if it pleases your honor.

ANGELO:
See that Claudio
is executed by nine tomorrow morning.
Bring him his confessor, and let him be prepared;
for that's the end of his pilgrimage.

ESCALUS:

ELBOW:
Come, take them away. If these are good people in
a commonwealth that do nothing but use their abuses in
common houses, then I know no law. Take them away.

ANGELO:
How now, sir! What's your name? And what's the matter?

ELBOW:
If it pleases your honor, I am the poor duke's
constable, and my name is Elbow. I lean upon
justice, sir, and I bring here before your good
honor two notorious benefactors.

ANGELO:
Benefactors? Well; what benefactors are they? Are
they not malefactors?

ELBOW:
If it pleases your honor, I don't know well what they
are. But they are precise villains, that I am sure
of; and they are void of all profanation in the world that
good Christians ought to have.

ESCALUS:
This is coming off well; here's a wise officer.

ANGELO:
Go to. What quality are they of? Is your name Elbow?
Why don't you speak, Elbow?

POMPEY:
He cannot, sir; he's out at the elbow.

ANGELO:
What are you, sir?

ELBOW:
He, sir! A tapster, sir; a part-time bawd; one that
serves a bad woman; whose house, sir, was, as they
say, pulled down in the suburbs; and now she
professes to run a hot-house, which, I think, is a very ill house too.

ESCALUS:
How do you know that?

ELBOW:
My wife, sir, whom I detest before heaven and your honor—

ESCALUS:
What? Your wife?

ELBOW:
Yes, sir; whom, I thank heaven, is an honest woman—

ESCALUS:
Do you detest her for that reason?

ELBOW:
I say, sir, that I will detest myself also, as well as
she, that this house, if it is not a bawd's house,
it is a pity of her life, for it is a naughty house.

ESCALUS:
How do you know that, constable?

ELBOW:
Well, sir, by my wife; who, if she had been a woman
who was cardinally inclined, might have been accused of
fornication, adultery, and all uncleanliness there.

ESCALUS:
By the woman's means?

ELBOW:
Yes, sir, by Mistress Overdone's means. But as she
spat in his face, so she defied him.

POMPEY:
Sir, if it pleases your honor, this is not so.

ELBOW:
Prove it before these scoundrels here, you honorable
man; prove it.

ESCALUS:
Do you hear how he misplaces his words?

POMPEY:
Sir, she came in great with child; and longing,
saving your honor's reverence, for stewed prunes.
Sir, we had but two in the house, which at that very
distant time stood, as it were, in a fruit-dish, a
dish of some three-pence; your honors have seen
such dishes; they are not China dishes, but very
good dishes—

ESCALUS:
Go to, go to. It doesn't matter about the dish, sir.

POMPEY:
No, indeed, sir, not a pin. You are right in that.
But to the point. As I say, this
Mistress Elbow, being, as I say, with child, and
being great-bellied, and longing, as I said, for
prunes; and having but two in the dish, as I said,
Master Froth here, this very man, having eaten the
rest, as I said, and, as I say, paying for them very
honestly; for, as you know, Master Froth, I could
not give you three-pence back.

FROTH:
No, indeed.

POMPEY:
Very well. You being then, if you remember,
cracking the stones of the aforesaid prunes—

FROTH:
Yes, so I did indeed.

POMPEY:
Why, very well; I was telling you then, if you remember,
that such a one and such a one were past the cure of the thing
you know of, unless they kept a very good diet, as I told you—

FROTH:
All this is true.

POMPEY:
Why, very well, then—

ESCALUS:
Come, you are a tedious fool. To the purpose. What
was done to Elbow's wife, that he has cause to
complain of? Come to what was done to her.

POMPEY:
Sir, your honor cannot come to that yet.

ESCALUS:
No, sir, nor do I mean to.

POMPEY:
Sir, but you shall come to it, by your honor's
leave. And, I beseech you, look into Master Froth
here, sir; a man of four-score pounds a year; whose
father died at Hallowmas. Wasn't it at Hallowmas,
Master Froth?

FROTH:
All Hallows' Eve.

POMPEY:
Why, very well; I hope there are truths here. He, sir,
was sitting, as I say, in a lower chair, sir; it was in
the Bunch of Grapes, where indeed you have a delight
to sit, have you not?

FROTH:
I have so; because it is an open room and good for winter.

POMPEY:
Why, very well, then; I hope here be truths.

ANGELO:
This will last out a night in Russia,
when the nights are longest there. I'll take my leave,
and leave you to the hearing of the case;
hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.

ESCALUS:
I think no less. Good morrow to your lordship.
Now, sir, come on. What was done to Elbow's wife, once more?

POMPEY:
Once, sir? There was nothing done to her once.

ELBOW:
I beseech you, sir, ask him what this man did to my wife.

POMPEY:
I beseech your honor, ask me.

ESCALUS:
Well, sir; what did this gentleman do to her?

POMPEY:
I beseech you, sir, look at this gentleman's face.
Good Master Froth, look upon his honor; it's for a
good purpose. Does your honor mark his face?

ESCALUS:
Yes, sir, very well.

POMPEY:
No; I beseech you, mark it well.

ESCALUS:
Well, I do so.

POMPEY:
Does your honor see any harm in his face?

ESCALUS:
Why, no.

POMPEY:
I'll be supposed upon a book, his face is the worst
thing about him. Good, then; if his face is the
worst thing about him, how could Master Froth do the
constable's wife any harm? I would know that from
your honor.

ESCALUS:
He's in the right. Constable, what do you say to it?

ELBOW:
First, if it pleases you, the house is a respected
house; next, this is a respected fellow; and his
mistress is a respected woman.

POMPEY:
By this hand, sir, his wife is a more respected
person than any of us all.

ELBOW:
Scoundrel, you lie; you lie, you wicked scoundrel! The
time has yet to come that she was ever respected
by man, woman, or child.

POMPEY:
Sir, she was respected with him before he married her.

ESCALUS:
Which is the wiser here? Justice or Iniquity? Is
this true?

ELBOW:
Oh, you villain! Oh, you scoundrel! Oh, you wicked
Hannibal! I was respected with her before I was married
to her! If I was ever respected with her, or she
with me, do not let your worship think I am the poor
duke's officer. Prove this, you wicked Hannibal, or
I'll have my action of battery on you.

ESCALUS:
If he gave you a box on the ear, you might have had an
action of slander too.

ELBOW:
Indeed, I thank your good worship for it. What is it
your worship's pleasure that I should do with this wicked scoundrel?

ESCALUS:
Truly, officer, because he has some offenses in him
that you would discover if you could, let him
continue in his courses until you know what they
are.

ELBOW:
Indeed, I thank your worship for it. You see, you
wicked scoundrel, now, what has come upon you. You are
to continue now, you scoundrel; you are to continue.

ESCALUS:
Where were you born, friend?

FROTH:
Here in Vienna, sir.

ESCALUS:
Are you of eighty pounds a year?

FROTH:
Yes, if it pleases you, sir.

ESCALUS:
So. What trade are you in, sir?

POMPEY:
A tapster; a poor widow's tapster.

ESCALUS:
Your mistress's name?

POMPEY:
Mistress Overdone.

ESCALUS:
Has she had any more than one husband?

POMPEY:
Nine, sir; overdone by the last.

ESCALUS:
Nine! Come here to me, Master Froth. Master
Froth, I would not have you acquainted with
tapsters. They will draw you, Master Froth, and you
will hang them. Get you gone, and let me hear no
more of you.

FROTH:
I thank your worship. For my own part, I never
come into any room in a tap-house, but I am drawn
in.

ESCALUS:
Well, no more of it, Master Froth. Farewell.
Come here to me, Master Tapster. What's your
name, Master Tapster?

POMPEY:
Pompey.

ESCALUS:
What else?

POMPEY:
Bum, sir.

ESCALUS:
Truly, and your bum is the greatest thing about you;
so that in the most beastly sense, you are Pompey the
Great. Pompey, you are partly a bawd, Pompey,
however you color it by being a tapster, are you
not? Come, tell me the truth; it shall be the better for you.

POMPEY:
Truly, sir, I am a poor fellow that would live.

ESCALUS:
How would you live, Pompey? By being a bawd? What
do you think of the trade, Pompey? Is it a lawful trade?

POMPEY:
If the law would allow it, sir.

ESCALUS:
But the law will not allow it, Pompey; nor shall it
be allowed in Vienna.

POMPEY:
Does your worship mean to geld and spay all the
youth of the city?

ESCALUS:
No, Pompey.

POMPEY:
Truly, sir, in my poor opinion, they will go to it then.
If your worship will take order for the drabs and
the knaves, you need not fear the bawds.

ESCALUS:
There are some pretty orders beginning, I can tell you.
It is but beheading and hanging.

POMPEY:
If you head and hang all that offend that way for
ten years together, you'll be glad to give out a
commission for more heads. If this law holds in
Vienna for ten years, I'll rent the fairest house in it
for three-pence a bay. If you live to see this
come to pass, say Pompey told you so.

ESCALUS:
Thank you, good Pompey; and, in return for your
prophecy, listen to me. I advise you, let me not find
you before me again on any complaint whatsoever;
no, not for dwelling where you do. If I do, Pompey,
I shall beat you to your tent, and prove a shrewd
Caesar to you; in plain dealing, Pompey, I shall
have you whipped. So, for this time, Pompey, fare you well.

POMPEY:
I thank your worship for your good counsel,
but I shall follow it as the flesh and fortune shall
better determine.
Whip me? No, no; let a carman whip his jade.
The valiant heart is not whipped out of his trade.

ESCALUS:
Come here to me, Master Elbow; come here, Master
constable. How long have you been in this place of constable?

ELBOW:
Seven and a half years, sir.

ESCALUS:
I thought, by your readiness in the office, that you had
continued in it for some time. You say, seven years together?

ELBOW:
And a half, sir.

ESCALUS:
Alas, it has been a great pain to you. They do you
wrong to put you on it so often. Are there not men
in your ward sufficient to serve it?

ELBOW:
Faith, sir, few of any wit in such matters. As they
are chosen, they are glad to choose me for them; I
do it for some piece of money, and go through with
it all.

ESCALUS:
See that you bring me the names of some six or seven of
the most sufficient of your parish.

ELBOW:
To your worship's house, sir?

ESCALUS:
To my house. Fare you well.
What time is it, do you think?

Justice:
Eleven, sir.

ESCALUS:
I pray you home to dinner with me.

Justice:
I humbly thank you.

ESCALUS:
It grieves me for the death of Claudio;
but there's no remedy.

Justice:
Lord Angelo is severe.

ESCALUS:
It is but needful.
Mercy is not itself, that often looks so;
pardon is still the nurse of a second woe.
But yet—poor Claudio! There is no remedy.
Come, sir.

Servant:
He is hearing a case; he will come straight away.
I'll tell him about you.

Provost:
Please, do.
I'll know
his pleasure; maybe he will relent. Alas,
he has but offended in a dream!
All sects, all ages smack of this vice; and he
is to die for it!

ANGELO:
Now, what's the matter, Provost?

Provost:
Is it your will that Claudio shall die tomorrow?

ANGELO:
Did I not tell you yes? Did you not have an order?
Why do you ask again?

Provost:
Lest I might be too rash.
Under your good correction, I have seen,
when, after an execution, a judgment has
repented of its doom.

ANGELO:
Go to; let that be mine.
Do your office, or give up your place,
and you shall be well spared.

Provost:
I crave your honor's pardon.
What shall be done, sir, with the groaning Juliet?
She's very near her hour.

ANGELO:
Dispose of her
to some more fitting place, and do it with speed.

Servant:
Here is the sister of the condemned man,
who desires access to you.

ANGELO:
Does he have a sister?

Provost:
Yes, my good lord; a very virtuous maid,
and she is to be shortly of a sisterhood,
if she is not already.

ANGELO:
Well, let her be admitted.
See that the fornicatress is removed.
Let her have what is needful, but not lavish, means;
there shall be an order for it.

Provost:
God save your honor!

ANGELO:
Stay a little while.
You're welcome. What's your will?

ISABELLA:
I am a woeful suitor to your honor,
if it would but please your honor to hear me.

ANGELO:
Well; what's your suit?

ISABELLA:
There is a vice that I most abhor,
and most desire should meet the blow of justice;
for which I would not plead, but that I must;
for which I must not plead, but that I am
at war between my will and my will not.

ANGELO:
Well; the matter?

ISABELLA:
I have a brother who is condemned to die.
I do beseech you, let it be his fault,
and not my brother.

Provost:

ANGELO:
Condemn the fault and not the actor of it?
Why, every fault is condemned before it is done.
Mine would be the very cipher of a function,
to fine the faults whose fine stands in the record,
and let the actor go by.

ISABELLA:
Oh, a just but severe law!
I had a brother, then. Heaven keep your honor!

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
Must he really die?

ANGELO:
Maiden, there is no remedy.

ISABELLA:
Yes; I do think that you might pardon him,
and neither heaven nor man would grieve at the mercy.

ANGELO:
I will not do it.

ISABELLA:
But can you, if you would?

ANGELO:
Look, what I will not do, that I cannot do.

ISABELLA:
But if you could do it, and do the world no wrong,
if your heart were so touched with remorse
as mine is for him?

ANGELO:
He's been sentenced; it's too late.

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
Too late? Why, no; I, that do speak a word,
may call it back again. Well, believe this,
no ceremony that belongs to great ones,
not the king's crown, nor the deputed sword,
the marshal's truncheon, nor the judge's robe,
become them with one half so good a grace
as mercy does.
If he had been as you and you as he,
you would have slipped like him; but he, like you,
would not have been so stern.

ANGELO:
Please, be gone.

ISABELLA:
I wish to heaven I had your potency,
and you were Isabel! Should it then be thus?
No; I would tell you what it was to be a judge,
and what it was to be a prisoner.

LUCIO:

ANGELO:
Your brother is a forfeit of the law,
and you are but wasting your words.

ISABELLA:
Alas, alas!
Why, all the souls that ever were, were once forfeit;
and He that might have best taken the advantage
found out the remedy. How would you be,
if He, which is the top of judgment, should
but judge you as you are? Oh, think on that;
and then mercy will breathe within your lips,
like a new-made man.

ANGELO:
Be content, fair maid;
it is the law, not I, that condemns your brother.
If he were my kinsman, brother, or my son,
it would be thus with him. He must die tomorrow.

ISABELLA:
Tomorrow! Oh, that's sudden! Spare him, spare him!
He's not prepared for death. Even for our kitchens,
we kill the fowl of the season. Shall we serve heaven
with less respect than we do minister
to our gross selves? Good, good my lord, think about it.
Who is it that has died for this offense?
There are many who have committed it.

LUCIO:

ANGELO:
The law has not been dead, though it has slept.
Those many would not have dared to do that evil,
if the first that did infringe the edict
had answered for his deed. Now it is awake,
it takes note of what is done; and, like a prophet,
it looks in a glass, that shows what future evils,
either new, or newly-conceived by remissness,
and so in progress to be hatched and born,
are now to have no successive degrees,
but, before they live, to end.

ISABELLA:
Yet show some pity.

ANGELO:
I show it most of all when I show justice;
for then I pity those I do not know,
which a dismissed offense would afterwards gall;
and I do right to him that, by answering for one foul wrong,
does not live to act another. Be satisfied;
your brother dies tomorrow; be content.

ISABELLA:
So you must be the first that gives this sentence,
and he, that suffers. Oh, it is excellent
to have a giant's strength; but it is tyrannous
to use it like a giant.

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
If great men could thunder
as Jove himself does, Jove would never be quiet,
for every paltry, petty officer
would use his heaven for thunder;
nothing but thunder! Merciful Heaven,
you rather with your sharp and sulphurous bolt
split the un-wedgeable and gnarled oak
than the soft myrtle. But man, proud man,
dressed in a little, brief authority,
most ignorant of what he is most assured of,
his glassy essence, like an angry ape,
plays such fantastic tricks before high heaven
as make the angels weep; who, with our spleens,
would all laugh themselves mortal.

LUCIO:

Provost:

ISABELLA:
We cannot weigh our brother with ourselves.
Great men may jest with saints; it is wit in them,
but in the less, it is a foul profanation.

LUCIO:
You're right, girl; more of that.

ISABELLA:
That which in the captain is but a choleric word,
is flat blasphemy in the soldier.

LUCIO:

ANGELO:
Why do you put these sayings on me?

ISABELLA:
Because authority, though it errs like others,
has yet a kind of medicine in itself,
that skins the vice on the top. Go to your bosom;
knock there, and ask your heart what it knows
that's like my brother's fault. If it confesses
a natural guiltiness such as his,
let it not sound a thought upon your tongue
against my brother's life.

ANGELO:

ISABELLA:
Gentle my lord, turn back.

ANGELO:
I will think about it. Come again tomorrow.

ISABELLA:
Listen to how I'll bribe you. Good my lord, turn back.

ANGELO:
What! Bribe me?

ISABELLA:
Yes, with such gifts that heaven shall share with you.

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
Not with fond shekels of the tested gold,
or stones whose rates are either rich or poor
as fancy values them; but with true prayers
that shall be up at heaven and enter there
before sunrise, prayers from preserved souls,
from fasting maids whose minds are dedicated
to nothing temporal.

ANGELO:
Well; come to me tomorrow.

LUCIO:

ISABELLA:
May heaven keep your honor safe!

ANGELO:

ISABELLA:
At what hour tomorrow
shall I attend your lordship?

ANGELO:
At any time before noon.

ISABELLA:
Save your honor!

ANGELO:
From you, even from your virtue!
What's this, what's this? Is this her fault or mine?
The tempter or the tempted, who sins the most?
Ha!
Not she. Nor does she tempt. But it is I
that, lying by the violet in the sun,
do as the carrion does, not as the flower,
and corrupt with the virtuous season. Can it be
that modesty may more betray our sense
than a woman's lightness? Having enough waste ground,
shall we desire to raze the sanctuary
and pitch our evils there? Oh, fie, fie, fie!
What are you doing, or what are you, Angelo?
Do you desire her foully for those things
that make her good? Oh, let her brother live!
Thieves have authority for their robbery
when judges steal themselves. What, do I love her,
that I desire to hear her speak again,
and feast upon her eyes? What is it I am dreaming of?
Oh, cunning enemy, that, to catch a saint,
you bait your hook with saints! Most dangerous
is that temptation that goads us on
to sin in loving virtue. Never could the strumpet,
with all her double vigor, art, and nature,
once stir my temper; but this virtuous maid
subdues me quite. Even until now,
when men were fond, I smiled and wondered how.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Hail to you, provost! I think that's what you are.

Provost:
I am the provost. What is your will, good friar?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Bound by my charity and my blessed order,
I have come to visit the afflicted spirits
here in the prison. Do me the common right
to let me see them and to make me know
the nature of their crimes, so that I may minister
to them accordingly.

Provost:
I would do more than that, if more were needful.
Look, here comes one: a gentlewoman of mine,
who, falling in the flaws of her own youth,
has blistered her report. She is with child;
and he that got it, is sentenced; a young man
more fit to do another such offense
than to die for this.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
When must he die?

Provost:
As I think, tomorrow.
I have provided for you. Stay a while,
and you shall be conducted.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Do you repent, fair one, of the sin you carry?

JULIET:
I do; and I bear the shame most patiently.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I'll teach you how you shall arraign your conscience,
and try your penitence, to see if it is sound,
or hollowly put on.

JULIET:
I'll gladly learn.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Do you love the man that wronged you?

JULIET:
Yes, as I love the woman that wronged him.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
So then it seems your most offensive act
was mutually committed?

JULIET:
Mutually.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Then your sin was of a heavier kind than his.

JULIET:
I do confess it, and I repent it, father.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is fitting so, daughter. But lest you repent,
as that the sin has brought you to this shame,
which sorrow is always towards ourselves, not heaven,
showing we would not spare heaven as we love it,
but as we stand in fear—

JULIET:
I do repent, as it is an evil,
and I take the shame with joy.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
There, rest.
Your partner, as I hear, must die tomorrow,
and I am going with instruction to him.
May grace go with you, God bless you!

JULIET:
Must he die tomorrow! Oh, injurious love,
that gives me a life, whose very comfort
is still a dying horror!

Provost:
It's a pity about him.

ANGELO:
When I would pray and think, I think and pray
to several subjects. Heaven has my empty words;
while my invention, not hearing my tongue,
anchors on Isabel. Heaven is in my mouth,
as if I did but only chew his name;
and in my heart, the strong and swelling evil
of my conception. The state, on which I studied,
is like a good thing, being often read,
that has grown feared and tedious; yes, my gravity,
in which—let no man hear me—I take pride,
I could with boot change for an idle plume,
which the air beats for nothing. Oh, place, oh, form,
how often do you with your case, your habit,
wrench awe from fools and tie the wiser souls
to your false seeming! Blood, you are blood.
Let's write 'good angel' on the devil's horn.
It's not the devil's crest.
What now! Who's there?

Servant:
One Isabel, a sister, desires access to you.

ANGELO:
Teach her the way.
Oh, heavens!
Why does my blood thus muster to my heart,
making it unable for itself,
and dispossessing all my other parts
of necessary fitness?
So the foolish throngs play with one that swoons;
they all come to help him, and so they stop the air
by which he should revive. And even so,
the general, subject to a well-wished king,
quit their own part, and in obsequious fondness
crowd to his presence, where their untaught love
must needs appear as an offense.
How now, fair maid?

ISABELLA:
I have come to know your pleasure.

ANGELO:
That you might know it, would please me much better
than to demand what it is. Your brother cannot live.

ISABELLA:
Even so. Heaven keep your honor!

ANGELO:
Yet he may live for a while; and, it may be,
as long as you or I. Yet he must die.

ISABELLA:
Under your sentence?

ANGELO:
Yes.

ISABELLA:
When, I beseech you? So that in his reprieve,
whether longer or shorter, he may be so prepared
that his soul does not sicken.

ANGELO:
Ha! Fie, these filthy vices! It would be as good
to pardon him that has stolen from nature
a man already made, as to remit
their saucy sweetness that coin heaven's image
in stamps that are forbidden. It is all as easy
falsely to take away a life truly made
as to put metal in restrained means
to make a false one.

ISABELLA:
It is set down so in heaven, but not on earth.

ANGELO:
Do you say so? Then I shall pose you quickly.
Which would you rather, that the most just law
now took your brother's life; or, to redeem him,
you gave up your body to such sweet uncleanness
as she that he has stained?

ISABELLA:
Sir, believe this,
I would rather give my body than my soul.

ANGELO:
I am not talking about your soul. Our compelled sins
stand more for number than for account.

ISABELLA:
What do you say?

ANGELO:
No, I'll not guarantee that; for I can speak
against the thing I say. Answer me this:
I, now the voice of the recorded law,
pronounce a sentence on your brother's life.
Might there not be a charity in sin
to save this brother's life?

ISABELLA:
If it pleases you to do it,
I'll take it as a peril to my soul,
it is no sin at all, but charity.

ANGELO:
If it pleased you to do it at the peril of your soul,
it would be an equal poise of sin and charity.

ISABELLA:
That I beg for his life, if it is a sin,
heaven let me bear it! You granting my suit,
if that is a sin, I'll make it my morning prayer
to have it added to my faults,
and nothing of your answer.

ANGELO:
No, but hear me.
Your sense does not pursue mine. Either you are ignorant,
or you seem so craftily; and that's not good.

ISABELLA:
Let me be ignorant, and good in nothing,
but graciously to know that I am no better.

ANGELO:
Thus wisdom wishes to appear most bright
when it does tax itself; as these black masks
proclaim an enshielded beauty ten times louder
than beauty could, if it were displayed. But mark me;
to be received plainly, I'll speak more grossly.
Your brother is to die.

ISABELLA:
So.

ANGELO:
And his offense is so, as it appears,
accountable to the law upon that pain.

ISABELLA:
True.

ANGELO:
Admit no other way to save his life—
as I do not subscribe to that, nor to any other,
but in the loss of the question—that you, his sister,
finding yourself desired by such a person,
whose credit with the judge, or his own great place,
could fetch your brother from the manacles
of the all-building law; and that there were
no earthly means to save him, but that either
you must lay down the treasures of your body
to this supposed one, or else let him suffer;
what would you do?

ISABELLA:
As much for my poor brother as for myself.
That is, if I were under the terms of death,
I would wear the impression of keen whips as rubies,
and strip myself to death, as to a bed
that I have been sick with longing for, before I would yield
my body up to shame.

ANGELO:
Then your brother must die.

ISABELLA:
And it would be the cheaper way.
It would be better for a brother to die at once,
than for a sister, by redeeming him,
to die forever.

ANGELO:
Were you not then as cruel as the sentence
that you have slandered so?

ISABELLA:
Ignominy in ransom and free pardon
are of two houses. Lawful mercy
is nothing akin to foul redemption.

ANGELO:
You seemed of late to make the law a tyrant;
and you rather proved the sliding of your brother
to be a merriment than a vice.

ISABELLA:
Oh, pardon me, my lord; it often happens,
to have what we would have, we do not speak what we mean.
I somewhat excuse the thing I hate,
for his advantage, whom I dearly love.

ANGELO:
We are all frail.

ISABELLA:
Otherwise, let my brother die,
if not a companion, but only he
owes and succeeds your weakness.

ANGELO:
No, women are frail too.

ISABELLA:
Yes, as the glasses where they view themselves;
which are as easily broken as they make forms.
Women! Help Heaven! Men mar their creation
by profiting from them. No, call us ten times frail;
for we are as soft as our complexions are,
and as credulous to false prints.

ANGELO:
I think it is well.
And from this testimony of your own sex—
since I suppose we are made to be no stronger
than faults may shake our frames—let me be bold;
I do arrest your words. Be what you are,
that is, a woman; if you are more, you are none;
if you are one, as you are well expressed
by all external warrants, show it now,
by putting on the destined livery.

ISABELLA:
I have no tongue but one. Gentle my lord,
let me entreat you to speak the former language.

ANGELO:
Plainly, understand, I love you.

ISABELLA:
My brother loved Juliet,
and you tell me that he shall die for it.

ANGELO:
He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.

ISABELLA:
I know your virtue has a license in it,
which seems a little fouler than it is,
to entice others.

ANGELO:
Believe me, on my honor,
my words express my purpose.

ISABELLA:
Ha! Little honor to be much believed,
and a most pernicious purpose! Seeming, seeming!
I will proclaim you, Angelo; look for it.
Sign me a present pardon for my brother,
or with an outstretched throat I'll tell the world aloud
what kind of man you are.

ANGELO:
Who will believe you, Isabel?
My unsoiled name, the austereness of my life,
my word against you, and my place in the state,
will so outweigh your accusation,
that you shall stifle in your own report
and smell of calumny. I have begun,
and now I give my sensual race the rein.
Fit your consent to my sharp appetite;
lay by all nicety and prolixious blushes,
that banish what they sue for; redeem your brother
by yielding up your body to my will;
or else he must not only die the death,
but your unkindness shall draw out his death
to a lingering sufferance. Answer me tomorrow,
or, by the affection that now guides me most,
I'll prove a tyrant to him. As for you,
say what you can, my false outweighs your true.

ISABELLA:
To whom should I complain? If I told this,
who would believe me? Oh, perilous mouths,
that bear in them one and the same tongue,
either of condemnation or of approval;
bidding the law to make a courtesy to their will,
hooking both right and wrong to their appetite,
to follow as it draws! I'll go to my brother.
Though he has fallen by the prompting of his blood,
yet he has in him such a mind of honor,
that, if he had twenty heads to tender down
on twenty bloody blocks, he would yield them up,
before his sister should stoop her body
to such abhorred pollution.
Then, Isabel, live chaste, and, brother, die.
Our chastity is more than our brother.
I'll tell him yet of Angelo's request,
and fit his mind to death, for his soul's rest.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
So then you hope for a pardon from Lord Angelo?

CLAUDIO:
The miserable have no other medicine
but only hope.
I have hope to live, and I am prepared to die.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Be absolute for death; either death or life
shall thereby be the sweeter. Reason thus with life:
if I lose you, I lose a thing
that none but fools would keep. You are a breath,
servile to all the skyey influences,
that dost afflict this habitation, where you keep,
hourly. Merely, you are death's fool;
for him you labor to shun by your flight,
and yet you run toward him still. You are not noble;
for all the accommodations that you bear
are nursed by baseness. You are by no means valiant;
for you fear the soft and tender fork
of a poor worm. Your best of rest is sleep,
and you often provoke that; yet you grossly fear
your death, which is no more. You are not yourself;
for you exist on many a thousand grains
that issue out of dust. You are not happy;
for what you have not, you still strive to get,
and what you have, you forget. You are not certain;
for your complexion shifts to strange effects,
after the moon. If you are rich, you are poor;
for, like an ass whose back with ingots bows,
you bear your heavy riches but for a journey,
and death unloads you. You have no friend;
for your own bowels, which call you sire,
the mere effusion of your proper loins,
do curse the gout, the serpigo, and the rheum,
for not ending you sooner. You have neither youth nor age,
but, as it were, an after-dinner's sleep,
dreaming on both; for all your blessed youth
becomes as aged, and begs the alms
of palsied old age; and when you are old and rich,
you have neither heat, affection, limb, nor beauty,
to make your riches pleasant. What is yet in this
that bears the name of life? Yet in this life
lie hidden more thousand deaths. Yet we fear death,
that makes these odds all even.

CLAUDIO:
I humbly thank you.
To sue to live, I find I seek to die;
and, seeking death, I find life. Let it come on.

ISABELLA:

Provost:
Who's there? Come in. The wish deserves a welcome.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Dear sir, I'll visit you again before long.

CLAUDIO:
Most holy sir, I thank you.

ISABELLA:
My business is a word or two with Claudio.

Provost:
And you are very welcome. Look, signior, here is your sister.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Provost, a word with you.

Provost:
As many as you please.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Bring me to hear them speak, where I may be concealed.

CLAUDIO:
Now, sister, what's the comfort?

ISABELLA:
Why,
as all comforts are; most good, most good indeed.
Lord Angelo, having affairs in heaven,
intends you as his swift ambassador,
where you shall be an everlasting legate.
Therefore, make your best appointment with speed;
tomorrow you set out.

CLAUDIO:
Is there no remedy?

ISABELLA:
None, but such a remedy as, to save a head,
to cleave a heart in two.

CLAUDIO:
But is there any?

ISABELLA:
Yes, brother, you may live.
There is a devilish mercy in the judge,
if you'll implore it, that will free your life,
but it will fetter you until death.

CLAUDIO:
Perpetual imprisonment?

ISABELLA:
Yes, just so; perpetual imprisonment, a restraint,
though you had all the world's vastness,
to a determined scope.

CLAUDIO:
But in what way?

ISABELLA:
In such a way that, if you consent to it,
it would strip your honor from the trunk you bear,
and leave you naked.

CLAUDIO:
Let me know the point.

ISABELLA:
Oh, I fear you, Claudio; and I quake,
lest you should entertain a feverous life,
and respect six or seven more winters
than a perpetual honor. Do you dare to die?
The sense of death is mostly in the apprehension;
and the poor beetle, that we tread upon,
in corporal sufferance finds a pang as great
as when a giant dies.

CLAUDIO:
Why do you give me this shame?
Do you think I can fetch a resolution
from flowery tenderness? If I must die,
I will encounter darkness as a bride,
and hug it in my arms.

ISABELLA:
There spoke my brother; there my father's grave
did utter forth a voice. Yes, you must die.
You are too noble to conserve a life
in base appliances. This outward-sainted deputy,
whose settled visage and deliberate word
nips youth in the head and conceals follies
as a falcon does a fowl, is yet a devil.
If his filth within were cast out, he would appear
a pond as deep as hell.

CLAUDIO:
The pretentious Angelo!

ISABELLA:
Oh, it is the cunning livery of hell,
to invest and cover the most damned body
in pretentious guards! Do you think, Claudio,
that if I would yield him my virginity,
you might be freed?

CLAUDIO:
Oh heavens! It cannot be.

ISABELLA:
Yes, he would give it to you, from this rank offense,
so that you could offend him still. This night is the time
that I should do what I abhor to name,
or else you will die tomorrow.

CLAUDIO:
You shall not do it.

ISABELLA:
Oh, if it were but my life,
I would throw it down for your deliverance
as frankly as a pin.

CLAUDIO:
Thanks, dear Isabel.

ISABELLA:
Be ready, Claudio, for your death tomorrow.

CLAUDIO:
Yes. Does he have affections in him,
that he can thus bite the law by the nose,
when he would force it? Surely, it is no sin,
or of the seven deadly sins, it is the least.

ISABELLA:
Which is the least?

CLAUDIO:
If it were damnable, he being so wise,
why would he, for a momentary trick,
be perpetually fined? Oh, Isabel!

ISABELLA:
What does my brother say?

CLAUDIO:
Death is a fearful thing.

ISABELLA:
And a shamed life is a hateful one.

CLAUDIO:
Yes, but to die, and go we know not where;
to lie in cold obstruction and to rot;
this sensible, warm motion to become
a kneaded clod; and the delighted spirit
to bathe in fiery floods, or to reside
in the thrilling region of thick-ribbed ice;
to be imprisoned in the viewless winds,
and to be blown with restless violence round about
the pendent world; or to be worse than the worst
of those that lawless and uncertain thought
imagine howling. It is too horrible!
The weariest and most loathed worldly life
that age, ache, poverty, and imprisonment
can lay on nature is a paradise
to what we fear of death.

ISABELLA:
Alas, alas!

CLAUDIO:
Sweet sister, let me live.
What sin you do to save a brother's life,
nature dispenses with the deed so far
that it becomes a virtue.

ISABELLA:
Oh, you beast!
Oh, faithless coward! Oh, dishonest wretch!
Will you be made a man out of my vice?
Isn't it a kind of incest, to take life
from your own sister's shame? What should I think?
Heaven shield my mother played my father fair!
For such a warped slip of wilderness
never issued from his blood. Take my defiance!
Die, perish! If but my bending down
could reprieve you from your fate, it should proceed.
I'll pray a thousand prayers for your death,
and not a word to save you.

CLAUDIO:
No, hear me, Isabel.

ISABELLA:
Oh, fie, fie, fie!
Your sin is not accidental, but a trade.
Mercy to you would prove itself a bawd.
It's best you die quickly.

CLAUDIO:
Oh, hear me, Isabella!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Vouchsafe a word, young sister, but one word.

ISABELLA:
What is your will?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
If you could dispense with your leisure, I would by and
by have some speech with you. The satisfaction I
would require is also for your own benefit.

ISABELLA:
I have no superfluous leisure; my stay must be
stolen out of other affairs; but I will attend you for a while.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Son, I have overheard what has passed between you
and your sister. Angelo never had the purpose to
corrupt her; he has only made an essay of her
virtue to practice his judgment with the disposition
of natures. She, having the truth of honor in her,
has made him that gracious denial which he is most
glad to receive. I am a confessor to Angelo, and I
know this to be true; therefore, prepare yourself for
death. Do not satisfy your resolution with hopes
that are fallible. Tomorrow you must die; go to
your knees and make ready.

CLAUDIO:
Let me ask my sister for her pardon. I am so out of love
with life that I will sue to be rid of it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Hold you there. Farewell.
Provost, a word with you!

Provost:
What is your will, father?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
That now you have come, you will be gone. Leave me
for a while with the maid. My mind promises with my
habit that no loss shall touch her by my company.

Provost:
In good time.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
The hand that has made you fair has made you good.
The goodness that is cheap in beauty makes beauty
brief in goodness; but grace, being the soul of
your complexion, shall keep the body of it ever
fair. The assault that Angelo has made on you,
fortune has conveyed to my understanding; and, but
that frailty has examples for his falling, I should
wonder at Angelo. How will you do to content this
substitute, and to save your brother?

ISABELLA:
I am now going to resolve him. I would rather my
brother die by the law than that my son should be
unlawfully born. But, oh, how much is the good duke
deceived in Angelo! If he ever returns and I can
speak to him, I will either open my lips in vain, or
discover his government.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
That will not be much amiss. Yet, as the matter
now stands, he will avoid your accusation; he was only
making a trial of you. Therefore, fasten your ear on my
advice. To the love I have in doing good, a
remedy presents itself. I do make myself believe
that you may most uprighteously do a poor, wronged
lady a merited benefit; redeem your brother from
the angry law; do no stain to your own gracious
person; and much please the absent duke, if
peradventure he shall ever return to have a hearing of
this business.

ISABELLA:
Let me hear you speak further. I have the spirit to do
anything that does not appear foul in the truth of my spirit.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Virtue is bold, and goodness is never fearful. Have
you not heard of Mariana, the sister of
Frederick the great soldier who was lost at sea?

ISABELLA:
I have heard of the lady, and good words went with her name.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
She should have married this Angelo; she was affianced
to him by oath, and the nuptial was appointed. Between
the time of the contract and the limit of the
solemnity, her brother Frederick was wrecked at sea,
having in that perished vessel the dowry of his
sister. But mark how heavily this befell the
poor gentlewoman. There she lost a noble and
renowned brother, who in his love toward her was ever most
kind and natural; with him, the portion and sinew of
her fortune, her marriage-dowry; with both, her
combinate husband, this well-seeming Angelo.

ISABELLA:
Can this be so? Did Angelo leave her so?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He left her in her tears, and did not dry one of them
with his comfort; he swallowed his vows whole,
pretending to have discovered dishonor in her. In short,
he bestowed her on her own lamentation, which she still
wears for his sake; and he, a marble to her tears,
is washed with them, but does not relent.

ISABELLA:
What a merit it would be in death to take this poor maid
from the world! What corruption in this life, that
it will let this man live! But how out of this can she be of any avail?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is a rupture that you may easily heal. And the
cure of it not only saves your brother, but keeps
you from dishonor in doing it.

ISABELLA:
Show me how, good father.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
This forenamed maid still has in her the continuance
of her first affection. His unjust unkindness, that
in all reason should have quenched her love, has,
like an impediment in the current, made it more
violent and unruly. Go to Angelo; answer his
request with a plausible obedience; agree with
his demands to the point; only refer yourself to
this advantage, first, that your stay with him may
not be long; that the time may have all shadow and
silence in it; and the place should be convenient.
This being granted in course—and now follows
all—we shall advise this wronged maid to take your
place, and go in your stead. If the encounter
acknowledges itself hereafter, it may compel him to
her recompense. And here, by this, your brother is
saved, your honor is untainted, the poor Mariana is
advantaged, and the corrupt deputy is exposed. I will
frame the maid and make her fit for his attempt. If you
think it is well to carry this as you may, the doubleness
of the benefit defends the deceit from reproof.
What do you think of it?

ISABELLA:
The image of it gives me content already; and I
trust it will grow to a most prosperous perfection.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It lies much in your holding up. Hurry speedily
to Angelo. If he entreats you to his
bed for this night, give him a promise of satisfaction. I will
go to Saint Luke's presently. There, at the moated
grange, this dejected Mariana resides. At that
place, call upon me; and dispatch with Angelo, so that
it may be done quickly.

ISABELLA:
I thank you for this comfort. Fare you well, good father.

ELBOW:
No, if there is no remedy for it, but that you will
needs buy and sell men and women like beasts, we
shall have the whole world drinking brown and white bastard wine.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh heavens! What stuff is here?

POMPEY:
It was never a merry world since, of two usuries, the
merriest was put down, and the worse was allowed by
order of law a furred gown to keep him warm; and
furred with fox and lamb-skins too, to signify that
craft, being richer than innocency, stands for the facing.

ELBOW:
Come your way, sir. Bless you, good father friar.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
And you, good brother father. What offense has
this man committed against you, sir?

ELBOW:
Well, sir, he has offended the law. And, sir, we
take him to be a thief too, sir; for we have found
upon him, sir, a strange picklock, which we have
sent to the deputy.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Fie, sirrah! A bawd, a wicked bawd!
The evil that you cause to be done,
that is your means to live. Just think
what it is to cram a stomach or clothe a back
from such a filthy vice. Say to yourself,
from their abominable and beastly touches
I drink, I eat, I array myself, and I live.
Can you believe your living is a life,
so stinkingly depending? Go and mend, go and mend.

POMPEY:
Indeed, it does stink in some way, sir; but yet,
sir, I would prove—

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No, if the devil has given you proofs for sin,
you will prove his. Take him to prison, officer.
Correction and instruction must both work
before this rude beast will profit.

ELBOW:
He must go before the deputy, sir; he has given him
a warning. The deputy cannot abide a whoremaster. If
he is a whoremonger, and he comes before him, he would be
as good as gone a mile on his errand.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
That we were all, as some would seem to be,
from our faults, as faults from seeming, free!

ELBOW:
His neck will come to your waist—a cord, sir.

POMPEY:
I spy comfort; I cry for bail. Here's a gentleman and a
friend of mine.

LUCIO:
How now, noble Pompey! What, at the wheels of
Caesar? Are you being led in triumph? What, is there
none of Pygmalion's images, newly made into a woman, to be
had now, for putting one's hand in one's pocket and
extracting it clutched? What is your reply, ha? What
do you say to this tune, matter, and method? Is it
not drowned in the last rain, ha? What do you say,
Trot? Is the world as it was, man? Which is
the way? Is it sad, and few words? Or how? What is
the trick of it?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Still like this, and like this; still worse!

LUCIO:
How is my dear morsel, your mistress? Is she still procuring, ha?

POMPEY:
In truth, sir, she has eaten up all her beef, and she
is herself in the tub.

LUCIO:
Why, it's good; it's the right of it; it must be
so. It's always your fresh whore and your powdered bawd,
an unshunned consequence; it must be so. Are you going
to prison, Pompey?

POMPEY:
Yes, in faith, sir.

LUCIO:
Why, it is not amiss, Pompey. Farewell. Go, and say I
sent you there. For debt, Pompey? Or how?

ELBOW:
For being a bawd, for being a bawd.

LUCIO:
Well, then, imprison him. If imprisonment is the
due of a bawd, why, it is his right. He is a bawd,
doubtless, and of antiquity too; bawd-born.
Farewell, good Pompey. Commend me to the prison,
Pompey. You will turn into a good husband now, Pompey; you
will keep the house.

POMPEY:
I hope, sir, that your good worship will be my bail.

LUCIO:
No, indeed, I will not, Pompey; it is not the custom.
I will pray, Pompey, that your bondage may increase. If
you do not take it patiently, why, your mettle is the
more. Adieu, trusty Pompey. Bless you, friar.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
And you.

LUCIO:
Does Bridget still paint, Pompey, ha?

ELBOW:
Come your ways, sir; come.

POMPEY:
You will not bail me, then, sir?

LUCIO:
Then, Pompey, nor now. What news is there abroad, friar?
What news?

ELBOW:
Come your ways, sir; come.

LUCIO:
Go to your kennel, Pompey; go.
What news, friar, of the duke?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I know none. Can you tell me of any?

LUCIO:
Some say he is with the Emperor of Russia; others
say he is in Rome. But where is he, do you think?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I don't know where; but wherever he is, I wish him well.

LUCIO:
It was a mad, fantastical trick of him to steal away from
the state, and to usurp the beggary he was never born
to. Lord Angelo is acting like a duke well in his absence; he
is putting transgression to it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He does well in it.

LUCIO:
A little more leniency toward lechery would do no harm in
him. He is a little too crabbed in that way, friar.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is too general a vice, and severity must cure it.

LUCIO:
Yes, in good sooth, the vice is of a great kindred;
it is well allied. But it is impossible to extirpate
it completely, friar, until eating and drinking are put
down. They say this Angelo was not made by man and
woman after this downright way of creation. Is it
true, do you think?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
How should he be made, then?

LUCIO:
Some report that a sea-maid spawned him; some, that he
was begotten between two stock-fishes. But it is
certain that when he makes water, his urine is
congealed ice; that I know to be true. And he is a
motion generative; that's infallible.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You are pleasant, sir, and speak quickly.

LUCIO:
Why, what a ruthless thing this is in him, for the
rebellion of a codpiece to take away the life of a
man! Would the duke that is absent have done this?
Before he would have hanged a man for getting a
hundred bastards, he would have paid for the nursing of
a thousand. He had some feeling for the sport. He
knew the service, and that instructed him to be merciful.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I never heard that the absent duke was much detected for
women; he was not inclined that way.

LUCIO:
Oh, sir, you are deceived.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It's not possible.

LUCIO:
Who, not the duke? Yes, your beggar of fifty; and
his habit was to put a ducat in her clack-dish. The
duke had his quirks. He would get drunk too;
let me inform you of that.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You are surely doing him wrong.

LUCIO:
Sir, I was one of his intimates. The duke was a shy fellow,
and I believe I know the cause of his
withdrawal.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
What, I pray you, might be the cause?

LUCIO:
No, pardon me; it's a secret that must be locked within the
teeth and the lips. But this I can let you
understand, the greater part of the subjects held the
duke to be wise.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Wise! Why, there is no question but that he was.

LUCIO:
A very superficial, ignorant, un-weighing fellow.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Either this is envy in you, folly, or a mistake.
The very stream of his life and the business he has
helmed must, upon a warranted need, give him a better
proclamation. Let him be but testified to in his own
bringings-forth, and he shall appear to the
envious as a scholar, a statesman, and a soldier.
Therefore, you speak unskillfully. Or if your
knowledge is more, it is much darkened by your malice.

LUCIO:
Sir, I know him, and I love him.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Love talks with better knowledge, and knowledge with
dearer love.

LUCIO:
Come, sir, I know what I know.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I can hardly believe that, since you don't know what
you are speaking of. But, if ever the duke returns, as our
prayers are that he may, let me ask you to make your
answer before him. If what you have spoken is honest,
you will have the courage to maintain it. I am bound to call
upon you; and, I pray you, your name?

LUCIO:
Sir, my name is Lucio; I am well known to the duke.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He shall know you better, sir, if I may live to
report you.

LUCIO:
I am not afraid of you.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, you hope the duke will return no more; or you
imagine me to be too un-harmful an opponent. But indeed, I
can do you little harm; you'll forswear this again.

LUCIO:
I'll be hanged first. You are deceived in me,
friar. But no more of this. Can you tell me if
Claudio will die tomorrow or not?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Why should he die, sir?

LUCIO:
Why? For filling a bottle with a funnel. I wish
the duke we are talking about would return again. This
ungenitured agent will unpeople the province with
continence; sparrows must not build in his
house-eaves, because they are lecherous. The duke
yet would have dark deeds darkly answered; he would
never bring them to light. I wish he were returned!
Indeed, this Claudio is condemned for un-trussing.
Farewell, good friar. I pray you, pray for me. The
duke, I say to you again, would eat mutton on
Fridays. He's not past it yet, and I say to you,
he would mouth with a beggar, though she smelled of brown
bread and garlic. Say that I said so. Farewell.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No might nor greatness in mortality
can escape censure; back-wounding calumny
strikes the whitest virtue. What king is so strong
that he can tie up the gall in the slanderous tongue?
But who comes here?

ESCALUS:
Go; away with her to prison!

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
Good my lord, be good to me; your honor is accounted
a merciful man; good my lord.

ESCALUS:
Double and triple admonition, and still you forfeit in
the same kind! This would make mercy swear and play
the tyrant.

Provost:
A bawd of eleven years' continuance, may it please
your honor.

MISTRESS OVERDONE:
My lord, this is one Lucio's information against me.
Mistress Kate Keepdown was with child by him in the
duke's time; he promised her marriage. His child
is a year and a quarter old, come Philip and Jacob.
I have kept it myself; and see how he goes about to abuse me!

ESCALUS:
That fellow is a fellow of much license. Let him be
called before us. Away with her to prison! Go to;
no more words.
Provost, my brother Angelo will not be altered;
Claudio must die tomorrow. Let him be furnished
with divines, and have all charitable preparation.
If my brother were to work by my pity, it would not be
so with him.

Provost:
If it pleases you, this friar has been with him, and has
advised him for the entertainment of death.

ESCALUS:
Good evening, good father.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
May bliss and goodness be on you!

ESCALUS:
From where are you?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Not of this country, though my chance is now
to use it for my time. I am a brother
of a gracious order, lately come from the See
on special business from his holiness.

ESCALUS:
What news is there abroad in the world?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
None, but that there is so great a fever on
goodness, that the dissolution of it must cure it.
Novelty is only in request; and it is as dangerous
to be aged in any kind of course, as it is virtuous
to be constant in any undertaking. There is scarce
enough truth alive to make societies secure; but
enough security to make fellowships accursed. Much
upon this riddle runs the wisdom of the world. This
news is old enough, yet it is every day's news. I
pray you, sir, of what disposition was the duke?

ESCALUS:
One that, above all other strifes, contended
especially to know himself.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
What pleasure was he given to?

ESCALUS:
He would rather rejoice to see another merry, than be merry at
anything which professed to make him rejoice. He was a
gentleman of all temperance. But let us leave him to
his events, with a prayer that they may prove prosperous;
and let me desire to know how you find Claudio
prepared. I am made to understand that you have
given him a visitation.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He professes to have received no sinister measure
from his judge, but most willingly humbles himself
to the determination of justice. Yet he had framed
for himself, by the instruction of his frailty, many
deceiving promises of life; which I, by my good
leisure, have discredited to him, and now he is
resolved to die.

ESCALUS:
You have paid the heavens your function, and the
prisoner the very debt of your calling. I have
labored for the poor gentleman to the extremest
shore of my modesty. But my brother justice, I have
found so severe, that he has forced me to tell him
he is indeed Justice.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
If his own life answers the straightness of his
proceeding, it will become him well; in which, if he
chances to fail, he has sentenced himself.

ESCALUS:
I am going to visit the prisoner. Fare you well.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Peace be with you!
He who will bear the sword of heaven
should be as holy as he is severe;
he should be a pattern in himself to know,
grace to stand, and virtue to go;
paying no more nor less to others
than by weighing his own self-offenses.
Shame to him whose cruel striking
kills for faults of his own liking!
Twice, three times the shame on Angelo,
to weed my vice and let his grow!
Oh, what may a man hide within him,
though he may have an angel on the outward side!
How may a likeness made in crimes,
making a practice on the times,
to draw with idle spiders' strings
most ponderous and substantial things!
I must apply craft against vice.
With Angelo tonight shall lie
his old betrothed but despised one;
so disguise shall, by the disguised,
pay with falsehood false exacting,
and perform an old contracting.


MARIANA:
Break off your song, and hurry away quickly.
Here comes a man of comfort, whose advice
has often stilled my brawling discontent.
I beg your mercy, sir; and I could well wish
you had not found me here so musical.
Let me excuse myself, and believe me so,
my mirth greatly displeased me, but it pleased my woe.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It's good; though music often has such a charm
to make bad things good, and to provoke good things to harm.
I pray you, tell me, has anybody inquired
for me here today? Much upon this time have
I promised to meet here.

MARIANA:
You have not been inquired after.
I have sat here all day.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I do constantly believe you. The time has come even
now. I shall crave your forbearance a little. Maybe
I will call upon you soon, for some advantage to yourself.

MARIANA:
I am always bound to you.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Very well met, and you are welcome.
What is the news from this good deputy?

ISABELLA:
He has a garden enclosed with a brick wall,
the western side of which is backed by a vineyard;
and to that vineyard is a planked gate,
that makes its opening with this bigger key.
This other one commands a little door
which leads from the vineyard to the garden;
there I have made my promise
upon the heavy middle of the night
to call upon him.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
But will you, on your own knowledge, find this way?

ISABELLA:
I have taken a due and wary note of it.
With whispering and most guilty diligence,
in an action full of precept, he showed me
the way twice over.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Are there no other tokens
agreed upon between you concerning her observance?

ISABELLA:
No, none, but only a repair in the dark;
and that I have possessed him that my most stay
can be but brief; for I have made him know
that I have a servant that comes along with me,
that waits upon me, whose persuasion is
that I have come about my brother.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It's well borne up.
I have not yet made known to Mariana
a word of this. What, ho! Within! Come forth!
I pray you, be acquainted with this maid;
she comes to do you good.

ISABELLA:
I do desire the like.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Do you persuade yourself that I respect you?

MARIANA:
Good friar, I know you do, and have found it so.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Take, then, this companion of yours by the hand,
who has a story ready for your ear.
I shall attend your leisure. But make haste;
the vaporous night approaches.

MARIANA:
Will it please you to walk aside?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, place and greatness! Millions of false eyes
are stuck upon you. Volumes of reports
run with these false and most contrary quests
upon your doings. A thousand escapes of wit
make you the father of their idle dreams
and rack you in their fancies.
Welcome, how did it go?

ISABELLA:
She'll take the enterprise upon herself, father,
if you advise it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is not my consent,
but my entreaty too.

ISABELLA:
You have little to say
when you depart from him, but, soft and low,
'Remember now my brother.'

MARIANA:
Don't worry about me.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Nor, gentle daughter, do not fear at all.
He is your husband on a pre-contract.
To bring you thus together is no sin,
since the justice of your title to him
flourishes the deceit. Come, let us go.
Our corn is to be reaped, for our tithe is yet to be sown.

Provost:
Come here, sirrah. Can you cut off a man's head?

POMPEY:
If the man is a bachelor, sir, I can; but if he is a
married man, he's his wife's head, and I can never
cut off a woman's head.

Provost:
Come, sir, leave me your quibbles, and give me a
direct answer. Tomorrow morning, Claudio
and Barnardine are to die. Here in our prison, there is a common
executioner, who in his office lacks a helper. If
you will take it upon you to assist him, it shall
redeem you from your chains; if not, you shall have
your full time of imprisonment and your deliverance
with an un-pitied whipping, for you have been a
notorious bawd.

POMPEY:
Sir, I have been an unlawful bawd for time out of mind;
but yet I will be content to be a lawful hangman. I
would be glad to receive some instruction from my
fellow partner.

Provost:
What, ho! Abhorson! Where's Abhorson, there?

ABHORSON:
Are you calling, sir?

Provost:
Sirrah, here's a fellow who will help you tomorrow in
your execution. If you think it is fitting, make a contract with
him by the year, and let him stay here with you; if
not, use him for the present and dismiss him. He
cannot plead his reputation with you; he has been a bawd.

ABHORSON:
A bawd, sir? Fie upon him! He will discredit our mystery.

Provost:
Go to, sir; you weigh equally; a feather will turn
the scale.

POMPEY:
Please, sir, by your good favor—for surely, sir, a
good favor you have, except that you have a hanging
look—do you call, sir, your occupation a mystery?

ABHORSON:
Yes, sir; a mystery.

POMPEY:
Painting, sir, I have heard say, is a mystery; and
your whores, sir, being members of my occupation,
using painting, do prove my occupation a mystery.
But what mystery there should be in hanging, if I
should be hanged, I cannot imagine.

ABHORSON:
Sir, it is a mystery.

POMPEY:
Proof?

ABHORSON:
Every true man's apparel fits your thief. If it is
too little for your thief, your true man thinks it is
big enough; if it is too big for your thief, your
thief thinks it is little enough. So every true man's
apparel fits your thief.

Provost:
Are you agreed?

POMPEY:
Sir, I will serve him; for I find that your hangman is
a more penitent trade than your bawd; he more
often asks for forgiveness.

Provost:
You, sirrah, provide your block and your axe
tomorrow at four o'clock.

ABHORSON:
Come on, bawd; I will instruct you in my trade; follow me.

POMPEY:
I do desire to learn, sir. And I hope, if you have
occasion to use me for your own turn, you shall find
me ready; for truly, sir, for your kindness I owe you
a good turn.

Provost:
Call hither Barnardine and Claudio.
The one has my pity; not a jot the other,
being a murderer, though he were my brother.
Look, here's the warrant, Claudio, for your death.
It is now dead midnight, and by eight tomorrow
you must be made immortal. Where's Barnardine?

CLAUDIO:
He is as fast asleep as a guiltless laborer
when he lies stiffly in the traveler's bones.
He will not wake.

Provost:
Who can do any good on him?
Well, go, and prepare yourself.
But, listen, what noise is that?
Heaven give your spirits comfort!
By and by.
I hope it is some pardon or reprieve
for the most gentle Claudio.
Welcome, father.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
May the best and most wholesome spirits of the night
envelop you, good Provost! Who has called here of late?

Provost:
None, since the curfew rang.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Not Isabel?

Provost:
No.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
They will, then, before long.

Provost:
What comfort is there for Claudio?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
There's some in hope.

Provost:
He is a bitter deputy.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Not so, not so; his life is paralleled
even with the stroke and line of his great justice.
He does with holy abstinence subdue
that in himself which he spurs on his power
to qualify in others. If he were stained with that
which he corrects, then he would be tyrannical;
but this being so, he is just.
Now they have come.
This is a gentle provost. It is seldom that
the steeled jailer is the friend of men.
What now! What noise? That spirit is possessed with haste
that wounds the un-resisting postern with these strokes.

Provost:
He must stay there until the officer
arises to let him in. He has been called up.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Do you have no countermand for Claudio yet,
but that he must die tomorrow?

Provost:
None, sir, none.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
As near the dawning, provost, as it is,
you shall hear more before morning.

Provost:
Happily,
you know something; yet I believe there is no
countermand; we have no such example.
Besides, upon the very siege of justice,
Lord Angelo has professed the contrary
to the public ear.
This is his lordship's man.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
And here comes Claudio's pardon.

Messenger:

Provost:
I shall obey him.

DUKE VINCENTIO:

Provost:
I told you. Lord Angelo, perhaps thinking me remiss
in my office, awakens me with this unusual
putting-on; it seems strange to me, for he has not used it before.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Please, let's hear.

Provost:

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Who is that Barnardine who is to be executed in the
afternoon?

Provost:
A Bohemian born, but nursed and bred here; one
that has been a prisoner for nine years.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
How did it happen that the absent duke had not either
delivered him to his liberty or executed him? I
have heard it was always his manner to do so.

Provost:
His friends still worked for reprieves for him. And,
indeed, his deed, until now in the government of Lord
Angelo, did not come to an undoubtful proof.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Is it now apparent?

Provost:
Most manifest, and not denied by himself.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Has he borne himself penitently in prison? How
does he seem to be touched?

Provost:
A man that apprehends death no more dreadfully than
as a drunken sleep; careless, reckless, and fearless
of what's past, present, or to come; insensible of
mortality, and desperately mortal.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He needs advice.

Provost:
He will hear none. He has always had the liberty
of the prison; if you gave him leave to escape from here, he
would not. He is drunk many times a day, if not many days
entirely drunk. We have very often woken him up, as if
to carry him to execution, and shown him a seeming
warrant for it. It has not moved him at all.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
More of him later. There is written in your brow,
provost, honesty and constancy. If I do not read it
truly, my ancient skill is beguiling me; but, in the
boldness of my cunning, I will lay myself in hazard.
Claudio, whom you have a warrant to execute here, is
no greater a forfeit to the law than Angelo who has
sentenced him. To make you understand this in a
manifested effect, I crave but a four-day respite;
for which you are to do me both a present and a
dangerous courtesy.

Provost:
Please, sir, in what?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
In delaying his death.

Provost:
Alas, how may I do it, having the hour limited,
and an express command, under penalty, to deliver
his head in the view of Angelo? I may make my case
the same as Claudio's, to cross this in the smallest way.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
By the vow of my order, I guarantee you, if my
instructions may be your guide. Let this Barnardine
be executed this morning, and his head be brought to Angelo.

Provost:
Angelo has seen them both, and will discover the favor.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, death is a great disguiser; and you may add to it.
Shave his head, and tie his beard; and say it was
the desire of the penitent to be so bared before his
death. You know the course is common. If anything
falls to you upon this, more than thanks and good
fortune, by the saint whom I profess, I will plead
against it with my life.

Provost:
Pardon me, good father; it is against my oath.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Were you sworn to the duke, or to the deputy?

Provost:
To him, and to his substitutes.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You will think you have made no offense, if the duke
avouches for the justice of your dealing?

Provost:
But what is the likelihood of that?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Not a resemblance, but a certainty. Yet since I see
you are fearful, so that neither my coat, my integrity, nor my
persuasion can easily attempt you, I will go
further than I meant, to pluck all fears out of you.
Look you, sir, here is the hand and seal of the
duke. You know the character, I have no doubt; and the
signet is not strange to you.

Provost:
I know them both.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
The contents of this is the return of the duke. You
shall read it over at your pleasure soon; where you
shall find, that within these two days he will be here.
This is a thing that Angelo does not know; for he this
very day receives letters of a strange tenor;
perhaps of the duke's death; perhaps of his entering
into some monastery; but, by chance, nothing of what
is written. Look, the unfolding star is calling up the
shepherd. Do not put yourself into amazement at how these
things should be. All difficulties are but easy
when they are known. Call your executioner, and off
with Barnardine's head. I will give him a present
shrift and advise him for a better place. Yet you
are amazed; but this shall absolutely resolve you.
Come away; it is almost clear dawn.

POMPEY:
I am as well acquainted here as I was in our house
of profession. One would think it were Mistress
Overdone's own house, for there are many of her old
customers here. First, here's young Master Rash; he's in
for a commodity of brown paper and old ginger,
for ninescore and seventeen pounds; of which he made
five marks, in ready money. Indeed, then ginger was not
much in request, for the old women were all dead.
Then there is here one Master Caper, at the suit of
Master Three-pile the mercer, for some four suits of
peach-colored satin, which now makes him a
beggar. Then we have here young Dizy, and young
Master Deep-vow, and Master Copperspur, and Master
Starve-lackey the rapier and dagger man, and young
Drop-heir that killed lusty Pudding, and Master
Forthlight the tilter, and brave Master Shooty the
great traveler, and wild Half-can that stabbed
Pots, and, I think, forty more; all great doers in
our trade, and are now 'for the Lord's sake.'

ABHORSON:
Sirrah, bring Barnardine here.

POMPEY:
Master Barnardine! You must rise and be hanged.
Master Barnardine!

ABHORSON:
What, ho, Barnardine!

BARNARDINE:

POMPEY:
Your friends, sir; the hangman. You must be so
good, sir, as to rise and be put to death.

BARNARDINE:

ABHORSON:
Tell him he must awake, and that quickly too.

POMPEY:
Please, Master Barnardine, wake up until you are
executed, and sleep afterwards.

ABHORSON:
Go in to him, and fetch him out.

POMPEY:
He is coming, sir, he is coming; I hear his straw rustle.

ABHORSON:
Is the axe on the block, sirrah?

POMPEY:
Very ready, sir.

BARNARDINE:
How now, Abhorson? What's the news with you?

ABHORSON:
Truly, sir, I would desire you to get to your
prayers; for, look you, the warrant has come.

BARNARDINE:
You rogue, I have been drinking all night; I am not
fitted for it.

POMPEY:
Oh, the better, sir; for he that drinks all night,
and is hanged betimes in the morning, may sleep the
sounder all the next day.

ABHORSON:
Look you, sir; here comes your ghostly father. Are
we joking now, do you think?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Sir, induced by my charity, and hearing how hastily
you are to depart, I have come to advise you, comfort
you, and pray with you.

BARNARDINE:
Friar, not I. I have been drinking hard all night,
and I will have more time to prepare myself, or they
shall beat out my brains with billets. I will not
consent to die this day, that's certain.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, sir, you must. And therefore I beseech you,
look forward on the journey you shall go.

BARNARDINE:
I swear I will not die today for any man's
persuasion.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
But hear me.

BARNARDINE:
Not a word. If you have anything to say to me,
come to my ward; for I will not leave there today.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He is unfit to live or die. Oh, what a gravelly heart!
After him, fellows; bring him to the block.

Provost:
Now, sir, how do you find the prisoner?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
A creature unprepared, un-meet for death;
and to transport him in the mind he is in
would be damnable.

Provost:
Here in the prison, father,
there died this morning of a cruel fever
one Ragozine, a most notorious pirate,
a man of Claudio's years; his beard and head
are just of his color. What if we omit
this reprobate until he is well inclined;
and satisfy the deputy with the visage
of Ragozine, who is more like Claudio?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, it is an accident that heaven provides!
Dispatch it immediately; the hour is drawing on
that was prefixed by Angelo. See that this is done,
and sent according to the command; while I
persuade this rude wretch to die willingly.

Provost:
This shall be done, good father, presently.
But Barnardine must die this afternoon.
And how shall we continue Claudio,
to save me from the danger that might come
if he were known to be alive?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Let this be done.
Put them in secret holds, both Barnardine and Claudio.
Before the sun has made its daily greeting
to the under generation twice, you shall find
your safety manifested.

Provost:
I am your free dependant.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Quick, dispatch, and send the head to Angelo.
Now I will write letters to Angelo—
the provost, he shall bear them, whose contents
shall witness to him that I am near home,
and that, by great injunctions, I am bound
to enter publicly. I will desire him
to meet me at the consecrated fount
a league below the city; and from there,
by cold gradation and well-balanced form,
we shall proceed with Angelo.

Provost:
Here is the head; I'll carry it myself.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is convenient. Make a swift return;
for I would commune with you about such things
that want no ear but yours.

Provost:
I'll make all speed.

ISABELLA:

DUKE VINCENTIO:
The tongue of Isabel. She's come to know
if her brother's pardon has come here yet.
But I will keep her ignorant of her good,
to make her heavenly comforts out of despair,
when it is least expected.

ISABELLA:
Ho, by your leave!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Good morning to you, fair and gracious daughter.

ISABELLA:
The better, given to me by so holy a man.
Has the deputy yet sent my brother's pardon?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He has released him, Isabel, from the world.
His head is off and has been sent to Angelo.

ISABELLA:
No, but it is not so.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is no other. Show your wisdom, daughter,
in your close patience.

ISABELLA:
Oh, I will go to him and pluck out his eyes!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You shall not be admitted to his sight.

ISABELLA:
Unhappy Claudio! Wretched Isabel!
Injurious world! Most damned Angelo!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
This neither hurts him nor profits you a jot;
therefore, forbear it; give your cause to heaven.
Mark what I say, which you shall find
to be a faithful verity by every syllable.
The duke is coming home tomorrow; no, dry your eyes;
one of our convent, and his confessor,
gives me this instance. He has already carried
notice to Escalus and Angelo,
who are preparing to meet him at the gates,
there to give up their power. If you can, direct your wisdom
in that good path that I would wish it to go,
and you shall have your desire on this wretch,
the duke's grace, revenge to your heart,
and general honor.

ISABELLA:
I am directed by you.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
This letter, then, give to Friar Peter;
it is the one he sent me of the duke's return.
Say, by this token, I desire his company
at Mariana's house tonight. Her cause and yours
I will perfect him with, and he shall bring you
before the duke, and to the head of Angelo
you shall accuse him home and home. For my poor self,
I am combined by a sacred vow
and shall be absent. Go with this letter.
Command these fretting waters from your eyes
with a light heart; do not trust my holy order,
if I pervert your course. Who's here?

LUCIO:
Good evening, Friar. Where's the provost?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He is not within, sir.

LUCIO:
Oh, pretty Isabella, I am pale at my heart to see
your eyes so red. You must be patient. I am forced
to dine and sup with water and bran; I dare not for
my head fill my belly; one fruitful meal would set
me to it. But they say the duke will be here
tomorrow. By my troth, Isabel, I loved your brother.
If the old fantastical duke of dark corners had been
at home, he would have lived.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Sir, the duke is very little beholden to your
