reports; but the best is, he does not live in them.

LUCIO:
Friar, you don't know the duke as well as I do.
He's a better woodsman than you take him for.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Well, you'll answer for this one day. Fare you well.

LUCIO:
No, wait; I'll go along with you.
I can tell you pretty tales about the duke.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You have told me too many of him already, sir, if
they are true; if not true, none would have been enough.

LUCIO:
I was once before him for getting a wench with child.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Did you do such a thing?

LUCIO:
Yes, indeed, I did. But I was forced to forswear it;
they would have otherwise married me to the rotten medlar.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Sir, your company is fairer than it is honest. Rest you well.

LUCIO:
By my troth, I'll go with you to the lane's end.
If bawdy talk offends you, we'll have very little of
it. No, friar, I am a kind of burr; I shall stick.

ESCALUS:
Every letter he has written has disavowed the other.

ANGELO:
In a most uneven and distracted manner. His actions
show much like madness. I pray to heaven his wisdom is
not tainted! And why should we meet him at the gates, and
re-deliver our authorities there?

ESCALUS:
I don't know.

ANGELO:
And why should we proclaim it an hour before his
entering, that if any crave redress for injustice,
they should exhibit their petitions in the street?

ESCALUS:
He shows his reason for that: to have a dispatch of
complaints, and to deliver us from devices
hereafter, which shall then have no power to stand
against us.

ANGELO:
Well, I beseech you, let it be proclaimed early
in the morning; I'll call you at your house. Give
notice to such men of sort and suit as are to meet
him.

ESCALUS:
I shall, sir. Fare you well.

ANGELO:
Good night.
This deed is quite un-shaping me, making me unpregnant
and dull to all proceedings. A deflowered maid!
And by an eminent body that enforced
the law against it! But that her tender shame
will not proclaim against her maiden loss,
how might she tongue me! Yet reason dares her not;
for my authority bears such a credible weight,
that no particular scandal can ever touch it
but it confounds the breather. He should have lived,
except that riotous youth, with its dangerous sense,
might in the times to come have taken revenge,
by so receiving a dishonored life
with the ransom of such shame. I wish he had lived!
Alas, when once we have forgotten our grace,
nothing goes right. We would, and we would not.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Deliver these letters to me at the right time.
The provost knows our purpose and our plot.
The matter being afoot, keep to your instruction,
and always hold to our special drift;
though sometimes you may blench from this to that,
as the cause may minister. Go and call at Flavius's house,
and tell him where I am staying. Give the same notice
to Valentinus, Rowland, and to Crassus,
and bid them bring the trumpets to the gate;
but send me Flavius first.

FRIAR PETER:
It shall be speeded well.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I thank you, Varrius; you have made good haste.
Come, we will walk. There are other of our friends
who will greet us here soon, my gentle Varrius.

ISABELLA:
I am loath to speak so indirectly.
I would say the truth; but to accuse him so,
that is your part. Yet I am advised to do it;
he says, to veil my full purpose.

MARIANA:
Be ruled by him.

ISABELLA:
Besides, he tells me that, if by chance
he speaks against me on the adverse side,
I should not think it strange; for it is a medicine
that is bitter to a sweet end.

MARIANA:
I wish Friar Peter—

ISABELLA:
Oh, peace! The friar has come.

FRIAR PETER:
Come, I have found you a most fitting stand,
where you may have such an advantage on the duke,
that he shall not pass you. The trumpets have sounded twice;
the generous and gravest citizens
have reached the gates, and the duke is very near
to entering. Therefore, go from here, away!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
My very worthy cousin, fairly met!
Our old and faithful friend, we are glad to see you.

ANGELO:
May a happy return be to your royal grace!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Many and hearty thankings to you both.
We have made inquiry of you; and we hear
such goodness of your justice, that our soul
cannot but yield you forth to public thanks,
forerunning more requital.

ANGELO:
You are making my bonds still greater.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Oh, your desert speaks loud; and I would wrong it,
to lock it in the wards of a covert bosom,
when it deserves, with characters of brass,
a forted residence against the tooth of time
and the erasure of oblivion. Give me your hand,
and let the subject see, to make them know
that outward courtesies would fain proclaim
favors that keep within. Come, Escalus,
you must walk by us on our other hand;
and you are good supporters.

FRIAR PETER:
Now is your time. Speak loud and kneel before him.

ISABELLA:
Justice, O royal duke! Cast your regard
upon a wronged, I would have fain said, a maid!
O worthy prince, do not dishonor your eye
by throwing it on any other object
until you have heard me in my true complaint
and have given me justice, justice, justice, justice!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Relate your wrongs; in what? By whom? Be brief.
Here is Lord Angelo, who will give you justice.
Reveal yourself to him.

ISABELLA:
Oh, worthy duke,
you are bidding me to seek redemption from the devil.
Hear me yourself; for what I must speak
must either punish me, if I am not believed,
or wring redress from you. Hear me, oh, hear me, here!

ANGELO:
My lord, I fear her wits are not firm.
She has been a suitor to me for her brother,
who was cut off by the course of justice—

ISABELLA:
By the course of justice!

ANGELO:
And she will speak most bitterly and strangely.

ISABELLA:
Most strangely, but yet most truly, I will speak.
That Angelo is forsworn; is it not strange?
That Angelo is a murderer; is it not strange?
That Angelo is an adulterous thief,
a hypocrite, a virgin-violator;
is it not strange and strange?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No, it is ten times strange.

ISABELLA:
It is no truer that he is Angelo
than that all this is as true as it is strange.
No, it is ten times true; for truth is truth
to the end of reckoning.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Away with her! Poor soul,
she speaks this in the infirmity of her senses.

ISABELLA:
O prince, I conjure you, as you believe
there is another comfort than this world,
that you do not neglect me, with the opinion
that I am touched with madness! Do not make impossible
that which but seems unlikely. It is not impossible
but that one, the wickedest villain on the ground,
may seem as shy, as grave, as just, as absolute
as Angelo; even so may Angelo,
in all his dressings, characters, titles, and forms,
be an arch-villain. Believe it, royal prince.
If he is less, he's nothing; but he's more,
if I had more names for badness.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
By my honesty,
if she is mad—as I believe she is no other—
her madness has the oddest frame of sense,
such a dependency of one thing on another,
as I have ever heard in madness.

ISABELLA:
Oh, gracious duke,
don't harp on that, nor don't banish reason
for inequality; but let your reason serve
to make the truth appear where it seems hidden,
and hide the false which seems true.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Many that are not mad
have, surely, more lack of reason. What would you say?

ISABELLA:
I am the sister of one Claudio,
who was condemned upon the act of fornication
to lose his head; he was condemned by Angelo.
I, in probation of a sisterhood,
was sent to by my brother; one Lucio
was then the messenger—

LUCIO:
That's me, if it please your grace.
I came to her from Claudio, and I asked her
to try her gracious fortune with Lord Angelo
for her poor brother's pardon.

ISABELLA:
That's him indeed.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You were not asked to speak.

LUCIO:
No, my good lord;
nor did I wish to hold my peace.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I wish you to do so now, then;
please, take note of it. And when you have
a business for yourself, I pray to heaven you will then
be perfect.

LUCIO:
I guarantee your honor.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
The warrants are for yourself; take heed to it.

ISABELLA:
This gentleman told some of my tale—

LUCIO:
Right.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It may be right; but you are in the wrong
to speak before your time. Proceed.

ISABELLA:
I went
to this pernicious, villainous deputy—

DUKE VINCENTIO:
That's somewhat madly spoken.

ISABELLA:
Pardon it;
the phrase is to the matter.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Mended again. The matter; proceed.

ISABELLA:
In brief, to set aside the needless process,
how I persuaded, how I prayed, and knelt,
how he refuted me, and how I replied—
for this was of much length—the vile conclusion
I now begin with grief and shame to utter.
He would not, but by the gift of my chaste body
to his concupiscible, intemperate lust,
release my brother; and, after much debate,
my sisterly remorse confuted my honor,
and I did yield to him. But the next morning early,
his purpose having been sated, he sends a warrant
for my poor brother's head.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
This is most likely!

ISABELLA:
Oh, that it were as likely as it is true!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
By heaven, you foolish wretch, you don't know what you are speaking of,
or else you have been suborned against his honor
in a hateful practice. First, his integrity
stands without blemish. Next, it makes no sense
that he should pursue with such vehemence
faults that are proper to himself. If he had so offended,
he would have weighed your brother by himself
and not have cut him off. Someone has set you on.
Confess the truth, and say by whose advice
you came here to complain.

ISABELLA:
And is this all?
Then, oh you blessed ministers above,
keep me in patience, and with ripened time
unfold the evil which is here wrapped up
in countenance! Heaven shield your grace from woe,
as I, thus wronged, go from here unbelieved!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I know you would be glad to be gone. An officer!
To prison with her! Shall we thus permit
a blasting and a scandalous breath to fall
on him so near us? This must be a practice.
Who knew of your intent and coming here?

ISABELLA:
One that I wish were here, Friar Lodowick.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
A ghostly father, it is likely. Who knows that Lodowick?

LUCIO:
My lord, I know him; he is a meddling friar.
I do not like the man. If he had been a layman, my lord,
for certain words he spoke against your grace
in your retirement, I would have thrashed him soundly.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Words against me? This is a good friar, it seems!
And to set this wretched woman on here
against our substitute! Let this friar be found.

LUCIO:
Just last night, my lord, she and that friar,
I saw them at the prison. A saucy friar,
a very scurvy fellow.

FRIAR PETER:
Blessed be your royal grace!
I have stood by, my lord, and I have heard
your royal ear abused. First, this woman has
most wrongfully accused your substitute,
who is as free from any touch or soil with her
as she is from one that is un-gotten.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
We did not believe any less.
Do you know that Friar Lodowick that she speaks of?

FRIAR PETER:
I know him for a man divine and holy;
not scurvy, nor a temporary meddler,
as he's reported by this gentleman;
and, on my trust, a man that never yet
did, as he vouches, misreport your grace.

LUCIO:
My lord, most villainously; believe it.

FRIAR PETER:
Well, he may come in time to clear himself;
but at this instant, he is sick, my lord,
of a strange fever. Upon his mere request,
having come to know that there was a complaint
intended against Lord Angelo, I came here,
to speak, as from his mouth, what he knows
is true and false; and what he, with his oath
and all probation, will make fully clear,
whenever he is brought to court. First, for this woman.
To justify this worthy nobleman,
so vulgarly and personally accused,
you shall hear her disproved to her eyes,
until she herself confesses it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Good friar, let's hear it.
Do you not smile at this, Lord Angelo?
Oh heaven, the vanity of wretched fools!
Give us some seats. Come, cousin Angelo;
in this I'll be impartial; you be the judge
of your own cause. Is this the witness, friar?
First, let her show her face, and after speak.

MARIANA:
Pardon me, my lord; I will not show my face
until my husband bids me.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
What, are you married?

MARIANA:
No, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Are you a maid?

MARIANA:
No, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
A widow, then?

MARIANA:
Neither, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Why, you are nothing then. Neither maid, widow, nor wife?

LUCIO:
My lord, she may be a punk; for many of them are
neither maid, widow, nor wife.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Silence that fellow. I wish he had some cause
to prattle for himself.

LUCIO:
Well, my lord.

MARIANA:
My lord, I do confess I was never married;
and I confess besides I am no maid.
I have known my husband; yet my husband
does not know that he ever knew me.

LUCIO:
He was drunk then, my lord. It can be no better.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
For the benefit of silence, I wish you were so too!

LUCIO:
Well, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
This is no witness for Lord Angelo.

MARIANA:
Now I come to it, my lord.
She that accuses him of fornication,
in the very same manner, accuses my husband,
and she charges him, my lord, with such a time
when I'll depose I had him in my arms
with all the effect of love.

ANGELO:
Does she charge more than me?

MARIANA:
Not that I know of.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
No? You say your husband.

MARIANA:
Why, just, my lord, and that is Angelo,
who thinks he knows that he never knew my body,
but knows he thinks that he knows Isabel's.

ANGELO:
This is a strange abuse. Let's see your face.

MARIANA:
My husband bids me; now I will unmask.
This is that face, you cruel Angelo,
which you once swore was worth looking on;
this is the hand which, with a vowed contract,
was fast locked in yours; this is the body
that took away the match from Isabel,
and did supply you at your garden-house
in her imagined person.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Do you know this woman?

LUCIO:
Carnally, she says.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Sirrah, no more!

LUCIO:
Enough, my lord.

ANGELO:
My lord, I must confess I know this woman.
And five years ago, there was some talk of marriage
between myself and her; which was broken off,
partly because her promised proportions
came short of the agreement, but mainly
because her reputation was devalued
in levity. Since which time of five years,
I have never spoken with her, seen her, nor heard from her,
upon my faith and honor.

MARIANA:
Noble prince,
as there comes light from heaven and words from breath,
as there is sense in truth and truth in virtue,
I am affianced as this man's wife as strongly
as words could make up vows. And, my good lord,
just last Tuesday night, in his garden-house,
he knew me as a wife. As this is true,
let me safely rise from my knees,
or else forever be fixed here,
a marble monument!

ANGELO:
I was but smiling until now.
Now, good my lord, give me the scope of justice.
My patience here is touched. I do perceive
that these poor, informal women are no more
but instruments of some more mightier member
that sets them on. Let me have my way, my lord,
to find this practice out.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Yes, with all my heart,
and punish them to the height of your pleasure.
You foolish friar, and you pernicious woman,
in league with her that's gone, do you think your oaths,
though they would swear down each particular saint,
were testimonies against his worth and credit
that's sealed in approbation? You, Lord Escalus,
sit with my cousin; lend him your kind pains
to find out this abuse, and where it comes from.
There is another friar that set them on;
let him be sent for.

FRIAR PETER:
I wish he were here, my lord! For he indeed
has set the women on to this complaint.
Your provost knows the place where he resides,
and he may fetch him.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Go and do it instantly.
And you, my noble and well-warranted cousin,
whom it concerns to hear this matter out,
do with your injuries as seems best to you,
in any chastisement. I will leave you for a while;
but do not you stir until you have well determined
upon these slanderers.

ESCALUS:
My lord, we'll do it thoroughly.
Signior Lucio, did you not say you knew that
Friar Lodowick was a dishonest person?

LUCIO:
'A cowl does not make a monk.' He is honest in nothing
but in his clothes; and he is one that has spoken most
villainous speeches of the duke.

ESCALUS:
We shall entreat you to stay here until he comes and
to enforce them against him. We shall find this friar a
notable fellow.

LUCIO:
As any in Vienna, on my word.

ESCALUS:
Call that same Isabel here once again; I would like to speak with her.
Please, my lord, give me leave to question; you
shall see how I'll handle her.

LUCIO:
Not better than he, by her own report.

ESCALUS:
Do you say so?

LUCIO:
Well, sir, I think, if you handled her privately,
she would sooner confess. Perhaps, publicly,
she'll be ashamed.

ESCALUS:
I will go about it in a dark way with her.

LUCIO:
That's the way; for women are light at midnight.

ESCALUS:
Come on, mistress. Here's a gentlewoman who denies all
that you have said.

LUCIO:
My lord, here comes the rascal I spoke of; here with
the provost.

ESCALUS:
In very good time. Do not speak to him until we
call upon you.

LUCIO:
Mum.

ESCALUS:
Come, sir. Did you set these women on to slander
Lord Angelo? They have confessed you did.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It's false.

ESCALUS:
What! Do you know where you are?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Respect to your great place! And let the devil
be sometimes honored for his burning throne!
Where is the duke? It is he who should hear me speak.

ESCALUS:
The duke is in us; and we will hear you speak.
See that you speak justly.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Boldly, at least. But, oh, poor souls,
have you come to seek the lamb here of the fox?
Good night to your redress! Is the duke gone?
Then your cause is gone too. The duke is unjust,
thus to retort your manifest appeal,
and to put your trial in the villain's mouth
which you have come here to accuse.

LUCIO:
This is the rascal; this is the one I spoke of.

ESCALUS:
Why, you unreverend and unhallowed friar,
is it not enough that you have suborned these women
to accuse this worthy man, but, in a foul mouth
and in the witness of his own ear,
to call him a villain? And then to glance from him
to the duke himself, to tax him with injustice?
Take him from here; to the rack with him! We'll tear you
joint by joint, but we will know his purpose.
What! Unjust!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Don't be so hot. The duke
dares no more stretch this finger of mine than he
dares to rack his own. I am not his subject,
nor am I provincial here. My business in this state
made me a looker-on here in Vienna,
where I have seen corruption boil and bubble
until it ran over the stew; there are laws for all faults,
but the faults are so countenanced, that the strong statutes
stand like the forfeits in a barber's shop,
as much in mock as in mark.

ESCALUS:
Slander to the state! Away with him to prison!

ANGELO:
What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio?
Is this the man that you told us of?

LUCIO:
It's him, my lord. Come here, goodman baldpate.
Do you know me?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I remember you, sir, by the sound of your voice. I
met you at the prison, in the absence of the duke.

LUCIO:
Oh, did you so? And do you remember what you said about the duke?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Most notably, sir.

LUCIO:
Do you so, sir? And was the duke a fleshmonger, a
fool, and a coward, as you then reported him to be?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You must, sir, change places with me, before you make
that my report. You, indeed, spoke so of him; and
much more, much worse.

LUCIO:
Oh, you damnable fellow! Did I not pluck you by the
nose for your speeches?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I protest, I love the duke as I love myself.

ANGELO:
Listen to how the villain would close now, after his
treasonable abuses!

ESCALUS:
Such a fellow is not to be talked with. Away with
him to prison! Where is the provost? Away with him
to prison! Lay enough bolts on him. Let him
speak no more. Away with those giggling women too, and
with the other confederate companion!

DUKE VINCENTIO:

ANGELO:
What, does he resist? Help him, Lucio.

LUCIO:
Come, sir; come, sir; come, sir; fie, sir! Why, you
bald-pated, lying rascal, you must be hooded, must
you? Show your knave's face, with a pox on you!
Show your sheep-biting face, and be hanged for an hour!
Won't it come off?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You are the first knave that ever made a duke.
First, provost, let me bail these three gentlewomen.
Don't sneak away, sir; for the friar and you
must have a word soon. Lay hold of him.

LUCIO:
This may prove worse than hanging.

DUKE VINCENTIO:

ANGELO:
Oh, my dread lord,
I should be guiltier than my guiltiness,
to think I can be undiscernible,
when I perceive your grace, like a divine power,
has looked upon my passes. Then, good prince,
hold no longer session upon my shame,
but let my trial be my own confession.
Immediate sentence then and subsequent death
is all the grace I beg.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Come here, Mariana.
Say, were you ever contracted to this woman?

ANGELO:
I was, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Go and take her from here, and marry her instantly.
You do the office, friar; which when it is consummated,
return him here again. Go with him, provost.

ESCALUS:
My lord, I am more amazed at his dishonor
than at the strangeness of it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Come here, Isabel.
Your friar is now your prince. As I was then
advertising and holy to your business,
not changing my heart with my habit, I am still
attorneyed at your service.

ISABELLA:
Oh, give me pardon,
that I, your vassal, have employed and pained
your unknown sovereignty!

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You are pardoned, Isabel.
And now, dear maid, may you be as free to us.
Your brother's death, I know, sits at your heart;
and you may marvel why I obscured myself,
laboring to save his life, and would not rather
make a rash remonstrance of my hidden power
than let him be so lost. Oh, most kind maid,
it was the swift celerity of his death,
which I thought came on with a slower foot,
that ruined my purpose. But, peace be with him!
That life is a better life, past fearing death,
than that which lives to fear. Make it your comfort,
so happy is your brother.

ISABELLA:
I do, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
For this new-married man approaching here,
whose salt imagination has yet wronged
your well-defended honor, you must pardon
for Mariana's sake. But as he adjudged your brother—
being a criminal, in a double violation
of sacred chastity and of a promise-breach
dependent on it, for your brother's life—
the very mercy of the law cries out
most audibly, even from his own tongue,
'An Angelo for Claudio, death for death!'
Haste still pays haste, and leisure answers leisure;
like does quit like, and MEASURE still for MEASURE.
Then, Angelo, your fault is thus manifested;
which, though you would deny it, denies you any advantage.
We condemn you to the very block
where Claudio stooped to death, and with like haste.
Away with him!

MARIANA:
Oh, my most gracious lord,
I hope you will not mock me with a husband.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
It is your husband who has mocked you with a husband.
Consenting to the safeguard of your honor,
I thought your marriage was fitting; otherwise, the imputation,
because he knew you, might reproach your life
and choke your future good. As for his possessions,
although they are ours by confiscation,
we do instate and make you a widow with them,
to buy you a better husband.

MARIANA:
Oh my dear lord,
I crave no other, nor no better man.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Never crave him; we are definitive.

MARIANA:
Gentle my liege,—

DUKE VINCENTIO:
You are but losing your labor.
Away with him to death!
Now, sir, to you.

MARIANA:
Oh, my good lord! Sweet Isabel, take my part;
lend me your knees, and for all my life to come,
I'll lend you all my life to do you service.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Against all sense, you are importuning her.
If she were to kneel down in mercy for this fact,
her brother's ghost would break his paved bed,
and take her away in horror.

MARIANA:
Isabel,
sweet Isabel, just kneel by me;
hold up your hands, say nothing; I'll speak all.
They say, the best men are molded out of faults;
and, for the most part, they become much better
for being a little bad. So may my husband.
Oh Isabel, will you not lend a knee?

DUKE VINCENTIO:
He dies for Claudio's death.

ISABELLA:
Most bounteous sir,
look, if it pleases you, on this condemned man,
as if my brother were alive. I partly think
that a due sincerity governed his deeds,
until he looked on me. Since it is so,
let him not die. My brother had but justice,
in that he did the thing for which he died.
As for Angelo,
his act did not overtake his bad intent,
and it must be buried but as an intent
that perished by the way. Thoughts are not subjects;
intents are but merely thoughts.

MARIANA:
Merely, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Your suit is unprofitable; stand up, I say.
I have thought of another fault.
Provost, how did it happen that Claudio was beheaded
at an unusual hour?

Provost:
It was so commanded.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Did you have a special warrant for the deed?

Provost:
No, my good lord; it was by a private message.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
For which I do discharge you of your office.
Give up your keys.

Provost:
Pardon me, noble lord.
I thought it was a fault, but I did not know it;
yet I did repent of it, after more advice;
for testimony of which, one in the prison,
that should have died by a private order,
I have kept alive.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Who is he?

Provost:
His name is Barnardine.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
I wish you had done so by Claudio.
Go and fetch him here; let me look upon him.

ESCALUS:
I am sorry, that one so learned and so wise
as you, Lord Angelo, have always appeared to be,
should slip so grossly, both in the heat of blood,
and in the lack of tempered judgment afterward.

ANGELO:
I am sorry that I procure such sorrow.
And it sticks so deep in my penitent heart
that I crave death more willingly than mercy;
it is my deserving, and I do entreat it.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Which one is that Barnardine?

Provost:
This one, my lord.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
A friar told me about this man.
Sirrah, you are said to have a stubborn soul,
that apprehends no further than this world,
and you square your life accordingly. You are condemned.
But, for those earthly faults, I quit them all;
and I pray you to take this mercy to provide
for better times to come. Friar, advise him;
I leave him to your hand. Who is that muffled fellow?

Provost:
This is another prisoner that I saved,
who should have died when Claudio lost his head;
he is almost as like to Claudio as himself.

DUKE VINCENTIO:

LUCIO:
In faith, my lord, I spoke it but according to the
trick. If you will hang me for it, you may; but I
would rather it would please you if I might be whipped.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Whipped first, sir, and hanged after.
Proclaim it, provost, all around the city.
Is any woman wronged by this lewd fellow,
as I have heard him swear himself there is one
whom he got with child? Let her appear,
and he shall marry her. The nuptial finished,
let him be whipped and hanged.

LUCIO:
I beseech your highness, do not marry me to a whore.
Your highness said just now, I made you a duke.
Good my lord, do not recompense me by making me a cuckold.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Upon my honor, you shall marry her.
Your slanders I forgive; and with that,
I remit your other forfeits. Take him to prison;
and see that our pleasure in this is executed.

LUCIO:
Marrying a punk, my lord, is being pressed to death,
whipped, and hanged.

DUKE VINCENTIO:
Slandering a prince deserves it.
She, Claudio, that you wronged, see that you restore her.
Joy to you, Mariana! Love her, Angelo.
I have confessed her and I know her virtue.
Thanks, good friend Escalus, for your much goodness.
There is more behind that is more to be grateful for.
Thanks, provost, for your care and secrecy.
We shall employ you in a worthier place.
Forgive him, Angelo, that brought you home
the head of Ragozine for Claudio's.
The offense pardons itself. Dear Isabel,
I have a motion that much concerns your good;
to which if you will incline a willing ear,
what's mine is yours and what is yours is mine.
So, bring us to our palace; where we'll show
what is yet behind, that is fitting for you all to know.

SLY:
I'll get you, in faith.

Hostess:
A pair of stocks, you rogue!

SLY:
You are a baggage. The Slys are no rogues; look in
the chronicles; we came in with Richard the Conqueror.
Therefore, a few words; let the world slide. Sessa!

Hostess:
You will not pay for the glasses you have broken?

SLY:
No, not a penny. Go by, Jeronimy. Go to your cold
bed, and warm yourself.

Hostess:
I know my remedy; I must go and fetch the
third-borough.

SLY:
Third, or fourth, or fifth borough, I'll answer him
by law. I'll not budge an inch, boy. Let him come,
and kindly.

Lord:
Huntsman, I charge you, take good care of my hounds.
Brach Merriman, the poor cur, is exhausted;
and couple Clowder with the deep-mouthed brach.
Did you not see, boy, how Silver made it good
at the hedge-corner, in the coldest fault?
I would not lose that dog for twenty pounds.

First Huntsman:
Why, Belman is as good as he is, my lord;
he cried upon it at the slightest loss
and twice today picked out the dullest scent.
Trust me, I take him for the better dog.

Lord:
You are a fool. If Echo were as fleet,
I would esteem him worth a dozen such.
But feed them well and look after them all.
Tomorrow I intend to hunt again.

First Huntsman:
I will, my lord.

Lord:
What's here? Someone dead, or drunk? See, is he breathing?

Second Huntsman:
He is breathing, my lord. If he were not warmed with ale,
this would be a bed but cold to sleep so soundly.

Lord:
Oh, monstrous beast! How like a swine he lies!
Grim death, how foul and loathsome is your image!
Sirs, I will practice on this drunken man.
What do you think, if he were carried to bed,
wrapped in sweet clothes, with rings put on his fingers,
a most delicious banquet by his bed,
and brave attendants near him when he wakes,
would not the beggar then forget himself?

First Huntsman:
Believe me, lord, I think he cannot choose but to.

Second Huntsman:
It would seem strange to him when he woke up.

Lord:
Even as a flattering dream or a worthless fancy.
Then take him up and manage the jest well.
Carry him gently to my fairest chamber
and hang it all around with my wanton pictures.
Balm his foul head in warm, distilled waters
and burn sweet wood to make the lodging sweet.
Procure me music, ready for when he wakes,
to make a dulcet and a heavenly sound;
and if he chances to speak, be ready straight away
and with a low, submissive reverence
say, 'What is it your honor will command?'
Let one attend him with a silver basin
full of rose-water and bestrewn with flowers,
another bear the ewer, the third a diaper,
and say, 'Will it please your lordship to cool your hands?'
Let someone be ready with a costly suit
and ask him what apparel he will wear;
another tell him of his hounds and horse,
and that his lady mourns at his disease.
Persuade him that he has been a lunatic;
and when he says he is, say that he is dreaming,
for he is nothing but a mighty lord.
Do this and do it kindly, gentle sirs.
It will be a most excellent pastime,
if it is managed with modesty.

First Huntsman:
My lord, I guarantee you we will play our part,
so that he will think by our true diligence
that he is no less than what we say he is.

Lord:
Take him up gently and put him to bed;
and each one to his office when he wakes.
Sirrah, go and see what trumpet it is that is sounding.
Perhaps it is some noble gentleman that means,
traveling on some journey, to rest here.
How now! Who is it?

Servant:
If it pleases your honor, there are players here
that offer their service to your lordship.

Lord:
Tell them to come near.
Now, fellows, you are welcome.

Players:
We thank your honor.

Lord:
Do you intend to stay with me tonight?

A Player:
If it pleases your lordship to accept our duty.

Lord:
With all my heart. This fellow I remember,
since I once saw him play a farmer's eldest son.
It was where you wooed the gentlewoman so well.
I have forgotten your name; but, surely, that part
was aptly fitted and naturally performed.

A Player:
I think it was Soto that your honor means.

Lord:
It's very true. You did it excellently.
Well, you have come to me at a happy time;
all the more so because I have some sport in hand
in which your cunning can assist me much.
There is a lord who will hear you play tonight.
But I am doubtful of your modesties;
lest, by over-eyeing his odd behavior—
for his honor has never yet heard a play—
you break into some merry passion
and so offend him; for I tell you, sirs,
if you should smile, he will grow impatient.

A Player:
Fear not, my lord. We can contain ourselves,
even if he were the most antic person in the world.

Lord:
Go, sirrah, take them to the buttery,
and give them a friendly welcome, every one.
Let them want nothing that my house affords.
Sirrah, you go to Bartholomew, my page,
and see him dressed in all suits like a lady.
That done, conduct him to the drunkard's chamber;
and call him 'madam,' and do him obeisance.
Tell him from me, as he will win my love,
that he should bear himself with honorable action,
such as he has observed in noble ladies
accomplished by them unto their lords.
Let him do such duty to the drunkard
with a soft, low tongue and lowly courtesy,
and say, 'What is it your honor will command,
wherein your lady and your humble wife
may show her duty and make known her love?'
And then with kind embraces, tempting kisses,
and with a declining head into his bosom,
bid him shed tears, as if he were overjoyed
to see her noble lord restored to health,
who for these seven years has esteemed him
no better than a poor and loathsome beggar.
And if the boy does not have a woman's gift
to rain a shower of commanded tears,
an onion will do well for such a shift,
which, being closely conveyed in a napkin,
shall, in despite, enforce a watery eye.
See this dispatched with all the haste you can.
Soon I'll give you more instructions.
I know the boy will well usurp the grace,
voice, gait, and action of a gentlewoman.
I long to hear him call the drunkard his husband,
and to see how my men will keep themselves from laughter
when they do homage to this simple peasant.
I'll go in to counsel them; perhaps my presence
may well abate the over-merry spleen
which would otherwise grow into extremes.

SLY:
For God's sake, a pot of small ale.

First Servant:
Will it please your lordship to drink a cup of sack?

Second Servant:
Will it please your honor to taste of these preserves?

Third Servant:
What clothes will your honor wear today?

SLY:
I am Christopher Sly; do not call me 'honor' or
'lordship.' I have never drunk sack in my life; and if
you give me any preserves, give me preserves of
beef. Never ask me what clothes I'll wear; for I
have no more doublets than backs, no more stockings
than legs, nor no more shoes than feet; no,
sometimes more feet than shoes, or such shoes that my
toes look through the over-leather.

Lord:
Heaven, cease this idle humor in your honor!
Oh, that a mighty man of such descent,
of such possessions and so high esteem,
should be infused with so foul a spirit!

SLY:
What, would you make me mad? Am I not Christopher
Sly, old Sly's son of Burtonheath, a
peddler by birth, a card-maker by education, a
bear-herd by transmutation, and now by present profession a tinker?
Ask Marian Hacket, the fat ale-wife of Wincot, if
she doesn't know me. If she says I am not fourteen pence
on the score for sheer ale, score me up for the
lyingest knave in Christendom. What! I am not
distraught. Here's—

Third Servant:
Oh, this is what makes your lady mourn!

Second Servant:
Oh, this is what makes your servants droop!

Lord:
From this it comes that your kindred shun your house,
as if they were beaten from here by your strange lunacy.
Oh, noble lord, think of your birth,
call home your ancient thoughts from banishment,
and banish from here these abject, lowly dreams.
Look how your servants attend on you,
each in his office, ready at your beck.
Do you want music? Listen! Apollo is playing,
and twenty caged nightingales are singing.
Or do you want to sleep? We'll take you to a couch
softer and sweeter than the lustful bed
that was purposefully trimmed up for Semiramis.
Say you want to walk; we will bestrew the ground.
Or do you want to ride? Your horses shall be trapped,
their harnesses studded all with gold and pearl.
Do you love hawking? You have hawks that will soar
above the morning lark. Or do you want to hunt?
Your hounds shall make the sky answer them
and fetch shrill echoes from the hollow earth.

First Servant:
Say you will course; your greyhounds are as swift
as breathed stags, yes, fleeter than the roe.

Second Servant:
Do you love pictures? We will fetch you straight away
Adonis painted by a running brook,
and Cytherea all hidden in the sedges,
which seem to move and wanton with her breath,
even as the waving sedges play with the wind.

Lord:
We'll show you Io as she was a maid,
and how she was beguiled and surprised,
as lively painted as the deed was done.

Third Servant:
Or Daphne roaming through a thorny wood,
scratching her legs so that one shall swear she is bleeding,
and at that sight, sad Apollo will weep,
so workmanly the blood and tears are drawn.

Lord:
You are a lord, and nothing but a lord.
You have a lady far more beautiful
than any woman in this waning age.

First Servant:
And until the tears that she has shed for you,
like envious floods, have overrun her lovely face,
she was the fairest creature in the world;
and yet she is inferior to none.

SLY:
Am I a lord? And do I have such a lady?
Or am I dreaming? Or have I been dreaming until now?
I am not sleeping. I see, I hear, I speak;
I smell sweet savors and I feel soft things.
Upon my life, I am a lord indeed
and not a tinker, nor Christophero Sly.
Well, bring our lady here to our sight;
and once again, a pot of the smallest ale.

Second Servant:
Will it please your mightiness to wash your hands?
Oh, how we rejoice to see your wit restored!
Oh, that you once more knew but what you are!
For these fifteen years you have been in a dream;
or when you were awake, you were so awake as if you were asleep.

SLY:
These fifteen years! By my faith, a goodly nap.
But did I never speak during all that time?

First Servant:
Oh, yes, my lord, but very idle words.
For though you lay here in this goodly chamber,
yet you would say you were beaten out of the door;
and you would rail upon the hostess of the house;
and you would say you would present her at the court leet,
because she brought stone jugs and no sealed quarts.
Sometimes you would call out for Cicely Hacket.

SLY:
Yes, the woman's maid of the house.

Third Servant:
Why, sir, you know no house nor no such maid,
nor no such men as you have reckoned up,
as Stephen Sly and did John Naps of Greece
and Peter Turph and Henry Pimpernell
and twenty more such names and men as these
which never were nor no man ever saw.

SLY:
Now, may the Lord be thanked for my good amends!

ALL:
Amen.

SLY:
I thank you. You shall not lose by it.

Page:
How is my noble lord?

SLY:
Well, I am doing well, for there is enough cheer here.
Where is my wife?

Page:
Here, noble lord. What is your will with her?

SLY:
Are you my wife and you will not call me husband?
My men should call me 'lord.' I am your goodman.

Page:
My husband and my lord, my lord and my husband;
I am your wife in all obedience.

SLY:
I know it well. What must I call her?

Lord:
Madam.

SLY:
Alice madam, or Joan madam?

Lord:
'Madam,' and nothing else. So lords
call ladies.

SLY:
Madam wife, they say that I have dreamed
and slept for about fifteen years or more.

Page:
Yes, and the time seems like thirty to me,
being all this time abandoned from your bed.

SLY:
It is much. Servants, leave me and her alone.
Madam, undress yourself and come to bed now.

Page:
Thrice noble lord, let me entreat of you
to pardon me yet for a night or two,
or, if not so, until the sun has set.
For your physicians have expressly charged,
at the peril of you incurring your former malady,
that I should yet absent myself from your bed.
I hope this reason stands for my excuse.

SLY:
Yes, it stands so that I can hardly
wait so long. But I would be loath to fall into
my dreams again. I will therefore wait in
spite of the flesh and the blood.

Messenger:
Your honor's players, hearing of your amendment,
have come to play a pleasant comedy;
for so your doctors hold it very fitting,
seeing that too much sadness has congealed your blood,
and melancholy is the nurse of frenzy.
Therefore, they thought it would be good for you to hear a play
and to frame your mind to mirth and merriment,
which bars a thousand harms and lengthens life.

SLY:
Well, I will, let them play it. Is not a
comedy a Christmas game or a tumbling trick?

Page:
No, my good lord; it is more pleasing stuff.

SLY:
What, household stuff?

Page:
It is a kind of history.

SLY:
Well, well, I'll see it. Come, madam wife, sit by my side
and let the world slip. We shall never be younger.

LUCENTIO:
Tranio, since for the great desire I had
to see fair Padua, a nursery of arts,
I have arrived in fruitful Lombardy,
the pleasant garden of great Italy;
and by my father's love and leave I am armed
with his good will and your good company,
my trusty servant, well approved in all things,
here let us breathe and perhaps institute
a course of learning and ingenious studies.
Pisa, renowned for its grave citizens,
gave me my being, and my father first,
a merchant of great traffic throughout the world,
Vincentio, come of the Bentivolii.
Vincentio's son, brought up in Florence,
it shall become him to serve all hopes conceived,
to deck his fortune with his virtuous deeds.
And therefore, Tranio, for the time I study,
I will apply myself to virtue and that part of philosophy
that treats of happiness,
to be achieved especially by virtue.
Tell me your mind; for I have left Pisa
and have come to Padua, as he that leaves
a shallow puddle to plunge into the deep,
and with satiety seeks to quench his thirst.

TRANIO:
Pardon me, gentle master of mine,
I am in all things affected as you are;
I am glad that you thus continue your resolve
to suck the sweets of sweet philosophy.
Only, good master, while we admire
this virtue and this moral discipline,
let's be no stoics nor no stocks, I pray;
or so devoted to Aristotle's checks
that Ovid is completely cast out and abjured.
Balk logic with the acquaintances that you have
and practice rhetoric in your common talk;
use music and poesy to quicken you;
the mathematics and the metaphysics,
fall to them as you find your stomach serves you.
No profit grows where no pleasure is taken.
In brief, sir, study what you most affect.

LUCENTIO:
Thank you, Tranio, you advise well.
If, Biondello, you were to come ashore,
we could at once get ourselves ready,
and take a lodging fit to entertain
such friends as time in Padua shall beget.
But wait a while. What company is this?

TRANIO:
Master, some show to welcome us to town.

BAPTISTA:
Gentlemen, do not importune me any further,
for you know how firmly I am resolved;
that is, not to bestow my youngest daughter
before I have a husband for the elder.
If either of you both love Katharina,
because I know you well and love you well,
you shall have leave to court her at your pleasure.

GREMIO:

KATHARINA:
I pray you, sir, is it your will
to make a fool of me amongst these mates?

HORTENSIO:
Mates, maid! What do you mean by that? No mates for you,
unless you were of a gentler, milder mold.

KATHARINA:
In faith, sir, you shall never need to fear.
I know it is not half way to her heart;
but if it were, do not doubt that her care would be
to comb your head with a three-legged stool
and paint your face and use you like a fool.

HORTENSIA:
From all such devils, good Lord deliver us!

GREMIO:
And me too, good Lord!

TRANIO:
Hush, master! Here's some good pastime coming.
That wench is stark mad or wonderfully froward.

LUCENTIO:
But in the other's silence I do see
a maid's mild behavior and sobriety.
Peace, Tranio!

TRANIO:
Well said, master; mum! And gaze your fill.

BAPTISTA:
Gentlemen, so that I may soon make good
what I have said, Bianca, you get inside.
And let it not displease you, good Bianca,
for I will love you no less, my girl.

KATHARINA:
A pretty peat! It is best to
put a finger in her eye, if she knew why.

BIANCA:
Sister, be content in my discontent.
Sir, I humbly subscribe to your pleasure.
My books and instruments shall be my company,
to look on and practice by myself.

LUCENTIO:
Hark, Tranio! You may hear Minerva speak.

HORTENSIO:
Signior Baptista, will you be so strange?
I am sorry that our good will affects
Bianca's grief.

GREMIO:
Why will you coop her up,
Signior Baptista, for this fiend of hell,
and make her bear the penance of her tongue?

BAPTISTA:
Gentlemen, be content; I am resolved.
Go in, Bianca.
And because I know she takes the most delight
in music, instruments, and poetry,
I will keep schoolmasters within my house,
fit to instruct her youth. If you, Hortensio,
or Signior Gremio, you, know any such,
refer them here; for to cunning men
I will be very kind, and liberal
to my own children in their good upbringing.
And so farewell. Katharina, you may stay;
for I have more to discuss with Bianca.

KATHARINA:
Why, and I trust I may go too, may I not? What,
shall I be appointed hours; as though, perhaps, I
did not know what to take and what to leave, ha?

GREMIO:
You may go to the devil's dam. Your gifts are so
good, that no one here will hold you. Their love is not
so great, Hortensio, but that we may blow our nails
together, and fast it fairly out. Our cake is dough on
both sides. Farewell. Yet for the love I bear my
sweet Bianca, if I can by any means light on a fit
man to teach her that in which she delights, I will
recommend him to her father.

HORTENSIO:
So will I, Signior Gremio. But a word, I pray.
Though the nature of our quarrel has never yet brooked
a parley, know now, upon advice, that it touches us both,
so that we may yet again have access to our fair
mistress and be happy rivals in Bianca's love, to
labor and effect one thing especially.

GREMIO:
What's that, I pray?

HORTENSIO:
Well, sir, to get a husband for her sister.

GREMIO:
A husband! A devil.

HORTENSIO:
I say, a husband.

GREMIO:
I say, a devil. Do you think, Hortensio, though
her father is very rich, that any man is so very much a fool
as to be married to hell?

HORTENSIO:
Tush, Gremio, though it is beyond your patience and mine
to endure her loud alarms, why, man, there are good
fellows in the world, if a man could find them, who
would take her with all her faults, and money enough.

GREMIO:
I cannot tell; but I would as soon take her dowry with
this condition, to be whipped at the high cross
every morning.

HORTENSIO:
Faith, as you say, there's little choice in rotten
apples. But come; since this bar in the law makes us
friends, it shall be so far forth friendly
maintained, all by helping Baptista's eldest daughter
to a husband, we will set his youngest free for a husband,
and then have at it afresh. Sweet Bianca! Happy man
be his dole! He that runs fastest gets the ring.
What do you say, Signior Gremio?

GREMIO:
I am agreed; and I wish I had given him the best
horse in Padua to begin his wooing that would
thoroughly woo her, wed her, and bed her, and rid the
house of her! Come on.

TRANIO:
I pray, sir, tell me, is it possible
that love should of a sudden take such hold?

LUCENTIO:
O Tranio, until I found it to be true,
I never thought it possible or likely;
but see, while I stood idly looking on,
I found the effect of love in idleness.
And now, in plainness, I confess to you,
that you are as secret and as dear to me
as Anna was to the queen of Carthage,
Tranio, I burn, I pine, I perish, Tranio,
if I do not achieve this young, modest girl.
Counsel me, Tranio, for I know you can;
assist me, Tranio, for I know you will.

TRANIO:
Master, it is no time to chide you now;
affection is not rated from the heart.
If love has touched you, nothing remains but so,
'Redeem yourself, a captive, for as little as you can.'

LUCENTIO:
Thank you, lad, go forward; this contents me.
The rest will be a comfort, for your counsel is sound.

TRANIO:
Master, you looked so long at the maid,
perhaps you did not notice the pith of it all.

LUCENTIO:
Oh yes, I saw sweet beauty in her face,
such as the daughter of Agenor had,
that made great Jove humble himself to her hand,
when with his knees he kissed the Cretan strand.

TRANIO:
Did you see no more? Did you not notice how her sister
began to scold and raise up such a storm
that mortal ears could hardly endure the din?

LUCENTIO:
Tranio, I saw her coral lips move,
and with her breath she did perfume the air.
Sacred and sweet was all I saw in her.

TRANIO:
No, then, it's time to stir him from his trance.
I pray, wake up, sir. If you love the maid,
bend your thoughts and wits to achieve her. This is how it stands:
her eldest sister is so cursed and shrewd
that until the father rids his hands of her,
master, your love must live as a maid at home;
and therefore he has closely cooped her up,
because she will not be annoyed with suitors.

LUCENTIO:
Ah, Tranio, what a cruel father he is!
But are you not advised, that he took some care
to get her cunning schoolmasters to instruct her?

TRANIO:
Yes, indeed, I am, sir; and now it's plotted.

LUCENTIO:
I have it, Tranio.

TRANIO:
Master, on my hand,
both our inventions meet and jump in one.

LUCENTIO:
Tell me yours first.

TRANIO:
You will be a schoolmaster
and undertake the teaching of the maid.
That's your device.

LUCENTIO:
It is. Can it be done?

TRANIO:
Not possible; for who will bear your part,
and be Vincentio's son here in Padua,
keep house and ply his book, welcome his friends,
visit his countrymen, and banquet them?

LUCENTIO:
Enough; be content, for I have it all figured out.
We have not yet been seen in any house,
nor can we be distinguished by our faces
as either man or master. So it follows thus:
you shall be the master, Tranio, in my stead,
and keep house and servants as I should.
I will be someone else, some Florentine,
some Neapolitan, or a meaner man from Pisa.
It's hatched and it shall be so. Tranio, at once,
undress yourself; take my colored hat and cloak.
When Biondello comes, he will wait on you;
but I will first charm him to keep his tongue.

TRANIO:
So you had need.
In brief, sir, since it is your pleasure,
and I am tied to be obedient;
for so your father charged me at our parting,
'Be serviceable to my son,' he said,
although I think it was in another sense;
I am content to be Lucentio,
because I love LucentIO so well.

LUCENTIO:
Tranio, be so, because Lucentio loves;
and let me be a slave, to achieve that maid
whose sudden sight has enthralled my wounded eye.
Here comes the rogue.
Sirrah, where have you been?

BIONDELLO:
Where have I been! No, what now! Where are you?
Master, has my fellow Tranio stolen your clothes? Or
have you stolen his? Or both? Please, what's the news?

LUCENTIO:
Sirrah, come here. This is no time to jest,
and therefore, frame your manners to the time.
Your fellow Tranio here, to save my life,
is putting on my apparel and my countenance,
and I, for my escape, have put on his;
for in a quarrel since I came ashore,
I killed a man and I fear I was seen.
You wait on him, I charge you, as is fitting,
while I make my way from here to save my life.
Do you understand me?

BIONDELLO:
Me, sir! Not a whit.

LUCENTIO:
And not a jot of Tranio in your mouth.
Tranio has changed into Lucentio.

BIONDELLO:
The better for him. I wish I were so too!

TRANIO:
So could I, in faith, boy, to have the next wish after,
that Lucentio indeed had Baptista's youngest daughter.
But, sirrah, not for my sake, but for your master's, I advise
you to use your manners discreetly in all kinds of companies.
When I am alone, why, then I am Tranio;
but in all other places, I am your master Lucentio.

LUCENTIO:
Tranio, let's go. There is one more thing that
you must execute yourself, to make yourself one of these wooers. If
you ask me why, it is enough that my reasons are both good
and weighty.

First Servant:
My lord, you are nodding; you are not paying attention to the play.

SLY:
Yes, by Saint Anne, I am. A good matter, surely.
Is there any more of it?

Page:
My lord, it has only just begun.

SLY:
It's a very excellent piece of work, madam lady.
I wish it were done!

PETRUCHIO:
Verona, for a while I take my leave,
to see my friends in Padua, but of all,
my best beloved and approved friend,
Hortensio; and I think this is his house.
Here, sirrah Grumio; knock, I say.

GRUMIO:
Knock, sir! Whom should I knock? Has any man
rebuked your worship?

PETRUCHIO:
Villain, I say, knock me here soundly.

GRUMIO:
Knock you here, sir! Why, sir, what am I, sir, that
I should knock you here, sir?

PETRUCHIO:
Villain, I say, knock on this gate
and rap me well, or I'll knock your knave's head.

GRUMIO:
My master has grown quarrelsome. I should knock
you first,
and then I know who will come off the worst.

PETRUCHIO:
Will it not be?
Faith, sirrah, if you won't knock, I'll ring it;
I'll try how you can sol, fa, and sing it.

GRUMIO:
Help, masters, help! My master is mad.

PETRUCHIO:
Now, knock when I bid you, sirrah villain!

HORTENSIO:
What's happening! What's the matter? My old friend Grumio!
And my good friend Petruchio! How are you all in Verona?

PETRUCHIO:
Signor Hortensio, have you come to part the fight?
'With all my heart, I am glad to see you,' may I say.

HORTENSIO:
'Welcome to our house, my most honored signor
Petruchio.' Rise, Grumio, rise. We will compound
this quarrel.

GRUMIO:
No, it doesn't matter, sir, what he alleges in Latin.
If this is not a lawful case for me to leave his
service, look you, sir, he told me to knock him and rap
him soundly, sir. Well, was it fit for a servant to
use his master so, being perhaps, for all I see,
thirty-two, a pip out? Whom I wish to God I had
knocked well at first, then Grumio would not have come by the worst.

PETRUCHIO:
A senseless villain! Good Hortensio,
I told the rascal to knock on your gate
and I could not get him to do it for my life.

GRUMIO:
Knock at the gate! O heavens! Didn't you speak these
words plainly, 'Sirrah, knock me here, rap me here,
knock me well, and knock me soundly'? And now you come
with, 'knocking at the gate'?

PETRUCHIO:
Sirrah, be gone, or do not talk, I advise you.

HORTENSIO:
Petruchio, have patience; I am Grumio's pledge.
Why, this is a heavy chance between him and you,
your ancient, trusty, and pleasant servant Grumio.
And tell me now, sweet friend, what happy gale
blows you to Padua here from old Verona?

PETRUCHIO:
Such a wind as scatters young men through the world,
to seek their fortunes farther than at home
where small experience grows. But in a few words,
Signior Hortensio, this is how it stands with me:
Antonio, my father, is deceased;
and I have thrust myself into this maze,
perhaps to marry and thrive as best I may.
I have crowns in my purse and goods at home,
and so I have come abroad to see the world.

HORTENSIO:
Petruchio, shall I then come roundly to you
and wish you a shrewd, ill-favored wife?
You would thank me but a little for my counsel.
And yet I'll promise you she shall be rich
and very rich. But you are too much my friend,
and I'll not wish you to her.

PETRUCHIO:
Signior Hortensio, between such friends as we are,
few words suffice; and therefore, if you know
one who is rich enough to be Petruchio's wife,
as wealth is the burden of my wooing dance,
be she as foul as was Florentius's love,
as old as Sibyl and as cursed and shrewd
as Socrates' Xanthippe, or worse,
she does not move me, or at least, does not remove
the edge of my affection, even if she were as rough
as the swelling Adriatic seas.
I have come to marry wealthily in Padua;
if wealthily, then happily in Padua.

GRUMIO:
No, look you, sir, he is telling you flatly what his
mind is. Why, give him enough gold and marry him to
a puppet or a small doll; or an old hag with not
a tooth in her head, though she has as many diseases
as fifty-two horses. Why, nothing comes amiss,
so long as money comes with it.

HORTENSIO:
Petruchio, since we have stepped this far in,
I will continue what I broached in jest.
I can, Petruchio, help you to a wife
with enough wealth, and who is young and beautiful,
brought up as best becomes a gentlewoman.
Her only fault, and that is fault enough,
is that she is intolerably cursed
and shrewd and froward, so beyond all measure
that, if my state were far worse than it is,
I would not wed her for a mine of gold.

PETRUCHIO:
Hortensio, peace! You do not know the effect of gold.
Tell me her father's name and that is enough;
for I will board her, though she chides as loud
as thunder when the clouds in autumn crack.

HORTENSIO:
Her father is Baptista Minola,
an affable and courteous gentleman.
Her name is Katharina Minola,
renowned in Padua for her scolding tongue.

PETRUCHIO:
I know her father, though I do not know her;
and he knew my deceased father well.
I will not sleep, Hortensio, until I see her;
and therefore, let me be thus bold with you
to give you over at this first encounter,
unless you will accompany me there.

GRUMIO:
I pray you, sir, let him go while the mood lasts.
On my word, if she knew him as well as I do, she
would think scolding would do little good on him.
She may perhaps call him half a score of knaves or so.
Why, that's nothing; if he begins once, he'll rail in
his rope-tricks. I'll tell you what, sir, if she
stands up to him but a little, he will throw a figure in
her face and so disfigure her with it that she
shall have no more eyes to see with than a cat.
You do not know him, sir.

HORTENSIO:
Wait, Petruchio, I must go with you,
for my treasure is in Baptista's keeping.
He has the jewel of my life in his hold,
his youngest daughter, the beautiful Bianca,
and he is withholding her from me and from others,
suitors to her and rivals in my love,
supposing it a thing impossible,
for the defects I have rehearsed before,
that Katharina will ever be wooed;
therefore, Baptista has taken this order,
that no one shall have access to Bianca
until Katharina the cursed has gotten a husband.

GRUMIO:
Katharina the cursed!
A title for a maid, of all titles the worst.

HORTENSIO:
Now my friend Petruchio will do me a favor,
and offer me, disguised in sober robes,
to old Baptista as a schoolmaster
well-versed in music, to instruct Bianca;
so that I may, by this device, at least
have leave and leisure to make love to her
and court her by herself, unsuspected.

GRUMIO:
Here's no knavery! See, to beguile the old folks,
how the young folks lay their heads together!
Master, master, look about you. Who goes there, ha?

HORTENSIO:
Peace, Grumio! It is the rival of my love.
Petruchio, stand by for a while.

GRUMIO:
A proper stripling and an amorous!

GREMIO:
Oh, very well; I have perused the note.
Listen to me, sir. I'll have them very fairly bound.
All books of love, see that at any hand;
and see that you read no other lectures to her.
You understand me. Over and beside
Signior Baptista's liberality,
I'll mend it with a largess. Take your paper too,
and let me have them very well perfumed,
for she is sweeter than perfume itself
to whom they go. What will you read to her?

LUCENTIO:
Whatever I read to her, I'll plead for you
as for my patron, be you so assured,
as firmly as if you were still in my place.
Yes, and perhaps with more successful words
than you, unless you were a scholar, sir.

GREMIO:
Oh, this learning, what a thing it is!

GRUMIO:
Oh this woodcock, what an ass it is!

PETRUCHIO:
Peace, sirrah!

HORTENSIO:
Grumio, mum! God save you, Signior Gremio.

GREMIO:
And you are well met, Signior Hortensio.
Do you know where I am going? To Baptista Minola.
I promised to inquire carefully
about a schoolmaster for the fair Bianca.
And by good fortune, I have lighted well
on this young man, for his learning and behavior
are fit for her turn, and he is well-read in poetry
and other books, good ones, I warrant you.

HORTENSIO:
It is well; and I have met a gentleman
who has promised to help me to another,
a fine musician to instruct our mistress;
so I shall not be behind in my duty
to the fair Bianca, so beloved of me.

GREMIO:
Beloved of me; and that my deeds shall prove.

GRUMIO:
And that his bags shall prove.

HORTENSIO:
Gremio, this is no time to vent our love.
Listen to me, and if you speak me fair,
I'll tell you news that is indifferently good for either of us.
Here is a gentleman whom I met by chance,
who, upon an agreement from us to his liking,
will undertake to woo the cursed Katharina,
yes, and to marry her, if her dowry pleases him.

GREMIO:
So said, so done, is well.
Hortensio, have you told him all her faults?

PETRUCHIO:
I know she is an irksome, brawling scold.
If that is all, masters, I hear no harm.

GREMIO:
No, you say so to me, friend? What countryman are you?

PETRUCHIO:
I was born in Verona, the son of old Antonio.
My father is dead, and my fortune lives for me;
and I do hope to see good and long days.

GREMIO:
Oh sir, such a life, with such a wife, would be strange!
But if you have a stomach for it, then in God's name,
you shall have me assisting you in all things.
But will you woo this wild-cat?

PETRUCHIO:
Will I live?

GRUMIO:
Will he woo her? Yes, or I'll hang her.

PETRUCHIO:
Why did I come here but for that intent?
Do you think a little din can daunt my ears?
Have I not in my time heard lions roar?
Have I not heard the sea, puffed up with winds,
rage like an angry boar chafed with sweat?
Have I not heard great ordnance in the field,
and heaven's artillery thunder in the skies?
Have I not in a pitched battle heard
loud alarms, neighing steeds, and trumpets' clang?
And do you tell me of a woman's tongue,
that gives not half so great a blow to hear
as a chestnut in a farmer's fire?
Tush, tush! Scare boys with bugs.

GRUMIO:
For he fears none.

GREMIO:
Hortensio, listen.
This gentleman has happily arrived,
my mind presumes, for his own good and ours.

HORTENSIO:
I promised we would be contributors
and bear the charge of his wooing, whatever it may be.

GREMIO:
And so we will, provided that he wins her.

GRUMIO:
I wish I were as sure of a good dinner.

TRANIO:
Gentlemen, God save you. If I may be bold,
tell me, I beseech you, which is the readiest way
to the house of Signior Baptista Minola?

BIONDELLO:
He that has the two fair daughters. Is that he you mean?

TRANIO:
Even him, Biondello.

GREMIO:
Listen, sir; you don't mean her to—

TRANIO:
Perhaps, him and her, sir. What have you to do with it?

PETRUCHIO:
Not her that chides, sir, at any hand, I pray.

TRANIO:
I love no chiders, sir. Biondello, let's go away.

LUCENTIO:
Well begun, Tranio.

HORTENSIO:
Sir, a word before you go.
Are you a suitor to the maid you are talking about, yes or no?

TRANIO:
And if I am, sir, is it any offense?

GREMIO:
No; if you will get you hence without more words.

TRANIO:
Why, sir, I ask, are not the streets as free
for me as for you?

GREMIO:
But she is not so.

TRANIO:
For what reason, I beseech you?

GREMIO:
For this reason, if you want to know,
that she is the choice love of Signior Gremio.

HORTENSIO:
That she's the chosen of Signior Hortensio.

TRANIO:
Gently, my masters! If you are gentlemen,
do me this right; hear me with patience.
Baptista is a noble gentleman,
to whom my father is not at all unknown;
and if his daughter were fairer than she is,
she might have more suitors, and me for one.
Fair Leda's daughter had a thousand wooers;
then well one more may fair Bianca have.
And so she shall; Lucentio shall make one,
though Paris came in the hope of speeding alone.

GREMIO:
What! This gentleman will out-talk us all.

LUCENTIO:
Sir, give him his head. I know he'll prove a jade.

PETRUCHIO:
Hortensio, to what end are all these words?

HORTENSIO:
Sir, let me be so bold as to ask you,
have you ever seen Baptista's daughter yet?

TRANIO:
No, sir; but I do hear that he has two,
the one as famous for a scolding tongue
as the other is for beautiful modesty.

PETRUCHIO:
Sir, sir, the first is for me; let her go by.

GREMIO:
Yes, leave that labor to great Hercules;
and let it be more than Alcides' twelve.

PETRUCHIO:
Sir, understand this of me in truth:
the youngest daughter whom you are listening for,
her father keeps from all access of suitors,
and will not promise her to any man
until the elder sister is first wed.
The younger is then free, and not before.

TRANIO:
If it is so, sir, that you are the man
that must help us all, and me amongst the rest,
and if you break the ice and do this feat,
achieve the elder, and set the younger free
for our access, whose fortune shall be to have her,
he will not be so graceless as to be ungrateful.

HORTENSIO:
Sir, you say well and you conceive well;
and since you do profess to be a suitor,
you must, as we do, gratify this gentleman,
to whom we all are generally beholden.

TRANIO:
Sir, I shall not be slack. In sign of which,
if it please you, we may contrive this afternoon,
and quaff carouses to our mistress's health,
and do as adversaries do in law,
strive mightily, but eat and drink as friends.

GRUMIO:
Oh, an excellent motion! Fellows, let's be gone.

HORTENSIO:
The motion's good indeed, and let it be so.
Petruchio, I shall be your welcome guest.

BIANCA:
Good sister, do not wrong me, nor wrong yourself,
to make a bondmaid and a slave of me;
that I disdain. But as for these other trifles,
unbind my hands, and I'll pull them off myself,
yes, all my clothes, down to my petticoat;
or whatever you command me, I will do,
so well I know my duty to my elders.

KATHARINA:
Of all your suitors, here I charge you, tell
me whom you love the best. See that you do not dissemble.

BIANCA:
Believe me, sister, of all the men alive,
I have never yet beheld that special face
which I could fancy more than any other.

KATHARINA:
Minion, you lie. Is it not Hortensio?

BIANCA:
If you like him, sister, I swear here
that I'll plead for you myself, but you shall have
him.

KATHARINA:
Oh, then, it seems, you fancy riches more.
You will have Gremio to keep you fair.

BIANCA:
Is it for him that you envy me so?
No, then you are jesting, and now I well perceive
that you have but jested with me all this while.
I pray you, sister Kate, untie my hands.

KATHARINA:
If that was a jest, then all the rest was so.

BAPTISTA:
Why, what's this, dame! From where does this insolence grow?
Bianca, stand aside. Poor girl! She is weeping.
Go and ply your needle; do not meddle with her.
For shame, you hussy of a devilish spirit,
why do you wrong her that never wronged you?
When did she cross you with a bitter word?

KATHARINA:
Her silence flouts me, and I'll be revenged.

BAPTISTA:
What, in my sight? Bianca, get you inside.

KATHARINA:
What, you won't let me? No, now I see
she is your treasure, she must have a husband;
I must dance barefoot on her wedding day
and for your love to her, lead apes in hell.
Don't talk to me. I will go and sit and weep
until I can find an occasion for revenge.

BAPTISTA:
Was ever a gentleman so grieved as I am?
But who comes here?

GREMIO:
Good morning, neighbor Baptista.

BAPTISTA:
Good morning, neighbor Gremio.
God save you, gentlemen!

PETRUCHIO:
And you, good sir! Pray, do you not have a daughter
called Katharina, who is fair and virtuous?

BAPTISTA:
I have a daughter, sir, named Katharina.

GREMIO:
You are too blunt. Go to it in an orderly fashion.

PETRUCHIO:
You are wronging me, Signior Gremio. Give me leave.
I am a gentleman of Verona, sir,
that, hearing of her beauty and her wit,
her affability and bashful modesty,
her wondrous qualities and mild behavior,
am bold to show myself a forward guest
within your house, to make my eye the witness
of that report which I have so often heard.
And, as an entrance to my entertainment,
I do present you with a man of mine,
cunning in music and the mathematics,
to instruct her fully in those sciences,
of which I know she is not ignorant.
Accept of him, or else you do me wrong.
His name is Licio, born in Mantua.

BAPTISTA:
You're welcome, sir; and he, for your good sake.
But as for my daughter Katharina, this I know,
she is not for your turn, the more's my grief.

PETRUCHIO:
I see you do not mean to part with her,
or else you do not like my company.

BAPTISTA:
Do not misunderstand me; I speak only as I find.
Where are you from, sir? What may I call your name?

PETRUCHIO:
Petruchio is my name; Antonio's son,
a man well known throughout all Italy.

BAPTISTA:
I know him well. You are welcome for his sake.

GREMIO:
Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray,
let us, that are poor petitioners, speak too.
Baccare! You are marvelously forward.

PETRUCHIO:
Oh, pardon me, Signior Gremio; I would be glad to be doing.

GREMIO:
I don't doubt it, sir; but you will curse your
wooing. Neighbor, this is a very grateful gift, I am
sure of it. To express the same kindness, I myself,
that have been more kindly beholden to you than
any, freely give to you this young scholar,
that has been studying at Rheims for a long time; he is as cunning
in Greek, Latin, and other languages, as the other is
in music and mathematics. His name is Cambio; please,
accept his service.

BAPTISTA:
A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio.
Welcome, good Cambio.
But, gentle sir, I think you walk like a stranger.
May I be so bold as to know the cause of your coming?

TRANIO:
Pardon me, sir, the boldness is my own,
that, being a stranger in this city here,
I make myself a suitor to your daughter,
to Bianca, who is fair and virtuous.
Nor is your firm resolve unknown to me,
in the preferment of the eldest sister.
This liberty is all that I request,
that, upon knowledge of my parentage,
I may have a welcome among the rest that woo
and free access and favor as the rest.
And, toward the education of your daughters,
I here bestow a simple instrument,
and this small packet of Greek and Latin books.
If you accept them, then their worth is great.

BAPTISTA:
Lucentio is your name; from where, I pray?

TRANIO:
From Pisa, sir; son to Vincentio.

BAPTISTA:
A mighty man of Pisa; by report
I know him well. You are very welcome, sir.
You take the lute, and you the set of books;
you shall go and see your pupils presently.
Holla, within!
Sirrah, lead these gentlemen
to my daughters; and tell them both,
these are their tutors. Bid them use them well.
We will go and walk a little in the orchard,
and then to dinner. You are most welcome,
and so I pray you all to think yourselves.

PETRUCHIO:
Signior Baptista, my business requires haste,
and I cannot come to woo every day.
You knew my father well, and in him, me,
left as the sole heir to all his lands and goods,
which I have improved rather than decreased.
Then tell me, if I get your daughter's love,
what dowry shall I have with her as a wife?

BAPTISTA:
After my death, one half of my lands,
and in possession, twenty thousand crowns.

PETRUCHIO:
And, for that dowry, I'll assure her of
her widowhood, if she survives me,
in all my lands and leases whatsoever.
Let specialties be therefore drawn between us,
so that the covenants may be kept on either hand.

BAPTISTA:
Yes, when the special thing is well obtained,
that is, her love; for that is all in all.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, that is nothing. For I tell you, father,
I am as peremptory as she is proud-minded;
and where two raging fires meet together,
they consume the thing that feeds their fury.
Though a little fire grows great with a little wind,
yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all.
So I to her and so she yields to me;
for I am rough and I do not woo like a baby.

BAPTISTA:
You may well woo, and may you have good speed!
But be prepared for some unhappy words.

PETRUCHIO:
Yes, to the proof; as mountains are for winds,
that do not shake, though they blow perpetually.

BAPTISTA:
How now, my friend! Why do you look so pale?

HORTENSIO:
For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.

BAPTISTA:
What, will my daughter prove to be a good musician?

HORTENSIO:
I think she'll sooner prove to be a soldier.
Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.

BAPTISTA:
Why, then you cannot break her to the lute?

HORTENSIO:
Why, no; for she has broken the lute on me.
I just told her she was mistaking her frets,
and I bowed her hand to teach her the fingering;
when, with a most impatient, devilish spirit,
'Frets, do you call these?' she said; 'I'll fume
with them.'
And, with that word, she struck me on the head,
and my head made a way through the instrument;
and there I stood amazed for a while,
as if I were on a pillory, looking through the lute;
while she called me a rascal fiddler
and a twangling Jack; with twenty such vile terms,
as if she had studied to misuse me so.

PETRUCHIO:
Now, by the world, she is a lusty wench;
I love her ten times more than I ever did.
Oh, how I long to have some chat with her!

BAPTISTA:
Well, go with me and don't be so discomfited.
Proceed in practice with my younger daughter;
she's apt to learn and thankful for good turns.
Signior Petruchio, will you go with us,
or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?

PETRUCHIO:
I pray you do.
I will wait for her here,
and I will woo her with some spirit when she comes.
Say that she rails; why then I'll tell her plainly
that she sings as sweetly as a nightingale.
Say that she frowns, I'll say she looks as clear
as morning roses newly washed with dew.
Say she is mute and will not speak a word;
then I'll commend her volubility,
and say she utters piercing eloquence.
If she tells me to pack, I'll give her thanks,
as though she had asked me to stay with her for a week.
If she refuses to wed, I'll ask for the day
when I shall ask the banns and when we shall be married.
But here she comes; and now, Petruchio, speak.
Good morrow, Kate; for that's your name, I hear.

KATHARINA:
You have heard well, but you are somewhat hard of hearing.
They that talk of me call me Katharina.

PETRUCHIO:
You lie, in faith; for you are called plain Kate,
and bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the cursed;
but Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom,
Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate,
for dainties are all Kates, and therefore, Kate,
take this of me, Kate of my consolation;
hearing your mildness praised in every town,
your virtues spoken of, and your beauty sounded,
yet not so deeply as belongs to you,
I myself am moved to woo you for my wife.

KATHARINA:
Moved! In good time. Let him that moved you here
remove you from here. I knew you at the first,
you were a movable.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, what's a movable?

KATHARINA:
A joint-stool.

PETRUCHIO:
You have hit it. Come, sit on me.

KATHARINA:
Asses are made to bear, and so are you.

PETRUCHIO:
Women are made to bear, and so are you.

KATHARINA:
No such jade as you, if you mean me.

PETRUCHIO:
Alas! good Kate, I will not burden you;
for, knowing you to be but young and light—

KATHARINA:
Too light for such a swain as you to catch;
and yet as heavy as my weight should be.

PETRUCHIO:
Should be! Should—buzz!

KATHARINA:
Well taken, and like a buzzard.

PETRUCHIO:
Oh, slow-winged turtle! Shall a buzzard take you?

KATHARINA:
Yes, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.

PETRUCHIO:
Come, come, you wasp; in faith, you are too angry.

KATHARINA:
If I am waspish, you had best beware my sting.

PETRUCHIO:
My remedy is then, to pluck it out.

KATHARINA:
Yes, if the fool could find where it lies.

PETRUCHIO:
Who doesn't know where a wasp wears its sting? In its tail.

KATHARINA:
In his tongue.

PETRUCHIO:
Whose tongue?

KATHARINA:
Yours, if you talk of tails. And so farewell.

PETRUCHIO:
What, with my tongue in your tail? No, come again,
good Kate; I am a gentleman.

KATHARINA:
That I'll try.

PETRUCHIO:
I swear I'll cuff you, if you strike again.

KATHARINA:
So you may lose your arms.
If you strike me, you are no gentleman;
and if you are no gentleman, why then you have no arms.

PETRUCHIO:
A herald, Kate? Oh, put me in your books!

KATHARINA:
What is your crest? A coxcomb?

PETRUCHIO:
A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.

KATHARINA:
No cock of mine; you crow too much like a coward.

PETRUCHIO:
No, come, Kate, come; you must not look so sour.

KATHARINA:
It is my fashion, when I see a crab.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, there's no crab here; and therefore don't look sour.

KATHARINA:
There is, there is.

PETRUCHIO:
Then show it to me.

KATHARINA:
If I had a glass, I would.

PETRUCHIO:
What, do you mean my face?

KATHARINA:
Well aimed for such a young one.

PETRUCHIO:
Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.

KATHARINA:
Yet you are withered.

PETRUCHIO:
It's with cares.

KATHARINA:
I don't care.

PETRUCHIO:
No, hear me, Kate. In truth, you won't escape so.

KATHARINA:
I will chafe you if I stay. Let me go.

PETRUCHIO:
No, not a bit. I find you passing gentle.
It was told to me that you were rough and coy and sullen,
and now I find that report to be a liar;
for you are pleasant, gamesome, and very courteous,
but slow in speech, yet as sweet as spring-time flowers.
You cannot frown, you cannot look askance,
nor bite your lip, as angry wenches will,
nor do you have any pleasure in being cross in conversation,
but you entertain your wooers with mildness,
with gentle conference, soft and affable.
Why does the world report that Kate limps?
Oh, slanderous world! Kate is as straight and slender
as the hazel-twig, and as brown in hue
as hazel nuts, and sweeter than the kernels.
Oh, let me see you walk. You do not halt.

KATHARINA:
Go, fool, and command those whom you keep.

PETRUCHIO:
Did ever Diana so become a grove
as Kate does this chamber with her princely gait?
Oh, may you be Diana, and let her be Kate;
and then let Kate be chaste and Diana be sportful!

KATHARINA:
Where did you study all this goodly speech?

PETRUCHIO:
It is extempore, from my mother-wit.

KATHARINA:
A witty mother! Otherwise her son would be witless.

PETRUCHIO:
Am I not wise?

KATHARINA:
Yes; keep yourself warm.

PETRUCHIO:
Indeed, so I mean, sweet Katharina, in your bed.
And therefore, setting all this chat aside,
thus in plain terms: your father has consented
that you shall be my wife; your dowry has been agreed on;
and, whether you will or not, I will marry you.
Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn;
for, by this light, by which I see your beauty,
your beauty, that does make me like you well,
you must be married to no man but me;
for I am he who was born to tame you, Kate,
and to bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate
as conformable as other household Kates.
Here comes your father. Never make a denial;
I must and will have Katharina as my wife.

BAPTISTA:
Now, Signior Petruchio, how are you speeding with my daughter?

PETRUCHIO:
How but well, sir? How but well?
It would be impossible for me to speed amiss.

BAPTISTA:
Why, what's wrong, daughter Katharina! Are you in your dumps?

KATHARINA:
Do you call me your daughter? Now, I promise you,
you have shown a tender, fatherly regard,
to wish me to be wed to a half-lunatic;
a mad-cup ruffian and a swearing Jack,
that thinks he can face the matter out with oaths.

PETRUCHIO:
Father, it is thus: yourself and all the world,
that talked of her, have talked amiss of her.
If she is cursed, it is for policy,
for she is not froward, but as modest as a dove;
she is not hot, but as temperate as the morning;
for patience, she will prove a second Griselda,
and for her chastity, a Roman Lucrece.
And to conclude, we have agreed so well together,
that on Sunday is the wedding-day.

KATHARINA:
I'll see you hanged on Sunday first.

GREMIO:
Hark, Petruchio; she says she'll see you
hanged first.

TRANIO:
Is this your speeding? No, then, good night to our part!

PETRUCHIO:
Be patient, gentlemen; I am choosing her for myself.
If she and I are pleased, what is that to you?
It is bargained between us two, being alone,
that she shall still be cursed in company.
I tell you, it is incredible to believe
