how much she loves me. Oh, the kindest Kate!
She hung about my neck; and kiss on kiss
she vied so fast, protesting oath on oath,
that in a twink she won me to her love.
Oh, you are novices! It is a world to see,
how tame, when men and women are alone,
a weak wretch can make the curstest shrew.
Give me your hand, Kate. I will go to Venice,
to buy apparel for the wedding-day.
Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests;
I will be sure my Katharina shall be fine.

BAPTISTA:
I don't know what to say, but give me your hands;
God send you joy, Petruchio! It's a match.

GREMIO:
Amen, say we. We will be witnesses.

PETRUCHIO:
Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu;
I will go to Venice; Sunday is coming apace.
We will have rings and things and fine array;
and kiss me, Kate, we will be married on Sunday.

GREMIO:
Was ever a match clapped up so suddenly?

BAPTISTA:
Faith, gentlemen, now I am playing a merchant's part,
and I am venturing madly on a desperate mart.

TRANIO:
It was a commodity that was fretting by you.
It will bring you gain, or perish on the seas.

BAPTISTA:
The gain I seek is, quiet in the match.

GREMIO:
No doubt but that he has gotten a quiet catch.
But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter.
Now is the day we have long looked for.
I am your neighbor, and I was the first suitor.

TRANIO:
And I am one that loves Bianca more
than words can witness, or your thoughts can guess.

GREMIO:
Youngling, you cannot love as dearly as I.

TRANIO:
Graybeard, your love does freeze.

GREMIO:
But yours does fry.
Skipper, stand back. It is age that nourishes.

TRANIO:
But it is youth that flourishes in ladies' eyes.

BAPTISTA:
Be content, gentlemen. I will compound this strife.
It is deeds that must win the prize; and he of you both
that can assure my daughter the greatest dower
shall have my Bianca's love.
Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?

GREMIO:
First, as you know, my house within the city
is richly furnished with plate and gold;
basins and ewers to lave her dainty hands;
my hangings are all of Tyrian tapestry;
in ivory coffers I have stuffed my crowns;
in cypress chests my arras counterpoints,
costly apparel, tents, and canopies,
fine linen, Turkey cushions bossed with pearl,
valance of Venice gold in needlework,
pewter and brass and all things that belong
to a house or housekeeping. Then, at my farm,
I have a hundred milch-kine to the pail,
sixscore fat oxen standing in my stalls,
and all things answerable to this portion.
I myself am struck in years, I must confess;
and if I die tomorrow, this is hers,
if while I live she will be only mine.

TRANIO:
That 'only' came in well. Sir, listen to me.
I am my father's heir and only son.
If I may have your daughter as my wife,
I'll leave her three or four houses as good,
within the rich walls of Pisa, as any one
that old Signior Gremio has in Padua;
besides two thousand ducats a year
of fruitful land, all of which shall be her jointure.
What, have I pinched you, Signior Gremio?

GREMIO:
Two thousand ducats a year of land!
My land does not amount to so much in all.
That she shall have; besides an argosy
that is now lying in Marseilles' road.
What, have I choked you with an argosy?

TRANIO:
Gremio, it is known that my father has no less
than three great argosies; besides two galliasses,
and twelve tight galleys. These I will assure her,
and twice as much, whatever you offer next.

GREMIO:
No, I have offered all, I have no more;
and she can have no more than all I have.
If you like me, she shall have me and mine.

TRANIO:
Why, then the maid is mine from all the world,
by your firm promise. Gremio is out-vied.

BAPTISTA:
I must confess your offer is the best;
and, if your father makes her the assurance,
she is your own; otherwise, you must pardon me,
if you should die before him, where's her dower?

TRANIO:
That's but a cavil. He is old, and I am young.

GREMIO:
And may not young men die, as well as old?

BAPTISTA:
Well, gentlemen,
I am thus resolved: next Sunday, you know,
my daughter Katharina is to be married.
Now, on the following Sunday, Bianca shall
be your bride, if you give this assurance;
if not, Signior Gremio.
And so, I take my leave, and I thank you both.

GREMIO:
Adieu, good neighbor.
Now I do not fear you.
Sirrah, young gamester, your father would be a fool
to give you all, and in his waning age
to set his foot under your table. Tut, a toy!
An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.

TRANIO:
A vengeance on your crafty, withered hide!
Yet I have faced it with a card of ten.
It's in my head to do my master good.
I see no reason but that the supposed Lucentio
must get a father, called 'the supposed Vincentio';
and that's a wonder. Fathers commonly
do get their children; but in this case of wooing,
a child shall get a sire, if I do not fail in my cunning.

LUCENTIO:
Fiddler, forbear; you are growing too forward, sir.
Have you so soon forgotten the entertainment
that her sister Katharina welcomed you with?

HORTENSIO:
But, you wrangling pedant, this is
the patroness of heavenly harmony.
Then give me leave to have the prerogative;
and when we have spent an hour in music,
your lecture shall have leisure for as much.

LUCENTIO:
You preposterous ass, that never read so far
as to know the reason why music was ordained!
Was it not to refresh the mind of man
after his studies or his usual pain?
Then give me leave to read philosophy,
and while I pause, you may serve in your harmony.

HORTENSIO:
Sirrah, I will not bear these boasts of yours.

BIANCA:
Why, gentlemen, you do me a double wrong,
to strive for that which rests in my choice.
I am no breeching scholar in the schools;
I'll not be tied to hours nor appointed times,
but I'll learn my lessons as I please myself.
And, to cut off all strife, here we sit down.
You take your instrument, and you play for a while;
his lecture will be done before you have tuned it.

HORTENSIO:
You'll leave his lecture when I am in tune?

LUCENTIO:
That will be never. Tune your instrument.

BIANCA:
Where did we leave off last?

LUCENTIO:
Here, madam:
'Here flowed the Simois; here is the Sigeian land;
here stood the high palace of old Priam.'

BIANCA:
Construe them.

LUCENTIO:
'Hic ibat,' as I told you before, 'Simois,' I am
Lucentio, 'hic est,' son to Vincentio of Pisa,
'Sigeia tellus,' disguised thus to get your love;
'Hic steterat,' and that Lucentio that comes
a-wooing, 'Priami,' is my man Tranio, 'regia,'
bearing my port, 'celsa senis,' so that we might
beguile the old pantaloon.

HORTENSIO:
Madam, my instrument is in tune.

BIANCA:
Let's hear it. Oh, fie! The treble is jarring.

LUCENTIO:
Spit in the hole, man, and tune it again.

BIANCA:
Now let me see if I can construe it. 'Hic ibat
Simois,' I don't know you, 'hic est Sigeia tellus,' I
don't trust you; 'Hic steterat Priami,' take heed
he doesn't hear us, 'regia,' don't presume, 'celsa senis,'
don't despair.

HORTENSIO:
Madam, it is now in tune.

LUCENTIO:
All but the bass.

HORTENSIO:
The bass is right; it's the base knave that is jarring.
How fiery and forward our pedant is!
Now, for my life, the knave is courting my love.
You little pedant, I'll watch you better yet.

BIANCA:
In time I may believe, yet I mistrust.

LUCENTIO:
Do not mistrust it. For, surely, Aacides
was Ajax, so called from his grandfather.

BIANCA:
I must believe my master; otherwise, I promise you,
I would still be arguing about that doubt.
But let it rest. Now, Licio, to you.
Good masters, do not take it unkindly, I pray,
that I have been so pleasant with you both.

HORTENSIO:
You may go and walk, and leave me for a while.
My lessons make no music in three parts.

LUCENTIO:
Are you so formal, sir? Well, I must wait,
and watch as well; for, unless I am deceived,
our fine musician is growing amorous.

HORTENSIO:
Madam, before you touch the instrument,
to learn the order of my fingering,
I must begin with the rudiments of art;
to teach you the gamut in a briefer sort,
more pleasant, pithy, and effectual,
than has been taught by any of my trade.
And there it is in writing, fairly drawn.

BIANCA:
Why, I am long past my gamut.

HORTENSIO:
Yet read the gamut of Hortensio.

BIANCA:

Servant:
Mistress, your father prays that you leave your books
and help to dress your sister's chamber up.
You know tomorrow is the wedding day.

BIANCA:
Farewell, sweet masters both; I must be gone.

LUCENTIO:
Faith, mistress, then I have no cause to stay.

HORTENSIO:
But I have cause to pry into this pedant.
I think he looks as though he were in love.
Yet if your thoughts, Bianca, are so humble
as to cast your wandering eyes on every stale,
then seize whoever you please. If I once find you ranging,
Hortensio will be quit with you by changing.

BAPTISTA:

KATHARINA:
It's no shame but mine. I must, forsooth, be forced
to give my hand, against my heart,
to a mad-brained ruffian full of spleen,
who wooed in haste and means to wed at leisure.
I told you, I did, that he was a frantic fool,
hiding his bitter jests in blunt behavior.
And, to be known as a merry man,
he'll woo a thousand, set the day of marriage,
make feasts, invite friends, and proclaim the banns;
yet he never means to wed where he has wooed.
Now the world must point at poor Katharina,
and say, 'Lo, there is mad Petruchio's wife,
if it would please him to come and marry her!'

TRANIO:
Patience, good Katharina, and Baptista too.
Upon my life, Petruchio means but well,
whatever fortune keeps him from his word.
Though he is blunt, I know him to be very wise;
though he is merry, yet he is also honest.

KATHARINA:
I wish Katharina had never seen him though!

BAPTISTA:
Go, girl; I cannot blame you now for weeping;
for such an injury would vex a very saint,
much more a shrew of your impatient humor.

BIONDELLO:
Master, master! News, old news, and such news as
you have never heard of!

BAPTISTA:
Is it new and old too? How can that be?

BIONDELLO:
Why, is it not news to hear of Petruchio's coming?

BAPTISTA:
Is he here?

BIONDELLO:
Why, no, sir.

BAPTISTA:
What then?

BIONDELLO:
He is coming.

BAPTISTA:
When will he be here?

BIONDELLO:
When he stands where I am and sees you there.

TRANIO:
But tell me, what about your old news?

BIONDELLO:
Why, Petruchio is coming in a new hat and an old
jerkin, a pair of old breeches that have been turned inside out three times, a pair
of boots that have been used as candle-cases, one buckled,
the other laced, an old rusty sword taken from the
town armory, with a broken hilt, and without a chape;
with two broken points. His horse is hipped with an
old, mothy saddle and stirrups of no kindred;
besides, it is possessed with the glanders and is likely to get a
running nose; it is troubled with the lampas, infected
with the fashions, full of windgalls, sped with
spavins, affected with the yellows, past the cure of the fives,
stark spoiled with the staggers, eaten by bots,
swayed in the back and shoulder-shotten;
he is near-legged in front and has a half-checked bit
and a head-stall of sheep's leather which, being
restrained to keep him from stumbling, has been
often burst and is now repaired with knots; one girth is
pieced six times and he has a woman's crupper of velvet,
which has two letters for her name fairly set down
in studs, and here and there is pieced with packthread.

BAPTISTA:
Who is coming with him?

BIONDELLO:
Oh, sir, his lackey, for all the world caparisoned
like the horse; with a linen stock on one leg and a
kersey boot-hose on the other, gartered with a red
and blue list; an old hat and 'the humour of forty
fancies' pricked in it for a feather. He is a monster, a
very monster in apparel, and not like a Christian
footboy or a gentleman's lackey.

TRANIO:
It's some odd humor that is pricking him to this fashion;
yet he often goes but meanly-appareled.

BAPTISTA:
I am glad he's come, however he comes.

BIONDELLO:
Why, sir, he is not coming.

BAPTISTA:
Didn't you say he was coming?

BIONDELLO:
Who? That Petruchio came?

BAPTISTA:
Yes, that Petruchio came.

BIONDELLO:
No, sir, I am saying his horse is coming, with him on its back.

BAPTISTA:
Why, that's all the same.

BIONDELLO:
No, by Saint Jamy,
I'll bet you a penny,
a horse and a man
is more than one,
and yet not many.

PETRUCHIO:
Come, where are these gallants? Who's at home?

BAPTISTA:
You are welcome, sir.

PETRUCHIO:
And yet I have not come well.

BAPTISTA:
And yet you do not halt.

TRANIO:
Not as well-dressed
as I wish you were.

PETRUCHIO:
If it were better, I would rush in thus.
But where is Kate? Where is my lovely bride?
How is my father? Gentlemen, I think you are frowning.
And why is this goodly company gazing,
as if they saw some wondrous monument,
some comet or unusual prodigy?

BAPTISTA:
Why, sir, you know this is your wedding-day.
First we were sad, fearing you would not come;
now we are sadder, that you have come so unprepared.
Fie, doff this habit, which is a shame to your estate,
and an eyesore to our solemn festival!

TRANIO:
And tell us, what important occasion
has so long detained you from your wife,
and sent you here so unlike yourself?

PETRUCHIO:
It would be tedious to tell, and harsh to hear.
It is enough that I have come to keep my word,
though in some part I was forced to digress;
which, at more leisure, I will so excuse
that you shall be well satisfied withal.
But where is Kate? I am staying too long from her.
The morning is wearing on, it is time we were at church.

TRANIO:
Do not see your bride in these irreverent robes.
Go to my chamber; put on some of my clothes.

PETRUCHIO:
Not I, believe me. I will visit her thus.

BAPTISTA:
But I trust you will not marry her like this.

PETRUCHIO:
Good sooth, even thus; therefore, have done with words.
To me, she is married, not to my clothes.
If I could repair what she will wear in me,
as I can change these poor accoutrements,
it would be well for Kate and better for myself.
But what a fool I am to chat with you,
when I should be bidding good morrow to my bride,
and sealing the title with a lovely kiss!

TRANIO:
He has some meaning in his mad attire.
We will try to persuade him, if it is possible,
to put on better clothes before he goes to church.

BAPTISTA:
I'll go after him, and see the outcome of this.

TRANIO:
But to her love, it concerns us to add
her father's liking. To bring this to pass,
as I have previously told your worship,
I am to get a man—whoever he may be,
it doesn't matter much, we'll fit him to our turn—
and he shall be Vincentio of Pisa;
and he will make an assurance here in Padua
of greater sums than I have promised.
So you shall quietly enjoy your hope,
and marry sweet Bianca with consent.

LUCENTIO:
If it were not that my fellow schoolmaster
is watching Bianca's steps so narrowly,
it would be good, I think, to steal our marriage;
which, once performed, let all the world say no,
I'll keep my own, despite all the world.

TRANIO:
That, by degrees, we mean to look into,
and watch our advantage in this business.
We'll outsmart the graybeard, Gremio,
the narrow-prying father, Minola,
the quaint musician, the amorous Licio;
all for my master's sake, Lucentio.
Signior Gremio, did you come from the church?

GREMIO:
As willingly as I ever came from school.

TRANIO:
And is the bride and bridegroom coming home?

GREMIO:
A bridegroom, you say? He is a groom indeed,
a grumbling groom, and the girl shall find that out.

TRANIO:
Is he more cursed than she is? Why, that's impossible.

GREMIO:
Why, he's a devil, a devil, a very fiend.

TRANIO:
Why, she's a devil, a devil, the devil's dam.

GREMIO:
Tut, she's a lamb, a dove, a fool compared to him!
I'll tell you, Sir Lucentio, when the priest
should ask if Katharina would be his wife,
'Yes, by God's wounds,' he said; and he swore so loud,
that, all amazed, the priest let fall the book;
and, as he stooped again to take it up,
the mad-brained bridegroom gave him such a cuff
that down fell the priest and the book, and the book and the priest.
'Now take them up,' he said, 'if any of you wish.'

TRANIO:
What did the wench say when he rose again?

GREMIO:
She trembled and shook; for he stamped and swore,
as if the vicar meant to cheat him.
But after many ceremonies were done,
he called for wine. 'A health!' he said, as if
he had been aboard a ship, carousing with his mates
after a storm; he quaffed off the muscatel
and threw the sops all in the sexton's face;
having no other reason
but that his beard grew thin and hungrily
and seemed to ask him for sops as he was drinking.
This done, he took the bride about the neck
and kissed her lips with such a clamorous smack
that the whole church echoed at the parting.
And I, seeing this, came away from there for very shame;
and after me, I know, the rest are coming.
There was never such a mad marriage before.
Hark, hark! I hear the minstrels play.

PETRUCHIO:
Gentlemen and friends, I thank you for your pains.
I know you think you will dine with me today,
and have prepared a great store of wedding cheer;
but so it is, my haste calls me away from here,
and therefore I mean to take my leave here.

BAPTISTA:
Is it possible you will go away tonight?

PETRUCHIO:
I must go away today, before the night comes.
Don't make it a wonder; if you knew my business,
you would entreat me to go rather than to stay.
And, honest company, I thank you all,
that have beheld me give myself away
to this most patient, sweet, and virtuous wife.
Dine with my father, drink a health to me;
for I must go from here; and farewell to you all.

TRANIO:
Let us entreat you to stay until after dinner.

PETRUCHIO:
It may not be.

GREMIO:
Let me entreat you.

PETRUCHIO:
It cannot be.

KATHARINA:
Let me entreat you.

PETRUCHIO:
I am content.

KATHARINA:
Are you content to stay?

PETRUCHIO:
I am content that you shall entreat me to stay;
but yet I will not stay, no matter how you entreat me.

KATHARINA:
Now, if you love me, stay.

PETRUCHIO:
Grumio, my horse.

GRUMIO:
Yes, sir, they are ready. The oats have eaten the horses.

KATHARINA:
No, then,
do what you can, I will not go today;
no, nor tomorrow, not until I please myself.
The door is open, sir; there lies your way;
you may be jogging while your boots are green.
As for me, I'll not be gone until I please myself.
It's likely you'll prove to be a jolly, surly groom,
that takes it upon you so roundly at the first.

PETRUCHIO:
Oh Kate, be content; please, don't be angry.

KATHARINA:
I will be angry. What do you have to do?
Father, be quiet; he shall stay at my leisure.

GREMIO:
Yes, indeed, sir, now it's beginning to work.

KATHARINA:
Gentlemen, let's go forward to the bridal dinner.
I see a woman may be made a fool,
if she doesn't have the spirit to resist.

PETRUCHIO:
They shall go forward, Kate, at your command.
Obey the bride, you that attend on her;
go to the feast, revel and domineer,
carouse full measure to her maidenhead,
be mad and merry, or go and hang yourselves.
But as for my bonny Kate, she must go with me.
No, don't look big, nor stamp, nor stare, nor fret;
I will be the master of what is my own.
She is my goods, my chattels; she is my house,
my household stuff, my field, my barn,
my horse, my ox, my ass, my anything;
and here she stands, and whoever dares to touch her,
I'll bring my action against the proudest of them
that stops my way in Padua. Grumio,
draw your weapon, we are beset with thieves;
rescue your mistress, if you are a man.
Fear not, sweet wench, they shall not touch
you, Kate.
I'll shield you against a million.

BAPTISTA:
No, let them go, a couple of quiet ones.

GREMIO:
If they hadn't gone quickly, I would have died from laughing.

TRANIO:
Of all the mad matches, there was never the like.

LUCENTIO:
Mistress, what is your opinion of your sister?

BIANCA:
That, being mad herself, she's madly mated.

GREMIO:
I guarantee it, Petruchio has been Kated.

BAPTISTA:
Neighbors and friends, though the bride and
bridegroom are wanting
to supply the places at the table,
you know there is no lack of junkets at the feast.
Lucentio, you shall supply the bridegroom's place,
and let Bianca take her sister's room.

TRANIO:
Shall sweet Bianca practice how to be a bride?

BAPTISTA:
She shall, Lucentio. Come, gentlemen, let's go.

GRUMIO:
Fie, fie on all tired jades, on all mad masters, and
all foul ways! Was ever a man so beaten? Was ever
a man so berated? Was ever a man so weary? I am sent
ahead to make a fire, and they are coming after to
warm themselves. Now, if I were not a little pot and soon
hot, my very lips might freeze to my teeth, my
tongue to the roof of my mouth, my heart in my
belly, before I would come by a fire to thaw me. But
I, by blowing the fire, shall warm myself; for,
considering the weather, a taller man than I will
take cold. Hello, ho! Curtis.

CURTIS:
Who is that calling so coldly?

GRUMIO:
A piece of ice. If you doubt it, you may slide
from my shoulder to my heel with no greater a run
but my head and my neck. A fire, good Curtis.

CURTIS:
Are my master and his wife coming, Grumio?

GRUMIO:
Oh, yes, Curtis, yes. And therefore, fire, fire; don't cast
on any water.

CURTIS:
Is she as hot a shrew as she's reported to be?

GRUMIO:
She was, good Curtis, before this frost. But, you
know, winter tames man, woman, and beast; for it
has tamed my old master and my new mistress and
myself, fellow Curtis.

CURTIS:
Away, you three-inch fool! I am no beast.

GRUMIO:
Am I but three inches? Why, your horn is a foot long; and
so long am I at the least. But will you make a
fire, or shall I complain about you to our mistress,
whose hand, she being now at hand, you shall soon
feel, to your cold comfort, for being slow in your hot office?

CURTIS:
I pray you, good Grumio, tell me, how goes the world?

GRUMIO:
A cold world, Curtis, in every office but yours; and
therefore, fire. Do your duty, and have your duty; for
my master and mistress are almost frozen to death.

CURTIS:
There's a fire ready; and therefore, good Grumio, the news.

GRUMIO:
Why, 'Jack, boy! Ho! boy!' and as much news as
will thaw.

CURTIS:
Come, you are so full of trickery!

GRUMIO:
Why, therefore fire; for I have caught an extreme cold.
Where's the cook? Is supper ready, the house
trimmed, the rushes strewn, the cobwebs swept; the
serving-men in their new fustian, their white
stockings, and every officer with his wedding-garment on?
Are the jacks fair within, the jills fair without,
the carpets laid, and everything in order?

CURTIS:
Everything is ready; and therefore, I pray you, the news.

GRUMIO:
First, know that my horse is tired; my master and
mistress have fallen out.

CURTIS:
How?

GRUMIO:
Out of their saddles and into the dirt; and thereby
hangs a tale.

CURTIS:
Let's have it, good Grumio.

GRUMIO:
Lend me your ear.

CURTIS:
Here.

GRUMIO:
There.

CURTIS:
This is to feel a tale, not to hear a tale.

GRUMIO:
And therefore it's called a sensible tale. And this
cuff was but to knock at your ear, and to beseech
your listening. Now I begin. First, we came down a
foul hill, my master riding behind my mistress—

CURTIS:
Both on one horse?

GRUMIO:
What's that to you?

CURTIS:
Why, a horse.

GRUMIO:
You tell the tale. But if you had not crossed me,
you would have heard how her horse fell and she
under her horse; you would have heard in how
miry a place, how she was covered in mud, how he left her
with the horse upon her, how he beat me because
her horse stumbled, how she waded through the dirt
to pull him off me, how he swore, how she prayed,
who had never prayed before, how I cried, how the
horses ran away, how her bridle was burst, how I
lost my crupper, with many things of worthy memory,
which now shall die in oblivion and you will return
unexperienced to your grave.

CURTIS:
By this reckoning, he is more of a shrew than she is.

GRUMIO:
Yes; and that you and the proudest of you all shall
find when he comes home. But what am I talking about?
Call forth Nathaniel, Joseph, Nicholas, Philip,
Walter, Sugarsop and the rest. Let their heads be
sleekly combed, their blue coats brushed, and their
garters of an indifferent knit. Let them curtsy
with their left legs and not presume to touch a hair
of my master's horse-tail until they kiss their
hands. Are they all ready?

CURTIS:
They are.

GRUMIO:
Call them forth.

CURTIS:
Do you hear, ho? You must meet my master to
countenance my mistress.

GRUMIO:
Why, she has a face of her own.

CURTIS:
Who doesn't know that?

GRUMIO:
You, it seems, that are calling for company to
countenance her.

CURTIS:
I am calling them forth to give her credit.

GRUMIO:
Why, she has come to borrow nothing from them.

NATHANIEL:
Welcome home, Grumio!

PHILIP:
How now, Grumio!

JOSEPH:
What, Grumio!

NICHOLAS:
Fellow Grumio!

NATHANIEL:
How now, old lad?

GRUMIO:
Welcome, you;—how now, you;— what, you;—fellow,
you;—and thus much for a greeting. Now, my spruce
companions, is everything ready, and are all things neat?

NATHANIEL:
All things are ready. How near is our master?

GRUMIO:
He is at hand, having alighted by now; and therefore, be
not—by God's passion, silence! I hear my master.

PETRUCHIO:
Where are these knaves? What, no man at the door
to hold my stirrup nor to take my horse!
Where is Nathaniel, Gregory, Philip?

ALL SERVING-MEN:
Here, here, sir; here, sir.

PETRUCHIO:
Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir! Here, sir!
You logger-headed and unpolished grooms!
What, no attendance? No regard? No duty?
Where is the foolish knave I sent before?

GRUMIO:
Here, sir; as foolish as I was before.

PETRUCHIO:
You peasant swain! You whoreson malt-horse drudge!
Did I not tell you to meet me in the park,
and to bring along these rascal knaves with you?

GRUMIO:
Nathaniel's coat, sir, was not fully made,
and Gabriel's pumps were all un-pinked in the heel;
there was no link to color Peter's hat,
and Walter's dagger had not come from its sheathing.
There were none fine but Adam, Ralph, and Gregory;
the rest were ragged, old, and beggarly;
yet, as they are, they have come here to meet you.

PETRUCHIO:
Go, rascals, go, and fetch my supper in.
Where is the life that I recently led—
where are those—Sit down, Kate, and welcome.—
Sound, sound, sound, sound!
Why, when, I say? No, good sweet Kate, be merry.
Off with my boots, you rogues! You villains, when?
It was the friar of orders grey,
as he walked forth on his way:—
Out, you rogue! You are plucking my foot awry.
Take that, and mend the plucking off of the other.
Be merry, Kate. Some water, here; what, ho!
Where's my spaniel Troilus? Sirrah, get you from here,
and bid my cousin Ferdinand to come here.
One, Kate, that you must kiss, and be acquainted with.
Where are my slippers? Shall I have some water?
Come, Kate, and wash, and you are heartily welcome.
You whoreson villain! Will you let it fall?

KATHARINA:
Patience, I pray you; it was an unwilling fault.

PETRUCHIO:
A whoreson, beetle-headed, flap-eared knave!
Come, Kate, sit down; I know you have a stomach.
Will you give thanks, sweet Kate; or else shall I?
What's this? Mutton?

First Servant:
Yes.

PETRUCHIO:
Who brought it?

PETER:
I.

PETRUCHIO:
It's burnt; and so is all the meat.
What dogs are these! Where is the rascal cook?
How did you dare, you villains, to bring it from the dresser,
and serve it thus to me that do not love it?
There, take it to you, trenchers, cups, and all;
you heedless joltheads and unmannerly slaves!
What, are you grumbling? I'll be with you straight.

KATHARINA:
I pray you, husband, don't be so disquieted.
The meat was well, if you were so contented.

PETRUCHIO:
I tell you, Kate, it was burnt and dried away;
and I am expressly forbidden to touch it,
for it engenders choler, and plants anger;
and it would be better that both of us did fast,
since, of ourselves, we are choleric,
than to feed it with such over-roasted flesh.
Be patient; tomorrow it shall be mended,
and, for this night, we'll fast for company.
Come, I will bring you to your bridal chamber.

NATHANIEL:
Peter, have you ever seen the like?

PETER:
He is killing her in her own humor.

GRUMIO:
Where is he?

CURTIS:
In her chamber, giving her a sermon on continence;
and he rails, and swears, and scolds, so that she, poor soul,
doesn't know which way to stand, to look, or to speak,
and she sits as if she has just risen from a dream.
Away, away! For he is coming here.

PETRUCHIO:
Thus have I politically begun my reign,
and it is my hope to end it successfully.
My falcon is now sharp and very empty;
and until she stoops, she must not be full-gorged,
for then she will never look upon her lure.
I have another way to man my haggard,
to make her come and know her keeper's call,
that is, to watch her, as we watch these kites
that bate and beat and will not be obedient.
She has eaten no meat today, nor shall she eat any;
last night she did not sleep, nor shall she sleep tonight.
As with the meat, I'll find some undeserved fault
about the making of the bed;
and here I'll fling the pillow, there the bolster,
this way the coverlet, and another way the sheets.
Yes, and amidst this hurly, I intend
that all is done in reverend care of her;
and in conclusion, she shall watch all night.
And if she chances to nod, I'll rail and brawl
and with the clamor keep her still awake.
This is a way to kill a wife with kindness;
and thus I'll curb her mad and headstrong humor.
He that knows a better way to tame a shrew,
now let him speak. It is a charity to show.

TRANIO:
Is it possible, friend Licio, that Mistress Bianca
fancies any other but Lucentio?
I tell you, sir, she is leading me on.

HORTENSIO:
Sir, to satisfy you in what I have said,
stand by and mark the manner of his teaching.

LUCENTIO:
Now, mistress, are you profiting from what you are reading?

BIANCA:
What, master, are you reading? First, resolve me that.

LUCENTIO:
I am reading what I profess, the Art of Love.

BIANCA:
And may you prove, sir, to be a master of your art!

LUCENTIO:
While you, sweet dear, prove to be the mistress of my heart!

HORTENSIO:
Quick proceeders, indeed! Now, tell me, I pray,
you that dared to swear that your mistress Bianca
loved no one in the world as well as Lucentio.

TRANIO:
Oh, despiteful love! Unconstant womankind!
I tell you, Licio, this is wonderful.

HORTENSIO:
Make no more mistakes. I am not Licio,
nor a musician, as I seem to be;
but one that scorns to live in this disguise,
for such a one as leaves a gentleman,
and makes a god of such a scoundrel.
Know, sir, that I am called Hortensio.

TRANIO:
Signior Hortensio, I have often heard
of your entire affection to Bianca;
and since my eyes are a witness of her lightness,
I will with you, if you are so contented,
forswear Bianca and her love forever.

HORTENSIO:
See how they kiss and court! Signior Lucentio,
here is my hand, and here I firmly vow
never to woo her anymore, but I forswear her,
as one unworthy of all the former favors
that I have fondly flattered her with.

TRANIO:
And here I take the unfeigned oath,
never to marry her though she would entreat.
Fie on her! See, how beastly she courts him!

HORTENSIO:
I wish all the world but he had quite forsworn!
For me, so that I may surely keep my oath,
I will be married to a wealthy widow,
before three days pass, who has loved me as long
as I have loved this proud, disdainful haggard.
And so farewell, Signior Lucentio.
Kindness in women, not their beautiful looks,
shall win my love. And so I take my leave,
in resolution as I swore before.

TRANIO:
Mistress Bianca, may you be blessed with such grace
as belongs to a lover's blessed case!
No, I have caught you napping, gentle love,
and I have forsworn you with Hortensio.

BIANCA:
Tranio, you are jesting. But have you both forsworn me?

TRANIO:
Mistress, we have.

LUCENTIO:
Then we are rid of Licio.

TRANIO:
In faith, he'll have a lusty widow now,
that shall be wooed and wedded in a day.

BIANCA:
God give him joy!

TRANIO:
Yes, and he'll tame her.

BIANCA:
He says so, Tranio.

TRANIO:
Faith, he has gone to the taming-school.

BIANCA:
The taming-school! What, is there such a place?

TRANIO:
Yes, mistress, and Petruchio is the master;
he teaches eleven and twenty long tricks,
to tame a shrew and charm her chattering tongue.

BIONDELLO:
Oh master, master, I have watched so long
that I am dog-weary. But at last I spied
an ancient angel coming down the hill,
who will serve the turn.

TRANIO:
What is he, Biondello?

BIONDELLO:
Master, a merchant, or a pedant,
I don't know what; but formal in his apparel,
in his gait and countenance, he surely looks like a father.

LUCENTIO:
And what of him, Tranio?

TRANIO:
If he is credulous and trusts my tale,
I'll make him glad to seem to be Vincentio,
and give an assurance to Baptista Minola,
as if he were the real Vincentio.
Take in your love, and then let me alone.

Pedant:
God save you, sir!

TRANIO:
And you, sir! You are welcome.
Are you traveling far, or are you at the farthest?

Pedant:
Sir, I am at the farthest for a week or two,
but then I am going up farther, as far as Rome,
and so to Tripoli, if God lends me my life.

TRANIO:
What countryman are you, I pray?

Pedant:
Of Mantua.

TRANIO:
Of Mantua, sir? Indeed, God forbid!
And you have come to Padua, careless of your life?

Pedant:
My life, sir! How, I pray? For that goes hard.

TRANIO:
It is death for anyone from Mantua
to come to Padua. Do you not know the cause?
Your ships are stayed at Venice, and the duke,
for a private quarrel between your duke and him,
has published and proclaimed it openly.
It is a marvel, but since you have just arrived,
you might have heard it proclaimed about.

Pedant:
Alas! sir, it is worse for me than that;
for I have bills for money by exchange
from Florence and must deliver them here.

TRANIO:
Well, sir, to do you a courtesy,
this I will do, and this I will advise you.
First, tell me, have you ever been to Pisa?

Pedant:
Yes, sir, I have often been in Pisa,
Pisa renowned for its grave citizens.

TRANIO:
Among them, do you know one Vincentio?

Pedant:
I do not know him, but I have heard of him;
a merchant of incomparable wealth.

TRANIO:
He is my father, sir; and, to tell the truth,
in his countenance he somewhat resembles you.

BIONDELLO:

TRANIO:
To save your life in this extremity,
I will do you this favor for his sake;
and do not think it the worst of all your fortunes
that you are like Sir Vincentio.
His name and credit you shall undertake,
and in my house you shall be friendly lodged.
See that you take upon yourself as you should;
you understand me, sir. So you shall stay
until you have done your business in the city.
If this is a courtesy, sir, accept of it.

Pedant:
Oh sir, I do; and I will always repute you
as the patron of my life and liberty.

TRANIO:
Then go with me to make the matter good.
This, by the way, I let you understand;
my father is expected here every day,
to pass an assurance of a dower in marriage
between me and one of Baptista's daughters here.
In all these circumstances, I'll instruct you.
Go with me to clothe you as becomes you.

GRUMIO:
No, no, forsooth; I dare not for my life.

KATHARINA:
The more my wrong, the more his spite appears.
What, did he marry me to famish me?
Beggars, that come to my father's door,
upon entreaty, have a present alms;
if not, they meet with charity elsewhere.
But I, who never knew how to entreat,
nor ever needed to entreat,
am starved for meat, giddy for lack of sleep,
kept waking with oaths and fed with brawling.
And what spites me more than all these wants,
is that he does it under the name of perfect love;
as who should say, if I were to sleep or eat,
it would be a deadly sickness or else a present death.
I pray you, go and get me some repast;
I care not what, so it be wholesome food.

GRUMIO:
What do you say to a neat's foot?

KATHARINA:
It's very good. Please let me have it.

GRUMIO:
I fear it is too choleric a meat.
How do you say to a fat tripe finely broiled?

KATHARINA:
I like it well. Good Grumio, fetch it for me.

GRUMIO:
I cannot tell; I fear it is choleric.
What do you say to a piece of beef and mustard?

KATHARINA:
A dish that I do love to feed upon.

GRUMIO:
Yes, but the mustard is a little too hot.

KATHARINA:
Why then, the beef, and let the mustard rest.

GRUMIO:
No, then, I will not. You shall have the mustard,
or else you will get no beef from Grumio.

KATHARINA:
Then both, or one, or anything you will.

GRUMIO:
Why then, the mustard without the beef.

KATHARINA:
Go, get you gone, you false, deluding slave,
that feeds me with the very name of meat.
Sorrow on you and all the pack of you,
that triumph thus upon my misery!
Go, get you gone, I say.

PETRUCHIO:
How is my Kate? What, sweeting, are you all downcast?

HORTENSIO:
Mistress, what cheer?

KATHARINA:
Faith, as cold as can be.

PETRUCHIO:
Pluck up your spirits; look cheerfully upon me.
Here, love; you see how diligent I am
to dress your meat myself and bring it to you.
I am sure, sweet Kate, this kindness merits thanks.
What, not a word? No, then you do not love it;
and all my pains are sorted to no proof.
Here, take away this dish.

KATHARINA:
I pray you, let it stand.

PETRUCHIO:
The poorest service is repaid with thanks;
and so shall mine be, before you touch the meat.

KATHARINA:
I thank you, sir.

HORTENSIO:
Signior Petruchio, fie! You are to blame.
Come, mistress Kate, I'll bear you company.

PETRUCHIO:

Haberdasher:
Here is the cap your worship did bespeak.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, this was molded on a porringer;
a velvet dish. Fie, fie! It's lewd and filthy.
Why, it's a cockle or a walnut-shell,
a knack, a toy, a trick, a baby's cap.
Away with it! Come, let me have a bigger one.

KATHARINA:
I'll have no bigger. This fits the time,
and gentlewomen wear such caps as these.

PETRUCHIO:
When you are gentle, you shall have one too,
and not until then.

HORTENSIO:

KATHARINA:
Why, sir, I trust I may have leave to speak;
and I will speak; I am no child, no baby.
Your betters have endured me to say my mind,
and if you cannot, it is best you stop your ears.
My tongue will tell the anger of my heart,
or else my heart, concealing it, will break,
and rather than it shall, I will be free
even to the uttermost, as I please, in words.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, you say true; it is a paltry cap,
a custard-coffin, a bauble, a silken pie.
I love you well, in that you do not like it.

KATHARINA:
Love me or love me not, I like the cap;
and I will have it, or I will have none.

PETRUCHIO:
Your gown? Why, yes. Come, tailor, let us see it.
Oh, mercy, God! What masking stuff is here?
What's this? A sleeve? It's like a demi-cannon.
What, up and down, carved like an apple-tart?
Here's snip and nip and cut and slish and slash,
like to a censer in a barber's shop.
Why, what, in the devil's name, tailor, do you call this?

HORTENSIO:

Tailor:
You told me to make it orderly and well,
according to the fashion and the time.

PETRUCHIO:
Indeed, I did; but if you remember,
I did not tell you to mar it to the time.
Go, hop me over every kennel home,
for you shall hop without my custom, sir.
I'll have none of it. Hence! Make your best of it.

KATHARINA:
I never saw a better-fashioned gown,
more quaint, more pleasing, nor more commendable.
It's likely you mean to make a puppet of me.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, it's true; he means to make a puppet of you.

Tailor:
She says your worship means to make
a puppet of her.

PETRUCHIO:
Oh, monstrous arrogance! You lie, you thread,
you thimble,
you yard, three-quarters, half-yard, quarter, nail!
You flea, you nit, you winter-cricket, you!
Braved in my own house with a skein of thread?
Away, you rag, you quantity, you remnant;
or I shall so beat you with your yard
that you shall think on prating while you live!
I tell you, I, that you have marred her gown.

Tailor:
Your worship is deceived; the gown is made
just as my master had direction.
Grumio gave the order how it should be done.

GRUMIO:
I gave him no order; I gave him the stuff.

Tailor:
But how did you desire it should be made?

GRUMIO:
Well, sir, with a needle and thread.

Tailor:
But did you not request to have it cut?

GRUMIO:
You have faced many things.

Tailor:
I have.

GRUMIO:
Don't face me. You have braved many men; don't
brave me. I will neither be faced nor braved. I say to
you, I told your master to cut out the gown; but I did
not tell him to cut it to pieces. Therefore, you lie.

Tailor:
Why, here is the note of the fashion to testify.

PETRUCHIO:
Read it.

GRUMIO:
The note is a lie in his throat, if he says I said so.

Tailor:

GRUMIO:
Master, if ever I said loose-bodied gown, sew me in
the skirts of it, and beat me to death with a bottom
of brown thread. I said a gown.

PETRUCHIO:
Proceed.

Tailor:

GRUMIO:
I confess the cape.

Tailor:

GRUMIO:
I confess two sleeves.

Tailor:

PETRUCHIO:
Yes, there's the villany.

GRUMIO:
There's an error in the bill, sir; an error in the bill.
I commanded that the sleeves should be cut out and
sewn up again; and that I'll prove on you,
though your little finger is armed with a thimble.

Tailor:
This that I say is true. If I had you
in a place where, you would know it.

GRUMIO:
I am for you straight. You take the
bill, and give me your measuring stick, and don't spare me.

HORTENSIO:
God bless you, Grumio! Then he shall have no odds.

PETRUCHIO:
Well, sir, in brief, the gown is not for me.

GRUMIO:
You are in the right, sir. It is for my mistress.

PETRUCHIO:
Go, take it up for your master's use.

GRUMIO:
Villain, not for your life. Take up my mistress's
gown for your master's use!

PETRUCHIO:
Why, sir, what's your conceit in that?

GRUMIO:
Oh, sir, the conceit is deeper than you think for.
Take up my mistress's gown for his master's use!
Oh, fie, fie, fie!

PETRUCHIO:

HORTENSIO:
Tailor, I'll pay you for your gown tomorrow.
Take no unkindness from his hasty words.
Away! I say; commend me to your master.

PETRUCHIO:
Well, come, my Kate; we will go to your father's
even in these honest, mean clothes.
Our purses shall be proud, our garments poor;
for it is the mind that makes the body rich;
and as the sun breaks through the darkest clouds,
so honor peers in the meanest habit.
What is the jay more precious than the lark,
because his feathers are more beautiful?
Or is the adder better than the eel,
because his painted skin contents the eye?
Oh, no, good Kate; neither are you the worse
for this poor furniture and mean array.
If you account it a shame, lay it on me;
and therefore be merry. We will go from here forthwith,
to feast and sport us at your father's house.
Go, call my men, and let us go straight to him;
and bring our horses to Long-lane end;
there we will mount, and we will walk there on foot.
Let's see; I think it is now about seven o'clock,
and we may well come there by dinner-time.

KATHARINA:
I dare assure you, sir, it's almost two;
and it will be supper-time before you get there.

PETRUCHIO:
It shall be seven before I get on my horse.
Look, whatever I speak, or do, or think to do,
you are still crossing it. Sirs, let it alone.
I will not go today; and before I do,
it shall be what o'clock I say it is.

HORTENSIO:

TRANIO:
Sir, this is the house. Does it please you that I call?

Pedant:
Yes, what else? And if I'm not deceived,
Signior Baptista may remember me,
from nearly twenty years ago, in Genoa,
where we were lodgers at the Pegasus.

TRANIO:
It is well; and hold your own, in any case,
with such austerity as befits a father.

Pedant:
I guarantee it.
But, sir, here comes your boy;
it would be good if he were schooled.

TRANIO:
Don't you worry about him. Sirrah Biondello,
now do your duty thoroughly, I advise you.
Imagine it were the real Vincentio.

BIONDELLO:
Tut, don't worry about me.

TRANIO:
But have you done your errand to Baptista?

BIONDELLO:
I told him that your father was in Venice,
and that you were expecting him this day in Padua.

TRANIO:
You're a fine fellow. Have a drink for that.
Here comes Baptista. Set your countenance, sir.
Signior Baptista, you are happily met.
Sir, this is the gentleman I told you of.
I pray you, be a good father to me now,
and give me Bianca for my patrimony.

Pedant:
Wait, son!
Sir, with your leave. Having come to Padua
to collect some debts, my son Lucentio
made me acquainted with a weighty matter
of love between your daughter and himself.
And, for the good report I hear of you,
and for the love he bears to your daughter
and she to him, to not keep him waiting too long,
I am content, in a good father's care,
to have him matched. And if you please to like
no worse than I, upon some agreement,
you shall find me ready and willing
with one consent to have her so bestowed;
for I cannot be curious with you,
Signior Baptista, of whom I hear so well.

BAPTISTA:
Sir, pardon me in what I have to say.
Your plainness and your shortness please me well.
It is very true that your son Lucentio here
loves my daughter, and she loves him,
or they are both deeply dissembling their affections.
And therefore, if you say no more than this,
that like a father you will deal with him
and give my daughter a sufficient dower,
the match is made, and all is done.
Your son shall have my daughter with my consent.

TRANIO:
I thank you, sir. Where then do you know best
for us to be betrothed and for such assurance to be taken
as shall stand with the agreement of both parties?

BAPTISTA:
Not in my house, Lucentio; for, you know,
pitchers have ears, and I have many servants.
Besides, old Gremio is always listening;
and we might happily be interrupted.

TRANIO:
Then at my lodging, if it pleases you.
My father is lying there; and there, this night,
we'll pass the business privately and well.
Send for your daughter with your servant here.
My boy shall fetch the scrivener presently.
The worst is this, that, at such short notice,
you are likely to have a thin and slender pittance.

BAPTISTA:
I like it well. Biondello, hurry home,
and bid Bianca make herself ready straight away;
and, if you will, tell her what has happened,
that Lucentio's father has arrived in Padua,
and how she is likely to be Lucentio's wife.

BIONDELLO:
I pray to the gods that she may with all my heart!

TRANIO:
Don't dally with the gods, but get going.
Signior Baptista, shall I lead the way?
Welcome! One meal is likely to be your cheer.
Come, sir; we will make it better in Pisa.

BAPTISTA:
I follow you.

BIONDELLO:
Cambio!

LUCENTIO:
What do you say, Biondello?

BIONDELLO:
Did you see my master wink and laugh at you?

LUCENTIO:
Biondello, what of that?

BIONDELLO:
Faith, nothing; but he has left me here behind, to
expound the meaning or moral of his signs and tokens.

LUCENTIO:
I pray you, moralize them.

BIONDELLO:
Then thus. Baptista is safe, talking with the
deceiving father of a deceitful son.

LUCENTIO:
And what of him?

BIONDELLO:
His daughter is to be brought by you to the supper.

LUCENTIO:
And then?

BIONDELLO:
The old priest of Saint Luke's church is at your
command at all hours.

LUCENTIO:
And what of all this?

BIONDELLO:
I cannot tell; except that they are busy about a
counterfeit assurance. You take assurance of her,
'with the privilege of printing alone.' To the
church; take the priest, the clerk, and some sufficient
honest witnesses. If this is not what you are looking for,
I have no more to say, but to bid Bianca farewell for
ever and a day.

LUCENTIO:
Do you hear, Biondello?

BIONDELLO:
I cannot wait. I knew a girl who got married in an
afternoon as she was going to the garden for parsley to
stuff a rabbit; and so may you, sir. And so, adieu,
sir. My master has appointed me to go to Saint
Luke's, to ask the priest to be ready to come when
you come with your appendix.

LUCENTIO:
I may, and will, if she is so contented.
She will be pleased; then why should I doubt?
Happen what may, I'll go about her roundly.
It shall go hard if Cambio goes without her.

PETRUCHIO:
Come on, in God's name; once more toward our father's.
Good Lord, how bright and goodly the moon shines!

KATHARINA:
The moon! The sun. It is not moonlight now.

PETRUCHIO:
I say it is the moon that shines so bright.

KATHARINA:
I know it is the sun that shines so bright.

PETRUCHIO:
Now, by my mother's son, and that's myself,
it shall be the moon, or a star, or whatever I please,
before I journey to your father's house.
Go on, and fetch our horses back again.
Evermore crossed and crossed; nothing but crossed!

HORTENSIO:
Say as he says, or we shall never go.

KATHARINA:
Forward, I pray, since we have come so far,
and let it be the moon, or the sun, or whatever you please.
And if you please to call it a rush-candle,
from now on I vow it shall be so for me.

PETRUCHIO:
I say it is the moon.

KATHARINA:
I know it is the moon.

PETRUCHIO:
No, then you are lying. It is the blessed sun.

KATHARINA:
Then, God be blessed, it is the blessed sun.
But it is not the sun, when you say it is not;
and the moon changes even as your mind.
Whatever you will have it named, even that it is;
and so it shall be so for Katharina.

HORTENSIO:
Petruchio, go your ways; the field is won.

PETRUCHIO:
Well, forward, forward! Thus the bowl should run,
and not unluckily against the bias.
But, wait! Company is coming here.
Good morrow, gentle mistress. Where are you going?
Tell me, sweet Kate, and tell me truly too,
have you ever seen a fresher gentlewoman?
Such a war of white and red is within her cheeks!
What stars spangle the heavens with such beauty,
as those two eyes become that heavenly face?
Fair, lovely maid, once more good day to you.
Sweet Kate, embrace her for her beauty's sake.

HORTENSIO:
He will make the man mad, to make a woman of him.

KATHARINA:
Young, budding virgin, fair and fresh and sweet,
where are you going, or where is your abode?
Happy are the parents of so fair a child;
happier is the man, whom favorable stars
allot to you for his lovely bed-fellow!

PETRUCHIO:
Why, how now, Kate! I hope you are not mad.
This is a man, old, wrinkled, faded, and withered,
and not a maiden, as you say he is.

KATHARINA:
Pardon, old father, my mistaken eyes,
that have been so bedazzled with the sun
that everything I look on seems green.
Now I perceive you are a reverend father;
pardon me, I pray you, for my mad mistaking.

PETRUCHIO:
Do, good old grandsire; and also make known
which way you are traveling. If you are going along with us,
we shall be joyful of your company.

VINCENTIO:
Fair sir, and you my merry mistress,
that with your strange encounter much amazed me,
my name is called Vincentio; my dwelling is Pisa;
and I am bound for Padua; there to visit
a son of mine, which I have not seen for a long time.

PETRUCHIO:
What is his name?

VINCENTIO:
Lucentio, gentle sir.

PETRUCHIO:
We met happily; the happier for your son.
And now by law, as well as by reverend age,
I may call you my loving father.
The sister to my wife, this gentlewoman,
your son has by now married. Do not wonder,
nor be grieved. She is of good esteem,
her dowry is wealthy, and she is of worthy birth;
besides, she is so qualified as may befit
the spouse of any noble gentleman.
Let me embrace old Vincentio,
and let us wander to see your honest son,
who will be full of joy at your arrival.

VINCENTIO:
But is it true? Or else is it your pleasure,
like pleasant travelers, to break a jest
upon the company you overtake?

HORTENSIO:
I do assure you, father, that it is so.

PETRUCHIO:
Come, go along, and see the truth of this;
for our first merriment has made you jealous.

HORTENSIO:
Well, Petruchio, this has put me in heart.
I'm going to my widow! And if she is froward,
then you have taught Hortensio to be untoward.

BIONDELLO:
Softly and swiftly, sir; for the priest is ready.

LUCENTIO:
I'm flying, Biondello. But they may chance to need you
at home; therefore, leave us.

BIONDELLO:
No, in faith, I'll see the church on your back; and
then I'll come back to my master's as soon as I can.

GREMIO:
I am marveling that Cambio has not come all this while.

PETRUCHIO:
Sir, here's the door, this is Lucentio's house.
My father's is more toward the market-place;
I must go there, and here I leave you, sir.

VINCENTIO:
You shall not choose but to drink before you go.
I think I shall command your welcome here,
and, by all likelihood, some cheer is toward.

GREMIO:
They're busy inside; you were best to knock louder.

Pedant:
Who's that knocking as if he would beat down the gate?

VINCENTIO:
Is Signior Lucentio within, sir?

Pedant:
He's inside, sir, but he can't be spoken to.

VINCENTIO:
What if a man brings him a hundred pounds or two, to
make merry with?

Pedant:
Keep your hundred pounds to yourself. He won't
need any, as long as I live.

PETRUCHIO:
No, I told you your son was well beloved in Padua.
Do you hear, sir? To leave frivolous circumstances,
I pray you, tell Signior Lucentio that his father has
come from Pisa, and is here at the door to speak with him.

Pedant:
You lie. His father has come from Padua and is here
looking out of the window.

VINCENTIO:
Are you his father?

Pedant:
Yes, sir; so his mother says, if I may believe her.

PETRUCHIO:

Pedant:
Lay hands on the villain. I believe he means to
cheat somebody in this city under my countenance.

BIONDELLO:
I have seen them in the church together. God send
them good shipping! But who is here? My old
master Vincentio! Now we are undone and brought to nothing.

VINCENTIO:

BIONDELLO:
I hope I may choose, sir.

VINCENTIO:
Come here, you rogue. What, have you forgotten me?

BIONDELLO:
Forgotten you! No, sir. I could not forget you, for I
never saw you before in all my life.

VINCENTIO:
What, you notorious villain, did you never see
your master's father, Vincentio?

BIONDELLO:
What, my old, worshipful old master? Yes, indeed, sir.
See where he is looking out of the window.

VINCENTIO:
Is it so, indeed?

BIONDELLO:
Help, help, help! Here's a madman who will murder me.

Pedant:
Help, son! Help, Signior Baptista!

PETRUCHIO:
Please, Kate, let's stand aside and see the end of
this controversy.

TRANIO:
Sir, who are you that you offer to beat my servant?

VINCENTIO:
What am I, sir! No, what are you, sir? Oh, immortal
gods! Oh, fine villain! A silken doublet! A velvet
hose! A scarlet cloak! And a tall hat! Oh, I
am undone! I am undone! While I play the good
husband at home, my son and my servant spend all at
the university.

TRANIO:
What's this! What's the matter?

BAPTISTA:
What, is the man a lunatic?

TRANIO:
Sir, you seem a sober, ancient gentleman by your
habit, but your words show you to be a madman. Why, sir,
what does it concern you if I wear pearl and gold? I
thank my good father, I am able to maintain it.

VINCENTIO:
Your father! Oh, you villain! He is a sailmaker in Bergamo.

BAPTISTA:
You are mistaken, sir, you are mistaken. Please, what do
you think his name is?

VINCENTIO:
His name! As if I did not know his name. I have brought
him up ever since he was three years old, and his
name is Tranio.

Pedant:
Away, away, you mad ass! His name is Lucentio and he is
my only son, and the heir to my lands, Signior Vincentio.

VINCENTIO:
Lucentio! Oh, he has murdered his master! Lay hold
of him, I charge you, in the duke's name. Oh, my
son, my son! Tell me, you villain, where is my son Lucentio?

TRANIO:
Call forth an officer.
Carry this mad knave to the jail. Father Baptista,
I charge you to see that he is forthcoming.

VINCENTIO:
Carry me to the jail!

GREMIO:
Stay, officer. He shall not go to prison.

BAPTISTA:
Don't talk, Signior Gremio. I say he shall go to prison.

GREMIO:
Take heed, Signior Baptista, lest you are
tricked in this business. I dare swear this
is the real Vincentio.

Pedant:
Swear, if you dare.

GREMIO:
No, I dare not swear it.

TRANIO:
Then you were best to say that I am not Lucentio.

GREMIO:
Yes, I know you to be Signior Lucentio.

BAPTISTA:
Away with the dotard! To the jail with him!

VINCENTIO:
Thus strangers may be hailed and abused. Oh,
monstrous villain!

BIONDELLO:
Oh! We are spoiled and—over there he is. Deny him,
forswear him, or else we are all undone.

LUCENTIO:

VINCENTIO:
Does my sweet son live?

BIANCA:
Pardon, dear father.

BAPTISTA:
How have you offended?
Where is Lucentio?

LUCENTIO:
Here's Lucentio,
the true son to the true Vincentio;
who, by marriage, has made your daughter mine,
while counterfeit suppositions have blurred your eyes.

GREMIO:
Here's a plot, with a witness, to deceive us all!

VINCENTIO:
Where is that damned villain Tranio,
that faced and braved me in this matter so?

BAPTISTA:
Why, tell me, is not this my Cambio?

BIANCA:
Cambio has been changed into Lucentio.

LUCENTIO:
Love wrought these miracles. Bianca's love
made me exchange my state with Tranio,
while he bore my countenance in the town;
and happily I have arrived at last
at the wished-for haven of my bliss.
What Tranio did, I myself forced him to do;
then pardon him, sweet father, for my sake.

VINCENTIO:
I'll slit the villain's nose, who would have sent
me to the jail.

BAPTISTA:
But do you hear, sir? Have you married my daughter
without asking for my good will?

VINCENTIO:
Do not fear, Baptista; we will content you, go to. But
I will go in, to be revenged for this villainy.

BAPTISTA:
And I, to sound the depth of this knavery.

LUCENTIO:
Don't look pale, Bianca; your father will not frown.

GREMIO:
My cake is dough; but I'll go in among the rest,
out of hope of everything, but my share of the feast.

KATHARINA:
Husband, let's follow, to see the end of this ado.

PETRUCHIO:
First kiss me, Kate, and we will.

KATHARINA:
What, in the middle of the street?

PETRUCHIO:
What, are you ashamed of me?

KATHARINA:
No, sir, God forbid; but I am ashamed to kiss.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, then let's go home again. Come, sirrah, let's go.

KATHARINA:
No, I will give you a kiss. Now, please, love, stay.

PETRUCHIO:
Is not this well? Come, my sweet Kate.
Better once than never, for it is never too late.

LUCENTIO:
At last, though it took a long time, our jarring notes agree.
And it is time, when the raging war is done,
to smile at the scrapes and perils that are overblown.
My fair Bianca, bid my father welcome,
while I, with the same kindness, welcome yours.
Brother Petruchio, sister Katharina,
and you, Hortensio, with your loving widow,
feast with the best, and you are welcome to my house.
My banquet is to close up our stomachs,
after our great good cheer. Please, sit down;
for now we sit to chat as well as to eat.

PETRUCHIO:
Nothing but sit and sit, and eat and eat!

BAPTISTA:
Padua affords this kindness, son Petruchio.

PETRUCHIO:
Padua affords nothing but what is kind.

HORTENSIO:
For both our sakes, I wish that word were true.

PETRUCHIO:
Now, for my life, Hortensio fears his widow.

Widow:
Then never trust me, if I am afraid.

PETRUCHIO:
You are very sensible, and yet you miss my sense.
I mean, Hortensio is afraid of you.

Widow:
He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.

PETRUCHIO:
Roundly replied.

KATHARINA:
Mistress, what do you mean by that?

Widow:
Thus I conceive of him.

PETRUCHIO:
Conceives of me! How does Hortensio like that?

HORTENSIO:
My widow says, this is how she conceives her tale.

PETRUCHIO:
Very well mended. Kiss him for that, good widow.

KATHARINA:
'He that is giddy thinks the world turns round.'
I pray you, tell me what you meant by that.

Widow:
Your husband, being troubled with a shrew,
measures my husband's sorrow by his own woe.
And now you know my meaning.

KATHARINA:
A very mean meaning.

Widow:
Right, I mean you.

KATHARINA:
And I am mean indeed, in comparison to you.

PETRUCHIO:
To her, Kate!

HORTENSIO:
To her, widow!

PETRUCHIO:
A hundred marks, my Kate will put her down.

HORTENSIO:
That's my office.

PETRUCHIO:
Spoken like an officer; have at you, lad!

BAPTISTA:
How does Gremio like these quick-witted folks?

GREMIO:
Believe me, sir, they butt together well.

BIANCA:
Head, and butt! A hasty-witted body
would say your head and butt were a head and a horn.

VINCENTIO:
Yes, mistress bride, has that awakened you?

BIANCA:
Yes, but it has not frightened me; therefore, I'll go to sleep again.

PETRUCHIO:
No, that you shall not. Since you have begun,
I'll have at you for a bitter jest or two!

BIANCA:
Am I your bird? I mean to shift my bush;
and then pursue me as you draw your bow.
You are all welcome.

PETRUCHIO:
She has prevented me. Here, Signior Tranio.
This bird you aimed at, though you did not hit her;
therefore, a health to all that shot and missed.

TRANIO:
Oh, sir, Lucentio slipped me like his greyhound,
which runs himself and catches for his master.

PETRUCHIO:
A good, swift simile, but somewhat currish.

TRANIO:
It's well, sir, that you hunted for yourself.
It's thought your deer is holding you at bay.

BAPTISTA:
Oh ho, Petruchio! Tranio has hit you now.

LUCENTIO:
I thank you for that jibe, good Tranio.

HORTENSIO:
Confess, confess, has he not hit you here?

PETRUCHIO:
He has galled me a little, I confess;
and, as the jest did glance away from me,
it is ten to one it has maimed you two outright.

BAPTISTA:
Now, in good sadness, son Petruchio,
I think you have the most shrewish of all.

PETRUCHIO:
Well, I say no. And therefore, for assurance,
let each one of us send for his wife;
and he whose wife is the most obedient
to come at first when he sends for her,
shall win the wager which we will propose.

HORTENSIO:
Content. What is the wager?

LUCENTIO:
Twenty crowns.

PETRUCHIO:
Twenty crowns!
I'll venture that much on my hawk or hound,
but twenty times as much on my wife.

LUCENTIO:
A hundred then.

HORTENSIO:
Content.

PETRUCHIO:
It's a match! It's done.

HORTENSIO:
Who shall begin?

LUCENTIO:
That I will.
Go, Biondello, and tell your mistress to come to me.

BIONDELLO:
I'm going.

BAPTISTA:
Son, I'll be your half if Bianca comes.

LUCENTIO:
I'll have no halves; I'll bear it all myself.
How now! What news?

BIONDELLO:
Sir, my mistress sends you word
that she is busy and she cannot come.

PETRUCHIO:
What! She is busy and she cannot come!
Is that an answer?

GREMIO:
Yes, and a kind one too.
I pray to God, sir, that your wife does not send you a worse one.

PETRUCHIO:
I hope for better.

HORTENSIO:
Sirrah Biondello, go and entreat my wife
to come to me forthwith.

PETRUCHIO:
Oh, ho! Entreat her!
No, then she must needs come.

HORTENSIO:
I am afraid, sir,
do what you can, yours will not be entreated.
Now, where is my wife?

BIONDELLO:
She says you have some goodly jest in hand.
She will not come. She bids you to come to her.

PETRUCHIO:
Worse and worse; she will not come! Oh vile,
intolerable, not to be endured!
Sirrah Grumio, go to your mistress;
say, I command her to come to me.

HORTENSIO:
I know her answer.

PETRUCHIO:
What?

HORTENSIO:
She will not.

PETRUCHIO:
The fouler fortune is mine, and there's an end to it.

BAPTISTA:
Now, by my holy dame, here comes Katharina!

KATHARINA:
What is your will, sir, that you send for me?

PETRUCHIO:
Where is your sister, and Hortensio's wife?

KATHARINA:
They are sitting and conferring by the parlor fire.

PETRUCHIO:
Go and fetch them here. If they refuse to come,
swing them soundly forth to their husbands.
Away, I say, and bring them here straight away.

LUCENTIO:
Here is a wonder, if you talk of a wonder.

HORTENSIO:
And so it is. I wonder what it bodes.

PETRUCHIO:
Indeed, it bodes peace, and love, and a quiet life,
and awful rule and right supremacy;
and, to be short, what not, that's sweet and happy?

BAPTISTA:
Now, may good fortune befall you, good Petruchio!
You have won the wager; and I will add
to their losses twenty thousand crowns;
another dowry to another daughter,
for she is changed, as if she had never been.

PETRUCHIO:
No, I will win my wager even better yet,
and show more signs of her obedience,
her newly-built virtue and obedience.
See where she comes and brings your froward wives
as prisoners to her womanly persuasion.
Katharina, that cap of yours does not become you.
Off with that bauble, and throw it under your foot.

Widow:
Lord, let me never have a cause to sigh,
until I am brought to such a silly pass!

BIANCA:
Fie! What a foolish duty you call this!

LUCENTIO:
I wish your duty were as foolish too.
The wisdom of your duty, fair Bianca,
has cost me a hundred crowns since supper-time.

BIANCA:
The more fool you, for wagering on my duty.

PETRUCHIO:
Katharina, I charge you, tell these headstrong women
what duty they owe their lords and husbands.

Widow:
Come, come, you're mocking. We will have no telling.

PETRUCHIO:
Come on, I say; and first begin with her.

Widow:
She shall not.

PETRUCHIO:
I say she shall, and she shall begin with her.

KATHARINA:
Fie, fie! Unknit that threatening, unkind brow,
and do not dart scornful glances from those eyes,
to wound your lord, your king, your governor.
It blots your beauty as frosts do bite the meads,
it confounds your fame as whirlwinds shake fair buds,
and in no sense is it fitting or amiable.
A woman who is moved is like a fountain that is troubled,
muddy, ill-seeming, thick, and bereft of beauty;
and while it is so, no one, no matter how dry or thirsty,
will deign to sip or touch one drop of it.
Your husband is your lord, your life, your keeper,
your head, your sovereign; one that cares for you,
and for your maintenance commits his body
to painful labor both by sea and land,
to watch the night in storms, the day in cold,
while you lie warm at home, secure and safe;
and he craves no other tribute at your hands
but love, fair looks, and true obedience;
too little payment for so great a debt.
Such duty as the subject owes the prince,
even such a woman owes to her husband;
and when she is froward, peevish, sullen, and sour,
and not obedient to his honest will,
what is she but a foul, contending rebel
and a graceless traitor to her loving lord?
I am ashamed that women are so simple
to offer war where they should kneel for peace;
or to seek for rule, supremacy, and sway,
when they are bound to serve, love, and obey.
Why are our bodies soft and weak and smooth,
unfit for toil and trouble in the world,
but that our soft conditions and our hearts
should well agree with our external parts?
Come, come, you froward and unable worms!
My mind has been as big as one of yours,
my heart as great, my reason perhaps more,
to bandy word for word and frown for frown;
but now I see our lances are but straws,
our strength as weak, our weakness past compare,
that we seem to be the most which we are indeed the least.
Then vail your stomachs, for it is no boot,
and place your hands below your husband's foot.
In token of which duty, if he pleases,
my hand is ready; may it do him ease.

PETRUCHIO:
Why, there's a wench! Come on, and kiss me, Kate.

LUCENTIO:
Well, go your ways, old lad; for you shall have it.

VINCENTIO:
It's a good hearing when children are toward.

LUCENTIO:
But a harsh hearing when women are froward.

PETRUCHIO:
Come, Kate, we'll go to bed.
We three are married, but you two are sped.
It was I who won the wager, though you hit the white;
and, being a winner, God give you good night!

HORTENSIO:
Now, go your ways; you have tamed a cursed shrew.

LUCENTIO:
It's a wonder, by your leave, that she will be tamed so.

Master:
Boatswain!

Boatswain:
Here, master. What's the news?

Master:
Good, speak to the mariners. Get to it, quickly,
or we'll run ourselves aground. Bestir, bestir.

Boatswain:
Hey, my hearts! Cheer up, cheer up, my hearts!
Quickly, quickly! Take in the topsail. Tend to the
master's whistle. Blow, until you burst your wind,
if there's enough room!

ALONSO:
Good boatswain, have care. Where's the master?
Play the men.

Boatswain:
I pray you now, keep below.

ANTONIO:
Where is the master, boatswain?

Boatswain:
Don't you hear him? You are marring our labor. Keep to your
cabins. You are assisting the storm.

GONZALO:
No, good, be patient.

Boatswain:
When the sea is. Go away! What do these roarers care
for the name of a king? To your cabin. Silence! Don't trouble us.

GONZALO:
Good, yet remember whom you have on board.

Boatswain:
None that I love more than myself. You are a
counselor; if you can command these elements to
silence, and work the peace of the present, we will
not handle a rope anymore; use your authority. If you
cannot, give thanks that you have lived so long, and make
yourself ready in your cabin for the mischance of
the hour, if it so happens. Cheer up, good hearts! Out
of our way, I say.

GONZALO:
I have great comfort from this fellow. I think he
has no drowning mark on him; his complexion is
perfect for the gallows. Stand fast, good Fate, to his
hanging. Make the rope of his destiny our cable,
for our own does little to help. If he is not
born to be hanged, our case is miserable.

Boatswain:
Down with the topmast! Quick! Lower, lower! Bring
her to try with the main-course.
A plague upon this howling! They are louder than
the weather or our office.
Yet again! What are you doing here? Shall we give up
and drown? Do you have a mind to sink?

SEBASTIAN:
A pox on your throat, you bawling, blasphemous,
uncharitable dog!

Boatswain:
You work then.

ANTONIO:
Hang, you cur! Hang, you whoreson, insolent noisemaker!
We are less afraid to be drowned than you are.

GONZALO:
I'll warrant him against drowning; though the ship were
no stronger than a nutshell and as leaky as an
un-stanched wench.

Boatswain:
Lay her a-hold, a-hold! Set her two courses off to
sea again; lay her off.

Mariners:
All is lost! To your prayers, to your prayers! All is lost!

Boatswain:
What, must our mouths be cold?

GONZALO:
The king and prince are at their prayers! Let's assist them,
for our case is the same as theirs.

SEBASTIAN:
I'm out of patience.

ANTONIO:
We are merely cheated of our lives by drunkards.
This wide-chapped rascal—I wish you might lie drowning
in the washing of ten tides!

GONZALO:
He'll be hanged yet,
though every drop of water swears against it
and gapes at its widest to glut him.

ANTONIO:
Let's all sink with the king.

SEBASTIAN:
Let's take leave of him.

GONZALO:
Now I would give a thousand furlongs of sea for an
acre of barren ground, long heath, brown furze, anything.
May the wills above be done! But I would be glad to
die a dry death.

MIRANDA:
If by your art, my dearest father, you have
put the wild waters in this roar, allay them.
The sky, it seems, would pour down stinking pitch,
but that the sea, mounting to the welkin's cheek,
dashes the fire out. Oh, I have suffered
with those that I saw suffer. A brave vessel,
who had, no doubt, some noble creature in her,
was dashed all to pieces. Oh, the cry did knock
against my very heart. Poor souls, they perished.
If I had been any god of power, I would
have sunk the sea within the earth before
it should have so swallowed the good ship and
the freighting souls within her.

PROSPERO:
Be collected.
No more amazement. Tell your piteous heart
there's no harm done.

MIRANDA:
Oh, woe the day!

PROSPERO:
No harm.
I have done nothing but in care of you,
of you, my dear one, you, my daughter, who
are ignorant of what you are, knowing nothing
of where I come from, nor that I am more better
than Prospero, the master of a full, poor cell,
and your no greater father.

MIRANDA:
To know more
never meddled with my thoughts.

PROSPERO:
It is time
I should inform you further. Lend me your hand,
and pluck my magic garment from me. So,
lie there, my art. Wipe your eyes; have comfort.
The direful spectacle of the shipwreck, which touched
the very virtue of compassion in you,
I have with such provision in my art
so safely ordered that there is no soul—
no, not so much perdition as a hair
that befell any creature in the vessel
which you heard cry, which you saw sink. Sit down;
for you must now know more.

MIRANDA:
You have often
begun to tell me what I am, but you have stopped
and left me to a bootless inquisition,
concluding, 'Stay, not yet.'

PROSPERO:
The hour has now come;
the very minute bids you open your ear;
obey and be attentive. Can you remember
a time before we came to this cell?
I do not think you can, for then you were not
quite three years old.

MIRANDA:
Certainly, sir, I can.

PROSPERO:
By what? By any other house or person?
Tell me the image of anything
that has stayed with your remembrance.

MIRANDA:
It is far off,
and rather like a dream than an assurance
that my remembrance warrants. Did I not have
four or five women who once tended to me?

PROSPERO:
You had, and more, Miranda. But how is it
that this lives in your mind? What else do you see
in the dark backward and abysm of time?
If you remember anything before you came here,
you may remember how you came here.

MIRANDA:
But I do not.

PROSPERO:
Twelve years ago, Miranda, twelve years ago,
your father was the Duke of Milan and
a prince of power.

MIRANDA:
Sir, are you not my father?

PROSPERO:
Your mother was a piece of virtue, and
she said you were my daughter; and your father
was the Duke of Milan; and you were his only heir
and a princess of no worse issue.

MIRANDA:
Oh, the heavens!
What foul play did we have, that we came from there?
Or was it blessed that we did?

PROSPERO:
Both, both, my girl.
By foul play, as you say, we were heaved from there,
but blessedly helped here.

MIRANDA:
Oh, my heart bleeds
to think of the trouble that I have turned you to,
which is from my remembrance! Please, go on.

PROSPERO:
My brother and your uncle, named Antonio—
I pray you, mark me—that a brother should
be so perfidious!—he whom, next to yourself,
I loved of all the world and to whom I put
the management of my state; as at that time
it was the first through all the signories
and Prospero the prime duke, being so reputed
in dignity, and for the liberal arts
without a parallel; those being all my study,
I cast the government upon my brother
and grew a stranger to my state, being transported
and rapt in secret studies. Your false uncle—
do you attend me?

MIRANDA:
Sir, most heedfully.

PROSPERO:
Being once perfected in how to grant suits,
how to deny them, who to advance and who
to trash for over-topping, he newly created
the creatures that were mine, I say, or he changed them,
or else he newly formed them; having both the key
of the officer and the office, he set all the hearts in the state
to whatever tune pleased his ear; so that now he was
the ivy which had hidden my princely trunk,
and had sucked my verdure out of it. You are not attending.

MIRANDA:
Oh, good sir, I am.

PROSPERO:
I pray you, mark me.
I, thus neglecting worldly ends, all dedicated
to closeness and the bettering of my mind
with that which, but by being so retired,
was over-prized at all popular rates, in my false brother
awoke an evil nature; and my trust,
like a good parent, did beget of him
a falsehood in its contrary as great
as my trust was; which had indeed no limit,
a confidence without bound. He, being thus lorded,
not only with what my revenue yielded,
but with what my power might else exact, like one
who, having into truth, by telling of it,
made such a sinner of his memory,
as to credit his own lie, he did believe
he was indeed the duke; out of the substitution
and executing the outward face of royalty,
with all its prerogatives. Hence his ambition grew—
do you hear?

MIRANDA:
Your tale, sir, would cure deafness.

PROSPERO:
To have no screen between this part he played
and him he played it for, he needs will be
absolute Milan. Me, poor man, my library
was a dukedom large enough. Of temporal royalties
he thinks me now incapable; he confederates—
so dry was he for sway—with the King of Naples
to give him an annual tribute, to do him homage,
to subject his coronet to his crown and to bend
the dukedom, yet unbowed—alas, poor Milan!—
to a most ignoble stooping.

MIRANDA:
Oh, the heavens!

PROSPRO:
Mark his condition and the event; then tell me
if this could be a brother.

MIRANDA:
I would sin
to think anything but nobly of my grandmother.
Good wombs have borne bad sons.

PROSPERO:
Now, the condition.
The King of Naples, being an enemy
inveterate to me, hearkens to my brother's suit;
which was, that he, in lieu of the premises
of homage and I know not how much tribute,
should presently extirpate me and mine
out of the dukedom and confer fair Milan
with all the honors on my brother. Whereon,
a treacherous army was levied, and one midnight
fated to the purpose, Antonio opened
the gates of Milan, and, in the dead of darkness,
the ministers for the purpose hurried me
and your crying self from there.

MIRANDA:
Alas, for pity!
I, not remembering how I cried out then,
will cry it over again. It is a hint
that wrings my eyes to it.

PROSPERO:
Hear a little further,
and then I'll bring you to the present business
which is now upon us; without which, this story
would be most impertinent.

MIRANDA:
Why did they not
destroy us that hour?

PROSPERO:
Well demanded, wench.
My tale provokes that question. Dear, they did not dare,
so dear was the love my people bore me, nor did they set
a mark so bloody on the business, but
they painted their foul ends with fairer colors.
In short, they hurried us aboard a bark,
and carried us some leagues to sea; where they prepared
a rotten carcass of a boat, not rigged,
nor with tackle, sail, or mast; the very rats
had instinctively quit it. There they hoist us,
to cry to the sea that roared to us, to sigh
to the winds whose pity, sighing back again,
did us but a loving wrong.

MIRANDA:
Alas, what trouble
was I then to you!

PROSPERO:
Oh, you were a cherubim
that did preserve me. You smiled,
infused with a fortitude from heaven,
when I had decked the sea with drops full of salt,
and groaned under my burden; which raised in me
an undergoing stomach, to bear up
against what should ensue.

MIRANDA:
How did we get ashore?

PROSPERO:
By divine Providence.
We had some food and some fresh water that
a noble Neapolitan, Gonzalo,
out of his charity, being then appointed
master of this design, did give us, with
rich garments, linens, stuffs, and necessaries,
which have since been of much help; so, of his gentleness,
knowing I loved my books, he furnished me
from my own library with volumes that
I prize above my dukedom.

MIRANDA:
I wish I might
but ever see that man!

PROSPERO:
Now I arise.
Sit still, and hear the last of our sea-sorrow.
Here in this island we arrived; and here
I, your schoolmaster, have made you more profit
than other princesses can, who have more time
for vainer hours and tutors who are not so careful.

MIRANDA:
Heavens thank you for it! And now, I pray you, sir,
for it is still beating in my mind, what was your reason
for raising this sea-storm?

PROSPERO:
Know thus far forth.
By a most strange accident, bountiful Fortune,
now my dear lady, has brought my enemies
to this shore; and by my prescience
I find that my zenith depends upon
a most auspicious star, whose influence
if I do not now court but omit, my fortunes
will ever after droop. Here, cease more questions.
You are inclined to sleep; it is a good dullness,
and give way to it. I know you cannot choose.
Come away, servant, come. I am ready now.
Approach, my Ariel, come.

ARIEL:
All hail, great master! Grave sir, hail! I have come
to answer your best pleasure; whether it be to fly,
to swim, to dive into the fire, to ride
on the curled clouds, to your strong bidding, I will task
Ariel and all his quality.

PROSPERO:
Have you, spirit,
performed to the point the tempest that I bade you to?

ARIEL:
To every article.
I boarded the king's ship; now on the beak,
now in the waist, the deck, in every cabin,
I flamed with amazement. Sometimes I would divide,
and burn in many places; on the topmast,
the yards and bowsprit, I would flame distinctly,
then meet and join. Jove's lightnings, the precursors
of the dreadful thunder-claps, were not more momentary
and sight-outrunning; the fire and cracks
of sulphurous roaring made the most mighty Neptune
seem to be besieged and made his bold waves tremble,
yes, and his dread trident shake.

PROSPERO:
My brave spirit!
Who was so firm, so constant, that this coil
would not infect his reason?

ARIEL:
Not a soul
but felt a fever of the mad and played
some tricks of desperation. All but the mariners
plunged into the foaming brine and quit the vessel,
which was then all on fire with me. The king's son, Ferdinand,
with his hair standing on end—then like reeds, not hair—
was the first man that leaped; he cried, 'Hell is empty
and all the devils are here.'

PROSPERO:
