Why, that's my spirit!
But was this not near the shore?

ARIEL:
Close by, my master.

PROSPERO:
But are they, Ariel, safe?

ARIEL:
Not a hair perished;
on their sustaining garments, not a blemish,
but fresher than before. And, as you told me,
I have dispersed them in troops about the isle.
The king's son I have landed by himself;
whom I left cooling the air with sighs
in an odd angle of the isle and sitting,
his arms in this sad knot.

PROSPERO:
Of the king's ship,
the mariners, say how you have disposed of them,
and all the rest of the fleet.

ARIEL:
The king's ship is safely in the harbor;
in the deep nook, where you once
called me up at midnight to fetch dew
from the still-vexed Bermudas, there she is hidden.
The mariners are all stowed under the hatches;
who, with a charm joined to their suffered labor,
I have left asleep. And as for the rest of the fleet
which I dispersed, they have all met again
and are upon the Mediterranean float,
bound sadly home for Naples,
supposing that they saw the king's ship wrecked
and his great person perish.

PROSPERO:
Ariel, your charge
is exactly performed. But there's more work.
What is the time of day?

ARIEL:
Past the mid-season.

PROSPERO:
At least two hours. The time between six and now
must be spent most preciously by both of us.

ARIEL:
Is there more toil? Since you are giving me pains,
let me remind you what you have promised,
which you have not yet performed for me.

PROSPERO:
What now? Moody?
What is it you can demand?

ARIEL:
My liberty.

PROSPERO:
Before the time is out? No more!

ARIEL:
I beg you,
remember I have done you worthy service;
I have told you no lies, made you no mistakes, and served
without any grudge or grumblings. You promised
to reduce my sentence by a full year.

PROSPERO:
Do you forget
from what a torment I freed you?

ARIEL:
No.

PROSPERO:
You do, and you think it is much to tread the ooze
of the salt deep,
to run upon the sharp wind of the north,
to do my business in the veins of the earth
when it is baked with frost.

ARIEL:
I do not, sir.

PROSPERO:
You lie, you malignant thing! Have you forgotten
the foul witch Sycorax, who with age and envy
had grown into a hoop? Have you forgotten her?

ARIEL:
No, sir.

PROSPERO:
You have. Where was she born? Speak; tell me.

ARIEL:
Sir, in Algiers.

PROSPERO:
Oh, was she so? I must
once a month recount what you have been,
which you forget. This damned witch Sycorax,
for manifold mischiefs and terrible sorceries
to enter human hearing, from Algiers,
you know, was banished. For one thing she did,
they would not take her life. Is this not true?

ARIEL:
Yes, sir.

PROSPERO:
This blue-eyed hag was brought here with a child
and was left here by the sailors. You, my slave,
as you report yourself, were then her servant;
and, because you were a spirit too delicate
to act her earthy and abhorred commands,
refusing her grand behests, she did confine you,
by the help of her more potent ministers,
and in her most unmitigable rage,
into a cloven pine; within which rift,
imprisoned, you did painfully remain
for a dozen years; within which space she died
and left you there; where you did vent your groans
as fast as mill-wheels strike. Then this island was—
save for the son that she did give birth to here,
a freckled whelp hag-born—not honored with
a human shape.

ARIEL:
Yes, Caliban, her son.

PROSPERO:
Dull thing, I say so; he, that Caliban
whom I now keep in service. You know best
what torment I found you in; your groans
did make wolves howl and penetrate the breasts
of ever-angry bears. It was a torment
to lay upon the damned, which Sycorax
could not undo again. It was my art,
when I arrived and heard you, that made the pine
gape and let you out.

ARIEL:
I thank you, master.

PROSPERO:
If you murmur more, I will rend an oak
and peg you in his knotty entrails until
you have howled away twelve winters.

ARIEL:
Pardon, master;
I will be correspondent to your command
and do my spiriting gently.

PROSPERO:
Do so, and after two days,
I will discharge you.

ARIEL:
That's my noble master!
What shall I do? Say what; what shall I do?

PROSPERO:
Go and make yourself like a nymph of the sea. Be subject
to no sight but yours and mine, invisible
to every other eyeball. Go and take this shape
and come here in it. Go, hence with diligence!
Awake, dear heart, awake! You have slept well; awake!

MIRANDA:
The strangeness of your story put
heaviness in me.

PROSPERO:
Shake it off. Come on;
we'll visit Caliban my slave, who never
gives us a kind answer.

MIRANDA:
He is a villain, sir,
I do not love to look at him.

PROSPERO:
But, as it is,
we cannot miss him. He makes our fire,
fetches in our wood, and serves in offices
that profit us. What, ho! Slave! Caliban!
You earth, you! Speak.

CALIBAN:

PROSPERO:
Come forth, I say! There's other business for you.
Come, you tortoise! When?
Fine apparition! My quaint Ariel,
listen in your ear.

ARIEL:
My lord, it shall be done.

PROSPERO:
You poisonous slave, gotten by the devil himself
upon your wicked dam, come forth!

CALIBAN:
As wicked a dew as my mother ever brushed
with a raven's feather from an unwholesome fen,
may it drop on you both! May a south-west wind blow on you
and blister you all over!

PROSPERO:
For this, be sure, tonight you shall have cramps,
side-stitches that shall pen up your breath; urchins
shall, for that vast of night that they may work,
all exercise on you; you shall be pinched
as thick as a honeycomb, each pinch more stinging
than the bees that made them.

CALIBAN:
I must eat my dinner.
This island is mine, by Sycorax my mother,
which you take from me. When you first came,
you stroked me and made much of me, you would give me
water with berries in it, and teach me how
to name the bigger light, and how the less,
that burn by day and night. And then I loved you
and showed you all the qualities of the isle,
the fresh springs, the brine-pits, the barren places and the fertile ones.
Cursed be I that I did so! May all the charms
of Sycorax, toads, beetles, and bats, light on you!
For I am all the subjects that you have,
who was at first my own king. And here you sty me
in this hard rock, while you keep from me
the rest of the island.

PROSPERO:
You most lying slave,
whom stripes may move, not kindness! I have treated you,
filth as you are, with human care, and lodged you
in my own cell, until you sought to violate
the honor of my child.

CALIBAN:
Oh ho, oh ho! I wish it had been done!
You prevented me; I would have peopled this
isle with Calibans otherwise.

PROSPERO:
Abhorred slave,
who will not take any print of goodness,
being capable of all ill! I pitied you,
I took pains to make you speak, and I taught you each hour
one thing or another. When you, a savage, did not
know your own meaning, but would gabble like
a most brutish thing, I endowed your purposes
with words that made them known. But your vile race,
though you did learn, had that in it which
good natures
could not abide to be with; therefore, you were
deservedly confined into this rock,
you who had deserved more than a prison.

CALIBAN:
You taught me language; and my profit on it
is, I know how to curse. May the red plague rid you
for teaching me your language!

PROSPERO:
Hag-seed, hence!
Fetch us in fuel; and be quick, you're best,
to answer other business. Are you shrugging, malice?
If you neglect or do unwillingly
what I command, I'll rack you with old cramps,
fill all your bones with aches, and make you roar
so that the beasts will tremble at your din.

CALIBAN:
No, I pray you.
I must obey. His art is of such power,
it would control my dam's god, Setebos,
and make a vassal of him.

PROSPERO:
So, slave; hence!
Come unto these yellow sands,
and then take hands.
When you have curtsied and kissed,
and the wild waves are quiet,
foot it featly here and there;
and, sweet sprites, bear the burden.
Hark, hark!

FERDINAND:
Where should this music be? In the air or on the earth?
It sounds no more. And surely, it waits upon
some god of the island. Sitting on a bank,
weeping again for the wreck of the king my father,
this music crept by me upon the waters,
allaying both their fury and my passion
with its sweet air. From there I have followed it,
or it has drawn me, rather. But it is gone.
No, it begins again.
Full fathom five your father lies;
of his bones are coral made;
those are pearls that were his eyes.
Nothing of him that does fade
but does suffer a sea-change
into something rich and strange.
Sea-nymphs hourly ring his knell.
Hark! Now I hear them—Ding-dong, bell.

FERDINAND:
The ditty reminds me of my drowned father.
This is no mortal business, nor any sound
that the earth owes. I hear it now above me.

PROSPERO:
Advance the fringed curtains of your eyes
and say what you see over there.

MIRANDA:
What is it? A spirit?
Lord, how it looks about! Believe me, sir,
it has a brave form. But it is a spirit.

PROSPERO:
No, wench; it eats and sleeps and has such senses
as we have, such. This gallant which you see
was in the shipwreck; and, but that he is something stained
with grief that is beauty's canker, you might call him
a goodly person. He has lost his fellows
and is straying about to find them.

MIRANDA:
I might call him
a divine thing, for I have never seen anything natural
so noble.

PROSPERO:

FERDINAND:
Most surely, the goddess
on whom these airs attend! Vouchsafe my prayer
that I may know if you remain upon this island;
and that you will give some good instruction
on how I may bear myself here. My prime request,
which I do last pronounce, is, oh you wonder!
If you are a maid or not?

MIRANDA:
No wonder, sir;
but certainly a maid.

FERDINAND:
My language! Heavens!
I am the best of them that speak this speech,
if I were but where it is spoken.

PROSPERO:
What? The best?
What would you be, if the King of Naples heard you?

FERDINAND:
A single thing, as I am now, that wonders
to hear you speak of Naples. He does hear me;
and that he does, I weep. I myself am Naples,
who, with my eyes, which have never since been at ebb, beheld
the king my father wrecked.

MIRANDA:
Alas, for mercy!

FERDINAND:
Yes, in faith, and all his lords; the Duke of Milan
and his brave son being two.

PROSPERO:

MIRANDA:
Why does my father speak so un-gently? This
is the third man that I have ever seen, the first
that I have ever sighed for. May pity move my father
to be inclined my way!

FERDINAND:
Oh, if you are a virgin,
and your affection has not gone forth, I'll make you
the queen of Naples.

PROSPERO:
Wait, sir! One more word.
They are both in each other's power; but this swift business
I must make uneasy, lest too light winning
make the prize light.
One more word; I charge you
that you attend me. You are usurping here
the name you do not owe; and you have put yourself
upon this island as a spy, to win it
from me, the lord of it.

FERDINAND:
No, as I am a man.

MIRANDA:
There's nothing ill that can dwell in such a temple.
If the ill spirit has so fair a house,
good things will strive to dwell with it.

PROSPERO:
Follow me.
Do not speak for him; he is a traitor. Come;
I'll manacle your neck and feet together.
You shall drink sea-water; your food shall be
the fresh-brook mussels, withered roots, and husks
in which the acorn was cradled. Follow.

FERDINAND:
No;
I will resist such entertainment until
my enemy has more power.

MIRANDA:
Oh dear father,
don't make too rash a trial of him, for
he's gentle and not fearful.

PROSPERO:
What? I say,
my foot my tutor? Put your sword up, traitor;
you who make a show but do not dare to strike, your conscience
is so possessed with guilt. Come from your guard,
for I can disarm you here with this stick
and make your weapon drop.

MIRANDA:
I beseech you, father.

PROSPERO:
Go away! Do not hang on my garments.

MIRANDA:
Sir, have pity;
I'll be his surety.

PROSPERO:
Silence! One more word
shall make me scold you, if not hate you. What!
An advocate for an imposter! Hush!
You think there are no more such shapes as he,
having seen but him and Caliban. Foolish wench!
To most men, this is a Caliban
and they are angels to him.

MIRANDA:
My affections
are then most humble; I have no ambition
to see a goodlier man.

PROSPERO:
Come on; obey.
Your nerves are in their infancy again
and have no vigor in them.

FERDINAND:
So they are;
my spirits, as in a dream, are all bound up.
My father's loss, the weakness which I feel,
the wreck of all my friends, nor this man's threats,
to whom I am subdued, are but light to me,
if I might but through my prison once a day
behold this maid. All other corners of the earth,
let liberty make use of; I have enough space
in such a prison.

PROSPERO:

MIRANDA:
Be of comfort;
my father is of a better nature, sir,
than he appears by his speech. This is unwonted,
what just now came from him.

PROSPERO:
You shall be as free
as the mountain winds. But then, you must exactly do
all points of my command.

ARIEL:
To the syllable.

PROSPERO:
Come, follow. Do not speak for him.

GONZALO:
I beseech you, sir, be merry; you have cause,
and so do we all, of joy; for our escape
is much beyond our loss. Our hint of woe
is common; every day some sailor's wife,
the masters of some merchant and the merchant
have just our theme of woe. But for the miracle,
I mean our preservation, few in millions
can speak like us. Then wisely, good sir, weigh
our sorrow with our comfort.

ALONSO:
Please, be quiet.

SEBASTIAN:
He receives comfort like cold porridge.

ANTONIO:
The visitor will not give him over so.

SEBASTIAN:
Look, he's winding up the watch of his wit;
by and by it will strike.

GONZALO:
Sir,—

SEBASTIAN:
One. Tell.

GONZALO:
When every grief is entertained that is offered,
it comes to the entertainer—

SEBASTIAN:
A dollar.

GONZALO:
Sorrow comes to him, indeed. You
have spoken truer than you intended.

SEBASTIAN:
You have taken it more wisely than I meant you should.

GONZALO:
Therefore, my lord,—

ANTONIO:
Fie, what a spendthrift he is of his tongue!

ALONSO:
I beg you, spare me.

GONZALO:
Well, I have done. But yet—

SEBASTIAN:
He will be talking.

ANTONIO:
Which of them, he or Adrian, for a good
wager, will begin to crow first?

SEBASTIAN:
The old cock.

ANTONIO:
The cockerel.

SEBASTIAN:
Done. What's the wager?

ANTONIO:
A laugh.

SEBASTIAN:
It's a match!

ADRIAN:
Though this island seems to be a desert—

SEBASTIAN:
Ha, ha, ha! So, you're paid.

ADRIAN:
Uninhabitable and almost inaccessible—

SEBASTIAN:
Yet—

ADRIAN:
Yet—

ANTONIO:
He could not miss it.

ADRIAN:
It must needs be of a subtle, tender, and delicate
temperance.

ANTONIO:
Temperance was a delicate wench.

SEBASTIAN:
Yes, and a subtle one; as he most learnedly delivered.

ADRIAN:
The air breathes upon us here most sweetly.

SEBASTIAN:
As if it had lungs, and rotten ones.

ANTONIO:
Or as if it were perfumed by a fen.

GONZALO:
Here is everything advantageous to life.

ANTONIO:
True; except for the means to live.

SEBASTIAN:
Of that there is none, or little.

GONZALO:
How lush and lusty the grass looks! How green!

ANTONIO:
The ground is indeed tawny.

SEBASTIAN:
With a touch of green in it.

ANTONIO:
He is not missing by much.

SEBASTIAN:
No; he is but completely mistaking the truth.

GONZALO:
But the rarity of it is—which is indeed almost
beyond credit—

SEBASTIAN:
As many vouched-for rarities are.

GONZALO:
That our garments, being, as they were, drenched in
the sea, still hold their freshness and
glosses, being rather newly-dyed than stained with
salt water.

ANTONIO:
If but one of his pockets could speak, would it not
say he lies?

SEBASTIAN:
Yes, or he is very falsely pocketing his report.

GONZALO:
I think our garments are now as fresh as when we
first put them on in Africa, at the marriage of
the king's fair daughter Claribel to the King of Tunis.

SEBASTIAN:
It was a sweet marriage, and we are prospering well on our return.

ADRIAN:
Tunis was never graced before with such a paragon as
their queen.

GONZALO:
Not since the time of the widow Dido.

ANTONIO:
Widow! A pox on that! How did that widow get in?
Widow Dido!

SEBASTIAN:
What if he had said 'widower Aeneas' too? Good Lord,
how you take it!

ADRIAN:
'Widow Dido' you said? You are making me study that.
She was of Carthage, not of Tunis.

GONZALO:
This Tunis, sir, was Carthage.

ADRIAN:
Carthage?

GONZALO:
I assure you, it was Carthage.

SEBASTIAN:
His word is more than the miraculous harp; he has
raised the wall and the houses too.

ANTONIO:
What impossible thing will he make easy next?

SEBASTIAN:
I think he will carry this island home in his pocket
and give it to his son for an apple.

ANTONIO:
And, by sowing its kernels in the sea, he will bring
forth more islands.

GONZALO:
Yes.

ANTONIO:
Why, in good time.

GONZALO:
Sir, we were talking about how our garments seem now
as fresh as when we were at Tunis for the marriage
of your daughter, who is now queen.

ANTONIO:
And the rarest that ever came there.

SEBASTIAN:
Hold on, I beseech you, widow Dido.

ANTONIO:
Oh, widow Dido! Yes, widow Dido.

GONZALO:
Is not, sir, my doublet as fresh as the first day I
wore it? I mean, in a way.

ANTONIO:
That sort was well fished for.

GONZALO:
When I wore it at your daughter's marriage?

ALONSO:
You cram these words into my ears against
the stomach of my sense. I wish I had never
married my daughter there! For, coming from there,
my son is lost and, in my opinion, she too,
who is so far removed from Italy
that I will never see her again. Oh, you my heir
of Naples and of Milan, what strange fish
has made his meal on you?

FRANCISCO:
Sir, he may live.
I saw him beat the surges under him,
and ride upon their backs; he trod the water,
whose enmity he flung aside, and breasted
the most swollen surge that met him. His bold head
he kept above the contentious waves, and he oared
himself with his good arms in a lusty stroke
to the shore, that over his wave-worn basis bowed,
as if it were stooping to relieve him. I do not doubt
that he came alive to land.

ALONSO:
No, no, he's gone.

SEBASTIAN:
Sir, you may thank yourself for this great loss,
that you would not bless our Europe with your daughter,
but would rather lose her to an African;
where she is at least banished from your eye,
who has cause to wet the grief of it.

ALONSO:
Please, be quiet.

SEBASTIAN:
You were kneeled to and importuned otherwise
by all of us, and the fair soul herself
was weighed between loathness and obedience, at
which end of the beam should bow. We have lost your
son,
I fear, for ever. Milan and Naples have
more widows in them of this business's making
than we bring men to comfort them.
The fault is your own.

ALONSO:
So is the dearest of the loss.

GONZALO:
My lord Sebastian,
the truth you speak lacks some gentleness
and the right time to speak it in. You are rubbing the sore,
when you should be bringing the plaster.

SEBASTIAN:
Very well.

ANTONIO:
And most like a surgeon.

GONZALO:
It is foul weather in all of us, good sir,
when you are cloudy.

SEBASTIAN:
Foul weather?

ANTONIO:
Very foul.

GONZALO:
If I had a plantation on this isle, my lord—

ANTONIO:
He would sow it with nettle-seed.

SEBASTIAN:
Or docks, or mallows.

GONZALO:
And if I were the king of it, what would I do?

SEBASTIAN:
Escape being drunk for want of wine.

GONZALO:
In the commonwealth, I would do all things by contraries;
for I would admit no kind of traffic;
no name of a magistrate;
letters should not be known; riches, poverty,
and the use of service, none; contract, succession,
boundary, bound of land, tillage, vineyard, none;
no use of metal, corn, or wine, or oil;
no occupation; all men would be idle, all;
and women too, but innocent and pure;
no sovereignty—

SEBASTIAN:
Yet he would be king of it.

ANTONIO:
The latter end of his commonwealth forgets the
beginning.

GONZALO:
All things in common nature should produce
without sweat or endeavor. Treason, felony,
sword, pike, knife, gun, or the need of any engine,
I would not have; but nature should bring forth,
of its own kind, all foison, all abundance,
to feed my innocent people.

SEBASTIAN:
No marrying among his subjects?

ANTONIO:
None, man; all are idle: whores and knaves.

GONZALO:
I would govern with such perfection, sir,
as to excel the golden age.

SEBASTIAN:
God save his majesty!

ANTONIO:
Long live Gonzalo!

GONZALO:
And—do you mark me, sir?

ALONSO:
Please, no more. You are talking nothing to me.

GONZALO:
I do well believe your highness; and
I did it to minister an occasion to these gentlemen,
who are of such sensible and nimble lungs that
they always use to laugh at nothing.

ANTONIO:
It was you we were laughing at.

GONZALO:
Who, in this kind of merry fooling, am nothing
to you. So you may continue and laugh at
nothing still.

ANTONIO:
What a blow was given there!

SEBASTIAN:
If it had not fallen flat-long.

GONZALO:
You are gentlemen of brave metal; you would lift
the moon out of her sphere, if she would continue
in it for five weeks without changing.

SEBASTIAN:
We would do so, and then go bat-fowling.

ANTONIO:
No, good my lord, don't be angry.

GONZALO:
No, I assure you; I will not adventure
my discretion so weakly. Will you laugh
me to sleep, for I am very heavy?

ANTONIO:
Go to sleep, and listen to us.

ALONSO:
What, are you all so soon asleep! I wish my eyes
would, with themselves, shut up my thoughts. I find
they are inclined to do so.

SEBASTIAN:
If it please you, sir,
do not omit the heavy offer of it.
It seldom visits sorrow; when it does,
it is a comforter.

ANTONIO:
We two, my lord,
will guard your person while you take your rest,
and watch your safety.

ALONSO:
Thank you. I am wondrously heavy.

SEBASTIAN:
What a strange drowsiness possesses them!

ANTONIO:
It is the quality of the climate.

SEBASTIAN:
Why
does it not then make our eyelids sink? I do not find
myself disposed to sleep.

ANTONIO:
Nor do I; my spirits are nimble.
They all fell together, as if by consent;
they dropped, as if by a thunder-stroke. What might,
worthy Sebastian? Oh, what might?—No more—
And yet, I think I see it in your face,
what you should be. The occasion speaks to you, and
my strong imagination sees a crown
dropping upon your head.

SEBASTIAN:
What, are you awake?

ANTONIO:
Do you not hear me speak?

SEBASTIAN:
I do; and surely
it is a sleepy language and you are speaking
out of your sleep. What is it you said?
This is a strange repose, to be asleep
with your eyes wide open; standing, speaking, moving,
and yet so fast asleep.

ANTONIO:
Noble Sebastian,
you are letting your fortune sleep—or rather, die; you are winking
while you are waking.
